Back to hub

petal & thorn

even petals can cut, and thorns can bloom…

0: Prologue

The world only knew half of her.

Petal: the idol, the angel, the smile that sold out arenas and held the nation's breath in the palm of her hand.
Thorn: the gamer, the ghost, the girl who never quite stopped running from the blood-soaked echo of her own name.

They called her Solar like it meant warmth, like it erased the cold corners of her story.
But even the sun casts shadows.

On stage, under violet lights, she was flawless. Lips stained coral, eyes rimmed with shimmer, fingers curled around a mic like it had always been hers.
She bowed like she believed in gratitude. She sang like it never hurt to be seen.

But behind the stage, behind the glassy screens and flawless filters, she lived twice.

By day, she belonged to Le Sserafim—a sisterhood of glitter and steel.
By night, she disappeared into flickering pixels, into Overwatch lobbies and 3 a.m. scrims, into a world where her name wasn't branded or beautiful—just clutch.

And somewhere in between, she fell in love with silence.
The kind only broken keyboards and unsent messages know.

People said idols couldn't be real.
Gamers couldn't be graceful.
Girls couldn't be both.

She said nothing.

But she remembered the bruises of her childhood, remembered how Chaerin's arms once kept her together when the walls were screaming.
She remembered Jaehyun's voice over the phone, warning her of shadows he couldn't name.
She remembered the way Rascal looked at her—not as a product, not as a pretty face—but as a teammate.

And she remembered the moment she saw him.

Jungwon.

A storm in the shape of a boy. The calm before every disaster she didn't know she was begging for.

She hadn't planned to fall in love with him.
Or Minji.
Or the chaos that would follow.

She just wanted to play the game.

But the game had other rules.
And so did the world.

And soon, the world would learn:

Even petals can cut.
And thorns... can bloom.

1: Respawn

At 6:00 a.m., Solar was already awake.

Not from an alarm. She hadn't needed one in years.

The city outside still slept. Buildings loomed like sleeping giants beneath a velvet sky, their windows faintly glowing with the last flickers of midnight. The apartment was cold, quiet, humming with silence—the kind that used to make her skin crawl when she was younger, when silence meant someone was angry, or watching, or gone.

Now, it was a comfort. A rare one.

She rolled over in bed and stared at the ceiling. The paint was uneven up close. A crack ran down one corner, barely visible unless you were searching for something wrong.

She wasn't sure how long she stayed there, breathing in the sterile stillness of the dorm.

Another day of pretending she didn't want more.

Her setup was modest—by idol standards, at least. A triple-monitor gaming rig took up one corner of the Le Sserafim dorm's shared living room. Neon blue cables tangled around ring lights and a battered mousepad. Custom pink headphones dangled off one monitor. A mountain of empty energy drink cans rose beside them like a shrine to caffeine-fueled escape.

She sat at the edge of her gaming chair, legs folded beneath her. Her headset clicked into place with practiced ease. The screens flared to life in the dim room, casting light across her face. She looked tired, but not dull. Her body ached from dance rehearsals. Her calves throbbed from endless jumps. Her lower back burned from yesterday's stunts.

But her fingers were fast. Her eyes were sharp.

In-game, everything else fell away.

"Kill the Ana. No sleep, no nade. Let's go... we dive here—three, two—go."

Her voice was crisp, measured. She didn't shout, didn't command. Just dropped the words like stepping stones and trusted the team to follow.

They did.

A glimmer of tracer light blinked across the screen. Her Pulse Bomb landed behind the enemy Reinhardt—timed perfectly to clip the supports. The killfeed lit up. Team kill.

Victory.

The match ended in golds and soft light.

She leaned back, just slightly. A slow exhale escaped her lips.

"Another one," she whispered.

She queued again.

A shuffling sound broke the stillness behind her.

"Tell me you've at least eaten," came Yunjin's voice, muffled under a blanket. She padded into the room, hair a mess, hoodie slipping off one shoulder.

Solar didn't look away from the screen. "Coffee counts."

"No, it doesn't." Yunjin dropped onto the couch, squinting at the game. "You're already Top 500 again?"

Solar shrugged. "Not trying to flex, but yeah."

Yunjin yawned, then rubbed at her eyes like a concerned raccoon. "You've been on edge all week. What's going on?"

Solar hesitated. The hum of the PC filled the gap.

Then she said it.

"I'm auditioning."

Yunjin blinked, suddenly more awake. "Auditioning? Like... for what? A drama? A new group?"

A pause.

Solar finally turned.

"San Francisco Shock."

Silence stretched thin between them.

Yunjin stared. "Wait—the Overwatch League team?"

Solar nodded.

"They scouted me."

It happened last week.

A DM from a burner account. No profile picture. No bio. She'd almost deleted it on sight.

hey. you're solar, right?

we've been watching you.

Then came the Discord link. She hesitated before clicking. The username in the call made her breath hitch.

Crusty.

The head coach.

His voice was low, even. No frills. "You have instincts we can't teach. If you're interested... we're holding a private trial."

She didn't ask how they found her. She already knew.

Her VODs were out there—Top 500 ranked Tracer, consistent winrate, shotcalls as sharp as scalpels. She just never expected anyone to notice. Not for real.

The call lasted twelve minutes.

Long enough to change everything.

The audition was held on a Sunday night, in a locked server. No press. No fans. Just five players—four starters, one trial.

And Rascal.

She knew him the moment he spoke. Calm, clipped Korean-English. Confident without being arrogant. The kind of voice that cut through noise like a blade.

"Solar? That's your handle?"

"Yeah."

"Nice name."

"Thanks. Yours isn't bad either."

He chuckled. "Jeez. Hope you play better than you flirt."

Solar smirked. "Guess you'll find out."

She didn't expect it to click so fast. Their synergy was instant—like muscle memory from a life she never lived. Rascal set up the fights with surgical precision; she danced through the chaos like lightning.

They didn't just win.

They dominated.

By match three, one of the tank mains joked, "Yo, just rename the team Rascal & Solar."

The next morning, she woke up to a notification.

WELCOME TO THE SHOCK!

She stared at it until her eyes burned. Her hand trembled slightly as she shut off the screen.

Then she got ready.

Back in idol mode.

Hair curled, soft and shiny. Face painted in peach tones. Uniform pressed and clean. She smiled in all the right ways on the way to the van. Chaewon hummed beside her. Eunchae dozed off on Yunjin's shoulder.

The ride to the studio was quiet. Familiar. Fake.

They were filming a CF for skincare. The lights were brutal. The makeup had to be adjusted every twenty minutes to keep her glow "dewy, not sweaty."

"Smile more," the director barked. "Solar, bring the warmth. Your eyes are too cold."

She adjusted her posture. Flashed the practiced smile. The one she perfected years ago to survive interviews, fan meets, her father's criticism.

It wasn't fake.

It just wasn't hers.

Her mind drifted.

To a payload push. A last-second stall. Rascal's voice in her ear:

"You're better than you think you are."

Later, alone in the dressing room, she unlocked her phone.

A new message.

RASCAL: today was sick.
RASCAL: they loved you.
RASCAL: let me know if you ever want to duo outside of scrims.
RASCAL: or... hang out.

She stared at the blinking cursor. A beat passed.

SOLAR: is that an invite?

RASCAL: depends.
RASCAL: do you want one?

She didn't answer.

Not yet.

That night, Solar stood on the rooftop just above their dorms. A hoodie hung loose over her dress. Her breath ghosted in the cold air. Below, the Seoul skyline glittered like circuitry.

She hadn't been up here in years. Not since high school. Not since sneaking out with Chaerin to smoke flavored vape pens and pretend the world didn't hurt as much as it did.

They used to count stars and swap secrets. Solar had told her once that she wanted to be everything—singer, gamer, fighter, ghost.

Back then, Chaerin believed her.

She pulled the hoodie tighter.

Somewhere beneath the ache, a thrill stirred. Not fear—hunger.

To play. To fight. To be seen as more than just another pretty face in a polished lineup.

To matter.

Elsewhere in the city, a girl named Minji lay in bed with an iPad balanced on her chest, watching a fancam of Solar from their last stage. Her heart pounded in a way she didn't fully understand. It wasn't just admiration. It wasn't just envy.

She watched the clip three times before locking her screen and turning away, cheeks warm.

In a different apartment, Jungwon scrolled past a Twitter clip of an Overwatch team fight. His thumb paused.

Tracer blinked in and out of frame—clean, sharp, dangerous.

He didn't know her name yet.

But he would.



2: Split Screens

The official welcome package arrived by courier.

It was waiting for Solar when she got back to the dorm—sealed in matte-black wrapping with the SF Shock logo embossed in gold. She stared at it for a full thirty seconds before peeling it open.

A custom Shock jersey with her gamer tag: SOLAR across the back. A sleek headset, still warm from packaging. A letter—hand-signed by Crusty, short and clipped like the man himself:

You're one of us now. Don't waste it.

It didn't feel real until she tried on the jersey.

The fabric was softer than she expected. It fit like armor.

But the second she caught her reflection in the dorm mirror—idol makeup half-smudged, earrings still dangling, shoulders slightly hunched—Solar saw the cracks. This version of her didn't belong in that jersey. Not yet.

A knock came at the door.

Yunjin.

"You look like someone just confessed their love and immediately got hit by a bus," she said, stepping inside.

"Basically..." Solar tossed the box lid aside. "I'm in."

Yunjin's eyes widened. "You're officially Shock?"

Solar nodded, still gripping the hem of the jersey like a lifeline. "Yeah."

"Holy shit."

Yunjin crossed the room and hugged her tight, smelling faintly of dry shampoo and espresso. "I'm so proud of you. I'm also terrified for you, but I'll save the lecture."

"You're not gonna tell me to quit?"

"No. I get it. You've wanted this longer than you've wanted to debut."

Solar blinked, surprised. "Is it that obvious?"

"You light up when you play," Yunjin said. "Like... actually light up. Even more than when we perform."

Solar didn't answer.

She didn't have to.

The first team meeting was that night—private Discord call. No cameras. Just voice chat and game footage streaming between them.

Crusty's voice came through first—curt, no-nonsense, like he'd smoked ten cigarettes and still didn't have time for your excuses.

"We're running mock comps tomorrow. Rascal, cover comms with Solar. Super, take main callouts. Nero—try not to tilt the entire continent this time."

Nero scoffed immediately, dragging out the syllables like he couldn't believe the slander. "Why am I being singled out? I was a delight last time."

Crusty didn't even pause. "Because last time you flamed a support until they rage-quit and messaged our sponsor. Twice."

"Allegedly," Nero drawled, audibly smirking.

Solar bit down a laugh and muted her mic—but the snort still slipped out.

Nero perked up. "Oh-ho. Newbie thinks I'm funny."

She unmuted with a flat tone, dry as desert air. "I think you're loud."

Super howled in delight. "OHHHH! Bro, she came for your entire lineage."

"She bites," Nero muttered, amused despite himself.

Solar didn't miss a beat. "Just don't give me rabies."

Laughter exploded through the channel.

Rascal's voice finally chimed in, light and teasing with a lazy lilt. "God, I missed this chaos."

"You missed me," Super shot back dramatically. "Don't lie."

"I missed peace and quiet," Rascal said, deadpan.

Crusty's sigh was audible—like someone exhaling a headache. "Focus up. Next person who memes during strat talk is off scrims tomorrow."

The silence that followed was deeply fake.

Super cleared his throat with exaggerated formality. "Yes sir, coach sir. No tomfoolery here. Just wholesome esports professionalism."

Glister finally piped up, voice higher, warm and eager. "I think Solar and Rascal are kinda cracked together already."

Crusty groaned. "Don't start shipping your teammates."

But Nero was already typing in team chat:
solar/rascal OTP 🔥

Solar rolled her eyes so hard it hurt.

The call settled into strategy talk—map pools, scrim pairings, footage review. Solar tracked every detail like it was choreography. But her brain kept drifting to Rascal's voice. Low. Measured. Always one beat calmer than the chaos around him.

They were paired for the next day's scrims.

When the call ended, Rascal messaged her directly:

RASCAL: u good with genji/tracer split?
SOLAR: totally. you flex to cover the dive, i'll poke pressure.
RASCAL: synergy. love it.
SOLAR: also. if you ever flame me like you flamed nero last season, i'm unplugging your router.
RASCAL: lol deal.
RASCAL: also
RASCAL: thanks for not being weird about the team. a lot of ppl get weird.
SOLAR: oh, you mean like the girl who DMed your alt account 83 times last fall?
RASCAL: BRO
SOLAR: i'm not like other fangirls. i'm worse.
RASCAL: i'm scared and intrigued

The next day started before sunrise.

Solar did her makeup on autopilot. Something about war paint made her feel sharper. She skipped breakfast again. Her stomach was too tight.

The scrim server was up by 9:00 a.m.

By 9:05, Rascal was calling plays like they were in the finals.

Solar kept up without thinking. She didn't need to think. She just knew.

Her Tracer was a blur—slipping behind lines, baiting cooldowns, blinking into impossible angles and sticking Pulse Bombs with surgical precision. Rascal tracked her like a sixth sense. His Echo peeled pressure, dropped sticky bombs on whoever tried to dive her, and cleared a path with such clarity it felt rehearsed.

But it wasn't rehearsed.

It was just real.

By the end of the first hour, Super had declared them "actual soulmates." Nero grumbled, "It's disgusting." Glister typed again:
solar/rascal OTP

Crusty, unamused, called for another round.

In the dorm, Solar collapsed face-first into the couch.

Yunjin looked up from her guitar. "How'd it go?"

"Rascal hard-carried me," she mumbled into the cushion. "Like emotionally."

"Aw..."

"He told me I was cracked."

"That's gamer for love confession."

"Right?"

They giggled like teenagers.

Then the mood shifted.

Yunjin lowered her voice. "You gonna tell the others?"

"Eventually... when it's real."

"It is real."

Solar hesitated. "It's real in the game. But our company isn't gonna be thrilled about me playing tanky Korean boys instead of rehearsing for a comeback."

"You're not playing them," Yunjin teased. "Yet."

Solar threw a pillow at her.

That night, she dreamed of rooftops again.

Only this time, she wasn't alone.

Chaerin was there—Gucci boots kicking the edge, black nails tapping her phone.

"Why are you hiding?" Chaerin asked.

"I'm not."

"You always lie when you're about to do something reckless."

Solar looked down at the city. "It's not reckless. It's mine."

Chaerin smiled like she used to—feral, soft, wild.

"Then go get it, big sister."

The dream stuck with her all morning.

She typed and deleted a dozen messages to Chaerin, never sending them. Instead, she opened Instagram and posted a selfie—Shock jersey just barely visible at the bottom of the frame, lips glossed, eyes cold.

Within minutes, it was viral.

Among the likes and fire emojis, one comment stood out:

MINJI_OFFICIAL: beautiful as always. ✨

Solar stared.

Then typed:
SOLAR: you watching me? ;)

She hovered over send.

Paused.

Deleted it.

Across the city, Minji stared at the photo until her stylist called her name for the fourth time. She didn't respond. Just looked at that unreadable expression—Solar's face lit from behind, mouth slightly parted, gaze like the inside of a locked door.

"Minji," Hanni whispered, peering at her phone. "Are you okay?"

Minji locked the screen. "I'm fine."

But she wasn't.

She was falling.

Jungwon noticed too. He scrolled past the post at first. Then scrolled back.

Clicked it. Looked again.

There was something in her eyes. Something he'd never seen in idols. Not even in himself.

A kind of hunger.

He didn't like it. And he didn't understand why.

That evening, Solar logged back into the server.

Rascal was already there.

Waiting.

"Hey," he said.

"Hey," she echoed.

And just like that, they queued up again—silent understanding passing between them as easily as the cursor across her screen.

A new match. A new future.

And maybe—just maybe—a beginning neither of them could yet name.














3: Ghost Frames

The schedule hit her inbox at 11:42 p.m.

Solar blinked at the screen, eyes burning. She was still on the Shock server, reviewing scrim footage while Rascal cracked jokes in voice chat and Nero flamed everyone's decision-making like he got paid for it.

But the new message dragged her attention back to earth.

FROM: HYBE SCHEDULING
SUBJECT: VARIETY + PERFORMANCE BLOCK | URGENT
NOTES: Call time 6:00 a.m. sharp. You'll be performing Fearless and taping the talk segment. Costumes pending. Vocal check at 7:15.

Solar leaned back, exhaling slowly through her nose. Of course it was that song. Of course they wanted the most physically demanding routine the day after her second Shock scrim.

"Solar, you good?" Rascal's voice cut in—half teasing, half curious.

"Yeah," she replied automatically, forcing lightness into her tone. "Just reading the worst email of my life."

"From Nero?"

"Worse. My company."

Rascal laughed, warm and easy. "Rip."

She stared at his name lighting up the Discord overlay. Part of her wanted to say more. To explain. But another part of her—older, trained—just smiled into the silence.

"Guess I better log off," she said, her voice tight with something like guilt. "Call time in six hours."

"You're wild," Rascal replied, and she could hear the smile in it. "Sleep fast."



She didn't.

Instead, she lay in bed staring at the ceiling until her alarm dragged her up at 5:00 a.m.

Makeup. Hair. Idol mode.

Her limbs were leaden, her knees sore, her face hollow beneath concealer. But she showed up on set smiling.

"Great to see you again, Solar," the director said, already half-distracted. "Just remember to stay lively during the quiz section. Your face kind of drops when you're not talking."

Chaewon glanced at her, concern flickering behind her lashes.

"You okay?" she murmured as they passed in the hallway.

Solar smiled without teeth. "Always."



The stage was hot. The lights were blinding.

"Again!" barked the choreographer. "Hit the end pose sharper. Solar, you're late on the turn."

She nodded once, chest heaving, and reset. Her whole body screamed, but she didn't flinch.

After the fourth take, her vision swam.

"Water," Yunjin muttered, shoving a bottle into her hands. "You're running on battery fumes."

"I'm fine," Solar rasped.

"No, you're stubborn."

They sat side by side in the corner of the green room later, floor cool against their legs. Yunjin tuned her guitar absently, eyes flicking up every so often.

"You skipped lunch again."

"Didn't mean to."

"Right."

Solar sighed, dropping her head onto her knees. "I can't fall behind on either side. Not with Shock, not with this. If I drop one thing, I drop everything."

Yunjin strummed a chord, soft and low.

"You're not dropping anything," she said. "You're just not resting either."

There was no reply for that. Just the low buzz of exhaustion pressing against her skull.



Back at the dorm, Solar peeled off her stage outfit like it offended her. Her skin itched under the glitter. Her throat burned from overuse. She collapsed into her gaming chair and booted up her rig before her hair was even dry.

Scrims started in ten minutes.

She didn't miss a beat.



But Crusty noticed.

Halfway through the second map, Solar whiffed a critical Pulse Bomb. Then she missed a call. Then another.

"Hold," Crusty said through comms. "Pause."

Solar froze.

"Solar, you okay?"

"I'm fine," she said quickly. "Just a bad round."

A pause. Then Rascal's voice, lower than usual.

"You're quiet," he said. "Like... real quiet."

"I had a full day shoot," she admitted, swallowing hard. "My bad."

Silence.

Then Crusty said, not unkindly, "Let's sub her out this round. Rest. Eat something."

Solar sat in stunned silence as the match resumed without her.

She didn't log off. Just turned off her mic and sat there, staring at her keyboard.



A message popped up.

RASCAL: u okay?
RASCAL: not mad. just checking.
RASCAL: ur hands looked tired.
RASCAL: i know that look lol. used to get it in contenders.

She stared at the screen. Then typed slowly.

SOLAR: i'm not dropping this.
RASCAL: didn't say u were.
RASCAL: but u don't gotta bleed for it either.

Another message came in, seconds later.

RASCAL: take the jersey off if u ever forget why u put it on.
RASCAL: then put it back on when ur ready.

She read it twice. Three times.

Then got up, peeled off the jersey, folded it neatly, and set it on the desk.



Later, in the kitchen, Chaewon was cleaning something that didn't need cleaning.

Solar stepped in quietly.

"Hey," she said.

Chaewon didn't turn around. "I heard from Yunjin."

Solar waited.

"I don't care that you're playing with the Shock," Chaewon said, scrubbing harder. "I care that you're hurting and pretending you're not."

"I'm not trying to lie."

"I know," she said softly. "That's what worries me."



That night, Solar sat in bed, the room lit only by her phone screen.

She typed a message to Rascal. Deleted it. Typed another.

Finally settled on:

SOLAR: still want this. just needed today to suck.

The reply came almost instantly.

RASCAL: same.

She smiled. Just a little. Enough to feel like she could breathe again.



Across the city, in a pristine backstage dressing room, Minji watched the same performance for the third time.

The stage lights framed Solar like a silhouette drawn in gold—fierce, focused, but... off. Her usual spark was dimmer. Her voice a fraction too tight. Her gaze somewhere far away.

Minji watched the playback on her phone, earbuds in, screen inches from her face.

The stylist behind her fussed with lip tint. Hanni was singing something soft in the corner. But Minji didn't move.

Her thumb hovered above the screen, just barely brushing against the pause button. Solar's face froze mid-spin—half a smile, eyes unreadable.

Minji stared.

Then whispered to herself, so quiet no one heard:

"...where are you going?"


4: Static

It started with a tweet.

No tag. No mention. Just a side-by-side clip: one frame of a Shock scrim, the other from Solar's ranked stream six months ago—same Tracer mechanics, same blink patterns, same pixel-perfect bomb stick.

The caption:
"No way this isn't Solar on Shock. The movement? Identical. 👀"

By morning, it had 40,000 likes.

Then came the edits—slow-mo comparisons, montages, someone syncing her dance lines from "Fearless" with her killstreaks. A fan account posted:
"Can she break ankles and Rein shields? Queen behavior."

The real blow hit when a compilation went viral on TikTok.
Solar x Rascal | 'ENEMY' edit 🔥💥💘
1.2 million views in six hours.
The comments were chaos:

"wait why are they lowkey hot together?"
"bro i know a hard-carry situationship when i see one."
"does HYBE know their main vocalist is out here diving payloads and hearts?"

Solar watched it in stunned silence on the dorm bathroom floor at 7:48 a.m., mascara wand in one hand, her phone vibrating with notification after notification.

Then came the knock.

Yunjin.

"You're trending," she said flatly.

"I know."

"You okay?"

Solar's hands were trembling slightly. "It's just fans."

"It's never just fans," Yunjin said softly. "You know how this works."




The summons came at noon.

HYBE MANAGEMENT – MEETING ROOM 3A

The kind of message that doesn't ask—it tells.

Solar sat on the far side of a long glass table, jersey buried beneath a denim jacket. A senior PR rep sat across from her, flanked by a manager and someone from the branding team. All of them looked like they hadn't slept in years.

"Let me start by saying we're proud of you," the PR lead began, clasping her hands with the tension of someone about to deliver bad news. "You're ambitious. Strategic. That's why you debuted. That's why you're here."

Solar waited.

The PR rep glanced at her tablet. "But as you've seen, there's been... speculation."

"Leaks," the branding rep added with a tight smile. "Which fans love, but sponsors don't."

"We're not telling you to stop," the manager cut in—unusual for him to speak first. "We're telling you to be smart."

Solar didn't flinch. "Define smart."

The room exhaled.

"No public-facing posts in the jersey. No streaming under your name during off hours. No fanservice with your teammates."

Solar blinked. "Fanservice?"

"Shipping edits," the PR woman said briskly. "Hashtag pairings. You know how this goes. You're dating in the public eye, even if you're not. And if it's with another public figure—especially a male one—it turns volatile fast."

She bit down the urge to laugh.

"You're saying I can play for them... as long as I don't like anyone."

"We're saying," the woman replied, "don't give anyone a reason to think you do."

The silence was suffocating. Solar stared at the edge of the table. Her reflection stared back—composed, blank, razor-edged.

And then she spoke.

"I'm going to keep playing," she said quietly. "I'll keep it private. I won't use my name. But I'm not quitting."

The branding rep opened his mouth. The PR woman raised a hand to stop him.

"You're talented," she said finally. "Just don't forget—you're also watched."

She didn't go straight back to the dorm.

Instead, Solar wandered. Hoodie up, mask on, phone clenched in her hand. The city moved around her in a blur of noise and light—neon signs blinking past her peripheral, pedestrian chatter bleeding together into static.

Her legs moved on their own. No destination. Just distance.

From HYBE. From the headlines. From the weight of being watched.

She ducked into a café with tinted windows, ordered a coffee she didn't drink, and sat alone in the corner booth, back against the wall.

Her phone buzzed again.

NEW DM: JUNGWON_OFFICIAL

She stared at the notification for a moment. The little bubble felt heavier than it should've.

Then tapped it.

JUNGWON: people are watching.
JUNGWON: careful who you trust.

No emojis. No context. Just blunt concern masquerading as a warning.

Solar exhaled slowly. Typed back:

SOLAR: you too. you're not invisible either.

The typing dots blinked. Paused. Disappeared. Then finally reappeared:

JUNGWON: i'm not the one being dissected.
JUNGWON: i just notice when someone's slipping.

Her heart kicked once in her chest.

SOLAR: what do you want?

JUNGWON: just making sure you know what this costs.

She didn't reply.

Didn't have to.

She knew.

All too well.

By the time she returned to the dorm, the sun had dipped below the skyline, leaving the world washed in indigo. Her jersey still sat on her chair, folded neatly like a secret. She didn't touch it.

Instead, she powered on her PC.

Discord opened automatically. She hesitated before clicking online. For once, she wasn't ready.

Still, she joined the Shock server. Rascal was already in a voice channel, Super and Glister bantering in the background over some half-serious VOD critique.

She hovered.

Then clicked in.

"Look who lives," Super crowed. "Miss Top 500 K-idol herself."

"Hey," Solar said. Her voice didn't crack, even if something beneath it did.

Rascal didn't say anything at first. Then—

"You okay?" he asked. His tone was quieter than usual. Gentle.

"Yeah." She tried to sound casual. "Just got... talked at."

Super whistled. "Ah. The 'we love you but don't screw this up' meeting?"

"More like 'we love the exposure but don't be a person' meeting," she replied dryly.

Laughter burst from the others—Glister first, then Super's wheezing snort, and finally Rascal's softer chuckle.

"I've had six of those," Rascal muttered. "They don't get less stupid."

Some of the weight in her chest loosened.

"Scrim tomorrow?" she asked.

"Of course," Rascal replied. "Crusty's been holding Nero on a leash waiting for you."

Solar smiled.

Then, after a pause:

"Hey," she added. "Thanks for... not making it weird."

"You mean not posting thirst traps with our synergy clips?" Rascal teased.

"I mean... not treating me like a gimmick."

There was a beat.

Then, sincere this time:

"Was never gonna."

And that was it. No overdone sentiment. No performance.

Just a truth she didn't know she needed to hear.




The next day, she was sharper.

Not because she'd rested—she hadn't—but because the fire was back. The stakes had changed. Now, she wasn't just proving herself to her team or her fans.

She was defending her right to exist in both worlds.

Solar hit every Pulse Bomb. She dove on time, tracked Rascal like a shadow. They dominated.

Even Nero, voice dry and unimpressed as ever, grunted, "Guess the star's back."

"She never left," Rascal muttered.

Solar heard it.

Didn't say anything.

But her mouth twitched upward.




That night, she found herself on the rooftop again. Same wind. Same hum of the city below.

She pulled her hoodie tight against the chill and sat cross-legged at the edge, phone glowing in her lap.

Her messages were full.

Yunjin. Chaerin. Rascal. Fans. Mentions. Warnings.

She scrolled, then paused on Rascal's last message:

RASCAL: take the jersey off if u ever forget why u put it on.
RASCAL: then put it back on when ur ready.

She stared at it for a long time.

Then stood.

Walked back inside.

And pulled the jersey over her hoodie.

Not for the followers.

Not for the press.

Not for the people hoping she'd screw up.

For her.














5: Fault Lines

The first time Solar saw Minji again, they were both wearing too much makeup.

The lights on set washed everyone out, so the stylists layered it on like armor. But it wasn't enough to hide the quiet undercurrent running just beneath their skin.

They were filming a joint variety special—Le Sserafim, New Jeans, and two rookie boy groups in a chaotic lineup of charades, Q&A games, and high-energy dancing. The kind of event that forced idols to smile like their careers depended on it.

Because they did.

Solar stood in the corner of the waiting room, adjusting the cuff of her jacket, trying not to let her brain spiral. The Shock leak was still gaining traction online. HYBE had gone quiet again—not an apology, not a clarification, not a word. The silence was more terrifying than the scolding.

Across the room, Minji sat on a low couch, sipping water like it offended her. Her posture was perfect. Her expression was unreadable. Her gaze wandered aimlessly, until it landed on Solar.

They locked eyes.

Neither of them looked away.

"Yunjin," Solar murmured under her breath.

"I see her," Yunjin said without looking up from her phone. "She's not a threat."

"She's also not blinking."

Yunjin finally glanced up. "Okay. Maybe a little."

Minji rose—slow, deliberate, elegant in a way that felt almost rehearsed. Every step was measured. Every blink calculated.

Trailing behind her was Hanni, bouncing on her heels like a well-meaning golden retriever. "Hi, unnie!" she chirped.

Solar smiled reflexively. "Hey."

Minji stopped a step away, hands clasped lightly, her voice quiet but perfectly clear. "You look well."

"So do you," Solar replied. She hated how stiff her voice sounded. Like it didn't belong to her.

There was a beat of silence, heavy and delicate at the same time.

Then Minji tilted her head ever so slightly. "Rascal's Echo was clean last night."

Solar blinked. Her heart stuttered.

Yunjin's head snapped up. "Sorry?"

Minji's face didn't change. "Nothing. Just... the stream. It popped up on my feed."

Hanni coughed into her sleeve. "So anyway! Can't wait to play charades! I hope we don't get partnered with Jake again. He cheats."

The tension cracked—just a hairline fracture, but enough to breathe.

Minji stepped back. "Good luck today."

"You too," Solar said.

Just as Minji turned away, a familiar voice joined them—cool, neutral, almost bored.

"Solar."

She looked up—and there was Jungwon.

Flawless as always. Buttoned-up collar. Cold eyes.

His gaze flicked from her to Minji, then back again. There was something in the look—curiosity, maybe. Or calculation.

"You're everywhere lately," he said.

It wasn't a compliment.

Before she could respond, he walked past, not waiting for a reply.

Minji's posture stiffened, but she didn't look back.

Yunjin leaned in, dry as dust. "Did he just negg you on national television?"

"Apparently," Solar muttered.

They didn't talk about it again.

But later, while smiling for the camera and laughing at a joke she didn't hear, Solar could feel it—two gazes in two corners of the room, pressing into her spine like static.





That night, she didn't go straight home.

The car dropped her at the dorms, but she didn't move. Just stood on the sidewalk, her jacket zipped up to her chin, breath fogging in the cold.

A horn blared from across the street.

A black convertible idled under the streetlight. Music thumped faintly from the speakers, bass rattling the air.

And behind the wheel—sunglasses at night, glossy red nails tapping the steering wheel—was Chaerin.

"Get in, loser," she called. "We're going to corrupt your soul."

Solar blinked. "What the hell are you—"

"I was in the area. By which I mean I got bored, checked your schedule, and decided to rescue you from your spiral. You're welcome."

The passenger door swung open before Solar could argue.

She climbed in.





The bar was the kind of place that didn't need a name. Just neon lights and secrets.

It smelled like citrus and sweat and money. The booths were velvet. The lights were red.

Chaerin ordered drinks before Solar could say no—glowing cocktails with dry ice pouring over the rim like potion smoke.

"You look like hell," she said brightly.

"Thanks."

"No, seriously. You look like you haven't slept in a week and you've been living off instant ramen and emotional repression."

Solar took a long sip. It burned like truth.

Chaerin watched her over the rim of her glass. "So. Rascal."

Solar choked.

"Oh my God," Chaerin grinned. "You like him."

"I do not."

"You hesitated."

"I choked on alcohol!"

"You hesitated so loud I heard it across timelines."

Solar rubbed her face. "We're teammates."

"You're teammates with everyone. You don't look at Nero like that."

"I don't look at Rascal like anything!"

"Girl. You look at him like he's a save point."

There was a pause.

"...What?"

"Like you feel safer near him," Chaerin said, suddenly serious. "Like when you're about to die in a game and you finally find the glowing circle. That's what you look like."

Solar blinked.

For once, she didn't have a comeback.

Chaerin downed the rest of her drink, then sighed. "I'm proud of you, you know."

Solar turned her head sharply. "Wait, what?"

"You heard me."

"That's... weirdly heartfelt for you."

"I'll say something unhinged in like ten minutes, don't worry."

They clinked their glasses together, and the neon glow caught in Solar's lashes like fireflies.




It was almost 2 a.m. when she got home. Her makeup was wiped clean. Her body was heavy, but her mind wouldn't rest.

She sat at her PC. Turned on the monitor.

Discord auto-launched.

RASCAL: custom?

She stared.

SOLAR: yeah. just us?

RASCAL: if you want.

They dropped into voice chat.

No cameras. No filters. Just sound and stillness and something neither of them dared name.

The first few games were mechanical. Smooth. Clinical.

Then Rascal spoke.

"You okay?"

"I don't know."

"You've been moving like someone's watching."

"They are."

"I know," he said softly. "That's the problem."




They queued again.

Solar played angry. Free. Sharp enough to bleed.

At one point, she blinked into a 1v3, dropped a Pulse, recalled out with one health left.

The whole team exploded.

"Jesus, you're cracked," Rascal laughed.

"You're just now realizing?"

"Nah. Just didn't know cracked could look like that."

A beat.

Solar's voice was soft. "Sometimes I think I'm only good at this because everything else feels harder."

"Same," he replied.

No hesitation.




After their final win, neither of them left the queue.

The silence stretched between them like thread.

Solar exhaled. "What are we doing?"

Rascal didn't pretend not to understand.

"I don't know," he said. "But I don't want to stop."

Her throat felt tight.

"I should sleep."

"Yeah."

But neither of them logged off.

Eventually, she whispered, "Goodnight."

Rascal's voice was quiet, almost tender. "Night, Solar."




Minutes later, her phone buzzed.

RASCAL: did i push it?

She stared at the message, phone lighting up her face in the dark.

And typed nothing.












6: Collapse

It started in the way he didn't say good morning.

Usually, Rascal was already online by the time Solar logged into the Shock server—halfway through a match, throwing pings, or typing something stupid in team chat like "Suuuup nerds." But today, the channel was quiet. His status said "Online," but he hadn't said anything to her.

Not even a "yo."

Solar brushed it off at first. Everyone had off days.

But the silence stayed.

During scrims, his callouts were short. Clinical. No jokes. No teasing. No "let's go duo, cracked queen."

It shouldn't have mattered.

It did.

"Dive in three," Rascal said flatly during a team fight. "Solar, blink left."

She followed, executed the combo perfectly. They won the fight. But when she turned to say "nice play," his comm had already gone quiet.

Later, when Crusty debriefed the team, even Super noticed the shift.

"Yo, why do you two sound like coworkers in a tax firm?" he joked.

Nero didn't even look up from his keyboard. "Maybe they broke up and forgot to tell us."

Solar didn't laugh.



That night, she paced in her room for half an hour before messaging him.

SOLAR: hey. everything okay?

He replied ten minutes later.

RASCAL: all good. just tired.

Short. Neutral. Like they hadn't stayed up talking in whispers just a few nights ago.

Like nothing had happened.

She stared at the screen until her eyes burned.

Then, finally:

SOLAR: did i do something?

This time, it took him longer to answer.

RASCAL: no.
RASCAL: i just didn't want to be another thing you had to survive.

She read the message again and again, the words dull and sharp all at once.

What did that even mean?

What did she mean—to him?

She didn't respond.

Because if she let herself answer, she might tell him the truth.

That she didn't want to survive anything without him.



The next day, the rehearsal room was a mess of wires, ring lights, and too many idol groups in one space.

HYBE had planned a special collaboration stage—some massive PR event uniting their biggest names. It was chaos disguised as opportunity. Solar barely made it through the door before a stylist shoved a new mic pack into her hands and motioned her toward the wardrobe station.

She hadn't slept. Rascal hadn't messaged her again. Her head felt full of fog and static.

"Solar," someone called.

She turned—expecting Yunjin.

But it was Jungwon.

He stood by the hallway door, hands in his pockets, expression unreadable.

"I need to talk to you."

Solar blinked. "Now?"

He didn't answer. Just turned and walked into the empty dressing room beside the stage.

For a moment, she debated walking the other way.

But her feet moved before she made the choice.

The room was cold, quiet, and smelled like powder and vinyl.

"What?" she said, arms crossed.

Jungwon didn't look at her. "You act like I'm the enemy."

"You act like you know me."

Finally, he met her eyes. "I don't. But I know your type."

"Oh, great," she snapped. "Here we go."

"The ones who burn hot, then fall harder," he said evenly. "The ones who mistake momentum for invincibility."

Something inside her went still.

"I'm not falling," she said.

He tilted his head. "Aren't you?"

They stood there in silence, the air between them humming with something unspoken.

Then, softer:

"You remind me of someone I used to be."

That stopped her cold.

Jungwon's voice didn't shake, but something in his eyes did.

"That's why I can't ignore you."

Before she could speak, someone called for him from the hallway.

Jungwon turned.

Paused.

Then, without looking back: "Be careful who you let in."

And just like that, he was gone.

Leaving Solar in the mirror-lit quiet, unsure if she wanted to scream or follow him.


Later that night, her phone buzzed.

JAEHYUN: I'm outside.

That was all.

No warning. No context.

Solar stared at the screen like it was a ghost.

Then she threw on a jacket and went downstairs.

A black sedan idled at the curb. Sleek. Immaculate. Not HYBE-issued.

Jaehyun leaned against the passenger door, arms crossed, sunglasses still on despite the streetlight glow.

"You really shouldn't text like that," Solar said as she approached. "Feels like I'm about to be kidnapped."

He didn't smile. "Get in."

She sighed. "You know, normal siblings just text memes."

"I'm not a normal sibling," he said flatly, opening the door.

She climbed in.



They didn't drive far—just circled a block near the dorms and parked under a shadowed overpass. The kind of place where conversations didn't get overheard.

Jaehyun killed the engine. The silence was heavy.

Then he turned to her.

"You're too visible."

Solar blinked. "That's kind of the point. I'm an idol."

"I'm not talking about the stage."

She frowned. "If this is about that dumb Twitter thread—"

"It's not dumb when it's getting traction in places it shouldn't be," he said sharply.

He reached into the glovebox and handed her an envelope.

Inside: printed screenshots. Discord leaks. Fan edits. A Reddit thread titled "Is Rascal Dating A Kpop Idol?"

Her blood went cold.

"Where did you get this?"

"I have my ways," Jaehyun said.

"You're spying on me now?"

"I'm protecting you," he snapped. "From people who don't play fair. From companies that will hang you the moment you become a liability."

She clenched the folder. "Rascal isn't—"

"I don't care who he is," Jaehyun cut in. "I care that you're slipping."

Solar stared at him. "Why does it matter to you?"

His voice was low. Dangerous. "Because when they come for you, they'll come for all of us. You don't get to fall apart in public, Solar. You don't get that luxury."

She hated how calm he sounded.

Like he'd already planned for her to fail.

"I'm not going to fall," she said.

Jaehyun leaned back. Adjusted his sunglasses.

"You always say that. Until you do."

She didn't say anything.

Didn't have to.

Before she stepped out of the car, he spoke again—quieter this time.

"Don't forget who taught you how to survive."

And with that, the door clicked shut behind her.



Solar made it inside the dorm before she started shaking.

At first, it was just her hands—trembling at the edges, fingers twitching as she fumbled with the lock on the bathroom door. Then her breath caught in her throat.

And stayed there.

She dropped to the floor, back against the cold tile, hoodie still on, heart punching against her ribs like it was trying to escape.

No sound. No tears. Just pressure.

Like the whole world was closing in and she couldn't find the exit.

Her vision blurred.

She tried to breathe. Couldn't.

Her chest heaved, but no air came. Her mouth opened in silence.

And then—knocking.

Soft at first. Then firmer.

"Solar?"

Yunjin's voice.

No response.

The door creaked open.

And Solar couldn't hide it anymore.

Her arms were wrapped around her knees. Her face buried. Her whole body shivering like it was mid-earthquake.

"Hey—hey. It's okay."

Yunjin dropped to the floor beside her, no hesitation, no judgment.

She pulled Solar into her arms, gently, like gathering something already half-broken.

Solar didn't say anything. She couldn't.

But she clung.

Tight.

Yunjin's voice was barely above a whisper. "You're okay. You're not alone. Just breathe with me, okay? In, out. Like music."

They sat there for what felt like hours.

Yunjin held her like she had nowhere else to be.

Eventually, Solar's breath evened. Her body stopped shaking.

But the ache remained.

Not loud.

Just deep.

Heavy.

Yunjin smoothed her hair back. "You don't have to be anything right now. Not strong. Not brave. Not perfect."

Solar didn't answer.

She didn't need to.

She just buried her face in Yunjin's shoulder and let the silence hold her.

That night, she fell asleep in the bathroom, curled in Yunjin's lap.

The room was dark.

The air was still.

And somewhere across the city, Rascal stared at his phone, a message still unsent.

RASCAL: you were never something i wanted to let go of.









7: Tipping Points

The fans didn't notice it.

Not the tired drag behind her smile. Not the way her eyes didn't quite crinkle the way they used to. Not the half-second delay in her laugh.

They saw Solar—the idol. Radiant. Poised. Perfect.

She sat behind the fan-sign table in a soft lavender outfit, hair curled just right, pen poised like a queen's scepter. Fans filed by with albums and nervous giggles. Some wore Shock jerseys. Others held up their phones with Rascal edits on TikTok looping quietly in the background.

She signed every album. Smiled for every picture. Answered every question.

"Do you like Rascal?" one fan asked bluntly, holding up their phone.

Solar blinked, then laughed—carefully. "He's a great teammate."

Next.



After the event, backstage was dim and quiet. Yunjin pressed a cold drink into her hand.

"You're not blinking."

Solar took the bottle, unscrewed it slowly. "Didn't realize that was a crime."

Yunjin didn't smile. "I'm serious. You're here, but you're not here."

Solar didn't answer. Just sipped the drink.

Chaewon joined them, holding her tablet like it was a bible. "We've got vocal practice next. Then concept review."

Solar closed her eyes for a second too long.

Chaewon frowned. "You okay?"

"Fine," Solar said. Too fast.

Chaewon didn't argue. But she didn't leave either.

"You don't have to be everything at once," she said gently.

"I'm not."

"You are," Yunjin added. "You always are."

Before Solar could answer, Eunchae flopped into the chair beside them.

"Okay, but can we talk about that Rascal montage that hit a million likes? The fan edit where he's looking at her like she just carried the payload with her heart?"

Yunjin groaned.

Solar buried her face in her hands.

Chaewon just sighed, "God help us all."

They laughed—tired, but real.

For a moment, Solar almost felt like she was still standing.



Solar didn't expect Enhypen to be there.

The schedule had said vocal booth and filming segment with New Jeans. A two-hour shoot. In and out. But when she stepped into the waiting room with Yunjin, the energy shifted immediately.

Chaewon clocked it first. "They didn't tell us Enhypen was coming."

Yunjin muttered, "They never do when it's for PR stunts."

There was laughter in the corner. Jake. Sunoo. Ni-ki spinning in a chair.

And then—Jungwon.

He sat with perfect posture, phone in hand, dressed in a fitted slate sweater that made him look annoyingly expensive. His eyes flicked up the moment Solar entered.

He said nothing.

Neither did she.

"Of course," Yunjin whispered beside her. "Because the universe is petty."



The shoot started off light—an interview table set for a "casual hangout" concept. One girl from each group. One boy. Questions drawn from a bowl. Laughs on cue.

Solar pulled a slip.

"Who in the room do you think would be the most difficult to live with?"

Yunjin grinned across from her.

Solar's eyes drifted—to Jungwon.

He met her gaze. Unblinking.

The moment stretched.

"Jake," Solar said smoothly, holding up the paper. "He's too handsome. I'd get nothing done."

Jake raised both arms like a champion. "I humbly accept."

But her heart was thudding.



Later, off-camera, she ducked into the hallway to breathe. Alone for ten seconds. Just ten.

"Solar."

She turned.

Jungwon stood behind her, arms crossed. Still unreadable.

"You're good at that."

"At what?"

"Pretending."

She blinked. "You don't even know me."

He stepped closer. Just a little.

"But I see you."

Her breath caught.

"You're unraveling," he said softly. "And you're doing it so quietly no one even knows what they're watching."

She laughed. Bitter. "You're very poetic for someone who barely talks."

"I talk when it matters."

He wasn't angry. Just calm. Distant.

And somehow—right.

"Then what am I supposed to say to that?" she asked.

He looked at her like the answer was obvious.

"Nothing. Just... don't forget to notice the difference between attention and care."

She didn't respond.

Couldn't.

Because in that second, she wasn't sure what anyone saw in her anymore.

Only that Jungwon had seen too much.



Back on set, someone asked where she'd gone.

"Nowhere," Solar said.

But her hands wouldn't stop trembling.











8: Under the Spotlight

The email came in at 2:17 a.m.

[Subject: HYBE UNIT STAGE COLLABORATION – CONFIRMED]

Solar didn't open it right away. She was still lying in bed, one arm over her eyes, Rascal's unsent message lingering in the back of her mind like a ghost she couldn't touch.

But curiosity won.

She tapped the screen, scanned the lineup.

And froze.

[LE SSERAFIM x ENHYPEN COLLAB: Solar & Jungwon – Vocal/Choreo Duet]



The first rehearsal was at the company's private studio.

No cameras. No fans. Just concrete floors, mirrored walls, and a choreographer who liked to clap aggressively and say things like "closer," and "again," and "more eye contact."

Solar arrived early. Stretched in silence. Ignored the pounding in her chest.

Jungwon showed up five minutes late.

No apology. Just a nod. Eyes dark and unreadable.

They didn't speak until the music started.

The choreography was smooth, intricate. Hands brushing shoulders. Body turns that ended with breath close enough to feel. No room for pretense.

"Again," the choreographer said.

Solar stepped forward. Jungwon mirrored.

They moved like magnets.

It was too much.

Not enough.

She pulled away after the third run-through.

"I need water."

Jungwon followed her to the corner, where the cooler sat.

"You okay?" he asked.

She didn't look at him.  "Peachy."

"You're stiff."

She scoffed.  "Gee, thanks."

"I mean your movements," he clarified. "You dance like you're afraid of being seen."

Solar turned then, sharply. "And you look at people like you've already made up your mind."

The silence stretched between them. Thick. Breathing.

"I have," he said softly.

That threw her.

She stared. He didn't elaborate.

Just walked back to center.


By the fifth run, the choreographer was clapping.  "That's it. That's the energy. It's intimate. Tense. Perfect."

It wasn't perfect.

It was a loaded gun with no safety.



Back in the dressing room, Yunjin watched the footage on her phone.

"You're in trouble," she said without looking up.

Solar slumped onto the couch beside her.  "Why?"

"Because you didn't blink once during the bridge."

"I was performing."

"No," Yunjin said. "You were feeling."

Solar didn't respond.

Because she didn't know how to lie about that.




Solar barely had time to peel off her stage lashes before the knock came at the dorm door.

Chaerin didn't wait for an answer.

She waltzed in like she owned the place, Gucci bag on one arm, iced Americano in the other, sunglasses perched on her head even though it was nearly 10 p.m.

"Your hallway lighting is atrocious," she announced to no one.

Solar blinked.  "What the hell are you doing here?"

"Surprise!" Chaerin dropped her bag and collapsed onto the couch. "Campus is boring. My roommate has a boyfriend. I needed a hit of your chaos."

Yunjin poked her head out from the kitchen, eyes wide.  "Is that... your sister?"

"In the flesh," Solar muttered.

Chaerin grinned.  "And you must be the famous Yunjin. You're prettier than Solar said. Are you two dating yet?"

"Jesus," Solar groaned.



They settled into the living room with leftovers and chaos energy. Chaerin had a playlist going within minutes—half K-pop, half early 2000s emo.

She pulled up the video of Solar and Jungwon's rehearsal.

Solar snatched the phone.  "How do you even have this?"

Chaerin just sipped her drink, smug.  "I have my ways."

She raised an eyebrow as the video played.  "That boy is looking at you like you're either his muse or his next mistake."

Solar didn't respond.

But she didn't hit pause either.



Later, after Yunjin had gone to bed and the dorm was quiet, Solar and Chaerin sat on the rooftop, knees pulled to their chests, sharing a half-eaten bag of chips like teenagers.

"Remember when we used to sneak out and dance up here?" Chaerin asked.

Solar nodded.

"Remember Jihoon?" Chaerin added, not looking at her.

Solar went still.

The name hit like a pebble dropped in a still lake. Not violent. Just echoing.

"Uhuh," she mutters finally. "I remember."

"He ruined you," Chaerin said softly.

"No," Solar whispered. "I let him."

They sat in silence.

Then Chaerin nudged her shoulder.  "So what's this Jungwon boy gonna do?"

Solar didn't answer.

Didn't have one.

But when she looked up, the stars were out.

And they felt closer than they had in years.



The photo dropped at 3:04 p.m.

Solar was in the middle of stretching for rehearsal when her phone buzzed three times in rapid succession. Group chat. Twitter alert. Slack message from her manager.

She opened Twitter first.

And there it was.

A blurry but unmistakable photo of her and Jungwon exiting the practice room. Not touching. Not smiling. But close. Too close.

The caption read:
"solarwon is REAL???"

The replies were worse. Screenshots. Zoom-ins. Comparisons to Rascal. Fancams. Fan wars. Accusations.

#SOLWON was trending by 3:12 p.m.

Her heart dropped.

She opened her group chat with Le Sserafim. Yunjin had already messaged:

YUNJIN: Don't say anything yet. We're handling it.
CHAEWON: PR is already on a statement draft.
EUNCHAE: wait is this bad or like... free promo

Solar couldn't breathe.

A message popped up from an unknown number:

UNKNOWN: careful who sees you in soft focus.

She stared at it until it disappeared. Deleted.

Then her phone vibrated again.

MINJI: congrats on the attention.

No emoji. No punctuation. Just ice.

Solar wanted to scream.

Then came the final blow.

A ping from Discord.

RASCAL: is this the part where i ask if i should be worried

She stared at the message. Read it again. And again.

But she didn't respond.

She didn't know how to.

The noise was too loud.

The room too bright.

Everything spinning.

Someone called her name.

She looked up.

The rehearsal space blurred around the edges.

And all Solar could think was—

This is how it starts.




















9: Unspoken Things

Solar noticed it the second she logged in.

Rascal wasn't in voice yet.

The rest of the Shock were already there—Super joking loudly about how Glister needed to switch shampoos, Nero grumbling about ping, Crusty going over comp rotations like nothing had happened.

But Rascal's slot sat empty.

She tried not to read into it.

Until ten minutes passed.

Then twenty.

She scrolled through old messages. The last one was still unread.

She debated sending a new one.

Didn't.

Then, finally—he joined.

"Sup," came his voice. Flat. No sing-song tease. No cheeky insult. Just one syllable, clipped and hollow.

Solar blinked at her monitor, sitting a little straighter. "Hey," she said quietly.

He didn't answer.

Not even a delay. Just... nothing.


They loaded into the scrim. Map: Circuit Royal.

Solar's hands were steady, but her head was screaming. Every comm line felt sharp around the edges. Her Tracer still danced, still blinked and bombed and baited, but it felt like muscle memory. Like she was watching someone else wear her skin.

And Rascal?

Rascal was all business.

He gave clear comms. Precise timings. Called cooldowns with surgical efficiency.

But there was no warmth. No banter. No quiet compliments tucked into a kill confirm. No half-laughed insults after a clutch play.

It was like someone had hit "mute" on their friendship.

The team noticed.

Glister hesitated before one fight, then broke the silence with a forced chuckle. "Guess our duo synergy's on break today, huh?"

Super made it worse. "Wow, awkward silence buff. Y'all good or...?"

Crusty sighed. "Focus up. We're behind in tempo."

They won the scrim anyway.

But it felt like a loss.



Solar ripped off her headset the moment the screen went dark. Sat in the shadows of her room, the blue glow of the monitor reflecting in her eyes like a spotlight she couldn't turn off.

Then—

A message.

RASCAL: just don't get distracted. that's how people start losing.

She stared at it, the cursor blinking beneath his name like it was daring her to respond.

She didn't.

Just locked her screen. Let the silence swell.



That night, just after midnight, her phone buzzed again.

A voice note.

From him.

She almost didn't open it.

Almost.

But her thumb hovered. Then tapped.

His voice was quiet. Lower than usual. Like he'd recorded it in the dark, maybe after everyone else was asleep. Maybe when the silence got too loud.

"...wasn't trying to be cold," it started. "I just—needed a minute. You're everywhere right now. And it's not your fault. But it's hard to play next to someone who makes the whole room tilt."

Pause.

A breath.

"You're still cracked. You're still you. Just... don't forget why you're here. And don't break my heart, alright?"

Click.

Message ended.

No follow-up.

No second message.

Just silence.



The HYBE building at night was a different kind of quiet.

Not peaceful. Just hollow.

Solar moved down the hall with her hoodie up, face bare, earbuds in but no music playing. Her body ached from practice. Her mind felt heavier.

She was halfway to the elevator when she saw her.

Minji.

Alone, leaning against the vending machine at the far end of the corridor like she'd been waiting—or like she just didn't feel like leaving yet.

Their eyes met.

Neither of them moved.

Minji looked beautiful, of course. She always did. High ponytail. Black sweats. Gold earrings that caught the light just enough to feel intentional. Her expression unreadable.

Solar's heart kicked.

She considered walking past. Pretending she didn't see her.

But she didn't.

She stopped.

"Hey," Solar said softly.

Minji didn't reply right away. Just looked at her.

Then: "Do you like him?"

The words were clean. Unflinching. Like they'd been rehearsed.

Solar blinked. "I..."

She didn't finish.

Minji didn't let her.

"Was I ever someone you saw?" she asked. "Or just someone who saw you first?"

The fluorescent light above them flickered faintly.

Solar wanted to explain. She wanted to say it wasn't like that. That everything was blurry right now. That she never meant to hurt anyone. That she didn't even know what she wanted.

But none of it would've mattered.

Minji didn't need an answer.

She already had one.

So Solar just said, "I'm sorry."

And Minji nodded once.

"Me too."

Then she turned and walked down the hall, her steps soft and certain, like she'd already made peace with a war that hadn't even been declared.

Solar stood there until the elevator doors opened.

She didn't get in.



The conference room felt colder than usual.

Maybe it was the fluorescent lights. Maybe it was the way no one looked her in the eye.

Solar sat with her hands folded in her lap, nails biting into her palm, trying to control her breathing. Her manager sat beside her, lips pressed thin. Across the table, two HYBE executives flipped through a slim folder like it weighed more than it should have.

The room smelled faintly of coffee and toner.

No one spoke for a long time.

Then one of them—a woman with a sleek bob and perfect posture—closed the folder.

"You've become a liability."

Solar didn't flinch. She'd rehearsed that line in her head all morning.

The other executive—older, with silver at his temples—spoke next. "This company supports your growth. Your ambitions. But you knew the expectations when you signed."

Her manager shifted beside her. "The photo was an unfortunate angle. There's no proof of anything inappropriate—"

"We're not here to debate optics," the woman said sharply. "We're here to remind her of her contract."

She pushed the folder forward.

Solar opened it with shaking fingers.

There it was. In plain, unfeeling language.

A clause: full disclosure of any personal entanglements that could impact group image, endorsements, or brand affiliations.

"If this continues," the man said calmly, "you'll have to choose."

Solar looked up.

"What does that mean?"

"It means," the woman replied, "that you either maintain your image and your position here—or you risk both careers."

Silence.

Her throat felt tight. Her pulse was screaming.

"I'm not dating him," she said. It wasn't a lie. Not yet.

They stared at her.

"We don't care if you are," the man said. "We care what the public thinks."

Solar swallowed. "So I'm supposed to stop being seen in public? Stop being human?"

"You're supposed to stay out of headlines that aren't about your work," the woman snapped. "You want to play with the Shock? Fine. But no more rumors. No more photos. No more scandal."

She slid the folder back.

"You're walking a thin line, Solar. Don't make us decide for you."



Outside, the air was sharp and bright and full of noise.

Solar walked down the block alone, hoodie up, hands buried in her sleeves. Her chest was hollow. Her head a riot.

Then her phone buzzed.

She looked down.

No name. No words. Just a link.

She clicked.

A video clip.

Her and Jungwon.

On stage. Rehearsal footage, leaked somehow. The way he looked at her. The way she didn't look away.

The internet was already eating it alive.

Solar locked her phone.

And for a second—just one—she wished she could disappear.

























10: Spotlight Burn


Solar didn't know what to expect when she logged into comms that afternoon.

Part of her wondered if Rascal would still be quiet. Distant. Cordial in that polite, aching way that made her want to scream. Another part wondered if he'd just avoid her altogether—keep it professional and forget everything that had existed in the space between their banter.

But he was already there when she joined.

The first thing she heard was his laugh.

It was small. Real. The kind that hit like sunlight through a window you didn't know you missed.

"Yo," he said when she entered. "Finally. Was starting to think you ghosted."

Solar hesitated, then smiled slightly. "You wish."

The rest of the team piled in. Nero grumbling. Super immediately shouting over Glister about some meme. It felt... familiar. Almost.

But there was something different in Rascal's voice now.

Lighter, maybe. Or forced.

She couldn't tell.

They queued into practice matches. Their synergy was back—mostly. The moves were there. The timing. But not the instinct.

Not the click.

At one point, Rascal made a perfect flank play. She followed up with a Pulse Bomb. Triple kill.

"Nice," he said, almost too soft for the mic to pick up.

It shouldn't have meant anything.

But it did.

After scrims, Solar lingered.

So did he.

There was a pause—long, soft static between them—before Rascal finally said:

"You okay?"

His voice wasn't teasing. No smirk behind the words. Just that quiet, cautious tone he rarely used.

Solar swallowed.

"I don't know," she admitted.

He chuckled, but it didn't reach his throat. "That bad, huh?"

"I feel like I'm... splitting," she murmured. "Like if I keep doing this, something's going to give."

Rascal didn't fill the silence.

Didn't offer advice.

Just sat with it.

Then finally, he said:

"You don't have to tell me everything. I get it. But don't disappear on me next time. Just say something. Even if it's dumb."

Solar looked down at her keyboard.

"I'm not used to people sticking around when I get messy."

"You're not messy," Rascal said. "You're tired. And tired people forget how much they matter."

Then he logged off.

And the silence that followed felt less like an ending—and more like a pause.



Yunjin didn't say anything at first.

She just stood in the kitchen, arms crossed, watching Solar drop her bag by the door like she was made of static and silence. The dorm was too quiet. Eunchae was out. Chaewon asleep early. It was just them.

Solar opened the fridge and stared at it like the answer to her life was somewhere between the leftover curry and expired almond milk.

"You gonna keep pretending everything's fine?" Yunjin said finally.

Solar shut the fridge. Slowly.

"I'm not pretending."

Yunjin scoffed. "You're literally the best actor I know. You should've debuted as a drama lead."

Solar turned, leaning against the counter. Her eyes were tired. Her voice was flat.

"What do you want me to say?"

"I want you to stop treating me like I'm just your bandmate."

That landed hard.

Solar blinked. "You're not."

"Then why don't you talk to me anymore?" Yunjin's voice cracked slightly, like she wasn't expecting it to. "You told Rascal you're not okay. You told him. You even told Jungwon something back in that studio. But me? Nothing."

Solar stared at her.

"I didn't mean to—"

"You never mean to," Yunjin snapped. "But you do it anyway."

Silence.

Yunjin looked away, wiping under her eye with her sleeve like it didn't matter. Like she wasn't crying.

"I'm not mad that you're close with them," she said. "I'm mad that you used to look at me like I was your safe place. And now I feel like a placeholder."

Solar's heart thudded.

"You're not a placeholder."

"Then prove it."

Solar stepped closer, but stopped short. She didn't know how to explain the noise in her head. The pressure. The way everything felt too loud or too distant or too much all at once.

"I'm trying," she said.

Yunjin didn't answer.

She just turned away, quietly.

And Solar stood there, still holding her breath, like maybe if she didn't move, everything might stop falling apart.



It started with the wrong lyric.

They were filming a quick performance for a music broadcast—one of those pre-recorded segments that aired between interviews. Simple choreography. Pre-synced vocals. Nothing Solar hadn't done a thousand times before.

But her head was somewhere else.

Yunjin hadn't said more than two words since their talk. Chaewon was watching her more closely than usual. Her manager had started hovering again, asking if she was getting enough sleep.

Solar said yes.

She lied.

When the cameras started rolling, everything felt slightly off-axis. The lights too bright. The music too loud. Her heart thudded in her chest, but not with rhythm—just weight.

Halfway through the second verse, she missed her cue.

Not by much. A half-second. A single beat.

But enough.

The playback didn't cover it. The cut couldn't save it. She saw the staff flinch. Saw the choreographer glance at the monitor. Even Eunchae looked back mid-step, confused.

They didn't reshoot.

Just moved on.

Solar wiped her face with a towel afterward, trying not to shake.

"Hey," Yunjin said softly, approaching her from the side. "You okay?"

Solar nodded. "I just blanked for a second."

Yunjin didn't push.

Didn't say "I told you so."

Just handed her a water bottle and walked away.



That night, she logged into scrims late.

Crusty wasn't pleased.

"You're running soft today," he said after the second loss. "That last push was on you. You blinked past support pressure and left Rascal open."

"I didn't mean to—"

"You did," Crusty snapped. "You didn't focus. Don't make excuses."

Solar felt the words crawl into her skin like ice.

The next round went worse.

She missed a Pulse Bomb. Ate a sleep dart. Let Nero get picked without peeling for him.

"God, can we stop pretending we're a promo team?" Nero growled. "Some of us are here to win."

Super tried to calm him, but the damage was done.

Rascal didn't say anything.

When the round ended, Solar yanked off her headset and sat in silence, breathing hard.

The monitor glowed. Discord pinged.

GLISTER: hey don't beat yourself up
RASCAL: it happens
RASCAL: just breathe

But she didn't respond.

She couldn't.

Not yet.



Danielle posted the story late.

Just past midnight. Black and white selfie. Eyes cast down, lips barely parted. The caption read:

"it's always the ones who want to be seen the most who forget who was looking first."

No tags. No geotag. Nothing but the storm that followed.

Within an hour, fan pages were speculating. Threads exploded. A few old clips resurfaced—Minji and Solar laughing backstage, one photo of Jungwon and Solar side by side from a rehearsal. A blurry frame of Rascal's stream where he smiled after reading one of Solar's callouts.

The internet had decided.

Solar was being passed around.

She closed the app.

The silence in the dorm felt hostile.

She tried to sleep.

Failed.

Eventually, she pulled on a hoodie and slipped out, phone in hand, no destination in mind. She just needed to move. To breathe something that didn't smell like sweat and pressure.

She made it halfway down the block when she saw him.

Jungwon.

Leaning against the railing outside the rehearsal building, hood up, phone in hand. He looked like he'd been standing there for a while.

He didn't flinch when she approached.

Just looked up. Met her eyes.

They didn't say anything.

Not at first.

Then—

"You okay?" he asked softly.

It wasn't pity. It wasn't concern. It was... presence.

Solar blinked.

"No," she whispered.

Jungwon nodded once.

Then, gently, he held out the drink in his hand.

"Peach tea. Thought you might like it."

She took it. Fingers brushing.

Then sat beside him on the rail.

For the next fifteen minutes, they didn't speak.

Just shared the air. The night. The silence.

And it was the first time in days that Solar felt like she could actually exhale.















11: The Weight of Wanting

The studio felt like a fishbowl.

Glass walls. Harsh lighting. Too many voices that said too little.

Solar sat on the edge of the practice room bench, retying her shoelaces even though they weren't loose. The day had already been long—two dance rehearsals, one fitting, and a last-minute vocal run-through for a charity stage performance involving multiple idol groups.

New Jeans was there.

Enhypen, too.

She hadn't seen Minji yet. Or Jungwon. But she felt the tension prickling under her skin like static.

Her phone buzzed with another Twitter notification she didn't want to read. The peach tea post had gone viral overnight. Fan threads were dissecting everything: her past, her stage expressions, the way she blinked during interviews. She was everyone's favorite conversation again. For all the wrong reasons.

A girl from another group—someone from a mid-tier label Solar barely knew—passed behind her.

Not quietly.

"She's really doing the rounds, huh?" the girl said to her friend, not bothering to lower her voice. "Rascal, Jungwon, whoever else next week. Wonder if she's actually good at anything, or just pretty enough to get away with it."

The words hit like ice water.

Solar didn't turn. Didn't react.

But her hands clenched.

And then—

Footsteps.

A pause.

Jungwon's voice, calm and low but razor-sharp, cut through the room like a blade.

"Careful," he said.

The girls froze.

Jungwon stepped forward from the corner, pulling his hood down, his face unreadable. He looked directly at the girl who had spoken.

"Jealousy's loud," he snaps. "Makes people say things they'd never say if they were actually happy."

The girl stammered something. Her friend pulled her away, muttering an apology that didn't sound like one.

Jungwon didn't move.

He looked at Solar.

Their eyes locked.

She couldn't speak. Didn't know what to say.

So she didn't.

Jungwon gave her one small nod.

Then walked past her—back straight, calm like nothing had happened.

But everything had.

Solar sat still for a long time.

Hands shaking.

Face hot—not with shame, but something else.

Something like gratitude. Something like guilt.

Something like being seen.



Solar had just slipped out of the practice room when she heard the screech.

Not a scream.

Tires.

She barely had time to turn before a matte-black SUV rolled into the back lot like it owned the place. The windows were tinted. The driver looked terrified. The passenger door flung open before the vehicle had fully stopped.

Out stepped Chaerin.

In full chaos-mode.

Gucci sunglasses. Cropped hoodie. Thigh-high boots that looked entirely impractical for a Tuesday afternoon. She had a venti iced Americano in one hand and an attitude in the other.

"Unnie!" she called, strutting across the parking lot like it was a runway. "Why are you always going viral for having a face?"

Solar blinked. "What are you doing here?"

"Checking in on my emotionally constipated sister. Duh." Chaerin stopped in front of her, lowering her sunglasses. "You look like shit. It's working for you."

"I had a long day."

"Yeah, I saw. Some trainee girl tried to drag you. Then Jungwon went full mafia boy and shut her down."

Solar narrowed her eyes. "How do you know that already?"

Chaerin smirked, sipping her drink. "Because someone leaked rehearsal footage and your fandom's already editing fan cams like it's a K-drama."

Solar groaned and leaned against the wall. "I can't breathe lately."

"Same," Chaerin replied. "But that's because I just had my ribs cinched for this top."

There was a beat of silence.

Then—softly—Chaerin said, "You okay?"

It was a real question.

Solar didn't lie. "No."

Chaerin's tone softened, still teasing, but gentler now. "Is it the gamer boy? Or the idol boy?"

"I don't even know anymore."

"Because," Chaerin continued, looping her arm through Solar's, "Rascal is lowkey kind of hot. But Jungwon? Jungwon's got the whole 'brooding protector with a dark past' vibe."

Solar stared at her. "You've never even met him."

"I don't have to. I've seen his MCountdown fancams. The boy could open a door and I'd be pregnant."

Solar choked on a laugh.

Then paused. "Why'd you really come?"

Chaerin looked at her, suddenly serious.

"Because you stopped answering my texts. And when that happens, it usually means you're spiraling."

"I'm not—"

"You are." Chaerin squeezed her arm. "And I get it. You're balancing two lives and trying not to shatter either. But maybe talk to someone before you implode this time."

Solar went quiet.

"I missed you," she said finally.

"I missed me too," Chaerin said. "Honestly? I'm great."

But then she pulled her sister into a hug.

And didn't let go for a long time.



Solar's room was dark when she logged on for scrims.

The curtains were drawn, but not fully closed—just enough to let in the muted blur of the city beyond. Her fingers hovered over the keyboard longer than usual. She stared at the Discord icon for a full minute before clicking it.

The server was already busy.

"Yo!" Super's voice burst through first. "We're live, baby. Time to humble some egos."

Nero groaned. "Super, if you start monologuing again, I swear—"

"I'm literally creating a motivational environment."

Rascal laughed. Just once.

But Solar noticed it.

It was shorter than usual. Clipped. Like the sound had been edited down in post.

They ran through warmups, nothing major. Solar played clean—if a little quiet. But Rascal? Rascal was perfect. Unshakable. Smooth mechanics, flawless combos, airtight callouts.

Just... no jokes.

No side comments. No teasing. No "yo Solar, you cooking?" or "I carried, you're welcome."

He didn't ignore her.

But he didn't talk to her either.

By the third match, Glister cracked a joke about the lack of banter.
"Damn, Rascal's in robot mode today."

"Focus," Rascal said, calm. Almost bored.

The silence that followed was louder than anything.

They ended the session on a win. Clean sweep. Crusty was pleased. Nero actually complimented Solar's Pulse Bomb timing. Super declared himself the "scrim MVP" and muted before anyone could argue.

Solar stayed in the channel a little longer.

Just her and Rascal.

She wanted to say something. Ask if she'd done something wrong. Tell him she missed the jokes. The way his voice used to light up when they popped off together. The way she didn't realize how much she needed that until it vanished.

Instead, she typed:

SOLAR: hey
SOLAR: are we okay?

He didn't reply for a while.

She watched the typing bubble blink on... then off... then on again.

Finally:

RASCAL: yeah
RASCAL: you're busy. i get it.
RASCAL: don't worry. i won't be weird.

Solar stared at the screen.

She almost typed something back—something dumb, like "you're not weird." Or "I miss how it was."

But she didn't.

Because maybe he meant it. Or maybe he didn't. Maybe it wasn't about her at all.

She shut her laptop without responding.

And in the quiet that followed, she felt something curl in her chest.

Something small. Something that sounded a lot like loss.



Danielle found Minji in the dressing room hallway, leaning against the mirror with her headphones in.

She didn't ask if Minji was busy.

Just started talking.

"You always did like broken girls," she said sweetly.

Minji didn't move. Didn't even take out her earbuds.

Danielle stepped closer.

"First Soorin, now Solar," she went on, voice soft like a secret. "You like the ones who look like they're holding it together until you touch them and realize it's all tape and glass."

Minji finally looked up.

Pulled out one earbud.

"I'm not doing this with you."

Danielle smiled.

"I'm just saying. Solar's fun. Complicated. Tragic in a way that makes people want to save her. But that's the thing about people like her—they don't want to be saved. They just want to burn."

Minji stood.

Calmly.

"You talk a lot for someone who got left behind."

Danielle's smile cracked.

Just slightly.

Minji didn't flinch.

"At least Solar looked at me like I was real," she said. "You only ever looked in mirrors."

And then she walked away.

Danielle didn't follow.

She just stared at her reflection.

And didn't like what she saw.



Solar's solo stage wasn't supposed to be a big deal.

Just a simple acoustic set—one of the show's soft interlude segments to balance the louder performances. A ballad she had helped write, about resilience, about staying when you want to run.

She almost backed out.

Almost said she was too tired. Too sick. Too anything.

But when she stepped onstage, the lights were soft. The guitar was tuned. And for the first time in days, no one was yelling. No one was fighting. No one was waiting to misunderstand her.

It was just her and the mic.

And the song.

She sang low. Steady. Each word unfolding like a bruise.

And something shifted.

Not in the crowd. Not in the cameras.

Inside her.

When it ended, she didn't bow.

Just stood there, eyes shining, breath shaky.

Backstage, Jungwon was watching from the shadows.

And in a quiet greenroom in San Francisco, Rascal clicked the livestream closed and whispered, "There she is."

The world hadn't changed.

But Solar had.

And that was enough—for now.
