12: Things You Don’t Say Out Loud

For the first time in weeks, the world slowed down.

It didn't stop—not completely. There were still rehearsals, livestream check-ins, practice calls, brand shoots, and barely enough sleep to count as rest. But something in the pace softened. The ache in Solar's chest dulled, like it had been iced from the inside out.

Yunjin sent her dumb memes again. Nothing heavy. Just cats with tiny sunglasses and edits of their own MVs with fart sound effects. Solar laughed at her phone in the van, the kind of laugh that slipped out without trying.

Chaewon made tea every night and didn't ask questions. Eunchae curled up beside her on the couch and shoved her toes under Solar's legs like she always used to. And Hanni, a member of New Jeans that had become a caring friend? Hanni showed up one afternoon with a bag of snacks and said, "I decided you looked too haunted on stage. I'm fixing it."

Solar didn't fight it.

She just let herself be surrounded.

On the gaming side, Shock had cooled, too. Crusty praised her stats. Nero only insulted her twice in two hours—a record. Glister asked if she wanted to co-stream next week. Even Super DM'd her a clip from scrims titled "when ur bestie clutches and ur heart does the stanky leg."

It wasn't perfect.

But it was enough to breathe again.



Two days after her solo stage aired, Solar was walking back to the dorm alone—late, hoodie up, headphones in—when she felt the shift.

It was raining. Not hard. Just enough to fog up her glasses and make the streetlamps blur. Her shoes slapped wet against the pavement. She had one hand in her pocket, the other clutching a convenience store peach tea she hadn't even meant to buy.

That's when she saw him.

Jungwon.

Standing under the overhang just outside the bodega, hood pulled up, hands in his jacket. He wasn't looking at her. He was looking at the sky.

Solar slowed.

She should've walked past.

But she didn't.

He didn't move when she approached.

Didn't glance at her. Didn't pretend he hadn't seen. Just kept staring up at the sky, watching the water fall in thin, steady lines past the flickering neon signs.

Solar stopped beside him, close enough that their shoulders almost touched.

For a long time, neither of them said anything.

The rain padded against the pavement like static. Somewhere in the distance, a horn honked. A cat darted into an alley. The world kept moving.

She cracked the seal on her peach tea, took a sip.

Jungwon finally looked at her.

"You always pick the same drink?" he asked, voice low.

She blinked. "What?"

He nodded toward the bottle in her hand. "Peach tea."

Solar stared at it. Smiled—just barely.

"Didn't even realize," she said.

Jungwon tilted his head, then glanced away. "You looked tired."

"I am."

"Still singing. Still playing."

She let out a breath. "Trying."

A beat.

Then he said, "It's okay not to be okay."

She huffed a quiet laugh. "That's what everyone says."

"Most people don't mean it."

"And you do?"

He looked at her again.

This time, the gaze didn't drift.

It settled.

"I mean it when it's you," Jungwon said.

Solar's breath caught.

It wasn't a confession. It wasn't even a declaration. But something in the way he said it—gentle, certain, like it was the simplest truth in the world—made her feel like her bones had turned to glass.

"I don't know what I'm doing," she whispered. "I feel like I'm holding everything together with tape and lies."

Jungwon didn't respond right away.

He just leaned slightly closer. Not enough to touch. Just enough for her to feel the warmth of his presence in the cold.

"You don't have to be okay," he said again, softer this time. "Not with me."

Solar blinked fast, rain mixing with the heat gathering at the corners of her eyes.

"I don't want to hurt anyone," she admitted. "Not Rascal. Not Minji. Not you."

"You haven't."

"I might."

Jungwon looked down, hands still buried in his pockets.

"You will," he said simply. "That's what people do. We hurt. We get hurt."

Then, slowly, he looked up.

"And we keep showing up anyway."

Something broke in her then.

Not in a painful way. More like a dam loosening. The breath she hadn't realized she'd been holding. The tension she'd kept in her spine for days, weeks, months.

"Why do you show up?" she asked.

Jungwon's jaw flexed. His eyes searched hers.

Then, finally:

"Because you don't scare me."

Not the fame. Not the fire. Not the way her name trended alongside boys she barely knew or with teammates who saw her shine a little too brightly.

"You don't scare me," he repeated. "And I think maybe that scares you."

Solar let the silence hold them.

Let it stretch between them like a string that had never quite broken.

When she finally turned to go, she didn't say goodbye.

But she left the peach tea beside him, unopened.

And Jungwon didn't call after her.

He just stood in the rain.

And drank it.



The message came the next morning.

Solar had barely peeled herself off the mattress, hair still damp from the rain she hadn't dried properly the night before, when her phone buzzed with a Discord ping.

RASCAL:
hey
not trying to bother you
just figured... idk
scrims felt weird lately

She stared at it for a long time, heart slow and heavy.

Then the typing bubble reappeared.

RASCAL:
miss hearing you trash talk nero
he's getting cocky again
someone needs to humble him

Solar smiled.

Just a little.

Then the final message came.

RASCAL:
you don't owe me anything
but if you wanna queue sometime
no pressure
just
miss the way we played

There was no guilt in it. No bitterness. No passive-aggression.

Just him.

Quiet. Honest. A little awkward.

Like always.

Solar reread it three times.

Then typed.

SOLAR:
i miss it too
i'll log on tonight

She hovered over send for a full minute.

Then tapped it.

And for the first time in days, the weight in her chest shifted.

Not gone.

But lighter.

Like maybe—just maybe—something still wanted to grow.



Danielle didn't post the photo herself.

That would've been too obvious.

Instead, it appeared on a burner account. One that had posted obscure idol gossip before. Nothing too serious. Nothing too fake. Just real enough to sting.

The photo was blurry, taken from a distance.

But the timing was perfect.

Solar and Jungwon. Outside the bodega. In the rain. Close enough to read as something more. Not kissing. Not even touching. But the framing made it look intimate. Cinematic, almost.

The caption was simple:

who else is seeing this new ship 👀

Within hours, it was trending.

#SOLWON
#BOYINTHERAIN
#PEACHTEAHEARTBREAK

Solar found out from Yunjin.

She was brushing her teeth when Yunjin burst into the bathroom with her phone.

"Did you see this?"

Solar took the phone and stared.

The comments were already spiraling.

"wasn't she just flirting with rascal?"
"jungwon deserves better"
"this girl's playing overwatch and kpop boys like it's her job"

Solar sat down on the edge of the tub.

The toothbrush clattered to the floor.

Her phone buzzed in her own pocket now—notifications exploding. Mentions. DMs. A missed call from HYBE. Another from her brother.

She didn't answer any of them.

She just stared at the screen, the photo burned into her eyes.

She remembered the rain.

The quiet.

The way Jungwon had said, "You don't scare me."

And for a moment, she had believed it.

But now—

Now she didn't know who was really watching. Or why.

And deep in her stomach, something started to curl again.

Not hope.

Not yet.

Something colder.

Something like fear.

















13: The Echo Chamber

The conference room was too cold.

Solar sat in the middle of a long, polished table, flanked by silence and the soft whir of an overhead projector. The blinds were half-drawn. A bottle of water sat untouched in front of her. Across the table: two managers, one PR rep, and Chaewon—silent, arms folded.

Nobody smiled.

"Let's talk about image," said the PR rep, a woman with a neat blazer and a smile like a closing door.

Solar didn't answer.

"Your solo performance was exceptional," one of the managers said quickly, trying to soften things. "It charted well. You trended globally. That's all good."

"But..." the PR rep continued, "it came with complications."

A click. The projector blinked to life.

There it was.

The bodega photo. Blurry. Rain-drenched. Cinematic.

And below it: trending tags. SOLWON. PEACHTEAHEARTBREAK. Screenshots of fan theories. Edits of her and Jungwon. Speculation pieces titled "Overwatch Star Turned Heartbreaker?"

"People are talking," the PR rep said flatly. "We need to manage the narrative."

Solar kept her face neutral.

Chaewon shifted beside her. Not speaking. Not yet.

"This isn't just about you," the PR rep went on. "It's about the brand. The group. The contracts you're tied to. We can't afford confusion in the market."

"I wasn't doing anything wrong," Solar said quietly.

"No one's accusing you," one of the managers said.

"But perception is everything," the rep added.

A beat.

Then Solar asked, "So what are you asking me to do?"

"Distance," the PR rep said. "From Jungwon. From speculation. From controversy."

Solar blinked.

"And what if I don't?"

A pause.

The woman's voice didn't rise.

"You don't want to lose control of your story, Solar."

That landed harder than shouting ever could.

Beside her, Chaewon finally spoke.

"She's not a scandal," she said, voice steady. "She's the best thing we have."

Nobody answered.

Because everyone knew she was right. But right didn't always win in these rooms.

Solar stood.

"I'll be careful," she said.

But her voice didn't sound careful at all.

It sounded like a match.

Striking.



Solar logged into Discord that night with a weight pressing behind her eyes.

The PR meeting still echoed in her head. Every word. Every insinuation. Every reminder that she was something to be managed. Marketed.

She wasn't sure what she expected from scrims.

Awkward silence?

Snide comments?

But the moment she joined voice chat, Super screamed, "LET'S GET THIS BREAD," like they hadn't just trended for 24 hours straight.

Glister laughed. Nero groaned.

And Rascal? Rascal just said, "Yo. You good?"

Solar hesitated.

Then: "I'm here."

"Cool," Rascal replied, casual. "You're on Tracer. I'll cover high ground. Let's fry some braincells."

That was it. No questions. No pressure. Just... normal. But not cold.

Never cold.

If anything, Rascal played cleaner than ever. His Echo peeled every dive off her without missing a beat. His callouts were tighter. His ult economy was tuned like a piano. And every time she blinked in with low health, he was already there—bombs flying, comms clear.

Midway through the third map, Nero made a joke about paparazzi. "Should we start charging for group pics?"

Solar rolled her eyes.

Before she could respond, Rascal cut in.

"They can ship whatever," he said flatly. "She's still my duo."

It was casual. Said like it didn't matter. But Solar's hand tightened on her mouse.

Later, in the team chat, Glister typed:

glister: rascal got real one energy today
glister: u two back to soul binding or what

Rascal didn't answer. Just typed:

rascal: queue again tomorrow?

Solar typed back:

solar: yeah. same time.

Her chest didn't hurt as much after that.



It was past midnight when Solar's phone buzzed again.

CHAERIN:
i saw the stupid post
do i need to throw hands or is this a verbal beatdown kind of day

Solar stared at the screen, already bracing herself. Thirty minutes later, her sister showed up at the dorm. Gucci sunglasses. At night.

"Did you break in?" Solar asked, bleary-eyed as she opened the door.

"Don't worry about it," Chaerin said, pushing past her like she owned the place. "I bring justice. And also snacks."

She tossed a convenience store bag onto the table. Chips. Energy drinks. A half-melted ice cream bar.

"I'm fine," Solar said.

"Liar," Chaerin replied, flopping dramatically onto the couch.

"I really am," Solar insisted.

Chaerin peeled off her sunglasses. Her eyeliner was perfect. Her glare was deadly.

"You are the Solar. K-pop prodigy. Tracer queen. Flame-haired god-tier siren of Seoul."

Solar blinked. "Are you... drunk?"

"Just high on rage."

Chaerin sat up. "You know what people don't like? A woman who's too many things. Too loud. Too skilled. Too hard to pin down. They don't know what to call you, so they call you a problem."

Solar didn't answer. So Chaerin leaned forward.

"They don't get to define you," she said. "Not the labels. Not the fans. Not your damn agency. You don't belong in their narrative."

She paused. "You write your own."

Solar didn't cry. But something in her chest cracked wide open. She sank onto the couch beside her sister and let her head fall onto Chaerin's shoulder.

"I missed you," she mumbled.

"I know."

Chaerin put her arm around her. And for a while, they didn't say anything. Just sat there.

In silence.

In safety.



The dorm was dark when Solar finally made it back to her room.

Chaerin had crashed on the couch, wrapped in a blanket she'd dramatically declared "a symbol of war." The storm outside had faded into a soft drizzle, tapping quietly against the windows.

Solar lay back on her bed, phone cradled to her chest, letting the stillness wrap around her like gauze. That's when the notification lit up.

Minji.

A voice message. She stared at it.

Not a text. Not a meme. Not an emoji pretending things hadn't changed.

Just a soft, glowing circle and the timestamp: 0:37.

Solar hesitated. Then tapped play.

Minji's voice came through—quiet, clear, and maybe just the tiniest bit shaky.

"Hey. I... um. Sorry, I didn't know if I should even say anything. I saw what happened. The photo. The tags. The usual mess."

A pause.

"I know it probably doesn't matter, but... you looked beautiful. Not, like, in a headline way. Just... you. You looked like you."

Another pause, softer this time.

"I don't know how to say this without making it worse. I'm not trying to add to the noise. I just wanted to say... I hope you're okay. That's all."

The message ended with the faintest inhale—like she was going to say more, but didn't. Solar didn't move for a long time.

She didn't play it again. Didn't type a reply.

She just stared at the ceiling, the phone still warm in her hand. Somewhere between silence and something else. Somewhere between old feelings and new storms.

Somewhere between being seen and not knowing what to do with it.
























14: The Space Between

The voice note sat unopened.

Not because she hadn't listened. She had. Twice.

Once when it arrived. Once again, alone in her room, curled under a blanket that smelled like green tea and fabric softener.

Now, it just sat there. A glowing circle. A timestamp. 0:37. Waiting.

Solar didn't know what she expected—an answer? A reason? A way for everything to make sense again?

She didn't get any of that.

Just Minji's voice, soft and hesitant, saying things no one else dared say.

You looked beautiful.
Not in a headline way.
Just... you.

Solar stared at her screen until her vision blurred.

Then she pulled her hoodie over her head, tied the drawstrings tight, and went to brush her teeth.



Yunjin leaned against the doorway, arms crossed, watching her quietly.

"You've been weird all day."

Solar spit into the sink. "Thanks."

"No, like... emotionally weird. That slow-walking-in-music-video kind of weird."

Solar rolled her eyes and dried her face with a towel. "I'm fine."

"Liar."

Yunjin stepped closer, leaning against the counter. Her expression wasn't teasing anymore.

"Is it about her?"

Solar didn't answer.

Yunjin didn't need her to.

"She messaged you, didn't she?"

A beat.

Then, softly: "Yeah."

"What'd she say?"

"That she hoped I was okay."

Yunjin studied her. "And?"

"She said I looked like... me."

Yunjin tilted her head. "That's not a bad thing."

"I don't know what that means anymore," Solar whispered.

The silence between them stretched.

Then Yunjin nudged her with an elbow. "Well, if it helps, I think you're still you."

Solar looked over, eyes glassy. "Yeah?"

"Yeah. Just... you with better posture and less black eyeliner."

That made her laugh.

Just a little.

But it was enough.



The radio station smelled like instant coffee and plastic headphones.

Le Sserafim crammed into the green room, all tangled limbs and laughter. Eunchae had already knocked over a stack of cue cards, and Yunjin was tuning her guitar for a surprise acoustic segment that no one had asked for.

Solar sat cross-legged on the couch, sipping a bottled tea and trying to breathe through the slight ringing in her ears. The radio lights felt hotter than usual. Everything did, lately.

The show was supposed to be fun—casual. A midweek boost of idol serotonin. They'd done it before, joked around, played games, answered questions about skincare routines and favorite ramen brands.

Today felt different.

Maybe it was the photo. The voice note. The heaviness in her chest that hadn't left.

She adjusted her mic as they stepped into the booth.

The host grinned like a man who thrived on viral moments.

"Le Sserafim!" he beamed. "Back in the studio, looking flawless as ever. You girls have been everywhere lately. Comebacks, performances, and now—rumor has it—some solo stardom?"

Solar smiled on cue.

Chaewon took the lead. "We've all been working hard! Solar especially. She's basically our sixth job."

Laughter.

Eunchae leaned in. "Solar's PC setup is better than NASA's. She could launch a rocket mid-scrim."

More laughter.

The host chuckled. "Speaking of scrims... Solar, you've been getting a lot of attention lately. Music. eSports. Even a few... spicy photos on Twitter."

Solar didn't flinch.

"I play. I perform. Sometimes people take pictures," she said smoothly.

Yunjin gave her a tiny nod.

The host pressed on, all smiles.

"Ever feel like you're living two lives? Is it hard, balancing everything—and still figuring out who you want to be?"

A pause.

Solar's mouth opened.

But then her eyes drifted past the glass.

New Jeans had arrived. They were waiting outside the studio window, just beyond the booth.

Minji caught her gaze for half a second.

But it wasn't Minji she noticed first.

It was Danielle.

Standing still. Arms crossed. Phone lifted like she was recording. No smile. No reaction. Just... watching.

Unblinking.

Solar's throat tightened.

"I think..." she said slowly, "...I think I'm allowed to be more than one thing."

The host raised his brows, but nodded.

"Well said."

The interview moved on.

Games. Shoutouts. Eunchae making a joke about dating apps that got bleeped in post.

But Solar never stopped feeling it.

That stare.

Later, on her break, she checked her phone.

Danielle's Instagram story was still up.

A blurry shot of a mic stand. The radio station logo visible in the background.

Captioned:
Some girls know how to stay in their lane. Others crash.

No tags.

But she knew exactly who it was for.



The hallway outside the station was quiet when Solar stepped out for air.

The interview was over. Her makeup was starting to melt at the corners. Her hands smelled like hand sanitizer and static.

She needed quiet.

What she got instead was Jaehyun.

Leaning against the wall like he'd been there for hours. Arms folded. Sunglasses on—despite the fact that it was cloudy and barely noon.

Solar froze.

"What are you doing here?" she asked.

He didn't answer immediately.

"You trended for twenty-two hours," he said finally. "I figured someone should check in."

"I don't need a babysitter."

"No," Jaehyun agreed. "You need a better sense of danger."

Solar bristled. "Don't start."

"I'm not starting. I'm warning you."

His voice was so calm it was terrifying.

"People are watching you now," he said. "Not just fans. Agencies. Contracts. Investors. You're not just a girl with a mic and a mouse anymore."

"I know that."

"Then act like it."

That landed like a slap.

"I didn't do anything wrong," she shot back.

"You let your guard down," Jaehyun replied. "With the boy. With the world. You gave them a version of you they can twist."

"You don't know him."

"I know exactly who he is."

Solar stepped closer. "You don't get to decide who I trust."

"I don't need to," Jaehyun said. "But don't come crying when he disappears."

The silence after that was so heavy it could have broken glass.

He pushed off the wall.

"I've cleaned up too many messes already," he said. "Try not to be one of them."

And then he walked away.

Leaving her standing there, shaking—but not sure if it was anger, or fear, or both.



She didn't go home right away.

Instead, Solar walked.

No direction. No destination. Just... away.

The rain from earlier had left the streets glistening. Neon signs smeared across puddles. Her shoes splashed through them without care. Every step felt heavy. Like she was dragging the weight of other people's stories behind her.

You're too visible.
You're too much.
You're not careful enough.
You don't belong.

They all echoed.

By the time she got back to the dorm, her hoodie was damp, her fingers cold.

Everyone was asleep.

She sat on the floor of her bedroom with the lights off. Just the glow of her screen lighting the dark.

Then, quietly, she opened her notes app and began to type:

I don't know who I am when I'm not trying to be enough.
Not the best. Not the first. Not the most.
Just... enough.
I keep giving people pieces of me.
And every time, there's a little less left.
What if I run out?

She stared at it. Then hit backspace. Deleted it all.

She scrolled. Past Rascal. Past Minji.

Paused. Then tapped a name she hadn't spoken aloud in a long time.

JIHOON.

Her fingers hovered. Then typed:

still up?

She stared at the message. Didn't hit send.

Then, slowly... she did. No reply.

Just the message marked "delivered." She turned off her phone. Laid on her back on the cold floor.

And finally let herself cry.
















15: The Things That Stay

The message was still there when Solar woke up.

JIHOON:
yeah
you ok

Three words. No punctuation. Just like always.

It was nothing. It was everything.

The time stamp read 3:17 a.m.—the hour when the world felt too soft and your brain turned traitor.

She didn't reply. Didn't even breathe for a moment. Just stared at the screen like it might answer the question for her.

You okay?

She wanted to be. God, she wanted to be.

But Jihoon always knew how to ask the wrong question at the right time.



Yunjin walked in without knocking.

She always did when she was worried.

Solar quickly flipped her phone over, but not fast enough.

Yunjin froze mid-step. "Tell me you didn't."

Solar stayed quiet.

Yunjin's voice dropped. "Jihoon?"

Still, Solar didn't speak.

"Are you serious right now?" Yunjin asked, moving closer. "After everything he did?"

"I didn't say anything back."

"But you messaged him."

"It was just one message," Solar muttered.

"One message is all it ever takes with him."

There was a silence.

Not loud. Not dramatic.

Just... sharp.

"I wasn't thinking," Solar said, voice brittle.

"That's the problem," Yunjin replied. "You never think when it comes to him."

She sat down on the edge of the bed, quieter now. "You remember what happened the last time you answered a text from him? You left rehearsal in the middle of the night. You came back three days later without your phone and with bruises on your ribs. You lied to everyone."

Solar looked down at her hands.

"Yun..." she whispered. "He knew me before any of this. Before the cameras. Before the rules. When I was still... just me."

"Yeah," Yunjin said gently. "And he helped you forget who that girl was."

That landed hard.

Solar didn't reply.

Yunjin stood again. "I'm not mad. But I'm not gonna sit here and watch you backslide into a version of yourself you barely survived."

Then she paused at the door.

"You don't owe him closure," she said. "You owe yourself peace."

The door clicked shut behind her.

Solar didn't move.

Just stared at the glowing screen until it dimmed.



The Shock scrim started late.

Everyone was running behind—Super hadn't logged in yet, Nero was stuck in traffic, Glister was supposedly "cleaning his desk," which everyone knew meant "eating cold pizza and watching anime."

Only Rascal and Solar were on the call. For once, the silence wasn't awkward.

Rascal cleared his throat. "You okay?"

Solar didn't answer right away.

"I'm here," she said finally.

"Not what I asked."

She smiled softly. "I know."

There was a quiet moment. Just the faint hum of a server fan in the background.

Then Rascal said, "You've been blinking into walls."

Solar laughed under her breath. "Wow. Romantic."

"I mean emotionally," he replied. "You've been off."

"I'm just tired."

"You don't have to lie," Rascal said, his voice unusually steady. "Not here."

That cracked something open. She didn't cry. But her voice wavered.

"I don't know how to do any of this," she whispered. "Not the game, not the music, not... all of it at once."

"You don't have to be perfect."

"I don't know how to be anything else."

Rascal let out a slow breath.

"I used to think if I didn't win, I didn't exist," he said. "Like, if I wasn't top of the leaderboard, I was nothing."

Solar blinked. "What changed?"

"I realized the leaderboard doesn't remember you when you burn out." He paused. "I just want to matter to the right people."

Solar swallowed hard. She didn't say anything.

But in that moment, she knew she'd remember those words for a long, long time.



Yunjin didn't say anything the next morning. She made coffee. Burned toast. Tuned her guitar. But she didn't look at Solar. Which meant something was brewing.

It wasn't until later—when Solar came back from rehearsals, hair damp and mood heavier than her dance bag—that she noticed the open laptop on the kitchen counter.

Yunjin was scrolling through photos. Not selfies. Not edits. Old posts. Blurry tags. Names she hadn't seen in years.

Solar blinked. "What are you doing?"

"Research," Yunjin said simply, not looking up.

"On?"

Yunjin clicked on something, narrowed her eyes.

"Your ex," she replied. "And a girl who's starting to make me nervous."

Solar's stomach twisted. "You're not—"

"I'm not accusing anyone," Yunjin said, raising a hand. "But I was looking through old party photos. The kind people don't tag but never delete."

She turned the screen. It was a photo from some rooftop party, dated nearly four years ago. Jihoon was in the background, barely visible.

So was someone else. Blonde. Smiling. Half-turned toward the camera.

Danielle.

It could've been coincidence. Could've been nothing. But it didn't feel like nothing.

"They ran in the same circles," Yunjin said. "At least once."

Solar stared at the screen. Something cold unfurled in her chest.

"She's been watching me since the day I joined," she whispered.

Yunjin nodded. "And now I'm watching her."



The music show was chaos.

Backstage was a tangle of wires and hairspray, managers yelling over earpieces, and idols weaving around one another like dancers in a crowded dream.

Solar was quiet through it all. She'd done this a hundred times. The prep. The cameras. The pretending. But today, her skin felt too tight, her heart two steps behind every beat.

They were heading to the stage when she saw her.

Minji.

New Jeans was just coming off their set, flanked by stylists and staff, cheeks still flushed from performance. And there she was.

Their eyes met across the hallway—just for a second.

It stopped Solar mid-step. Minji's face softened. The tiniest bit. Like a ghost of a smile wanted to reach the surface.

But then she looked away.

Just like that. Gone. No words. No wave. No time.

Solar stood there, blinking through the echo of it, like she'd walked into a memory instead of a person.

Chaewon nudged her. "You okay?"

Solar nodded automatically. "Yeah. Just—saw someone."

Chaewon didn't press.

But the ache stayed.

All through the performance, through the bows and camera flashes. It stayed.



The dorm was dark. Everyone was asleep.

Except Solar. She couldn't.

Her thoughts kept looping—Minji's face, Jihoon's message, Jaehyun's voice, Rascal's words. All of it spun in circles too fast to outrun.

So she got up. Slipped down the hall. No makeup. Bare feet.

The practice room was quiet, lit only by the soft glow of the hallway nightlight. To her surprise, Yunjin was already there.

Sitting on the floor, legs crossed, guitar resting in her lap like it belonged to her heartbeat. She didn't speak when Solar walked in.

Just handed her a notebook. Opened to a blank page.

Solar took it. Sat beside her.

For a while, they didn't talk. Just let the quiet stretch between them like a bridge they were building plank by plank.

Then, slowly, Solar began to write.

Not for the label. Not for the charts.

Just for herself.

For the girl who cried on the floor.
For the one who blinked into walls.
For the version of her that didn't know if she'd ever feel whole again.

She didn't know when the lyrics stopped being sad and started becoming something else. Maybe it was around the second chorus.

Maybe it was when Yunjin started to hum with her, soft and off-key. She looked down at the page and read the line she hadn't remembered writing:

I'm not a headline / I'm a heartbeat in motion.

Solar blinked.

Then smiled.

It was the first time in days she felt like herself again.






16: What Comes Undone

It started with a leak.

Someone in the building had recorded a sliver of their late-night writing session—just the edge of Solar's voice, raw and unguarded, layered over Yunjin's guitar. It wasn't supposed to be public. But now it was everywhere.

Fans called it "the heartbreak demo."
Clips were circulating on Twitter with captions like:

she sounds like she's bleeding through the mic.
who hurt her. be honest.

Solar didn't respond to any of it.

She barely opened her phone that day—until the screen lit up with a name she hadn't seen in weeks.

MINJI:
That song...
It sounded like you.

Just that. No emojis. No follow-up. Solar stared at the message for a long time. Her heart beat in places she'd forgotten existed.

She didn't reply. The next night, another message.

MINJI:
If you ever need quiet.
I have a rooftop.

There was no pressure in the words. No expectations.
Just an offering. Something gentle. Solar read it again.

And again.

Then she locked her phone and held it to her chest—like maybe it would steady her breathing.



Solar wasn't expecting to see him.

She was just dropping off paperwork—standard agency errands, makeup still clinging to her jawline from a late shoot, hair half-dried from rushing out of the dorm. The HYBE building buzzed like it always did—interns darting through hallways, stylists dragging garment bags, music pulsing faintly behind closed doors.

She was halfway to the elevators when she heard his voice.

Low. Even. Too familiar.

"Solar."

She turned.

And there he was.

Jungwon.

Black hoodie, hair tucked under a beanie, eyes sharp even when half-hidden behind his fringe. He looked like someone trying not to be recognized—and failing anyway.

They stood in the hallway, just out of traffic, invisible in plain sight.

Neither of them spoke first.

Then: "You okay?" he asked.

Solar blinked. "Were you?"

Jungwon didn't answer right away. His gaze dropped to the floor, then returned to her with a kind of resigned weight.

"People made up stories," he said. "About the rain. About us."

She studied him. "And you didn't correct them."

"Nope."

"Why not?"

He looked at her—really looked. Not the way someone stares at a screen or a stage. The way you stare at something that's haunted your sleep.

"Because for once," he said, voice steady, "they weren't wrong."

That silence stretched again—like a held breath.

"I didn't want the attention," Solar murmured. "Not like that."

"I didn't want it either," Jungwon said. "But I didn't regret it."

That made her blink. She didn't know what she expected. But not that.

"I watched the whole city talk about a moment they didn't live," he said quietly. "And for the first time, I didn't care."

Solar crossed her arms, not defensive—just grounding herself.

"Was it real to you?" she asked, finally.

Jungwon nodded. "'Course."

She didn't speak. Her throat felt too full of questions. He stepped closer. Not too close. Just enough to be intentional.

"I'm not good at this," he said. "Whatever this is."

"I noticed."

"But I don't want to pretend it didn't happen. Or act like you were just... a headline."

Something cracked in her.

Not broken—just unguarded.

"I think I needed to hear that," she whispered.

"I didn't say it for you," Jungwon replied. "I said it for me."

They stood there in the hum of hallway lights, too much between them and not enough. Then a manager called his name from around the corner. Fast footsteps. Pressure returning.

Jungwon didn't move yet.

"I'll see you around," he said softly.

Solar nodded.

He turned away.

And for a moment, she let herself watch him go.

The moment Jungwon disappeared down the hall, Solar felt the world tilt. Not violently. Just... subtly. Like her center had shifted half a step to the left.

She took the stairs instead of the elevator. Needed movement. Air. Something that didn't hum with too much memory.

The lobby was quieter this time of day. Only a few staffers passing through, voices low, the automatic doors whispering open and closed.

Solar pushed through them into the fading sunlight.

And froze.

Across the street, standing half in shadow beneath the overhang of a bus stop, was Jihoon.

Same hoodie. Same stance. Same unreadable gaze. Her stomach dropped like a stone.

He didn't wave. Didn't move. Just stood there, watching her.

Like he'd been waiting.

The last time she'd seen him in person, she'd been sixteen and shaking. She remembered the smell of smoke on his collar. The sound of sirens in the distance. The feel of her own voice cracking in her throat as she told him to leave.

Now he was here.

Still.

Still that same gravity pulling at the darkest parts of her.

Solar didn't move. Didn't blink. She could've gone to him.

She could've screamed. Instead, she turned.

Took the long way home.



The email came just after midnight.

Solar was curled up on the dorm couch, half-asleep, reruns of some old sitcom murmuring in the background. Yunjin had already passed out next to her, blanket over her head, guitar abandoned at her feet.

Her phone buzzed once.

She checked it, expecting some pointless app notification.

Instead:

FROM: Coach Crusty
SUBJECT: SHOCK TRAVEL SCHEDULE – READ NOW

Solar sat up straight. Her heart started beating too fast. She opened the message.

The subject line wasn't a joke.

The Overwatch League had officially announced the kickoff tournament—prelims in Seoul, media week, in-person scrims.

Mandatory attendance.
No exceptions.

And then the line that sent heat rushing to her ears:

"You'll meet the team at the hotel next Friday. Be sharp."

She reread it twice. Next Friday.

She was going to meet them.

Super. Smurf. Nero. Glister.

Rascal.

Her fingers hovered over her phone, frozen in the glow of the screen.

Somewhere in another dorm, maybe halfway across the city—or halfway across the world—Rascal had read the same words.

She didn't know if she was ready. But it was happening.

It was real.

And it was closer than she'd ever dared to imagine.










17: First Contact

The night before, Solar didn't sleep. She tried. She really did.

She brushed her teeth twice. Took a shower at 2 a.m. Rearranged her suitcase four separate times. Folded her jersey, then unfolded it, then laid it out on the bed like she was prepping for battle.

Yunjin sat cross-legged on the floor, eating peanut butter from the jar and watching her like she was a wildlife documentary.

"You're spiraling," she said, mouth half-full.

"I'm fine," Solar lied.

"You're packing and repacking like you're auditioning for a movie about emotional instability."

Solar glared. "He's going to see me. In person."

"Yes, and?"

"And what if I look different? Or sound different? What if it's weird?"

Yunjin set the spoon down. "You are different. You're not some internet version of yourself. You're real. If he doesn't like that, he's stupid."

Solar exhaled through her nose. Sat on the edge of the bed, face in her hands.

"It's not even about liking," she said. "It's just... I don't want to disappoint him. Or ruin it. Whatever it is."

Yunjin was quiet for a beat.

Then: "Want me to go instead? I could wear your jersey, trash talk Super, make Rascal fall in love with me."

Solar groaned into her hands.

Yunjin leaned over and hugged her from behind, forehead bumping against Solar's spine.

"You're not going to ruin anything," she whispered. "You've already built something real. Showing up won't break it. It'll prove it."

They stayed like that for a while.

Later that night, long after Yunjin had gone to bed, Solar sat alone in the dark, legs curled on the couch, staring at her phone.

A message came in.

RASCAL:
see u soon
no pressure
just bring ur cracked aim and ur terrible jokes

Solar smiled.

Her fingers hovered over the keyboard.

SOLAR:
only if u bring the bad haircut and ego

RASCAL:
deal

She didn't know what tomorrow would look like.

But for the first time all week, she believed it might be okay.



The car ride to the hotel was quiet.

Solar sat in the back seat, staring out at the Seoul skyline, her jersey folded neatly in her lap. HYBE had arranged a blacked-out van and an assistant to escort her, as if she were heading to war instead of a media shoot.

Her heart was already doing that fluttery thing again—tight and off-rhythm, like a beat drop that never landed.

The assistant didn't talk much. Just updated her occasionally.

"Shock team lands in an hour. You're scheduled for press photos at 3."

Solar nodded. She kept her earbuds in, but didn't press play.


The hotel was enormous—one of those polished glass towers in Gangnam, all marble floors and soft jazz echoing in the lobby. A concierge greeted her by name, bowing with practiced warmth.

Her suite was on the twenty-first floor.

The moment she walked in, she dropped her bags and exhaled. Hard.

It was too clean. Too still. The view from the window was postcard-perfect, but she barely looked.

She paced the room twice.

Checked her hair. Checked it again.

Then sat down on the edge of the bed and stared at the jersey.



The media shoot was painless. Mostly.

She stood in front of a branded Shock backdrop while a staff photographer snapped shots of her in uniform—full glam, hair curled, expression somewhere between deadly and charming.

"Smile more," the photographer said. She didn't.

"You're a pro," he muttered. "I'll make it work."

Afterward, she touched up her eyeliner in the bathroom and stared at herself.

You're real now, she thought. Not a screen. Not a voice on Discord. Real. And that terrified her more than anything.



Back in the lobby, she waited.

Just her and a security guard and the soft tick of the hotel clock.

And then, at 3:14 p.m., the van pulled up. She saw the team before they even came inside.

Super jumped out first—bright red hoodie, arms thrown wide like he'd just won a championship. "SEOUL BABYYYYYY!"

Smurf followed, laughing. Nero climbed out behind him, dragging a suitcase with a broken wheel and cursing in three languages.

Glister was last—headphones still on, clearly mid-song. Solar rose to her feet. The front doors slid open.

Super spotted her first.

"IS THAT HER? IS THAT SOLAR?"

She flushed immediately. "Nope. Decoy."

Super galloped toward her like a golden retriever. "Oh my god, you're shorter than I imagined. But like—taller emotionally."

"What does that even mean?" she asked, trying not to laugh.

"Don't worry," he said, slinging an arm around her like they'd known each other for years. "We'll toughen you up. Smurf's gonna teach you how to talk trash, and I'll teach you how to be trash."

Nero rolled his eyes. "God, it's louder in person."

Smurf grinned. "You look great, Solar. Really glad you're here."

Glister gave a shy wave from behind his headphones. "You're even prettier than your Twitter avi," he mumbled.

She managed a breathless smile. But her eyes were on the door. Because he hadn't walked in yet.

The others were talking—joking, dragging their bags, Super already trying to organize an impromptu snack run—but she barely heard it.

And then...

He stepped inside.

Rascal.

No headset. No screen. Just him.

Black jacket over his Shock jersey. Messy hair. Sharp eyes. A little taller than she expected. A little quieter, too.

He didn't rush. He didn't say anything at first.

Just looked at her.

And the rest of the lobby faded.



For a second, they didn't move.

They just took each other in.

The digital versions of themselves peeling away, pixel by pixel.

He smiled first.

Small. Careful. Real.

"Hey," he said.

It hit her somewhere in the chest.

"Hey," she echoed, quieter.

"You're real," he said, almost like a joke.

"Guess so."

He stepped closer. Not too close. Just enough.

There was a pause. Then—awkwardly, sweetly—he opened his arms.

"Can I...?"

Solar didn't think. She stepped forward and hugged him. It wasn't long. It wasn't dramatic.

But it was everything. He was warm. Solid. Smelled faintly like jet lag and lavender laundry detergent.

When they pulled apart, he looked at her like he didn't quite believe it yet. "You nervous?" he asked, voice low.

"Terrified."

"Same," he admitted. Then added, "But I'm glad you're here."

And just like that—like it was nothing, like it was everything—they walked toward the elevators together, side by side. The others followed, laughing too loud, dragging chaos behind them.

But Solar didn't hear any of it.

She only heard Rascal. Still beside her. Still real.

Still here.



The next morning was a blur of camera flashes, stylists, and caffeine.

Shock Media Day.

Solar sat in a makeup chair, half-listening to the sound of Super yelling something about hot sauce from three rooms over.

A stylist curled the last strand of her hair and pinned it behind her ear. Her Shock jersey had been tailored slightly at the waist—fitted enough to be flattering, loose enough to remind her she was still supposed to be an athlete.

"You look incredible," the makeup artist said.

Solar blinked at her reflection. "You sure I don't look like a child in cosplay?"

"You look like someone who's about to ruin lives in-game," the woman smirked. "Trust me."

She didn't—but the compliment stuck.



The shoot took place in a massive event room converted into a makeshift studio. Orange lighting lit up the Shock logo behind them as they were called in for group shots. The photographer called names like a roll call.

"Smurf. Super. Nero. Glister. Rascal. Solar."

Solar took her place near the end. Rascal came to stand beside her.

Close enough that their arms brushed.

"Ready?" he asked, voice low.

She nodded.

"You're trembling," he added, grinning.

She elbowed him gently. "It's the lighting. It's dramatic."

The photographer adjusted angles. "Okay—group photo. Smurf and Glister, step in. Rascal and Solar, I want you two closer. Good. Yes. There's chemistry."

Solar's breath hitched. Rascal didn't say anything. Just stood a little closer.

The photographer's voice floated behind the camera. "Smile like you just won a championship. Look at each other—yeah, perfect. Now Rascal, can you put your hand on her shoulder?"

Rascal raised a brow at her. A silent you okay with that?

She nodded once. He did.

Click. Snap. Another flash.

The photographer lowered the camera, visibly pleased. "You two photograph like fire and gasoline."

Solar wasn't sure if that was a compliment. But her face was warm.



Later came the interviews.

They sat in pairs on stools, a mic between them and a bored-looking producer flipping through cue cards.

Rascal and Solar went last.

"Okay," the interviewer began, "So... everyone online seems to agree you two are kind of cracked together."

Rascal grinned. "Chemistry's illegal in most states."

Solar raised an eyebrow. "He says that to all his teammates."

"Not true," Rascal shot back. "Only the cute ones."

She didn't blink. "So just me, then."

The crew laughed. One of the techs dropped his clipboard. The interviewer leaned in. "What's the secret to your synergy?"

Solar answered, smooth as glass. "He listens. Most players just want to be heard."

That shut the room up. Even Rascal blinked at her.

She looked at the mic. "He lets me be the playmaker. And when I fall short, he's already there."

The silence was sharp. Respectful. Rascal nudged her foot gently under the table. "You're gonna make me blush."

"You can't. You're undead."

Their laughter, this time, felt different. Quieter.

More real.



By the end of the shoot, Twitter was already on fire.

#Solarcal was trending.

Clips of their banter were looped with romantic music. Fans were debating if they were dating, soulmates, or just peak duo material.

Solar saw the edits from the hotel room that night. She didn't comment. Didn't retweet.

But she watched one loop of their team photo—his hand on her shoulder, her face turning toward him—at least ten times. And every time, she saw something new.



The hallways were dark by the time Solar slipped out of her room.

Past midnight. The chaos of media day had faded. Her heels were off, her hair pinned up in a lazy bun, jersey swapped for a hoodie. Her limbs ached in that deep, satisfying way—like she'd lived too much life in one day.

She didn't bother texting him. She just knew where he'd be.

The hotel's practice suite was in the basement—converted from an old ballroom, now lined with PCs and dim orange lights. One side of the room was empty, quiet. The other? Lit up by the glow of a single monitor.

Rascal sat there, hunched slightly forward, headset slung around his neck, hand on the mouse like it was second nature. He didn't flinch when she walked in.

"Took you long enough," he said without turning.

"You psychic now?" she murmured.

He finally looked over. Smiled, just a little. "You always come when you're thinking too loud."

She slid into the chair beside him, fingers tapping the desk like a nervous tic. They didn't speak at first. He queued up a custom game—just the two of them.

Tracer and Echo. Of course.

They didn't need to say anything. They knew each other's moves better than they knew their own names. Each dash, blink, glide, and flick of the mouse was a language only they understood.

Solar died first.

"Trash," Rascal teased.

"Cheating," she retorted.

"Copium."

They both laughed. And then... it slowed. The trash talk faded. The clicks softened. They sat, lit by pixel glow, just breathing in sync.

Finally, Rascal set his headset down. "You're better in real life," he said.

Solar blinked. "At the game?" she asked.

He shook his head. "At being you."

Her throat caught. "That's a weird thing to say," she whispered.

"Yeah. I'm kind of weird."

They didn't touch. Didn't lean in. But the space between them felt magnetic.

"I was scared to meet you," she admitted.

"I still am," he said.

Solar looked at him—really looked. And for once, she didn't feel like she had to be anyone else. Just her. And that was enough.

They stayed there, shoulders barely apart, eyes on the monitor, neither saying what they were thinking. Because sometimes silence was safer than the truth.

And sometimes, the truth was already obvious.

By 2 a.m., they were still sitting there. Not gaming. Not talking. Just... existing. Together.

18: Cracks & Clarity

Solar had never felt her fingers this stiff before a match.

Not even when she debuted.

Not even when she stood under a stage light that felt hot enough to melt bone.

But here, sitting in the front row of a real tournament PC café—Shock jerseys pressed and present, overhead lights buzzing with quiet menace—it felt different. More final. More exposed.

Real.

"Okay, headsets on," Crusty barked, pacing like a war general in sneakers. "We run two maps. Keep it clean. Rascal, Super, run lead. Solar—trust your instincts."

She nodded. Swallowed.

The game launched. Her Tracer spawned.

The first minute was okay. Safe. Measured.

Then came the flub.

She blinked in too deep—caught in a grav before Rascal could peel. Died fast. The feed lit up red.

Super's voice cut through: "Yo, Solar, you okay? You're moving like Nero on a bad ping."

"Rude," Nero muttered.

Solar's throat clenched. "Yeah. Sorry. I'm—fine."

Rascal spoke quietly, only for her: "Reset. Breathe. We're good."

But her aim felt off. Her pacing was behind. She kept checking the minimap like a beginner.

They lost the round.

Again.

Crusty's voice was stone.

"Regroup in two. Everyone out."

She yanked her headset off and pressed her forehead to her hands.

The weight of her jersey suddenly felt suffocating.

She stood up to leave, but Crusty intercepted her at the door.

"Walk," he said.

They took the long hall behind the PC room—lined with vending machines and posters from Shock championships past.

"You're tight," Crusty said, not unkindly.

"I know."

"You're not here because you're perfect. You're here because you're passionate. You're fire. And fire doesn't apologize."

She stared at the floor.

"I just don't want to let anyone down."

"You will," he said flatly. "That's part of the job. But what matters is that you come back swinging. You understand?"

She looked up. Nodded.

"Good. Get back in there."

And she did.



Back in her room after scrims, Solar collapsed onto the mattress fully clothed, the air conditioning humming overhead like static in her chest.

She scrolled through her notifications mindlessly, fingers twitching with leftover adrenaline. Most of it was harmless—fan edits, fancams, tagged photos from today's media day. The usual chaos.

Until she opened her DMs. It was a message from a blank burner account. No icon. No bio. Just an image.

A photo of Jungwon—at some press event maybe a year ago, his face half-obscured by a passing umbrella.

But it wasn't him that unsettled her.

It was the fact that she was in the background. Barely visible. Standing behind a barricade in a crowd of fans, head down, mask on.

You wouldn't notice unless you were looking for her. And someone had been. Her stomach turned.

There was no caption. No emoji. Nothing. Just the image.

Her thumb hovered over the block button, but her hands were too cold to move. A new message popped up—not from the same account.

JUNGWON:
Did you get that weird post too?
I think someone's watching us.

She didn't reply. Not yet.

She just sat there, spine locked, phone trembling in her grip. Because she knew who it was.

Danielle hadn't said anything in weeks. And now, she didn't have to.



She didn't remember falling asleep.

One minute she was staring at that photo, heart pounding.
The next—darkness. No dreams. Just a throb behind her eyes and the weight of too much adrenaline settling into her spine.

The morning light woke her.

And with it, a new notification.

MINJI 🐇
You looked really happy yesterday.
Hope it was real.

No emojis. No punctuation. Just quiet, intentional words. Solar sat up in bed, the phone cradled in both hands like it might break if she held it wrong. Minji didn't message often. Not directly. Not anymore.

She read it again. And again.

The thing was—Minji had always known how to say the right thing at the exact wrong time.

Solar typed out a reply:

It was.

Paused. Deleted it. Typed again:

It felt real.

Deleted that, too.

In the end, she just locked her screen and set the phone aside. Some things weren't ready to be answered.



Solar was brushing her teeth when Super barged into her room. "We're ordering snacks and bullying Glister in Rascal's suite. You're coming."

Toothpaste still in her mouth, she pointed to her pajamas. "I'm not even dressed."

"Even better. You'll fit the vibe."

Ten minutes later, she was curled up on a floor pillow, hoodie over her knees, surrounded by half a dozen boys and a sea of chip bags, soju cans, and phone chargers.

Rascal was on the bed, Glister beside him, already being peer-pressured into downloading a cursed mobile game by Nero.

Smurf had decided to host a one-man ASMR mukbang in the corner.

Super, of course, was narrating like a Twitch streamer on meth.

"And here we have Rascal, the stoic support with an inner world so vast it scares him to feel. Tragic... Romantic... Soft little man."

"Shut up," Rascal muttered, throwing a mini pillow at him.

"Hey!" Super caught it midair. "Violence is a love language!"

Solar laughed so hard she nearly spilled her drink.

Nero turned to her. "You didn't tell us you were cracked at impressions."

"I wasn't trying to."

"She's modest," Rascal added, taking a slow sip from his can. "Also lethal."

"Aw, he thinks I'm dangerous," Solar cooed.

"I think you're emotionally violent."

"Even better."

Smurf shoved a handful of shrimp chips in his mouth. "You guys flirt so weird."

Solar blushed and reached for a nearby chip bag just to avoid responding.



It was past 2 a.m. by the time they calmed down.

The room was dim now, just the soft blue glow of someone's idle laptop screen. Half the team had fallen asleep where they sat. Glister was snoring with a jacket over his head.

Solar moved to stand, but Rascal was already beside her. "I'll walk you."

"You don't have to—"

"I want to."

They walked in silence, the hallway quiet but not uncomfortable. At her door, she hesitated. "Thanks," she said softly. "For... all of it."

Rascal looked at her, tired but warm. "You're doing better than you think."

She nodded.

"Get some sleep," he added.

Then, with a little wave, he turned and walked back down the hall.

She watched him go, heart strange and heavy in the best way.

Not love. Not yet.

But maybe the start of something that mattered just as much.



The rooftop was colder than she expected.

Solar had borrowed Rascal's hoodie—he'd left it draped over the back of a chair, and it smelled like detergent and mint gum. She tugged it tighter around herself as she stepped onto the concrete, the city stretched wide beneath her.

The skyline blinked like a slow heartbeat. She didn't come up there to cry.

But the moment she leaned against the railing, it hit her anyway.

Everything. The pressure. The headlines. The weight of being two people at once.

Her fingers gripped the cold metal. Hard. She let herself feel it. All of it.

Jihoon's voice still echoed in the parts of her she didn't talk about. The ones that told her she wasn't enough. That she'd never be.

She'd fought for her future like a wild animal. And now that she had it, she was terrified to lose it.

A quiet sob slipped out before she could catch it.

"I'm okay," she whispered to the sky. "I'm okay, I'm okay—"

She wasn't. But she would be.

She stayed there for a while. Long enough to let the storm inside her pass. Long enough to remember why she loved all of this in the first place.

The games.
The music.
The fight.
And the people who saw her—even when she was breaking.

Solar wiped her face on her sleeve and looked out at Seoul one more time. Then she turned around.

And went back inside.










19: Somewhere Soft to Land

The clip was less than thirty seconds.

It wasn't even dramatic. No tears. No big gestures. Just a moment—barely noticeable, unless you were watching for it.

Solar and Rascal on the scrim stage, sitting side-by-side. She was laughing—head tilted back, cheeks pink from exertion. He leaned over with a bottle of water, uncapped it, handed it to her. She took it without looking, fingers brushing his. Their shoulders touched. Neither moved away.

Then came the line.

Someone off-camera had made a comment—some teasing quip about her being "carried."
Rascal's voice cut in, low and clear:

"She carries all of us. You just don't notice."

The clip had been posted by a fan page with 200 followers.

Within an hour, it had 80,000 views.
By the end of the day, it was trending.

#SolarRascal
#HardCarryDuo
#KpopxEsports

The comments were split down the middle:

"Ugh she's always making it about her."
"Can she just pick a career already?"
"Why is she even on the team???"
"Let them be cute in peace, you jealous freaks."
"They have more chemistry than half the romcoms I've seen this year."
"Protect Solar at all costs."

Solar read them all.

Then she read them again.
And again.

Until the words weren't words anymore, just static scrolling across her screen. Her fingers trembled. She dropped her phone.

It bounced against the hotel comforter and landed face-down.

She stared at it.

Her ears were ringing.

No one knocked on the door.
No one called.

She didn't expect them to.

She curled her knees to her chest and tucked her face against them. The air felt too heavy. The room too quiet.

Her hands were still shaking when she stood up and slipped her hoodie on. Her lanyard with the Shock badge caught on the zipper as she walked out into the hallway.

The elevator was too slow. So she took the stairs.

By the time she reached the first floor, her eyes were glassy—but dry.

She didn't cry.
But it was close.



The lobby was empty when she stepped into it—dimly lit, with soft echoes of distant conversation. Tournament call time wasn't for another thirty minutes. Most of the team would still be upstairs, running warm-ups or nursing caffeine.

Solar found the corner near the vending machines and sank to the floor, legs drawn up, hoodie pulled over her hands. She stared at the carpet's strange swirling pattern like it held answers.

"Hey."

The voice wasn't loud.

She looked up slowly.

Rascal stood a few feet away, wearing joggers, a crumpled Shock hoodie, and that familiar sheepish expression he always got when he didn't know how to enter a moment gently.

"Didn't mean to scare you," he added, voice softer now. "Saw the elevator go past my floor. Figured it was you."

Solar tried to smile. It came out broken.

Rascal didn't comment. Just walked over and sat beside her—back against the wall, legs outstretched, close enough that their shoulders nearly touched but didn't.

For a while, neither of them said anything.

Then, quietly, Solar muttered, "I feel like I'm ruining everything."

Rascal blinked. "What?"

"The team. The focus. Our image. I just wanted to play. And now I'm a headline again."

He looked at her. Not with pity—but with something heavier. Something careful.

"You didn't ask to be a headline."

"Doesn't matter. I am."

Rascal picked at the seam of his hoodie. "You know what I think?" he said after a beat. "I think most people don't know what to do with a girl who can outplay them and still look like a magazine cover."

Solar scoffed. "Thanks?"

"It's a compliment. You're terrifying."

She laughed, quietly. But it cracked midway through. She bit her lip hard.

"I just... I don't know how long I can keep this up. HYBE's already given me two talks. I can't afford another. And now the fans think we're—"

She cut herself off.

Rascal's eyes didn't move from hers.

"I know," he said simply.

A long pause.

Then she whispered, "Do you think I'm a problem?"

"No," he said, instantly.

She flinched.

He leaned back against the wall again, exhaling through his nose.

"You're not a problem, Solar. You're just brave enough to be seen. That scares people."

She blinked hard.

"I don't want to drag the team down."

"You don't. You push us forward."

Her throat ached. Rascal turned slightly, his arm brushing hers. He didn't look at her when he said it:

"I'll tell them we're not a thing, if it helps."

Solar swallowed. "It's not about that."

"Okay..."

Another pause.

"You're busy," he added. "I get it."

She turned toward him fully now, tears stinging behind her eyes. "I don't want to lose you."

"You won't." Rascal finally looked at her. His gaze wasn't soft—it was steady. "I'm not going anywhere. Not unless you tell me to."

She didn't answer. Instead, her hand reached for his.

And for the first time since the video went viral, the noise quieted. Even just for a little while.



The quiet ended abruptly when Rascal's phone buzzed in his pocket.

He sighed heavily, pulling it out and glancing at the screen.

"Crusty," he said softly, half-smiling. "Says we're meeting in ten. Apparently, Nero's already trying to start drama with the opposing team."

Solar felt a smile tug at the corner of her mouth. "Of course he is."

Rascal stood first, extending a hand. Solar hesitated, just a heartbeat, before taking it. He lifted her gently, letting go the instant she was steady on her feet.

"You good?" he asked, voice low and careful.

"Good enough," she said quietly.

He nodded, accepting it.

Together, they moved toward the elevators. The doors opened, revealing Glister and Super mid-argument about optimal snack choices for peak gaming performance.

"I'm telling you," Super insisted dramatically, "sugar is the key to victory."

"And I'm telling you, it's protein," Glister shot back.

Nero leaned against the elevator wall, rolling his eyes. "The only thing winning today is my sanity—if you two shut up."

The doors shut behind them, closing the team into a small, chaotic bubble.

"Solar!" Super noticed her first. "You okay? Saw your phone blowing up earlier. Twitter's wild, dude."

Solar smiled thinly. "Yeah, it's fine. I'm used to it."

"No," Glister corrected gently. "You're stronger than it."

Super elbowed Glister affectionately. "When did you get wise?"

"I always was," Glister shrugged, smiling shyly. "No one noticed."

"Aw, Glister's grown up," Nero teased. "Guess we don't need Rascal's elderly wisdom anymore."

Rascal gave Nero a mock-serious glare. "Respect your elders."

The elevator dinged. The doors slid open. Crusty stood waiting, clipboard in hand, focused and unamused.

"Let's go," he said sharply, turning without waiting for their reply.

They followed immediately, falling into formation like trained soldiers. The tournament room buzzed with electricity as they entered—screens glowing, headsets waiting. Solar took a deep breath, adjusting her headset. She caught Rascal's eye across the room. He gave a tiny, confident nod.

No more fear. No more headlines. Just this—just the game.

She exhaled slowly, feeling steady for the first time that day.

"Alright, Shock," Crusty's voice cut clearly through the comms. "Let's show them what we came for."

Solar gripped her mouse, pulse settling.

"Let's do this," she said quietly, to herself more than anyone else.

And she meant it.

The lights dimmed. Screens flickered to life, washing the stage in vibrant color.

Solar's heart thundered, headphones snug against her ears. Her pulse beat like a metronome, steadying as the announcer's voice boomed through the arena.

"Ladies and gentlemen—welcome to the Summer Cup! The San Francisco Shock versus Seoul Dynasty!"

The crowd erupted into cheers, the sound vibrating through Solar's bones. She could feel their anticipation—almost taste it.

The countdown appeared.

3...2...1...

"Let's go!" Super's voice cut through clearly, commanding and sure.

Solar instantly slipped into focus, hands moving automatically. The world narrowed down to the vivid hues of the screen.

"Solar, flank left," Rascal called smoothly.

She darted forward as Tracer, pulse bomb in hand. She moved swiftly, slipping behind enemy lines with practiced ease.

She felt it—her instincts humming, every nerve alive and alert. Pulse pounding, mind crystal-clear.

"Solar, careful—"

Nero's warning came too late. Enemy Widowmaker's bullet grazed by, inches from her head. Solar inhaled sharply but kept moving.

"Widow's looking at Solar!" Glister shouted, urgency sharp.

"I got you." Rascal's voice was firm.

In seconds, Rascal's Genji sliced across her view, deflecting incoming shots. He carved a path, clearing the threat. Solar threw the pulse bomb.

"Enemy supports down!" Crusty's voice crackled through the comms, edged with rare excitement.

"Push, push!" Super called out, voice electric.

The next few moments blurred together—precision shots, split-second saves, heart-pounding escapes. Solar's breath came in rapid, controlled bursts, fingers flying over the keys.

Then, the final play. Solar's Tracer danced dangerously close to enemy lines, drawing their fire. Rascal's Genji appeared beside her—perfectly timed.

"Now!" she cried.

Together, they executed the move they'd practiced endlessly, perfectly synchronized.

Enemy team wiped. The objective secured. The victory screen flashed golden across every monitor.

For a heartbeat, Solar couldn't breathe. Then the cheers hit her like a tidal wave—roaring and deafening, vibrating through the arena floor.

Super threw his headset down, shouting triumphantly. Glister laughed—giddy and disbelieving. Nero was screaming something entirely incoherent.

Solar's eyes found Rascal. He was smiling, breathless, shaking his head as if stunned. She stood up, legs trembling, and stepped toward him without thinking. Before she could hesitate, he met her halfway, pulling her into a hug that felt like coming home.

"We did it," he whispered, voice raw with emotion.

"No," she corrected, smiling through sudden tears. "You carried."

He laughed softly, letting her go.

"You finally admit it," he teased gently.

Solar shook her head, still smiling, eyes burning.

All around them, the crowd's cheers blurred into one deafening wave of validation. For the first time, Solar felt like she truly belonged—not just as an idol trying to prove herself—but as a teammate.

As a player. As someone worthy of being seen.



The team poured out into the backstage lounge, laughter and excitement echoing off the walls. Nero was the loudest—already reenacting his favorite moments, hands gesturing wildly.

"And did you see that pulse bomb? Solar, you absolute menace!"

Solar laughed, cheeks warm, shaking her head. "Only because Rascal cleared the way."

"Oh, don't be humble now," Rascal teased, lightly elbowing her. "You practically won that last fight by yourself."

Super threw an arm around Glister, grinning. "Protein, right?"

Glister rolled his eyes, grinning back. "Maybe sugar helped."

Smurf sank into the couch, beaming contentedly. "We really did it, guys."

Crusty entered last, a rare smile breaking his normally stoic demeanor. "Good job. You all played like champions today."

"Aw, Coach, don't get soft now," Nero joked. "We might start thinking you like us."

Crusty gave him a flat look, but his lips twitched upward.

Solar leaned against the wall, quietly taking it all in. She felt lighter—like she'd shed layers of weight she'd been carrying for too long. Rascal appeared beside her, holding two water bottles. He offered one silently.

"Thanks," she murmured.

He nodded, taking a long sip from his own bottle. "See? Told you we'd carry the world."

Solar smiled softly, looking down at the bottle in her hands. "Yeah, you did."

He hesitated, eyes gentle. "Hey. Seriously, you're good. More than good. You belong here."

Her heart warmed, something deep and comforting. "Thank you," she whispered.

Before she could overthink it, Nero broke the moment with a dramatic announcement. "Team dinner! My treat—actually, wait, no. Crusty's treat."

Crusty sighed deeply, feigning annoyance, though clearly amused. "Fine. But you're not picking the place."

Cheers erupted again, and the room felt full—full of laughter, pride, and something undeniably like family. Solar glanced around once more, imprinting this moment in her memory. Here, surrounded by these people who'd become something more than teammates, she felt a belonging she'd nearly forgotten existed.

And quietly, just to herself, she finally believed: She deserved this.

All of it.

20: Balancing Act

HYBE's headquarters felt different today—warmer, somehow, despite the familiar sterile gleam of marble floors and glass walls.

Solar sat alone in the conference room, fingertips tracing absent circles on the polished table. She was nervous, pulse fluttering unevenly in her throat. But unlike previous meetings, today's anxiety was tinged with cautious hope rather than dread.

The door opened smoothly. Manager Kang stepped inside first, followed by two senior executives she recognized vaguely from quarterly briefings.

"Good afternoon, Solar," Kang greeted with an unusually soft smile, taking his seat across from her. The executives nodded politely as they sat down.

She forced a polite smile in return, hands neatly clasped in her lap.

"We watched your tournament yesterday," one executive—a woman in her fifties named Director Han—spoke first. Her voice was gentle yet authoritative, her expression carefully neutral. "You performed exceptionally well."

Solar relaxed slightly, her shoulders losing some of their tension. "Thank you."

Kang cleared his throat softly, meeting her eyes earnestly. "We've noticed the shift in public sentiment. Positively, I should add. You're becoming quite the topic, even outside typical idol spheres."

"Which is good," Director Han interjected smoothly, "but also delicate."

Solar's throat tightened. Here it came—the warnings, the boundaries.

Director Han continued, her tone carefully measured. "We admire your ability to manage both worlds, truly. But you must remember, you carry our image as much as your own."

Solar nodded slowly, heart sinking slightly. "I understand."

"We believe you do," Kang reassured her quickly, voice softer now. "And that's why we support your continued presence in eSports. It's brought a unique and fresh attention to our brand. However..."

Solar waited quietly for the catch.

"We need you to remain aware of how quickly public opinion can shift. Just... be careful." Kang's voice softened even further, revealing genuine care beneath the corporate calm. "Your reputation is precious. Guard it carefully."

Solar swallowed gently, nodding once again. "I will."

Director Han rose smoothly from her chair, nodding approvingly. "Excellent. Keep making us proud, Solar."

As the executives filed out, Kang lingered briefly, meeting her gaze again, warmth in his eyes.

"You did great yesterday," he said quietly. "Truly."

Solar offered him a small, genuine smile. "Thank you. It means a lot."

He smiled warmly in return, closing the door behind him. Left alone again, Solar exhaled slowly, the weight she'd carried into the room feeling a little lighter. Validation, however careful, was still validation. And today, it felt like exactly what she needed.



The change in media was immediate. Articles praised her resilience, her dual talent, and her groundbreaking role in bridging eSports with K-pop.

Solar scrolled through her social media with awe, reading headlines that celebrated rather than criticized.

"Solar: Redefining Idol Expectations."
"San Francisco Shock's Secret Weapon Shines!"

Her chest swelled with pride, even as the sudden praise felt surreal.

Then she saw Danielle's post. It was subtle—a cryptic Instagram story featuring an old, broken doll, captioned simply, "Fragile things break easily."

Solar's skin prickled. A threat hidden in plain sight. She locked her phone, breathing unevenly.



The hallway outside the HYBE recording studio was quiet when Solar spotted Jungwon, leaning casually against the wall as if waiting for someone. His gaze lifted slowly to hers, eyes unreadable.

"Congratulations," he said softly, voice carrying that familiar intensity. "Saw your game. Impressive."

Solar felt her cheeks warm. "Thanks."

His eyes narrowed slightly, thoughtfully. "You and Rascal make a good team."

It wasn't quite jealousy—but it felt close. Solar hesitated, heart racing.

"Yeah," she said softly. "We do."

Jungwon nodded slowly, pushing off the wall. As he passed her, he murmured softly enough only she could hear, "Just be careful who you trust."



Later that night, a notification pinged softly from her phone. It was Minji.

"Congrats, Solar. You were amazing. Can we talk sometime soon?"

Solar stared at the screen, her heart softening. Minji's vulnerability always tugged at something deep inside her. Carefully, she replied.

"Yeah, I'd like that."



It was nearly midnight when Solar found Rascal online. His Discord icon green, welcoming.

"You awake?" she messaged.

Seconds later, he called. His voice was warm, comforting as always. "You okay?"

"Yeah," she sighed, smiling despite herself. "Just a lot today."

They settled into an easy silence, broken by Rascal's gentle question. "What's it like? Being so... public? An idol, I mean."

Solar considered it thoughtfully. "Exhausting. Like being watched all the time, even when you're alone. You have to be perfect—constantly."

"Sounds hard," he murmured.

"It is," she admitted softly. "But it's also rewarding. When I'm on stage, and everything just clicks, it feels worth it."

"And gaming?" Rascal's voice carried gentle curiosity.

"Different," Solar smiled faintly. "It's easier. Lighter. With the Shock... I get to just be me. I don't have to be flawless all the time."

He was quiet for a moment. "Do you regret it? This double life?"

"No," Solar said, certain now. "I think I need both sides. One reminds me I'm strong; the other reminds me it's okay to not always have to be."

Rascal sighed softly, voice vulnerable now. "I just... I worry I'm holding you back sometimes."

"You're not," she whispered, heart aching gently. "You help me breathe." There was a long silence, full of unspoken truths.

"Good," he finally said softly. "Because you deserve that. To breathe. To feel human."

She closed her eyes, holding those words close. In this careful balancing act of hers, Rascal had quietly become the most stable thing she knew.

And right now, she needed stable more than ever.















21: Aftershock

The quiet café Minji chose felt like an escape, nestled in an alley away from prying eyes and judgment. The afternoon sun filtered through gauzy curtains, casting gentle patterns over the wooden tables.

Minji sat opposite Solar, fingers lightly tracing the rim of her untouched coffee cup. Her eyes flicked upwards cautiously, soft uncertainty clear in her expression.

"Solar, I—" Minji began, voice catching slightly. Her fingers twisted the edge of the paper napkin until it frayed. "I've gone over this in my head so many times. And every version ends with me either saying too much or not enough." She paused, breath trembling softly. "I don't even know how to say this."

Solar watched quietly, her chest tight with a mix of empathy and anxious anticipation. "Take your time," Solar murmured softly, her voice barely rising above the low jazz playing in the background. She leaned in just slightly, signaling with her presence that she wasn't going anywhere.

Minji's eyes filled with a quiet longing, her words barely above a whisper. "I've never felt...like this. About anyone." She hesitated, cheeks flushed. "Especially not...another girl. It's confusing."Solar's breath caught softly.

She reached gently across the table, lightly brushing Minji's fingertips. "It's okay," she murmured gently. "Feelings don't have to make sense all the time."

Minji exhaled shakily, eyes closing briefly. "But they should, right? Everyone expects me to have everything together. To always know exactly who I am." Her voice trembled slightly. "With you, I just feel... real. Like I can breathe differently around you. Not because you make me feel small or dazed, but because you look at me like I'm not broken. Even when I don't know who I am, you don't try to fix me. You just... let me be."

Solar felt her throat tighten, moved by Minji's vulnerability. "Maybe that's enough, Minji," Solar said quietly, her throat tightening. "Maybe feeling real is all we're ever really looking for. The rest... titles, labels, approval—that's for everyone else."

Minji's eyes softened further, her hand inching across the table as if to close the distance. Her lips parted slightly—like she was about to say something far too brave—when her phone buzzed abruptly, shattering the delicate moment like glass.

Her face paled slightly as she read the screen. "It's Danielle. She... wants to talk."

Solar stiffened involuntarily. Danielle's earlier message flashed vividly in her mind.

"Be careful," Solar whispered, her voice quieter now, almost hollow. There was a warning buried beneath the words, something instinctual and protective.

Minji nodded quietly, looking torn. "I will. But Solar, please...think about what I said?"

"I will," Solar promised softly.

They parted ways quietly, leaving Solar feeling strangely adrift, heart caught between tenderness and quiet unease.




The unease crystallized hours later at a promotional event—a simple stage greeting. It should have been routine, safe.

Solar's smile froze as she stepped onto the stage, eyes catching sight of Danielle in the crowd, her stare sharp and unsettling. Suddenly, the stage lights flickered. Solar stumbled, barely catching herself against a railing as the set momentarily darkened.

Whispers filled the room, concerned murmurs rippling outward. Solar's heart raced unevenly as she caught Danielle's faint smile, disturbingly serene amidst the chaos.

Backstage, after the lights flickered and the whispers started, Solar ducked into a narrow hallway lined with wires and dusty backup equipment. Her breaths came shallow. She didn't like how familiar this creeping fear felt—like being followed home at night, even when no one was behind you.

Jungwon appeared from the shadows like he'd already been looking for her."You alright?" he asked again, but his tone this time was quieter, almost reverent. His brows were drawn together in sharp concern.

"I'm fine," she muttered, but her voice betrayed the truth. She wasn't.

He stepped closer, gaze searching. "I know what fear looks like, Solar. I've lived in it. And I've watched people hide it behind makeup and applause." His voice dropped low. "Don't pretend for me."

She blinked up at him, surprised. "Why do you care so much?"

There was a long pause. His eyes didn't flinch."Because no one ever cared like that for me."




Returning to the dorm, Solar found Yunjin already buried in research, laptop open, eyes narrowed in concentration.

"I've been digging," Yunjin declared without looking up. "Danielle has history, Solar. Like, disturbing stuff. Obsessions with other trainees, strange accidents."

Solar's stomach turned uneasily. "Accidents?"

"Nothing proven, but there's a pattern," Yunjin said grimly. "Every time someone stood between her and something she wanted, things got ugly."

Solar shivered involuntarily, images of Danielle's cold gaze resurfacing.

"You're not facing this alone," Yunjin promised fiercely. "I won't let her touch you."

Solar smiled weakly, her fingers tightening around Yunjin's in a silent thank-you. In a world that sometimes felt like it only took, Yunjin always gave.



Late that evening, the gaming session with Rascal felt strained, quieter than usual. His laughter was softer, jokes forced.

"You okay?" Solar finally asked gently, breaking the uneasy quiet.

Rascal hesitated, then sighed softly. "Yeah, just...thinking, I guess."

Solar waited patiently, sensing his hesitation. "About what?"

He paused again, vulnerability slipping through. "You and Jungwon. You guys seem...close lately."

Solar's heart sank softly, hearing the quiet hurt in his voice. "Rascal—"

"It's okay," he interrupted softly, clearly struggling to mask his feelings. "I just...sometimes I worry I'm holding you back. That you need more than I can give."

Solar's chest ached sharply. "No, you're not. You never could."
