He exhaled softly, warmth returning to his voice slowly. "Good. Because you deserve someone who makes your life easier, not harder."

"You already do," she whispered quietly, truth heavy in her voice. 


The gentle silence stretched warmly between them, understanding deeper now.

"I saw the photos," he admitted eventually. "The ones with Jungwon."

"Nothing happened," she said quickly.

"I know," Rascal said. "It's not about that. It's just... you're out there, with the world watching. And I'm in this voice channel, acting like we're the same."

"You are the same," she whispered. "You matter. More than you think."

There was a long pause. Then, gently, "Sometimes it feels like I'm a side quest in your main story."

"Don't say that."

"I mean it in the best way," he murmured. "You're destined for all this. I'm just trying not to get in the way of your light."

"You're not in the way. You're the reason I don't burn out."



Jaehyun called the next morning, voice crisp and clipped with authority.

"We need to talk," he announced without preamble, clearly agitated. "Your name's everywhere. This Jungwon, Solar—he's trouble. I've looked into him."

Solar bristled instinctively, defensive. "Jaehyun, stop. He's not—"

"I'm your brother," he cut in sharply, voice softer but firm. "I have to protect you. Just be careful."

The call ended abruptly, leaving Solar frustrated and conflicted.

Chaerin arrived shortly afterward, Gucci sunglasses perched casually atop her head.

"What'd Jaehyun say now?" she demanded knowingly, rolling her eyes. "Let me guess—overprotective nonsense?"

Solar smiled weakly. "Basically."

Chaerin sighed dramatically, settling next to her gently. "Look, you've always been the bravest out of all of us. Follow your heart, Solar. Jaehyun means well, but this is your life."

Solar leaned against her sister softly, comforted by Chaerin's quiet support. "Thank you."

Chaerin squeezed her hand gently, voice soft. "Always."

As the sun dipped low, casting soft gold over their quiet moment, Solar realized how precarious her world had become. Yet, surrounded by people who fiercely loved and protected her, she also understood something crucial:

She was not alone.

Whatever lay ahead, she could face it—surrounded by strength and love that would not let her fall.

Her mind thought back to Minji's words... she'd hesitated longer than before, visibly fighting her own instincts. "When I first saw you on stage, I thought... 'She's untouchable.' Not just because you were talented or pretty, but because you didn't look like you were trying to be anything other than who you are. I've spent my whole life performing other people's expectations. But you made me want to question that."

Solar swallowed. Her fingers curled around her cup. "I think I used to be scared of being seen too. Not in the spotlight. But... really seen."

Jaehyun's voice on the phone cut back through her thoughts, it was gruff, but older siblings had a way of making everything sound like a command. "You don't see what I see. These boys, their companies, their ambitions—they'll cut your throat with a smile."

"Jungwon isn't like that," Solar had insisted.

There was silence on the other end. "Neither was Jihoon. Until he was."

Solar's hands tightened on the phone. That name still had a way of slicing through her calm. Jaehyun's voice had softened. "I'm not trying to control you. I just... can't watch it happen again."
That night, Solar sat on her bed, legs tucked under her, phone face-down beside her. She stared at her reflection in the dark window, face half-lit by the city outside.

She thought about Minji's eyes. Jungwon's warning. Rascal's quiet fears. Her siblings' protection. Her life wasn't a double life. It was one whole, messy, complicated thing.

But for the first time in a while, she didn't feel like she was drowning in it. She was swimming. She was surviving. And maybe—just maybe—she was winning.






22: Last Looks

The photos from Solar's recent shoot were already everywhere.

A high-gloss magazine had featured her under a bold, clean title: "The Idol Who Plays to Win." The subheader was worse. Something about being a "new kind of girl"—pretty, powerful, unpredictable.

The interview hadn't meant to be personal. It was supposed to be light: a day in the life of an idol who plays video games. But then the journalist had asked something weirdly sharp: "Which part of you is real—the one on stage, or the one with the controller?"

Solar hadn't had an answer at the time. So she'd said: "Maybe the real me is the one no one sees."

That was the line that went viral.

She hadn't read the quote again. She didn't need to. It echoed through fan posts, tweet threads, and heated debates like it had unlocked some secret about her that she hadn't even known she was hiding.

Part of her was flattered. But a bigger part was afraid. She hadn't meant to say something true.

Rascal hadn't messaged her since the tournament ended.

The Shock boys were packing up. The hotel lobby was full of flight confirmations and plastic-wrapped souvenirs and Glister trying to force a plushie into Nero's duffel bag. Everyone was loud and tired and happy.

Except Rascal.

He wasn't cold. Just... distant.

So Solar texted him.

SOLAR: you busy?

RASCAL: just chilling

SOLAR: can I come hang out? just us?

RASCAL: yeah. last chance, huh?

She didn't know why that hurt.

His hotel room looked the same as always—lived in, a little cluttered, filled with the subtle chaos of someone always half-somewhere else. A pair of headphones dangled off the side of the desk. Two open cans of Red Bull sat side by side like they were keeping each other company.

Rascal opened the door and smiled like he'd practiced it.

Solar stepped in slowly. "Feels weird that you're leaving."

"We'll be back," he said, shrugging. "Unless Crusty trades me."

"Don't joke like that," she muttered, curling onto the corner of the bed. Her hoodie sleeves swallowed her hands.

He joined her, just close enough for their knees to brush. "You're blowing up."

She snorted. "That's one way to put it."

"No seriously," he said. "You're the talk of the scene. Even my friends in Seoul who don't follow OWL know your handle now."

"I didn't ask for any of that."

"I know."

The pause stretched too long. So she asked it.

"Are we okay?"

Rascal hesitated.

"You're busy. I get it."

"Don't say it like that."

He looked at her then, really looked. "I'm proud of you. But I also know what happens when people get too big. They don't mean to leave people behind. But they do."

"I would never—"

"I know," he said. "But I also know I'm not the story. I'm just one of the chapters."

"That's not fair."

He smiled faintly. "It's not about fair. It's about facts."

"I don't want you to go."

"I don't want to go either."

And for a moment, it felt like they'd known each other longer than they had. Like time had bent around them. Something unspoken. Something unfinished.

She leaned against him gently. "Thank you. For everything."

His voice was soft. "Always."

Later that night, she found Minji's voice note buried in her inbox.

She sat cross-legged in bed, the dorm quiet except for the hum of the AC. Her room was a safe, still place—but the moment she pressed play, her chest tightened.

"Hey," Minji's voice whispered. "I saw your interview. The thing you said about masks... it stuck. I don't know what that means for us. I just... miss you."

She played it again. And again. She didn't answer.

Not yet.

The next morning brought something darker. A message. No name. No profile photo. A screenshot. Zoomed in. Fuzzy. But unmistakable.

It was her and Jungwon—caught in a quiet moment backstage after the rainstorm. Her face turned up toward his, his hand barely brushing her arm.

The caption read: Some things don't stay secret forever.

Solar's hands went cold. She ran straight to Yunjin.

Yunjin didn't even blink. She grabbed the phone, studied the message, then stood up so fast her chair toppled.

"I swear to GOD if Danielle—"

"Yunjin—"

"No. This isn't funny anymore. This is dangerous. This is psychotic."

"I don't want to start a war."

"You're already in one," Yunjin snapped. "And I am not watching you get picked apart piece by piece just to stay quiet."

Solar didn't argue. Because Yunjin was right.

She didn't expect to run into Jungwon at HYBE.

They collided by accident—her arms full of rehearsal notes, him exiting a conference room. The impact nearly knocked her sideways.

His hands shot out. Steadying. Familiar.

"You okay?" he asked.

She nodded. "Someone sent me a picture of us."

His jaw flexed. "What?"

"It was anonymous."

"Let me see it."

"I deleted it."

He paused. Then—gently—"You should've kept it."

"I didn't want to look at it anymore."

His voice dropped low. "I would've handled it."

"I don't need you to fix everything," she said quietly.

"I know."

He reached into his coat pocket and handed her a small charm. Inside was a pressed flower—delicate, violet blue, sealed behind acrylic.

"I think it was yours," he said. "The stylist gave it back. Said you dropped it."

She stared down at it.

"I kept it," Jungwon murmured. "I didn't know why. I just did."

Solar's throat tightened. "Thank you."

"You're not alone," he said. "Even if you don't say anything, I see it. All of it."

And somehow, that felt like the most dangerous truth of all.

Chaewon knocked that night. No makeup. Hoodie on. Her hair in a low bun.

"How are you? Are you alright?" she asked.

Solar nodded.

"I'm worried," Chaewon said. "Not because I think you're failing. But because I think you're carrying too much alone."

Solar blinked fast.

"We don't need you to be perfect," Chaewon said. "We just need you to be here. To let us in."

And Solar, for the first time in days, let herself cry. Not because she was weak. But because she didn't have to be strong, not with them.

Not tonight.


















23: Between the Silence

It wasn't that the dorm felt empty.

It still buzzed with music practice, hair dryers, Eunchae yelling about some Overwatch update in the living room. Chaewon still made tea at night, Sakura still watched dramas with headphones on, and Yunjin still paced the hallway talking to herself like a girl plotting national security.

She missed the way Super would yell 'DOMINANT!' after a good play, how Glister called her 'unnie' even though they were the same age, and Rascal—God, Rascal—how he'd always linger in the voice channel even after practice ended, cracking one last joke before finally logging off. They weren't just teammates anymore. They were... hers. A piece of something she didn't even realize she'd wanted so badly until it was gone.

But something was missing.

Solar sat on the floor of her room with her laptop open and a custom Shock Discord server glowing in the corner. The usual late-night noise—Rascal's teasing, Super's delusions, Glister's bubbly chaos—was gone. The boys were back in San Francisco. Real scrims had resumed. The team house was loud again.

She tried watching a show with Eunchae, but her laughter felt two seconds too late, like her brain was buffering real life. Even Chaewon's warm tea didn't help—the kind with honey and dried jujube slices that usually made her feel grounded. Tonight, it just tasted like water and weight.

Just... not here.

She missed it.

Missed them.

Missed him.

Solar sighed and clicked into a replay file from the tournament—her Tracer dash, Rascal's Echo shielding her flank. It was a perfect fight. The kind that made people say words like "chemistry" and "unbreakable."

Her phone buzzed. A text from Eunchae: *you playing or being emo again lol*

It wasn't fear exactly. It was something grittier. Something cold in her ribs and hot behind her eyes. The kind of panic that came with remembering all the things she never said. All the things he still knew. The way he smiled like he had secrets and worse—like he had hers.

Solar laughed weakly and sent back a gif of SpongeBob crying into a keyboard. Eunchae replied with seven skull emojis.




The next day, Solar left HYBE through the back entrance—hood up, mask on, hair tucked. She wasn't expecting to see anyone. Definitely not him.

It wasn't what he said that haunted her. It was what he didn't say. The way his eyes scanned her like a riddle he already knew the answer to. Like he was daring her to say the truth out loud. That part of her still ached when she saw his name. That part of her still remembered.

Jihoon stood across the alley beside a vending machine, sleeves rolled up, eyes unreadable.

Her heart stopped.

He looked exactly the same and nothing like how she remembered. Still sharp. Still slightly dangerous. Still that weird, beautiful mess she'd never untangle.

"Wow," he said, voice low and amused. "Didn't think I'd see you again this fast. What was it this time—boredom or guilt?"

She froze.

"Or maybe you just missed me watching you." He said it with a grin, like it was a joke. Like it wasn't chilling.

Her hands curled into fists. "What do you want?"

"Nothing." He stepped back. "Just passing through. Like always."

And then he was gone. Like smoke.

She didn't know what she wanted him to say. Just that something in her chest cracked open whenever he was near. Like a lock turning. Like something too big to name trying to push its way out.

She didn't breathe again until she was in the elevator.




That night, her phone lit up again.

MINJI: I don't know what's wrong with me.

The message sat there for two full minutes.

Yunjin started staying up later, watching the security cams HYBE refused to admit were bugged. She kept a spreadsheet now—every weird DM, every missing item, every slightly off moment in a schedule. 'I swear she's in the system,' she whispered. 'Danielle's not just stalking. She's studying us.'

Then another.

It felt like static lived in her bones now. That constant hum of tension. The weight of eyes she couldn't see. Of expectations she didn't ask for. She kept telling herself to breathe. To sleep. To eat. But the days were blurring again, and she didn't know what was real and what was performance.

MINJI: I said I didn't care. I meant it. I was trying to mean it.

Pause.

MINJI: But then I saw you two again. I saw the way you look at him. And the way he looks at you like he'd set the world on fire to keep you warm.

Her voice cracked in the quiet. It wasn't even words—just a small, animal sound. A sound she didn't know she could make. The kind that only slips out when everything else is exhausted—language, logic, willpower. She pressed the phone to her cheek like it could hold her up.

Solar's chest tightened.

MINJI: I hate how I feel. I hate that I can't stop watching. I hate that I still want to text you when I know I shouldn't.

Then:

MINJI: Please don't forget me.

Solar wiped at her face with the corner of her sleeve. The words weren't cruel, but they burned anyway. Because Minji wasn't the kind of girl who unraveled. She was silk and steel and starlight. But this message was something else—cracked, breathless, human. The kind of message you only send when you're at war with your own heart. And Solar didn't know if she could be anyone's battlefield right now.

Solar stared at the screen so long it dimmed twice.

She didn't reply.

She couldn't.




The shoot was supposed to be a formality—just a promotional set for a summer HYBE campaign. But Jungwon was there.

They didn't say much at first. Just the usual stylists, hair prep, awkward nods.

Then the director called for group photos, and Solar found herself walking beside him, just slightly too close.

"You okay?" he asked softly, without looking at her.

"I'm fine."

"I saw the press... The DM leaks."

Solar's breath caught. He looked at her then. Really looked.

Jungwon's eyes had this quiet gravity to them, like the world slowed down whenever he looked directly at her. She didn't know how to explain it—just that it made her feel seen in a way that wasn't about fame or beauty or performance. It made her feel like herself, and that was the scariest part.

"I haven't stopped thinking about that night."

She didn't answer. Couldn't. Her mouth felt like paper. Jungwon didn't push. Just stood near her, quietly shielding her from the chaos around them.

Later, in a still hallway, he touched her sleeve.

"I know you don't trust easily," he said. "But I do see you."

And that was somehow more terrifying than anything Danielle had done.




The next incident was small. At first.

Sakura's vocal monitor was missing. Then found in the sink. Cut wires. No one had touched it, apparently.

A few days later, Yunjin got a package. Inside were screenshots—doctored photos of Solar standing with different boys, cropped to look like scandal. The captions were cryptic. Threatening.

Yunjin lost it.

"She's escalating," she whispered. "This is calculated. I swear she's trying to break you down, bit by bit."

Solar didn't speak. She just walked into the bathroom and locked the door.

She sat on the cold tile floor for almost an hour. Makeup still on. Hoodie sleeves damp with the water she'd splashed on her face.

She felt like she was dissolving. Like there were too many pieces and not enough glue.

A knock came. Then silence. Then a voice.

Her own. Shaky. Uncertain. She reached for her phone and scrolled past every name she knew.

Yunjin...

Chaewon...

Rascal...

And then—

Jungwon.

She didn't think. Didn't plan.

Her hands shook as she hovered over his name. Jungwon. They didn't know each other. Not really. Not deeply. But there was something in her that knew he would answer. That knew, if he heard her voice cracking in the dark, he'd come running anyway.

She just hit call. It rang.

Once. Twice. Then—

"Hello?" His voice was low, confused, soft from sleep.

Solar didn't speak.

He waited. "Solar?"

She opened her mouth. Closed it. Pressed the phone tighter to her ear. "I don't know why I called you," she said finally. "I just... I didn't know who else."

A beat. Then, quietly:

"I'm glad you did."

And somehow, she felt like she could breathe again. Even if just for tonight.




















24: A Little Light

Solar didn't tell anyone about the call.

Not Yunjin. Not Chaewon. Not even Rascal.

It stayed tucked beneath her ribcage like a secret ember—small, glowing, and quietly terrifying. She didn't know why she called Jungwon that night, didn't even remember unlocking her phone. It was like something inside her moved before she did. And when his voice answered—low, confused, gently waiting—she felt her lungs catch like the first breath after drowning.

She'd been walking on that breath ever since.




The next morning, she rose early and left the dorm with no makeup, hoodie drawn low. The café was tucked in a quiet alley behind an old bookstore, the kind of place where the espresso was too bitter and the seats were all secondhand velvet. She used to come here with Yein before everything changed.

The same barista was still there. He didn't recognize her.

Good.

She ordered a lavender latte and sat in the back corner by the dusty ficus. She tucked herself into the corner like an old secret and tried to remember what peace tasted like. The chair creaked under her, fabric familiar and sun-faded. It smelled like dust and ink and memory. For a moment, she just closed her eyes and listened—to the hiss of milk, the quiet rustle of pages, the kind of ambient hush she hadn't known since before fame wrapped around her name like a noose. A lyric notebook sat open on the table in front of her, pen uncapped, blank page glowing with expectation.

But instead of lyrics, she just wrote her name.

Not "Solar."

Her real name.

Over and over. Like she was trying to remember who that girl even was.




By noon, the van picked them up for a HYBE variety shoot.

Eunchae was already bouncing in her seat. "They said we're doing a cooking challenge today! I'm gonna sabotage all of you."

Yunjin rolled her eyes. "You say that every time and then panic and cry when the onions fight back."

Chaewon handed Solar a coffee. "You okay?"

Solar nodded. "Just tired."

Chaewon didn't push.

The shoot was loud and bright and full of fake laughter. Solar played along, letting Eunchae dump flour on her hair during the challenge, pretending not to notice when Yunjin "accidentally" broke her egg yolk.

But during a break, one of the junior producers made an offhand joke.

"Solar, didn't you use to date that guy who showed up in Dispatch with half of Itaewon?"

The smile froze on her face.

"Can't believe you're the responsible one now."

Yunjin's head snapped around so fast it nearly cracked. The air in the room shifted, like all the sugar had dissolved. Eunchae dropped her chopsticks. Even the cameraman looked away. But Solar kept smiling. Her body knew the script even when her soul didn't.

Solar just laughed. Quiet. Controlled.

But when they cut for lunch, she went to the dressing room and sat with her hands in her lap for a long, long time.




That night, Rascal messaged her.

RASCAL: yo. u alive?

SOLAR: barely.

RASCAL: relatable. u wanna scrim or vent or flame super? i'm flexible.

SOLAR: flame super. always.

He sent a laughing emoji, then followed up:

RASCAL: u been quiet.

Solar stared at the screen.

Typed. Deleted. Typed again.

SOLAR: just life. idol stuff. you know.

A beat passed.

RASCAL: u ever want me to come out there... idk. say the word. 

There was something terrifyingly kind about the offer. Like he meant it. Like he'd pack a bag tomorrow if she needed him to. That scared her more than rejection ever could.

Her breath caught.

She started typing: Would you really?

Then deleted it. Too real. Too much.

SOLAR: i'll let you know. :)




Across town, Jungwon lay on his bed, one earbud still in, phone dark on his chest.

He hadn't played the voice note again. He didn't need to.

He remembered every word. Every tremor in her voice. Every breath between the silence.

He didn't know why she called him. But he hadn't stopped thinking about it since. It wasn't just the sound of her voice. It was what it meant. That in a moment of unraveling, when the walls came down, she'd called *him*. Not her best friend. Not her siblings. *Him*. He didn't know what that said about her. But he knew what it said about him.

He kept seeing her—on the monitors at rehearsal, on his feed, in the split second he closed his eyes. Like some thread had been tied between them without his consent.

And now it tugged.

Constantly. He didn't reach out. Didn't want to spook her.

But he watched. He waited.

And when the moment came, he knew he'd move.




The photo showed up the next day. Solar from behind, walking into a rehearsal studio. Blurry, taken from across the street.

Captioned: "ran into an old friend ;)"

No source. No tags. No explanation. But Danielle reposted it to her story with a sunflower emoji. Yunjin saw it first.

"She's getting sloppy," she said through gritted teeth. "Or she wants us to know."

Solar stared at the photo. The angle was wrong. The distance was wrong. The feeling was very, very right.




That night, Solar didn't eat. Didn't shower.

She sat on her bed with the lights off, hoodie sleeves pulled over her hands. The silence roared louder than any crowd. She thought of every version of herself—idol, gamer, sister, shadow. She didn't know which one deserved comfort. Or which one Jungwon had heard on the phone that night.

She thought about Jihoon. About that time he left her on a street corner after a party, mascara bleeding into her lips, phone battery dead. She thought about how she swore she'd never feel that small again.

Then she thought about Jungwon's voice. How steady it sounded, even at 2AM. How he didn't ask for anything. Just waited.

Her phone was in her hand before she noticed.

She didn't call him this time.

But she didn't call anyone else either.






















25: Quiet Things

The hallway was too quiet.

Fluorescent lights buzzed softly overhead, casting long shadows against the polished floor. Solar stood with her back against the green room door, hoodie pulled tight over her chest. Across from her, Jungwon sat on a worn leather bench—arms crossed, ankle resting on his knee. Hood up, jaw tense, eyes lowered.

They'd just finished a closed-door media training session for HYBE artists. A new initiative, they'd said. "Brand reinforcement," whatever that meant. Most of it was reminders about scandals, online etiquette, and keeping private matters private.

Solar hadn't spoken a word the whole session. Neither had Jungwon.

But now they were the last two left. Everyone else had filtered out with polite bows and tired nods. And yet, neither of them had moved. Not really.

He wasn't scrolling. He wasn't fidgeting.

He was just... there.

Their eyes met for the first time in weeks. Neither of them spoke. Neither of them smiled.

The air between them felt thick, like fog before a storm.

He looked different than she remembered—tired, maybe. Leaner, sharper. But still him. Still that quiet kind of steady that made the whole world feel slower around him.
Still the boy who answered her call in the rain without needing a reason.

She folded her arms, matching his posture without meaning to.

"Been a while," he said at last, voice low.

She nodded. "Yeah..."

Another pause. Another breath.

"You okay?" he asked, without looking at her.

Solar hesitated. It was a simple question. Too simple.

"No," she said. "But I'm managing."

Jungwon's mouth twitched, not quite a smile. "You always say that."

"Because it's always true."

He glanced over finally, eyes dark and unreadable. "I saw the photos. The comments."

"I figured."

A longer silence this time. Not awkward. Just full. Heavy.

"Are you mad I didn't say anything?" he asked.

She blinked. "No. I didn't expect you to."

"But you called."

Her breath caught.

"I know."

Jungwon didn't press. He just leaned forward, forearms resting on his knees. "I've been thinking about that night."

"Don't," she said, too fast. Then softer, "It was nothing. Just... a moment."

Jungwon studied her for a long time, his gaze so steady it almost felt cruel. "It didn't feel like nothing."

Solar looked away. The hallway hummed.

"You're not a moment," he said quietly.

She looked back up.

"Neither am I."

The door behind her clicked. Voices echoed down the hall—staff, managers, someone calling for him. Jungwon stood.

"Take care of yourself," he said, stepping past her.

But just as he brushed by, he stopped—close enough that his shoulder nearly grazed hers.

His voice dropped to a whisper.

"If it ever stops feeling like nothing... you know where to find me."

Then he was gone.

And Solar was left staring at the space he'd just filled, the echo of his words pressing against her ribs like a secret.



Later that night, Solar couldn't sleep.

Not from excitement. Not from caffeine. Just from that specific kind of tired that lives in your bones, beneath your ribs, under the skin where silence makes a home.

She curled on her side, her blanket tangled around her legs, her phone glowing soft and blue in her hands.

She shouldn't have gone digging. But she did.

Her fingers tapped through her hidden folders—the ones no manager knew about. No stylists. No Chaerin. Not even Yunjin. They were buried under innocent names: 📷, 🎧, 🌸, 🥡. The kind of folders you name when you're trying to forget what's inside.

Inside 📷 were memories she'd spent years trying to hate.

Photos of late-night bus rides. Vinyl booths at 24-hour diners. His hand—ringed fingers curled around a Bic lighter. Blurry smiles. Neon lights washing everything in red.

Jihoon always looked good in red.

One picture made her freeze.

A car dashboard. Her mom's old Tucson. His boots kicked up, windshield fogged from the heater, a pack of smokes tossed on the dash next to her half-drunk iced Americano. It was nothing, really. No people in the frame. No caption.

But she knew the night.

God, she knew it.

That was the night he left her.

Not in some dramatic, explosive way. No yelling. No threats. He just... vanished.

They were at a house party in Hongdae—packed, loud, smelling like beer and hairspray and cheap cologne. Jihoon got jealous. Over what, she didn't even remember. A guy who asked her about her earrings, maybe. Something stupid. Something harmless.

He called her fake.

Said, "You like being watched, don't you?"

Said, "Go find someone else to baby you."

And then he was gone. Just like that. No message. No goodbye. Just smoke in the air and silence in her phone. She called him five times. Texted once.

"Please don't leave me here."

He read it. Didn't respond.

For two hours, she wandered the streets of Seoul. Her heels cut into her skin, mascara bleeding into the corners of her mouth. Her phone battery died somewhere near the 7-Eleven on Donggyo-ro, so she sat down on the curb and waited.

Not for him. She wasn't that naïve.

She waited for her pulse to settle. For her hands to stop shaking. For the feeling of being disposable to pass.

Some stranger walked by and offered her a ride. She said no. But she thought about it for a full minute.

Because Jihoon wasn't coming back.

And because a part of her believed she deserved it. The abandonment. The humiliation. The numbness in her legs. The ache in her chest.

She never told anyone.

Never told Chaerin, who would've shown up with a baseball bat. Never told Yunjin, who would've hunted Jihoon down and posted his license plate on Twitter. Never told Jaehyun, who might've actually committed a crime.

Because then it would've been real. Then she'd have to admit that it wasn't an accident. That he didn't forget her. He chose to leave her behind.

The next day, she smiled for the camera like nothing happened.

Because what else was she supposed to do?

The screen dimmed in her hand.

Solar blinked hard, chest heavy, throat dry. She locked her phone and flipped it screen-down on the pillow. But the glow of it still burned behind her eyes.

She curled tighter, hoodie sleeves pulled over her hands, and let the tears come slow.

Not loud. Not violent.

Just a slow, steady leak of something she'd buried too deep to name.

You survived him.

But that thought didn't comfort her anymore.

Because sometimes survival meant learning how to carry the worst parts of yourself.
The parts someone else left behind. And tonight, they felt heavier than ever.



Yunjin didn't bother knocking anymore.

She stepped into Solar's room with the confidence of a girl who knew where the spare key was hidden—emotionally, physically, spiritually. A giant sweater hung off her shoulder, her hair in a messy braid, glasses sliding down her nose. Laptop under one arm. A look in her eye that said: we're doing this.

Solar didn't even ask.

"You look like a librarian about to uncover government corruption."

Yunjin dropped the laptop on the bed. "Close. I'm building a conspiracy board."

"Oh god..."

"No pins. Yet."

Solar blinked. "You're serious?"

"Deadly." Yunjin opened the screen, and a spreadsheet the size of Saturn filled the display. Names. Dates. IP address fragments. Screencaps of anonymous forum posts with timestamps circled in red. Danielle's face. Danielle's stylist's alt account. A suspiciously well-edited video that cropped Solar out of a group performance and replaced her with sparkles and Minji fan edits.

"This is..." Solar's voice faded. "How long have you been doing this?"

"Since the first leak."

"You mean the one from two weeks ago?"

Yunjin looked at her. "No. I mean the first one. Like, back when your Shock jersey post got spammed with weird bot comments about how you didn't 'deserve the rank.'"

Solar stared. "You think Danielle's behind all this?"

Yunjin's fingers flew across the keyboard. "No. I know she is. Or at least, someone working overtime in her name."

"Why?"

"Because you're the one thing she can't control," Yunjin said simply. "She can't out dance you. She can't outplay you. And Jungwon..." Her mouth twisted. "Jungwon didn't look at her the way he looked at you. Not once."

Solar flinched. Not because it wasn't true—but because she'd spent days trying not to replay that look in her head. The one in the hallway. The one in the rain. The one she didn't know how to live up to.

"She's playing the long game," Yunjin muttered. "But I'm better at chess."

She pulled up a private message thread. "I messaged someone. A friend of a friend who's a cybersecurity grad student. He's helping me trace some of the metadata behind these burner accounts. If they slip up just once—just once—we can prove it's her."

Solar didn't know what to say. Her throat ached with gratitude she didn't know how to voice.

"I don't want you to fight for me," she whispered.

"I'm not," Yunjin said. "I'm fighting with you. You don't have to be a headline to matter."

And just like that, the room didn't feel so small anymore.




Later that night, Solar sat at her desk, giving up on sleep. It started with a Discord ping.

RASCAL: u up?

Solar smiled for the first time in hours. The clock read 12:47 a.m. Her room was dark except for the faint glow from her monitor. Everyone else in the dorm was asleep, even Yunjin after their detective talk. The silence was deafening. But his message felt like a knock on her window from across the world.

SOLAR: technically
RASCAL: want to queue a few?
RASCAL: just chill games. no strats. no tryhards. just stupid fun.

She didn't even hesitate.

SOLAR: gimme 3 mins

She slid into her chair, adjusted her headset, and booted up the game. A soft jingle. A familiar login screen. For a moment, she forgot everything except the way the keys felt under her fingers.

The call connected.

"Yo," Rascal said, his voice loose with sleep and caffeine.

"Yo," she echoed.

There was no tension. No awkwardness. Just the warm kind of quiet that only comes from someone who doesn't expect you to be okay.

"Okay," Rascal said. "Rules for tonight's comp: one—no yelling. Two—only meme picks. Three—first person to die has to say something embarrassing."

"Are these actual rules or are you just trying to make me admit I had a crush on Zenyatta in 2017?"

"...you WHAT?!"

Solar burst into laughter, the kind that curled her stomach and fogged her glasses. Rascal wheezed on the other end.

"You're sick," he gasped. "You need therapy."

"I had therapy!" she laughed. "He just had a calm voice and floating orbs. What do you want from me?"

They queued into a match. Rascal locked Torbjörn. Solar picked Mei. It was chaos.

They both died instantly.

"Okay," Rascal said, trying to catch his breath. "Your turn. Truth time."

Solar hesitated. Then quietly: "Sometimes I'm scared this whole thing is going to fall apart."

He didn't ask which part. The game. The career. The balance. All of it.

He just said, "Same."

There was a pause. Not heavy. Just honest.

"But also," he added, "you're cracked, you know that?"

She smiled again. "You don't have to say that."

"I'm not saying it to be nice," he said. "I'm saying it because I've played with the best. And you're one of them. Idol or not. You belong."

Her throat tightened. "Thanks."

Rascal didn't push. Just launched a flurry of turret spam and cursed in three languages when he missed a shot.

The match ended in defeat, but neither of them left queue. They played two more.

And when Solar finally logged off, hours later, something about the weight on her chest had lightened.

Not gone. But quieter.

Because sometimes the safest kind of love isn't loud.
It's just being heard when you didn't realize you'd been silent too long.




After Rascal finally went to sleep, Solar was once again alone with her thoughts.

The dorm lights were off, the streets outside still wet from earlier rain, and the only sound was the hum of the fridge down the hall. Solar sat on the balcony floor with her knees drawn to her chest, hoodie sleeves pulled over her hands, a half-eaten banana milk sweating on the tile beside her.

Her phone sat facedown next to it.

She hadn't touched it in over an hour.

She was scared to.

There was something terrifying about the quiet after a storm. How it made you realize what you'd survived—and how much you were still carrying. The past few weeks had been a blur of pressure, performance, eyes on her at every angle. She was expected to dazzle. To dominate. To not mess up.

She didn't know how to exist in silence anymore.

She let her head fall back against the wall. The city lights flickered below. Somewhere out there, fans were still dissecting her expressions. People she didn't know had opinions about her smile, her aim, her posture, her worth. She hadn't asked for that. Not this much of it.

And yet... she wanted all of it, didn't she?

She wanted the stage and the payload.
She wanted her girls and her team.
She wanted to make music, win games, love people—fully, fearlessly, without apologizing for wanting too much.

But tonight, she just wanted to be a person.

No jersey. No lashes. No defense matrix.

Just Solar. The girl with tired eyes and too many secrets. The girl who once waited alone outside a convenience store with a dead phone and a bruised heart. The girl who now had a family of six teammates who joked her back to life. The girl who had Yunjin—her best friend, her detective, her constant. The girl who wasn't sure what Rascal meant to her yet, but knew it mattered.

And... the girl who called Jungwon in the rain.

She still didn't know why she did that.

Didn't know what it meant.

All she knew was that in her moment of breaking, his voice had steadied her like a bridge she didn't know she'd been standing on. Quiet. Strong. Unmoving.

A vibration buzzed through the phone.

A new notification.

MINJI: I saw the new wave of press. You okay?
MINJI: I know things are messy right now, but... you're stronger than they'll ever understand.

Solar stared at the screen, chest tightening.

There was something different about this message.

It wasn't polished. It wasn't distant. It was messy. Human. Minji had always been perfect, composed. But this? This felt like someone reaching out across a battlefield, unsure of which side they stood on anymore.

Solar didn't respond.

Not yet.

Instead, she whispered to the stars: "I don't know what I'm doing."

But somehow, it didn't sound like defeat this time.

It sounded like the start of something honest.
























26: Ghosts in the Room

The text came at 3:08 p.m.

JIHOON:
You look different when you're scared.
Still pretty, though.

Solar's heart dropped.

She was in the HYBE elevator when it came in—alone, headed up to a costume fitting, earbuds in, muted vocals playing. But the second she saw the name, the air in her lungs turned to concrete. Her finger hovered over the lock button. She didn't touch it.

She read the message again.

Her hands were shaking.

When the doors opened, she stepped out with the calm, polished grace of someone trained to smile through disaster. But inside? Her pulse screamed. Her stomach turned. She didn't know how he got her number again. Didn't know how he knew where she'd be.

She didn't tell anyone.

Not yet.

She needed to think.



She sat in her dorm with the lights off. Her computer screen glowed softly in the corner, Discord open, Rascal's name green and online. They had talked earlier—bantered, played a few games, laughed. But it felt different now. Not colder, just... quieter. Like something unspoken hovered in the space between them.

She typed a message. Then deleted it.

Then tried again.

SOLAR:
hey, you good?

Nothing for a while.

Then finally:

RASCAL:
yeah
just tired lol
scrims are rough this week

She stared at the screen.

That was it.

That was all she got.



The next day, Jungwon showed up at the HYBE building.

Not to see her. At least, that's what he told himself. But he didn't leave.

He lingered in the rehearsal hallway, hoodie pulled low, jaw tight. He didn't say a word when he passed Solar in the corridor. Just nodded once—sharply. Like he was checking that she was still breathing.

She paused when she saw him. Something in his expression made her chest tighten.

"What's wrong?" she asked.

He looked at her, unreadable.

Then, softly: "You saw him again, didn't you?"

Solar blinked. "What?"

Jihoon. He meant Jihoon.

She didn't answer. Jungwon didn't press.

"I ran into him," he said finally, voice low like he didn't want the hallway to hear. "He was outside the building."

Her breath caught. "What did you say to him?"

Jungwon's gaze sharpened. "I told him not to come near you again."

Her heart flipped and sank all at once. "And if he doesn't listen?"

Jungwon's jaw flexed. "Then he and I are going to have a very different conversation."

It was dangerous, the way he said it.

Not performative. Not posturing.

Real.

And for the first time in days, Solar didn't feel alone.

But it scared her, too—how easily Jungwon seemed to understand the darkness that haunted her. How much he saw without her having to say anything at all.



That night, Yunjin barged into her room with fire in her eyes and a USB drive clenched in one fist.

"I have her," she hissed.

Solar blinked. "Who?"

Yunjin shoved the laptop open. Pulled up footage. Chat logs. A clipped message trail between Danielle's manager's assistant and a burner account that led back to the same IP as a Seoul-based influencer account that had leaked the rooftop photos weeks ago.

"She's been planting stuff," Yunjin said. "This wasn't just hate comments. It's sabotage. Intentional. Coordinated."

Solar didn't speak.

Because she knew.

She'd felt it. In the way things spiraled every time she got a little happier. A little more visible.

"But now?" Yunjin grinned, dangerous. "We have receipts."



And still, Solar felt like she was unraveling.

At midnight, she curled up in bed and stared at the dark. Tried not to think about Jihoon's voice. Or Rascal's silence. Or the way Jungwon's hand had brushed hers as they passed in the hallway—so light, she wasn't sure it even happened.

She almost called Rascal. Thumb hovered over his name. But something stopped her. Not shame. Not fear.

Just... it didn't feel right. Not tonight.

Then she hovered over Jungwon's. And that didn't feel right either.

Instead, her fingers moved before her brain could stop them. She tapped open a different thread.

SOLAR:
i don't know why i'm texting you.
maybe because you're the only one who saw me when i didn't want to be seen.
maybe because i feel like i'm breaking again.
maybe i just needed someone who wouldn't lie to me.

She hit send. Seconds later, three dots appeared. Then stopped. Then appeared again.

Solar closed her eyes. And finally, Minji's message came through:

MINJI:
then i guess it's a good thing i was already looking.




It was raining again.

Not the romantic kind. The messy, gray kind that turned Seoul into a watercolor blur. Solar had just finished rehearsals and was exiting the building when she saw them.

Three figures. One black car. One argument.

Her heart stopped.

Jihoon stood under a crooked umbrella, soaked collar clinging to his neck, hands stuffed in his pockets like he had nothing to lose. Across from him—Jungwon, all sharp lines and stormy silence, jaw tense like he hadn't blinked in minutes. And just behind them, arms folded, sunglasses on despite the clouds, was Jaehyun.

Her brother.

Her ghost.

Her shield.

She froze under the eaves of the building. Half-hidden behind the glass doors. Watching.

Jihoon smirked, saying something low, casual—too casual. Jungwon took a step forward, his body language all quiet warning. He didn't shout. He didn't flinch. He just stood his ground like a wall.

And Jaehyun? He didn't move at all. He simply tilted his head slightly, unreadable behind the lenses.

Solar couldn't hear what they were saying. But she didn't need to.

She saw it in Jungwon's clenched fists. In Jihoon's lazy shrug. In the way Jaehyun's fingers hovered near the inside pocket of his coat like there was something heavier than words in there.

And then Jihoon looked up. Straight at her.

He smiled. Like he knew she was there the whole time.

Solar stepped back instinctively. Her heart jumped into her throat. Jungwon turned, eyes following Jihoon's gaze—and saw her.

His expression didn't change.

But the next second, he was walking toward her, fast and quiet, his umbrella now forgotten. She met him halfway under the awning.

He didn't touch her. Just stood there, rain slicking his hair back, eyes searching hers like they were both out of breath.

"You okay?" he asked.

She nodded.

Then, quieter: "What were you saying to him?"

Jungwon looked away for a beat. "Nothing that hasn't been coming for a long time."

"Did you—" Her voice caught. "Did you threaten him?"

He didn't answer right away.

Then, finally: "I reminded him what happens when you follow someone home without their consent."

Solar swallowed hard. "What did he say?"

Jungwon's jaw tensed. "Something about how you used to like it."

Her stomach twisted.

"I almost hit him," Jungwon admitted. "Your brother stepped in."

She looked past him. Jaehyun was still there, still unmoving, still terrifying in the quietest way. Jihoon had already slipped away—like a shadow that never really leaves.

"Thank you," she whispered.

Jungwon met her eyes again. "Don't thank me. Just promise me you won't talk to him. No matter what he says."

"I wasn't going to."

"I know." His voice softened. "But he knows where your weak spots are. And people like him don't care how much you've healed. They only care that they can still hurt you."

The rain echoed between them. Solar wanted to cry.

Instead, she said: "Why are you here?"

Jungwon blinked. "What?"

"You didn't have to come. You could've texted. Or let Jaehyun handle it. Why you?"

He hesitated. Something flickered in his expression. Then he said, "Because I couldn't stop thinking about the way your voice broke the night you called me."

Her breath caught.

"And," he added, almost too quietly to hear, "because I don't want to only be someone you call when you're breaking."

Solar didn't speak. Couldn't. Her throat felt like glass. Jungwon stepped back, finally. Gave her space.

"Go home," he said. "Get some rest. I've got your back."

And just like that, he was gone.

A few minutes later, Solar stepped into the van. Her phone buzzed in her pocket. A new message.

JAEHYUN:
he won't come near you again.
i made sure of it.

No emojis. No punctuation. Just fact. She leaned her head against the window and closed her eyes. Exhausted. Safe. Haunted.

All at once.




Solar stared at the blank Discord screen for almost ten minutes.

No new messages.

Rascal had sent her a meme that morning—something stupid about Overwatch patch notes—but she hadn't replied. She was too drained. Too shaken from the Jihoon encounter. Too tangled in the ache of Jungwon's voice, still echoing like a secret she hadn't meant to hear.

But now?

She wanted to talk to him. She needed the safety of him. The way he made things feel light. Easy.

She opened his chat.

SOLAR:
you still awake?

The three dots appeared almost immediately.

RASCAL:
ye
just finished scrims.
wbu?

SOLAR:
long day.
saw someone i didn't want to see.

RASCAL:
damn
u good?

She hesitated. Then typed:

SOLAR:
not really

It sat there for a while. Then:

RASCAL:
want to queue?

Not want to talk. Not want to call. Just... play. Like always.

And Solar realized—maybe this was how he coped. He didn't know how to ask. He just showed up in the only way he knew how.

SOLAR:
actually...
i just kinda wanted to talk tonight. if that's okay.

This time, the dots didn't appear.

Not right away.

Eventually:

RASCAL:
yeah ofc
just
not sure how good i'll be at that lol

She smiled faintly.

SOLAR:
that's okay. i just like hearing you. even if you don't say much.

A pause. Then the call rang.

His voice was scratchy from hours of comms. "Hey."

"Hey," she said back, curling up under her blanket.

There was silence.

But it wasn't bad.

Not until he said, softly, "You're busy lately."

She blinked. "What do you mean?"

"I mean..." He laughed a little, but it was hollow. "You're everywhere. Music shows. Interviews. Headlines. Even when you're not trying to be."

"I didn't ask for that."

"I know. I know." He paused. "It's just... weird. Playing with you, then seeing your face on a billboard right after."

She didn't know what to say.

"I think I forget sometimes," he added, "that you're not... mine."

That last word slipped out like a whisper he didn't mean to say. Solar's heart caught.

"I'm no one's," she said quietly.

"Right," Rascal murmured. "That's what I meant."

Silence again.

Then, as if to pull the tension back down, he chuckled: "You're cracked though. Just so you know. I watched your POV from the Summer Cup again."

"You're such a nerd."

"Yeah, well. Nerds win tournaments."

"Thanks for still playing with me."

He hesitated. Then: "You make the game fun."

That was all.

And yet, when the call ended twenty minutes later, Solar couldn't shake the ache in her chest. Because Rascal wasn't pulling away out of anger. Or ego.

He was pulling away because he thought she deserved more.

And she didn't know how to tell him that he was the only thing lately that didn't feel like work.



It was 2:13 a.m. when it hit her.

The silence.

The real kind—not the peaceful sort she'd learned to love over the years, but the kind that echoed. The kind that made her feel like she wasn't real unless someone was watching.

Solar sat on the floor of her dorm bedroom, knees pulled to her chest, hoodie sleeves swallowed around her hands. Her computer was off. Her phone screen dim. Rascal's name still sat at the top of her recent calls. Jungwon's number hovered like gravity in her contacts.

But she didn't call either of them.

She didn't call Yunjin, or Eunchae, or Chaerin.

She just stared at the floor like it was going to split open and tell her who she was.

Her ears rang. Her skin felt too tight. Her breath was coming in shallow, uneven gasps she didn't notice until her vision began to spot.

She pressed her palms to her face and tried to hold herself together.

But everything felt too loud inside her. Jihoon's voice. The look in Jungwon's eyes. The quiet goodbye she wasn't ready for in Rascal's.

And suddenly she wasn't just sad—she was terrified.

Because she was doing it again.

Slipping into that place. The one she swore she'd never go back to. The one where she convinced herself she was hard to love. Too much. Too complicated. Too public. Too haunted.

She reached for her phone blindly.

Her thumb hovered.

Rascal. Jungwon. Rascal. Jungwon.

Neither felt right.

And then she saw Minji's name.

No heart emoji. No saved photo. Just Minji. Sitting there like she'd been waiting in the wings this whole time.

Solar didn't know what possessed her.

Maybe it was the fact that Minji had messaged her that day. Something soft. Something fragile. One of those late-night whispers you only send when you mean it.

MINJI:
I didn't reach out because I thought I could help. I reached out because I didn't want to feel alone watching you go through this. I don't know what that means, but I'm here.

Solar hadn't replied.

Until now.

Her fingers moved on instinct.

SOLAR:
are you awake

Three dots.

Then:

MINJI:
yeah
you okay?

Solar's throat tightened. She didn't answer.

Then her screen lit up.

Incoming call.

Solar stared at it. Let it ring once. Twice.

Then she answered.

There was silence at first.

Just soft breathing on the other end.

Then Minji's voice: "Talk to me."

Solar opened her mouth—and the tears came first.

Not quiet ones. Not polite.

Real ones. Gasping, heaving, unstoppable.

She pressed the phone to her cheek like it was the only thing anchoring her.

"I don't know what's wrong with me," she whispered. "I'm supposed to be happy. Everything's good. I should be good. But I feel like I'm breaking and I don't know how to stop."

Minji didn't rush to fix it. Didn't tell her to breathe or calm down or be grateful.

She just said, very quietly:

"You don't have to be good all the time."

Solar sobbed harder.

"You don't have to be strong every second," Minji added. "You're allowed to want someone to hold it with you. Even if you don't know who that person is yet."

Solar didn't respond. Couldn't. But something in her broke open.

And for the first time in weeks, she felt... seen.

Not as an idol.

Not as a gamer.

Not as a girl someone used to love.

Just as a person.

On the other end of the line, Minji didn't ask for anything.

She just stayed.















27: Between the Words

The sunlight hit her face like a betrayal.

Solar groaned and turned her head into the pillow, but it was too late—her mind was already awake, already racing. Her eyes burned like she hadn't slept, which was technically true. The hours had passed, sure, but sleep had never arrived. Just the sound of Minji's voice echoing in her chest, and the heaviness that came after releasing too much too fast.

She sat up slowly, legs heavy beneath the blanket. Her phone buzzed, but she didn't look at it. If it was Rascal, she wasn't ready. If it was Minji, she really wasn't ready.

The dorm was quiet except for the distant hum of a blow dryer and the soft thuds of footsteps padding across the living room carpet. Solar pulled a hoodie over her head and stumbled toward the kitchen in search of coffee—or, failing that, a reason to keep moving.

Eunchae was already there, crouched in front of the pantry in an oversized sweatshirt and fuzzy slippers, humming off-key and dancing like she was on a live stream. A spoon hung from her mouth.

When she turned and saw Solar, she froze.

"...Whoa," she said, pulling the spoon out and pointing it like a weapon. "You look like a haunted doll."

Solar blinked. "Good morning to you too."

Eunchae grinned, unbothered. "Did you sleep in a thunderstorm? You've got, like, emotional fog rolling off you."

"Shut up." But Solar's lips twitched.

Chaewon was at the stove, stirring something that smelled faintly like red bean porridge. She glanced over her shoulder with a knowing look.

"Did you sleep?" she asked.

Solar nodded, too quickly.

Chaewon arched a brow, unimpressed.

"I had weird dreams," Solar offered as a lazy save.

"Was one of them a nightmare where you drowned in your own denial?" Eunchae asked, hopping up onto the counter.

Solar opened the fridge and let the cold air bite her face. "Can we not turn my morning into a group intervention?"

Eunchae held up her hands like she was innocent. "Just saying. You've got 'spiraling main character' written all over you today."

Solar reached for the iced coffee, unscrewed the cap, and took a long sip. Behind her, Chaewon's voice softened. "If you need to talk, we're here."

Solar didn't turn around. "I'm fine."

A lie she'd learned to make sound like truth.

The moment hung in the air a second too long, then broke as Yunjin walked in with her guitar case slung over her back. She paused in the doorway, taking one look at Solar, and offered a half-smile.

"Morning," she said gently.

Solar nodded, but didn't meet her eyes. They didn't need to talk.

Not yet. But Yunjin would wait.

She always did.



The HYBE building always felt too clean.

Too polished. Too bright. Like even the silence had been sanitized.

Solar stood in the elevator, arms folded, watching the numbers tick upward. Floor 8. Floor 9. Floor 10. She hadn't reapplied her lip gloss. She hadn't said more than five words since leaving the dorm.

It wasn't until the doors slid open that she saw him.

Jungwon.

Standing against the opposite wall like he'd been waiting for someone. Or maybe nothing. Maybe he was just there—cool and composed in a dark hoodie, mask covering half his face, hair damp like he'd just finished practice. He wasn't looking at her.

Until he was.

And when he saw her, something shifted.

Not a smile. Not surprise. Just... recognition.

Solar's steps slowed as she passed him. They didn't speak until they were side by side, heading down the hall.

"You didn't sleep," Jungwon said, low and even.

She didn't answer. He didn't expect her to.

After a beat, she muttered, "Didn't feel like it."

He didn't look at her. Just kept walking.

"You don't have to lie to everyone."

Solar blinked at the floor. Her throat tightened.

"I'm not—"

"You're tired," he said, almost gently. "That's not weakness."

They reached the hallway where their paths split—hers to the stylist's lounge, his toward the rehearsal wing. She turned slightly, expecting him to keep walking.

But he stopped. Looked at her, eyes sharp beneath the mask.

"You should rest, Lee Yuna."

Her name landed like a thunderclap in the silence. She hadn't heard it from him since the rain.

He started to walk away. But then paused. Without turning, he added, voice even softer: "Next time... don't disappear."

And then he was gone.

Solar stood in the middle of the hallway, heartbeat loud in her ears. And for just a second, the building didn't feel so clean anymore.

It felt real. Like something raw was about to break through.




The Shock server lit up before she even put her headset on.

[smurf joined the channel]
[glister joined the channel]
[rascal joined the channel]
[super joined the channel]
[nero joined the channel]

Solar sank into her chair and tugged her mic down with a slow breath. Her hand hovered over the Discord icon for a moment longer than it should've. Just a second of hesitation. She shook it off.

[solar joined the channel]

"Look who finally decided to show up," Super greeted, his voice bright and obnoxiously chipper. "Was the idol throne too comfy today?"

"I was wrangling my backup dancers," Solar deadpanned.

"Ohhhh," Nero drawled. "She's still got bite."

"Careful," Glister chimed in. "You'll provoke the OTP again."

Solar blinked. "The what?"

Super cackled. "Bro, they're already calling you and Rascal 'The Unbreakable Duo' in Discord. I saw a fan edit this morning. It was uncomfortably romantic."

"No one asked you to save it to your phone," Nero muttered.

"I had to for research purposes!"

Solar waited for Rascal's laugh to follow. But it didn't.

Instead, his voice came through—level, neutral. "Can we run the Havana dive comp? Solar, you take Tracer. I'll flex Echo."

That was it. No teasing. No "let's go, cracked queen" or sarcastic "don't throw."

Just a callout. Solar nodded, even though he couldn't see her. "Yeah. Got it."

They queued in silence.

The match was fine. They won. Her Pulse Bombs were clean. His dupe timing was perfect. But something was missing. Like they were playing side by side but a screen still stood between them.

At one point, Nero mumbled, "Damn. Y'all got divorced or something?"

Super howled. "Tragic. I'll miss the power couple arc."

Rascal didn't respond. And neither did Solar.

That silence spoke louder than either of them could. They played two more games. No banter. No DMs. Just clipped strategy calls and muted sighs.

When she finally logged off, the ache wasn't about losing a game. It was about losing something that felt too close to real.

And not knowing how to ask for it back.




It was late when Solar stepped out of the shower—hair damp, skin flushed from heat, oversized hoodie clinging to her like armor. The dorm lights were dimmed, and most of the girls were already asleep. The hallway smelled faintly of lavender detergent and strawberry sheet masks.

She was halfway to her room when she heard it.

"Hey," Yunjin's voice came from the living room, soft but sharp. "You've been weird."

Solar blinked, caught in the half-light. "Wow. No warm-up or anything?"

Yunjin sat on the couch, guitar balanced across her lap. She wasn't playing. Just holding it. Fingers resting on the strings like they were an excuse to be still.

"I'm not trying to corner you," she said. "Just talk."

Solar sighed, walking over and dropping beside her with a quiet thud. The couch dipped under her weight.

Yunjin didn't look at her.

"I heard you crying the other night," she said quietly. "And I didn't say anything. Because I figured if you wanted to talk, you would."

Solar looked down at her knees.

"I'm fine."

"You're lying."

"I'm managing," Solar amended.

There was a pause. Then Yunjin turned her head, eyes soft but full of steel. "Is this about Jihoon?"

Solar's breath caught. The name settled between them like a dropped match.

"I saw his name on your phone last week," Yunjin continued. "Didn't snoop. Just saw it pop up. And I remembered the way you used to flinch when he texted you."

"I didn't open it," Solar whispered. "I just... I don't know why I reached out."

Yunjin set her guitar down. Carefully. Like she didn't want to startle the moment. "You don't have to justify it to me," she said. "I just need to know you're safe."

"I am."

Another pause.

"Ish."

Yunjin offered a small, tired smile. "That's better than a lie."

They sat in silence for a moment, the kind that only best friends can survive. Then Yunjin's expression shifted. Subtle. Tense.

"One more thing."

Solar glanced over.

"I saw Danielle," Yunjin said. "Again."

"What?"

"Outside the building. Yesterday. She wasn't doing anything. Just standing near the loading dock. Wearing a cap. Sunglasses."

Solar's stomach twisted. "Are you sure it was her?"

Yunjin nodded. "I got close enough to see. She didn't see me, though."

The air in the room changed. Heavier. Slower.

"She's not stalking me," Solar said, but it sounded weak.

"She might not be yet. But something about it felt... off. Like she was waiting for something."

Solar pressed her palms into her eyes. "Why is everything suddenly falling apart?"

Yunjin leaned over, gently bumped her shoulder. "It's not. You're just seeing more clearly now."

The words didn't fix anything. But they didn't need to. They just needed to be true.




The next day, it was time for yet another performance... almost. The back hallway of the music program was narrow, white, and too quiet—just the faint buzz of LED lights and the soft thud of bass leaking through soundproof doors.

Solar wasn't even supposed to be in this wing. Her stylist had run back for a pair of backup heels, and she was just wandering, needing space to breathe without being "on."

Then she turned a corner and saw her.

Minji.

Standing in front of a vending machine like she was thinking about making a decision but not really seeing anything in front of her. Her stage makeup was flawless. Her expression wasn't.

She looked... tired.

Not in the physical sense.

In the way that meant something inside her was carrying too much.

Solar froze mid-step. Minji looked up—and their eyes met like flint against stone.

Neither of them said anything at first.

The silence held. Minji didn't smile. She didn't run. She just... stayed.

"I wasn't expecting to see you," Solar said, her voice quieter than she meant it to be.

Minji blinked, slow and unreadable. "Me neither."

A beat passed. Then another.

"I got your message," Solar said softly. "From the other night."

Minji nodded, looking down at the machine again. "I wasn't really expecting you to reply," she murmured. "I just... I don't know."

Solar stepped closer. Not too close. Just close enough for it to feel like a choice.

"You okay?" she asked.

Minji finally looked at her. Her eyes didn't sparkle like they usually did. No glamor. No gloss. Just tired honesty.

"You looked like you needed someone," she said.

That was it. No metaphors. No confessions. Just that. And it cracked something open inside Solar that she didn't know was still fragile.

"I did," she whispered. "I guess I still do."

Minji didn't say anything. But she didn't leave either.

They stood there a moment longer, in the sterile hallway filled with nothing but their breath and too many words they weren't brave enough to say.

And then, slowly, Minji reached into the vending machine, pressed a button, and when the drink clattered down, she picked it up and handed it to Solar without looking.

Peach soda.

The same kind Solar had once mentioned liking—offhandedly, during a group shoot months ago.

Solar stared at it. Minji shrugged. "You looked like you needed something sweet."

Then she walked away. And Solar stood there, holding the can, unsure if she wanted to laugh or cry.

Maybe both.




That night, Solar didn't log in to play.

She didn't even open her launcher.

She just stared at the Discord server icon glowing softly in the corner of her screen—like it was waiting for her. Like it knew something had shifted and wanted to remind her that the door was still there. If she wanted it.

Her mouse hovered over it.

Then clicked.

The Shock server loaded—quiet tonight. Only a few online. Super was livestreaming something, probably yelling about patch notes. Nero was idle. Rascal's status was green. Active.

Her heart skipped.

They hadn't spoken—really spoken—since the tournament. Since that sweet, stupid goodbye in the hotel room that tasted like rain and things unsaid.

She clicked on his name.

The chat history loaded. Still filled with half-finished jokes, scrim notes, dumb memes.

And at the bottom—

A new message.

RASCAL:
hey
we good?

Just that.

Two words.

And yet her throat tightened like he'd shouted.

She stared at the blinking cursor for too long. Typed.

yeah
just tired

Deleted it.

Typed again.

all good
sorry i've been distant

Deleted that too.

She sighed, leaned her forehead against the edge of her desk, and finally typed:

SOLAR:
yeah
just tired

She hit send.

Immediately, a reply.

RASCAL:
ok
get some rest

She stared at those four words for a long, long time.

They weren't cold.

They weren't angry.

But they weren't close either.

They were a gentle letting go. A quiet step back.

He wasn't mad.

He was just... protecting himself.

And maybe, somewhere deep down, Solar understood.

She closed the chat and leaned back in her chair, eyes burning, heart louder than it had any right to be.

Sometimes, the silence between two people said more than words ever could.

And in that silence, Solar felt the weight of everything she hadn't said.

Not yet.

Not to him.

Not to anyone.





28: Falling Into Focus

The mirrors in the rehearsal room were too bright.

Every turn of Solar's body caught in angles she didn't recognize. Movements that should've been muscle memory felt stiff, clumsy. The music played like it was underwater, and she was always half a beat too slow.

"Five, six, seven, hit—Solar, again."

Chaewon's voice was calm, but her eyes flicked up with concern.

Solar blinked.

Nodded.

Tried again.

Her knees bent, foot dragged, arms snapped upward—and missed. Again. Her ankle twisted wrong. Not bad. Not enough to fall. But it pulled, and her breath caught.

"That's it for now," their choreographer finally sighed, pinching the bridge of her nose. "Ten-minute break."

Solar didn't move. Just stood there, hands on her hips, heartbeat thudding in her ears.

"Yuna."

Chaewon's voice again. Solar looked up.

The Le Sserafim leader crossed the room slowly, towel draped over her shoulder. She offered it without a word, her eyes warm and tired.

Solar took it and sank to the floor, back against the mirror.

"You're not sleeping, are you?" Chaewon asked quietly.

Solar didn't answer. Not right away.

Then: "I'm just... adjusting."

Chaewon knelt down beside her, folding one leg under. "You don't have to pretend with us. Not all the time."

The words hit harder than she meant them to.

"I'm not pretending."

"You are," Chaewon said gently. "But that's okay. We all do, sometimes."

Solar closed her eyes. Let the towel rest against her forehead.

"I feel like I'm failing everything at once."

"You're not."

"It feels like it."

Chaewon didn't correct her this time. Just stayed close. Just stayed.

That felt like enough.

When they returned to the dorm, the lights were off in the main room. Eunchae was curled up on the floor in a sea of snack wrappers and half-folded blankets, muttering something about a dream involving a dog with Yunjin's face.

Solar gave a weak smile and slipped past her, heading to her room.

She dropped her bag on the floor, kicked off her shoes, and lay on the bed without turning on the light.

Everything was spinning too slowly.

She opened her phone. Notifications blurred past. Mentions. Articles. Edits of her in the Shock jersey again, this time with a fan caption:

Does she ever rest?

She locked the screen.

"No," she whispered to herself. "I don't."




The next morning came fast and sharp.

Solar was halfway through her second espresso when a staff coordinator pulled her aside with a tight smile.

"Something came for you," they said, handing over a thin manila envelope. No sender. No label.

"Fan mail?" Solar asked.

The staff shrugged. "Found it in the downstairs locker mail drop. Thought it might've been misfiled."

Solar took it without thinking, shoved it in her tote, and went about her day.

It wasn't until hours later—alone in the makeup room between segments, a rare pocket of stillness—that she remembered it.

She pulled it from her bag.

The envelope was unsealed. Inside: one photo. A print. Slightly bent. Old. She knew the moment she saw the edges.

The grainy image showed a streetlight. A parked car. And her, leaning against Jihoon's shoulder—laughing, eyes squinted, drunk on summer and teenage recklessness.

She was sixteen. He was seventeen.

Her hair was darker then. Shorter. She wore one of Chaerin's jackets and the same necklace she'd thrown in the Han River three years ago.

The back of the photo had something written in thick, black pen.

"Funny how nothing's changed."

Her fingers went numb. She stared at the words until they started to blur. Jihoon didn't need to say anything else.

This was the message.

He was watching. He remembered. And worst of all— He still believed she belonged to him.

She shoved the photo back into the envelope, stuffed it at the bottom of her bag, and zipped it shut.

She didn't tell Yunjin. Didn't tell anyone. Because saying it out loud would make it real.

And Solar wasn't ready to be haunted again.




Solar logged into Discord with a pit in her stomach.

The Shock server was already buzzing. Scrim day. Everyone was early. Nero was complaining about patch nerfs in #strats.

Super was streaming himself chugging a protein shake with "dominance" written on his forehead in Sharpie. Glister had already shared four memes before 10 a.m.

And Rascal was there, too. Online. Green dot. Status: "chillin."

But not a single message to her. Not like before.

No hey. No ping. No "u ready, duo?" Just silence. She joined the voice channel.

"Solar, finally!" Super exclaimed. "We were about to start betting on what time you'd show."

"Only took you two years," Nero added with a smirk.

Solar tried to laugh. "I bring the fashionably late chaos. You're welcome."

They chuckled. Even Rascal chuckled.

But when she said, "Hey, Rascal," he only responded with:

"Yo."

That was it. Just yo. No teasing. No soft sarcasm. No "solar my carry queen".

It felt like walking into your favorite room and finding all the pictures missing from the walls.

In-game, their synergy was still there. Rascal peeled for her instinctively. Solar covered his flanks without thinking. Their comms were clean. But the warmth?

Gone.

After scrims, Solar hesitated in the team voice chat. Just her and Rascal, lingering. She finally said it.

"I miss when you called me cracked."

A pause.

Then Rascal exhaled a laugh. "I still think it," he said. "Just figured you were... busy."

She swallowed. "I'm not that busy."

"You're busy being brilliant," he said, light, like a joke. But it sounded like goodbye. And before she could say anything else, he disconnected.

Just—

Left.

Solar stared at the empty call box for a long, long time.

The screen felt colder than usual.




It was raining again.

Not hard. Just that soft, misty kind that clung to your sleeves and hair, too light to justify an umbrella but too heavy to ignore.

Solar pulled her hoodie tighter over her head and took the side exit out of the HYBE building. Practice had run late. Everyone was scattered. She just wanted a moment to breathe.

The alley beside the building was empty—quiet enough to hear her own thoughts, which was both a blessing and a curse.

She was halfway down the block when she noticed him. Leaning against the low wall across from the entrance, hands in his pockets, hood pulled low—

Jungwon.

He looked up as if he'd felt her presence before seeing her. Solar slowed.

"Waiting for someone?" she asked, voice low.

He didn't smile, but something in his eyes flickered—recognition, maybe. Or something quieter. "Nope," he said. "Just needed air."

She nodded and looked up at the cloudy sky. "Same."

For a moment, they just stood there. Rain touching both of them. Breath fogging in the chill. "Busy week?" he asked finally.

Solar gave a small laugh. "That's one way to put it."

Jungwon tilted his head slightly. "It doesn't stop, does it? The pressure."

She looked over at him. He wasn't speaking like someone being polite. He was speaking like someone who knew.

"No," she said. "But you get stronger."

He raised a brow. "Or colder."

Solar's heart tensed. She knew exactly what he meant. She looked away. "I don't want to be cold."

"Then don't be," Jungwon said simply. "Let the world burn around you. Just don't let it freeze you."

She turned back toward him, blinking. "Is that your strategy?"

He gave a half-shrug. "Depends on the day."

There was a beat of silence. Then he said, almost too quietly. "You looked tired today."

Solar blinked again. "You saw me?"

"I see a lot," Jungwon replied.

It wasn't flirtatious. It wasn't gentle. It was just true. She felt it in her chest—how he saw her. Not the stage version. Not the girl on camera. Just her.

She nodded slowly. "You always this observant?"

He looked at her. And this time, he did smile. "Only when it matters."

The rain picked up. He pulled his hood tighter.

"Walk safe," he murmured, already turning.

"You too," she called after him.

But he didn't look back. He didn't have to.

And she didn't know why—

—but her heart ached anyway.



The dorm was still when Solar returned.

Everyone else was asleep, or at least pretending to be. Even the city outside had softened, the usual buzz of Seoul replaced with the hush of late hours and wet pavement.

She dropped her bag by the door and walked barefoot to the bathroom. The light overhead flickered to life. She didn't look at the mirror right away.

She just stood at the sink, fingers curled around the cool edge of the porcelain, breathing.

In. Out. In again. Slow. Hollow.

Then finally, she looked up.

And there she was.

Pale. Exhausted. Eyes ringed with shadows that concealer couldn't hide. Hair frizzed from the rain. A Shock scrim hoodie over her Le Sserafim tee. Mascara smudged just beneath one eye like a bruise.

She didn't look like an idol. She didn't look like an athlete. She looked like a girl who didn't know who she was anymore.

Her throat tightened. "Who even are you?" she whispered to her reflection.

The girl didn't answer. Just stared back, eyes too wide. Too tired.

Solar reached up and slowly peeled off the hoodie. It fell to the tile with a soft thump. Then she peeled off the lashes. The eyeliner. The gloss.

Wiped the last of the highlighter from her cheekbones.

Layer by layer. Until it was just her.

Yuna Lee.

No stage name. No top 500 player tag.

Just a girl who used to dance in her bedroom when no one was watching. Who used to dream about saving her sister from the edge. Who used to think love had to hurt to be real.

Her hand reached for the counter.

The Shock jersey was there, folded neatly. She hadn't worn it since the tournament.

She held it now like it was something sacred. Not to put it on. Just to remember why it mattered.

Why she mattered.

"You're still in here," she whispered. "Somewhere." The girl in the mirror blinked back tears.

But didn't cry. Not yet.

Solar ran a hand over the fabric—familiar, soft, heavy with everything it meant. She didn't know what tomorrow would bring.

Or the next scandal. Or the next loss.

But she knew she wasn't giving this up. Not the game. Not the music. Not herself. She wouldn't become a ghost again. She couldn't.

She sat down on the cold bathroom floor, jersey in her lap, and finally let her head fall into her hands.

Just for a minute. Just to feel it.

Not collapse. Not surrender.

Just the honesty of being tired.

And when she stood again—wiped her eyes, folded the jersey, and turned off the light—there was still pain.

But there was also clarity.















29: Smoke & Spark

The HYBE building felt unusually empty.

Solar hadn't meant to stay this late—just a quick stop to drop off paperwork for upcoming promotions, confirm rehearsal slots, and maybe, if she was lucky, avoid the nagging anxiety that clung to her like static. She was tired. Her limbs ached from dance practice, her head buzzed from a full morning scrim. She just wanted to breathe.

The elevator doors slid open.

And she saw him.

Jihoon.

Leaning casually against the wall near the main exit, dressed in all black like he thought he was in a movie. Black cap pulled low, hands in the pockets of a designer jacket, chewing gum like he had all the time in the world.

For a moment, she thought her brain was glitching. That her stress had conjured him. A hallucination.

But then he smiled.

Slow. Familiar. Like poison wrapped in velvet.

"What a coincidence," he hums, voice low and lazy. "It's the talk of the Internet right now, the Solar." His words were blatantly sarcastic, dripping of the slightest taunt that made her wince.

Solar stopped walking. The hallway behind her suddenly felt miles long.

She blinked, trying to piece together something—anything—to say that didn't sound like a scream.

Jihoon tilted his head, like he was studying her. "You look good. Exhausted, but good. Idol life suits you, huh?"

Her hands curled at her sides.

"What is it this time, Jihoon?" she huffed, voice sharp, brittle.

He shrugged, pushing off the wall with a casual grace that made her stomach twist. "Nothing. Just came by to talk business. A friend of a friend's starting a label. Thought I'd say hi while I was in the area."

Liar.

She could hear it in the way he said it. That offhand cruelty, always dressed up as charm. Jihoon didn't come here for business. He came for her. To haunt her. To remind her that he still existed. That no matter how far she ran, he was always close enough to touch the hem of her new life.

She turned to walk past him.

But he stepped in front of her. Not blocking her—just standing close enough that she had to look up.

"I saw that match, by the way," he murmured. "Your little esports thing. You're good. Real good."

Her pulse kicked up.

Jihoon smiled again, smaller this time. Crueler. "And the guy you're playing with? The one with the accent and the weak wrists?"

"Don't."

He leaned in. His breath smelled like mint and arrogance.

"Just saying. You always did like broken things."

Her heart was pounding now. Not fear—rage. A slow boil. The same kind she used to feel when they fought and he'd laugh through it like it was foreplay.

"You don't get to show up and talk to me like this," she snapped, stepping back.

Jihoon's eyes flickered. Something behind them turned colder.

"I'm just saying hi."

"No," she corrects, her voice rising. "You're reminding me that you can. That you still think you can get in my head."

He looked at her for a long moment, that smile finally fading.

Then, just before he turned to leave, he murmured, "You texted me, remember?"
