The words hit her like a slap.

He walked away without waiting for a response.

Solar stood frozen in the hallway long after he disappeared, trembling from the weight of it.

Yes. She had texted him.

Just once. That night in the hotel, when her heart had been breaking and the world felt too loud. She hadn't said anything. Just typed his name. Hit send. Regretted it before the message delivered.

But he had seen it.

And now he was here.

Like she'd summoned him.




The quiet of the HYBE lobby had returned.

But Solar hadn't moved.

She stood in the same spot, fists clenched, chest heaving in short, shallow breaths. Her skin prickled like it was too tight, too exposed. Jihoon's words looped in her head on a jagged reel: You always did like broken things... You texted me, remember?

She wanted to scream. Or run. Or dissolve entirely.

Instead, she blinked fast and shoved past the rising nausea in her throat, forcing herself toward the building's exit.

The doors slid open—and she nearly collided with someone stepping in.

Jungwon.

Black hoodie, hood up. Head down. He looked like he'd meant to pass unnoticed—until he saw her.

His gaze sharpened instantly.

"Solar?" His voice wasn't soft. It was alert. Focused.

She froze.

She didn't want him to see her like this.

Didn't want him to see the faint tremble in her fingers, or the crack in her mascara where she'd blinked too hard to keep from crying.

But he already had.

He stepped closer, subtly scanning her face. "What happened?"

She shook her head, trying to move past him. "Nothing. I'm fine."

He didn't let her pass.

"Jihoon was here, wasn't he." It wasn't a question.

She flinched. That was all the answer he needed.

Jungwon's jaw tightened, just slightly. "What did he say to you?"

"Nothing," she lied again.

He scoffed—quiet, disbelieving. "You're shaking."

"I'm fine." But her voice cracked on the last word.

Jungwon stared at her for a long, tense moment. Then—so slowly she barely noticed—he raised one hand, as if to touch her shoulder. But he stopped just short.

"Why do you keep doing that?" he asked, his voice lower now. Not angry—just... tired. "Pretending everything's okay when it's not."

"Because I don't need another person trying to fix me," she whispered.

His gaze darkened. "I'm not trying to fix you. I'm trying to stop people from breaking you more."

The words landed with a thud. They stood there in the quiet, the lobby lights humming overhead, the weight of too many things unsaid pressing down on both of them.

Finally, she looked up.

"I don't even know you that well," she said, barely more than a breath.

But the words didn't push him away. If anything, Jungwon took a half-step closer.

"Then get to know me," he advises. "Because I'm not going anywhere."

It should've felt comforting. It didn't. It felt dangerous.

Because the way he said it—the quiet certainty of it—stirred something in her chest she wasn't ready to name.



Yunjin wasn't trying to be nosy.

She was just really, really good at pattern recognition. And something about Danielle had never sat right.

It started with the rain.

The night of the showcase, when Solar had come home shaking and silent. When blurry, too-perfect photos hit the internet within hours—of her and Jungwon. Of his arm around her. Of her lips parted like she'd been crying. The captions were vicious, the comments worse. HYBE's PR team moved fast, but the fire had already spread.

And who had posted the first set?

A fan account—unverified. But the metadata? Yunjin had pulled it before it got taken down.

Geotagged.

To a rooftop across the street from the venue. That account didn't post again.

Yunjin stared at the screenshot now, her laptop propped open beside her guitar case. In the quiet of the Le Sserafim dorm, with the others out rehearsing, she clicked through files like she was tuning chords.

The timestamps were too perfect. The angle was too clean.

And the watermark—barely visible—matched another account. One she'd seen before. Danielle had been tagged in it last fall, on a post she'd commented on with a little heart emoji. Most people wouldn't have noticed.

Yunjin noticed everything. Her phone buzzed.

SOLAR: u alive?

YUNJIN: i'm busy.
YUNJIN: also i might be uncovering a conspiracy. again.
SOLAR: i'm scared
YUNJIN: you should be

She smirked.

But the unease in her gut didn't lift. Because if her hunch was right—and it always was—this wasn't just petty drama. It was sabotage. The kind that looked like coincidence until it unraveled everything.

Danielle had access. She had motive. And she had the kind of honeyed reputation that made people hesitate to question her.

Yunjin didn't hesitate.

She opened a folder labeled "Danielle 🍯🐍" and added three new screenshots to it.

Later, she'd tell Solar. Not yet. Not until she was sure.

Because if Danielle really was behind this... Yunjin wouldn't just expose her. She'd ruin her.




It was supposed to be a quick walk.

Just a loop around the building after rehearsal, long enough to clear Solar's head. But Seoul nights had a way of swallowing time. The streets hummed with neon, her hoodie pulled low, and for once—no one recognized her.

She turned a corner near the back lot of the HYBE building, half-lost in thought—

And nearly collided with someone walking out the side entrance.

Minji.

Her hair was damp, curled slightly at the ends like she'd just come from vocal practice. No makeup. Just sweatpants and an oversized sweatshirt with "NEW JEANS" stitched small across the front.

Both girls froze.

For a moment, neither of them said anything. The hallway lights above them flickered once, then steadied.

Minji's eyes searched her face. "You okay?"

Solar hesitated. Then nodded, barely. "Yeah. Just... tired."

"Long day?"

"Long month."

Minji exhaled a quiet breath that could've been a laugh or a sigh. "Same."

There was an awkward pause—one of those sticky silences that tugged at something unspoken.

Then, softly, Minji asked, "Can I ask you something?"

Solar blinked. "Sure."

Minji looked away, then back. "How do you do it?"

"Do what?"

"This," she murmured, gesturing vaguely toward the sky. "The music. The spotlight. All of it. And still manage to feel like... you?"

Solar didn't answer right away.

Instead, she leaned against the railing, staring out at the dark horizon. "I don't always manage it," she admitted. "Sometimes I fake it. Sometimes I break. Sometimes I lose myself for a little while. But then I remember the things that feel real. Like the girls. My team. The way it feels when I hit a clean Pulse Bomb or the silence right before a chorus drop. That's what I hold onto."

Minji nodded slowly. Her eyes shimmered—not teary, but thoughtful. Like something about the words sank deeper than she meant to let them.

"You always sound like you've figured it out," she whispered. "And I hate how much I envy that."

Solar turned to her, a small frown forming. "Minji..."

But Minji cut her off gently. "I'm okay. Just... don't stop being real. Even when people try to cut that out of you."

She didn't wait for a response. Just gave a faint, half-embarrassed smile and turned to leave. But halfway down the hall, she paused. Glanced back.

"By the way," she said quietly, "you were incredible in that tournament."

Solar's heart flickered in her chest.

"Thanks," she murmured.

Minji smiled again—barely there, but warm. Then disappeared through the stairwell door without another word.




The dorm was dark when she got home.

No glowing monitor, no Yunjin humming from the other room. Just stillness. The kind that pressed against your skin and made every step feel too loud.

Solar kicked off her shoes and sank into the couch.

Her phone buzzed.

RASCAL: you ok?
RASCAL: saw the tag trending. it's bs.

She stared at the screen.
Typed. Deleted.
Typed again: yeah, i'm fine.
Then deleted that too.

She dropped the phone onto the table like it burned.

Her hands were still trembling.

You texted me, remember?
You always did like broken things.

Jihoon's voice lived in her skull like mold—clinging, quiet, impossible to scrub out.

He had this way of making her feel like she was back there. Sixteen. Barely standing. Not yet an idol, not yet a name. Just a girl too lost to know how far she'd already fallen.

She curled in on herself, arms tucked around her knees. A sound caught in her throat—a laugh or a sob, she couldn't tell.

Everyone said they were there for her.

Yunjin. Rascal. Even Jungwon, in that strange, unreadable way of his.

But some part of her never believed it.

She'd learned early that people leave. That they say I'm here until it gets inconvenient. Until the cracks show. Until they realize she's not as soft or lovely as she pretends to be.

So she smiled. She performed. She carried it.

Even now. Her breathing hitched. No one was around to see her break, so she let herself do it.

Just a little.

A single tear slipped down her cheek. She wiped it away quickly, angrily, like it betrayed her.

You're stronger than this.
You're not sixteen anymore.

But tonight, her armor didn't feel like steel. It felt like paper. The kind that tears when you hold it too tight.




















30: Armor & Echoes

The mirrored walls gleamed with late-afternoon light, blurred by sweat smudges and the occasional fingerprints from chaotic water breaks.

Solar's limbs screamed. Her shirt clung to her spine, soaked and heavy. Her heart still thundered from the last run-through of ANTIFRAGILE, but she forced herself to hold the final pose—head thrown back, arms sharp and defiant—until the music faded to silence.

Only then did she stumble back a step, breathless.

Chaewon collapsed first, rolling onto her back with a theatrical groan. "I'm filing a complaint," she wheezed. "This choreo should come with hazard pay and chiropractic coverage."

"You say that after every comeback," Eunchae giggled, flopping down beside her. "You're just getting old."

"I'm twenty-four," Chaewon muttered.

"Exactly." Eunchae gave her a dramatic look of faux pity. "Ancient."

Across the room, Kazuha moved like she wasn't tired at all—her arms stretching over her head in fluid arcs, calves flexed, neck rolled with dancer precision.

Solar admired her silently. Kazuha had this rare discipline, a peace to her presence that was nothing like the constant noise in Solar's head.

Yunjin flopped down next to Solar with a groan, dragging a towel over her neck. "You okay?"

Solar didn't answer right away. She sat cross-legged, elbows on knees, breathing through her nose, trying not to let the pounding in her chest feel like panic.

"I'm fine."

"You always say that." Yunjin offered her a water bottle, then leaned in and murmured, "But I saw you during the second chorus. You blanked out."

"I didn't."

"You did."

Solar took the water, twisting the cap with damp fingers. "I'm just tired."

"Yeah," Yunjin replied, voice low. "But you're not just tired."

They both fell silent. Around them, the other girls' voices filled the room in that warm, chaotic way they always did post-rehearsal. Sakura was crouched near the speaker, adjusting the playlist, while Chaewon and Eunchae debated whether they should order Boba or just crawl to their beds and die there.

"I'm starting to think you might have a secret twin," Eunchae said suddenly, pointing at Solar. "Because I swear you've been in two places at once lately."

Solar blinked. "What?"

"Like—you're here, killing choreo, slaying stages—but then you're also top 500 in Overwatch, and somehow managing to trend every two days because a boy looked at you sideways."

Solar rolled her eyes, but Eunchae grinned. "Just saying. If there's a clone of you running around, I want her skincare routine."

Sakura, finally satisfied with the music, walked over and sat down beside them. Her gaze was quiet but sharp. "You've been different."

"Different how?" Solar asked cautiously.

"Sharper. Like you're bracing for something."

Solar opened her mouth to deflect—but paused.

Because they weren't wrong. She was bracing. Every day felt like a collision waiting to happen.

"It's nothing," she said at last. "Just pressure."

Kazuha joined them, kneeling gracefully, a towel slung over one shoulder. "Pressure isn't always bad. But it builds." She looked straight at Solar. "Don't wait until it breaks something."

The weight of those words landed heavier than expected.

Solar looked down at the water bottle in her hands, the plastic denting slightly from her grip. Then she looked up, catching each of their faces—Chaewon's soft concern, Eunchae's messy affection, Sakura's quiet perception, Kazuha's calm gravity, and Yunjin's sharp, knowing loyalty.

These are my girls, she thought.

And somehow, that made it harder to admit how much she was unraveling.

So she just nodded, quiet. "Thanks, guys..."

No one pushed. No one needed to. The room filled with soft chatter again—plans for dinner, outfit checklists, a mention of the upcoming music show schedule.

But the air felt warmer now. Less like a battlefield. More like a home.

And Solar let herself breathe, just a little deeper.




Back at the dorm, the girls had scattered like dandelion seeds after rehearsal—some into the showers, some straight into bed, and Eunchae into the kitchen, muttering something about needing her "post-dance instant ramen or she'd literally collapse."

Solar peeled off her practice clothes, tied her hair up in a loose bun, and crawled into bed. Her limbs still throbbed from rehearsal, but it wasn't the kind of pain that lulled you to sleep. It was the kind that stayed. The kind that pulsed under your skin like a warning.

She checked her phone out of habit, expecting the usual flood of Instagram tags, company emails, and maybe a late-night meme from Yunjin.

What she didn't expect was a message from Rascal.

It was short. Blunt, almost. But not unkind.

RASCAL: hey, sorry i've been quiet.
RASCAL: you've got a lot going on. didn't wanna crowd your space.
RASCAL: rooting for you always though. you're kinda killing it.

No jokes. No weird punctuation or half-English trash talk. No teasing emojis or follow-up gifs. Just... three lines of polite distance.

Solar stared at it for a long time.

It wasn't mean. It wasn't even cold. But it didn't feel like him.

Her thumbs hovered over the screen, unsure what to type. She wanted to say "you're not crowding me." She wanted to say "I miss you."

She didn't say either. Instead, she typed:

SOLAR: thanks.
SOLAR: hope you're doing okay too.

It looked pathetic the second she sent it. The three dots appeared. Then disappeared.

Then nothing.

She tossed her phone aside and let it fall face-down against the covers. It was fine. It was whatever. He was giving her space, right? That was supposed to be a good thing.

But instead, it felt like she'd lost something.

Like someone had quietly taken a step back and drawn a line in the dirt. And she hadn't even realized they were standing on opposite sides. She turned off the lamp.

And lay there, eyes open in the dark, waiting for sleep that wouldn't come.



The message came just after 1:30 a.m.

Solar's phone buzzed softly from where it had slipped between the mattress and the wall. She wasn't asleep. She hadn't even closed her eyes.

She retrieved it with numb fingers, half-expecting another message from Rascal. Maybe an apology. Maybe something to undo the space that had settled like fog between them.

But it wasn't Rascal. It was Minji.

MINJI: i saw the new photos. the ones of you leaving rehearsal.
MINJI: people are being so loud again.
MINJI: are you okay?

Solar's heart lurched in the quiet.

It wasn't what she expected. Not from Minji. Not from someone so composed, so perfectly arranged at all times—like a porcelain figure too fragile to knock off the shelf.

And yet, here was Minji. Breaking that silence first.

There were no periods. No careful phrasing. Just lowercase vulnerability. It read like it had been typed in one breath. As if she hadn't let herself think too long before sending it.

Solar read the message once. Then twice.

Minji had seen the photos—blurry candids of her exiting rehearsal with a scowl and sweat-drenched bangs, face bare, eyes hollowed out by exhaustion. They'd gone semi-viral. Not because of her expression, but because someone had zoomed in and claimed she looked angry at fans.

As if she had the energy to be angry at anything except herself.

Solar chewed the inside of her cheek, thumb hovering over the reply field.

She could say "I'm fine." She could say "Thanks for checking in."

But something about the text—its gentle unraveling—stopped her.  Three quiet lines. A softness she didn't let the world see. It wasn't much.

But it said: I see you.

And something in Solar cracked. Her fingers moved slowly, unsure of themselves.

SOLAR: yeah. i'm okay.
SOLAR: or i will be. thanks for checking in.

She started to write something else—something real, maybe. Something about how lonely it felt to be screamed about online for simply existing. About how even when the whole world watched her, she still sometimes felt invisible.

But then the screen dimmed. She didn't finish the thought.

Instead, she set her phone down again—face-up this time—and let herself stare at the ceiling until her chest stopped aching.

Minji hadn't replied. She didn't need to. Just knowing she'd thought of her... it was enough.

Or it had to be.




The scrim lobby was already full when Solar logged on. Smurf had changed his name again to something ridiculous—BIGDADDYBAP69—and Glister was in the middle of arguing with Nero over whether milk or water was better in ramen.

"Water, you psychopath," Nero growled.

"It's not a crime to want a little creaminess," Glister insisted, genuinely wounded.

"Hey," Solar said softly, slipping into comms. "Am I interrupting a culinary war or...?"

"Solar!" Smurf cheered. "Our cracked queen returns."

"I was gonna say war criminal," Nero muttered. "But sure, let's go with queen."

Super's voice boomed through the channel next. "Oh my god, we can finally stop pretending Rascal's been okay without you. Man's been playing like he lost custody of his left click."

"Shut up," Rascal grumbled, voice sharp—but not biting. It didn't make Solar laugh like it usually did.

She leaned forward, elbows on the desk, headset snug against her ears. "Sorry I've been out. Practice has been brutal."

"Welcome to idol hell," Super chirped. "Anyway, let's warm up on Lijiang. Nero, stop eating while queuing or I swear to God—"

"Try and stop me," Nero said through what sounded like a mouthful of chips.

They queued in, fast as always. And at first, it felt normal.

Her Tracer was steady. Blinks were clean, positioning solid. Rascal adjusted his Echo rotations to peel for her, just like always. The coordination was still there—the unspoken rhythm between them, the dance of trust and timing.

But something was different.

Solar felt it in the space between callouts. In the way Rascal didn't tease her after a misplay. In the way he kept his voice even, polite, efficient.

Not cold. Just... reserved.

When she landed a Pulse Bomb on the backline and wiped two, the channel erupted in cheers—but not from Rascal.

Just a soft, "Nice pick." That was all.

And maybe she was imagining it. Maybe she was reading into nothing. But it felt like someone had turned the volume down on them.

Like their frequency didn't quite match anymore. They played three more maps. They won all of them. But the victory felt thin. Like smoke instead of fire.

After the last match, Solar lingered in the post-game screen, scrolling through her stats without really seeing them. Rascal's name popped up in her DMs.

RASCAL: gg today. nice bombs.
RASCAL: you good?

She stared at the second message for a long time. It was such a small thing—two words, soft and open—but it made her throat tighten.

Because even though others had asked, it felt different coming from him—quiet, unprompted, unperformed. Just him, checking in, in the only way he knew how. And the fact that it was him, even in this quiet, uncertain way... it meant more than she could say.

SOLAR: yeah.
SOLAR: are you?

There was a pause. Then:

RASCAL: always better when you're around.

Her chest did that annoying thing again—twisting like her heart couldn't decide whether it was supposed to ache or swell.

But before she could reply, he went offline. Just like that.




It was nearly 2:00 a.m. when Solar padded into the Le Sserafim practice room alone.

The lights were dimmed. Just the pale blue hue of the city filtering in through the windows, tracing long shadows across the mirrors. The studio smelled like rosin and floor polish, like the echo of ten thousand hours spent perfecting something only others got to see.

She set her phone on the stand and opened the camera. No filter. No lighting. Just her, barefaced, hair messy from sleep, hoodie slung over her shorts.

This wasn't for a brand. Not for HYBE. Not for the fans.

This was for her.

The music she chose wasn't even from their comeback playlist. It was something old. A b-side no one ever danced to. The kind of song you only kept if it meant something to you. She let it start slow, let the rhythm wrap around her limbs like muscle memory.

And then she moved.

Not the sharp, charismatic precision she gave to stages. Not the sugar-slick angles of choreography rehearsed into her bones.

This was fluid. Raw. A little reckless.

She let her emotions drive it—every stutter of breath, every doubt, every heartbeat that had stung for reasons she couldn't name.

The feeling of Rascal's message sitting too gently in her chest. The way Minji's text had left her breathless. The silence of Jungwon's last look as she'd walked away.

The memory of Jihoon's voice still echoing in her ears like a ghost that refused to die.

It all poured out in motion.

She twisted, stepped, collapsed, rose—her body speaking the things her mouth couldn't say. The weight of being watched. The ache of being wanted for what she could give, not what she was. The pressure of balancing everything on a wire that always threatened to snap.

By the time the song ended, she was on her knees, palms flat on the floor, sweat slicking her spine.

She didn't cry.

But she wanted to.

Not from sadness. Not entirely.

Just from the weight of feeling everything at once and finally letting it move through her.

She lay back on the floor, heart hammering, chest rising in shaky waves.

This—this was why she still loved it. The music. The movement. The silence afterward.

This was hers.

No headlines. No rumors. No ships or scandals.

Just Solar, in a dark room, remembering that she existed long before anyone knew her name.

Her phone buzzed again. She let it go. Just for now.

She needed this quiet. This calm.

Because tomorrow, the noise would return. And she'd be ready.

31: Two Worlds, One Room

The Seoul sky was a quiet gray the morning they landed.

Solar stood barefoot in the kitchen, fingers curled around a steaming mug. Her phone buzzed softly beside her, the airport group chat lighting up.

Smurf had sent a sleepy selfie—hood up, eyes barely open—with the caption:
we're back 💤

She stared at it for a few seconds. Not in voice chat. Not halfway across the world.
Here.

She smiled to herself—barely—and took another sip of coffee.

Yunjin entered moments later, hair still damp from a shower, and flopped into the seat across from her. "You're up early."

"Couldn't sleep," Solar murmured. Her thumb hovered over the screen. "The Shock just landed."

Yunjin leaned over, peering at the photo. "Aw, look at Smurf. He looks like a baby duck."

"An exhausted duck," Solar replied, smiling faintly.

Yunjin grabbed her own mug. "Wait, are they just coming here straight from the airport or what?"

Solar hesitated.

"I mean... they don't really have plans until tomorrow's media shoot. And they're staying nearby." She glanced at Yunjin. "I don't know, I figured I'd just see them later."

Yunjin raised a brow. "Girl. You've been playing with these dudes for months. Rascal literally carried you through a meta collapse. Just invite them over."

Solar blinked. "What, like—to the dorm?"

"Why not? It's not like you're sneaking them into the Pentagon. They're your teammates. And I kind of want to see what a real-life Glister looks like."

Solar exhaled a laugh, then paused, thoughtful. "You think the girls would be okay with it?"

Yunjin shrugged. "We live with Eunchae. Nothing phases us anymore."

Solar bit her lip. "Alright. I'll message them."

By the time she finished her coffee, Rascal had replied to Smurf's photo with one of Super drooling against the airplane window and the caption:
humanity's last hope

Solar snorted.

Yunjin grinned into her cup. "God, they're gremlins."

Solar's phone buzzed again—Super replying with "we outsideeeee 😎" followed by sixteen variations of a flexing emoji.

"Well," Solar said, standing. "Guess I'm hosting world champions now."

Eunchae practically sprinted to the door, then hesitated dramatically like she was announcing royalty. "Ladies and gentlemen... the San Francisco Shock."

The door opened.

Five boys stood on the threshold—some with travel bags slung over shoulders, some already holding energy drinks, and all looking distinctly out of place in the pastel, plant-filled dorm hallway.

"Yo," Super grinned, stepping in first with a theatrical spin. "This place smells like flowers and responsibility."

"Take off your shoes," Chaewon scolded gently from the kitchen.

"Sorry, Mom," Super said, already hopping on one foot to comply.

Glister followed with a nervous wave. "Hi! This is so cool."

"Wow," Nero muttered, looking around with squinted eyes. "You guys... live like this?"

Smurf nodded solemnly. "It's so clean."

"We're idols," Yunjin deadpanned. "We are the reason Swiffer's exist."

Rascal entered last. No smile. Just a quiet nod, but his gaze found Solar instantly. She met it. Just for a second. Then looked away. Her heart beat a little too fast for a welcome party.

"So, uh," Super clapped his hands once. "We doing a grand tour or are we gonna awkwardly stand here while I slowly become one with your floor?"

Sakura emerged from her room just then, arms crossed, hair still damp from a shower. She gave them all a once-over before raising an eyebrow. "Is this all of you?"

Nero looked her straight in the eye. "You tell me."

The tension lasted half a second—then Glister elbowed Nero, panicking, and Sakura turned away with a soft scoff.

Solar swore she saw her smirk.

"Alright," she said, clapping her hands. "You've seen our foyer. Prepare for idol reality."

She led them deeper inside—down the narrow hall lined with framed debut photos and tiny wall hooks overflowing with scrunchies. The living room was already set up with snacks and floor cushions, a few candles flickering in glass jars. The coffee table was covered in board games and questionable snacks that Eunchae insisted were iconic.

Super pointed at a plushie on the shelf. "Did that thing blink?"

Chaewon gave him a look. "Only if you disrespect our décor."

They settled in like tourists in a museum: quietly overwhelmed but weirdly reverent. Solar couldn't help but smile. She caught Rascal watching her again across the room—his posture casual, but his eyes focused. There was a softness in his face she hadn't seen before. Not even on Discord.

Not even in scrims. A beat passed between them.

Then Eunchae pulled out a game and announced, "Alright, idols versus gamers. Losers do the TikTok dance of shame."

Glister's face lit up. "I've been preparing for this moment."

"Oh god," Nero muttered.

And just like that, the chaos began.

The living room became a warzone of aesthetic dissonance.

On one side: five elite Overwatch players in various states of social confusion. On the other: six idol girls who lived in rehearsed perfection. Somewhere in the middle: enough chips, candy, and fizzy drinks to fuel a LAN party and a comeback stage combined.

Super took charge immediately, standing like a camp counselor at the front of the room. "Alright, listen up. We're doing teams. Idols versus us. And by us, I mean the future losers, because I just remembered I suck at charades."

Eunchae beamed. "Perfect. Let the humiliation begin."

Nero sank further into the beanbag with his arms crossed. "I didn't agree to this."

"You never agree to anything," Rascal muttered from the couch beside him, holding a soda can like it might bite him.

"You're all very loud," Chaewon commented, sipping tea with the kind of grace that could only come from being the eldest girl in the room.

"That's how you know we're comfortable," Smurf chimed, finally taking his shoes off and folding his legs like he'd lived there for years.

Glister sat between Yunjin and Kazuha, trying so hard to not panic that his shoulders practically touched his ears. "This is... really different from team house."

Yunjin narrowed her eyes. "Different how?"

"I mean... no dishes in the sink," Glister blurted.

She smirked. "Good save."

Sakura and Nero hadn't spoken since their initial exchange.

He didn't look at her. Not directly. But Solar saw it—the way he'd glance sideways when Sakura passed, like he was waiting for her to say something she never did. Sakura wasn't tense. She was ice. Calm, unreadable, like she was watching a storm roll in but had already made peace with it.

Solar didn't know what the hell was going on there, but she made a mental note to ask Yunjin to investigate.

"Your place is insane," Super said as Eunchae handed him a random board game. "You guys always have this much food on hand?"

"No," Solar answered, "Eunchae panic-bought half a convenience store."

"For you!" Eunchae declared proudly. "Because I'm thoughtful."

"She's not," Yunjin said, arms crossed.

"I am! I even got Rascal those weird corn snacks he likes!" Everyone turned to Rascal.

He blinked. "...How do you know that?"

"I watch your streams," Eunchae replied.

He looked mildly haunted. "Oh god..."

"You eat like a child," she added.

"And you snore like a chainsaw," Rascal shot back.

Eunchae grinned. "Touché."

Amid the teasing, games, and laughter, Solar drifted a little.

She watched her two worlds colliding in real time—players yelling nonsense over Uno rules while Chaewon offered everyone fruit and Yunjin silently scoped out who was secretly in love with who.

It was chaos. And yet, it worked.

She caught Rascal's eye again—this time mid-laugh, his mouth open, eyes squinting. It hit her like a wave how rare it was to see him laugh like that. No camera. No pressure. Just... joy.

And the way he looked at her when he realized she was watching?

Like the room dimmed and they were the only two people left in it. Just for a second.

Then Glister knocked over a cup of cider and Nero screamed because it hit his sock, and the moment vanished like vapor.

Still. It lingered.



It was supposed to be a quick tour.

Just Solar showing Rascal her room so he could drop off his hoodie. That's it.

She hadn't realized what a mistake that might be until the door clicked shut behind them and suddenly the noise of the living room—the laughter, the shouting, the chaos—faded to a muffled hum.

Her bedroom was dimly lit, soft pink LED strips glowing under the shelves. Her bed was unmade. Posters and stage photos lined one wall; on the other, her setup glowed quietly—triple monitors dark, headset hanging like a crown from its stand.

Rascal stood in the center of the room, looking around like he wasn't sure if he should speak or bow.

"Whoa," he murmured. "This is... not what I expected."

Solar raised an eyebrow. "Why? Thought I slept in a coffin?"

"No, I just... I mean, there's awards in here."

He walked over to the floating shelf by her desk, tilting his head at the few trophies perched beside a stack of lyric notebooks and a faded Le Sserafim lightstick.

"MAMA Rookie of the Year," he read aloud. "Music Bank triple crown... And your face is on, like, every poster."

Solar shrugged. "I don't like putting them in the living room. Feels too... loud."

"Girl, you are loud. You're, like, K-pop loud."

She laughed, but it came out quiet. Rascal's tone had shifted—he was still joking, but there was something else underneath. Something awed. Maybe even intimidated.

He turned toward her, expression unreadable.

"You ever think about how weird this is?" he asked. "That I'm standing here, in a K-pop idol's room? Like... you're a big deal."

Solar leaned against her desk, arms crossed. "So are you."

"No," he said simply. "Not like this."

Something about the way he said it made her chest ache.

"You're on the Shock," she murmured. "Two-time champion. People would kill to play with you."

He smiled—just barely—but it didn't reach his eyes. "Yeah, but no one's writing think-pieces about what my hands look like holding a mouse."

Solar's stomach flipped. She stepped forward, suddenly serious. "Rascal..."

He looked up.

"You are a big deal," she said.

They were close now. Not touching. Not even a full step apart. But close enough that she could see the tension in his jaw. The way he wasn't breathing quite right.

"You're different," he said quietly. "You walk into a room and everyone notices. You don't even try."

"You think that's a good thing?"

He looked at her, really looked, and for the first time, there was no banter. No snark. Just honesty.

"I think it's dangerous."

The air between them shifted. Thickened. Solar's breath caught.

He didn't move. Didn't reach for her. But something in his posture tilted—like gravity was leaning him forward against his will.

And she...

God, she almost let it happen.

Her fingers twitched at her sides. Her pulse stuttered. She could feel it—that moment—coming. The one people always wrote about in fiction but never described quite right.

It wasn't lust. It was something messier. Hungrier. Lonelier.

She looked at him and saw all the things she didn't know how to want anymore—warmth, safety, ease.

And he looked at her like he saw all the things he didn't think he deserved.

They didn't kiss. But they could have. It would've been so easy. Instead, Solar turned away.

"I should get back out there," she said, voice barely above a whisper.

Rascal exhaled slowly. She couldn't tell if it was relief or regret.

"Yeah," he agreed. "Me too."

But neither of them moved for a long second. Then Solar opened the door—and the world came rushing back in.




The rest of the night blurred into noise.

Solar laughed when she was supposed to. Ate snacks. Won a round of charades with Glister by miming Titanic so well that Super fell off the couch. She played the part perfectly—idol, gamer, glue between two worlds colliding in her dorm's tiny living room.

But her mind was still in that bedroom.

Still standing too close to someone who looked at her like a question he didn't dare ask. She didn't catch Rascal's eye again. Not until the boys were getting ready to leave.

Smurf was hugging Chaewon like they were old friends. Super was giving Eunchae a dramatic bow and saying, "You have been an honor to lose to." Nero was already halfway down the hall muttering about "never stepping into pink lighting again."

And Rascal?

He was quiet. Hoodie back on, hands in his pockets, head ducked slightly like he didn't quite want to be noticed. She caught him by the door.

"Hey," she said softly.

He turned. Their eyes met. There was no crowd around them now. No excuse not to speak. No game to hide behind.

"I'm sorry," Solar whispered.

Rascal's expression didn't change. "For what?"

She hesitated. "I don't know. Just... for whatever that was."

He nodded once. Not dismissive, not cold. Just... steady. "You're busy," he murmured. "I get it."

"Rascal..."

He looked at her fully then. And smiled. It was small. A little tired. And so gentle it nearly broke her.

"Don't worry," he said. "I won't be weird." The words she felt she'd heard him say too many times.

And just like that, he turned and followed the others down the hall, his footsteps soft against the polished floor.

Solar stood there long after the door closed, blinking against the sting behind her eyes. That line again. He'd said it once before. A different version of it. Same tone. Same distance. Like he'd already decided not to ask her for anything more.

Because maybe he'd already decided she was never his to ask anything of.

And somewhere inside her, something caved in. Not dramatically. Not like a collapse. More like a quiet folding inward. A truth too heavy to carry and too fragile to say out loud. She went back to her room. Sat on the edge of her bed. Stared at the headset hanging from its stand.

Her fingers hovered over her phone.

No messages. None from Rascal. None from Jungwon. None from Minji.

Just her.

And the quiet echo of a moment that didn't happen—but almost did.

She didn't turn the lights on.

The pink glow under her desk hummed quietly, casting the walls in soft neon as Solar sat curled beneath her blankets, knees drawn to her chest, hoodie sleeves pulled over her hands. Rascal's hoodie. It still smelled faintly like him—fabric softener and something a little burnt, a little reckless.

The kind of comfort that didn't ask questions. She hadn't meant for anything to happen. But she hadn't meant to want it, either.

She pressed her forehead to her knees. The sheets were still warm from her body, her thoughts curling like smoke through her skull.

Everything felt too loud inside her. She didn't know what she wanted. That was the worst part.

Rascal made her feel safe. Like things could be easy. Like she didn't have to be perfect or poised or anything other than herself.

And Jungwon... She didn't even know Jungwon.

Not really.

But his voice still echoed in her head from the hallway at HYBE. That near-whisper, full of something unspoken and sharp. The way he looked at her like she was a locked room he wanted to break into.

They hadn't touched. Barely talked. But when he stared at her, she felt like gravity tilted.

Solar let out a slow breath, her chest aching in that hollow, familiar way she'd come to associate with nights like these. Nights where the stage lights were off, and no one was watching, and she couldn't pretend she wasn't scared.

Because the truth was—

She didn't know who she was becoming.

Was she Solar, the idol?

Was she Solar, the gamer?

Was she the girl who almost kissed Rascal?

Or the one who still couldn't stop thinking about the way Jungwon said her name like it meant something more?

She reached for her phone.

Opened Discord.

RASCAL — last online: 17 minutes ago.

She didn't message him. Didn't message anyone. Just stared.

Then, without meaning to, she whispered into the quiet:

"Why can't I ever be enough for anyone?"

No one answered. Only the soft hum of her computer and the dull ache of a heart pulled in too many directions.

And still—

Still—

She wanted more. Wanted all of it. The music. The matches. The mayhem. The love. Even the pain.

Especially the pain.

Because at least pain meant feeling something.

She closed her eyes. And let it hurt.










32: Wounds Don’t Wait

The dorm was quiet, but not the good kind.

Not the peaceful hush of early mornings or the lazy silence after laughter. This was the kind of quiet that crept under your skin. The kind that echoed too loudly when you moved, like even the walls were listening.

Solar stood at the bathroom sink, her toothbrush hanging uselessly from her hand. She wasn't brushing. Just staring.

At her face. At the eyes that didn't look like hers.

Her reflection was blank. Not tired. Not sad. Just... paused. Like her body was still catching up to everything her heart hadn't processed yet.

The moment in her room—her and Rascal, standing too close, saying too little—played again and again behind her eyes. The quiet tension. The way he looked at her like she was made of something breakable. The way neither of them moved.

And the way it almost happened.

Not just the kiss. The undoing. The unraveling. Something inside her had leaned forward, ready to give in. Ready to believe that maybe, this time, she could let herself want something without it ending in blood.

But she hadn't. Because wanting had always been dangerous.

She rinsed her mouth and splashed cold water on her face, hoping it would snap her out of it. It didn't. The chill just made her shiver. She grabbed a towel and dried off, then padded barefoot back to her room, hoodie sleeves pulled over her hands like armor.

Her foot caught on the corner of her rug. She stumbled, caught herself—and froze.

Because it hit her. The memory. Out of nowhere. Like a punch to the gut.

Not Rascal. Not this room. Another room. Another night.

She was sixteen. Jihoon was leaning against the window of that awful rooftop party, cigarette between his fingers, a bottle half-empty at his side. He was laughing at something she said—laughing too loudly, his voice already slurred.

And then—his hand on her wrist. Not painful. Just... heavy. Possessive.

He kissed her. Not soft. Not rough. Just sudden. Like it was his right. She hadn't said no. She hadn't said anything.

Not until the next morning when she woke up with the taste of smoke in her throat and the weight of shame sitting on her chest like a stone.

She'd buried that night so deep she forgot it had roots.

But now—now Rascal was looking at her with something gentle. And all she could think was: What if I break this too?

She sank onto her bed, drawing her knees to her chest. Her fingers twisted the edge of her sleeve until it stretched. Maybe Rascal was her soft place. But she didn't know how to land without crashing.

She didn't know how to be touched without bracing for impact. And that scared her more than anything.

She turned toward her desk.

The pink glow from her monitors was still on, humming gently. Her Discord still open. Rascal's name hovered near the top—grayed out. Offline. Gone.

She didn't blame him. Maybe she was just too much. Too complicated. Too loud inside. Too guarded for anyone to love without getting hurt.

She pulled the blankets over her head. If she stayed like this long enough, maybe the ache would go away.

But she knew it wouldn't.

Because some aches didn't heal with time.

Some wounds didn't wait for closure.

They just lingered.



The next morning, Solar woke before her alarm.

She lay still in the half-dark, blinking up at the ceiling as the soft hum of her phone vibrated against the nightstand. She knew what time it was without checking—too early. Too soon to be okay.

Her body moved on autopilot: get up, stretch, pull on leggings and a cropped hoodie, scrape her hair into a ponytail. The dorm was quiet except for the faint shuffle of Eunchae getting cereal and the gentle strum of a guitar through Yunjin's door.

Down the hall, someone was humming their warm-up scales. Another day, another rehearsal. Another chance to pretend everything inside her wasn't twisting into knots.

Solar reached for her phone, expecting group chat spam or maybe a random Shock meme from Super.

Instead, her chest tightened.

MINJI 
hey
i saw the photos. the media's ridiculous.
just wanted to say... i hope you're okay.
you don't have to answer. just thinking of you.

Solar read it three times.

It was soft. Unassuming. There was no flirting, no cryptic winks. Just raw concern typed out in lowercase letters. And that made it worse.

Because this wasn't just some idle message. This was from Minji—Minji, who was always polished, always poised, always at arm's length. Minji, who usually played the long game, said things with her eyes but never her hands.

This wasn't that.

This was cracked at the edges.

Solar stared at the screen, her thumb hovering over the keyboard. She started to type.

thank you. i'm okay.

Deleted it.

you didn't have to reach out. but it means a lot.

Deleted again.

Instead, she just... stared. Like the longer she looked, the more she could decode the spaces between each word. The slight falter in Minji's tone, even over text. The almost-apology baked into you don't have to answer.

Because maybe Minji knew.

Maybe she'd seen the way Solar looked at Jungwon in the choreography studio. The way she avoided the camera after every public blow-up. The way she never really seemed... safe.

Maybe she knew, and maybe this message was her way of saying, I see you anyway.

Solar's throat tightened.

She set the phone down face-down on the counter and reached for a glass of water, like the act of drinking something could wash away the tension ballooning in her chest.

But it didn't. Because the worst part was that she wanted to answer. She wanted to say thank you. She wanted to say I'm not okay. She wanted to say I don't know what I'm doing.

But what if saying that opened a door she wasn't ready to walk through?

What if Minji meant it?

What if she didn't?

What if Solar wanted her to?

She rubbed her eyes with the heel of her palms. Her fingers still smelled like cherry lip gloss from the night before. The same gloss she'd almost kissed Rascal with.

Her whole body ached with the weight of unsaid things.

Footsteps padded in behind her—Chaewon, fresh from the shower, towel draped around her neck.

"You okay?" she asked softly, eyes scanning Solar's face.

Solar smiled. A little too fast. "Yeah. Just tired."

Chaewon gave her a long look. Then stepped closer and gently touched her arm. "Just so you know, you don't have to be okay all the time. Not here."

Solar's smile flickered.

"I know."

But she didn't know how to not be okay. Not without unraveling completely.




The hallways of HYBE were always cold.

Solar hugged her hoodie tighter around herself as she stepped out of the rehearsal studio, the echo of bass still thudding through the floor. Her head throbbed. ANTIFRAGILE was wrecking her knees, her voice, her energy—and that was before she remembered she had scrims tonight with the Shock.

She rubbed her temple with one hand and didn't notice the figure walking toward her until it was too late.

Jungwon.

He looked like he'd just finished his own rehearsal—black joggers, sleeveless shirt clinging to his collarbones, damp hair pushed back with a hand towel slung around his neck. His eyes caught hers the moment she looked up. Neither of them slowed. It was like gravity pulled them forward.

They stopped just a step apart.

"...Hey," Solar said first, because someone had to.

Jungwon's voice was quiet, but not cold. "You okay?"

She hesitated. "Yeah. Just tired."

A pause. Then a subtle tilt of his head.

"You always say that."

She blinked.

It wasn't accusing. Just... true.

"I'm not lying," she said after a beat.

"No," he murmured. "But you're not telling the whole truth either."

There it was again—that calm, unnerving ability to see straight through her. She looked away.

"I didn't expect to run into you," she admitted, shifting her weight.

"I kind of hoped I would."

That made her look up again. Her chest tightened.

Their last conversation still clung to the air between them—those quiet words in the lobby, after Jihoon. The weight of his stare. The certainty in his voice when he told her he wasn't going anywhere.

But it hadn't felt comforting. Not then.

It felt like a promise she didn't know how to keep.

"You said that like it meant something," Solar said softly. "Back then."

Jungwon studied her face. "It did."

"Why?"

"Because you looked like you were ready to disappear," he said. "And I didn't want to let that happen."

She swallowed. "You barely knew me."

His answer came fast. "Still don't."

Something about that—how honest it was, how firm—unraveled her a little.

"But I wanted to," Jungwon added.

The hallway was quiet now. She could hear her own pulse.

"I still do," he said.

Solar stared at him. Her throat felt tight. She didn't know what to say. Everything felt sharp when he looked at her like that. Like she was a puzzle he already knew the answer to, and he was just waiting for her to say it out loud.

But she couldn't.

She wasn't ready.

So instead, she offered a half-smile and asked, "Didn't you say you weren't going anywhere?"

"I didn't." He stepped aside, letting her pass. But his voice followed her. "Still won't."

She paused just long enough to glance back—one last look.

Jungwon's gaze hadn't moved.

She didn't smile this time. But her eyes softened.

Then she turned and walked away, the echo of his presence still thrumming in her ribs like a second heartbeat.




Back in the practice room, Solar was the last one left.

The others had filtered out one by one—Chaewon with her thermos of tea, Eunchae dragging her tote bag like it owed her money, Yunjin waving and mouthing, Rest, before slipping out the door.

But Solar stayed.

The mirror stretched in front of her like a second self, and she stared at it, unmoving. Her body hurt. Not in the satisfying, worked-hard way. In the fragile, about-to-crack way. She felt like glass under pressure—one more push and she might shatter.

She pulled her hoodie up over her head and sat cross-legged on the polished floor. Her phone sat beside her, buzzing quietly with messages she didn't want to read.

RASCAL (9:31PM): see u tmr

MINJI (8:56PM): hope the company isn't being too hard on you lately.

YUNJIN (just now): u okkkk?

She locked the screen without responding. It wasn't that she didn't care. It was that she didn't know how to explain what this kind of loneliness felt like when you were surrounded by people who loved you.

The worst kind, really—the kind where the world roots for you, but you still feel like you're losing.

She thought about Rascal. That near-moment in her room. The quiet regret in his voice when he told her she was busy.

She thought about Jungwon. How he said he wasn't going anywhere. How she didn't know if she believed him—or if she was just afraid to.

She thought about Minji. The warmth in her messages. The soft strength she showed even when her voice shook. The way she saw Solar, even when Solar didn't want to be seen.

Her thoughts felt like tangled headphones—impossible to separate.

Solar closed her eyes.

For a moment, the silence was everything.

Then—she stood. Walked to the mirror. Looked at herself.

Not the idol. Not the gamer.

Just the girl.

Eyes tired. Shoulders hunched. Hoodie too big, sleeves frayed at the ends from being pulled over her hands too many times.

But still standing.

She stepped forward and touched the mirror—just with her fingertips. As if to remind herself she was still real.

"I'm doing my best," she whispered. Not to anyone else. Just to the reflection. Just to the part of her that still believed in something.

Then she grabbed her bag, slung it over one shoulder, and walked out.

The hallway lights flickered behind her as she disappeared into the quiet of the building, headphones around her neck, hoodie pulled low.

Still alone.

But maybe—just maybe—not as lost as she used to be.













33: The Double Life

The dorm was buzzing long before sunrise.

Solar stood under the fluorescent lights of the bathroom, gripping the edge of the sink. Her reflection blinked back at her—eyeliner half-drawn, stage lashes still untouched. Her hoodie was zipped to her chin, Rascal's name stitched just inside the collar, hidden against her throat.

Her phone buzzed from the sink.

RASCAL: good luck at comeback stage
RASCAL: see you for the tournament tonight

She stared at the message for a few seconds, heart twisting. Reassurance and reality, packed into two short lines. She responded quickly, thumbs flying before she could second-guess herself.

SOLAR: i'll be there
SOLAR: i promise

The bathroom door creaked open.

"Unnie?" Eunchae peeked her head in, half-asleep but already in full glam. "Hair's done. You're up."

"Got it," Solar murmured, capping her eyeliner with a practiced flick.

She stepped into the stylist's chair moments later, muscles still tight from yesterday's dance rehearsal. The salon lights made everything look artificial—too perfect, too bright. One of the staff handed her a protein bar. She took it but didn't eat.

"You're up first in the center again," their choreographer reminded her. "You know the new camera sequence, right?"

"Yeah," Solar replied automatically.

But her mind was split in two.

ANTIFRAGILE. Shock scrims. Comeback stage. Payload maps. Idol posture. DPS cooldowns.

She didn't know how to breathe without switching tabs in her brain.

By 11:00 a.m., Le Sserafim stood at the side of the music show stage, glittering under the studio lights. They were dressed in black and red, all sleek cuts and confidence. The crowd screamed loud enough to shake the floors when their name was announced.

Solar stood center. The beat dropped. The lights flared. And she moved.

Every muscle snapped into place. Her body remembered the choreography like second nature—hips cutting through the bassline, eyes flicking to the camera on cue. She hit every mark. Sang every line. Danced like the stage owed her something.

The audience chanted her name during the bridge.

SOLAR! SOLAR! SOLAR!

Her limbs burned. Her lungs begged for air. But her smile stayed sharp.

She bowed with the others after the final pose, sweat glistening on her skin, her heart thudding in her ears like a war drum.

"You killed it," Yunjin whispered behind her.

Solar nodded. "Thanks."

But her eyes had already drifted to the clock backstage.

Three hours until Shock check-in.

She was still wearing her in-ears when their manager pulled her aside. "We'll need you at the interview booth in twenty. Then a group TikTok shoot."

"I can't," she said. "I have another schedule."

The manager blinked. "What schedule?"

Solar hesitated. She knew she wasn't supposed to bring it up again—not after HYBE had reluctantly greenlit her esports contract with a dozen conditions.

But she'd made a promise.

"To Crusty. For the tournament tonight." She swallowed hard. "I need to go."

The manager didn't argue, just pinched the bridge of her nose and sighed. "Just... get back for the encore. Don't be late."

Solar changed in the car.

Black cargo pants. Her Shock jersey. Minimal makeup, hair tied back. Idol off, player on.

Her phone buzzed nonstop as the van weaved through traffic. Glister had sent memes. Smurf sent "u good???" in all caps. Nero had renamed their Discord channel to "where tf is solar."

She arrived at the venue with fifteen minutes to spare.

Her headset was already waiting at her station. Rascal looked up when she entered, his mouth twitching into a small smile.

"You made it."

"Barely."

"You good to play?"

Solar flexed her fingers. "Ask me when we win."

Crusty barked out the first set of map assignments. The crowd roared as their names flashed on the big screen above the arena. Fans were holding signs now.

Some of them said LE SSERAFIM'S SOLAR = THE REAL CARRY.

Solar strapped on her headset, heart pounding, and cracked her knuckles.

The match began.

The crowd was electric.

From her seat at the Shock bench, Solar could hear them through her noise-canceling headset—muffled cheers, shouted callouts, the thrum of bass-heavy intro music pulsing against the glass of the booth.

She adjusted her grip on the mouse. Her fingers trembled. Not from nerves. From fatigue.

She hadn't eaten since 8 a.m. She hadn't stopped moving since sunrise. Her shoulders ached from the choreo. Her thighs burned from the final pose she'd dropped into three times during camera rehearsals alone.

And now she had to play like her spot on the Shock wasn't a novelty. Like she belonged here. Like she wasn't an idol in cosplay.

Rascal's voice cut through the comms—firm, steady, right in her ear.

"Map one: Circuit Royal. We take high ground early, pressure backline."

"Copy," Solar murmured.

"Solar, I want you on Tracer. We need distraction. You okay?" He asked it softly, not like a command. Like a real question.

She breathed out once. "I'm good."

Super's voice followed, loud and loose. "Let's do this. Team comp: Shockwave Chaos. Let's break their ankles!"

Nero: "Let's break more than that."

The loading screen faded. The game began. And everything else disappeared.

Solar blinked into their backline, darting through alleys like a ghost. Her fingers moved faster than her thoughts. She stuck Pulse Bombs onto Ana's back like second nature. Blinked out of crowd control. Flanked and flinched and fell and got back up.

Every callout came like second language.

"Tracer no recall."

"Zen discorded."

"D.Va no mech."

"Dive in three... two... go."

They won the first round. Barely. Crusty's voice came in over the comms, sharp and clipped. "Sloppy at point B. Reset that dive tempo."

"Yes coach," they echoed.

Solar muted for a moment. Flexed her hands. Her wrist cracked. Her head throbbed. She pulled open a protein bar with her teeth. Rascal leaned toward her across the gap between their setups.

"Breathe," he mouthed. "Just breathe."

She nodded. Even smiled. Map two was Colosseo. It was a bloodbath. Solar went Echo. Rascal went Genji.

They danced across the battlefield like threads weaving the same fabric—dashing, deflecting, darting through enemy lines and peeling pressure off the cart.

When Rascal got a four-kill Dragonblade, Solar screamed out loud. When she clutched the final fight by sticking their Sojourn before overtime, he pounded his fist on the desk and yelled, "LET'S GO!"

Two maps up. Match point. The casters were screaming. Chat was exploding.

SOLAR IS NOT A GIMMICK.
SHE'S A THREAT.
#SHOCKSUPREMACY

Solar muted again and leaned forward, bracing her head in her hands. She was sweating. Badly. But her hands hadn't shaken once since the first round.

They loaded into Havana. Rascal called for a switch—he'd go Pharah, Solar would pocket him on Mercy. Glister would flex to be their second DPS. 

"Protect me," he said in comms, almost teasing.

"You wish," she snapped back. But she did.

She peeled pressure with Valk. Darted between rooftops. Pumped damage boost into every barrage. She died twice but rezzed once under fire, and when Rascal air-shotted their Echo mid-flight, Solar actually laughed out loud. The kind of laugh that didn't feel like a performance.

Final fight. Final hold. Shock had them at the choke.

"Last push," Super barked. "Hold here and it's ours."

The enemy support ulted in. Nero dropped Grav. Rascal went down. Solar still had rez.

Her screen flashed red. "Under fire!"

Pulse pounding, she darted across the kill zone. Pressed the key.

"Reviving teammate..." Rascal reappeared with one bar of health.

"Go," she said, not even thinking. He Dragonbladed again. The screen exploded in color.

VICTORY.

The match was over. The arena erupted.

Shock won.

Solar barely heard the casters as they screamed the final play-by-play. She barely felt her headset being pulled off. She sat there, breathing heavy, hands slack in her lap, the realization hitting all at once.

They won.

Not just despite her being there. Because she was there. The team surrounded her in a rush.

Super dragged her into a crushing hug. "YOU'RE A MONSTER."

Nero ruffled her hair like an annoying older brother. "Alright, alright, you're not useless. You're cracked."

Glister, beaming, threw both hands in the air. "Solar supremacy forever!!"

Rascal stepped in last. He didn't hug her. Not right away. He just looked at her—soft, amazed, like he'd seen something break open in the sky.

Then he pulled her into a gentle embrace. One arm, secure around her shoulders. His voice was barely a whisper.

"You were perfect."

She felt the sting behind her eyes. Not from exhaustion. From relief.

The kind that comes when you've fought like hell for something no one believed you could be. And won.




The hotel lobby was still pulsing with post-match adrenaline.

Cameras flashed as the Shock boys trickled in. Super was rewatching the final fight on his phone and narrating it like a sports commentator on speed. Smurf and Nero were arguing over who miscalled the Havana flank. Glister kept singing made-up victory songs while hugging anyone in reach.

Solar laughed along. She smiled for a few photos. She said thank you to a reporter who complimented her Pulse Bomb tracking. But her feet hurt. Her head buzzed. And through it all—Rascal hadn't said a word since the stage.

Until he did.

"You good?" he asked quietly, standing just behind her in the hallway near the elevator.

She turned, a little startled.

He wasn't smiling. Not like the others. Not like before.

Just watching her. His gaze steady. Like he was still seeing her from the booth—clear and sharp and unfiltered.

"I think I might pass out," she admitted, voice soft.

Rascal's mouth twitched, almost a smile. "Then maybe sit down before you start a new scandal."

She leaned against the wall, exhaling slowly. "What, you don't want the headline to be 'idol dies of euphoria after tournament win'?"

"I'd rather not lose my Mercy pocket to dehydration." His tone was light. But his eyes weren't. They stood there for a beat, suspended in the thrum of post-victory chaos behind them.

Then Rascal said, quietly, "Come with me."

She didn't ask where. She just followed.

They didn't go far—just down the hall, into one of the smaller meeting rooms Shock had been using for prep. It was dark now, the lights off, windows open to the Seoul night air. The city glowed in the distance—faint, humming, alive.

Rascal closed the door behind them.

No cameras. No teammates. No noise.

Just quiet. Just them.

Solar crossed her arms. "If this is where you tell me I need to calm down—"

"It's not," Rascal interrupted.

His voice was lower now. Something shifted in the way he was looking at her. Not playful. Not casual. Just...bare.

"You played like you've been on this team for years," he admits. "No one can say otherwise now."

"I wasn't trying to prove anything," she said, a little too fast.

He tilted his head. "Weren't you?"

Solar looked away. The silence that followed felt charged. Like the kind that builds right before lightning strikes.

Rascal stepped closer.

"The other day," he murmured. "At your dorm... That moment?"

She didn't breathe. "Yeah," she whispered.

"I didn't mean for that to happen."

"I know."

Another step.

"But I wanted it to."

Her chest pulled tight. "I know..."

And then there was no space left between them. It wasn't rushed. It wasn't loud. It was a kiss like a held breath—slow, tentative, searching.

Rascal's lips brushed hers like a question. And Solar kissed him back like she didn't have the answer.

His hand came to the side of her face, warm and gentle, and her fingers curled into the fabric of his hoodie. But even as her body moved closer, something in her chest stayed still. Frozen.

Because it felt good. It felt safe.

But it didn't burn. It didn't take over her. It didn't make her forget.

And Rascal felt it too. Because when they pulled apart—barely—he didn't smile. He just looked at her. Sad, soft, knowing. And just above a whisper, "This isn't your ending, is it?"

Solar swallowed hard. "I don't know what it is," she admitted.

Rascal nodded once. No anger. No drama. Just... quiet. "You don't owe me anything," he assures gently.

Her voice cracked. "But you deserve more than a maybe."

He touched his forehead to hers, eyes closing. "I'd rather be a maybe than a headline."

And that was the part that hurt the most. Because it meant he already knew. Already accepted it. Even if he didn't want to.

They stood like that for a long moment. Then Rascal stepped back. Just far enough to let the space between them settle.

"You should rest," he said.

"You too."

Neither of them moved for another full minute. Then Solar finally turned toward the door.

And this time, he didn't stop her.



Solar didn't go back to her hotel room right away. She wandered.

Through the echoing halls. Past the vending machines and water coolers and the faint sound of Super shouting into a phone in the distance.

Her body felt heavy in a strange way. Not from exhaustion. Not from the match. But from everything else. The kind of weight you carry in your chest. In your throat.

She stopped by the window at the end of the hallway. The Seoul skyline blinked far below, glittering like the stars she used to count with Chaerin on rooftops as kids.

And for a moment, she felt untethered. Like her body was here, but her mind—her heart—was somewhere else.

The tournament was over. They'd won. She had made it through the scheduling of her double life, balancing her hectic idol schedule with her more relaxed, but still intense, eSports.  She had played better than she ever had in her life. She had kissed someone who made her feel safe, wanted, seen.

And yet... She didn't feel whole.

Solar leaned her forehead against the cool glass. Her breath fogged the window as she exhaled slowly, shakily. Was this what it meant to chase two dreams at once? Was this what being "enough" felt like?

Because if so, then why did it still feel like she was falling short—of something. Of someone.

She thought about Rascal. The way he looked at her, not like a challenge to conquer, but like a person. A partner.

She thought about Jungwon. The way he didn't look at her like a person at all—at least not in a simple way. He looked at her like a cipher. Like a fuse already lit. Like he knew there was something in her that no one else had the right to touch.

And that terrified her. Because deep down, she wanted someone who didn't just understand her— She wanted someone who recognized her.

The her that existed before all this.

Before the jersey. Before the lashes and the cameras and the rehearsals. Before Jihoon.

She closed her eyes. For a split second, she could still feel Rascal's lips against hers. Warm. Gentle. Asking for nothing.

And then, just beneath that memory—like a whisper—Jungwon's voice from the HYBE hallway echoed.

"Then get to know me. Because I'm not going anywhere."

The ache sharpened. She wiped at her eyes before she could stop herself. Not crying. Just... tired.

Yeah. That's what she'd tell herself. Just tired.
















34: Two Suns Can’t Share a Sky

It started small.

A blurry photo. A random tweet. Barely a few hundred likes in the first hour.

"Why does this look like they were about to kiss?? 👀 #SolarShock"

The image was grainy, clearly snapped in the chaos after the tournament win — Solar still in her jersey, head thrown back mid-laugh, Rascal's face turned toward her, the space between them sharp and charged.

At first, it was just fan noise. Then a popular K-pop meme page reposted it. Then a major eSports commentator quote-tweeted it.

Then it exploded.

By morning, #SolarShock and #SolarScandal were trending in tandem across Twitter and TikTok, sandwiched between fancam threads and conspiracy videos analyzing the "micro-expressions" in Solar's face like it was a CIA file.

Some people were charmed:

"She's living the dream. Kpop idol AND top-tier gamer?? ICONIC."

Some were vicious:

"Of course she'd flirt her way onto the team. Typical."

Some were flat-out delusional:

"Can we talk about how Rascal is clearly uncomfortable in that photo?"

The takes got hotter by the minute.

Solar saw it all from her phone in the practice studio—curled on the floor between rehearsals, hoodie zipped to her chin, surrounded by makeup wipes and half-full water bottles. Her group members were quietly stretching, checking choreography notes. But her hands were clenched around her phone.

She didn't remember leaning that close. She didn't remember not leaning that close. Yunjin sat beside her, one leg bent, the other stretched across the mat. "It's going viral, huh."

Solar didn't respond.

Yunjin looked at the photo, then at her. "You want me to leak something worse so it gets buried?"

"Don't tempt me," Solar muttered.

Yunjin clicked her tongue. "Idiots. As if you need to flirt to be Top 500."

The door opened just then, and Chaewon entered, handing out energy drinks. She passed one to Solar wordlessly, a small squeeze of the hand all she offered.

Solar took it. Later that night, she opened Discord.

Shock Team Chat:

[Super]: bro what is happening
[Smurf]: yall broke the internet
[Glister]: i didn't know ppl cared this much about team synergy 😭
[Nero]: called it. OTP energy. chaos always finds rascal.

Rascal didn't say anything for hours. When he finally replied, it wasn't in the team chat. It was private.

[Rascal]: you good?

Solar hesitated.

[Solar]: just tired of everyone acting like I can't exist without being someone's scandal.

The reply came slower this time.

[Rascal]: welcome to my world.

She almost laughed. Almost.

[Solar]: you're not mad?

Another pause.

[Rascal]: no. just don't want to be a headline.
[Rascal]: or a distraction.

Something about the last word made her chest ache.

[Solar]: you're not a distraction.

No reply. Ten minutes passed. Then twenty. Then an hour. He didn't answer.

And in the quiet, that ache in her chest thickened—not sharp, but dull. Like a bruise that hadn't fully formed.

She refreshed the trending page again. There it was, bright and bold:

#SolarShock – 3M tweets

Beneath it, a side-by-side photo edit: her laughing face and Rascal's half-smile frozen in grainy amber. Someone had captioned it:

"Sometimes it's not about the kiss. It's about the moment before it."

Solar closed the app. She didn't want the moment anymore. Not if it meant losing the people who made it matter.

Her screen was still glowing when her manager texted.

[Manager, 9:13 AM]
Come to 3F conference room. HYBE wants to speak with you.

No punctuation. No emoji. Just a summons.

Solar exhaled slowly. Her pulse didn't spike—she was too used to this now.

She stood up. Tucked her phone away. And walked out of the studio like a girl heading to war.

The walls of HYBE's meeting room were spotless white, lit by recessed lights that made everything feel colder than it probably was. Solar sat across the long table with her hands folded tightly in her lap, palms damp. The win still buzzed in her chest — the adrenaline from the tournament hadn't fully left her body — but here, under this light, it felt like something she wasn't allowed to celebrate.

Her manager, Jiwoo, sat beside her. The woman had always been gentle, usually the buffer between Solar and the higher-ups. But today, her expression was unreadable.

Across from them, two senior executives flipped through a slim folder with Solar's name printed on the tab. It held nothing new. Just a printout of tweets, headlines, Discord screencaps. The now-infamous photo was paperclipped at the front.

Blurry. Tilted. Clearly taken mid-celebration. Rascal's face was half-turned, Solar's hand still raised in a fist pump — and yet somehow, it looked unmistakably intimate. It had gone viral within hours.

Jiwoo cleared her throat. "We understand this photo is... misleading."

Solar didn't flinch, though her heart thudded once against her ribs.

One of the execs, a man in his mid-forties, finally spoke. "It's not about misleading. It's about optics."

He said it like a surgeon describing a tumor.

"You've worked hard to get here," the second exec, a woman with sharp red nails, added. "You're still in your first year. This is not the time to be splitting your image."

Solar sat straighter. "With respect, I'm not splitting anything. Le Sserafim is my priority."

"But the public perception," the woman pressed, "is shifting. You're being branded as a gamer-idol hybrid. Do you understand how difficult that is to control? To market?"

Jiwoo glanced sideways at Solar, a warning in her eyes.

The man dropped the folder on the table. "We're not saying don't play. We're saying be careful. Tone down the esports branding. No more jerseys in selfies. No streams during comeback season. Focus on the group."

There it was. Not a punishment — a leash.

Solar's fingers curled into her lap. "We just won a major tournament."

"Yes," the man acknowledged. "And that's commendable. But your stage is with Le Sserafim. Don't forget where you started."

The words landed harder than she expected. She nodded, lips tight.

The meeting ended without fanfare.

Jiwoo didn't say anything until they were back in the elevator. She pressed the button for the lobby and leaned against the mirrored wall.

"They're not wrong," she said softly. "But they're also scared. They don't know how to control something that doesn't fit the mold."

"I never asked to be controlled," Solar murmured.

Jiwoo gave her a look — sympathetic, but firm. "You signed up to be an idol. That is control. You just found a second world that lets you breathe."

The elevator dinged. The doors opened.

Solar stepped out and walked through the lobby like her shoes were filled with cement. For the first time since the win, the joy felt dulled. Like someone had draped a thin gray film over it, reminding her: you can shine, but only in the directions they point you.

As she pushed open the glass doors, her phone buzzed in her pocket.

[Super]: ur a fkn beast. just saying.
[Smurf]: you carried. that's our tracer queen 👑
[Rascal]: don't let it get u down

Solar stared at the last message.

She typed a reply.
Deleted it.
Typed again.

[Solar]: HYBE had a meeting. They're not thrilled.
[Solar]: I'm still allowed to play, just... quieter.
[Solar]: I'll see you later for scrims?

Three dots appeared. Disappeared. Then reappeared.

[Rascal]: yeah. see u then

Short. Simple. Safe.

She pocketed the phone and stepped into the sunlight, her own reflection faint in the building's mirrored glass.

For a second, she wondered which version of her it was — the idol, the gamer, or the girl no one had ever asked to become both.




Late that night, in a dim backstage lounge, a phone screen glowed too brightly in the dark.

Danielle scrolled through Twitter with one hand, her thumb twitching with barely concealed agitation. The comments moved too fast to read. But she didn't need to read them.

She knew exactly what they said.

She'd seen the photo a dozen times already. The blurry shot of Solar and that boy—Rascal?—in the tunnel backstage after the tournament. The lighting was grainy. Their faces weren't even fully visible.

But the tension in their body language was unmistakable. Too close. Too familiar. Too much.

She didn't blink as she watched the retweets tick higher. #SolarScandal. #SolarShock. Fan accounts reposting clips of her dive kills mid-match with captions like "Idol by day, assassin by night."

Danielle's jaw clenched. A pulse beat behind her eyes. That dull, slow ache she always got when something wasn't right. And nothing about Solar had ever felt right.

At least not to her.

She didn't understand it. Couldn't explain it. But something about that girl—her voice, her posture, her laugh—made Danielle feel like she was staring at a painting hung crooked on the wall. Pretty, but... wrong.

Maybe it was the way Jungwon had looked at her once, during that dumb HYBE mixer. Or maybe it was how Hanni always smiled when Solar's name came up in conversation.

Or maybe it was just that Solar walked through the world like she belonged in every room she entered.

Danielle's fingers hovered over her screen. Then typed.

[DM to @OnlySunsetsStan]
you were right. she's reckless.
might want to look into her schedule... lol.
rumor is she's meeting that guy again this week.

She hit send.

The fan account had over 82k followers. The kind that dug into private flights, hair stylist gossip, hidden tattoos. The kind that didn't care about rules.

Danielle tossed her phone onto the couch and leaned back, fingers laced over her stomach. She stared up at the ceiling. Across the room, Haerin glanced up from her book. "Everything okay?"

Danielle smiled sweetly. "Of course," she said. "Just watching the stars align."




Solar didn't expect to see him again so soon.

Not here, anyway—on the underground level of HYBE, where the halls were lit like hotel lobbies and always smelled faintly of lemon cleaner and ambition.

She was walking back from a choreography review meeting, her body stiff, hair pulled into a half-up ponytail that still glittered with residual stage makeup. She had been too tired to wash it off. Too tired to pretend.

The doors to the stairwell opened with a soft hiss. And there he was.

Jungwon stood by the vending machine at the end of the corridor, sipping from a bottle of water. Hood up. Headphones slung around his neck. His gaze lifted the moment she stepped into view—as if he already knew she was there.

Neither of them spoke right away. The last time they had seen each other, the world had still been reeling from the rain.

Solar's heart stuttered in her chest.

She didn't want to talk about the photo. Didn't want to talk about Rascal. Or the tournament. Or the way her manager had stared through her during their last meeting like she was suddenly a problem to be managed instead of an artist to be nurtured.

But Jungwon didn't ask. He just looked at her. Like he was trying to read her through the cracks in her armor.

"Busy week," he murmured finally, his voice low, unassuming.

She nodded, not trusting herself to answer.

He took a slow step toward her—close enough now that she could hear the click of his water bottle cap. Close enough that she could smell that familiar clean scent of his hoodie: cedarwood, sweat, and something indescribably him.

"Everyone's watching you," Jungwon said, eyes never leaving hers. "But I see you."

Solar's throat tightened. The words hit something she didn't know was exposed.

She looked down at the floor, then back up—startled by how steady his gaze still was. How unbothered. Not by the rumors, not by the pressure, not by her.

"What do you see?" she asked softly.

Jungwon paused. Not because he didn't have an answer—but because he wanted to be sure. Then, in a voice that was almost a whisper: "Someone who's trying not to fall apart."

She flinched. Not because it was cruel—but because it was true.

He didn't reach for her. Didn't offer comfort or questions or judgment. He just stood there—silent, unwavering, like gravity in human form.

And for one split second, Solar thought: If I reached for him right now, would he hold me?

But she didn't. Instead, she offered the only thing she could manage: a nod. Small. Almost imperceptible. Jungwon's expression softened—not quite a smile, but something gentler.

"I'll see you around," he said, stepping back into the hallway's shadows.

She watched him go until the elevator swallowed him whole.

And then she stood there a little longer, arms wrapped around herself—not cold, but trying to remember how to breathe.




Minji sat curled in the corner of the New Jeans dorm's rehearsal room, legs tucked under her, hoodie sleeves pulled over her hands.

She was supposed to be reviewing lines for a CF shoot tomorrow. Hanni was across the room, humming absently while scrolling on her phone. Haerin had a book open in her lap but hadn't turned a page in twenty minutes. Danielle was nowhere to be found.

Minji's iPad glowed in her lap.

On the screen was a tweet.

blurry pic but is this who i think it is?? #SolarShock 😭🔥

Attached: a photo taken from the crowd at the Overwatch Summer Cup. Fuzzy, zoomed-in. Solar and Rascal, standing a little too close just after the final match. Rascal was half-turned, Solar's head tilted, eyes lifted toward his—and the look on her face...

Minji couldn't stop staring at it.

Not because it was shocking. Because it looked real. Her stomach churned.

She didn't even understand why.

Solar wasn't hers. There had never been a her and Solar—not outside of messy what-ifs and late-night thoughts that she stuffed into the corners of her mind like lint in a pocket.
