They hadn't talked in weeks, not really. Not since that message Minji didn't send. Not since she'd stared at Solar's comeback teaser until she memorized the lighting in her eyes.

But still—something about this photo made her want to scream. She exited Twitter, opened her notes app, and started typing.

you looked really happy in that photo
i don't know why it makes me feel like this
maybe it's because i miss you
or maybe i'm just—

She stopped. Deleted the last line.

Started again.

i saw the picture. everyone has.
just wanted you to know you still looked like you

Still looked like you. Minji stared at the sentence until her vision blurred. She didn't send it. Didn't even save the draft.

Just sat there, screen dimming slowly in her lap, fingers curled into her sleeves like she could disappear into them.

Across the room, Hanni glanced up. "Hey. You good?"

Minji blinked.

"Yeah," she said, voice thin. "Just tired."

Hanni gave her a small smile, unconvinced, then looked away. Minji's gaze drifted back to the iPad screen, now dark. But the image was still burned into her mind.

Solar's face. Soft. Real. Looking at someone the way Minji had always wished she would look at her. She swallowed hard.

And let the silence press in around her.






35: Until Next Time

The rain had stopped sometime after dinner, but the Seoul sky still wore that heavy, slate-gray hue that made you forget the sun ever existed.

The doorbell rang once—sharp and sudden. Yunjin paused mid-step, eyes flicking to Solar. "You get it."

Solar exhaled slowly, brushing invisible lint off her hoodie before making her way down the hall. Her legs ached—leftover stiffness from comeback rehearsals earlier in the day—but her chest felt heavier than anything her muscles could carry.

When she opened the door, the San Francisco Shock were already grinning.

Super threw his arms wide like a sitcom uncle. "One last invasion!"

"We brought snacks," Smurf announced solemnly, holding up a crinkled plastic bag. "And our crippling attachment issues."

Behind them, Rascal stood with his hands deep in his hoodie pockets, a soft smile curling at the edges of his mouth. He didn't speak right away—just met her gaze with something careful and unreadable.

Solar stepped back to let them in.

The dorm filled quickly with voices, footsteps, and the chaotic shuffle of jackets being shed and shoes kicked off. Glister tripped over the entryway rug. Nero complained immediately about how "everything smells too clean again." Super made a beeline for Eunchae, announcing he had come to avenge his last Uno defeat.

It should've felt like déjà vu. It didn't. It felt like something slipping through her fingers.

In the kitchen, Chaewon and Yunjin arranged fruit plates with an air of ritual. Eunchae was already pulling games out of the cabinet, stacking them with dramatic flair. The lights were dimmed, candles flickering low, casting golden shadows across the shelves lined with trophies and fan letters. Kazuha leaned against the counter, arms crossed, watching the madness with quiet amusement.

But it was Sakura who stood frozen in the hallway. Nero walked past her without looking. Or maybe he did look—just not directly.

Solar saw it.

She watched Nero's gaze flick toward Sakura for half a second too long. Watched Sakura's posture stiffen, just slightly, like someone catching a scent from a past life. Neither of them said a word.

Rascal joined her near the couch. "I've seen three different people almost trip over your stepstool," he murmured. "This place is a death trap."

Solar snorted. "We're idols. We fear nothing except carbs and uneven lighting."

He laughed, and it was low and genuine and warmer than she expected.

The room blurred with noise again—Glister shouting that the charades rules were rigged, Super announcing he had "a sixth sense for idol lies," Eunchae claiming she could "body any of them in League if HYBE unbanned her IP."

And through it all, Solar sat on the floor with her legs folded beneath her, Rascal beside her, their shoulders not quite touching. She didn't have to look at him to feel that he wasn't looking anywhere else.

Not yet. Not while she was this close.

"Wanna hide in my room for a minute?" Solar asked, voice light, almost playful—except it wasn't. Not really.

Rascal blinked at her, the sound of Super loudly explaining the rules of some board game echoing faintly from the living room. He tilted his head just slightly, then nodded once.

"Yeah. Sure."

She led him down the hall in silence. Her fingers trembled on the doorknob. She wasn't sure why.

When the door shut behind them, the noise softened into a faraway hum. Just like before, the room held its quiet glow—rose-colored LEDs casting a warm haze across the walls. Posters, trophies, the faint smell of her shampoo lingering in the air. It was her space. Her bubble. Her only place to exhale.

Rascal hovered just inside the doorway like he wasn't sure if he was allowed to fully enter. He looked different in this light—softer around the edges. His hoodie hung loose over his frame, sleeves pushed up to reveal the faded red marks on his wrists where his mouse strap always dug in during long scrims.

Solar sat on the edge of her bed and patted the space beside her.

"You can sit, you know," she said, forcing a tiny smile.

Rascal exhaled a soft laugh through his nose. "Wasn't sure if I'd get vaporized by idol energy."

"You've been here before."

"Yeah," he murmured. "And nearly short-circuited at the sight of your MAMA trophy."

He sat beside her. Not too close. Not far enough to feel safe. They sat there for a while without speaking. Not awkward. Just heavy. Finally, Solar broke the silence.

"You look tired," she said softly.

He shrugged, eyes fixed on the bookshelf across the room. "Everyone always looks tired next to you."

"That's not true."

He tilted his head toward her. "You're glowing. Even when you're burnt out."

She let the compliment hang there. Didn't smile. Didn't deflect. Just breathed through the weight of it.

"I don't know how to stop," she whispered after a while. "Even when it hurts. Even when I feel like I'm breaking apart."

Rascal didn't move. His voice, when it came, was lower than she expected.

"You think I don't get that?"

She turned toward him.

He wasn't looking at her. Not fully. But the pain in his expression was so plain, it felt like it cracked something open in the room.

"Sometimes I don't even know why I'm still playing," he said. "It's not fun like it used to be. It's pressure. It's people screaming your name one day and ripping you apart the next. It's... loneliness. Even when you're on a team, sometimes you feel like you're the only one drowning."

Solar's throat tightened.

"I get it," she said quietly. "I really, really get it."

He finally met her gaze. And something shifted.

Rascal leaned back slightly, arms resting on his knees. "I've dated people who wanted the version of me they saw in interviews. The funny guy. The cracked DPS. But none of them ever made me feel like... me. Like I could just exist without explaining myself."

Solar's pulse skittered. "Have you... been hurt?"

Rascal hesitated. Just for a second.

"Yeah," he said, voice like a paper cut. "A couple times. Makes you think twice about trying again."

Silence fell again. But now, it pulsed with something unspoken.

Solar's hands curled in her lap. "I've only really been with one person. My ex."

She didn't say his name. She didn't need to.

Rascal didn't ask. Just waited.

"He made me feel like I had to earn being loved," she said, eyes glassy. "Like if I wasn't perfect, or quiet, or apologetic enough, I didn't deserve to be wanted. And now, even when people are good to me, I don't... know what to do with it."

Her voice cracked at the end.

Rascal turned toward her fully now, expression so gentle it made her want to cry. He didn't reach for her. Didn't try to fix it. Just sat in it with her.

"You don't have to be perfect with me," he said.

Tears slipped down her cheeks before she realized they were coming.

"I don't even know what I am to you," she admitted. "I don't know if I'm a teammate, or a crush, or just some idol you're gonna forget when you fly back to San Francisco."

"You're not just anything," Rascal said. His voice was firm now. Quiet, but sure. "I think about you when I wake up. When I queue. When you're not online, I wonder if you're okay. I—"

He broke off. Swallowed.

"I don't know what this is," he said. "But I know I care about you. A lot. And I've been trying so hard not to, because I see how much you're carrying, and I don't want to be one more thing on your back."

Solar's breath hitched.

"You're not," she whispered.

He nodded. "Still. I know I'm not the end of your story. I know there's... someone else, too."

She looked at him sharply, startled.

"I've seen the photos," he added gently. "The ones with the guy from Enhypen."

"Jungwon," she whispered.

"Yeah."

Another silence.

Then, Solar reached out and touched his hand—just barely. Her fingers brushed his knuckles, trembling.

"I don't know what I want," she confessed. "But I want you to stay. Just... stay in my life, okay?"

Rascal turned his hand over and squeezed hers.

"I'm not going anywhere."

They didn't kiss. They didn't need to. Because this? This was something real. Something deeper than timing or titles.

Something that might not last—but mattered anyway. And for now, that was enough.




The dorm was quiet the next morning.

Too quiet.

The San Francisco Shock boys had flown back out at sunrise—Super sending a blurry photo of his passport with the caption "tell your floor i'll miss her" followed by two broken heart emojis. Glister had messaged the group chat at the airport, typing "I already miss Eunchae's chaos" while Nero threatened to throw his phone into the Pacific if anyone added him to another KakaoTalk group.

Rascal hadn't messaged at all.

But Solar could still feel the warmth of the night before, humming faintly under her skin like a fading ember. The laughter. The teasing. The absurdity of watching Super argue over a plushie with Chaewon while Smurf quietly shared orange slices with Kazuha. Even the awkward tension between Sakura and Nero—barely perceptible to most—had felt like something real. Something human.

For a moment, just one, everything had felt okay.

Like the two versions of herself—the idol and the gamer—had stopped fighting. Like maybe she could hold both. Maybe she didn't have to keep choosing.

But the morning after tasted bitter.

And she felt the familiar tug in her chest: the quiet dread that peace never lasted.

Solar stood in the Le Sserafim practice room, alone. The lights above buzzed faintly, casting a sterile white glow across the hardwood floor. The mirrored wall reflected her back at herself—a little hunched, still wearing the same hoodie from last night, hair in a loose bun, eyes shadowed with exhaustion.

She hadn't meant to come here early. But her body had moved before her mind caught up.

The room still smelled faintly like citrus cleaner and old perfume. She walked toward the stereo shelf to grab a water bottle she'd left behind.

And stopped.

It was small. So small it could've been nothing.

But there—resting just barely on the edge of the sound system—was a pack of sour peach rings.

Her favorite.

The exact brand. The kind they used to share on park benches, in stairwells, in the shadowed corners of high school classrooms they weren't supposed to be in. The kind she hadn't bought since she left him behind.

Her breath caught in her throat.

No one else knew.

Not the other girls. Not even Yunjin. Not Rascal. Not anyone on Earth but Jihoon.

She picked it up with two fingers like it might burn her.

The packaging crinkled in the silence, soft and sharp at once.

It was unopened.

And on the back—scrawled in pen—was a phone number.

Not his old one.

A new one.

She felt the blood drain from her face.

Her hands curled tightly around the pack until the plastic dug into her palm. She stood there frozen, a thousand thoughts firing at once, all of them screaming:

He was here.

She hadn't told anyone her rehearsal schedule this morning. No one was supposed to be in the building this early. And yet, the candy had been left perfectly in place. Not tossed. Not hidden.

Displayed.

She set it back down. Carefully. Like it might explode.

And then she stepped away.

One step.

Then another.

Her throat tightened. She didn't cry. She couldn't. Not here. Not yet.

But deep inside her chest, something started to rattle. Something old and ugly and clawing. That sick twist of control disguised as romance. That voice she thought she'd buried for good:

You texted me, remember?

Jihoon didn't have to show up to hurt her.

He just had to leave reminders.

Solar pulled her phone from her pocket and took a photo of the peach rings. Just one.

For now, she had a schedule to follow. A comeback to prepare for. A public face to wear.

But in her chest, her ribs felt too tight. Like the past had reached forward and curled its fingers around her throat.

She took a breath.

And walked back toward the mirror.

Practice would start soon.

And Jihoon?

Jihoon had made himself very clear.




The HYBE parking garage was colder than she remembered.

The kind of cold that seeped through the concrete and into your shoes, humming quietly in your bones. Solar didn't know why she came down here. Maybe just to breathe. Maybe to vanish for five minutes before the next wave of makeup, choreography, Discord calls.

The day had been nonstop. Her head still echoed with stage instructions and scrim stats. She leaned back against the cool metal of a parked van and closed her eyes.

Footsteps echoed down the row. Soft. Steady.

She didn't open her eyes—until the steps stopped beside her.

"Twice in one week," Jungwon murmured.

Her chest tightened.

She turned her head slightly. He stood a few feet away, hoodie up again like always, hands buried deep in his pockets. There was something about the way he looked at her—like she was a puzzle he'd already decided was worth solving.

"You stalking me now?" she asked lightly, the sarcasm weak at the edges.

He didn't smile. Just tilted his head. "You don't look okay."

"I'm fine," she replied too fast. Too sharp.

Jungwon didn't argue.

Instead, he looked out across the empty rows of cars. The silence between them stretched, long and strange. But not uncomfortable.

When he finally spoke, his voice was soft—barely above the hum of the lights overhead.

"Everyone's watching you," he said. "Waiting for you to mess up. Or shine. Or both."

Solar inhaled, slow and careful.

He glanced at her again, eyes unreadable. "But I pay attention."

She froze.

It wasn't flirtation. It wasn't pity. It was something else. Something more dangerous.

"I see the way you flinch when someone raises their voice," he added. "How you pretend to be unfazed, even when your hands say otherwise."

Her pulse jumped. "Don't psychoanalyze me."

"I'm not," he said simply. "I just... notice."

And maybe that was worse.

Because she wasn't used to being seen like that. She was used to being performed for. Consumed. Watched, not witnessed.

"What do you want from me?" she asked, quieter now.

Jungwon stepped forward, not closing the space entirely, but enough to make her feel it.

"I don't know," he admitted. "I just know I don't want to stop talking to you."

Her breath caught. And then—he stepped back. Gave her space like it was a gift. Like he wasn't going to demand anything she wasn't ready to give.

"Rest tonight," he said. "You've done enough."

She didn't reply right away. Her throat was tight. But she watched him go. And for the first time all day, she didn't feel the cold.




The dorm was quieter than it had any right to be.

Solar stood in her room, door closed, lights dim. The hum of the city filtered in faintly through the window, a reminder that the world outside never really stopped. But inside—it was still. Still in a way that felt heavy.

The kind of still that made you think too much.

Her phone was face down on the desk. Notifications off. Rascal hadn't messaged since the Shock boys left. She didn't blame him. They'd left without a big goodbye—just sleepy waves and tired smiles and one last joke from Super about stealing the pink beanbag chair.

The visit already felt like a dream.

The way they'd sprawled on her floor like they belonged there. The way Nero had glared at Sakura across the room, and Sakura had smirked like she knew something he didn't. The way Rascal had looked at her in the glow of her bedroom LED lights and whispered things he never would've said in voice chat.

It had all happened. And now it was gone.

Solar sat on the edge of her bed and curled her legs underneath her. She was still wearing her tournament jersey. Still hadn't taken off her makeup. Her hoodie—his hoodie—was folded neatly on her chair. Like a line she wasn't sure she could cross again.

Because that kiss? It hadn't left her.

Not when she washed her face. Not when she brushed her teeth. Not when she opened her notes app and tried to write lyrics about anything else.

It was still there. In the press of their closeness. In the way his voice had cracked just slightly when he said, "You deserve someone who gets all of you."

And she did. But something inside her—something old, cracked, and careful—still didn't believe it.

A ping lit her screen. A message. From Minji. Not a long one. Just three words.

Saw the photo.

Solar stared at it. Her thumb hovered. Another message followed, this one slower, hesitant.

Are you okay?

She didn't answer. Not yet. Instead, she let the weight of the question sit with her. Because the truth was, she didn't know. She felt... everything. Too much.

Exhaustion clung to her like a second skin. Her muscles throbbed from choreography, her eyes ached from stage lights, her hands were still sore from gripping a mouse all afternoon.

She was splitting herself into halves. Into quarters. Into fragments.

And still—she wanted more. More music. More matches. More moments where her heart forgot to be careful. But more meant burning.

And she could feel the edges of herself crisping already.

She dropped her head into her hands.

It wasn't about Rascal. Or Jungwon. Or Minji. Not really.

It was about the way she had carved herself into someone who could survive all this and forgot to ask if she was allowed to want softness too.

She sat in silence for a long time. Then reached for her headset.

The Discord server blinked online. Empty. Quiet.

Solar logged in anyway.

Not to scrim.

Just to hear the silence where Rascal's laugh used to be.

















36: No Place to Hide


The HYBE building was nearly empty.

It was past midnight, and the usual hum of staff, managers, and trainees had long faded into silence. Even the motion-activated lights in the hallway flickered with hesitation, casting elongated shadows on the white tile floor.

Solar didn't know what had compelled her to come back.

She told herself it was to grab her lyric notebook—the one she'd left behind in the practice room earlier that day. But in truth, she just hadn't wanted to go home yet. Not to the dorm. Not to her bed. Not to the weight of her thoughts.

She was still wearing her Shock jersey under her oversized hoodie, her makeup smudged from the long day, hair pulled up in a hasty bun. Her body ached, but her mind wouldn't quiet. Not after the tournament. Not after the comeback. Not after the kiss she still hadn't talked to anyone about.

The elevator doors opened with a soft ding.

The floor was quiet—eerily so. Even the vending machine down the hall had powered off, its usual fluorescent glow gone. Solar padded down the hallway, her sneakers nearly silent against the polished tile.

She opened the practice room door with a gentle push.

The lights were still on inside, dimmed to low rehearsal mode. Her water bottle was on the floor by the mirrors. Her lyric notebook sat on the speaker.

She stepped in, letting the door close behind her with a soft click.

It was only when she turned back toward the mirrors that she saw him.

Jihoon.

Sitting casually on the windowsill, legs stretched out, head tilted like he'd been waiting.

Solar froze.

For a second, she thought her body might stop working entirely. Like her lungs had forgotten how to breathe. Her skin went cold.

"What are you doing here," she whispered, more statement than question.

Jihoon's smile was slow. Familiar. Coated in something that felt like oil in her throat. "Nice to see you too," he drawled. "You always rehearse this late?"

Solar stepped back, fingers brushing against the door handle behind her.

"Get out."

"Not even gonna say hi?" he asked, standing now, brushing invisible lint from his jacket. "C'mon, Sol. We used to spend every night in rooms like this."

"You need to leave," she repeated, louder this time.

His eyes flicked to her hands, her posture, her visible shake.

"You're scared," he states, and for a moment, something behind his voice twisted. "Why are you scared of me?"

"Because you taught me to be," she snapped.

The silence that followed was thick. Jihoon didn't flinch. But the smile faded from his lips.

"You texted me," he said quietly. "That night in the hotel."

Solar's mouth went dry. "That was a mistake."

"No," he said, stepping forward. "It was real. That was you. The real you. Not this painted version HYBE parades around. Not the gamer girl everyone's shipping with her teammate. Just you. The one who used to beg me not to leave."

Her back hit the mirror. Her chest rose and fell in sharp, uneven waves. "You need to leave. Now."

Jihoon paused. For a moment, it looked like he might argue. But then, slowly, he reached into his jacket and pulled out a single folded sheet of paper. He walked to the speaker. Set it gently on top of her lyric notebook.

"I'm not here to fight," he murmured. "Just wanted to remind you, I never really left."

Then he turned and walked out, the door closing behind him with a soft hiss. Solar didn't move for a full minute. She couldn't.

Her fingers trembled. Her breathing came in shallow bursts. It felt like her skin wasn't hers. She stepped forward—slowly—and picked up the folded paper.

Inside was a photo. Not a threatening one. Not explicit. Just a picture. Her. From years ago.

Sixteen. In a high school uniform. Sitting on the bleachers behind their old campus. Smiling at something off-camera.

She hadn't even known this photo existed. Her hands shook. The message was clear: I still see you. I still have parts of you you've forgotten.

The room felt too small. Too hot. Too quiet. Solar dropped the paper like it had burned her and fled.

Her breath caught as she hit the hallway, practically sprinting toward the elevator. She didn't even realize she was crying until she caught her reflection in the elevator doors—mascara smudged, cheeks flushed, eyes wide with something close to panic.

She didn't know where she was going. But somehow, her fingers were already moving.

Typing.

Instinct.

And just like that—

JUNGWON: [shared location pin] dorm's this one. top floor. back elevator's open.

Solar didn't question how he knew. She just ran.







It took Solar longer than it should have to find the Enhypen dorm.

Her limbs moved on instinct. Her phone buzzed again with missed messages, most from Yunjin—where are you, please call me, are you okay—but she didn't have the words. Not yet. Not even for Yunjin.

When she reached the address Jungwon had sent in a single text—"Third floor. Just knock."—she stood in the hallway for nearly a full minute, breath trembling in her throat.

What was she doing?

She was Solar. Idol. Public figure. Professional. She wasn't supposed to show up like this, messy and shaken and not okay.

But she'd already pressed the doorbell. And before she could regret it, it opened.

Jungwon stood there, hoodie sleeves pushed to his elbows, hair damp like he'd just come from practice. His expression was calm, but his eyes—his eyes locked onto hers with surgical precision. Like they saw everything.

He stepped aside without a word. Solar entered quietly.

The dorm was dimly lit, soft yellow glow spilling across the floor from a kitchen light. It was clean, surprisingly so, but lived-in—jackets on chair backs, a half-finished drink on the counter. Somewhere down the hall, she heard muffled voices, laughter behind closed doors. The other boys must've been in their rooms.

"Come on," Jungwon said softly.

She followed him into the small living room. He didn't press her. Didn't ask what happened. He just nodded toward the couch.

Solar sank into it like she'd run ten miles barefoot. Her legs shook. Her hands felt numb. Her eyes were dry, but only because she'd cried too much already. She didn't know what she looked like. Mascara half-smeared. Hoodie damp from rain. Hair sticking to her temples. She wanted to hide—but she didn't.

Because Jungwon didn't flinch.

He just sat beside her. Not too close. But near enough that she could feel the warmth radiating off of him. That anchoring kind of heat.

She stared at the floor.

"It was him," she said at last.

Jungwon's voice was quiet. "Jihoon."

She nodded. A beat passed.

Then he asked, "Did he touch you?"

Solar's breath caught. "No... Just words. But the kind that... stay."

He didn't speak right away. But when he did, his voice was so soft it made her chest hurt.
"I don't like the way he looks at you."

Solar blinked, pulse still unsteady. Her voice came quieter than she expected.
"You remember him."

It wasn't a question. Just a quiet truth sitting between them.

Jungwon's jaw flexed. "Hard to forget." A pause. His next words were edged in steel.
"Especially after that night in the rain."

Her stomach twisted. She remembered it vividly—how Jungwon had stepped into Jihoon's path like a shadow with something sharp in his eyes, and how Jaehyun hadn't even needed to speak to make Jihoon take a step back. It had been raining so hard that night. The only thing louder than the storm had been the silence.

"He came back," she whispered. "I don't know why. Maybe just to remind me that he still can."

Jungwon's eyes were steady now, locked on hers.
"You don't owe him anything. Not your fear. Not your thoughts. Not even your silence."

Solar looked down at her hands. They were trembling, shaking, wouldn't stop. "I texted him... after the hotel, I don't know why... I tried to erase it before it delivered but — he saw it."

Jungwon didn't react the way she'd expected. He didn't even recoil, didn't try to ask why.

He just said, "You were hurting. That doesn't mean you wanted him back."

"I hate that he still knows how to find me," she whispered.

"He doesn't," Jungwon said, and this time his voice sharpened, just a little. "He doesn't know you. He just knows where to look. That's not the same."

And then—

She broke.

Not loudly. Not dramatically. But something in her just... cracked. Her body slumped forward and she clutched her own elbows like they were the only things keeping her upright. Her shoulders shook. Her breath hitched. And still, she didn't cry. Not again. She was too tired. Too wrung out.

And Jungwon moved.

He reached for her—not with hesitation, but with certainty. Not a romantic gesture. Not even a grand one.

Just... a hand. Gently on her back.

Then his other hand—folding lightly over hers.

And the moment their skin touched, Solar felt it.

That pull. That thing she'd been trying to ignore for weeks. The silent gravity of it. His hands were warm. Steady. And she—she was shaking like a leaf.

Her breath caught in her throat. "You're safe here," he assures, stern.

And then he pulled her in. Not into a kiss. Not into anything rushed or tangled. Just an embrace. Solar froze at first. Then slowly—so slowly—let herself lean.

Into his shoulder. Into the quiet. Into the feeling of someone holding her without asking for anything in return.

Jungwon's arms were strong. Not overly tight. Just enough to say I've got you.

And for the first time in what felt like years, Solar let herself believe it. No spotlights. No stage. No scrims. No pretending.

Just warmth. Just safety. Just this.

Her fingers curled into the hem of his hoodie. She didn't mean to. But she needed to hold onto something.

He let her.

And in that moment, something shifted.

Because this wasn't easy.

It wasn't light or casual or safe.

It was slow-burning. Deep-running. The kind of connection that scares you because you know—you just know—that once you touch it, you'll never forget how it feels.

Jungwon didn't speak again. He didn't have to. Because Solar, even in silence, heard everything in the way he held her.

And the way she stayed.




It was barely 4 a.m. when Solar stepped out of the Enhypen dorm.

The Seoul night was thick with humidity, the street quiet and slick with leftover rain. The sky hadn't yet decided whether it was night or morning, stuck in that in-between haze that made everything feel surreal.

She pulled the hood of her jacket up and kept her head down, grateful for the mask still covering her face. Jungwon had offered to walk her out, but she'd gently declined. He'd already done enough. She didn't trust herself not to stay if he looked at her again.

The moment in his arms lingered like smoke in her lungs.

It had been... secure.

Too secure. Like standing still at the edge of something massive and terrifying, knowing that if she jumped, she wouldn't be the same again.

But she hadn't jumped. Not yet. The quiet buzz of her phone broke the moment. A new message—this one from Yunjin... again. She'd clearly been worried sick.

where are you. please answer.

She replied quickly.
On my way. I'm okay. Tell Chaewon not to call the cops.

Yunjin's response came in seconds.
you BETTER be okay. when you get back I'm waking you up with a lecture and a frying pan.

Solar smiled faintly and turned the corner of the building. That's when it happened.

A single flash—quiet, quick, sharp.

She froze.

Her eyes darted upward. The apartment complex across the street had a small balcony on the third floor. Curtains fluttered. A silhouette moved.

Then stilled. Gone. It could've been anything. A passing car. A neighbor heading out early. The wind.

But Solar knew.

She knew when something was wrong. And this? This was wrong.

Her stomach twisted, but she forced herself forward. Kept walking. Kept her head down. Don't panic. Don't make a scene.

Don't let them see you scared.

It didn't matter.

By noon, the damage had been done.

Twitter. Dispatch. Every gossip site from Seoul to New York.

The headline was already trending:

"SOLAR LEAVING ENHYPEN DORM — Secret Relationship with Jungwon?"

Hashtags exploded.

#SolarScandal
#SolarShock
#HYBEdating
#JungwonProtectHer
#StayOutOfOurDorm

The photos weren't crystal clear, but that almost made them worse. The blur. The grain. The shadows. All of it left just enough room for imagination to fill the gaps.

And imagination, as the internet proved, was a brutal thing.

One photo showed Solar stepping out of the building, hood up. Another captured the faint outline of her back, the curve of her shoulder, the time-stamp glaring: 4:03 a.m.

The comments ranged from furious to feral:

"Typical idol privilege. She thinks she can do whatever she wants."
"She's going to ruin Jungwon's reputation."
"I KNEW she was using the Shock for clout."
"If she breaks Jungwon's heart I will personally show up at HYBE."
"She's brave for following both her passions. Let her live."
"Maybe she just had a sleepover. Y'all need to breathe."

Solar stared at her screen, feeling nothing and everything all at once. It was happening again.

That slow, inevitable spiral. One photo. One second. And now the headlines weren't about her skills or her victories. They were about who she was seen with.

They always were.

Somewhere across the world - San Francisco, California, in a coffee shop with warm lighting and soft jazz, Rascal saw the post.

He didn't gasp. Didn't flinch. He just stared.

The picture wasn't shocking. He recognized the dorm. He recognized her. His chest ached in a dull, familiar way.

Super leaned over his shoulder. "Is that—?"

Rascal locked his phone and slid it face down.

"Yup," he said simply.

Super frowned. "Dude..."

"I'm fine." He wasn't. But he also wasn't mad.

Because he'd seen it coming. Not the photo. Not the timing. But the truth behind it.

Solar's heart had always been louder than her words. And Rascal had heard it—every beat of it—when she didn't even know she was speaking.

He'd felt it the night she kissed him. And he'd known then, in the deepest part of him, that she wasn't meant to be his. He was the safety.

Jungwon was the storm.

And Solar? She was caught between the two. Still, it didn't mean the picture didn't hurt. It didn't mean the what-if didn't linger.

Super clapped a hand on his back. "You wanna talk about it?"

Rascal stared out the window, eyes unfocused. Then, softly:

"No. But thanks."

He smiled. The same tired, small smile he'd given Solar that night in her hallway.

And just like then, it meant everything.

Because Rascal still wasn't angry. He was just quietly breaking.







The dorm was too quiet.

Minji sat on the floor of the New Jeans practice room long after everyone else had gone home. Her legs were stretched out in front of her, hair tied in a messy knot at the base of her neck, a hoodie draped loose around her shoulders. Her phone rested screen-up on the floor beside her.

It buzzed again.

She didn't reach for it this time. She didn't need to. She knew what it was.

The photo was everywhere.

A grainy shot taken in the early hours of the morning. Solar—hood up, head down—leaving the Enhypen dorm. Her face wasn't fully visible, but it didn't matter. The world had already decided.

#SolarEnhypen
#JungWonAndOnly
#ScandalOrLove

And the worst part?

Jungwon hadn't denied it.

Neither had Solar.

Minji's chest ached with something she didn't have the words for. It wasn't jealousy. Not entirely. It was... grief. For a version of things that never got a chance to be real.

She pulled her phone into her lap and opened their chat.

solar 🍓🌙
Still pinned. Still unread.

She stared at the blinking cursor in the message box.

Typed.

You looked tired in that photo. Everyone saw it. But they still called you names.

Backspaced.

Typed again.

I keep trying not to care. But I do. And I hate that I do.

Deleted that, too.

Her thumb hovered.

She tried one more time.

It's not about him. It's you. It's always been you.

She didn't send it.

She never did.

Instead, she sat back, locked her phone, and pressed her head against the mirror behind her. Her own reflection stared back—sharp lines, tired eyes, a practiced calm.

Minji didn't cry. Not visibly. Not often.

But her hands trembled slightly in her lap.

And when she finally stood to leave, she felt like a storm held together only by skin.














37: The World’s Watching

The group chat lit up just past midnight.

RASCAL: yo
RASCAL: everyone online rn?
SUPER: bro it's literally tomorrow
GLISTER: we just got home??
SMURF: i'm in bed
NERO: what did you break
RASCAL: nothing
RASCAL: i want us to run comp tonight
SUPER: ???
SMURF: is this a trap
RASCAL: no
RASCAL: solar's online
RASCAL: she's not saying anything but you saw the news
RASCAL: i just thought we could game. make it normal again
NERO: ah
GLISTER: right.
SUPER: wait was that photo actually real?
SMURF: doesn't matter
NERO: yeah. not our business
GLISTER: but she looked really... tired
RASCAL: yeah
RASCAL: so don't be weird
RASCAL: don't ask anything
RASCAL: just get on
RASCAL: she needs to feel like this is still hers
SUPER: damn
SUPER: okay. gimme 5
SMURF: i'm logging in now
NERO: queue hell, here we come
GLISTER: i'll start the lobby

Rascal stared at the chat for a moment, watching their status lights shift one by one from gray to green.

He didn't know if Solar would join. But he knew she'd seen the messages. And even if she didn't reply... even if she ghosted every one of them...

At least she'd know they were here. Waiting. Ready. Rascal clicked on her name. Typed one line. Deleted it. Rewrote it.

RASCAL → SOLAR: you're still cracked. no scandal changes that.
RASCAL: you should come carry us.

He hit send.

Then he leaned back in his chair, tugged his hoodie sleeves over his hands, and waited.

The message came in just as Solar was slipping off her makeup.

Her reflection in the mirror was tired—mascara smudged, lips pale, the faint bruising under her eyes uncomfortably prominent. She looked like someone who was trying to hold it all together and knew she wasn't fooling anyone anymore.

Her phone buzzed once.

RASCAL → you: you're still cracked. no scandal changes that.
you should come carry us.

She stared at the screen.

It wasn't a long message. It wasn't flowery or dramatic. But the second she read it, something in her chest gave.

They were online. They wanted her there.

She hadn't touched Discord since the photo dropped. Not because she didn't want to—but because she was scared. Scared they'd bring it up. Scared they'd ask if she was okay. Scared they'd see her differently now. As a scandal, not a teammate.

Her thumb hovered over the app. She hesitated.

But then another ping came through—this time in the team server.

SMURF: solar! we saved your seat!
SUPER: i threw on the lucky skin just for u
GLISTER: please carry us
NERO: please carry him specifically
RASCAL: you comin or what

No questions. No pity.

Just the usual nonsense. The kind that meant she wasn't a headline to them. She was just... Solar. Their DPS. Their chaos generator. Their cracked teammate who happened to also be an idol.

She opened Discord. The voice channel was already full of chatter.

"Bro, if you pick Hanzo one more time—"
"Don't tell me how to live my life!"
"I'm literally begging you to just touch the objective once."
"Smurf blink twice if you're being held hostage."

Solar joined quietly, her headset clicking into place. There was a beat of silence—just one.

Then:

"SOLAR!" Super practically screamed.

"Finally," Smurf laughed.

"Oh thank god," Glister said, genuine relief in his voice. "We're saved."

"Queueing," Rascal said simply.

Solar's throat tightened.

No one said her name like it was breaking news. No one asked about the photos. No one mentioned the rumors, the trending tags, the articles.

Instead:

"Alright," Super barked like a fake coach. "Game plan. Nero throws, Rascal carries, Glister panics, Smurf is wholesome, and Solar does everything."

"Accurate," Rascal muttered.

Solar snorted. It was barely a sound—but it was a sound. They launched into their first match.

It wasn't perfect. The ping was weird. Nero started arguing with someone's mic echo. Glister accidentally ulted into a wall. Super died screaming. Rascal called himself "the GOAT" every five minutes and still got out-DPSed.

But none of it mattered. Because they weren't talking about her life. They were playing.

And somewhere between maps, Solar felt her muscles start to relax. The weight on her ribs loosened. Her laugh came out easier. Her hands steadied on the mouse.

This—this chaos, this comfort—was hers. She might've been an idol. A gamer. A headline. A question.

But right now? She was just one of them. And god, she needed that more than she'd realized.




The lights backstage were harsh and clinical—designed more for function than comfort. But Le Sserafim moved through them like the hallway was a runway, hair curled and glossy, glitter already catching in the creases of their eyelids.

Solar pulled her hoodie tighter around her frame, still flushed from the night before. The comfort of the Shock boys lingered like a balm on her skin. But it didn't protect her from the sound of stylists shouting, earpieces crackling, and music blaring from nearby rooms.

"Alright," Yunjin said, adjusting her mic pack as she sidled up beside Solar. "You slept, right?"

"Define 'slept,'" Solar muttered, fighting a yawn.

Yunjin gave her a look. "Don't make me mom you in front of the staff."

Solar raised her hands in surrender, but the tired smile on her face betrayed her. "I got a few hours. I'm fine."

"Fine," Yunjin echoed dryly. "The most suspicious word in the world."

Chaewon joined them moments later, elegant as ever despite balancing a protein bar in one hand and a clipboard in the other. "We're on after Enhypen's prerecord. Be ready in fifteen."

Solar blinked. "Enhypen's here?"

"Of course they are," Yunjin said, tossing her hair. "This whole building's a K-pop traffic jam."

Something in Solar twisted. But she pushed the thought down. Jungwon wasn't the only member of Enhypen, after all. She could go five minutes without being weird about it.

Probably.

She followed the girls to the dressing room and took her usual corner seat beside the window. The stylist flitted around her, curling the loose strands around her face and dabbing foundation along her jaw. She half-listened to Eunchae trying to get someone to trade lunch bento flavors.

Then she glanced to the side—toward Sakura.

The oldest member of the group sat on the small loveseat near the mirror wall, legs tucked beneath her, expression unreadable. Her phone was tilted away from the others, fingers flying across the keyboard.

Something about her posture was... off.

It wasn't the texting. It was the intensity. The way her eyes narrowed ever so slightly. The way her thumbs moved like she was arguing—or worse, defending. She didn't look like someone replying to a group chat or posting on social.

Then Solar noticed the app open on her screen.

Discord.

Her breath hitched.

Sakura didn't game. Not that Solar knew of. Not even casually. And yet... the unmistakable dark layout of the Discord interface was right there, glowing in her hands.

Before Solar could say anything, Kazuha passed behind her with a soft, "You good?"

She nodded instinctively. When she looked again, Sakura had already locked her phone.

"Ready to kill it?" Sakura asked, tone light as she stood and stretched.

Solar blinked. "Always."

But something unsettled lingered in her ribs. Because Sakura smiled—but it didn't quite reach her eyes. And Discord? Discord didn't belong here.

"Le Sserafim, standby in ten!"

The call came over the speaker just as the girls stepped into the hallway outside their green room. Solar adjusted the high-waisted belt of her Antifragile stage outfit, boots clicking softly on the polished floor. Behind her, Yunjin was fixing a strand of Chaewon's hair while Eunchae babbled about a vending machine incident that somehow involved a stuck bag of shrimp chips and a threat of violence.

The moment Solar turned the corner toward the stage entrance, she stopped.

So did the rest of them.

Enhypen stood a few feet ahead—some leaning against the wall, some clustered around their manager, dressed head to toe in sleek, dark performance gear. Their vibe was easygoing but sharp, like a pack of wolves pretending to be housepets. Jungwon stood near the front, head slightly tilted, black earpiece glinting under the fluorescent lights.

Sunghoon was the first to speak.

"Oh? It's the Antifragile girls," he said with a crooked grin, raising a brow. "Are we gonna get a sneak peek?"

Eunchae immediately grinned back. "Only if you survive my high kick."

Heeseung chuckled. "No spoilers. I'm here to witness stage destruction in real time."

Chaewon stepped forward with her trademark poise, nodding politely. "Good set."

"You too," Jay replied smoothly. "That prerecord was fire."

Solar's gaze flicked across each of them. She hadn't seen them like this before—up close, as boys, not silhouettes in headlines or faces in choreography.

Then her eyes met Jungwon's. Only for a second. But it hit her anyway.

His lips parted slightly like he might say something—but Sunoo elbowed him, laughing at some offhand comment, and the spell broke.

Kazuha gave a quiet bow. "See you on stage."

"Don't trip," Sunghoon teased.

"I never do," she quipped without missing a beat.

The hallway narrowed as they passed. The two groups brushed shoulders—barely—but Solar felt it anyway. That subtle shift in gravity. That sense that something had started, even if no one quite said what.

As they reached the wings, Yunjin leaned over and whispered, "Jungwon didn't even blink when he saw you."

Solar didn't respond. Because she'd felt it, too.

But it wasn't what he did that stayed with her.

It was what he didn't.




The applause still echoed in her ears when Solar stepped offstage.

Her chest heaved with leftover adrenaline, skin slick with sweat beneath her cropped jacket. A staff member handed her a water bottle, and she nodded absently, lips barely forming a thank-you.

Behind her, Eunchae squealed something about her ponytail not falling out mid-spin. Chaewon was already discussing adjustments for the encore. But Solar just walked.

Away from the noise. Away from the glow of backstage LEDs. Down a side hallway dimmer than the rest. She didn't know where she was going. Only that she needed a second.

Her boots slowed near a maintenance door. One of those odd backstage corners where the world felt paused. No footsteps. No staff. Just the quiet hum of the building, like it was breathing around her.

And then—

"Solar."

She turned.

Jungwon stood a few steps behind her. Alone. No manager, no members. Just him in a black hoodie, hands in his pockets, eyes fixed on her like he'd been searching.

He took one slow step forward. "You disappeared."

Her throat tightened. "I needed air."

"I figured."

The silence between them stretched—not heavy, not awkward. Just full. She looked at him properly now. Damp hair. Slight flush to his cheeks. He hadn't changed yet from stage clothes.

He looked... tired. Beautiful. Like a storm barely holding itself together. His eyes swept over her face. "You were incredible."

She blinked. "You watched?"

"Of course." It was the way he said it—like it wasn't a question. Like watching her wasn't optional.

She looked away, suddenly aware of every inch of her skin, the tightness in her chest, the ache in her limbs. The weight of everything she hadn't said.

"You always walk alone after a show?" he asked softly.

She tried to smile. It didn't land. "Only when I don't want to cry in front of a camera."

His jaw clenched just slightly. "I saw the picture," he said, almost reluctantly.

"Of course you did," she whispered.

"I didn't care."

Her head lifted.

"I mean, I cared that you were hurting. Not about what they're saying. Not about some photo taken by someone with too much time and no spine."

She stared at him.

And then—

He stepped closer. Just a little.

Close enough that she could smell the trace of cologne still clinging to his shirt. That clean, cold scent that somehow reminded her of rain.

"I don't want to ask if you're okay," he mutters under his breath. "Because I know you're not."

The words hit harder than she expected. A small breath escaped her, shaky. Her hands curled around the water bottle like it was the only thing keeping her upright.

"I just want you to know," he continued, voice low, "that you're not alone. Even when it feels like it."

She didn't mean to. She didn't plan it. But her body moved on instinct.

And when she stepped toward him, just slightly, Jungwon didn't flinch. Didn't hesitate.

His hand reached up—slow, steady—and touched the edge of her arm. A light, careful graze of fingers just above her elbow. Barely there. Like he was asking her if she'd allow it.

She did.

And something sparked. A warmth that shot through her chest and settled somewhere deep in her spine. Her breath hitched. His eyes flickered—like maybe he felt it too.

"I..." She paused. Her voice was barely audible. "Sometimes I feel like I'm splitting in two."

"I know."

His hand stayed there. Gentle. Not pressing. Not claiming. Just present. Solar stared at the ground, eyes blurring slightly.

Then he said it—so softly she almost missed it:

"You don't have to do it all alone."

And for the first time in days, she let her shoulders drop. Not in surrender. But in the smallest act of trust. She let him stay close.

Even if it scared her. Even if it burned.

Because something about Jungwon made the world quiet. Not safe. Not easy.

But quiet. 




It was late when Solar got back to the dorm.

The halls were dim, the LSF girls mostly tucked away in their rooms, faint music leaking through the walls from Chaewon's speaker. She peeled off her jacket in the entryway, shoulders slumping the moment the door clicked shut behind her.

She wasn't sure if she was more exhausted from the stage or from the gravity of Jungwon's words still echoing in her chest.

You don't have to do it all alone.

Solar padded into the kitchen to grab water, only to freeze when she found someone already there. Yunjin, sitting cross-legged on the couch with her guitar in her lap, plucking out soft, wandering chords. She looked up immediately.

"Hey," she said gently. "You're back."

"Barely," Solar muttered, cracking open the bottle.

Yunjin set the guitar aside, patting the seat beside her. "C'mere."

Solar hesitated. Then sat down slowly, curling her legs under herself.

The room was quiet except for the hum of the fridge and the soft creak of the couch beneath them. Yunjin waited a beat. Then asked, softly, "Want to talk about it?"

Solar didn't answer right away.

She looked at her best friend—at the way Yunjin was watching her without pressure, without pity. Just there. Steady. Kind. Like she'd always been.

And the words just started coming.

"It's like I'm being pulled in three different directions," she said, voice quiet and cracking. "And I don't even know which one is me."

Yunjin tilted her head. "Three?"

"Rascal. Jungwon. Minji." The names dropped like stones into the silence.

Yunjin's eyes didn't widen. She didn't gasp. Just nodded slowly, absorbing it all.

"I kissed Rascal," Solar admitted, her throat tightening. "It was... safe. Easy. He makes me feel like I can breathe again. Like I'm not broken."

"And Jungwon?"

Solar laughed softly, bitterly. "He terrifies me. But in the best way. It's like... when he looks at me, I feel seen. Too seen. Like he sees every part of me I've been trying to hide from everyone—including myself."

Yunjin nodded again.

"And Minji?" she asked gently.

Solar's face twisted. "She sent me a message after the scandal. Don't think she meant to send it, just... something she typed and probably thought she deleted. But it was raw. Scared. Like she didn't know what to do with how she felt about me."

She looked down at her hands, ashamed at how much they were shaking.

"I feel like I'm hurting all of them," she whispered.

Yunjin's voice was low and sure. "No, babe. You're just trying to figure out who you are."

Solar looked up. Met her eyes. "I don't even know what I want."

"You don't have to know. Not yet."

"But what if I ruin it?" Solar's voice cracked. "What if I ruin everything by wanting too much?"

Yunjin smiled—soft, sad, understanding. She reached over and took Solar's hand in hers.

"You've spent your whole life shrinking," she said. "Trying to be less messy, less loud, less you, because people kept telling you it was too much."

Solar's eyes welled up.

"I don't think you want too much," Yunjin said. "I think you were told you didn't deserve it."

Silence. Then—

"I just want you to be happy," Yunjin added quietly. "It doesn't matter who it is that makes you feel that way. And it definitely doesn't matter what anyone out there thinks about it."

Solar closed her eyes, and a tear slipped down her cheek. Yunjin squeezed her hand. And for the first time in what felt like weeks, Solar let herself lean.

Just for a moment. Just enough to feel held. Because maybe that's what she needed most.

Not answers. Not clarity. Just someone who wouldn't ask her to choose until she was ready.




The next morning was quiet.

Not peacefully quiet. Not the kind that settled over a room like warmth. No—this was the kind of quiet that crawled under your skin. That made your senses itch without knowing why.

Solar sat in the LSF practice room, stretching half-heartedly against the mirrored wall. Her limbs ached. Her head throbbed. Her mind hadn't stopped spinning since the night before. Not since Jungwon's touch. Not since Yunjin's quiet love.

She was halfway through her warm-up when the door creaked open.

She expected Eunchae. Maybe Kazuha.

Instead, Danielle slipped inside.

Alone.

Her smile was bright. Too bright. Like it had been practiced in the mirror.

"Morning, unnie," she said sweetly, closing the door behind her.

Solar blinked, caught off guard. "Oh. Hey."

Danielle walked in slowly, her steps light—almost too light. Like she wasn't walking so much as gliding. She sat on the floor across from Solar and tilted her head.

"You're here early."

Solar nodded, toweling off her neck. "Wanted to get in some extra reps before our rehearsal block starts."

"Of course," Danielle said with a soft laugh. "You've been working so hard lately. Everyone's watching you."

Solar stilled.

There was something odd about the way she said it. Not mean. Not even sarcastic. Just... layered. Like the words carried more weight than her voice allowed.

Danielle leaned forward, her eyes glittering. "It must be hard. Balancing so much. Being so loved and so hated at the same time."

Solar stared at her. Danielle's gaze didn't waver.

A slow smile crept across her face. "You looked beautiful in the photo, by the way."

Solar's blood ran cold. "What photo?" she asked carefully.

Danielle blinked innocently. "The one everyone's talking about. You, leaving that building. Alone, but not really." She tilted her head, voice a touch too airy. "Enhypen's dorm, wasn't it?"

Solar's stomach turned. "How do you know that?"

Danielle just smiled. "People notice things. And people talk. You'd be surprised what finds its way to me."

She said it like she was complimenting the weather. Solar sat up straighter, every nerve ending alert.

Danielle rose to her feet, brushing invisible lint from her uniform. "You look tired, unnie," she murmured. "You should be careful. Sleep's important. Especially when you've got so many eyes on you."

She walked to the door, pausing with her hand on the knob. "I'm rooting for you," she added softly, not turning around. "Truly."

Then she slipped out the door, leaving a strange chill behind her.

Solar didn't move for a long time. Rooting for her.

No... Watching her. There was a difference.

And Solar was only just beginning to understand how dangerous it could be.
























38: Glass Hearts, Steel Smiles

The backstage hallway buzzed with low chatter, flashing lights, and the faint pulse of bass leaking from the main stage. Le Sserafim had just wrapped their ANTIFRAGILE prerecord, and Solar was still catching her breath, adrenaline sharp beneath her skin. Her hair clung to her forehead, chest heaving beneath the sequined jacket that suddenly felt too tight.

"Okay," Chaewon said, tucking a loose strand behind Solar's ear as staff bustled around them. "You crushed it. Breathe."

Solar nodded. "Right. Breathing. Trying."

Yunjin gave her a gentle squeeze on the shoulder. "Enhypen's up next, so we've got like a fifteen-minute break while they set up."

Solar didn't even have time to process the words before she turned the corner—

And stopped. There he was. Jungwon.

Standing near the water table, hoodie sleeves pushed to his elbows, a towel draped around his neck. His group surrounded him—Heeseung cracking a joke, Sunghoon elbowing someone in mock protest—but he wasn't laughing. His eyes were already on her.

Like he felt her presence before she even turned the corner. Time didn't stop.

But it slowed.

Solar couldn't breathe.

Not because of the scandal. Not because of the whispering that had haunted her since that photo leaked. But because his gaze... it didn't ask anything of her. It didn't pry. It just saw her. And after everything—after the nights of pretending she was fine, of not knowing who she could fall apart in front of—that nearly undid her.

He stepped away from the group without a word. Just moved toward her with quiet intent, cutting through the noise and cameras and chaotic energy like he belonged there.

Like he belonged with her.

They met in the narrow corridor, just out of earshot from the others. The stage lights flashed violet in the background, painting shadows against the tiled floor.

"How are you?" he asked softly, voice low but firm.

She wanted to answer. She didn't.

He studied her face like he was reading something invisible. His brow furrowed slightly, and before she could speak—before she could think—he lifted one hand.

It hovered for a second.

Then gently—so gently—he brushed a strand of hair away from her temple, letting his fingers graze her skin.

The first time they touched. It wasn't grand. It wasn't dramatic. But Solar felt it like an electric surge straight to her chest.

"You don't have to say anything," Jungwon murmured. "I just wanted you to know... I don't believe any of it."

She blinked. "You mean the photos?"

He nodded once. "People see a headline and write their own story. I only care about your story."

Her throat tightened, realizing he must be talking about all of the ridiculous stories, articles, Tweets that were being made about the 'kind of person' she was. People digging up all the different leaked photos from her recently, ones with Rascal, Jungwon, whoever. They were all using them to call her names, hideous names, and say vile things she would never even think of. "I don't even know what that story is anymore."

He didn't look away. "Then write a new one."

The words sat between them like an anchor. She exhaled slowly, something fragile loosening in her chest.

From around the corner, a staff member called, "Enhypen standby!"

Jungwon tilted his head slightly, but didn't move.

"Go," she whispered. "You'll get yelled at."

He smiled, small but real. "I've been yelled at before." But he did step back. Just once.

Before he turned away, though—he reached out again. Just the lightest brush of his fingers across her wrist. Another tether. Another silent promise.

Then he was gone.

And Solar stood frozen backstage, stage lights flashing in rhythm with the beat of her pulse, wondering if maybe—for the first time—someone had finally reached her before she fell.





They passed each other in the hallway like planets grazing orbits.

The backstage corridor was narrow, dim, and pulsing faintly with bass from the main stage. Le Sserafim had just finished their B-side performance—Solar still catching her breath, her face flushed beneath leftover glitter. Minji stood near the vending machine, half-hidden by a poster taped to the wall. She had earbuds in but wasn't listening to anything.

She saw her before Solar saw her. That was the worst part.

Because in that moment—just a second or two—Minji let herself feel it.

That ache.

The stupid, fluttery, soul-wrenching ache she hated herself for. The way Solar's eyes darted across the hall, as if scanning for exits. The way her mouth was parted, like she was still catching up to her heartbeat. The way she walked like she was trying not to fall apart—but didn't know where to place her feet.

And God, the way Minji still wanted to reach for her. Even now.

She didn't. Of course she didn't.

Solar finally noticed her. Their eyes met.

It could've been cold. It could've been nothing.

But Solar hesitated.

And that hesitation—that one second where her expression faltered and softened—was everything.

Minji blinked like she was trying to erase the emotion from her face. She shifted her weight. Took out one earbud.

"Hey," she said, low.

Solar's lips twitched upward. Not a smile. Not quite. "Hey."

They stood there for a moment. Just looking.

There was so much Minji wanted to say. Things that lived in her throat like ghosts. Things that would've sounded stupid now, like:

I miss your voice.
You look tired—are you okay?
I saw the photo.
Do you still think of me?

But all she managed was: "Good stage."

Solar's brows lifted in surprise—maybe at the compliment, maybe at the tone. "Thanks," she murmured.

A beat passed.

Minji's hands clenched slightly at her sides. "Don't let them wear you down."

Solar tilted her head. "Them?"

Minji shrugged, looking away. "The noise. All of it. You know what I mean."

Solar didn't answer at first. Then, quietly: "I'm trying."

That was enough to crack something in Minji's chest. She nodded—quick, like if she stayed too long she'd say something unforgivable—and slipped back in the direction of her waiting members.

Solar watched her go.

Minji didn't look back.

But Solar did.

Just once.

And even from down the hall, Minji felt it.

That stupid, unbearable ache.





Minji had been counting beats for most of her life.

Eight counts. Sixteen. Reset. Again.

It was supposed to be comforting—the rhythm, the structure. The way choreography gave her something to obey when her mind felt too loud.

But today, it was off.

Everything was off.

The mirrored studio walls reflected five girls in synchronicity, but Minji only saw shadows and strain. Hyein's ponytail whipped a little too sharply. Haerin kept rubbing at her neck. Even Hanni—bubbly, unflappable Hanni—was glancing between Danielle and Minji like she was tracking a storm front.

And Danielle? Danielle was perfect.

Too perfect.

Her smile didn't break. Her steps hit too clean. She laughed a half-second too late at Hyein's joke, like she hadn't heard it.

Minji noticed all of it.

She didn't say anything.

"Again from the top," the choreographer called. "With energy this time."

They reset. The music started. The bass kicked in.

Minji moved on autopilot. Hit every beat. Nailed every turn. But her eyes kept flicking to Danielle, and something gnawed at the back of her throat.

After twenty minutes, they were granted a break. Everyone scattered.

Minji grabbed her water bottle and sat by the wall, letting her pulse settle. Her phone buzzed once. A Twitter notification—another trending thread with Solar's name in it.

She didn't open it.

Across the room, Danielle stood by the window with her back turned, phone angled low in her hand. Nothing unusual, on the surface. Except—Minji saw it. The flash of Discord. The fast scroll. The name "Solar" in the chat preview.

Minji frowned.

Danielle's thumb hovered over the screen, paused, then typed something Minji couldn't read. Her face was unreadable—blank, even.

Then, she smiled.

Not her stage smile. Not her bright, bouncy, Danielle smile.

It was... strange. Small. Crooked.

Predatory.

Minji's stomach flipped.

Before she could move, Danielle pocketed the phone and turned around—mask slipping neatly back into place.

She beamed. "Want to grab smoothies after this?"

Minji blinked. "What?"

"You and me," Danielle said sweetly. "We haven't hung out in forever."

The words were fine. Friendly, even. But they made the hairs on the back of Minji's neck stand up.

She forced a small nod. "Sure."

Danielle skipped off to join Hanni and Haerin.

Minji stayed seated.

Her water bottle sat forgotten at her side. Her fingers gripped her phone too tightly.

She didn't know what she'd just seen.

But suddenly, she felt very, very awake.




It was almost midnight when Solar's headset lit up.

She didn't notice at first—not until her third yawn into the hoodie sleeve she'd borrowed (read: stolen) from Yunjin's closet. Her laptop glowed with a paused YouTube tab of their Antifragile dance practice, and her phone, face-down beside her mouse, buzzed for the third time.

She flipped it over.

[crustySF]: u up

She blinked.

Then rolled her eyes, typing with one hand.

[solar]: it's 11:52

[crustySF]: still counts

[crustySF]: u busy?

[solar]: not really. tired though. comeback stuff is hell.

[crustySF]: saw the stage. looked clean af. congrats

[solar]: thanks :]

A beat passed. Then:

[crustySF]: don't hate me. need u for early scrims this week.

She groaned aloud.

[solar]: you said i could do afternoons!!

[crustySF]: i lied.

[crustySF]: we got matched for practice against Dallas on Seoul servers. it's ugly. we need ur ping.

Solar sighed and melted into her chair, rubbing her temple. The duality of her life felt like a never-ending tightrope—featherlight in the morning, steel boots by night.

But then another message popped up.

[crustySF]: look. i know ur plate's full.

[crustySF]: just do what u can. ur place on this team is earned, not owed.

She stared at that one for a long time.

It was rare for Crusty to say anything even vaguely sentimental. He coached like a tactician, not a therapist. But maybe he understood. Maybe he'd seen what was happening online, too. The comments, the hashtags, the photos.

Her dual identities had never clashed this hard before.

[solar]: i'll be there.

[crustySF]: of course u will. u cracked

She smiled, just a little.

[solar]: tell Super if he says "just don't die" one more time, i'm unplugging his router

[crustySF]: lmao deal

[crustySF]: night idol

[solar]: night coach

She closed her laptop gently. And for the first time in hours, the noise in her head dulled just enough to let her breathe.




















39: A Place to Land

The morning sun hit different today.

It filtered softly through the dorm window blinds, not sharp or glaring like it had been all week. Solar lay on her side in bed, half-wrapped in her Le Sserafim hoodie, watching the golden streaks dance across the ceiling. Her alarm had gone off ten minutes ago. She didn't need to get up yet. Not just yet.

There was a stillness in her chest she hadn't felt in a while. Not peace exactly—but something like it. A pause. A breath. A reminder that maybe—just maybe—she was still standing.

She rolled over and reached for her phone.

No missed calls. No emergencies. Just a group chat ping from Smurf about some new Overwatch patch notes and a sticker from Eunchae of a dancing banana.

She smiled faintly.

They were dumb things. Little things. But they felt good. Like a reminder that life went on even when she felt like she couldn't.

Her fingers hovered, then tapped open her messages.

No new ones from Rascal—not since the night of the scandal, when he sent that quiet reminder: "You're still cracked. No scandal changes that."

He hadn't messaged her again. And she hadn't either. But something about the space between them didn't ache the way it used to. It just... existed. Soft. Lingering. Honest.

And Jungwon—he hadn't messaged either. But that brief moment in the hallway, the brush of his hand, the way his gaze steadied hers like a tether—she hadn't stopped thinking about it.

They hadn't said much. But it had spoken louder than words.

Her thumb lingered on Jaehyun's contact.

She hadn't heard from him since the scandal broke. No scolding texts. No overprotective calls. Just silence. Which for Jaehyun meant one of two things: either he was giving her space—or he was stewing quietly, waiting for the perfect moment to intervene.

So when her phone buzzed with his name lighting up the screen, she didn't hesitate to answer.

"Hello?"

His voice was calm. Low. Almost bored. "You alive?"

She snorted softly. "Barely."

"Mm," he murmured. "Knew it. I saw your face on a trending chart and figured you were spiraling somewhere."

Solar sat up, pulling her knees to her chest. "I'm not spiraling."

"Lying doesn't suit you."

A beat passed. She let out a long breath.

"I'm trying."

"I know."

His tone shifted—less sarcastic, more like the older brother she rarely got to see behind the sunglasses and sleek black car.

"I just wanted to say," he continued, "you don't have to prove anything to anyone."

Solar blinked. "What?"

"You heard me. You're already doing more than most people ever will. Stop trying to break yourself in half to impress people who wouldn't pick up if you called."

Silence stretched between them.

Then, in a voice softer than she meant, she said, "I don't know how to stop."

"I know." His reply came quick. Certain. "But you'll learn. Just keep going. And when it gets too loud—call me."

Something tightened in her throat. "Thanks."

"I'm serious. One more headline about you collapsing from exhaustion and I'll fly back and pull you out of this myself."

"You wouldn't."

"Try me."

She laughed—really laughed—for the first time in days. The sound startled even her.

"Okay. I'll try not to collapse."

"Good." He paused. "Now go eat something. You sound like a granola bar."

Solar hung up and sat there for a long while, phone pressed to her chest, eyes closed.

The world still felt heavy. But her feet felt steadier beneath it.




The hallway outside the HYBE recording studios always smelled faintly of lemon disinfectant and exhaustion.

Solar slipped through it like a ghost, hoodie sleeves tugged over her hands, water bottle tucked under her arm. She was heading back from a vocal session that had gone longer than expected—notes, retakes, another run-through, another again. She was used to it. But today, she felt it in her bones.

Maybe it was the lack of sleep. Maybe it was the headlines. Maybe it was the way her heart felt stretched in too many directions, like it was fraying at the edges.

She rounded the corner—and nearly collided with him.

"Careful," Jungwon murmured, one hand catching her elbow to steady her.

The contact jolted her.

Just a touch. Just his hand brushing her sleeve. But it grounded her like gravity had remembered to hold her again.

"Sorry," she breathed, stepping back half a step—but not too far.

Jungwon didn't let go right away. His hand lingered just a second longer than it needed to. Just long enough to say you're not alone without words.

"You okay?" he asked quietly.

Solar let out a breath that wasn't quite a laugh. "You're the third person today to ask me that."

"But I'm the first who means it."

She looked at him then—really looked.

He looked tired too. Hair damp like he'd just come from practice, hoodie sleeves shoved up, a small bruise blooming near the bend of his elbow—maybe from choreography, maybe from overwork. But his eyes were sharp. Focused. And the way they looked at her made something inside her unclench.

"People are watching you," he said, voice lower now. "But they don't see you."

She froze.

The air thickened around them.

Jungwon tilted his head slightly, studying her like she was the only thing in the hallway worth looking at. "I don't think you realize how many people wish they were brave enough to live like you do."

Solar swallowed hard. "I don't feel brave."

"You don't have to." His voice was steady. "You just have to keep going."

A pause. Their eyes held.

Then Jungwon stepped aside—barely—but it felt like an opening. Not a goodbye.

"I'll see you around," he said.

Solar nodded. "Yeah."

But as he turned to walk away, she said his name—soft, like she wasn't sure why.

He paused.

Her voice came quiet. "Thanks for... catching me. Earlier."

He didn't smile. But something warm flickered in his eyes.

"I'll keep doing it," he said.

And then he was gone.

She stood there a moment longer, hand still tingling from where he'd touched her sleeve, breath caught in her throat.

One step closer.

That's what it felt like.

One step closer to something she wasn't ready for—but couldn't stop reaching toward.




The New Jeans practice room echoed with breathless laughter and rubber soles squeaking across polished floors.

Someone's phone was playing the same eight-count loop on repeat—over and over until Minji thought it might drive her insane. But she kept dancing. Kept smiling. Kept twirling through the choreo like nothing inside her was burning.

"Break time!" Hyein announced, collapsing onto the floor like a starfish.

"Water," Danielle panted, flopping dramatically onto the couch.

Haerin reached for her phone with sleepy precision, already disappearing into a quiet daze.

Minji didn't sit.

She walked to the corner of the room, her hoodie sticking to her back, fingers trembling slightly around the water bottle she didn't drink from.

Across the room, Hanni clocked it. Of course she did.

"You good?" she asked gently, joining her near the mirror wall. Her tone was soft—not pitying. Just aware.

Minji didn't answer right away. She stared at her own reflection instead. Her eyes looked tired. Lined with something she didn't know how to name.

Then, quietly: "I saw the fancam."

Hanni tilted her head.

"The collab stage," Minji clarified. "With Le Sserafim. And Enhypen."

Hanni raised an eyebrow. "Ah."

"There's a shot of Solar and Jungwon," Minji said, voice tight. "Just a moment. Barely a second. But—"

She didn't finish.

But she didn't need to.

It was all there in her face. In the flush rising to her cheeks. In the way her mouth pressed into a line to keep something from spilling out.

Hanni leaned against the mirror beside her. "You don't have to pretend you don't care."

Minji exhaled—long, shaky.

"I don't know what this is," she whispered. "It's not like I'm with her. Or like I ever said anything. But... when I see her with him... I feel like I'm drowning in a glass of water."

Hanni didn't offer advice. Didn't rush to fix it.

She just listened.

Minji's eyes dropped to her hands. They'd curled into fists without her noticing.

"There's this look on her face," Minji murmured. "When he's around. Like... she's trying not to fall. And he's the only one steady enough to catch her."

"And that bothers you?" Hanni asked.

Minji nodded once. "Because I want to be the one who steadies her."

It wasn't loud. It wasn't dramatic.

But it was real.

Minji turned her head, staring through the glass at the blurred hallway beyond.

"I think I might be losing," she said softly.

"Losing what?"

"I don't know." Her voice broke just slightly. "My chance, maybe. Or my courage."

Hanni placed a hand gently on her arm. "You haven't lost anything yet."

Minji didn't answer. Just leaned her head back against the mirror, eyes fluttering closed.

But the image was still there—burned behind her eyelids.

Solar's face lit by stage lights. Jungwon beside her. The crowd screaming.

And somewhere inside her, a part of Minji cracked open.

A part that wasn't ready to break—but was already starting to.




The Le Sserafim dorm was unusually quiet that night.

The others had retreated early—some to their rooms, some to their headphones. The buzz of promotions and chaos from the past few days had finally ebbed, leaving only the hum of the hallway lights and the soft padding of socks on wooden floors.

Solar sat curled on the edge of the couch, hair damp from a late shower, hoodie sleeves swallowed in her palms.

She hadn't touched her phone in an hour. And yet her mind buzzed with too many tabs open—Minji's message, Jungwon's silence, Jihoon's shadow, and that kiss with Rascal that still lingered like a question she couldn't answer.

Kazuha entered the living room quietly, a mug in her hand. She hesitated for only a moment before walking over and setting it on the table in front of Solar.

"Chamomile," she said softly. "You haven't been sleeping."

Solar blinked up at her. "I didn't say that."

Kazuha smiled faintly. "You didn't have to."

She didn't sit. Just stood there, calm and composed in her oversized tee and bare feet, like a ghost of grace passing through the room.

After a beat, Solar asked, "Do you ever feel like no matter how hard you try, you're still not enough?"

Kazuha didn't flinch.

Instead, she said, "Every time I mess up a move I've practiced for weeks. Every time I freeze in an interview because I can't find the right Korean word. Every time someone compares me to someone else." She paused. "But then I remember—perfection doesn't love me back. Only people do."

The words sank into Solar's chest like warm stones.

Kazuha tilted her head gently. "And the people who love you? The real ones? They don't ask you to be perfect. They just want you to come home to yourself."

Solar didn't trust her voice to respond, so she just nodded.

Kazuha gave her shoulder a soft squeeze, then walked off down the hall, leaving the mug behind—steam curling upward like an offering.

Not long after, Solar made her way to her room. But someone was already inside.

Sakura stood by her desk, her back to the door. Her phone was in her hand, the screen glowing faintly. She looked caught—not guilty, but contemplative.

"You good?" Solar asked, stepping in.

Sakura didn't turn around. "Your Discord's still open."

Solar frowned. "What?"

"I wasn't snooping," Sakura added. "It just... popped up. Message from your coach." She turned finally, eyes unreadable. "Crusty. Telling you your VOD review got pushed."

"Oh," Solar said, relaxing slightly. "Right."

Sakura studied her for a moment, then asked, "Do you ever get tired?"

"Of what?"

"Being split into two people."

Solar leaned against the doorframe, arms crossed. "All the time."

Sakura nodded like she already knew that. "Sometimes I think being an idol is just... remembering how to be human in a job that asks you not to be."

There was a pause. Then Sakura's voice dropped, softer. "You're still here, you know. Underneath all of it. Not lost."

That made something sharp press against Solar's throat. "I feel like I'm disappearing," she whispered. "Like no matter which path I take, I'm failing someone."

Sakura walked over, slow and sure, and placed something in Solar's hand.

It was a dried lavender stem.

"Keep this in your case," she murmured. "It helps."

Solar stared at it, overwhelmed by the gesture's simplicity.

Sakura moved toward the door, pausing just before she left. "You don't owe anyone the whole of you, Solar. Just the part that's still breathing."

And then she was gone.

Solar sat on her bed in the stillness that followed. The stem of lavender resting beside her. The chamomile mug still faintly warm on her tongue. And the echo of two girls who didn't ask questions, didn't demand answers—just gave her space to be soft again.

For the first time all day, she let herself cry.

Not because she was falling apart.

But because she wasn't alone in the pieces.
























40: The Spark That Stays

The headlines came before she even had time to catch her breath.

"K-pop's Dual Threat: Is Solar Spreading Herself Too Thin?"

"Idol-Turned-Gamer Sparks Industry Debate: Can One Person Really Do Both?"

"Le Sserafim's Solar Prioritizes Esports Over Comeback?"

"Disrespectful to Fans," One Commenter Says.

Solar stared at the screen until the words blurred. Her thumb hovered over the endless scroll of her news feed, flicking down out of habit, eyes moving but barely reading. The cold blue glow of her phone reflected in her pupils. She didn't blink.

A clip auto-played. Her face on stage—spinning during the final chorus of "ANTIFRAGILE," mouth open mid-line, eyes sharp—and then cut harshly to footage from the tournament. A flashy highlight reel of her Tracer landing a 3K, followed by shots of her and Rascal bumping shoulders in celebration.

The caption read:

"Too many hats, or true multi-talented queen?"

The comment section was split like glass: jagged edges, no safe place to land.

"She's not serious about either."
"This girl is a marketing gimmick and nothing more."
"God, let her live. She's carrying two industries on her back."
"I'd kill to have half her drive, wtf."

Solar closed the app. The silence that followed was almost deafening.
