She sat motionless in her bed, knees hugged to her chest, the oversized sleeves of her sweatshirt bunched up around her hands. Somewhere down the hall, she could hear Chaewon humming softly in the bathroom, and Eunchae slamming a cabinet shut. The faint scent of green tea drifted in from the kitchen, but it didn't soothe her like it usually did.

She felt like her chest was full of pins.

The kind that didn't quite pierce—but pressed just hard enough to bruise.

For the past few weeks, she'd been running at full speed. Living two lives. No—burning through two lives. Makeup remover and mouse clicks. Vocal warmups and ping spikes. One hour she was in fishnets and glitter under a thousand stage lights. The next, she was staring into the eye of a sniper on Havana while Rascal yelled callouts through Discord.

And somehow, people still thought she wasn't doing enough.

That she wasn't enough.

She reached for her water bottle. Her hands were trembling just slightly.

Why did it always come down to being torn in two?

A soft knock sounded on the door, but she didn't answer. A beat passed. Then whoever it was left her alone.

Good.

She needed to be alone right now.

Her eyes drifted to her desk across the room—lined with small trophies and glowing with the faint hum of pink LED lights beneath her monitor stand. Her Shock jersey was folded neatly on the back of her chair, still smelling faintly like the plastic wrapping it arrived in. Beneath it, a stack of lyric notebooks. Stained with coffee and highlighter ink.

Both halves of her life. Sitting there. Coexisting.

"You're not allowed to fall apart," she whispered to herself, her voice barely audible.

But she was tired of surviving on that sentence.

She rose slowly and crossed the room. Stood in front of the mirror above her desk and studied the reflection.

Her eyes looked heavy. Dark circles carved beneath her lashes. Her lips were pale, and her ponytail from earlier had slumped into something half-undone.

But there was something else.

Something in her eyes.

Steel.

Even buried beneath exhaustion, it was still there. The spark. The one Rascal had seen during that scrim when everything clicked. The one Jungwon had spoken to—soft and quiet and reverent—like he knew she was fighting battles no one else could see. The one Minji couldn't seem to look away from, no matter how hard she tried.

Solar reached up and wiped beneath her eyes. She didn't cry. Not today.

Instead, she pulled her hair back again, tighter this time.

There was a VLive to record. Then a dance practice. Then scrims.

And if she was lucky, maybe two hours of sleep.

But none of that mattered right now. Because for the first time in a while, the noise wasn't louder than her own heartbeat. The headlines, the comments, the looks, the doubts?

They weren't going to define her. She was going to do that.

She picked up her Shock jersey from the chair. Held it in both hands. Then folded it carefully and placed it inside her backpack—right on top of her stage makeup pouch.



The filming lights dimmed in the HYBE practice room as the final notes of ANTIFRAGILE echoed into silence. The Le Sserafim girls collapsed into each other, breathless and smiling, their VLive stream ending just seconds ago.

"That's a wrap, girls!" a staff member called.

Everyone clapped.

Solar pulled off her in-ear monitors, sweat still clinging to her temple. Her chest heaved from effort, but there was something else inside her now—something brighter than exhaustion. A fire that refused to burn out.

Yunjin threw her arm around Solar's shoulders. "We did it. ANTIFRAGILE is officially done."

Chaewon handed out towels, her voice warm but tired. "It feels like we just started promotions yesterday."

"Because we kind of did," Sakura muttered, tugging her sleeves over her hands.

"You were insane in that last dance break," Eunchae said, eyes wide. "Like, possessed."

Solar grinned, catching her breath. "Thanks."

Kazuha frowned slightly, tilting her head. "You're logging in for scrims now, aren't you?"

"Yep." Solar yanked her hoodie from her bag and pulled it over her stage top. "They're waiting."

Sakura crossed her arms. "You haven't even cooled down yet."

"You should rest," Chaewon added. "At least drink some water."

Solar zipped up her bag. "I'll hydrate at spawn."

Yunjin reached for her wrist gently. "Hey. Seriously. You've done enough for one day. They'll understand if you're late."

But Solar only smiled. Not a defiant smile. Not even stubborn. Just... peaceful. Centered.

"I want to be there," she said simply.

The room fell quiet. The girls watched her—worry in their eyes, yes, but something else too. Pride. Admiration.

Chaewon was the one who finally sighed and said, "Tell Rascal he owes us for every time we keep letting you run off like this."

"Deal." Solar gave a mock salute and headed for the door.

Behind her, the girls exchanged a look—the kind only sisters could share. The kind that said we can't stop her, but damn if we don't admire her for it.

The lobby of HYBE shimmered beneath the late afternoon sun, glass panels casting clean lines across the polished floor. The air was filled with the low hum of coordinated chaos—stylists herding idols to vans, staff shouting over radios, the ever-present rhythm of a building that never truly slept.

Solar's sneakers squeaked across the tile as she jogged through, bag slung over her shoulder, hoodie half-zipped. She was breathless but still smiling, like she was sprinting through the middle of a dream and didn't mind being interrupted.

Minji stepped into the lobby just in time to see her.

Solar's hoodie was zipped halfway, strands of hair sticking to her temples. Her sneakers squeaked against the tile as she hurried. She didn't even see them—New Jeans, clustered near the elevators with their manager—until Minji called out gently, "Unnie."

Solar paused mid-stride.

"Oh—hey!" she said, breathless but still smiling, like she was sprinting through the middle of a dream and didn't mind being interrupted.

Minji smiled back, more subdued. "Long day?"

"The longest," Solar replied. "We just wrapped ANTIFRAGILE. Like, literally just now."

She bowed politely toward the others. "Hi, Hanni. Hi, Haerin. Hi, Hyein."

Hyein waved with both hands. "You were sick on stage, unnie. Like, scary good."

"Yeah," Hanni added, tilting her head. "Didn't you have a scrim today too?"

Solar laughed, rubbing the back of her neck. "Yeah, I do. I'm actually on my way home now to log in."

Minji blinked. "Wait, right now?"

"Yup." Solar took a step backward, already moving. "Double life things, you know? Shock is waiting. I'll see you guys around, okay?"

She waved once more and jogged toward the building's glass doors, disappearing in a flash of motion and fading footsteps.

Silence.

Minji watched the door close behind her, the echo of her presence still humming in her ears.

She's always rushing somewhere.

Hanni leaned in a little, whispering, "She's insane. Like, in a good way."

Minji didn't reply right away.

She was still staring at the door.

There was something magnetic about Solar, and Minji hated how true it was. The way she lit up under pressure, the way her ambition didn't harden her but made her shine brighter. Minji had watched the fancams. She'd seen that moment again and again—Solar on stage, then Solar in scrims, then Solar standing still only for a second before sprinting toward the next impossible thing.

Minji didn't know if she wanted to chase her or be her.

Maybe both.

"She's everything I want to be," she whispered.

Then added—so soft only Hanni could hear—

"And everything I want."

Hanni turned to her, but Minji had already started walking toward the elevators.

She didn't say anything else.

Didn't need to.

Because sometimes longing wasn't loud. Sometimes it was a glance at a door after it closed, a breath you didn't know you were holding, and a heart that beat too fast when someone said your name in passing.

And Minji?

She was starting to realize she was in deeper than she ever meant to be.




The apartment was dark by the time Solar made it home.

She didn't bother turning on the lights—just kicked off her sneakers, dropped her bag by the door, and padded into her room on autopilot. The glow of her gaming setup was already waiting, bathing the walls in soft neon blues and purples. Her hands trembled slightly as she reached for the headset.

Everything had been so loud lately.

Articles questioning her mental state. Analysts debating whether she could keep up. Her own reflection in a mirror, asking questions she didn't have the courage to answer.

But here—this chair, this monitor, these Discord notifications stacked like unread love letters—this was hers.

She sat down slowly and slipped on her headset.

The familiar hum of the Shock server greeted her like a hug.

RASCAL: yo
RASCAL: you on?
RASCAL: we need you to carry
RASCAL: no pressure but smurf just used grav on a lamp post

Solar couldn't help it. She snorted.

Her phone buzzed a second later with a private message from Rascal.

RASCAL: you're still cracked. no scandal changes that.
RASCAL: get on and remind them who tf you are.

She typed back quickly.

SOLAR: thought you'd never ask
SOLAR: but only if smurf stops using grav on household objects

RASCAL: prolly wont happen ngl

She clicked into the call, the familiar pop of her mic lighting up.

"Yo," she said.

"AYYY THE STAR HAS LOGGED IN," Super cried. "I was about to start sobbing."

"Save your tears for when I out-damage you," Solar shot back.

"Ohhhhhh," Glister howled. "She's spicy tonight!"

Smurf giggled in the background. "I made cookies."

"No you didn't," Nero muttered.

"I thought about making cookies," Smurf corrected.

Solar leaned back in her chair, lips twitching. God, she needed this.

"Ready for some good ol' competitive chaos?" Rascal asked, his voice low and teasing in her ear.

"Ready," she replied, steady this time. "Let's get it."

They queued up as a team. The banter flew fast. Someone sneezed mid-fight. Nero accidentally ulted while alt-tabbing to read Reddit. Glister called Solar "queen of the high ground," and Super kept spamming the laugh emote until Rascal threatened to uninstall the game himself.

No one mentioned the article. No one brought up the scandal. They just played. Like it was any other night. Like she wasn't straddling two worlds on the edge of collapse.

Like she belonged.

After a particularly brutal win, Solar let out a breath she hadn't realized she was holding.

"You good?" Rascal asked, soft enough that it barely registered over the buzz of post-match stats.

She paused. Then, honest and quiet: "Yeah. I think I am."

"Cool," he said. "Now get back in queue. Super's already queuing us into another loss."

"I can hear you," Super grumbled.

Solar laughed for real this time—head tilted back, hand over her mouth, the kind of laugh that made her eyes sting.

She wasn't fixed. She wasn't perfect.

But she wasn't alone.





The street was quiet, lit only by a thin halo of light from the corner store's flickering sign.

Solar stood under the yellow awning, hoodie drawn tight around her ears, hands deep in her sleeves. The scent of damp concrete mixed with the faint sweetness of canned peach tea from the store behind her.

She hadn't meant to come here.

Not consciously.

But somehow, her feet had taken her back to the same cracked sidewalk where it all began—the night she'd broken, the night he saw her.

It wasn't the kind of place most people would remember.

But she did.

Because it was the first place she stopped pretending.

And tonight—after all the articles, the stares, the silence she couldn't scream through—it felt like the only place in the city where she might still recognize herself.

She closed her eyes.

Breathed in.

And then—

Footsteps.

Faint at first, but growing clearer. Measured. Steady.

Her eyes opened just as he turned the corner.

Jungwon.

Black hoodie pulled up. Shoulders hunched against the wind. His hair damp at the ends, clinging to his forehead like he'd been walking for a while. Like maybe—just maybe—he hadn't known where else to go, either.

He stopped when he saw her. Not in surprise. Just... stillness. Recognition. Like this was exactly where he hoped she'd be.

He approached quietly, his hands shoved into his jacket pockets.

"Hey," he said, his voice low, like the night might shatter if he spoke too loud.

Solar's throat tightened, but she nodded. "Hey."

He stood beside her, close enough that she could feel his warmth through the fabric of her hoodie, but not touching.

The silence stretched between them—not awkward, just... full. With everything neither of them knew how to say.

"You came back," Jungwon murmured.

She let out a breath. "You say that like I ever really left."

He glanced down at her—not a smirk, not a smile, just soft amusement. "I hoped you'd be here."

Something fluttered in her chest.

"I saw the articles," he said, eyes fixed on the glimmering street beyond. "All the noise. The headlines saying you're cracking, that you can't do both."

Solar's jaw tensed. "They always think they know everything."

"I don't think they see you," he said quietly. "Not really."

She looked up at him.

There was something different in his face tonight. Something grounded. Like he wasn't just offering comfort—he was standing guard.

"You're not breaking," Jungwon said. "You're rising. Even when it hurts."

Her heart gave a strange, aching thud. Like it wanted to reach for him before her brain could pull it back.

"You think so?" she asked, voice barely above the wind.

He turned to her, really turned.

And for once, there was no stoic mask. No practiced distance.

"I know so."

Solar didn't mean to lean into him. It just... happened.

Her shoulder pressed gently against his. Her head tilted until it rested on him, the way gravity might guide a falling leaf.

He didn't speak. Didn't move.

But she felt the way his body shifted, ever so slightly—just enough to meet her weight. To make space for her without asking why she needed it.

"I almost gave up," she whispered, not even sure why she was saying it out loud. "I thought... maybe they were right. That I'm too much."

Jungwon's breath hitched. "Then why didn't you?"

She stared out at the street. The peach tea poster in the window swayed in the breeze.

"I think..." She hesitated. Then let it out. "I think maybe you reminded me who I was. When I forgot."

A silence. Then: "I didn't want to be the reason you broke," he admits.

"You're not," she replied.

And somehow, saying it made it true.

She didn't know how long they stood like that—pressed together by nothing more than trust and tired hearts. But it felt like the first real moment she'd had all week.

No cameras.

No rehearsals.

No headlines.

Just them.

And the quiet.

And something unnamed humming between their hearts.

When she finally pulled away, Jungwon didn't stop her.

But he didn't look away either.

Their eyes locked, and for one breathless second, she thought he might say something else. Something that would change everything.

But he didn't.

Because it didn't need to be said.

Not yet.

She left first, hoodie still pulled up, footsteps light but steady.

Behind her, the peach tea sign flickered once more.

And Jungwon stayed, standing alone under the streetlight like a promise waiting to be kept.

















41: And She Still Runs

The clock read 12:17 a.m.

Solar blinked against the glow of her monitor, her reflection barely visible in the black stretch of the unlaunched game lobby. Her room was dark except for the LED underglow of her desk and the soft pulse of the Shock logo on her custom mousepad. She flexed her fingers. They ached. Everything ached. But she logged in anyway.

The familiar boot chime of Overwatch echoed in her headset.

A Discord ping followed instantly.

CRUSTY: Scrim room in 2. Let's lock in.
RASCAL: u alive solar
SUPER: finally
SMURF: she's cracked even when late lol
GLISTER: go easy on me pls 😭

Solar cracked her neck and slid on her mic.

"Sorry," she muttered. "Dance practice ran over. Had to sneak out before Chaewon guilt-tripped me into stretching for another hour."

"Ugh," Super groaned. "Can't believe I'm scrimming with a pop star. My knees hurt just thinking about it."

"You mean your ego?" Nero cut in dryly.

Rascal chuckled softly. "She's here now. Let's play."

Solar smiled a little. His voice had the steadiness she always relied on—quiet, teasing, never too loud.

The custom scrim server loaded.

Crusty's voice entered, calm and razor-sharp. "Focus. Grand Finals are in two weeks. No excuses. We play Seoul next. They know your habits. Don't give them what they expect."

Solar inhaled deeply. She wasn't just Solar anymore. She was SOLAR—Shock's flex DPS. A face in headlines. A threat in every comp. The rookie everyone was waiting to see fall apart.

And she wouldn't.

Not here.

Not tonight.

They launched the first map.

The pressure came instantly—triple dive, low cooldown brawls, mirror comps. Solar's Tracer danced between tanks, blinking into backlines with needlepoint precision. Rascal flexed to Echo, peeling pressure and lifting her from impossible corners. They didn't even need to say much anymore. Their synergy moved like instinct.

"Tracer no recall!" Rascal called once.

"Echo one—dove their Ana!" Solar fired back.

Pulse Bomb. Stick.

Team kill.

Even Crusty's voice cracked a little with satisfaction. "Good. Again."

They played four maps in a row. Sweat dampened Solar's lower back. Her arms trembled slightly with adrenaline. But she kept going. Kept winning. Kept proving.

Until the end of the fifth map.

They won. Barely. But it was enough.

There was a pause in the comms.

Then Crusty spoke. "This is what we need. That level. Always. If we want to take Grand Finals, we don't get to have off-days."

"Yes, coach," they echoed.

Solar reached up and rubbed her temple.

But Crusty wasn't done.

"Solar."

Her heart stuttered.

"Yes?"

"You're good. But Grand Finals are another beast. The moment we land in Seoul, people are going to expect you to fold. I don't want to see you fold."

Her jaw clenched. "I won't."

There was silence again.

Then: "Good."

One by one, the guys started logging off—Super with a yawn, Glister groaning about sleep, Smurf sending a row of sleepy emojis.

Solar lingered. Rascal hadn't left yet.

His icon blinked. Then his voice filled the space—calm, quieter than before. "You okay?"

She hesitated. "Yeah. Just... tired."

"You always say that," he said softly.

She looked at her monitor like it might offer answers. "It's true."

There was a pause. Then Rascal exhaled, a faint sigh wrapped in something gentler. "You were insane tonight. Cracked. Like... finals MVP cracked."

That made her laugh—weak, but real. "You're just saying that."

"No," he insisted. "I'm saying it because it's true."

Her chest ached. Not from exhaustion. From something else. He didn't say anything more after that. Just: "Get some sleep, Solar. Even superstars burn out."

Then he left the server. She sat there for a long time after, watching the empty comms.

The Grand Finals were coming. And for the first time in weeks— She didn't feel scared. She felt ready.




HYBE's underground parking garage was unusually quiet.

The fluorescent lights buzzed faintly overhead, casting long shadows across the concrete. A few vans idled in the distance, their engines a low rumble echoing against the walls. Staff voices floated above it all—laughing, coordinating, rushing. Business as usual.

Jungwon stood near one of the side exits, hood up, earbuds dangling from his neck. He was scrolling through something on his phone—distracted, but still alert in that way he always was. Tense without showing it.

He didn't see Jaehyun until the footsteps stopped beside him.

The shift was immediate. Subtle, but instinctive.

Jungwon looked up.

And there he was.

Jaehyun Lee. Sunglasses on even underground. Arms crossed. Posture calm, but eyes sharp enough to cut through glass.

Jungwon straightened. Slowly. Tucking his phone into his pocket. "Sunbae," he offered, low and polite.

Jaehyun said nothing at first. Just studied him. Jungwon didn't flinch under the weight of it.

After a long moment, Jaehyun finally spoke. "Didn't expect to see you here this late."

"I had dance rehearsal," Jungwon answered simply. "Solar too, I think."

Jaehyun's expression didn't change. "But she didn't come out with you."

It wasn't a question. "No," Jungwon said. "She stayed behind."

Jaehyun tilted his head. "You've been around her a lot lately."

Jungwon met his gaze. "Not as much as I want to be."

The words landed. A quiet beat stretched between them. It wasn't hostile. Not exactly. But it wasn't friendly either. It was protective. Possessive. Both of them.

"I know who you are," Jaehyun said at last. "I know what kind of life you live. I've seen what this industry does to people like her."

Jungwon's jaw flexed. "And what kind of people is that?"

"The ones who give everything. The ones who break quietly." There was no heat in Jaehyun's voice. Just cold truth.

Jungwon looked down for a moment, then back up. "She's already been broken," he corrects. "By people who were supposed to protect her."

Jaehyun's mouth twitched. Not quite a frown. Not quite guilt. But something in the middle. "You think I don't know that?"

"I think you know," Jungwon hums, carefully. "But you don't trust anyone else to hold her."

The silence that followed was dense. Jungwon stepped forward—not aggressive, not rude, just... firm. "I'm not trying to be her crutch. Or her escape. I just want to be the one she doesn't have to lie to."

Jaehyun's sunglasses caught the light. He studied Jungwon again. This time, less like a threat. More like a possibility.

"She's stubborn," Jaehyun said finally. "She doesn't let go of things easily. Not pain. Not people."

"I know."

"You willing to deal with that?"

Jungwon's voice didn't waver. "I already am."

That was it. No grand declarations. No chest-puffing displays. Just quiet certainty.

Jaehyun looked away, exhaled slowly, and nodded once. "She likes peach tea," he says, tone unreadable. "Not too sweet."

Jungwon blinked. The corner of Jaehyun's mouth twitched—just barely. Then he turned, walking away without another word.

Jungwon stood there alone in the garage, staring after him. His heart was pounding. But not from fear.

Something else entirely. Something that felt like permission.




The Le Sserafim practice room was nearly empty.

Evening light filtered in through the high windows, casting golden lines across the glossy floor. The mirror wall reflected only one figure—Solar, sitting in the center of the room, legs folded, earbuds dangling loosely around her neck.

She wasn't practicing.

Not really.

She'd already run the choreography twice. More out of habit than anything. Now, she was just stretching slowly, arms above her head, eyes distant—like maybe if she moved her body enough, she could quiet her mind.

The door creaked open.

She turned. Minji.

Still in her own practice gear—oversized sweatshirt, hair pulled into a low bun, a water bottle in one hand. Solar blinked, surprised. "Minji?"

"Hey," Minji said softly, stepping inside. "Yunjin said you were still here."

Solar nodded, unsure. "Yeah. Just... unwinding."

Minji closed the door behind her. The room felt quieter, somehow more private. She crossed the space slowly and sat down beside Solar, close—but not too close. Neither of them spoke right away.

Then Minji asked, "Are you okay?"

Solar laughed once, quietly. "That's the question of the year, huh?"

Minji looked at her. Really looked.

"I don't mean... 'Are you okay in front of the cameras.' I mean here." She tapped her own chest gently. "In the places no one sees."

Solar stared at the floor. Her voice dropped. "Some days... I don't even know how to answer that."

Minji nodded, her gaze steady. "I figured."

There was a beat. Then Solar sighed, voice low. "Everything's just... heavy. I feel like I'm always in two places at once. Trying to be two people at once."

Minji watched her.

Solar continued. "Everyone wants something. The public wants the idol. Crusty wants the player. HYBE wants the product. And I—" She broke off, then gave a tired smile. "I don't even know what I want half the time."

Silence wrapped around them. Minji's voice was quiet when she finally said, "I do."

Solar turned. Minji's expression was unreadable—but her eyes were shining. "I want to know you. Not just the parts that smile on stage or land headshots in overtime. I want... the in-between parts. The ones no one else sticks around long enough to see."

Solar felt her heart stutter. Her throat tightened.

Minji kept going, her voice steadier now. "You're always running, Solar. Even when you're standing still. I know you don't mean to be—but it's like your soul is always trying to outpace the next disappointment."

Solar looked down at her hands, fingers curled in her lap.

"I kissed Rascal," she whispered. "After the tournament. It wasn't planned. It just... happened."

Minji didn't flinch.

"I figured," she said softly.

Solar swallowed. "I thought maybe it would help. That it would make something click. But all I felt was this... sadness. Like even in something good, I still couldn't stop waiting for it to break."

Minji's voice was even quieter now. "And Jungwon?"

Solar flinched at his name.

"He makes it feel... like I don't have to run. Like I'm allowed to want things again. Real things."

Minji nodded slowly.

Then, after a breath—"And me?"

Solar's breath caught.

Minji leaned in just slightly, not touching, but near enough that Solar could feel the warmth of her body. Her next words were a whisper.

"You never leave my head. Even when I try to make you."

Solar turned to her.

The moment stretched—thick with unspoken things, years of denial wrapped in just a few inches of space. The kind of closeness that didn't beg to be acted on, just honored.

And maybe that was why, when Minji moved to hug her, Solar didn't pull away.

Their arms slid around each other, slow and sure. Not desperate. Not performative.

Just real.

When they pulled apart, they didn't speak. But their eyes lingered.




It was nearly midnight when Solar returned to the dorm.

The city outside hummed low with neon life, but inside—quiet. Peaceful. The kind of hush that wrapped around your shoulders like a warm blanket and whispered rest.

She moved through the darkened hallway quietly, careful not to wake the others. Her duffel bag hung from one arm, sore from dance rehearsals and an extra two hours of solo aim training afterward.

She stopped outside her room.

Something was off.

Not obvious. Just... a feeling.

She flicked on the overhead light.

Her room was exactly how she left it—hoodie draped over her desk chair, headset still resting neatly on its stand, LED strips casting soft lavender against the wall.

But then her eyes dropped to the floor.

A small box sat just inside the door. Wrapped in glossy, pale pink paper with a velvet ribbon tied perfectly in a bow.

Solar's stomach dropped.

No note.

No tag.

Just the box.

She glanced behind her. The hallway was empty.

Heart ticking faster now, she bent down and picked it up. The box was light. Inside, she found a single tube of lipstick—deep red. Matte. The kind she never wore.

Taped to the inside of the lid was a piece of paper. A single sentence typed neatly in a delicate serif font.

"I hope you'll wear this when you see him again."

That was it.

No signature.

Solar's mouth went dry.

Her fingers trembled slightly as she folded the note back up and closed the box.

She stood there, the gift still in her hands, suddenly aware of the silence in the dorm. The soft hum of the fridge. The faint sound of someone's playlist echoing through another room.

But it all felt... wrong.

She looked down at the box again.

And noticed something else.

The ribbon.

It wasn't just velvet.

It was twisted—tight around the box in a pattern she knew all too well. A sailor's knot.

Jihoon had once taught it to her. Back when things were good. When he thought knowing how to tie the perfect knot said something about control.

Her fingers went cold.

The note could've been from anyone.

But her gut whispered it wasn't.

And Danielle?

Danielle had that exact shade of lipstick. Solar had seen it on her desk in the New Jeans dressing room more than once.

She walked across the room and placed the box on her shelf, not looking at it again.

She didn't throw it away.

But she didn't open it again either.

She sat down at her desk, logged into Discord, and tried to shake the way her skin crawled.

No messages.

No pings.

Just the silence again.

That creeping, curling silence.

And in it, one thought kept clawing its way up her throat.

I'm being watched.


42: Static Between the Stars

The showcase hall buzzed like a hive—camera flashes, lightsticks flickering, fans chanting the girls' names with a rhythm that felt like thunder and worship all at once.

"Le Sserafim, fighting!"

Solar smiled as the call echoed from the pit. But it was a rehearsed smile. One she'd worn so many times it fit like a second skin.

They were backstage, seconds from stepping into the spotlight. The staff swarmed, adjusting hair, powdering cheeks, reapplying gloss. Solar sat still in the makeup chair, letting it all wash over her. A stylist adjusted her jacket—a deep crimson satin bomber with glittering embellishments along the sleeves—and tugged at the hem.

"Do you want to keep this on during the intro segment?" the stylist asked. "It might get hot."

Solar blinked. "I'll keep it on."

She didn't say why.

Didn't explain that underneath it—tucked against her skin like a second heartbeat—was her Shock jersey. SOLAR. Bold and stitched in orange thread. No one could see it. Not even her members. It was for her.

Just her. A quiet reminder of who she'd become. Of what she carried. Chaewon peeked into the dressing room. "Ten minutes."

Yunjin stepped up beside her, already mic'd and grinning. "You good?"

Solar nodded.

"You sure?"

"Yeah," Solar lied gently. "I'm good." She wasn't lying to hide. She was lying because she wanted it to be true.

They lined up backstage—her, Yunjin, Chaewon, Kazuha, Sakura, and Eunchae. The six of them shoulder to shoulder, waiting for the cue to go live.

The music swelled. The screen behind the stage flickered with vibrant visuals—"ANTIFRAGILE" in bright crimson.

Solar closed her eyes for just a second.

And remembered every version of herself who had dreamed of being right here.

Twelve-year-old her with scabbed knees and an old headset, playing ranked matches while her sister yelled from the kitchen to do her homework. Fifteen-year-old her clutching a mic in trembling fingers at the HYBE open audition. Seventeen-year-old her crying in the bathroom after Jihoon ghosted her. Nineteen-year-old her clicking "accept match" while the rest of the dorm slept. And now, twenty-year-old her—idol, gamer, survivor, daughter, sister, fractured and whole all at once.

The curtain rose. Light exploded.

And Le Sserafim walked onto the stage to the sound of a thousand voices screaming their names.

The showcase was a blur.

Fan Q&A, a few unreleased behind-the-scenes clips, and a stage performance of "ANTIFRAGILE" that had the fans chanting every word.

But in between the chaos—Solar had quiet moments. Tiny fragments of stillness.

When she caught sight of a fan in the front row holding a hand-painted sign that read "Two lives. One legend."

When her jacket slipped slightly during the VCR playback and she tugged it back down, hand brushing the edge of her Shock jersey underneath.

When a fan yelled, "Solar unnie, we believe in you!" and it didn't sound like it was only about music.

After the final stage, the girls returned backstage, drenched in sweat and adrenaline.

"I think I'm dying," Eunchae groaned, collapsing into a chair.

"You're thriving," Yunjin corrected, pulling her hair into a ponytail.

Solar peeled off her jacket as the stylists gathered for touch-ups before the post-show photo session. She caught a glimpse of herself in the mirror—Shock jersey clinging to her frame, makeup a little smudged, shoulders rising and falling fast.

She looked tired.

But she also looked alive.

Yunjin noticed. "You wore that under your outfit?"

Solar nodded. "Just for me."

Yunjin didn't tease. She just smiled, soft and real. "I hope you know how proud I am of you."

Solar blinked back something sharp in her eyes. "I'm trying," she whispered.

Yunjin looped an arm around her shoulder. "You're doing."

And Solar believed her, she swore she did. Even with the box on her shelf. Even with the articles swirling. Even with the fear that never really left her lungs.

Solar walked out of the dressing room and smiled for the cameras like she always had. But this time? It wasn't a mask.

It was armor.



The Le Sserafim dorm was unusually still that night.

Practice had run late. Most of the girls were already in their rooms, winding down, voices muffled behind closed doors. The hum of the fridge and the occasional clink of pipes were the only sounds left echoing in the quiet apartment.

Solar padded barefoot into the kitchen, still in her oversized rehearsal tee and sweatpants, hair damp from a rushed shower. Her muscles ached. Her eyelids stung. But her mind wouldn't stop spinning.

She reached for a water bottle in the fridge—then paused.

Sakura was already there, leaning back against the counter in a hoodie and leggings, a bottle cradled loosely in her hands.

They stared at each other for a beat.

"You good?" Solar asked, voice hushed.

Sakura didn't answer right away. She gestured toward the other bottle. "Water helps with thinking."

Solar gave a tired smile and took it, sitting on one of the bar stools.

The silence between them wasn't uncomfortable—just heavy. Like it had something to say if they were brave enough to listen.

After a long pause, Sakura finally spoke.

"There's this... guy," she said, voice barely above a whisper, like she wasn't sure if she was confessing or just thinking aloud. "We don't know each other that well. Barely at all, really. But there's something about him. The way he talks. The way he doesn't talk."

Solar tilted her head slightly, listening. She didn't press. Just let the words unravel on their own.

Sakura's fingers tightened slightly on the plastic bottle. "He's reckless. Sharp. Like he's always daring someone to look too close. I think he's been hurt more than he admits. Maybe he thinks if he stays loud enough, no one will see it."

There was a flicker in her voice—something she hadn't meant to let show. Something that sounded a lot like longing.

"I don't know what we are," Sakura continued. "Or if we're anything. But sometimes I think... the silences between us are louder than anything he's said."

Solar swallowed, throat tight.

Somewhere in the back of her mind, she thought about Rascal. Then Jungwon. The words she hadn't said. The spaces she'd left untouched.

Sakura looked at her then, eyes quiet but piercing.

"Some people leave bruises without ever touching you," she said. "And some people hold on so tight, you forget how to breathe."

A pause. "Just... don't let the noise drown you out, Solar. Or the quiet."

Solar let those words settle into her chest like drops of rain on glass. She looked down at the bottle in her hands, then back at Sakura.

"Sometimes I feel like something bad's coming," she whispered. "And I'm the only one who can hear it."

Sakura didn't flinch. She just nodded, like she understood more than she was letting on. Then she turned back toward the hallway.

"Goodnight," she said softly.

And just like that, she was gone—leaving Solar alone in the kitchen, her thoughts louder than ever.




The next day, HYBE's hallways buzzed with rehearsals, early meetings, and comeback prep. Solar moved quietly through the building, hoodie up, mask snug, sneakers squeaking faintly against the marble floors. She wasn't running today. Just... moving. Going through the motions.

Her heart was still in limbo—somewhere between exhaustion and everything she hadn't let herself feel.

She stepped into the lobby near the vending machines, mostly empty this time of day, and paused by the glass to fix her mask. Her fingers trembled, just slightly. She hadn't eaten since breakfast. She hadn't slept right in a week. There were still eyes on her everywhere—even when there weren't.

She closed her eyes.

And that's when she felt it.

A presence behind her.

She turned slowly.

Jungwon.

Dark hoodie, sleeves rolled to the elbows, earbuds dangling around his neck. He looked like he belonged to another story. Another rhythm. But the way he watched her—so still, so quietly intense—it was like he already knew the chapter she was trying not to write.

He stepped closer.
Not dramatic.
Not reckless.
Just... near enough that she could feel the warmth off his skin.

Jungwon raised one hand. Gently adjusted the hem of her hoodie where it had slipped from her shoulder—his knuckles brushing her cheekbone in the process.

The touch was brief.
But it short-circuited something inside her.

Then—softly:

"Who hurt you?"

Solar blinked.

The words hit like a pulse in her chest.

She didn't answer at first. Just stared past him, at nothing in particular. Like maybe if she looked far enough, she'd find an answer that didn't ache.

But all she found was herself.

And so she whispered, barely above breath:

"Too many people."

Her voice cracked. "All of it. The media. The pressure. The looks. The pretending. Jihoon. Danielle. Myself. I don't even know anymore."

Jungwon didn't flinch.
Didn't push.
Just... stood there.

"I keep trying to do everything right," she said. "But I feel like I'm disappearing."

His brow furrowed. Not with pity—but with something colder. Sharper. Protective.

He didn't say I'm sorry.

He didn't say Are you okay?

Instead, he said, quietly:

"Then let someone see you."
"The real you. Not just the idol. Not just the player."

Her throat tightened.
She met his gaze—really met it—and there it was again.

That silence.

That storm.

That pull.

She took a single step forward. Almost without realizing it. And he didn't move. Didn't look away. Like he'd been waiting. Like he'd always wait.

"I don't even know who the real me is anymore," she whispered.

"You will," he said.

There was something unshakable in his tone.

"And when you do," he added, "I'll still be here."

They stood there like that for a moment longer. Just long enough for it to feel like a promise.

Then Solar nodded. Just once.

And walked away.

But her chest was still burning.

Not from pain.

From hope.




The practice room reeked of hairspray and sour energy drinks. The mirrors were fogged from hours of movement, the floor slick with scuffed sneaker rubber and faint circles of sweat. New Jeans had been rehearsing nonstop for an upcoming award show—tightening formations, adjusting timing, repeating choreography until it blurred at the edges.

Minji was still catching her breath when Hanni plopped beside her on the bench, handing her a water bottle.

"Thanks," Minji murmured, unscrewing the cap. Her gaze drifted across the room. Danielle was laughing at something Haerin said, all sunshine and polished charm. Hyein danced behind them with a stuffed bear, pretending it was her stage partner.

Minji tried to focus. To reset.

But her brain wouldn't cooperate.

She kept seeing Solar's face from the day before—flushed, radiant, breathless as she waved goodbye. That fire in her step. That quiet sadness behind her eyes. That undeniable pull Minji couldn't name without her voice cracking.

She took a long sip of water, trying to drown the feeling.

"Hey." Hanni nudged her gently with her knee. "You okay?"

Minji nodded too fast. "Yeah. Just tired."

Hanni didn't press. But a few seconds later, she leaned in a little and said under her breath, "I wasn't gonna say anything, but... I overheard something weird earlier."

Minji turned slightly. "Weird how?"

Hanni's brows drew together. "Danielle. She was talking to her phone, kind of laughing, and I swear I heard her say something like... 'I hope she liked the little gift.'"

Minji blinked. "What?"

"At first I thought it was a joke. But then she got all weird when she saw I was there." Hanni looked at her, eyes darkening. "You don't think she—"

"I don't know," Minji cut in, voice tight. But her mind was already racing. A gift?

She thought of Solar.

Of that night Solar had texted her late, words clipped and vague:

"Someone left something in my room. I don't know who. It's... weird."

They never followed up.

Because Minji had been scared of what she'd say if she did.

Her stomach twisted.

Danielle had a way of hiding in plain sight. Of smiling through things that didn't deserve smiles. And now that she thought about it... Minji wasn't sure she'd ever seen Danielle not know something about someone she cared about.

She looked back at Hanni. "We need to be careful."

"About what?"

Minji's voice dropped. "Everything."

And suddenly, the air in the practice room felt colder.

Like something just shifted—but no one else had noticed yet.







43: Storm Warning

The Discord server was unusually quiet when Solar joined.

Not silent—Super was already mid-rant about some player's grip strength, Nero was spamming frog emojis in the team chat, and Glister was asking no one in particular whether Seoul still had that bakery with the milk bread that made him "see God." But beneath it all, there was tension. A hum. Like the static before lightning.

"Everyone here?" Crusty's voice cut through the noise—clipped, no-nonsense, the same tone that made analysts shiver and sponsors sigh with relief.

Solar glanced at the user list. Five icons lit up. Rascal's was the last to ping green.

"Here," he said. His voice was level, even warm, but not the same teasing tone she'd come to know by heart. No nickname. No low chuckle. Just... presence.

"Great," Crusty said. "Let's get into it."

The screen shifted as the coach began screen-sharing—tournament bracket on one tab, map pool on another. The Shock logo gleamed at the center of it all, bold and orange, flanked by the remaining top teams. Names Solar had grown up watching. Teams she used to idolize from a computer chair in a dim dorm room. Now, they were opponents. Rivals.

Targets.

"Scrim schedule for Seoul's set," Crusty continued. "Team lands Tuesday. Venue's prepped for media day Wednesday, then finals weekend. No public commitments for Solar. HYBE signed off on a light press presence."

Solar blinked. "Wait—they did?"

Crusty snorted. "They'd rather be invisible than take heat for getting in the way."

Super let out a wheeze. "Damn. Coach out here with the PR roast."

"Focus," Crusty snapped, but the edge in his voice wasn't angry. Just wired. Focused.

A few minutes passed as he ran through comp drafts and team pairing expectations. Solar followed along—half on muscle memory, half with simmering adrenaline. She felt Rascal's occasional callouts drift in: "We dive first round. Play fast." "I'll bait ult; Solar holds pulse." Efficient. Clean. But no jokes. No breath between lines.

It stung.

And it grounded her.

This was the Grand Finals. There wasn't room for anything else.

Crusty finally stopped on the final slide. A replay still of one of Solar's last matches—her Tracer mid-blink, Pulse Bomb seconds from landing.

He paused.

Then, his voice dropped into something quieter. Personal.

"Solar."

She sat up straighter.

"You've earned your slot. Not because of hype. Not because of marketing. Because you win fights and call smart and know how to trust your team."

The silence in the channel tightened, just for a second.

"Now prove it on the biggest stage."

Solar's chest ached. A mix of disbelief and pressure and something that felt suspiciously close to pride. She hadn't realized how badly she needed to hear it. How much it mattered, coming from someone who didn't care about K-pop or stage lighting or how many fans she had. Crusty only cared about the scoreboard.

And she'd earned her place on it.

"Copy," she said softly. "I won't let you down."

Super broke the tension like clockwork. "She's about to carry us so hard I'm gonna need a chiropractor."

"Bro," Glister said, "you already need one. You sleep like a shrimp."

Rascal snorted under his breath.

It wasn't much.

But it was something.

Solar smiled faintly. Let herself breathe.

"Alright," Crusty said. "Team flight Tuesday. Don't slack. Don't overthink. Play your game."

As the call ended and names began to vanish from the channel one by one, Solar lingered.

Rascal was still there.

Muted.

Present.

But when he finally left the call, it was quiet. No private message. No voice note. Just the familiar silence of someone trying to protect themselves.

And still—her screen glowed orange.

She was Shock.

She was ready.

Even if her heart still didn't know what it was fighting for.




Minji danced like she was trying to escape something.

Her breath came sharp with each count. Five, six, seven, eight—shoulder pop, half-turn, pivot into the chorus. Her reflection mirrored every move perfectly, but the girl in the mirror didn't feel like her. Too stiff. Too composed. Like a wax version of herself, automated and untouchable.

"From the top," the choreographer called.

She obeyed.

They'd been running the set for over an hour, prepping for a showcase stage later that week. The New Jeans girls were used to tight schedules, but Minji couldn't shake the sense that everything around her was louder than usual. The lights. The music. The weight in her chest.

By the time Hanni tripped slightly on a turn and the instructor called a five-minute break, Minji's arms were trembling. Not from exertion.

From restraint.

She moved to the corner of the studio, water bottle in hand, towel draped around her neck. But she didn't drink. Didn't wipe her face. Just stared at the polished wood floor, willing her thoughts into order.

"Okay," came Hanni's voice, light but insistent. "You're thinking too loud."

Minji blinked.

Hanni dropped to the floor beside her, legs stretched out, head tilted. "You've said exactly two words all morning, and one of them was 'again.' Spill, unnie."

Minji tried to deflect. "I'm just tired."

Hanni raised an eyebrow. "Tired doesn't make you rehearse like you're being haunted."

That almost earned a laugh. Almost.

"I don't know what I'm feeling," Minji said, voice quiet. She didn't mean to say it, but once it was out, it was like a crack in glass. Everything else followed.

"I keep replaying this moment in my head," she went on. "That rehearsal hallway. Solar and Jungwon. They weren't touching. They weren't even looking at each other, really. But the air between them..."

She didn't finish the sentence. She didn't have to.

Hanni's face softened. "You felt it?"

Minji nodded once. "I didn't even realize how much it would hurt to see them like that. Like they belonged in the same frame."

There was a pause. The kind of silence that stretched long enough to make her wonder if she'd said too much.

Then Hanni said, "Do you think you were jealous of Jungwon?"

Minji's stomach twisted. "No," she said, too fast. Then, slower, "I don't think so."

"Then what is it?"

Minji exhaled shakily. "I thought I understood her. Solar. I thought maybe that meant something."

Hanni didn't interrupt.

"I'd watch her," Minji continued. "On stage, yeah, but also... in those little moments. Backstage, when no one was looking. She smiles with her whole body when she thinks no one's watching. And when she zones out? She does this thing where she tugs at her sleeve with her thumb, like she's trying to ground herself."

Her voice cracked slightly. "I saw those things. I noticed them. I thought that meant I was—close. Like I was... special for seeing her like that."

Hanni's gaze didn't waver. "But she didn't choose you."

Minji flinched. "No... She didn't."

"And Jungwon?"

"I don't think he's trying to take her away from anyone," Minji said bitterly. "That's the worst part. He doesn't have to. He just exists near her, and she leans toward him like a plant finding light."

Her voice dropped to a whisper. "I wanted to be that light."

Hanni was quiet for a long moment. Then, gently: "Maybe it's not just that you wanted to see her."

Minji turned.

"Maybe," Hanni said, "you wanted her to see you, too."

The words landed like a blow.

Minji's hands clenched in her lap.

"I think," she said, barely audible, "I'm in love with her."

Saying it out loud was like opening a wound. Clean and devastating.

"I don't know what to do with that," she whispered. "I don't know how to carry it."

Hanni shifted closer, wrapping an arm around her shoulder. "You don't have to carry it alone. Or fix it. Or even understand it all right now."

Minji leaned into the contact, just slightly. "She doesn't feel the same."

"Maybe not," Hanni said. "Or maybe she doesn't know what she feels either."

"But I don't want to become a problem," Minji said. "I don't want her to look at me like I'm just another headline waiting to happen."

"You're not," Hanni said. "You're someone who loves quietly. That's not a threat. That's a gift."

Minji swallowed the lump rising in her throat.

"I just miss the version of me that didn't ache all the time."

Hanni tightened her grip. "She's still in there. Just waiting for you to give her some air."

For the first time that day, Minji closed her eyes. And breathed.




The dining table hadn't been this full in years.

Not since Chaerin's last rehab stint. Not since Yein started college. Not since Jaehyun stopped pretending he wasn't watching them from the shadows.

Now, all five Lee siblings were here—sitting around a spread of takeout containers and mismatched dishes, like some kind of dysfunctional peace treaty had been signed under a full moon.

Solar stabbed a dumpling with her chopsticks and glanced down the table. "I feel like I walked into a sitcom reboot where no one remembers what happened in season one."

Chaerin, halfway through her third soju shot, snorted. "Oh my God, you're right. I call chaotic bisexual icon."

"You already are," Yein muttered without looking up from her phone. "In the worst way."

Chaerin grinned. "Aw, baby sis, you missed me."

"No, I just forgot how loud you are."

"Girls," Solar warned.

"Girls?" Juyeon piped up. "So I'm what, a ghost? A genderless side character? Rude."

Yein looked up, expression blank. "You're comic relief, Juyeon."

"She's not wrong," Chaerin added cheerfully. "But you're a cute side character. Like the himbo who shows up to offer bad advice and skincare tips."

Juyeon beamed. "Okay, but actually, have you seen my jawline lately? I switched to that snail mucin serum Solar gave me and—"

"Focus," Jaehyun interrupted, voice calm but firm.

The table quieted instantly.

He hadn't spoken much since they sat down, but his presence was like a shadow on the wall—always there, even when unacknowledged. Arms crossed. Sunglasses on. Suit jacket still buttoned. A man who looked like he belonged in a drama about underground empires and repressed emotions.

Solar exhaled. "So... this is nice."

"That's not what you said last week when your scandal broke," Chaerin said innocently, biting into a spring roll.

"Wait, what scandal?" Juyeon asked through a mouthful of rice.

Yein didn't look up. "The one where netizens think Solar's dating a guy from an eSports team."

"Ooooh right. That hot one with the messy hair. What's his name again? Rampage? Rocket?"

"Rascal," Chaerin said, eyes glittering.

Juyeon snapped his fingers. "Yes! Him. Honestly, I see it."

"There is no it," Solar muttered.

"Oh, sweetie," Chaerin said, tilting her head. "You literally looked at him like he invented gravity during that Twitch clip. And don't even get me started on Jungwon."

Solar blinked. "What does Jungwon have to do with anything?"

Yein finally looked up. "You've been trending with both of them. Statistically, that's impressive."

"I'm not trending," Solar said. "I'm being dissected."

"Same thing," Yein replied, deadpan.

Chaerin leaned forward. "Wait. Be honest. Are you torn? Like, emotionally?"

Juyeon perked up. "Ooh, is this one of those idol love triangles? Like in those dating sims Chaerin used to play when she was high?"

"They were comfort games," Chaerin said, affronted. "Don't shame me."

Solar groaned. "You are all insufferable."

"You love us," Yein said flatly.

Solar reached for the kimchi like it might shield her from the chaos. "I love you the way people love thunder—respectfully and from a distance."

Jaehyun stood.

The others quieted again as he walked toward the balcony, hands tucked behind his back like a man sentencing someone to exile.

Solar didn't hesitate. She stood and followed him out.

The night air was crisp, cool against her flushed cheeks. The city glittered below—Seoul alive with noise and neon. Jaehyun stood at the railing, spine straight, face unreadable.

They stood in silence for a moment.

Then:

"Did you bring us all here just to monitor me?"

Jaehyun didn't turn. "You've been reckless."

"I've been busy."

"Busy is what people say when they don't want to admit they're overwhelmed."

Solar bristled. "I'm not overwhelmed."

"Then why are you twitching like someone's going to shove you off a cliff?"

"I'm not—" She stopped. Exhaled. "I'm trying."

Jaehyun finally turned to face her. His voice dropped lower. Formal. Cold. Protective. "You're being watched, Solar. Every frame, every blink, every whisper. And not just by fans. By people who want things from you. Or want to break you."

She didn't reply.

"I don't care if you're playing for a gaming team or performing on a stage. But if you're going to keep doing this... you need to know what's coming."

He paused. Then, with quiet finality: "Be ready for more storms. And don't trust anyone who hides from thunder."

She stared at him. For once, she saw the worry beneath the sunglasses. Not soft. But real. "I didn't ask for any of this," she whispered.

"You didn't have to," he replied. "You were always going to be the lightning."

They stood there a moment longer, the city humming below.

Then Jaehyun stepped back inside, closing the sliding door behind him with a quiet click. Solar stayed behind. Arms folded. Heart thudding. 



It started with a chill.

Not dramatic. Not sudden. Just a small shift in the air as Solar stepped into the Le Sserafim dorm bedroom she shared with Yunjin and Eunchae.

The others were out—vocal practice running late. The dorm was quiet. Still. Almost too still.

She set her bag down. Toed off her shoes. Rubbed at her temple like she could smooth out the lingering tension from the sibling dinner.

And then she noticed it.

Her gaming chair was turned the wrong way.

Not dramatically. Just a few degrees off. But she never left it like that—not angled toward the mirror. Not facing the bed.

She moved slowly, reaching for the back of it to adjust. As she did, her eyes caught something nestled just beneath the edge of her mousepad.

A box.

Small. Matte black. Tied with a ribbon so thin it looked like string.

She hadn't left it there.

And no one else should've been in her room.

Solar stared at it for a full thirty seconds before picking it up. It was light. Weightless, almost. Inside was a folded scrap of paper, creased sharply.

No name.

No signature.

Just six words, written in precise, looping handwriting:

you're not as careful as you think.

Her stomach dropped.

The ribbon slipped from her fingers.

It wasn't the first time.

The first had been subtle—an open locker at the HYBE building she could've sworn she'd shut. Then the misplaced lipstick in her bag, the one she hadn't used in months. Last week, someone had left a paper crane on her desk at the studio. No one claimed it. She'd told herself it was just a fan, or maybe Chaerin being weird.

But this—this was intentional.

Deliberate.

Personal.

Her hands trembled as she unfolded the tissue-paper lining beneath the note.

Inside, tucked like a final insult, was a printed screenshot.

From her latest Overwatch scrim.

Her Tracer blinking past a choke point. Rascal's Echo hovering above the frame. Her killfeed lighting up in gold.

It wasn't a fan grab. It was from her POV.

Someone had access to her VODs.

She swallowed. Her pulse thundered in her ears.

Only her team had access to those recordings.

And HYBE.

And, technically... anyone who'd ever touched her PC.

She backed away from the desk like it had teeth.

The bathroom mirror caught her reflection—face pale, shoulders hunched, eyes wide and frantic.

Danielle.

It had to be her.

The feeling had been there for weeks now. That uncanny sense of being studied. The look in Danielle's eyes when they passed in the hallway—bright, empty, dissecting. Like she was cataloguing every flaw, every secret.

The way she'd smiled the day Solar's scandal broke. Not gleeful. Not fake.

Just... satisfied.

Solar paced the room once. Twice. Her hand hovered over her phone.

Should she tell Yunjin?

Chaewon?

Rascal?

She stopped.

No.

Not yet.

If she was wrong, it would only make things worse. And if she was right—

The message was clear.

"You're not as careful as you think."

Which meant someone wanted her to be scared.

Solar exhaled slowly, forcing her shoulders down, heart pounding like a warning bell.

She picked up the box, note and all, and tucked it into the bottom drawer of her desk—beneath an old hoodie and a box of cables.

Then she sat at her PC and turned it on like nothing had happened.

As if the shadow clinging to her wasn't real.

But she knew better.

Danielle wasn't just watching her.

She was waiting.




She sent the message at 9:43 p.m.

No explanation. No emoji. No follow-up.

Need air. My roof.

Short. Blunt. The kind of message that only made sense if you were already carrying something too heavy to hold upright.

She didn't expect a reply.

But twenty minutes later, she heard the elevator ding. The soft click of the rooftop door unlocking. Footsteps—measured, deliberate—crossing the concrete.

Jungwon didn't say her name when he found her.

He just joined her at the edge, hoodie half-zipped, hair damp from either a shower or the night air. He looked like he'd come straight from practice—sweats, sneakers, wrists still red where he'd pulled off tape.

They stood in silence for a moment. The kind that didn't need breaking.

Down below, the Seoul skyline glittered like a city made of glass and nerves. Every window another camera. Every streetlight another eye.

Solar stared out at it, arms crossed tight against her chest. "I wasn't going to call anyone," she said finally.

"I figured."

"I'm fine," she added quickly. "I just needed... air."

"I didn't come to question that," Jungwon said.

She turned to glance at him.

He wasn't looking at her. Just out into the dark, the sharp line of his jaw illuminated by the faint red glow of a rooftop exit sign. His hands were buried in his pockets. His shoulders relaxed—but not casual. More like controlled softness. Practiced calm.

The kind that comes from being raised in rooms where emotion wasn't a luxury.

She exhaled.

"There was another note," she said. "In my room."

Now he looked at her. Carefully. "Another one?"

Solar nodded, swallowing back the tremor in her voice. "Same style. Same message, basically. Just... colder."

She didn't mention the ribbon. Or the box. Or the fact that the second "gift" came with a screenshot from her private VOD folder.

She wasn't ready to say it aloud yet. The moment she did, it would be real.

Jungwon stayed quiet.

Then, after a long pause, he asked softly, "Do you think it was her?"

"I don't know." Solar's voice cracked slightly. "But I can feel her watching. I keep brushing it off, like maybe I'm being paranoid. But it's not going away."

"I believe you."

Three words. Solid. Unshaken. She looked at him again. "Why?"

He shrugged once. "You're not the kind of person who invents fear just to feel something."

Solar let the silence settle between them again. Not heavy—just tired. Like a bruise forming beneath the skin.

"I hate that she makes me feel small," she said. "That I second-guess every breath. Every photo. Every name I type in my phone."

"You're not small," Jungwon said. "You just think you have to handle everything alone."

That one hit. She didn't answer right away.

Then: "Sometimes I feel like I'm carrying three versions of myself in my chest. The idol. The gamer. The girl no one really knows."

"Then give the third one a name," he said. "So she stops getting swallowed by the other two."

Her breath caught. Just slightly. It wasn't a command. It wasn't even advice. It was an invitation.

The wind picked up slightly, brushing hair across her face. She tucked it behind her ear and looked away again.

"I don't know if she has a name yet."

"Then stay long enough to find it," Jungwon advises.

They stood there for a long time after that. No noise. No words. Just presence.

The rooftop lights flickered once. A plane crossed the sky overhead. Somewhere on the street below, a bus braked with a low hiss.

"I'm sorry I dragged you out here," Solar murmured.

"You didn't drag me," he replied.

She smiled faintly. "You didn't even ask why I texted. You just came."

"I didn't need to know why."

She tilted her head. "Then why did you?"

He looked at her fully now. Eyes steady. Voice quiet. "I'm here. Whether you want me to be or not."

The words hung in the air like a secret waiting to be trusted. Solar didn't say anything. She didn't ask him to leave.

She just stepped a little closer—only by a breath—and stood there beside him, letting the silence be something she didn't have to fill.

Not tonight. And somewhere behind all the noise and shadows and eyes...

She felt the third version of herself—unnamed, unfinished—begin to breathe.



















44: Preparation, Not Peace

Solar didn't remember the last time the dorm was this quiet.

Not the performative kind of quiet—when everyone was "resting" but still checking emails, scrolling fan café posts, or reviewing stage footage with one AirPod in. This was different. A real hush. Heavy and slow like mist on a winter morning, curling into the corners of the apartment and settling in for a stay.

She stood barefoot in the hallway, hoodie sleeves too long, water glass in hand, blinking into the stillness like it might blink back.

HYBE had declared a full two-day break now that ANTIFRAGILE promotions had officially ended, and for once, they'd actually meant it. No secret interviews. No surprise practice blocks. Just silence.

And Solar hadn't known what to do with it.

Her phone had buzzed all morning—group chats lighting up with tournament talk, fan edits, late-night food plans. Super had sent something unhinged about smuggling a bag of shrimp chips onto the flight back to Seoul, while Glister kept changing his Discord status to fake leaks like: Solar x Rascal canon? 👀.

She hadn't replied to any of them yet.

Instead, she wandered the dorm.

The fridge had a sticky note from Chaewon that read:

Off to visit my parents. Rest or I'll sedate you. PS, don't eat my yogurt again or I swear.

Eunchae had left earlier with Kazuha—something about dance class and "duet TikToks," which Solar didn't have the emotional stamina to unpack. Sakura was gone too, though her laptop was still open on the kitchen table, Discord running in the background.

Only Yunjin remained—half-buried in a fleece blanket, one leg dangling off the couch, headphones in, humming faintly to whatever indie playlist she'd fallen asleep to. Her phone was lit with a paused voice message. Probably another unfinished rant to Solar.

Solar sipped her water and leaned against the windowsill, watching the sun crawl up the side of a nearby building. The glass felt cool under her fingertips.

Everything felt cool. Calm. Almost unreal.

It had been months since she'd felt this kind of stillness without guilt pressing down on her ribs. No makeup on. No call time looming. No comment section eating her alive.

Just her.

And the quiet.

For a long time, she didn't move. Just breathed. Let herself notice things: the smell of Yunjin's cinnamon tea still hanging in the air, the faint buzz of the fridge, the crease in the hallway rug that she kept tripping over but never fixed.

This was her life, and she was finally beginning to live it.

Not just survive it.

Solar set her glass down and hugged her knees to her chest, letting her chin rest on them.

She thought of the girl she was at the start of the year—restless, terrified, addicted to approval. The girl who lived like she was being hunted, not watched. The one who didn't believe she belonged anywhere unless someone else said so.

She wasn't gone. Not really. But she didn't run the show anymore.

Now there was room for someone else.

Someone steadier.

Someone who could breathe in this silence and not flinch.

And even though the next few days would be anything but quiet—the Grand Finals looming, Danielle still circling, Rascal growing distant, Jungwon stepping ever closer—Solar felt... ready.

Not invincible. Not fearless.

Just ready. That was new. That was hers.

Yunjin stirred just as the afternoon sun began to shift—amber light falling low across the living room rug, casting stripes of gold across her face. She stretched with a groan, kicked the blanket off dramatically, then blinked over at Solar, still curled on the armchair.

"You've been sitting there like a haunted doll for an hour," she croaked.

"I'm vibing," Solar hums.

Yunjin narrowed her eyes. "You don't vibe. You scheme. Or brood."

Solar shrugged. "Maybe I'm evolving."

Yunjin sat up slowly, hair sticking out on one side, makeup half-smudged from sleep. "You do seem weirdly... still today."

"I think it's the lack of forced smiling."

"No way. HYBE gave you the day off and you're not spiraling?"

Solar shook her head. "Not spiraling."

"Are you sure? Not even a tiny spiral?"

Solar smiled faintly. "More like a dot. But it's a quiet dot."

Yunjin pulled the blanket into her lap and looked at her properly now. "You feel different," she said softly. "Not in a bad way. Just... different."

Solar didn't answer immediately. Her gaze dropped to her knees, where her hands rested lightly. "I think I do too."

Yunjin tilted her head.

Solar exhaled. "I used to think rest was something I had to earn. That if I wasn't productive, I didn't deserve peace. Or love. Or a second chance."

Yunjin's expression softened.

"I've been running on guilt for so long, I forgot what it felt like to be okay," Solar said. "But today... I didn't hate myself when I woke up. I didn't want to apologize for existing."

The quiet between them stretched—not awkward, but reverent. Like the moment deserved space.

"I don't know what changed," Solar admitted. "Maybe everything's just caught up to me. Or maybe it's Jungwon. Or Rascal. Or... you."

Yunjin blinked. "Me?"

"You've always believed in me. Even when I didn't give you a reason to."

Yunjin scoffed, but her voice was thick. "Bestie. You are the reason."

Solar smiled, eyes damp. "Do you know how hard it is to believe in someone who hates themselves?"

"Do you know how hard it is to stop loving someone like you?" Yunjin shot back, but her tone wasn't sharp. It was raw. Fierce. "You think I stuck around because I liked your sense of humor? You've been a mess since day one."

"Wow... Slander."

"I stayed," Yunjin goes on, voice steady now, "because I knew this version of you was in there. Under all the armor. The exhaustion. The fear."

Solar looked at her. Really looked.

Yunjin reached across the space between them and tapped Solar's knee. "Maybe the version of you we all believed in... she's finally starting to believe in herself too."

Something cracked open in Solar's chest—gently. Not pain. Just release.

She nodded once. Quiet. Grateful. Yunjin smiled like she knew. Like she'd been waiting for this moment the whole time.

Then, just as quickly, she ruined it.

"Also, if you start meditating and drinking matcha, I'm filing a complaint."

Solar laughed. Loud and full. The kind that made her shoulders drop and her ribs ache.

"Noted," she said. "No matcha. No enlightenment."

"Good. You're already unbearable enough."

Solar leaned her head back against the chair and stared up at the ceiling. "I don't know what's coming next," she murmured.

"You never do," Yunjin replied. "But that's never stopped you before."




[ voice call: 🧼scrim-boys-only🧼 ]

Solar clicked in and was immediately greeted by yelling.

"—I'm saying if they touch my shrimp chips again, I'll commit an international incident," Super declared. "Like full courtroom meltdown. Tears. Cameras. News headlines."

"You cried last time because they made you throw away your Capri-Sun," Glister deadpanned.

"BECAUSE IT WAS LIMITED EDITION!"

"I still think you made that flavor up," Smurf muttered.

"I literally didn't! It was guava mango!"

"Bro..." Nero's mic crackled. "No one believes in mango propaganda but you."

Solar smiled before she could stop herself. The Discord call sounded exactly like it always had—too loud, chaotic, overlapping voices everywhere. And yet underneath the jokes, there was a low current of something else. Tension. Excitement. Nerves.

The Grand Finals were two days away. And for once, she wasn't watching them from her bedroom screen. She was part of the team.

"Solar's here," Super announced like a herald summoning royalty. "Everyone, pretend to be professional."

"Impossible," Glister muttered.

"I think I'm insulted," she said, settling into her chair and adjusting her mic. "But it's too early to tell."

"Welcome, Seoul queen," Super said. "Your loyal peasants are boarding the peasant plane soon."

"We're flying business class," Nero grumbled.

"Shut up, peasant."

Solar laughed, but her attention drifted slightly as her eyes caught a username in the list—rascal.

He hadn't said anything yet, but his light was green. He was there.

Always there lately. Quiet. Reliable. But guarded. He hadn't DMed her since their last tournament scrim three days ago. And when he spoke during practice, his voice was measured, professional. Still her duo. Still her shield in-game. But off the clock?

He felt a thousand miles away.

She'd tried not to take it personally. Tried to remember that everyone dealt with pressure in their own way. And yet... the distance stung.

Crusty's voice entered the chat like a knife slicing through the room.

"Alright, clowns. Final scrim's done. No last-minute panic. You fly out in eight hours. I want asses in seats or I swear I will start subbing in gold-ranked forum trolls."

"You did that once," Glister said.

"Exactly. Try me."

"You guys have to sit next to Nero," Smurf added helpfully. "So pack earplugs. Or sedatives."

"Don't tempt me," Nero muttered.

Solar listened with half a smile. She'd missed this. The noise. The familiarity. The way this team made her feel like she wasn't two different people split down the middle.

"Solar," Crusty said suddenly.

She blinked. "Yeah?"

"You've been pulling weight like a vet. And the press noise? You ignored it. You showed up."

She felt herself sit a little straighter.

"You've earned your slot. Earned your respect," Crusty continued. "But the Grand Finals aren't just another match. They're everything. Home crowd, livestreamed, world watching. You make one mistake and everyone sees it. You frag like you did last week? They'll remember that too."

Solar nodded slowly. "Understood."

"I don't want 'understood,'" he said. "I want fire."

She swallowed. Then said, quietly but clearly: "I'm ready."

There was a beat of silence. Then Rascal spoke.

"Seoul's loud," he said. His voice was low and even. "It'll try to make you doubt everything. But don't listen to the noise."

Solar froze. His voice was soft, but there was something in it. Not quite warmth. Not detachment either. Something in-between.

"You taught me how to play through noise," she said gently.

There was a small pause. A breath. Then Rascal replied, "Then you'll be fine."

The quiet that followed wasn't awkward, but it had weight. Like everyone in the call had heard something that didn't need translating.

Crusty cleared his throat. "Alright. Final checklist's in the server. Pack. Sleep. Hydrate. And if anyone misses the group flight photo, I'm photoshopping their face onto a potato."

"Again," Glister said.

"I still have PTSD from that edit," Smurf mumbled.

Solar grinned. "Wasn't that the one where you gave Super anime eyes?"

"I looked majestic," Super declared.

"You looked possessed," Nero corrected.

The call began to dissolve into the usual spam—Super typing in all caps about airport security conspiracies, Nero uploading a cursed meme, Smurf sending a link to Korean convenience stores near the venue. Solar lingered a moment longer, listening to the chaos.

Then she noticed Rascal's name was still online. He hadn't left. But he hadn't said anything else either.

Just as she was about to disconnect, a message popped up in their group chat:

rascal: stay cracked. seoul's yours.

Solar stared at it. Four words. No emoji. No sarcasm. Just a quiet send-off from someone who clearly wasn't sure what else to say.

And somehow, that made it matter more.

She hovered over the reply button.

Didn't type anything. Didn't need to.

"See you soon," she whispered instead.

Then she clicked out.




The hallway outside the HYBE practice rooms was too quiet for a Tuesday morning.

Solar adjusted her hoodie, tugged her mask higher over her face, and tried not to overthink the cold coil of dread in her stomach. She'd only come in to grab something from the vocal studio—a forgotten charger, nothing important—and she told herself it was fine.

Everything was fine.

The finals were tomorrow. Her team was arriving today. Danielle hadn't done anything in weeks—nothing concrete. Just a box, a note, a flicker of something in the corner of her vision she couldn't prove.

But the silence made it worse. It made her feel like the next hit was coming, and she didn't know where from.

She stepped into the hallway just as the elevator dinged. She almost didn't see her.

Danielle stepped out of the elevator like she belonged there. Smooth. Slow. Hoodie zipped halfway, hair tied back in a low ponytail. No makeup, no entourage. Just her.

But her eyes found Solar instantly. And they didn't move. Solar froze. Danielle didn't speak.

She just stared.

There was no expression on her face. Not cruelty. Not friendliness. Just... observation. Like she was looking through Solar, not at her.

It was worse than if she'd smiled.

Solar shifted slightly, gripping the strap of her bag. She tried not to show it—the instinct to recoil. The way her breath caught. The way her fingers twitched toward the pocket where her phone was.

She didn't say anything. Didn't want to give Danielle the satisfaction.

But as she turned to leave, Danielle moved too—just a single step forward. Barely noticeable. But it sent a chill straight down Solar's spine.

She walked faster. Didn't run. Didn't look back.

By the time she reached the front lobby, her heart was thudding like she'd sprinted three blocks.

She ducked into a side lounge and sat down on the bench, pulling her mask down, forcing herself to breathe.

It wasn't anything, she told herself. She didn't say anything. She didn't touch you.

But she knew.

She could feel it in the way Danielle had looked at her. Like she was waiting. Like she already knew where Solar would be, what time, what hallway. Like it was all part of a script Solar hadn't seen yet.

Solar still hadn't told anyone—not about how extensive it had gotten. She may have mentioned the weird gifts to Jungwon once, in vague terms, but she hadn't told him the truth: that she was being watched. Tracked. That she could feel Danielle in her space. She hadn't told Chaewon. Not Yunjin. Not Rascal.

Though... then again, it was likely Jungwon already knew.

She kept thinking she could handle it. That maybe she was overreacting. That acknowledging it would give it power.

But now... she wasn't so sure. She pulled out her phone with shaking fingers. No new messages.

A few old ones stared back at her—Rascal's last Discord ping. Jungwon's quiet "I'm here." Yunjin's selfies from the night before.

Solar scrolled past them. And for the first time in days, she felt small again. Not broken. But watched.




Solar was waiting just outside the HYBE underground parking lot when the van pulled up.

A nondescript black Hyundai, windows tinted, engine idling. The side door slid open before the car even came to a full stop.

"SEOUL BABY!" Super practically launched himself onto the pavement, arms wide like he was greeting a red carpet. "I HAVE RETURNED TO MY MOTHERLAND."

"You're from Philly..." Nero muttered, stepping out behind him with a duffel bag and a half-eaten energy bar.

"Heritage is a spectrum," Super replied.

Smurf hopped out next, grinning. "Solar!"

She grinned back. "Hey."

Glister followed, waving like he was walking into a press conference. "Don't tell me you forgot us already."

"As if I could," she said. "You haven't stopped spamming me for seventy-two hours."

"That's friendship," Super said proudly.

Then Rascal stepped out. Last, as usual.

No announcement. No shout. Just a quiet presence, backpack slung over one shoulder, hair tucked under a cap. His eyes found hers immediately—and for a second, Solar forgot the noise around them.

He smiled. A small one. The kind that didn't reach his eyes.

"Hey," he said.

"Hey."

That was it.

Before she could say more, Super threw an arm around her shoulders. "You ready for this? Home turf, big stage, millions watching, no pressure?"

"She lives for pressure," Glister said. "She's literally an idol."

"She's also our DPS queen now," Smurf added. "Let her cook."

Solar laughed, trying to shake off the quiet chill still clinging to her spine after seeing Danielle that morning. The boys were loud, present, grounding—and for a moment, she let herself lean into the noise.

"You guys jetlagged?"

"I transcended jetlag," Super said. "I'm in a new dimension of exhaustion and caffeine. I call it... shattercore."

"That sounds like a TikTok trend," Nero muttered.

"It will be," Super replied. "After we win finals and I drop my skincare routine."

Solar shook her head, but smiled anyway. "Crusty's waiting upstairs. Media check-in's at four. Dinner after."

"Dinner?" Smurf perked up. "Is it Korean BBQ?"

"If it's not, I'll cry," Glister muttered.

"Same," Rascal said quietly, barely above a whisper.

Solar turned slightly at the sound of his voice—but he was already walking ahead, following the others into the HYBE building. Her gaze lingered on him.

There was something in the way he moved. Not tired. Not cold. Just... closed off. Like he was holding his breath and didn't know when he'd be allowed to exhale.




The dorm was quiet.

Not silent—faint traffic hummed from the street below, and someone a few floors up was playing guitar just off-key enough to be annoying. But in Jungwon's room, everything was still.

He sat cross-legged on the floor, his desk lamp casting a soft circle of light across the bedspread. A sleek black notebook rested in front of him. Matte cover. No logo. No frills.

Just clean space.

Room to write something that mattered.

He held the pen a moment longer than necessary. Not hesitating. Just... considering.
