Then, in neat, careful handwriting, he wrote one line on the first page:

Name her. The third version of you.

He stared at the words for a long time.

He didn't know exactly when he'd started thinking about it. Maybe on the rooftop. Maybe before that—watching her fight through the chaos, the cameras, the way she walked like she was bracing for impact even when she smiled.

She was three people in one body, and none of them had been given the time to become whole.

But lately...

Lately, he could feel her finding herself.

And she didn't see it yet, but he did.

He closed the notebook slowly. Ran his fingers along the spine. Then slid it into a small cloth pouch—black with a copper tie, simple but quiet. Like her.

No ribbon. No note. Nothing flashy.

Just the weight of something real.

He tucked it gently into the side pocket of his jacket, careful not to bend the edges.

Then he sat back against the wall, closed his eyes, and waited.

The Grand Finals were tomorrow.

He didn't know what she'd face.

But he knew who she was becoming.

And maybe—just maybe—she was starting to know it too.























45: The Grand Moment

The morning of the Grand Finals arrived like a punch disguised as a sunrise.

Solar stood at the hotel room window, watching the city come alive below her. Seoul was already buzzing—trucks unloading sound equipment, fans lined up outside the venue in Shock orange and Titans blue, journalists refreshing their scripts, cameras sharpening their focus.

Somewhere in that crowd were people waiting for her to fail. She didn't care. Not today.

Today, she wasn't the idol-gamer hybrid, the scandal magnet, the girl who wasn't supposed to make it past a first-round scrim. She was the starting DPS for the San Francisco Shock.

And she was about to win.

Her hands were steady as she zipped up her jersey. Shock orange and black. Her tag stitched just above the heart: solar. Lowercase. Clean.

Behind her, the room was still—Chaewon had left early to meet with HYBE staff backstage, and Sakura had slipped her a quiet "you got this" before disappearing with her camera crew. Even Yunjin hadn't texted yet.

It was the kind of silence that felt intentional.

Solar appreciated it.

She tied her hair up, smoothed the back of her jersey, and grabbed her team pass from the dresser. On her way out, her phone buzzed.

smurf 🐸: we're in the car. 10 mins out.

glister: super says he's gonna throw hands if there's no red carpet

nero: tell him the red carpet is for winners

super: 🖕

Solar smiled.

She tapped out a response:

solar: hurry up before the Titans start celebrating early

She didn't wait for replies.

The venue was packed. Arena lights. Smoke machines. Giant LED screens flashing Shock vs Titans in neon colors that felt almost too bold to be real. It looked like a dream—or a memory already being edited for the highlight reel.

Crusty met her at the entrance to the player prep room. His suit was crisp, his expression unreadable.

"Head in it?" he asked.

She nodded.

"Good." He looked at her for a long beat. "No nerves?"

"Only fuel."

He cracked a rare smile. "That's what I like to hear."

Inside the prep room, the boys were already running warm-ups. Glister spamming headshots in aim trainer. Nero drinking something that smelled like battery acid. Smurf meditating in his chair while Super narrated it like a documentary voiceover.

Solar slid into her station, headset hanging off one ear as she logged in.

No one had to tell her what to do.

She flexed her fingers once. Stretched her neck. Took one breath.

And locked in Tracer.

Twenty minutes later, they were walking the tunnel to the main stage.

She heard the roar before she saw it—tens of thousands of people, cheering, screaming, holding signs that ranged from SHOCK HER to TRACER IS MOTHER. Cameras flashed. Spotlights turned the air into fog.

But Solar didn't flinch.

She kept walking.

Rascal was beside her, as always, but he didn't speak. Neither did she. They didn't have to.

Just before they stepped onto the stage, he looked at her and said, "Let's burn 'em down."

She met his eyes. And nodded. "Together."

The lights dropped.

A pulse of bass rumbled through the arena floor, followed by a swell of crowd noise so loud it rattled Solar's ribcage. Spotlights whipped across the stage as both teams emerged—San Francisco Shock in black and orange, Vancouver Titans in icy green and blue. The color war had begun.

A voice echoed through the arena speakers:

"Ladies and gentlemen, welcome to the 2025 Overwatch League Grand Finals!"

Fans screamed. Thundersticks clapped in rhythm. On-screen animations of Tracer and Reinhardt danced across the Jumbotron as the camera panned across each team.

Solar's face appeared on screen.

She didn't smile.

She just adjusted her headset and stared straight ahead.

The casters were already yelling over the noise.

"—and you cannot overstate the impact of Solar this season. From idol to icon, this is one of the most meteoric rises we've ever seen—"

"—and she's not just here for the PR. Her Tracer is deadly. Her coordination with Rascal? Lethal."

"The Titans are going to have to put her down early if they want to breathe."

The stage techs gave the go.

MAP 1: King's Row

Solar flexed her fingers once. The gloves Jungwon had given her weeks ago weren't on—too risky—but she still remembered the stitching. For the parts of you that don't get to rest.

This wasn't rest. This was war.

The game launched. Within seconds, the world blurred.

Adrenaline took over. Her screen lit up with movement—enemy supports darting behind cover, tanks absorbing fire, her cooldowns flashing in the corner. She blinked forward, dropped a Pulse Bomb, and—

DOUBLE KILL!

The crowd exploded. She barely registered it.

Rascal's voice came through comms, sharp and controlled. "Stick with me. Echo's diving left."

"I see it," she replied, already moving. "I'll cut them off."

KILLSTREAK: 4

Team Kill

"Let's GO!" Super shouted into comms.

The next round started. Titans came back swinging—more aggressive now, hunting her. She baited their tanks into a corner, blinked up behind the sniper, and—

Headshot

"Solar's cracked," Nero breathed.

"Keep it together," Crusty barked in their ears. "Map control. Don't get cocky."

Solar blinked into the next fight, fingers flying across the keys. Her blood felt like fire in her veins. She couldn't hear the crowd anymore.

Just the game. Just her.

And for once, she didn't feel like a guest in someone else's world. She belonged here. She owned it.

FINAL SCORE: SHOCK 3 - TITANS 0

The arena went absolutely feral. Confetti cannons burst. Orange and gold rained from the ceiling. Solar dropped her headset, breath catching as the gravity of it hit.

They'd won. They'd actually won.

Smurf jumped to his feet, arms wide. Super tackled Nero. Glister yelled something unintelligible into the crowd. Rascal stood slowly, blinking like he couldn't believe it either.

Solar stared at her screen. Tracer's idle animation bounced in the background, waiting.

She looked up. And the camera caught it.

The moment her expression cracked—not with fear or exhaustion, but joy.

Solar stood, half dazed, and turned toward her team.

And Rascal? He was already moving.

"AND THAT'S IT!! THE SAN FRANCISCO SHOCK ARE YOUR 2025 OVERWATCH LEAGUE GRAND CHAMPIONS!!"

The casters were practically screaming into the mic now. The crowd was unhinged—horns blaring, light sticks waving, signs shaking. The camera cut between stunned Titans players and the Shock bench as they exploded into each other's arms.

"THEY'VE DONE IT AGAIN! SOLAR—THE ROOKIE, THE WILDCARD, THE IDOL—JUST DELIVERED THE FINAL BLOW TO SECURE A 3–0 SWEEP!"

"Tracer diff! Tracer DIFF!"

"That was surgical—she played like she had nothing to lose and everything to prove!"

Confetti cannons burst above the stage. Gold and orange rained down like a storm.

Smurf was screaming. Glister was sobbing. Nero was punching the air. Super had grabbed a stray microphone and was shouting into it like a WWE announcer. "WE NEVER LEFT! WE NEVER LEFT!!"

Solar didn't even realize she was smiling until Smurf tackled her into a hug.

"You freaking legend!" he yelled into her ear.

Super was next, spinning her in a circle before letting her go. "That's MY DPS!"

Glister shoved a Shock flag into her hands. Nero wrapped an arm around her shoulders. Rascal stood just behind them all—smiling. Quiet. But proud.

She met his eyes for half a second. Something passed between them.

It happened backstage.

The rest of the team was still on the main stage doing interviews and taking photos. Crusty had pulled a few players aside for media briefings. Solar slipped into the hallway behind the press corridor—just for a breath. Just to reset.

But Rascal had followed her. She turned at the sound of his footsteps. Neither of them spoke.

The air between them still crackled with adrenaline. Her hair stuck to the back of her neck. His hoodie was half-zipped, and she could see the faint tremble in his fingers where he held onto nothing.

"You were insane out there," he said quietly.

"You were better," she replied.

His mouth twitched like he wanted to argue, but couldn't. They were close now. Too close. And for a moment—one impossible moment—Solar didn't think.

She just moved.

Their lips met with the suddenness of a misstep, like both of them had leaned forward at the same time and realized too late what was happening.

It wasn't soft. It wasn't planned. It was all heat and static and confusion.

And then it was over. They pulled apart like the air had cracked between them. Rascal stared at her, eyes wide. Horrified.

Solar didn't move. He took one step back.

"I—I didn't mean—" he started, then stopped.

She opened her mouth, but no sound came out. And then— Footsteps echoed down the hallway.

Rascal's gaze flicked toward the sound. He froze.

Jungwon.

Holding a small black notebook in one hand.

The air in the hallway shifted the moment Jungwon appeared.

Rascal didn't hear the footsteps at first. Just the static in his ears—the sound of his own breathing, too loud, too shallow. His heartbeat thudded against his ribs like it was trying to escape.

Then he felt it. That presence. He turned.

And there he was.

Jungwon stood at the far end of the corridor, perfectly still, framed by a sliver of golden light bleeding through the doorway behind him. The shadows of passing staff moved like ghosts past his shoulder, but he didn't flinch. His eyes were locked on Rascal.

He didn't look surprised. He looked like he'd known. In his left hand, a black notebook. Wrapped in fabric. Untouched.

Rascal's stomach dropped.

He didn't move. Couldn't.

Solar was still behind him, silent, stunned. Rascal didn't dare look at her now. Not after what just happened. Not when his lips still felt like they belonged to a mistake.

Jungwon's gaze didn't shift. Didn't drift.

It pinned Rascal in place like a spotlight. Not angry. Not accusatory. Just... unwavering. Like he was waiting to see who Rascal would decide to be.

"I didn't mean to," Rascal said suddenly, voice low and wrecked. He hated how small it sounded. "It was... heat of the moment," he went on. "Adrenaline. We weren't thinking. I swear—" He faltered.

The silence between them felt like it echoed. Jungwon didn't speak. Didn't move. Didn't need to.

Rascal stepped forward, just once, like that might somehow soften what had just happened. "She's confused. You know that. I know that."

Jungwon's brow didn't even twitch.

Rascal's voice cracked. "She's not ready for anything. Not with me. Not with you. She's—she's been through too much. I wasn't supposed to—"

He stopped. Exhaled. Tried again.

"I told myself I'd be her friend. Just that. Nothing more. I wanted to protect her. I meant to protect her."

Another pause. The notebook in Jungwon's hand shifted slightly as he adjusted his grip.

"She's got too many people pulling at her already," Rascal whispered. "I didn't want to be another one."

He swallowed hard, then said the thing that hurt the most.

"She deserves better."

Jungwon blinked. Once. It was the smallest thing. But it broke the silence like glass. And then, gently—so gently—it was his voice:

"Do you love her?"

Rascal felt like the floor tilted beneath him. His throat tightened. The words clawed at the back of his mouth, but none of them came out. He looked away. Then back.

"I—" His voice failed. "I don't know."

But Jungwon did. His expression didn't change. But something behind his eyes flickered. A shift. A quiet recognition. Like he understood something Rascal didn't even want to admit.

They stood like that for a long moment—two very different kinds of protectors. One bleeding silently. The other bracing for a war he hadn't asked for but would fight anyway.

Then Rascal stepped back. Not dramatically. Not in shame. But like someone surrendering the battlefield because he finally realized the war had already been lost.

He still didn't look at Solar. Didn't reach for her. Didn't say goodbye.

He just turned and walked away, footsteps echoing like regrets against the polished floor.

And Jungwon?

He stayed rooted in place.

The notebook still in his hand.

Solar didn't speak. Her mouth opened slightly—whether to call out, to apologize, or to disappear, she didn't know.

But Jungwon didn't look at her. Not yet. Instead, as the hallway emptied again, he exhaled once—soft and sharp.

Then turned around. And walked away.












46: Golden Hours

The first thing she noticed was the warmth.

Soft sunlight spilled across the hotel sheets, filtered through sheer curtains that swayed slightly from the hum of the heater. Her room smelled faintly like cheap flowers—courtesy of a congratulatory bouquet someone had delivered in the chaos last night—and the remnants of someone's leftover fries sat on the table by the window.

Her phone was buzzing. Again.

Solar groaned and turned her face into the pillow.

She hadn't even taken off her jersey—just collapsed after the afterparty, still in full Shock gear, makeup smeared, hair in a half-hearted ponytail that had given up hours ago. She'd meant to shower. To journal. To maybe write something nice to the boys on Discord.

But the moment she sat on the bed, it was over.

Now, her body ached. Not painfully, but in that earned kind of way. Like every cell had been used. Every nerve fired.

She rolled onto her back, blinking at the ceiling, then at the mess of her phone on the nightstand.

82 unread messages.

Multiple missed calls. Twitter notifications off the rails. Discord pings still chiming every few seconds. She reached for the device with a groggy hand and cracked one eye open.

trending: #SolarSweep
trending: San Francisco Shock
trending: Rascal + Solar (?????)

Oh no.

She opened her tagged photos first—her fingers still clumsy.

The first one was a clip. Tracer's POV—hers. A blink through the Titans' backline. A perfect pulsebomb drop. The killfeed lighting gold.

Then cut to her on stage, yanking off her headset, face lit up, team swarming her.

Solar. She's HIM. She's HER. She's EVERYTHING.

She scrolled.

Fanart. Memes. Super with his arms around her, yelling. Nero holding a sign someone had drawn of her in a crown. A video of her waving at the crowd, eyes wide like she couldn't believe it.

The comments were endless.

She just rewrote the script.
Rookie of the year? Try face of the league.
I can't even hate—she earned it.
Is Rascal in love or what.
That hug was... personal.

Her smile flickered. She remembered that hug. Smurf, Glister, Nero—chaotic, loud, joyful. Then Rascal. The quiet one.

The one who stood just behind the crowd and looked at her like something was breaking and healing at the same time.

The kiss had happened only moments later. Not planned. Not even wanted, exactly. Just... momentum. Proximity. Static. Too many feelings and no space between them.

And yet— Her heart hadn't jumped. It had stilled. Not because she didn't care about Rascal. She did. Deeply.

But because the moment should've felt right. And it didn't.

She sat up slowly, tugging her knees to her chest. The jersey pulled tight across her back. Her fingers shook slightly as she set the phone down again.

Her mind was still playing it in loops:

The heat of Rascal's breath. The way his hand hovered like he didn't know where to place it. Her own lips, soft but unmoved. The look on his face after—regret like a shadow falling too fast.

And then—

Jungwon.

Just... there. Silent. Still.

Holding that little black notebook like it had weight. He hadn't even looked at her. He'd only looked at Rascal.

And that—more than the kiss, more than anything—was what haunted her now.

Why? Why did it matter so much? Why did her stomach twist when she thought about disappointing him?

They weren't anything. Not really. They'd had a few intense conversations. A rooftop moment. A slow, quiet closeness that hadn't been named.

No touches. No promises. And yet— The idea that he'd seen her and Rascal and assumed something... made her feel sick. Not because of guilt. But because it meant something was shifting.

Inside her. Maybe it had been for a while.

Maybe every conversation with Jungwon—every stare, every shared silence—had been moving her closer to this moment. The realization that what she felt for him was not the same as what she felt for Rascal. It wasn't warm and simple. It was slow. Magnetic. Like gravity.

Dangerous.

Solar rested her forehead against her knees. She didn't want to think about this today. She wanted to celebrate.

To scream. To brag. To replay every kill she'd made in the Grand Finals until the clips were burned into her retinas.

She deserved that. She wanted that. And yet—

She sat in the silence of a hotel room, her fingers curled around a blanket she hadn't even remembered pulling over herself, wondering whether Jungwon was angry.

Not because he had a right to be. But because she didn't want him to be. Because it mattered.

And she didn't know what to do with that yet.



The dorm was quiet when Solar stepped inside.

Not silent—somewhere in the distance, Eunchae was humming off-key and the washing machine was thudding like it had a personal vendetta—but it was quiet enough to feel strange. After the roar of the arena, the screaming fans, the backstage chaos, this felt like stepping into another world.

Solar slipped off her shoes and dropped her bag by the door. She was still wearing her Shock jersey, but now it was paired with sweatpants and a tangled braid she'd thrown together in the car. Her eyeliner was halfway gone. There were glitter flakes stuck to her neck.

She wasn't planning to stay long.

Just long enough to grab fresh clothes, maybe a charger, and breathe.

The hotel was fine—lavish, even—but it wasn't hers. This was. This tiny little apartment full of snacks, lip balm, and chaos. Her sanctuary.

She made it three steps before a voice called out from the kitchen.

"You're not even gloating. Suspicious behavior."

Solar blinked.

Yunjin stood at the counter in an oversized t-shirt, holding a tub of cookie dough and a spoon like a microphone. Her hair was up in a bun, and she looked like she hadn't slept—but her eyes were too sharp to be tired.

"Excuse me?" Solar said.

"You," Yunjin said, pointing the spoon at her like a sword. "Should be screaming. Flipping furniture. Printing out memes of yourself. You just won the freaking Grand Finals, and you're walking in here like you forgot your laundry detergent."

Solar blinked again. Yunjin squinted. "I'm calling it. Something happened."

"Nothing happened."

"Liar."

"I'm just tired."

"You're Solar. Tired you still talks trash."

Solar hesitated, then crossed her arms. "Is this an interrogation?"

Yunjin licked cookie dough off the spoon. "Depends. Are you gonna tell me why you look like someone who won a trophy but lost a bet?"

There was a long pause. Then Solar sat on the armrest of the couch and exhaled. "I kissed Rascal."

Yunjin froze mid-bite. Then lowered the spoon.

"Okay... I need context. And possibly electrolytes."

"It wasn't like that," Solar said quickly. "It was—it wasn't planned. We were backstage. Adrenaline was high. We just... it happened."

Yunjin blinked.

Solar rubbed her face. "I didn't even think. One second we were talking, the next..."

"And?"

"And it felt wrong."

Yunjin didn't say anything. Just leaned against the counter.

"Not bad," Solar said. "Not disgusting. Just... wrong. Like I'd walked into someone else's memory."

Yunjin nodded slowly.

Solar looked down at her hands. "And then Jungwon was there."

"Oh."

"Yeah."

Yunjin walked over and handed her the tub of cookie dough. Solar accepted it without question.

"I don't know what he saw," Solar said. "But he looked at Rascal. Not me. And then he left."

"Yikes."

"I know."

"And now you're here. In your jersey. Not celebrating. Not screaming."

"I am happy," Solar said quickly. "I'm proud. I'm—this is everything I wanted."

"But?"

"But I keep thinking about him walking away."

Yunjin sat beside her on the couch. "You think he's mad?"

"I don't know. He didn't look mad. Just... calm. Quiet. But different."

Yunjin nodded.

"And I don't know why it matters so much."

"That he's mad?"

"That he saw."

There was a beat.

"Maybe," Yunjin said carefully, "because there's a part of you that wanted him to see something else."

Solar looked at her.

"And maybe," Yunjin added, even softer, "because you've always known it wasn't Rascal."

Solar didn't answer. She didn't need to. The silence said enough.

Yunjin nudged her shoulder. "You're not a villain, you know."

"I feel like one."

"You're not."

Solar dug her spoon into the cookie dough. "I just thought I'd feel clearer after winning."

Yunjin smiled faintly. "Sometimes the spotlight makes the shadows sharper."

Solar leaned her head on her shoulder.

And for a moment, everything went quiet again. Not from tension. But from knowing—really knowing—that someone saw you and stayed.




Minji hadn't meant to watch the stream live.

She told herself she was busy—dance practice, vocal warmups, a NewJeans concept meeting that dragged two hours longer than planned. But somehow, her phone had still found its way into her hand during the final match. And somehow, her thumb had opened the broadcast. And somehow, she had watched.

All of it.

Solar's Tracer blinked through the Titans like she was made of lightning. The casters were losing their minds. The Shock boys were yelling. Rascal was smiling.

And when the win screen hit—

Minji should've felt proud.

She did feel proud. Somewhere.

But it was buried under something else.

Now, she sat curled on the floor of the practice studio, hoodie pulled tight around her knees, watching the same five-second clip over and over again.

Solar hugging Rascal backstage.
His arm brushing her back.
Her lips parted like she might say something.
Then the camera cutting away—too fast to tell.

Too fast to prove anything.

But not fast enough to stop Minji's mind from filling in the blanks.

She tried to tell herself it was nothing. Solar hugged all her teammates. She was emotional. It had been a huge win.

But her stomach told a different story.

Minji paused the video again.

Zoomed in.

There—just for a breath—Solar's eyes had flickered. Not in happiness. Not in triumph.

In confusion.

Or maybe that was just what Minji wanted to see.

She tossed the phone onto the floor and let her head fall back against the mirror behind her.

Why did this matter so much?

She and Solar weren't anything. Not officially. Not even unofficially. They hadn't kissed. They hadn't confessed. They hadn't even finished a conversation without running from it.

And yet—

It felt like losing something she'd never gotten to hold.

A notification lit her phone screen again.

new tweet from @solar_updates:
"solar and rascal backstage after the win"

Minji didn't open it.

She just closed her eyes and whispered, "You said you weren't looking at anyone."

And for the first time in days— She didn't believe it.




Solar returned to the hotel around 6 p.m., hair still damp from her second shower of the day, hoodie soft against her skin, a fresh Shock beanie pulled low to hide her exhausted eyes.

The victory haze hadn't lifted yet.

Her body still moved like she was caught in a dream—loose, floaty, untouchable. But underneath the glow was something else. Something she hadn't named yet.

The banquet suite was on the 9th floor, down the hallway marked with a crooked "CONGRATS CHAMPIONS" sign someone had made on Canva at the last second. She could already hear the noise from outside the door—music, shouting, the occasional can being cracked open like it owed someone money.

She knocked once out of habit before nudging the door open.

"YO!" Glister shouted, leaping up from the couch like she was a long-lost cousin.

Super popped into view with a slice of pizza in one hand and an orange streamer in the other. "THE MVP HAS ARRIVED!"

"You guys are insane," Solar said, smiling before she even meant to.

"Accurate," Nero called from the floor, where he was currently building a precarious structure out of cookie boxes, solo cups, and one of Smurf's shoes. "But in our defense, we've been waiting for you to start."

"You're the final boss," Smurf added, nodding solemnly from the corner where he was buried in three blankets and drinking banana milk like it was a fine wine.

Solar stepped in fully now, letting the noise surround her. The air smelled like garlic bread, sugary drinks, and deodorant that had long since stopped working. Someone had plugged a speaker into the hotel TV, and a playlist of victory songs blasted on loop. Orange and black streamers hung half-heartedly from the ceiling fan.

This wasn't elegant. Or glamorous. Or even that well planned.

But it was theirs.

And it was perfect.

She dropped her bag by the wall just in time to be pulled into a celebratory pile-on.

Smurf grabbed her by the wrist. Nero shouted "GROUP HUG!" like a battle cry. Glister spun her in a half-circle before letting Super lift her clean off the floor.

For a moment, she let it happen.

Let herself feel it.

The chaos. The joy. The disbelief still settling in their bones.

They had done it.

She had done it.

And for a few seconds, the only thing that existed was this room.

Then she pulled back, brushing hair from her face, flushed with warmth.

"Where's Rascal?" she asked, casually.

Too casually.

The moment dipped—not a crash, but a definite shift. A breath pulled too tight.

Smurf cleared his throat. "He said he was tired. Wanted to chill in his room tonight."

"Jet lag," Nero added.

"Or maybe he's just emotionally constipated," Glister offered.

Super gave a half-laugh but didn't make a joke. Which meant it was serious.

Solar nodded slowly.

She didn't push.

She didn't ask if anyone had talked to him since the match. Didn't ask if he'd said anything about her. She just accepted it.

But it didn't sit right.

Not because Rascal wasn't celebrating.

But because he hadn't even tried to pretend.

She'd felt it in his voice during their last Discord call. Seen it in the way he looked at her after the kiss. And now that she was here—after the win, after the press, after everything—she felt the weight of his absence like an anchor tied around her ankle.

But no one else said it.

So she didn't either.

Instead, Super shoved a paper crown on her head.

"Solar-sunbaenim, would you care to make an acceptance speech?" he asked, bowing dramatically with his pizza still in hand.

Solar smirked. "I'd like to thank my fans, my aim trainer, and the six hours of sleep I haven't had since Tuesday."

"I'd like to thank your Pulse Bomb accuracy for giving me an anxiety attack," Nero added.

"Honestly," Smurf said, raising his banana milk like a toast.

They all laughed again. The moment repaired itself, stitched together with memes and sugar and the kind of love that didn't always need words.

Still—

As Solar wandered the room, she noticed the empty seat near the window.

It had Rascal's name on it, even if no one said so.

His jacket had been left behind, folded over the back of a chair. Someone had put a cupcake there, untouched. A drink sat sweating on the table beside it.

And Solar's gaze lingered a little too long.

He should've been here.

He would've known exactly how to tease Super back. He would've found a way to make the crown look cool. He would've said something low and dry like "Don't let it go to your head, MVP," and she would've rolled her eyes and laughed and felt the ground under her feet again.

But he wasn't here.

And the room, as full as it was, felt just one heartbeat too quiet.

47: Space Between

The hotel hallways always felt quieter the day after.

Less adrenaline. Less foot traffic. The posters and balloons were still up, but they drooped now—half-deflated reminders of the storm that had already passed.

Solar stood outside Rascal's door, hoodie sleeves pulled over her hands, fingers nervously curled inside the cuffs.

Room 915.

She stared at the number like it might unlock on its own.

For a second, she considered walking away. Pretending she'd never come. She could say she forgot which room was his. Or that she was just passing by.

But her feet didn't move.

And her hand was already lifting to knock.

Three soft raps. Not too loud.

She waited.

Nothing.

She tried again, a little firmer.

Still nothing.

She leaned in slightly, listening for movement—shuffling, a voice, maybe the quiet buzz of his headset—but the room was still.

She bit the inside of her cheek.

He was here. She knew he was. Smurf had texted earlier that Rascal hadn't left the floor. Hadn't joined them for food. Hadn't even opened the door when Super dropped off snacks.

Solar stepped back.

She stared at the door for another moment, then whispered, "Okay."

She didn't leave a note. Didn't text.

She just walked back down the hallway, hands shoved into her sleeves, trying not to think about the way the silence had felt like an answer.

She didn't hear from him until late that night.

She was lying on her hotel bed, scrolling through fan posts, trying to distract herself from everything she wasn't saying out loud, when the screen lit up.

rascal [🟢]:
sorry i didn't open the door.

needed some air.

you were incredible. i meant that.

and i'm still your duo. always.

just maybe from a little farther back for now.

Solar stared at the message for a long time.

She didn't cry.

But her throat went tight.

She typed three different responses.

Deleted them all.

Then finally sent:

solar:
you don't have to go far.

He didn't reply.

But she saw the "read" mark.

And that was enough.

Solar hadn't meant to run into anyone.

She just needed air. After rereading Rascal's message too many times, she pulled on a hoodie and wandered toward HYBE's practice building—not for work, just movement. Her limbs needed somewhere to go. So did her thoughts.

The lobby was quiet this late in the evening. A few trainees passed with tired faces and tangled earbuds. Solar walked on autopilot, turning down a side hallway toward one of the old recording rooms.

She was halfway around the corner when she almost walked straight into Heeseung.

"Oh—"

"Whoa—"

They both paused, startled, then awkwardly laughed.

"Look who it is," Heeseung said. "The Overwatch prodigy herself."

Solar blinked, tucking a loose strand of hair into her cap. "Please tell me you didn't see the Twitter threads."

"Bestie," he said seriously, "I saw the thread and the fancam remix."

She groaned. "Kill me."

"Absolutely not. MVPs live forever."

Before she could reply, a familiar voice echoed behind him.

"Did someone say MVP?"

Solar turned as Chaerin strolled up in platform sneakers, sunglasses in her hair, holding an iced coffee like it was an accessory.

"You didn't tell me you were coming here," Solar said suspiciously.

"And you didn't answer my text when I said 'why are you the internet's main character again and why do I love it,'" Chaerin replied, grinning. "Rude."

Solar gave her a look. "Wait... Why are you even here?"

Chaerin blinked. Then her smile twitched. "Visiting."

Solar raised an eyebrow. "Visiting who?"

Chaerin didn't answer. Instead, she casually looped her arm through Heeseung's like it was no big deal. Solar stared at them. Heeseung stared at the floor. Chaerin took a triumphant sip of her coffee.

"You're kidding."

"I never kid," Chaerin said sweetly.

Heeseung muttered, "It's not—it's complicated."

"Complicated like 'oops, we kissed,' or complicated like 'we might destroy each other and that's kind of hot'?"

"Yes," Chaerin replied.

Solar opened her mouth, then gave up. "I don't even have the energy."

"Good," Chaerin said. "You'll need that energy to address the actual chaos, anyway."

"Which is?"

"The fan theories," Chaerin said, pulling out her phone. "I give you: Exhibit A."

She held up a TikTok compilation set to dramatic violin music—slow shots of Rascal glancing at Solar, their victory hug slowed down to 0.5x speed, someone's voiceover saying, "That's not a platonic duo stare, besties."

Solar went bright red. "I hate this timeline."

"Too bad," Chaerin said, grinning. "The internet thinks you're in love."

"Well, I'm not."

"Then tell that to the people writing fanfiction in the comments," Chaerin shot back. "I just came to witness the fallout."

Heeseung choked on a laugh.

"I'm going to die in this hallway," Solar said.

Chaerin nudged her. "Seriously though. You okay?"

Solar hesitated. "I think so."

"You looked off after the match. Not sad. Just... somewhere else."

"I was." Solar took a slow breath. "I thought winning would feel cleaner."

"It never is," Chaerin said gently.

Solar looked at her sister. Looked at Heeseung. At the weirdness of the moment.

Then she smiled faintly. "Thanks. I guess."

"Don't thank me," Chaerin said. "I'm mostly here for the gossip and to steal Heeseung's snacks."

"You don't even like protein bars," Heeseung muttered.

"I like the power."

Solar laughed. And for a second—just a second—everything felt a little less messy.




Minji wasn't supposed to be checking her phone.

The practice room was dead silent except for the metronome ticking in the background. Hanni had gone to grab tea. The lights were dim. Her laptop sat open with choreo notes on the screen—but her eyes hadn't moved past her screen's reflection in ten minutes.

Because she'd seen it.

The clip.

Again.

Solar, in her Shock jersey, laughing. Ducking under Super's arm. Shouting something toward the camera. A second later, Rascal in the background—looking at her like she hung the damn stars.

Minji muted the video before it could play again.

It wasn't a kiss. There wasn't proof.

But the energy was there. And it made her stomach twist.

She tried to pretend it was just fan over-analysis. The internet read too much into everything. A look wasn't always loaded. A laugh didn't mean something.

Except Minji knew Solar's laugh.

Knew her enough to recognize the one she gave when she was trying too hard to act normal.

She picked up her phone. Scrolled to Solar's contact. Her fingers hovered for a full minute.

Then she typed:

you looked really happy yesterday.
congrats, btw.
i didn't say it before.

She stared at it. Backspaced half of it. Started again.

you were amazing. and you deserved it.
also... i hope you're okay.
not in a weird way. just... yeah.

She sighed. Deleted all of it. In the end, she sent nothing. Just locked her phone and leaned back against the mirror, the ache in her chest deep and quiet.

Because it wasn't just about jealousy. It was the silence. The space where they used to talk.

The soft tension between them that had once felt like gravity, and now felt like a hallway she'd been locked out of.

And the worst part? She still wanted in.




Solar didn't notice anything was wrong at first.

The hallway was quiet—too quiet for this part of the building. Late evening had turned the floor pale and bluish, the overhead lights humming gently, their buzz softened by the thick acoustic panels lining the walls. Somewhere in the distance, a piano played a lazy, off-tempo scale. Probably a trainee killing time.

She exhaled.

The encounter with Chaerin and Heeseung still clung to her like glitter after a show. Funny. Unexpected. Slightly horrifying. And yet... comforting, in that weird, sibling-chaotic way only Chaerin could deliver.

Solar turned the corner and approached the locker alcove near the third practice room—the one she and the girls used most often. She didn't need much. Just the hoodie she'd left earlier and the phone charger still coiled somewhere near the bottom of her bag.

She typed in the code. 2–5–2–0. Click. The locker swung open with a faint creak.

And something fluttered. Solar froze.

There, sitting dead center on top of her neatly folded hoodie, was a single piece of paper. Folded in half. Perfectly aligned. No logo. No label. No smudge of color. Just white, crisp, and deliberate.

Her breath caught. She hadn't left that there.

And no one else had access to her locker. Not even the other girls—not without the code. She reached out slowly, heart beginning to thrum in her throat.

The paper felt cold between her fingertips, like it had been sitting there longer than it should have. Her hands were steady, but her body wasn't. Something in her gut twisted—the same instinctive recoil she'd felt the night she found that note in her bag. The box on her desk. The silence that always followed.

Danielle.

She unfolded the paper.

And stared.

A photo.

It wasn't printed on glossy paper—just a plain, grainy black-and-white printout. The edges were jagged like it had been torn from a larger sheet. The quality was terrible, but the contents were clear.

It was her.

Walking beside Jungwon.

From behind.

The image had been taken from a distance, probably from a higher angle—maybe a stairwell or upper floor. The shot was slightly tilted, as if the person hadn't wanted to be seen. Solar and Jungwon weren't holding hands. They weren't smiling. Just... walking. Heads close together. Their posture relaxed. Familiar.

She remembered that day.

They'd talked for nearly an hour. Not about anything world-shattering—just schedules, siblings, game footage. He'd asked her if she'd ever considered switching to Echo, and she'd laughed and said, only if you main Lifeweaver.

It had been... normal.

Good.

Solar's throat went tight.

She flipped the photo over.

On the back, in delicate, looping handwriting:

Is this the version of you he likes best?

The words were small. Written with a steady hand. And somehow, they hit harder than anything Danielle had ever done.

Solar swallowed hard.

She looked at the image again.

Her back turned. Jungwon's profile just barely visible. A private moment—not intimate, but theirs.

Someone had seen them.

Someone had taken this.

And worse—

Someone had been close enough to hide it for weeks.

This wasn't just creepy anymore. It wasn't about spooking her or making her question herself.

This was a threat disguised as a whisper.

A pressure point. Pressed carefully.

Solar slowly lowered the paper.

Her reflection in the locker mirror looked off-color. Paler than usual. She blinked and tried to make her face neutral, but the weight in her chest wouldn't lift.

She'd been watched.

By someone who knew how to stay invisible.

And now, they were reaching into the spaces that didn't belong to them.

Is this the version of you he likes best?

Like she was something to edit. To change. To manipulate.

Not a person.

A projection.

Her hand curled into a fist.

She carefully refolded the paper and slid it into her hoodie pocket. Not because she wanted to keep it, but because she couldn't leave it behind.

The moment passed like a ripple.

But it left a chill under her skin.

She closed her locker—slowly, deliberately.

The metal clanged louder than expected.

And this time, when she walked down the hall toward the exit, she didn't put her earbuds in.

Didn't hum.

Didn't check her phone.

She just walked.

And when she passed the glass door leading out to the back lot, she caught her reflection.

She looked composed.

But her eyes flicked behind her.

Just once.

And the feeling was still there.

That flicker.

That heat on the back of her neck.

Like someone had been standing just out of frame the whole time.

Watching.

Measuring.

Waiting.




She didn't expect to see them.

She hadn't planned anything except going home. Still shaken from the photo. Still pretending her legs weren't made of smoke. The air outside HYBE was crisp with evening chill, and the streetlamps had just blinked on, casting everything in that yellow-orange hush.

Solar pulled her hoodie tight and took the back exit—the one near the staff lot. The one she always used when she wanted to avoid running into fans or lingering trainees. It was quieter here. Safer.

Usually.

She stepped onto the sidewalk and froze.

There, not twenty feet away, leaning against a black car like it was his, stood Jihoon.

And next to him—hair down, sleeves rolled, sipping a canned iced coffee with surgical nonchalance—was Danielle.

Solar's breath caught.

They were talking.

Not arguing. Not tense. Just... talking.

Like old friends.

Like something worse.

Jihoon laughed at something Danielle said, brushing hair out of his face the same way he used to when he was bored at parties. She smiled—not the bright idol smile. The other one. The one Solar had only ever seen once, in the reflection of a tinted window.

It looked like a weapon.

Jihoon noticed her first.

His eyes flicked up, and when he spotted her, his smirk bloomed instantly.

"Well," he drawled, "speak of the devil."

Danielle didn't even turn around.

Solar couldn't move.

Jihoon pushed off the car slowly, hands in his coat pockets, taking two steps toward her like this was a normal reunion.

"You always show up when we're talking about you," he said casually. "It's uncanny. You got that sixth sense or something?"

Solar didn't answer.

Her mouth was dry.

Her fingers curled around the inside seam of her hoodie pocket, still gripping the photo.

Danielle took one final sip from her coffee can.

Then said, without turning her head:

"It's rude to stare, unnie."

The silence after that was razor-sharp. Jihoon looked amused. Danielle looked... satisfied. Like this had been inevitable. Like they'd wanted her to see them.

Solar's feet finally obeyed her brain. She turned. Walked away. Not fast. Not slow. Just... gone. She didn't hear footsteps behind her. Didn't hear them follow.

But she felt it in her spine—the way their presence lingered like smoke on her clothes. And when she reached the end of the block, she finally let herself exhale. Not in relief.

But in recognition. Whatever she was caught in... It went deeper than she thought.

















48: Where It Hurts


Solar's body was already in motion before the beat dropped.

The mirrored walls flickered with light as her reflection snapped into formation—shoulders squared, gaze locked, the sweat-slick strands of her hair catching on the curve of her cheekbone as she pivoted. The dance practice room pulsed with the low bass of Unforgiven, the track playing on loop so many times it no longer felt like music but instinct.

Again. Again. Again.

Her movements were sharp now. Stronger than they'd ever been. But it had taken days of red-eyed rehearsals and sore muscles to get here. She wasn't flailing anymore. She wasn't collapsing into doubt. She was fighting—clean lines, precise power, unrelenting breath control—and she was winning.

Until—

Pain bloomed in her arm, sudden and hot, like something snapping just below the skin.

She stumbled, just slightly. Just enough that she caught herself with one hand on her knee, the music continuing without her.

"Shit," she whispered, winded.

The room didn't stop. No one clapped. No one scolded her. It was just her—alone, surrounded by reflections of a girl who refused to slow down.

She rolled her shoulder, testing it. Probably just a pulled muscle. Maybe a bruise. Nothing serious.

She stood back up. Hit play. And started again.

Twenty minutes later, her arm still ached, but the rhythm had returned to her body like muscle memory. She didn't even notice the door open.

Not until Yunjin's voice cracked across the room like a firework.

"Are you crazy?"

Solar flinched, mid-spin. Yunjin stood in the doorway holding two iced drinks, her brow furrowed in disbelief.

"You've been in here since eight this morning," she said, stepping inside. "You were supposed to be done three hours ago."

Solar pressed pause, chest rising and falling. "I was just cleaning a few things up."

Yunjin narrowed her eyes. "You're limping."

"I'm not limping."

"You are limping, and your arm's doing that thing where you keep rolling it like it'll magically pop back into place."

Solar stayed quiet. Not because Yunjin was wrong—but because she wasn't. And because it didn't matter.

"I'm getting stronger," Solar said simply. "You said I was falling behind. You were right. So I fixed it."

Yunjin softened, just a little. "I didn't mean kill yourself to get there."

"I'm not," Solar said, wiping her forehead with the sleeve of her hoodie. "I just—I want to be proud of how I perform this time. Not scared. Not surviving. Just... proud."

For a second, Yunjin didn't say anything. She just handed Solar the drink in her hand, letting the silence settle like warmth instead of judgment. "Okay," she said finally. "But take the damn ice pack when we get back to the dorm. Deal?"

Solar grinned. "Deal."




The van hummed quietly beneath them, the night outside flecked with city lights, flickering past the windows in streaks of orange and blue. Solar leaned her head back against the cool glass, eyes half-closed, her limbs still tingling from the final run-through of Unforgiven.

Yunjin sat beside her, scrolling absentmindedly through her phone, one leg curled beneath her, the other bouncing slightly in rhythm with the road. They were the only two in the van tonight—Kazuha had stayed behind to shower, Eunchae had been picked up early, and Chaewon was likely already passed out at the dorm. It was rare, this silence. This stillness.

Yunjin glanced sideways at her. "You didn't eat again."

Solar didn't open her eyes. "Not true. I had a protein bar."

"A protein bar is not food. That's like eating anxiety in bar form."

Solar cracked a soft laugh, but even that felt thin. "I wasn't hungry."

"You've been up since eight."

"Seven, actually. I wanted to warm up early."

Yunjin clicked her tongue. "Of course you did. Because you're not human, you're just some magical sleep-deprived K-pop-bot that runs on guilt and raw determination."

This time, Solar smiled, but didn't deny it. Her fingers toyed with the hem of her sleeve. "I just want to get it right. For the comeback. For... everything."

Yunjin's eyes lingered on her a second longer before she turned her phone screen off and tucked it away. The van turned a corner, city lights flaring across their faces, then dissolving into shadows.

"I know," she said finally. "But there's getting it right, and there's breaking yourself trying."

Silence pulsed between them.

"I'm not breaking," Solar said, quieter this time. "I'm pushing."

"Maybe," Yunjin said. "But even pushing needs breath."

They didn't speak for a while after that. The driver muttered something into his headset, the radio static hissed. Solar closed her eyes again, not to sleep, just to sink.

And then Yunjin's voice broke through—softer, threaded with something careful.

"Do you remember what you said after the finals?"

Solar didn't respond. But her shoulders shifted.

"You told me about what happened. About Rascal. And... Jungwon."

Her name caught there, in the space between those two.

"I remember," Solar said.

"You said it felt like a question. The kiss. Not an answer."

"I did say that."

"Do you still feel that way?"

A long pause. Then Solar let out a breath, soft and uneven. "Yeah. Maybe even more now. It wasn't a bad moment—it was warm. Safe. But..."

"But it didn't move you," Yunjin filled in gently.

Solar hesitated. "No. It did. Just not in the direction I thought it would. I thought it would anchor me. Instead, it kind of... woke something up. Something restless."

Yunjin leaned her head back too, now staring up at the roof of the van. "You've always been like this," she said. "You make it through the worst things and you never let them turn you mean. But you still punish yourself for not being stronger."

Solar turned to look at her. "Is that what I'm doing?"

"I don't know," Yunjin admitted. "But I see the way you talk about Jihoon now. It's different. You're not hiding anymore. You're not scared. And I don't think it's because he stopped being scary—I think it's because you changed."

Something in Solar's chest pulled tight.

"I'm not scared," she said. "I just don't want to give him anything else."

"That's strength too," Yunjin said, eyes kind. "But you don't owe him your silence either."

Those were the words Kazuha had said earlier in the week. They echoed now, but softer here, more rooted. Solar looked down at her hands. They didn't shake.

"He doesn't own anything in me anymore," she said slowly. "But sometimes... I still feel like I'm trying to prove it. Like if I work hard enough, win enough, smile bright enough, no one will ever guess what he did to me. Or what I let him."

Yunjin didn't interrupt. She let her talk.

"I want to be someone who's already healed," Solar whispered. "Not someone still trying to."

"Too bad," Yunjin said. "You're stuck being someone who's healing. One day at a time."

Solar laughed—unexpected and honest.

"I just don't want the girls to worry," she admitted.

"They will anyway," Yunjin said. "Because we do worry. But we also trust you. And if you ever do break again—guess what? We'll still be here. Picking up the pieces. Slapping them back into shape with glitter glue and hot sauce and chaos."

Solar snorted. "Hot sauce?"

"Metaphorical. Unless Chaerin's helping."

They both dissolved into quiet laughter, the kind that soothed and settled. Outside the van, the city blurred by. Inside, for a moment, everything felt still.

When they finally pulled up to the dorm, Solar didn't move right away. Yunjin was already halfway out of the seat when she paused.

"Oh," she added, as if remembering something offhand. "You got a ping earlier. From Rascal."

Solar blinked. "What?"

"On Discord. Crusty too. Said something about off-season scrims being chill again. Told you to pop in."

Solar stared at her. "You saw that?"

"I was next to you. Your phone buzzed. You looked like you were gonna throw it into the Han River."

She blushed faintly. "It wasn't like that."

"No?"

"It just... caught me off guard."

"Well," Yunjin said, nudging her with her knee. "Sounds like they still want you around. Esports girl."

Solar smiled, small and soft. "Yeah. I guess they do."

And this time, it didn't sound like a question.

The dorm door clicked shut behind them with a familiar thud. It smelled like lavender detergent and someone's leftover ramen. The hallway light flickered once, casting a gold sheen over the worn slippers by the door. Solar didn't even get a step in before Eunchae's voice rang out from the living room.

"Finally! We thought you died."

Chaewon popped her head out of the kitchen, wooden spoon in hand. "You missed dinner."

"I wasn't hungry," Solar murmured, pulling off her shoes.

Yunjin didn't miss a beat. "She's been at the studio since seven in the morning."

There was a beat of silence, then five voices overlapping.

"What?"

"Seven?!"

"Girl, what—"

"You're gonna die at this rate."

"Where's your self-preservation?!"

Solar winced as Kazuha came padding in from the hallway, arms crossed, already wearing her night moisturizer. "Unforgiven choreo is not going to run away," she said calmly. "You didn't even eat."

"I had a protein bar," Solar said feebly.

Chaewon blinked. "Okay, you're grounded."

Eunchae grabbed her hand dramatically. "You need an intervention."

But before they could swarm her further, Solar sighed and raised both hands like she was surrendering to a firing squad. "I know. I know, okay? But it's not about being perfect."

That quieted them.

"I'm just..." She took a breath. "I'm trying to be ready. Not just for the comeback. For—everything. I want to be at my best. I want people to stop seeing me as the girl who's always one misstep away from a scandal. Or a breakdown. Or... something."

Her voice softened on that last word. Something unspoken hanging in the air.

Yunjin stepped up beside her, nodding. "She's not trying to be perfect. She's trying to be whole. There's a difference."

Chaewon's expression shifted, the tension easing. Kazuha gave a tiny nod. Even Eunchae, who was half-joking just seconds ago, went quiet.

They moved into the living room together, settling down in the soft hum of the overhead light. Solar curled into the farthest end of the couch, legs tucked up, arms around her knees. Yunjin sat beside her. The others gathered around in a messy semi-circle, limbs folded, hair wet from showers, eyes clear but tired.

After a beat, Solar spoke again. Her voice was low, cautious. "There's more. Something I didn't say."

That pulled all eyes toward her.

"I've been thinking about something that happened after the Grand Finals," she said. "Or maybe... it's two things. Back to back."

She swallowed. "I kissed Rascal. After the tournament."

Chaewon blinked. Kazuha raised an eyebrow. Eunchae's jaw dropped. Yunjin, of course, looked utterly unbothered.

"You what?" Eunchae whispered.

"It wasn't—it wasn't anything crazy," Solar rushed. "It was soft. Sweet. But it felt like a question, not an answer."

"You said that already," Yunjin said, voice steady.

Solar nodded. "But I never said what happened after. I ran into Jungwon. In the hallway. Right after."

The air thinned. Not tense. Just tight.

"I didn't expect to see him," she said. "But he was just... there. And I don't even know what happened, but it felt like my entire brain went silent. Like I'd been trying to keep everything under control for hours, and then he looked at me, and it all slipped."

Kazuha's eyes softened. "What did he say?"

"Not much," Solar whispered. "But he didn't need to. And when he looked at me—just looked—I felt like I couldn't breathe."

She looked up slowly, cheeks flushed. "It's so weird. I've known him forever, but not like this. It's like... like something's pulling me toward him, and I don't know if it's real, or just trauma or fate or timing or—"

"Or maybe," Chaewon interrupted gently, "it is real. Just scary."

Solar pressed her fingers to her temple. "I don't want to fall into something that explodes my whole life."

"It already exploded," Yunjin said. "The moment you started choosing yourself."

The room fell silent again.

"But," Kazuha added, "you do know that having a thing with a member of Enhypen... it's going to make things complicated. More complicated than they already are."

"I know."

"We're not judging," Chaewon added. "We just want you to go in with your eyes open."

"I don't even know if he likes me that way," Solar said, almost laughing. "He's Jungwon. He's unreadable."

Eunchae snorted. "Girl, please. He looks at you like you're the sun that makes him blind on purpose."

That cracked the tension. Everyone laughed—even Solar, cheeks red and eyes shining.

"I'm proud of you," Chaewon said, reaching for her hand. "Not for kissing anyone. Just... for talking. For saying something out loud that's hard."

"You're allowed to want things," Kazuha added.

"And you're allowed to be confused," Yunjin said. "But you're not allowed to forget that we're on your side. No matter what."

Solar swallowed the lump rising in her throat. "Even if I ruin everything?"

"Especially then," Yunjin said. "That's what sisters are for."

Solar didn't reply, just leaned into her, head on her shoulder, letting her eyes fall closed for just a second. Maybe the world was still spinning out there—headlines, whispers, contracts and consequences—but here in this moment, everything felt still.




It's late by the time Solar slips away to her room. The dorm is dim and quiet, the kind of quiet that usually drives her thoughts into chaos—but tonight, she sits at her desk, fingers hesitating over her mouse, before finally clicking into Discord.

The ping from earlier is still there.
Crusty's message. Rascal's follow-up.
That whole thread of goofiness from the boys she hasn't seen in weeks.

She could ignore it.
No one would blame her.

But tonight... she kind of wants to feel like herself again.
The self they remind her she can be.

She double-clicks the voice channel.

Connected. "scrim chaos zone ☠️"

"—Yo!"
Super's voice hits her ears immediately. "Is that Solar? Am I hallucinating?"

"She lives!" Glister adds dramatically.

"Wait wait wait," Rascal says, mock-serious. "Is this a deepfake?"

Smurf lets out a soft laugh. "Nah. That's her. I can hear the existential dread."

Solar's lips curve, warmth blooming in her chest before she can help it. "You guys are so dramatic."

"Bro we've been abandoned," Glister insists. "Like orphans."

"Speak for yourself," Rascal replies. "I've been carrying scrims like a god. I didn't even miss her."

"That's a lie," Super deadpans.

"It is," Rascal admits. "Hi."

Solar laughs. Just a little. Just enough. "I can't stay long," she says. "We've got intense rehearsals for the new comeback. They're trying to kill us."

"Understood," Crusty says dryly, as if he's been summoned by her voice. "Pop in when you can. Off-season now. No pressure."

"Yeah, we're just running quick comps and Glister's feeding," Rascal mutters.

"I AM NOT—"

"Oh my god," Solar says, shaking her head, fingers already moving to queue with them. "You guys never change."

And for the next half hour, everything else falls away. There's no comeback stage. No HYBE meetings. No bruised feet. Just stupid callouts, inside jokes, her own voice echoing in laughter she hadn't realized she missed this much.

They don't mention the media. They don't ask how she's really doing. They just play. Like they always have. Like they always will.

Eventually, she mutes with a soft, "Love you guys, I'll be back soon."

And someone — probably Rascal, though he says it under his breath — replies:

"Told you she still belonged here."




The hall is narrow, humming faintly with recycled air and fluorescent lighting. A familiar ache settles into Solar's limbs as she rounds the corner near HYBE's third-floor practice studios, a water bottle clutched loosely in one hand, hoodie sleeve pushed up just enough to let her skin breathe.

Her earbuds are in, but not playing anything. It's just easier to keep them there — an unspoken do-not-disturb sign. She's been running choreo since 7 a.m. Her knees ache. Her elbow caught a dancer's sharp turn earlier, left a faint, purpling bloom just above the crook of her arm. Nothing major. Just one more quiet bruise she won't talk about.

She's halfway down the hallway when someone rounds the other end — walking quickly, head low.

They nearly collide. Solar startles. The cap of her water bottle flies off with a small clack, rolling toward the wall. A hand catches her elbow.

Firm. Steady.

"Watch it," a voice mutters — sharp, but controlled. Then, quieter, as recognition dawns, "...you."

Her breath catches. She blinks up. Jungwon.

Of course.

They stare at each other — too close, too suddenly still. There's a flicker in his expression, some complex cocktail of tension, instinct, and something rawer that flickers and dies before it can settle.

She moves back instinctively, brushing her sleeve down — too late. He saw it. His eyes narrow. His hand hovers for a second too long before dropping away.

"What's that?" he asks.

She follows his gaze to her arm. "Oh." She shrugs, tugging her hoodie fully down. "Practice."

His jaw ticks. "That happen today?"

She doesn't answer. She doesn't have to.

He exhales once through his nose, not quite scoffing, but close. "Maybe they should run their choreo better if it's leaving marks."

Solar blinks. It's not just irritation in his voice. It's heat. Possessiveness. She almost says, It's nothing. But it already was nothing. Until he made it feel like something.

"It's fine," she says instead. "Really."

He shakes his head, once. His lips are pressed thin. Then, quieter — like he's trying not to say it but can't help himself, "just... don't ignore it when it's worse."

Her throat tightens. He's already turning to leave when she speaks again.

"I wasn't ignoring it."

He stops.

She doesn't know why she says it. Maybe because he looks like he thinks she doesn't care about herself. Maybe because she wants to prove she does — even if it's messy, inconsistent, not always loud.

Jungwon turns back to look at her. There's a long beat of silence. His gaze lowers, then returns — searching, unreadable.

The hallway suddenly feels too quiet. And then, his voice — even softer:

"...I know."

That should be it. But it isn't.

Not when she's standing here remembering the last time she saw him — the echo of stage lights, the post-finals haze. The way they stood too close in the shadows. The way it almost felt like something inevitable.

His eyes flick down again, like he's trying to not remember the same thing. And then, before she can say anything else — he nods once and walks past her.

He doesn't brush her shoulder. Doesn't reach again. Doesn't look back. But the heat lingers in the hallway.

And Solar stands there for a long time, heart thudding dully in her chest, wondering how someone can say so little and still leave her feeling cracked open.

She glances down at her arm. The bruise is already fading.

But the tension he left behind?
Still fresh.




The glow of Solar's phone painted her ceiling in faint, bluish light.

She lay flat on her back, the bedsheets tangled loosely around her knees, chest rising and falling in slow, fatigued rhythm. Practice had left her sore—worn in the way that seeps into the bones, not just the muscles. Her arm ached from where she'd collided with Eunchae during formations, but it wasn't pain that kept her awake. Not really.

It was the silence.

The dorm had finally gone quiet. Yunjin was probably still awake in the next room, half-watching a drama while pretending not to worry. Sakura had left a sleepy message in the group chat before going to bed, reminding them to ice their legs and drink water. Solar had promised she would.

She hadn't.

Instead, she found herself here, thumb scrolling up, up, up through the archive of messages she hadn't opened in weeks.

Minji.

Some were funny. Stupid, even. A photo of a dumpling shaped like a turtle. A weird filter selfie Minji had sent with the caption: You'd still fall in love with me like this, admit it.

Solar exhaled sharply through her nose. Not quite a laugh.

Other messages were heavier. Ones that tugged at her like the tide.

Sometimes I feel like I'm living a life someone else picked for me.

Do you ever wish we could just disappear?

Solar's reply had been cautious. Always cautious.

I think I'd disappear with you.

She blinked back a sudden sting behind her eyes.

Her phone buzzed gently in her hand.

Minji 🐱 [11:42PM]
I wish I had been there. Are you okay?

Solar stared at the message for a long moment, chest tightening as the words settled. The softness in them made her heart ache. The timing made her wonder if Minji somehow knew. Had someone said something? Had she seen a photo—read between the lines of a schedule?

She didn't answer right away. Her fingers hovered, then fell away.

Another buzz.

But this one wasn't from Minji.

No contact name. Just a number. One she didn't recognize.

Unknown [11:43PM]
We're not finished yet.

The screen's light flickered as if it had dimmed on instinct. Her pulse surged.

The message had no punctuation. No context. But Solar's body responded like it understood something her mind hadn't yet caught up to. Her stomach turned cold. Her ears rang.

Her finger tapped the screen again. She copied the number. Opened it in her contacts list.

Nothing.

No prior messages. No linked name. No call history.

She sat up slowly, bedsheets sliding off her legs like water.

Was it Danielle?

The idea struck her not as a question, but a realization.

"We're not finished yet."

Not I'm not finished. We.

Like a group. A pact. Or a fractured piece of someone playing pretend. Like the girl who smiled in mirrors but looked dead behind the eyes when no one was watching.

Solar set her phone down, facedown.

Her reflection in the dark window stared back at her. Pale. Distant. Small.

She wrapped her arms around her knees and rested her forehead there, breathing slowly, forcing herself to stay grounded.

It was fine. Just a message. Words couldn't hurt her.

But still, she stayed like that for a long time, the quiet of the dorm thick with something that didn't feel like sleep.
























49: What You Don't Say

The dorm was still asleep.

No soft shuffle of Eunchae's socks on the tile. No hum of Sakura's electric kettle. No flicker of Chaewon's bedroom lamp through the crack in the door. Just the stillness of pre-dawn—pale light creeping in through the blinds and catching on the dust motes above Solar's bed.

She hadn't really slept.

Her legs ached with the kind of soreness that settled in deep, clinging to muscle like a bruise under the skin. One already bloomed faint purple on her thigh. Another, a thin line across her wrist where someone's sleeve had snagged her during the formation switch in the second chorus. Nothing serious. But they added up.

She pushed the blanket back and sat up, slowly. The air was cool against her back, her oversized t-shirt clinging damply to her skin.

She moved on autopilot—ice pack from the mini freezer, towel folded neatly across her lap. When she pressed the cold to her wrist, she didn't wince. Just inhaled, counted to five, and exhaled through her nose.

In the silence, her mind wandered where she didn't want it to go. That moment in the hallway. Danielle and Jihoon.

She hadn't seen them since. Hadn't mentioned it to Yunjin or Sakura or even Rascal—especially not Rascal. And definitely not Jungwon.

Solar leaned her head back against the wall and closed her eyes, ice pressed to her skin, breath steady.

She could still picture Danielle's expression.
Not flirtatious. Not surprised.
Just... neutral. As if there was nothing strange about her being alone with Solar's ex, walking away down that corridor like shadows bleeding into one.

It should've lit something on fire inside her. Anger, betrayal, fear.

Instead, she'd filed it somewhere deep. Behind the growing list of comeback goals, the ever-mounting scrim messages from Crusty, the ache in her body, the voices telling her she was too much and not enough in the same breath.

She couldn't let herself spiral now. She was stronger than that.

The ice pack slipped slightly. She adjusted it with her free hand, blinking hard. The clock read 6:08 AM. Their call time was 9. She could rest another hour. Try, at least.

Her gaze dropped to her phone on the nightstand.
Still on Do Not Disturb.
A missed Discord ping from the night before. Rascal. Crusty.

She hadn't opened it. Couldn't. The worlds were bleeding together again. And she was the thin, shaking line between them.

Solar pulled the blanket back up and laid down carefully, arm still cradling the ice. Her eyes didn't close right away. The ache in her body was nothing compared to the one gnawing inside her chest.

But she was still here.
Still showing up.
Still burning.

And she would keep burning. Until someone finally noticed the smoke.




The beat snapped like a whip. Bass-heavy and merciless.

Solar didn't blink. Didn't pause. Didn't breathe.

Her body launched forward into the next sequence, her sneakers skidding against the polished studio floor, arms cutting through the air like blades. Her chest burned, ribs tight and trembling, but she pushed through it—harder, sharper, faster.

She was always fast. That was her thing.

But now she was fast like something being chased.

Chaewon's voice called from behind, low but present, "Wait—Solar—"

Too late. She was already two counts ahead.

Eunchae stumbled to catch up.

The music blared on, pounding into the walls like a second heartbeat, and Solar's muscles screamed with it. Her shirt clung to her skin. She barely registered the ache until her right wrist gave a sharp warning flare. She faltered for a split second in the wave-arm motion, then masked it—twisting the pain into a flourish. A graceful, practiced deflection.

If you blinked, you'd miss it.

But Kazuha didn't blink.

She watched, a slight furrow forming in her brow as Solar dipped back, using the momentum of a spin to slide her wrist behind her lower back, pressing it there discreetly with her left hand. Not stretching. Not part of the choreo. Just hiding. Protecting.

Again.

"Pop—pop—pivot—good—again from the top!" the choreographer called from the front of the room, clapping to the rhythm.

No one moved.

Eunchae was still catching her breath, bent slightly forward, cheeks flushed.

Chaewon stepped out from formation, holding up a hand. "Let's pause for a second."

Solar didn't stop. She executed the spin again, jaw clenched.

"Unnie," Chaewon said more firmly, "Break."

The word echoed too loud in the room.

Solar froze mid-turn, shoulders tensed, mouth parted. The sweat on her temple glistened, eyes glassy with effort—but there was no trace of apology. Just determination. Just refusal.

Kazuha moved closer, brushing her fingers lightly across Solar's elbow. "Your wrist again?"

"I'm fine."

"You keep saying that."

Solar bent quickly to grab her towel from the edge of the mirror wall. Her hand trembled—not a lot, but enough. She twisted the towel tight, binding it around her wrist in a fluid motion. A move she'd done enough times to make it look routine. Casual.

But Kazuha's silence spoke volumes.

Chaewon's too.

From the corner, Eunchae plopped down dramatically, flopping over her knees like a noodle. "I think I forgot what my legs are for."

The others chuckled lightly, the tension briefly cut, but it lingered in the air like static. They could laugh. But Solar didn't.

She eased down to the floor more slowly than usual, back pressed to the mirror. Her knees pulled up, arms looped over them, wrist hidden behind her thigh now. Her phone buzzed in the pile by the stereo, but she didn't move to check it.

Kazuha sat beside her.

The others took water sips, adjusted ponytails, rolled out ankles.

Chaewon crouched next to Solar and lowered her voice. "You didn't take any of the other breaks either."

"I didn't need them."

"Maybe not then. But now you do."

There was no accusation. No challenge. Just... concern.

But Solar's eyes stayed low. Her lashes were damp. "I can still go."

Chaewon shook her head slightly. "This isn't about whether you can. You always can. It's about whether you should."

Silence.

Solar stared at her own reflection across the mirrored wall. She didn't recognize the version of herself slumped there, shoulders curved inward, towel-wrapped hand hidden like a secret. Her hair had half fallen from the elastic. Her lips were cracked.

She didn't feel powerful. She felt like glass with legs.

But quitting... even resting... would feel like defeat.

Kazuha finally broke the silence. "Do you ever wonder if you're fighting the wrong things?"

Solar turned to her, brows tightening slightly.

Kazuha didn't elaborate. She just sipped her water and offered a half-smile. "You looked like you were going to collapse, you know."

"I wasn't."

"No," she agreed, "but you looked like it."

Solar let out a breath she didn't realize she was holding. Something between a scoff and a laugh. "Nice."

"You're always beautiful, but scary sometimes."

Solar snorted. "Thanks."

Chaewon gave them both a look. "Alright. Five minutes."

The girls dispersed again, the atmosphere gently softening.

But Solar stayed rooted to the mirror, her injured wrist still cradled out of view. She didn't unwrap it. Didn't ask for ice. She just watched her reflection, watched the shape of her exhaustion, and refused to speak it aloud.

Because the truth was, she could keep going.
But she was starting to wonder if she should.

And that doubt—it scared her more than the pain ever had.



The mirrors are fogged at the edges, the floor streaked with sweat. The room hums with that post-practice quiet—tired feet shuffling, bottles uncapping, breath returning to normal. Solar stays sitting, towel draped over her neck, pretending to scroll through her phone.

Yunjin settles beside her, silent for a beat.

"I know that face," Yunjin finally says, voice low. "You saw something. Didn't you?"

Solar's fingers still on the screen. She doesn't look up.

"What makes you think that?"

"You only get this intense when you're trying not to fall apart." Yunjin leans back, sighs. "And you've been weird ever since the last recording day. Not just tired. Shaky. Defensive. You're hiding something."

Solar exhales slowly, the fight draining out of her.

"I think..." Her voice catches, then she steadies it. "I think Jihoon and Danielle are working together."

Yunjin's head jerks around so fast it nearly throws her off balance. "I'm sorry—what?"

"I saw them," Solar mutters. "Together. Talking. In that weird way he does when he's planning something. And her—she wasn't acting. Not with him."

For a long time, Yunjin says nothing. Her breath comes in slow, disbelieving waves.

"You saw Danielle—our Danielle—talking to your ex-boyfriend, who we already think is a manipulative psycho—and you didn't tell anyone?"

Solar looks away. Her jaw tightens, hands curling around her towel. "I didn't have proof. And the comeback is more important right now."

Yunjin stands abruptly, pacing. "That's not a small thing, Solar. That's not some little drama. That's... that's dangerous."

"I know." Her voice is quiet. "I know."

Yunjin stops in front of her. "Then why are you acting like it's not?"

Solar doesn't answer right away. She's thinking about the gifts now—the white box, the glittery ribbon, the scentless lotion she threw out, the ripped corner of an envelope she never opened. She thinks about the way her room feels sometimes when she's alone. Like someone's been there. Watching. Touching things they shouldn't.

She thinks about telling Yunjin now.

But she doesn't.

There's no evidence. No clear trail. And none of it matters compared to how she could barely keep her balance in that final dance run-through.

One war at a time, she tells herself again.

"I'm handling it," Solar says instead. "It's not a priority right now."

Yunjin studies her, eyes narrowing. "You always do that," she says. "Carry everything until you break."

Solar lifts a shoulder. "Better than dragging everyone down with me."

Yunjin kneels again, gaze gentler now. "You don't have to carry it alone."

"I'm used to it."

Yunjin flinches a little at that. Her hand hovers like she wants to reach out but doesn't. She doesn't push, doesn't prod further. Just says softly, "I'm here. Whenever you stop pretending it doesn't hurt."

Solar nods, barely. It's all she can offer.

And the room slips into silence once more.




The studio air was thick with humidity and effort—sweat clinging to skin, limbs aching from repetition. Music still pulsed faintly from the corner speaker, a low heartbeat that never really stopped, not even during water breaks.

Solar sat cross-legged by the wall, wiping her face with a towel. Her hoodie clung to her damp collarbone, her braid limp with sweat. Across the room, Eunchae chattered to Kazuha between deep stretches. Chaewon paced slowly with her water bottle, already checking notes for their next schedule.

Sakura dropped down beside Solar with a soft grunt, then turned her head slightly and said, "Hey, weird thing. Danielle stopped by earlier."

Solar glanced up. "What?"

"She came by the studio," Sakura repeated. "Didn't say anything. Just smiled really weird at me. Then left."

Solar's stomach knotted on instinct. Weird how? But she already knew.

Yunjin looked up from where she was tying her shoe. "Wait... What kind of weird?"

"Just..." Sakura made a face, then mimicked the slow, saccharine smile Danielle had given her. "Like she knew something... or was about to eat me."

Yunjin froze. Her eyes sharpened, lips twitching. "Oh, that witch is absolutely behind the gifts."

"What gifts?" Chaewon said quickly, turning toward them.

Yunjin didn't even pause. "She's plotting... with Jihoon."

"Wait—what?" Kazuha said, nearly dropping her bottle.

Sakura blinked. "Danielle and Jihoon?"

"Oh my god," Solar groaned, tipping her head back against the mirror. "I shouldn't have said anything."

"Too late," Yunjin snapped, standing now, pacing like a detective with a red string board. "It all lines up. The perfume. The ribbon. That empty Polaroid frame. Creepy as hell. No note. No name. Just this obsessive little breadcrumb trail of stalker energy."

Chaewon narrowed her eyes. "Wait, Solar—you got those gifts?"

Solar pressed the towel to her face. "Yes. A while ago. I told Minji about them once, but I didn't want to worry anyone."

Kazuha gawked. "You didn't even tell us?"

"I'm telling you now," Solar muttered. "And they weren't all at once. Just... spaced out. Weeks apart."

Eunchae sat up now, concerned. "Like what kind of stuff?"

"The perfume bottle was first," Solar said reluctantly. "It wasn't even opened. Expensive-looking, but the scent was too strong. Too girly. I never wear that kind of thing. Then a blue ribbon. No packaging. Just... lying on my desk. Like a reminder."

"The creepiest was the Polaroid frame," she added after a pause, her voice lower now. "It was empty. But there were finger smudges on the glass. Like someone had been holding a photo, then slid it out."

A beat of stunned silence.

Yunjin was seething now. "And Danielle just happens to be at the studio today, acting like a cartoon villain?"

"You think she's working with Jihoon?" Chaewon asked carefully.

"Either that or she's just as deranged on her own," Yunjin muttered, then looked at the others. "She's obsessed with Jungwon. But she sees Solar as the threat. Combine that with Jihoon slithering back into the picture, and boom. Unholy alliance."

"Jihoon's back?" Kazuha said, eyes wide. "Since when?"

Solar looked away, jaw tight.

Yunjin sighed. "He cornered her at HYBE. Twice. First in the lobby, then in the hallway after a practice. Said some shady shit. Made her cry. That's why she showed up at Enhypen's dorm that night."

"You went to their dorm?" Sakura blinked.

"After hours," Yunjin added. "Alone."

"Guys—" Solar tried to interrupt.

"You don't have to explain yourself," Chaewon said gently. "But that's serious."

"We need to keep an eye out," Yunjin said. "This isn't just some sasaeng thing anymore. It's calculated. Danielle's too polished to leave a paper trail. And Jihoon..." Her expression hardened. "He knows Solar inside out. He knows how to mess with her head."

"I'm handling it," Solar said softly. "I told Minji. She said she'd talk to security."

"That was months ago," Yunjin snapped. "And you've gotten more gifts since then, haven't you?"

Solar hesitated.
