"I'm just trying to focus on the comeback," she said finally, her voice thin. "I can't spiral every time someone sends me something weird. People send me weird shit all the time. I'm in the public eye. I'm in two industries that attract parasocial freaks."

"Yeah, and this one has fingerprints," Chaewon said pointedly.

"I know," Solar said. "But I just want to move. We have performances coming up. Fansigns. Stages. If I let this get in my head now, I'll ruin everything."

"You won't," Yunjin said. "And you don't have to choose between protecting yourself and doing your job."

"I'm not helpless," Solar said, sharper now. "I'm not that scared little girl anymore. And if they're trying to scare me—Danielle or Jihoon or whoever—they're going to be disappointed."

Yunjin stepped closer and crouched in front of her. "I know you're not scared. But it's not about fear. It's about being smart. You're being watched. Tracked. There's a pattern here. And if we don't speak up, it escalates."

Solar looked up. Her jaw clenched, her gaze unsteady.

"I'll think about it," she said.

"Don't," Yunjin said. "Just do it."

Around them, the girls shifted in closer. They weren't speaking now, but the weight of the moment settled like a quiet circle of protection.

Solar closed her eyes for a second. She wanted to believe it was all in her head. Just coincidence. Misplaced items. Overactive nerves. But Danielle's smile from earlier—through Sakura's eyes—still lingered. Sharp. Knowing. A threat dressed in lipstick.

And somehow... she knew. It wasn't over.



The dorm was silent, steeped in the kind of stillness that only settled after midnight—long after rehearsals had ended and the cameras had shut off, when makeup was wiped away and everything felt a little too real.

Minji sat curled at the edge of the couch, a blanket thrown loosely over her knees, phone glowing in her hand. The dim blue light etched shadows beneath her eyes. She scrolled slowly through a message thread, her thumb dragging over Solar's name.

Most of the thread was old—months old. Bursts of late-night chaos, gentle teasing, the occasional vulnerable line hidden between voice notes and schedules. But lately, the conversation had thinned. Tapered off.

Her thumb hovered over the last unsent message.

i don't know why i keep screwing this up.
i'm happy for you. i mean it. i just... wish it was me sometimes. is that stupid?

She stared at it.

Deleted it.

Rewrote it.

Deleted it again.

The cursor blinked back at her. Mocking.

A faint creak stirred down the hall, followed by soft footsteps.

Hanni appeared in the doorway, oversized hoodie swallowing her frame, holding a glass of water. She blinked blearily at Minji, then padded closer.

"You're still up?" Hanni asked.

Minji nodded without looking up. "Couldn't sleep."

Hanni sipped. "Danielle's been acting weirder than usual lately, right?"

Minji glanced over sharply. "What?"

"Just saying." Hanni shrugged. "She keeps zoning out. And yesterday she left her mic on by accident during check-in and she was muttering something. I couldn't make it out. Creeped me out, though."

Minji gave a breathy laugh, brushing hair behind her ear. "She's probably just tired."

"Mm," Hanni said, unconvinced. "Anyway. I'm crashing. You should too."

"Yeah," Minji murmured.

Hanni vanished down the hall, door clicking softly shut behind her.

But Minji didn't move.

She stayed there for a long moment, the blue light of her phone fading into black.

Then, quietly, her eyes flicked toward the far hallway.

Danielle's bedroom door was slightly ajar.

Barely a crack.

But enough.

Minji sat frozen, holding her breath.

Inside, Danielle sat hunched at her desk, the glow of her screen casting an eerie white sheen over her face. Her hair was pulled back messily, and she didn't seem to notice—or care—that her door wasn't fully shut.

Minji tilted her head slightly.

Just enough to catch a glimpse of what Danielle was looking at.

The image on her phone screen.

It was a photo. Zoomed in. Blurry.

But recognizable.

Solar.

And Jungwon.

Solar mid-step, turned slightly as if trying to avoid attention. Jungwon beside her, eyes fixed protectively ahead.

The picture was grainy. Not posed. Not fan-taken.

Minji's breath caught.

The angle was too low. Too close. It wasn't from a broadcast or press photo. This wasn't something that had made the rounds online.

This was personal.

Taken from nearby. Without their knowledge.

Watched.

Danielle stared at the image in absolute stillness. Not smiling. Not frowning. Just... blank.

Her expression was unreadable. Not jealous. Not giddy. Not gossipy.

Just still.

Minji's skin prickled.

She didn't move. Didn't breathe. Only watched.

And something inside her twisted.

She had always known Danielle was intense—competitive, particular, sometimes obsessive. But this wasn't competitiveness. This wasn't even fixation.

This was something else.

Something cold.

Minji slowly eased back into the couch, careful not to make a sound. Her heart pounded against her ribs, too loud in the silence. Her fingers curled around her phone, screen dimmed now, Solar's name still glowing faintly.

She could storm in.

She could say something.

She could ask.

But something held her back.

Because now the silence between her and Danielle no longer felt passive.

It felt dangerous.






50: Thin Air

There were nights in Seoul that felt like glass—too clear, too sharp. Nights where the wind carried whispers in its teeth, where streetlights blinked like tired eyes, where the whole world seemed to lean in just to listen.

Solar walked with her headphones in and the volume low, but she wasn't really listening. The song had looped three times already, some wordless lo-fi beat with a slow-drowning piano. Her jacket hung heavy on her shoulders, unzipped. Her hands stayed buried in her pockets like she was afraid of what they'd do if left alone.

No disguise. No hoodie. No mask. Just herself, raw and bare beneath the neon haze.

She told herself it wasn't about Jihoon.
Or Danielle.
Or Jungwon.

She told herself it was just air. Just walking. Just breathing.

Breathe in.
Breathe out.
Keep moving.

The storefronts were closed but still glowing—warm signs advertising bubble tea and hair salons and cheap late-night ramyeon. A cat darted out from behind a trash can, eyes catching gold in the lamplight. Solar didn't flinch. Her eyes followed it absently, tracking the flick of its tail until it vanished.

"You're fine," she whispered aloud. "You're not unraveling."

She liked the way the words fogged the air in front of her, disappearing before they ever had to land.

Her feet took her to the quieter part of the district, where the roads narrowed and curved, where the alleys felt like secrets too stubborn to spill. She hadn't meant to come this way. Or maybe she had. Maybe her body remembered routes her mind had tried to forget.

She stopped at the corner of a small intersection, where an old convenience store sat boarded up and a vending machine flickered beside it like it was dying.

She closed her eyes.

And when she opened them—

He was there.

Like a shadow stitched to the mouth of the alley.
Leaning against the wall like he'd been waiting.

Jihoon.

Her pulse didn't spike. That was the strangest part. No scream rose to her throat, no panic clawed at her chest. Instead, she stood still. Still as the air. Still as the moon.

"Late walk?" he said, like it was nothing. Like they hadn't buried something ugly between them years ago.

Solar didn't respond.

Jihoon stepped forward slowly, hands tucked into his coat pockets. His hair was a little longer now. His face still had that same unnerving softness—the kind that made people mistake him for kind.

"I used to run into you around here all the time," he said. "Back then."

Still, she said nothing.

He tilted his head. "No staff? No girls glued to your side? Just you?"

There was a gentleness in his voice that made her skin crawl. Like he was checking in. Like he cared.

"You always liked walking at night," he added. "Said it helped you think."

She stared at him. The way he stood, the way he didn't quite meet her eyes. The way he spoke with a layer of performance—too casual, too rehearsed.

This wasn't a coincidence. He hadn't stumbled into her.

He had waited.

"It's dangerous, you know," he said. "Out here alone. Especially with... everything."

Her jaw clenched.

He took another step, not too close. Just enough to make the shadows bend. "I saw your stage. The new one. You looked—"
His lips curved faintly. "Determined."

She didn't blink.

"You always go all in, don't you?" Jihoon said quietly. "Even when it's breaking you."

Something in her chest shifted, but she kept her shoulders straight.

"I'm not here to cause trouble," he murmured. "I just... wondered how you were holding up."

That line again. That fake concern he used to toss like a bone.
You look tired, babe.
I'm just saying this because I care.
You know you can't do this forever, right?

Solar took one slow breath.

Jihoon's voice lowered, almost intimate. "You've got a lot of eyes on you. A lot of people around who don't exactly wish you well."

There it was.

She didn't know if he meant Danielle. Or someone else. Or if he was trying to implant the seed just to see it grow crooked.

He met her eyes then, just for a second. His gaze wasn't threatening. That made it worse.

"I'm not the one you should be watching out for," he said softly.

Her nails bit into her palms.

Jihoon didn't move closer. He didn't smile. He just looked at her like he still thought he knew her. Like he believed the old version of her was still buried in there, somewhere beneath the glitter and grit.

Solar didn't say a word.

She turned on her heel and walked.

Each step felt like cutting through molasses.
Each breath tasted like rust.

But she didn't run.
She didn't give him the satisfaction.

Behind her, Jihoon didn't follow.
Didn't call her name.
Didn't say goodbye.

He just stayed in the alley, one shadow among many, watching as she disappeared into the night like a ghost walking home.

It was nearly 2AM by the time Solar got back to the dorm. She didn't cry. Didn't speak. Didn't take a shower. She just changed, crawled into bed, and stared at the ceiling until the shadows stopped shifting.

When she finally fell asleep, it felt like being dragged under by something that didn't care if she woke up.





The next morning came hard and fast.

Their schedule was a fire alarm—group practice at 9, vocal check-in after lunch, then back to choreography until midnight. ANTIFRAGILE was brutal in the best of ways. Explosive. Relentless. Every step a punch, every lyric a dare.

Solar told herself that was why her chest felt tight. Not because of last night.
Not because of Jihoon.

She was fine. She had to be.

She showed up to practice twenty minutes early, hair tied back, knee brace tucked beneath her sweatpants. Yunjin was already there stretching. Eunchae arrived humming, still rubbing sleep from her eyes. Sakura and Kazuha came in quietly, murmuring something about formation spacing.

Chaewon paused when she saw Solar. "You're pale," she said bluntly.

"I didn't sleep well."

"That's not—" Chaewon frowned. "Never mind."

Solar shook out her arms, rolled her neck, slapped her own cheeks lightly like it would jolt the exhaustion from her bones. It didn't help. She kept moving.

The choreography started like fire—sharp, burning, fast. It demanded precision but fed on chaos. Solar threw herself into it with everything she had.

She didn't miss a beat. But she also didn't blink. Didn't breathe right.

The hours blurred. She could feel the ache in her shoulders tightening like rope. Every time she bent her knees, she felt the sting. Her fingers went numb by the third run-through. She pretended she didn't notice.

"Let's take five," their choreographer called.

The girls collapsed to the floor in puddles of sweat and laughter. Eunchae flopped onto her back and stretched like a cat. Yunjin reached for her water bottle with trembling hands.

Solar didn't sit. She stood still in the center of the room, head bowed, jaw tight. Her vision blurred at the edges, like the world was slowly zooming out.

"Sis?" Yunjin said gently. "Come drink something."

"I'm good."

"You've barely had water all day—"

"I said I'm good."

Yunjin's brows drew together. "What's wrong?"

"Nothing," Solar bit out. "I'm fine. Just let me focus."

The room quieted a little. Sakura's gaze flicked over her. Chaewon's lips parted like she wanted to speak, but stopped. Then the music cued again. They rose.

Another take.

Solar pushed harder. She forced her body to obey. Her limbs were lead but she moved like she was floating—dangerous, hypnotic. Her hair clung to her neck. Sweat soaked through her tank top. Her heart felt like it was hammering from somewhere outside her body.

The bridge hit. The lights caught her face. And then—

Everything tilted.

For a second, she thought the floor had moved. Then the room pitched forward like a boat in a storm. Her legs buckled. She felt it in slow motion—the way her knee gave out, the way her chest seized up like a fist, the way her lips parted with a soundless gasp—

And then— Darkness.

"SOLAR—!"

"Someone get water—!"

"Unni, wake up—!"

She came to slowly. Blinking. The harsh studio light stabbed through her lashes. Shapes hovered above her—frantic, blurred. A hand cradled her head. Another gripped her wrist, checking her pulse. Someone was brushing her bangs back, murmuring in panic.

"Don't move her—wait—let her come back—"

"I knew something was off, I knew it—"

"She didn't eat this morning. She hasn't been eating right all week—"

"Should we call someone?"

Solar's throat was dry as sand. Her lips felt cracked. Her chest rose in tiny, shallow pulls of air. She blinked again—slower this time—and found Chaewon's face above her, pale with worry.

"Hey," Chaewon said softly. "You fainted. Just stay still."

Solar tried to sit up. Yunjin pressed a hand gently to her shoulder. "No, lie down."

"I'm okay," Solar rasped.

"You are not okay."

Kazuha knelt beside her, handing her a cold bottle of water. "Drink. Slowly."

Solar obeyed. Every sip stung going down. Her fingers trembled as she held the bottle. Sweat beaded along her temples despite the chill in the room.

Chaewon didn't let up. "We need to tell the staff."

"No."

"Solar—"

"No," she said again, sharper this time. "It's just low blood sugar. I overdid it. I'll be fine."

The girls exchanged glances. Chaewon's voice dropped. "This isn't just about today. You've been running yourself into the ground for weeks. We've seen it."

"I'm trying to keep up."

"You're leading us. We're trying to keep up with you."

Yunjin added, "And you're not giving yourself room to—breathe. To feel anything."

Solar looked away. Kazuha's voice was quiet but firm. "You don't have to be perfect."

Solar swallowed. The water burned in her throat. The edges of her vision had stopped blurring, but her chest still felt too tight. Not from the fall. From the weight. "I'm not telling the staff," she said again, softly. "If they think I'm slipping, they'll sideline me. Or cancel things. And we can't afford that. We're just getting momentum back."

Chaewon's lips thinned. "So you'll let your body collapse before that happens?"

"If I'm going to burn out," Solar murmured, "at least let it be worth something."

The silence that followed was deafening. Yunjin knelt beside her. "Is this about the comeback? Or everything else?"

Solar didn't answer. Because it wasn't just the comeback.

It wasn't just Jihoon. Or Danielle. Or the press. Or the pressure. It was all of it. All at once.

And the only way she knew how to survive was to keep going until the noise inside her head quieted.

Even if her body screamed. Even if her heart begged her to stop. Even if the girls—her sisters—saw her falling apart piece by piece.

She looked at them now. At their eyes rimmed with worry. Their trembling hands. Their unspoken ache. "I'm sorry," she whispered. "I didn't mean to scare you."

Chaewon softened. "We're not mad. We're scared for you."

Eunchae finally spoke, her voice small. "Please don't collapse like that again. I thought... I thought something bad happened."

Solar reached for her hand and squeezed. "I'll be okay," she lied. "Just need to rest. That's all."

No one believed her. But no one pushed further.

They helped her sit up slowly, then brought her food. Quiet things—rice porridge, bananas, a honey drink from the fridge. They let her rest in the corner of the studio, wrapped in a blanket Sakura found in the back room.

Practice was over for the day. But the weight of what happened had only just begun to settle. Solar watched them quietly, heart thudding.

And somewhere inside her, something whispered:
You can't keep this up forever.


51: If You Ever Want To

The hallway smelled like lemon floor cleaner and old vending machine coffee. Under the hum of fluorescent lights, Eunchae leaned against the beige wall, her oversized hoodie damp with sweat from another grueling practice session. She clutched a cold water bottle like it was the only thing keeping her grounded.

Across from her, Kazuha sat perched on the edge of a narrow windowsill, silent. Her long legs were folded in front of her, and her braid was coming loose, strands sticking to the side of her neck. Neither said anything for a long moment.

Only the occasional echo of trainee footsteps and a faint melody seeping from one of the recording studios filled the space.

Eunchae broke the silence first. "I really thought she hit her head." Her voice was too soft, like she was still afraid to say it out loud. Her thumb rubbed the condensation off her bottle. "I mean, one second she was dancing, and then—she just dropped. Like her body couldn't even pretend anymore."

Kazuha looked down. She didn't say I know, because she did. They'd all seen it. The sharp crack of limbs hitting the floor, the thud that came with it, the panic that rippled through their group as Solar's eyes fluttered, unfocused, and her body refused to cooperate.

"She kept saying she was fine," Kazuha murmured. "And we believed her."

Eunchae's throat tightened. "We had to. You saw her face. The second Chaewon suggested telling the staff, she looked so scared."

"She didn't want them to know." Kazuha leaned her head against the glass behind her. "But... maybe we should've told them anyway." She didn't say it cruelly. She wasn't blaming. If anything, it was aimed at herself. Her lips were pressed into a guilty line. "We should've told the staff," she said again, firmer now. "They need to know how much this routine is taking a toll on her health."

Eunchae chewed on her lower lip.

"I don't want to go against her wishes like that," she said. "She really doesn't want them to know. Not after everything. The media, the comeback, the scandal. She's just trying so hard not to make any more noise."

Kazuha closed her eyes.

In truth, they were all exhausted. UNFORGIVEN wasn't just demanding — it was devouring. Each day in the practice room felt like some twisted test of loyalty. How far could they go? How long could they endure? How brightly could they burn without completely incinerating themselves?

And Solar... she never stopped burning. Even now, after fainting, she'd laughed it off, brushed it away, tied her ponytail back up and asked if they could run the chorus again.

That wasn't bravery. It was desperation in disguise.

"I just want her to be okay," Eunchae whispered.

"I know," Kazuha mumbles, dully.

Neither of them noticed the boy who had passed the corner a moment earlier, pausing just out of sight.

Somewhere down the hall, close enough to hear, Jay stood still, heart stuttering.

He hadn't meant to eavesdrop. He was just coming back from the restroom, planning to grab his water bottle from the hallway shelf. But the words hit him like a low-pitched alarm bell.

Fainted. Didn't want the staff to know. We should've told them.

It took him a second to fully process it. Then, without thinking, he turned around.

The Enhypen practice room was quiet now. The main setlist had been run, speakers unplugged, and most of the members were sprawled in various states of rest.

Jake had his back against the wall, flipping through his phone. Sunghoon was lazily rolling a tennis ball underfoot, stretching his ankle. Niki was lying on the ground entirely, limbs splayed like a cartoon character who'd just been flattened by a truck.

Jay stepped inside, tension trailing behind him. Jake looked up. "Yo, you good?"

Jay didn't answer right away. He shut the door gently and turned to face them. "I think something happened to Solar."

That got their attention. Even Niki's head lifted. Sunghoon furrowed his brows. "What do you mean?"

Jay crossed his arms, his voice low. "I overheard Kazuha and Eunchae. They were talking near the vending machines. Eunchae said she thought Solar hit her head. They were talking about her fainting... during practice."

Jake sat up straighter. "Wait—what?"

"They said she didn't want anyone to know," Jay continued. "Didn't want the staff involved."

Sunghoon's mouth parted slightly. "You sure you heard that right?"

Jay nodded. "I'm sure."

There was a moment of collective silence. Heeseung, who had just reentered with a drink from the break room, paused mid-sip. "...She fainted?"

Jay's gaze flicked to him. "That's what they said."

Niki let out a soft whistle, rolling onto his side. Jake ran a hand through his hair. "Should someone... do something?"

Sunghoon shrugged, unsettled. "If she didn't want people to know, then..."

"That doesn't mean she's okay," Jay said.

It hung there for a second. And then the door opened again. Jungwon stepped inside.

Towel slung over his neck, hair damp from a quick rinse, he looked perfectly calm. But he slowed when he saw the way everyone was looking at him.

"What?" he asked, glancing between them.

No one spoke right away. Then Jay cleared his throat. "I think something happened to Solar."

Jungwon's eyes narrowed, just slightly. "Like what?"

"She fainted during practice," Jay said, voice calm, measured. "Eunchae told Kazuha. They were talking in the hallway. She didn't want the staff to find out."

Jungwon stilled. The reaction was microscopic, but it was there — the faintest flicker of tension beneath his skin, like a chord being plucked inside his chest.

He didn't ask for details. He didn't speculate. He just set the towel down, picked up his phone from the bench, and walked out the door.

Wordless. Like his body was already moving before his mind could decide.

Back in the Le Sserafim practice room, the atmosphere had gone quiet. Solar had finally relented, sitting down on the floor, legs stretched out, water bottle cradled in her lap. She was pale, her face washed out under the warm studio lights.

The girls had rallied around her — not loudly, not dramatically. Just soft words. A shared blanket. A cooling pack pressed against the back of her neck.

No one dared say what they were all thinking: She doesn't look okay.

Solar kept insisting she just needed a few minutes. That she skipped lunch, that her blood sugar was probably low, that it wasn't a big deal.

She was always so good at sounding convincing — even when she was falling apart.

But outside the room, someone was looking for her. And this time, she wouldn't be able to lie.




The hallway was still.

Muted. As if the building itself was holding its breath, tucked somewhere between the rhythmic basslines thumping through practice room walls and the echo of distant footfalls. The fluorescent ceiling lights buzzed quietly above Solar's head, washing everything in that sterile, too-white glow that always felt too harsh for her mood.

She was leaned back against the cool wall, one knee bent, foot pressed flat behind her. Her water bottle dangled from loose fingers. Her neck was slick with sweat, and her bangs stuck slightly to her forehead despite the blast of AC that reached this hallway. Her hoodie had been abandoned long ago, tied around her waist, revealing her tank top clinging to her ribs like a second skin. Her chest rose and fell too quickly, but she focused on slowing it down.

Headphones hung around her neck. Not playing. Just there—an accessory of avoidance. A shield.

She didn't notice him at first.

Not until a voice, low and level, broke the stillness behind her like the snap of a twig.

"You fainted during practice?"

Solar startled. The water bottle slipped from her hand and rolled lazily across the floor before bumping into the baseboard.

She turned quickly, finding Jungwon standing at the edge of the hallway, half-shrouded in shadow, arms crossed loosely. His eyes, though, were clear. Alert. Watching her too carefully.

"So it did get out," she said, voice light and brittle like a joke she wasn't really making.

She bent to retrieve the bottle, brushing her palm against the floor for a beat longer than necessary. When she rose again, her smile was already forming—tight at the corners, too practiced.

"Don't worry. I'm fine," she added, voice coated in airiness.

Jungwon didn't answer right away.

He stepped forward once, then paused again, gaze steady in that infuriating, unreadable way of his.

"That's not true."

Solar's smile didn't falter. But something in her throat tightened.

"Well," she said, unscrewing the cap of her water bottle, "that's the version we're going with."

"Why?"

His voice was quieter now, not challenging. Just... sincere. Unsettlingly so.

Solar shrugged. Took a sip. Let it dribble down her throat just slowly enough to delay answering.

"Because people freak out," she said, finally. "They make it a whole thing. And I really don't want a thing."

"You fainted," he repeated, like she hadn't heard herself.

"Yeah, okay. One time."

"That's not—" He stopped. Inhaled slowly. Adjusted his weight.

"Are you eating?" he asked.

She frowned.

"Yes."

"Sleeping?"

"Yes."

She was lying. She knew he knew. But she stared at him evenly, almost daring him to call her out.

He didn't take the bait. Instead, he exhaled through his nose and leaned back slightly, hands slipping into his pockets. His expression didn't change, but something about his silence felt heavier than anything he could've said.

"Look," she said, softer now. "It's just a comeback. We all push ourselves. You do it too."

Jungwon's jaw tensed, just slightly. His gaze flicked down to her hands—the way her fingers were curled too tightly around the bottle, the pale coloring of her knuckles.

"You're shaking," he murmured.

Solar followed his eyes, then tucked her hands behind her back without thinking.

"I'm just tired," she said.

"Exactly."

The word hung in the air.

She looked away. The floor tiles blurred for a second before snapping back into focus.

"I'm handling it," she muttered. "I'm keeping it together."

He didn't agree or disagree.

"And the night walks?" he asked after a beat. "Are they part of the routine too?"

That made her look up. Sharply.

"What?"

"Eunchae mentioned you've been sneaking out. Alone. No disguise."

Her face burned.

"She was worried," he added quickly. "Not gossiping."

"Of course she was. Because I'm the fragile one now, right?" Solar's voice came out sharper than she intended. "Because I fainted one time and suddenly I'm—what? The weak link?"

Jungwon's brows furrowed.

"That's not what anyone thinks."

"You don't know that."

Silence again.

This time, it stretched long. Tangled between their feet like invisible wires.

When she spoke again, her voice was quiet.

"Everyone keeps telling me I don't have to be perfect," she whispered. "But the second I'm not, everything changes. They worry. They question things. They pull back."

Jungwon took another step forward. Now just a few feet away. Close enough that she could hear the gentle creak of his sneakers on the tile.

"I won't pull back," he said.

Solar's breath hitched.

She looked at him then—really looked at him. The soft fall of his hair over his brow. The faint shadow under his eyes that meant he hadn't been sleeping well either. His jaw, tight but not clenched. His posture, open but hesitant. Like he was trying to approach a wounded animal without scaring it.

Something fluttered in her chest.

She didn't know if it was fear, or longing, or just the aching kind of safety that always came with him. The kind that made her feel seen in ways that terrified her. That stripped every mask clean off and left only the messy, breathless, trembling girl underneath.

And then—

Without a word, Jungwon reached into his jacket pocket and pulled something out.

A small, black notebook. No label. No decoration. Just matte, soft leather and a neatly bound spine.

He held it in one hand like it weighed nothing. But Solar could already feel it anchoring her.

"You don't have to talk," Jungwon said quietly, gaze never leaving hers. "Not to me. Not to anyone. But if your mind's racing, and you're scared to say it out loud... maybe write it down instead."

The words hit harder than they should have.

Solar stood frozen, staring at the notebook in his palm as though it were dangerous. As though touching it would make something unravel inside her.

Her breath caught.

Her fingers twitched.

But she didn't reach for it.

Not yet.

"Why are you doing this?" she asked, voice lower than before. Not suspicious. Just... scared. Raw. Like the question had slipped out of a deeper place she didn't usually let anyone near.

Jungwon didn't answer immediately.

He looked down. Then up again. A slight shrug of his shoulders, almost self-conscious.

"Because you're trying so hard not to fall apart," he said. "And I don't want to watch that happen."

There was no accusation in it. No pity. Just the weight of someone who saw her, and couldn't unsee it.

Solar didn't respond. Not with words.

Her fingers reached forward, slowly, like the motion itself might shatter her. She took the notebook from him. Their hands brushed—briefly, but enough for the static to bloom in her chest. She held the notebook like it might burn her if she gripped it too tightly.

She didn't say thank you. She didn't need to.

A silence stretched between them—long and slow, like dusk settling over a rooftop. Neither of them moved. The world outside the hallway faded.

And then, as if some thread had loosened in her chest, Solar let herself look at him. Really look. Jungwon stepped a little closer, just enough that she could feel the warmth of his presence. His eyes didn't waver. And for once, she didn't try to hide the mess behind her own.

She took a breath. Hesitated. Then—

"Back then," she said quietly, "in the hallway... you walked away."

Her tone wasn't blaming. Just honest. And a little hollow. Jungwon's gaze dropped for the first time.

"I did," he admits. A pause. "I didn't want to. But I saw what I saw. And I thought... maybe I didn't have a place anymore."

Solar held the notebook tighter. There it was. The thing that had never been spoken. The moment that haunted them both.

"I wasn't thinking," she said. "That day. I just... I was breaking, and I needed something to feel safe. I didn't mean for it to hurt you."

"It didn't," he said.

She looked at him, startled.

"It didn't hurt me," Jungwon clarified, voice even. "But it reminded me that I couldn't keep pretending nothing had changed. Between us... In me."

The ache in her chest pressed harder now. Not pain—just the unbearable closeness of almost. She wanted to say something. Anything. But every word stuck behind the knot in her throat.

Jungwon's fingers brushed the edge of her sleeve—just the slightest graze. It wasn't a pull. Wasn't a reach. Just a reminder.

"You don't have to explain," he said gently. "Not now. Not ever. I'm not asking for anything."

Another breath. Another silence. Then— "But if you ever want to say it," he added, softer still, "I'll listen. Always."

And that's when it happened. Not a kiss. Not even a touch. Just a moment.

The air between them changed—thick with something unspoken, almost unbearable. Her hand still clutched the notebook. His stood inches from hers. Neither moved. The space wasn't empty. It was full. Of memory. Of tension. Of something blooming too slowly to name.

Then— "Solar?" Yunjin's voice. Distant, but coming closer.

Solar startled. Jungwon blinked and stepped back just slightly, like a spell had broken.

"Jungwon?" another voice called. Probably Niki.

They didn't flinch. Just stood for another beat, letting the moment settle between them like dust in golden light.

"I should go," she murmured.

He nodded once. "Me too."

But neither of them moved right away. Solar finally turned first, footsteps soft against the polished floor.

Jungwon stayed behind for just a second longer. Watching. Not clinging. Not running. Just... there. And then they were gone. Not apart. Just waiting.

52: Support System

The dorm felt hollowed out after midnight—like the day had been scooped from it, leaving the shell of rooms and the thin hum of the fridge. Streetlight spilled through the blinds in a ladder of pale bars, the kind of light that made ordinary things look like props: a mug in the sink, a hoodie on the back of a chair, a pair of slippers turned neatly under the coffee table. Somewhere down the hall, a pipe ticked like a metronome warming up.

Minji sat on the end of the couch with her knees pulled to her chest, chin resting on her stacked forearms. Her phone was warm against her ankle where she'd tucked it, screen face-down to keep from glancing every five seconds. She told herself she wasn't waiting for anything. Not for a message. Not for sleep. Not for courage.

From the hallway, a low rectangle of light opened across the wood floor: Danielle's door, not quite closed.

Minji didn't get up. She just let her eyes slide there and stay.

Danielle sat at her desk with her back mostly turned, shoulders hunched in a way that used to mean "lyric idea" or "outfit brainstorm," the loose, concentrated posture of someone building something out of nothing. But lately, the hunch was different. Too tight. Too still. No music playing, no pencil tapping. Just the hard white square of her phone cupped in both hands, held too close to her face.

Minji's mouth was dry.

She told herself she could look away. That she should. That this was none of her business, that roommates are allowed to be strange and that artists are stranger and that she was only nervous because she hadn't slept enough and had too much tea and she was catastrophizing again, the way Hanni teased her for.

But the image from two nights ago rose anyway—uninvited, uncannily crisp. Danielle's screen caught in a sliver of light: a zoomed-in, blurry photograph of Solar and Jungwon mid-step, taken from the wrong angle, the wrong distance. Not a fan cam. Not a press photo. Watched.

Danielle's face in that glow had been unreadable. Not jealous. Not gleeful. Just... blank. Like a window with the lights off, reflecting nothing.

Minji pressed her lips together until the ache made her stop.

"Sleep," she told herself under her breath, because saying the word out loud sometimes tricked her body into obeying. "Sleep."

Her phone buzzed against her ankle. She startled, heart leaping, and then felt ridiculous for it. A weather alert, nothing else. The tiny mundanity of it steadied her for one breath, and then the light from Danielle's room shifted: the swivel of a chair; a shadow crossing the desk.

Danielle stood, phone still in her hand. She didn't leave the room. Didn't move for the door. She just stepped to the mirror, lifted the phone, and—Minji's stomach turned—took a picture of something on the wall. Not a selfie. Not even a clear shot. Just a small white rectangle taped beside the calendar.

The list?

They all had them. Minji's was above her vanity: rehearsal call times, hair and makeup blocks, the color-coded lines that kept the chaos in order. Danielle's was the same—except now she was photographing it in the middle of the night like the information might run away if she slept.

"Stop," Minji whispered into her sleeve, and she wasn't sure whether she meant Danielle or herself.

When Danielle lowered the phone, the glow lit her profile for a handful of seconds—cheekbone, lashes, mouth set in a line that wasn't sad or angry, just determined in a way that didn't belong to any version of Danielle Minji knew. Then she turned back to the desk and sat. The light sharpened again.

Minji slid her phone out and unlocked it with her thumb. If she waited for certainty, she'd never say anything. Certainty didn't come in real life; it came in stories, after the danger passed and someone narrativized it into a neat arc. In the moment, all you had were pricks of data: odd looks, the wrong smile, a photo with the angle all wrong, the way someone stayed awake like sleep had asked a question they didn't want to answer.

She opened Solar's thread. Their messages had thinned to utilitarian lately—schedules, a thumbs-up, the occasional sticker when one of them posted a stage and the other "happened" to see it within minutes. Minji stared at the empty text box. Words ballooned and collapsed.

She typed:

I think Danielle is—

Deleted it. Typed:

Are you awake?

Deleted it. Her thumbs hovered, then fell into a pattern older than her fear.

Be careful. Just... please be careful.

She stared at it for five full seconds, listening for the sound of her better judgment telling her to be specific, to warn, to name. The dorm made its small settling noises—the fridge cycling on, the pipe ticking again. In Danielle's room, the phone camera clicked once more, the soft synthetic shutter sound that made Minji's skin pull tight across her arms.

She hit send. The message sailed away, blue bubble blinking into place like an admission. She set the phone in her lap, palms against either side of it, like she could hold down whatever might come next.

In the doorway, Hanni's bedroom shifted and went still. Somewhere, a car horn honked three floors below and then cut off. The city breathed.

Minji leaned her head against the back of the couch and let her eyes close just long enough to see what they wanted to see: Solar laughing in a dressing room mirror with glitter in the corner of her eye; Solar in a hoodie big enough to swallow her, face bare, eating tteokbokki on the floor at 2 a.m.; Solar texting her a picture of a stray cat with the caption it's you (derogatory). And then, maddeningly, Solar looking up in a hallway with that stunned, guilty underlight in her expression, Jungwon a still point at the end of it, notebook in his hand like a promise he didn't know whether to keep.

Jealousy isn't the right word, she told herself, and she believed it. Not because she wasn't jealous, but because the feeling that swelled in her now was larger and more unwieldy than that—love thinned out with worry until it was almost transparent, protective in a way that made her chest ache. She could want and still want Solar safe. Both could be true; both had been true for months.

Her phone buzzed once in her hands. She jerked, then looked. No reply from Solar. Just the timestamp marking her message as "Delivered."

Of course. Promotions, rehearsals, shoots. Solar's hours braided together into a rope she'd been climbing bare-handed. And now—now there was the notebook too. Minji hadn't seen it, hadn't even known about it until Yunjin's offhand comment earlier at a shoot ("I swear, the way he looks at her...") had lodged lodestone-deep. She didn't know what she would have said if Solar had mentioned it. Congratulations? I'm happy for you? Be careful, anyway?

"Be careful." Useless advice when the danger had footsteps and a face Minji knew.

She forced herself to stand. The couch cushion sighed like relief beneath her. She padded to the kitchen on socked feet and poured water from the pitcher into a glass, the glug loud in the hush. She drank half in one go, then set the glass in the sink and stood gripping the edge of the counter until her knuckles blanched.

You're spiraling, she told herself, and then: maybe spirals are just circles that decided to go somewhere.

On her way back to her room, she paused in the hallway where the shadows from the blinds banded the floor. Danielle's door was still open the same two inches. Minji didn't get closer, didn't lean in. She stayed where she was and let her eyes adjust.

Inside, Danielle was motionless again, phone face-down now, both hands flat on the desk as if bracing. She wasn't watching a screen, wasn't scrolling. Just... sitting. Minji waited for some normal human fidget—the scratch of a wrist, the tuck of hair behind an ear—but nothing came. The stillness had edges.

Minji's throat clicked when she swallowed.

She thought of Solar's last few weeks like a string of beads: the backstage hallway, the kiss, the notebook that didn't get given, the gifts with no sender and too much intent, the night walk in a city that could turn unfriendly in a single breath, the fainting, the insistence she was fine. She thought of the way Yunjin carried everyone else's injuries like they were her own, of Chaewon's anger that came from love, of Eunchae crying in the practice room bathroom and pretending she wasn't when she came out. She thought of Jungwon's face the one time she'd actually seen it up close—the careful quiet, the gravity—and she did something she hadn't expected to do.

She prayed, quick and graceless and for no one in particular. Please. Let her be okay. Let tomorrow be boring.

She took one step, then another, and the floorboard by Danielle's door chose that moment to protest. Danielle's head lifted a fraction. Minji's breath snagged. She kept walking, the casual rhythm of it too studied, and turned into her own room, shutting the door with the softest click she could manage.

She sat on her bed in the dark, letting her eyes find the outlines of her own life—the poster taped above the dresser, the sweater draped over the chair, the notebook she used for hooks that never made it into songs. She took it into her lap and opened to a blank page.

For a second, she almost wrote Solar's name. She didn't. She wrote the date instead, and beneath it, four words, small and neat:

Danielle still awake. Watching. She stared, then added a fifth word, after a beat that felt like swallowing something too big. Worried.

Her phone, facedown on the comforter, buzzed once. She flipped it over. No reply from Solar. Just the thought of the message lighting up on a nightstand across the river, the language of care diluted to a single sentence inside a blinking screen.

She set the phone beside her pillow. The building sighed. Outside, a siren wound up and away into nothing. In the other room, a chair scraped softly against the floor and then fell quiet again.

Minji lay down and curled around herself, the way she used to as a trainee when sleep was the only thing that made the ache go quiet. Her eyes burned. She blinked until the ceiling turned to watercolor.

Maybe she was overreacting. Maybe tomorrow would be fine. Maybe the wrong smile had been a trick of light. Maybe the zoomed photo was nothing. Maybe the late nights were just late nights and the lists were just lists and she was just tired.

She closed her eyes on the word.

Be careful, she thought toward Solar, the way you think toward someone in a crowd so hard it feels like shouting. Please be careful.

In the next room, a phone camera made that soft little shutter sound again, too faint to prove, too loud to ignore. And Minji, who had always trusted herself least when it came to the people she loved, didn't move.

She didn't sleep for a long time.



The rehearsal room was heavy with heat, a low-grade fever that clung to every surface. The mirrored wall was hazed with condensation, a smear of sweat and breath that distorted the reflections into shifting ghosts. "Unforgiven" pulsed from the speakers again, bass thudding through the wood floor like a heartbeat too big for the space.

Solar's sneakers squeaked in the small pauses, every turn sharper than it needed to be. Her ponytail clung damply to the back of her neck, strands sticking across her temple, but she didn't slow. Each count was a promise she bit into: one, two, three, four—no wobble, no falter, no hesitation.

But the eyes were there. Always there. She could feel them like spotlights cutting through the beat.

Chaewon's in the mirror, her brow furrowed despite the softness of her voice when she called counts. Kazuha's lingering glances, quick but worried. Eunchae biting her lip so often that Solar wanted to snap at her to stop, though she didn't. And Yunjin—Yunjin, who masked her worry the worst, whose gaze burned like it was willing Solar to sit down, just once, just for a second.

When the chorus dropped into its power-heavy stomp, Solar's chest locked, tight and stuttering. She bit it back. Forced her breath into something that looked controlled. She wasn't going to fall. Not again.

"Again from the top," Chaewon called, voice pitched louder than usual.

They reset. The track began.

Halfway through the verse, when Solar pivoted too sharply and her wrist caught against Yunjin's, she winced before she could stop it. The flash of pain darted up her arm, hot and sudden.

She pretended it hadn't happened. Straightened. Moved. But Yunjin's head had already turned, sharp and unmissable.

By the time the song ended, Solar was doubled over, hands braced on her thighs, sucking air in through her teeth. She straightened before anyone could tell her to. The taste of iron clung to the back of her throat.

Chaewon wiped her forehead with her sleeve, eyes cutting sideways at Yunjin. The unspoken passed between them: She's going too hard again.

Chaewon finally said it aloud, voice quiet but steady. "We can't let her push herself like that again."

It wasn't for Solar. It wasn't to her. But the words hit anyway, sticking like burrs.

Solar pulled her ponytail tighter and straightened her spine until it ached. "I'm fine," she said, each word clipped. Her chest still heaved, betraying her. "Really. I just want to nail this comeback."

The silence that followed was louder than the music. Eunchae's mouth opened and closed, caught between loyalty and fear. Kazuha's gaze softened but didn't move away. Chaewon pressed her lips together, the set of her shoulders saying more than words could.

Solar turned back toward the mirror before anyone could answer. "Play it again."

But Chaewon only reached for the speaker remote, paused the track, and said, "Break."

The girls filed out gradually—water bottles, towels, the rustle of bags. Solar stayed behind, stretching one leg forward, then the other, folding over until her breath evened out.

Yunjin lingered too.

"Sol." Her voice was soft, but not tentative. A weight, not a question.

Solar glanced at her in the mirror. "What?"

Yunjin crossed the floor, crouched beside her, arms draped loosely over her knees. "I saw you with Jungwon yesterday."

The name in Yunjin's mouth made Solar flinch more than she wanted to admit. She snapped upright. "So?"

"So," Yunjin repeated, unbothered, steady. "Did you tell him about what happened?"

Solar blinked. Her throat closed for half a second before she forced her voice out. "He already knew. I guess one of the boys overheard."

Yunjin's brow ticked upward. She didn't push right away, letting the silence sit until it was heavier than the air in the room. Then, she leaned in slightly, gaze unwavering. "I can tell he really cares about you, Sol," she said, voice lower now, each word deliberate. "You just need to let him."

The words landed like a hand pressed flat to her chest. Not painful, not even unwelcome—just heavy, unavoidable.

Solar opened her mouth, but nothing came out. So she shut it again, eyes darting away.

Yunjin watched her for another second before pushing herself to her feet with a sigh. "Break," she repeated, this time gentler. Then she left her there, the echo of her words ringing louder than the chorus of Unforgiven.




By the time Solar made it back to the dorm, the hallway smelled like fabric softener and the ghost of hairspray. The others had scattered to showers or couches; someone's laughter drifted from the living room, thin and faraway. Solar dropped her bag by the door of her room, rolled her shoulders until something popped, and pushed in with the single lucid thought of face-planting into her pillow until the world blinked.

Chaerin was already on her bed.

Sprawled sideways across Solar's duvet like a cat who'd claimed a sunspot, she was scrolling on her phone with the kind of boredom that looked choreographed. Sunglasses were perched on top of her head despite the absence of sun; her boots (ridiculous and studded) were very much on Solar's blanket.

Solar stopped dead. "How did you get in here?"

Chaerin didn't look up. "Eunchae gave me the door code," she said, thumbs flicking. "You should really change it. It's literally your birthday, which is cute and also tragic."

Solar shut the door with her heel and gave the boots a look. "Feet."

Chaerin sighed like this request was an assault on personal freedoms, then kicked her boots off with two violent shoves. They toppled to the floor and lay there like defeated monsters. "Happy?" She finally glanced up, eyes flicking over Solar's face with the speed of someone who noticed everything and pretended not to. "You look like you lost a fight with a treadmill."

"Thanks," Solar muttered, going for the vanity to tug out a hair tie and catch the sweaty strands sticking to her neck. In the mirror, her cheeks were too pale and her eyes had that thin, overlit shine. She didn't look at it for longer than she had to. "Why are you here?"

Chaerin rolled to her stomach, chin propped on her forearms. "To be annoying. And—" She paused for effect, then shot Solar a sideways look. "Heeseung told me you fainted."

Solar blinked. "He—what?"

"Heeseung," Chaerin repeated, drawing the syllables out, enjoying herself. "Tall, quiet, annoyingly handsome, thinks he's slick when he's not? That Heeseung." She made a little ta-da motion with her phone. "He texted. Which, by the way, is a huge deal, because he hates texting. It's practically a confession of love."

Solar scowled despite the flush that crept up her neck. "For who?"

"Me, obviously," Chaerin said, deadpan, then scrunched her nose. "Don't look like that. Not that kind of love. Ew... He said it like—" Her voice softened, an impression slipping into unconcealed worry. "Like he was actually concerned. 'Is Solar okay?' Like that. With punctuation and everything."

Solar dropped onto the edge of the bed, keeping a healthy distance from her sister's sprawl. "I'm fine."

Chaerin tilted her head. "You fainted."

"It was hot. I hadn't eaten. Chaewon called a break. It's not a big deal."

Chaerin's mouth curled into a smirk that didn't reach her eyes. "So... are you seriously okay, or do I have to drag you out of practice myself? Because I can and I will. I once carried Juyeon out of a club like a sack of laundry... I've got technique." She hooked a finger at Solar's elbow like she might test the leverage.

Solar swatted her hand away, but the corner of her mouth betrayed her. "I'm seriously okay."

"Mm." Chaerin let the noncommittal sound hang there, then flipped onto her back and held her phone above her face, thumbs idle now. "You're doing the thing again."

"What thing?"

"The thing where your face goes all 'I'm a robot sent from the future to protect this comeback,'" Chaerin said, eyes tracing the ceiling. "And your body is like 'cool, but we're a meat suit and we have limits.'"

Solar groaned into her hands. "You're unbearable."

"Stylishly unbearable," Chaerin corrected, then peeked at her out of the corner of her eye. "Heeseung said you went down fast. He said the girls handled it, though." A beat. "And Jungwon looked like he wanted to break a wall when he heard."

Solar's fingers stilled where they were picking at a loose thread on the bedspread. "He didn't—Was he there?"

"No," Chaerin said. "Jay heard Eunchae and Kazuha talking in the hall like two worried grandmas... Then Jay told the boys, and Jungwon did his silent storm thing. Heeseung's words, not mine... Well, kind of mine." She propped herself up on her elbows, studying Solar's profile. "You gonna let him care about you, or are you gonna do your favorite trick where you pretend feelings are a rumor?"

Solar's exhale came out as a laugh that wasn't one. "Did Yunjin hire you?"

"I'm freelance," Chaerin said breezily. Then, softer: "I'm your sister."

The room shifted on that word, like a camera re-focusing. The dorm hum outside the door, the muffled TV from the living room, the hallway air freshener's faint citrus—they all receded under the weight of it. Solar dragged her palms down her face and let them fall to her lap.

"Chaer," she started, and stopped. When she spoke again, it was more honest than she'd intended. "I can't afford to be the story again right now."

Chaerin's expression flickered. The drama fell away, the quips went quiet. "I know."

"HYBE's breathing down our necks. The choreo's... a lot. The girls are watching me like I'll shatter every time I breathe too hard." Solar shrugged, helpless. "If people start whispering that I'm fragile—"

"—you'll set yourself on fire to prove them wrong," Chaerin finished, not unkindly. "I know that trick, too."

Solar stared at the floor where Chaerin's boots lay like punctuation. "I just want this comeback to be perfect."

"It won't be," Chaerin said, blunt in that way that somehow always landed as kindness. "Because nothing is. But you will be you, which is somehow worse for your enemies."

Solar huffed. "Who are my enemies?"

"Do you want alphabetical or by level of delusion?" Chaerin asked, then waved it off. "Point is: you don't have to die for a chorus."

"I'm not—" Solar began, and then stopped again, the rest of the sentence evaporating. "I'm not trying to die for anything."

Chaerin let the silence sit for a beat and then sat up, cross-legged, facing her. She picked up a throw pillow and hugged it, chin atop the edge. "Okay. So here's my very reasonable offer. You promise me you'll eat something before practice tomorrow, like a real something, not a tragic protein bar that tastes like drywall. You text me if your vision goes weird or your chest does that tight thing again. You do not, under any circumstances, try to be impressive at the cost of being alive." Her mouth tipped into a grin. "And in return, I will refrain from storming the dance studio in heels and making a scene. Mostly."

"Mostly," Solar echoed, amused despite herself.

"Mostly," Chaerin confirmed. She tossed the pillow at Solar's shoulder, gently. "And I am allowed to threaten you with sibling-level embarrassment at any time if you fail to comply."

Solar caught the pillow and hugged it back without thinking. The tension she'd been hauling around her scapulae eased a notch. "You're impossible."

"True." Chaerin watched her for a moment longer, and whatever calculation was happening behind her eyes softened into simple worry. "I'm serious, though. Don't make me cry on national television, idiot."

Solar's surprise must have flashed, because Chaerin looked away immediately, pretending to examine a chip in her manicure. "I won't," Solar said, and it came out gentler than a promise. "I won't make you cry."

"Good," Chaerin said briskly, as if the moment hadn't happened. "Because mascara is expensive and my brand is above sobbing. Also, if I ruin these lashes Heeseung will never let me live it down."

Solar lifted a brow. "He talks about your lashes?"

"He talks about everything when he's pretending not to talk," Chaerin said with a shrug that tried for offhand and landed on fond. "Anyway, point is: people who don't deserve you are going to try things. You know that." A beat. "Jungwon's not one of them."

The sentence slid in like a pin, neat and precise. Solar stared at her sister. "Since when are you Team Jungwon?"

"I'm Team Don't Be Stupid," Chaerin said. "Which occasionally overlaps with Team Jungwon. He's quiet, but he's not weak. There's a difference. He pays attention in ways that matter." She plucked at a thread on the duvet, smirk tilting back into place. "Also, he's hot... Don't make that face... I'm not blind."

"Please stop," Solar said, covering her eyes.

"I will not," Chaerin said cheerfully, then sobered one last time. "Promise me about the food?"

Solar let her hands fall and nodded. "Okay."

"And the texting."

"Okay."

"And no dying for a chorus."

Solar sighed. "Okay, Chaerin."

"Good." Chaerin slid off the bed in one graceful twist, scooped up her boots, and jammed them on without unzipping them because she was an agent of chaos and blisters. "I have to go pretend to study something so Jaehyun doesn't send me an essay-length lecture. He's in his 'ethics' era again. It's exhausting."

Solar snorted. "Tell him to write footnotes this time."

"No thanks, I like my peace." Chaerin crossed to the door and cracked it, then looked back, hand on the frame. "Rest. Or I swear to God, I'll livestream myself dragging you out of practice by your ponytail. And I'll tag everyone."

Solar saluted with two fingers. "Resting."

"Liar." But Chaerin smiled. "Whatever. Just... try."

"I will."

"Goodnight, idiot."

"Night, menace."

Chaerin slipped out. The door clicked shut.

Solar sat very still for a moment, listening to her sister's boots clatter away down the hall and the dorm's familiar sounds swell back in—the TV laugh track, someone rummaging for a snack, water racing through the pipes as a shower turned on. Her shoulders dropped another notch. On the dresser, her phone buzzed with a Discord ping she didn't check yet. On the nightstand, the little black notebook waited, patient as a breath you haven't taken yet.

"Don't make me cry on national television," Solar repeated under her breath, and—unexpectedly—smiled.

The dorm was finally quiet. Solar sat cross-legged on her bed, hair damp from a late shower, the glow of her phone screen the only light in the room. She scrolled aimlessly at first—news, fancams, muted messages she had no energy to answer.

Then the familiar Discord ping cut through the silence.

A cascade of messages lit up the corner of her screen:

Super: Hey Seoul superstar, you forget about us already?

Nero: Off-season scrims are dead without you.

Rascal: You should play sometime. You're still part of the team.

Solar blinked at the little flurry of words, her lips twitching upward despite the weight in her chest. She could almost hear Super's voice, half-mocking and dramatic; Nero's dry deadpan, which always hit harder because he rarely bothered; Rascal's quieter note, cutting through the noise with simple, grounding certainty.

Her thumbs hovered. For a second, she thought about ignoring it—about pretending she was too busy, too locked into idol mode to even acknowledge that part of herself. But the longer she stared, the warmer the thread became, wrapping around her like a tether to a different life.

Finally, she typed:

Busy with the comeback. Soon.

Three dots appeared immediately—Super, of course—before vanishing again. The channel went still, but Solar kept staring, the faint smile staying even after the light dimmed from her phone.

It was a small reminder. A soft one.

That she still belonged in both worlds.

That no matter how heavy the silence pressed in, somewhere, her family of chaos was still waiting.

Her gaze slid to the edge of the nightstand, where the small black notebook sat like a secret.

She reached for it slowly, as though it might vanish if she moved too quickly. The cover was smooth beneath her palm, edges slightly worn—as if Jungwon had carried it around for days before finally pressing it into her hand.

For a long time, she just held it there, thumb brushing the corner. Her pulse was too loud. Her throat too dry.

Finally, she flipped it open.

The first page wasn't blank.

Neat handwriting stretched across the top in dark ink—Jungwon's, steady and deliberate:

There's a version of you you haven't met yet.
When you find her, name her. Hold onto her.

Solar's chest tightened so hard she thought she might crack in two.

The words weren't dramatic. They weren't even long. But they cut sharper than anything anyone had said to her in months. Because he'd noticed. He'd seen the storm under her ribs, the way she was clawing for shape while trying to keep the world from noticing.

Her hand trembled as she reached for the pen tucked into the spine. She hovered over the page, heart hammering, and for a second she considered what she should write. Something powerful. Something worth the weight of the note.

Strong.
Fearless.
Unbreakable.

Her vision blurred. She pressed the tip of the pen to the paper and wrote instead, in small shaky letters beneath his words:

Tired.

The ink bled slightly into the paper. It wasn't pretty. It wasn't brave. But it was real.

She stared at it until her throat burned, then closed the notebook gently, like it was something fragile.

Clutching it to her chest, she curled onto her side.

Sleep came slow but certain, pulling her under with the weight of that one word—and the echo of his.














53: Tired But Trying

Morning at HYBE arrived with fluorescent certainty. The rehearsal wing was already awake when Solar stepped through the double doors, the air-conditioned chill skimming damp skin and raising a brief constellation of goosebumps along her forearms. Staff voices braided in low threads from the wings; a camera op rolled past with a gentle clatter, cables looping in even coils behind him. Above, the rigging grid disappeared into darkness, a quiet web of metal and lines.

Onstage, Unforgiven's opening riff bled from a speaker in test mode—volume low, bass more felt than heard. The track's swagger sounded oddly soft this early, like a lion clearing its throat.

Solar rolled her shoulders back and made herself smile in greeting as a stagehand passed. Echoes of her own footsteps bounced in the wings: hollow, controlled. The little black notebook was in her bag, tucked between a towel and a packet of electrolytes. She'd opened it the second her alarm went off, just long enough to see the first page again, to see his handwriting, to see her own small word beneath it. Tired. She'd snapped it shut and told herself the new word she wrote later might be different. Might be braver. Might be something like ready.

Chaewon stood at center, long hair braided down her back, hands on hips as she watched two techs mark the corners of formation with blue tape. Kazuha and Sakura were at the mirror line stretching their calves in quiet, synchronized lunges—heels pressed down, toes forward, breath steady. Yunjin hovered by the sound board with one headphone pressed to her ear, testing the playback that always sounded perfect until six lungs and two cameras were in the room. Eunchae, hoodie still up, danced in miniature—hands flicking, shoulders ticking, feet hardly moving, like she was saving fuel.

"Morning," Chaewon called without looking away, voice low but even. Her gaze tracked a tech on a ladder adjusting a wash angle. "Warm-up in five. Be kind to your knees today."

"You mean her knees," Yunjin murmured, not quietly enough, and caught Solar's eyes in the mirror. She softened it with a small smile. "Kidding."

"Not kidding," Eunchae muttered, then flushed when Solar lifted a brow.

Solar dropped her bag by the downstage mark and started the ritual she trusted more than sleep: slow neck rolls; shoulders up, back, down; arms opening until tendons sang. Her body had learned the grammar of pain months ago—what could be ignored, what would turn into a paragraph later if she didn't listen now. Today, the ache in her right wrist spoke first, a thin thread that tugged when she flexed. The knee answered next, a stiffness like a door that needed oil. Her chest stayed quiet, which felt like grace.

She checked the mirror. Pale, yes. But not ghostly. The high ponytail was neat; the mic pack sat flush under the cropped hoodie, cable threaded cleanly. She tapped the shell of the pack twice with two fingers—the same way she always did—as if the habit could make breath and beat agree to behave.

"Shoes," Chaewon said to the room, which meant everyone. "Water. If your ankle's taped, check it again. No surprises."

"Not even pleasant ones?" Yunjin asked.

"There are no pleasant surprises on a rehearsal day," Chaewon said, completely serious.

Kazuha came to stand beside Solar, mirroring her hamstring stretch without making a show of it. Up close, Zu's voice was barely more than a breath. "Sol, are you sure you don't want to tell the staff? Maybe take the morning off."

Solar kept her forehead near her shin a beat longer than necessary so she wouldn't answer with her face. Then she rose and shook out her arms. "Don't be silly," she said, grinning the way you grin to deflect a tear. "I'm fine. It's comeback time."

Kazuha's mouth pressed into a shape that wasn't quite a smile and wasn't quite a frown. She nodded once. "Okay."

The okay meant I'll drop it here and I'll keep watching.

Sakura padded over with the quiet certainty of someone who always has what you need and looped a resistance band around Solar's ankles. "Ten side steps," she said, not asking. "And breathe." She squeezed Solar's wrist once before stepping back, an I saw the flinch, and I'll pretend I didn't if you want me to.

Solar did the steps. The band dragged warm friction against her skin. She counted under her breath—one, two, three, four—because numbers were easier than everything else. The bass under the floor pulsed in time.

"All right," Chaewon said, clapping twice. "Let's mark once. No full-out. Cameras can watch spacing. I don't want anyone trying to be a hero before nine."

Yunjin snorted. "You're asking a lot."

"Just try," Chaewon said, dry.

They took their places. Solar felt the formation as something physical—Eunchae at two o'clock, light and fast; Sakura to her left, silk that turned into blades; Kazuha anchoring the back corner like a quiet warning; Yunjin the flexible hinge between center and wing; Chaewon the line they all trusted to be the line.

In the wing, a crew member lifted a hand: ready. Yunjin gave sound a thumb up. The track swelled; the honeyed taunt of the opening set the room's temperature.

They marked. It was sloppy on purpose. Small shoulders, hips ghosting through shapes, feet testing the floor instead of attacking it. Solar's body knew where to go without being asked. That was the strange cruelty of practice—you could be ordinary tired or bone-tired or soul-tired and the choreography still unfurled like a reflex you didn't remember choosing.

"Stop," Chaewon called gently at the end of the chorus, and the music obediently faded. "Spacing looks good." She didn't glance up from her tablet. "Solar, your three-quarter is drifting left by about an inch."

"Got it," Solar said.

"Eunchae, watch that elbow on the ripple. If you're early, you'll clip Yunjin's jaw."

"Sorry!" Eunchae squeaked.

"It's a good jaw," Yunjin said, patting her cheek in exaggerated absolution. "Let's keep it intact."

They ran the bridge on counts. Cleaned the footwork on the second pre-chorus. Argued amiably about a hand angle and a head tilt. Normal. The kind of friction that made the work better.

Still—the mirror whispered at Solar when the music did not. Pale. Careful. She ignored it, lifted her chin, rolled her shoulders back, and felt the click of vertebrae settle like beads.

"Water," Chaewon announced. "Two minutes."

Eunchae bounded offstage like someone had cut a string. Kazuha stayed where she was, rolling one ankle at a time. Sakura sat at the edge of the deck and tucked a stray hair behind her ear with tidy precision. Yunjin stepped to Solar's side and bumped her shoulder—a small, casual check, the kind you give a teammate at a starting line.

"You good?" Yunjin asked, phrasing it like a joke.

"Golden," Solar said.

"Liar," Yunjin replied, grinning, and handed over her bottle without a speech.

Solar drank. The water was cold enough to make her teeth sting. She swallowed carefully and felt it sink like a stone into a stomach that hadn't wanted breakfast. The room buzzed with small necessities: someone taping a shoe, someone testing a lav battery, a staffer on comms saying "copy" three times to someone who clearly didn't.

"Full-out," Chaewon said when two minutes had been exactly two minutes. "Cameras will roll for reference only. Don't worry about face. Give me breath."

"Can't promise the face thing," Yunjin said, and did an absurd model pout. Laughter loosened a knot somewhere behind Solar's sternum.

They reset. Little red lights winked alive on two side cameras. The room inhaled together. The track punched up—crisp, cocky—and this time they moved like the floor belonged to them. Solar let the first chorus chew through her legs and spit her out taller. She rode the micro-pauses like edges and let her hands cut clean lines in the air as if opening a window.

By the second verse, sweat slicked the nape of her neck. Her lungs were awake—the burn good, the rhythm clean. She felt the place where her body always threatened to drift—between the last pre-chorus and the dance break—and talked herself through it: one more, then one more, then one more. The world narrowed to counts and breath and the familiarity of six bodies moving like a single thought.

The dance break hit. Feet hit. Hands flashed. Solar's smile was real for three counts.

Then a slip—a whisper of rubber where it meant to grip. Her sneaker skidded half an inch on a patch of rosin that hadn't set. The jolt was nothing, a hiccup. But her right wrist caught the angle of the correction in a way that lit the tendon like a match.

She didn't flinch. She didn't need to. The pain smoldered under the movement and went out.

"Cut," Chaewon called on the last pose—right arm high, faces lifted toward the grid where the light would bloom for real. "Breathe."

They did. Camera tally lights blinked off. Somewhere above them, metal sighed—the faintest creak as the building settled. No one looked up. No one noticed.

"Good," Chaewon said, scrolling. "Closer than I thought. Solar, three-quarter was perfect that time. Yunjin, save a breath for the last chorus; your voice will hate you otherwise."

"My voice already hates me," Yunjin said.

"It hates you because you're dramatic," Sakura murmured.

Kazuha stepped into Solar's sightline, not blocking—just present. "Water," she said, not a question. Solar took the bottle and a longer pull, pulse drumming in her gums.

"You sure?" Kazuha asked again, softer now, the kind of question you can only answer honestly when the run is over and pride has loosened its grip.

Solar met her eyes in the mirror and gave a short, decisive nod. "I'm sure."

Zu didn't look convinced. She didn't argue. She filed it away, and when they took places again, she stood a little closer than usual.

They ran sections. Transitions. A stylist pinned a flyaway at Solar's crown that no one else could see. A tech nudged a floor mark exactly two centimeters. They rehearsed the bow they wouldn't give today, the quick formation shift to adjust for a camera, the little contingencies that make a live taping work.

Solar's body held. Quietly, stubbornly. Each time she told it one more, it believed her. That felt like a small miracle and a small betrayal.

During another water break, Chaewon drifted toward Yunjin with her voice pitched so it wouldn't carry—and carried anyway. "She's pale again," she said, soft in the way it gets when she hates naming what she can't fix with precision and practice. "Promise you're okay, Sol?"

Solar, three steps from the wing to grab a towel, heard it as clearly as if Chaewon had said her name first. The sentence landed like a coin dropped into a glass: clear, sharp, impossible to ignore.

She didn't turn. She pressed the towel to her face and made her voice steady. "I'm fine," she called, turning back with the smile already in place. "I just want to nail this comeback."

Yunjin's eyes softened and sharpened at once. Chaewon's did the opposite. Eunchae shuffled, chastened by a worry she hadn't voiced. Sakura's gaze traveled from Solar's mouth to her hands and back, as if the truth might be hiding in her grip.

Kazuha said nothing. She didn't need to. She stepped close enough that their shoulders almost touched at neutral.

"Okay," Chaewon said finally, not conceding so much as filing it for later. "Two more full-outs. Then we clean the ending and we're done."

"Copy," Yunjin said, offering a tiny salute that made the mood almost light.

They reset again. The track rose around them like a crown. Solar took her mark and let the mirror split her into two: the girl who said I'm fine and the one who might later write a different word to make it true. She adjusted her cable, flexed her right hand once to make sure the wrist would play nice, and looked straight ahead.

"Music," Chaewon said.

The rehearsal hallways at HYBE are their own city—white walls, fluorescent lights, basslines bleeding from doors that open and shut like breaths. Between one room and the next, groups collide: trainees, idols, staff on different clocks but sharing the same arteries.

Solar stepped out of the practice room with a water bottle in one hand and a towel slung over her shoulder. Her hoodie clung damp to her back; her hairline was soaked despite the AC that kept the corridors sharp with chill. She gripped the bottle too tight, knuckles pale against the plastic, and told herself no one would notice. Chin up. Composure in place.

From the other direction, Enhypen spilled out of Bite Me rehearsal. Laughter moved with them—Jake unwrapping a snack, Sunoo nudging Ni-ki for missing a count, Heeseung trailing with an unbothered stride.

And then—Jungwon.

The corridor narrowed at a bend. For a beat, no one spoke. The air seemed to hum louder than the muffled track playing behind Solar's door.

Her eyes found his before she could stop herself.

He looked like he always did—dark hair damp, jaw carved sharp by overhead lights—but something else lived in the way his gaze held her. His eyes flicked down to her hand clenched white around the bottle, then back to her face. He didn't speak. He didn't change expression. She knew he saw.

And she knew he knew.

Solar's chest tightened. She swallowed and gave the smallest lift of her chin. Not a smile. Not a greeting. A silent dare: don't ask. Don't make me say it.

For a moment he looked like he might stop. The corridor narrowed to two people. Everything else blurred at the edges.

"Come on, you're blocking traffic," Jake said lightly, tugging Jungwon's sleeve as he passed. Sunoo's hand brushed his back too, casual but insistent, pulling him with the group.

The spell broke. Jungwon's gaze lingered a fraction longer—unreadable—then he let himself be pulled away.

Solar exhaled, slow and careful, realizing she'd been holding her breath. She turned and pushed the practice-room door open again before anyone could see how her hand shook when she lifted the bottle for another sip.

The dorm was quiet by the time Solar got back, the kind of hush that only falls when schedules finally stop pulling everyone in different directions. Eunchae's soft snores drifted through the wall; in the kitchen, Sakura's kettle clicked off; Yunjin's laughter at an old drama faded as her door shut.

Solar sat cross-legged on her bed, hoodie sleeves pulled over her hands. The room smelled faintly of shampoo and fabric softener—ordinary, grounding. On the nightstand, the notebook Jungwon had given her waited.

She stared at it a long time. She'd opened it once, written the truth she couldn't say out loud—Tired—then closed it so quickly the ink barely had time to settle. Tonight felt different. Maybe it was the way his eyes had pinned her in the hallway, reading everything she hadn't said. Maybe it was the ache in her chest that hadn't left since she hit the floor last week.

Slowly, she reached for it. The cover was plain, black, unremarkable—heavy in her hands anyway, like it carried the weight of words no one wanted to speak. She flipped to the first page, eyes catching on his handwriting.

There's a version of you you haven't met yet. When you find her, name her. Hold onto her.

Her gaze dropped to the word she'd written beneath it. Tired. The letters still looked raw. She traced them with a fingertip, feeling their blunt honesty.

But tonight, something in her resisted leaving it there. She uncapped a pen and lowered it to the paper. Her hand hovered, afraid of saying too much. Then, small and steady, she added one word beneath the first:

Trying.

She exhaled. It was defiant, yes, and vulnerable—an admission that even if her body was fraying, even with Jihoon and Danielle like shadows at the edges, she hadn't stopped fighting. She wouldn't.

She shut the notebook gently and pressed her palm to the cover as if sealing the words inside. Then she leaned back against the pillows, the book still against her chest, and let her eyes close.

Outside the window, unnoticed, rain began. Somewhere in the distance, scaffolding groaned in the weather—only a building shifting its weight, nothing more.

54: The Stage Breaks

The stage looks bigger on recording days.

Not wider—the dimensions don't change—but the lights stretch it, the cables frame it, and the red tally lamps blinking alive on the cameras make everything feel like it's leaning forward, waiting. Solar stands on her spike mark and breathes through her nose until the air stops catching in her chest. The floor radiates a low heat up through the rubber soles of her sneakers. The LED wall behind them rolls through test colors—cyan, magenta, white—before settling on a slow, smoky gradient that someone in visual design swore "feels like defiant." There are clipboards at every edge of the world and walkie-talkies crackling in every shadow.

She tightens the strap on her mic pack and flattens the tape along the cable at the back of her neck. The tape tugs lightly when she turn-checks her head left-right-left. Her ponytail skims her shoulder blades. She can feel her pulse there. She is pale—she knows she is, no matter how good the foundation is—but the mirror in the green room said her eyes looked steady. That will have to be enough.

"Set in two," a stage manager calls, voice echoing from the tech table out front. The big crane camera floats in to test its path along the chorus curve, red light winking. A rigger up on the catwalk takes a knee and talks into his headset, eyes on a silver line that feeds a trim point. Crew move like a practiced tide—carts sliding, gels being swapped, someone jogging a coil of cable to the wing and laying it down perfectly straight.

Chaewon crosses into Solar's sightline with the leader's tablet tucked into the crook of her arm. Her braid is wound tight and sits between her shoulder blades like a metronome. "We'll do three full and notes," she says, calm without being cool. She drops her voice on the last line, only for them. "It's just us and the cameras. Breathe."

Yunjin glides by, sweatband on her wrist, already humming under her breath like her body can't help starting a warm-up even if her mouth doesn't mean to. She bumps Solar's shoulder with her own. "Don't make me body-slam you to the floor if you go heroic in the bridge," she says lightly, but her eyes track Solar's face the way you track a bruise you know is under a sleeve.
