"Noted," Solar answers, and tries on a grin that doesn't wobble.

Kazuha materializes at Solar's right, calm as a garden before rain. "Ten ankle circles," she says, and demonstrates, counting under her breath. "And if your wrist talks, drop the angle on eight. I'll cover your line so the camera doesn't notice."

Solar rotates her ankles. The left clicks, the right resists and then yields. The wrist—her right—sends up its usual dull message, not an alarm, just a reminder that it's there and has opinions. She shakes her hands out to the fingertips and unrolls her shoulders. "If your back snaps," she murmurs back, "tell it we have a shoot."

"It never listens," Kazuha says, lips twitching.

Sakura approaches with a resistance band looped in her hands. "Side steps," she says, looping the band around Solar's ankles as if it's a habit they share. "And drink." She holds out a bottle without looking away. "Please."

"Bossy," Solar jokes, but she obeys. The water is cold enough to make her teeth hum. She swallows carefully. The band pulls against the outside of her calves as she steps out and in, ten to the left, ten to the right. The movement warms a little space behind her knees. Breath finds a rhythm. The hum that's lived low in her chest since dawn—Don't faint again, don't scare them again, don't make this a thing—turns down one click.

Eunchae bounces up, hoodie half-zipped, cheeks already pink with excitement. She bounces once, stops herself because Chaewon hates needless bouncing on shoot days, and bounces once more anyway because she's Eunchae. "We're going to kill it," she says, solemn in the way only the youngest can be when she's trying to sound older. Her eyes dart to Solar. "Right?"

"Right," Solar says, and means it for both of them.

"Places," the stage manager calls again, closer this time. He points to A-cam. "Rolling for reference... now." A red light goes solid. "B-cam... rolling." Two red eyes, then three, then the crane's tally lamp flares to life. A sound tech crouches, checks the line on Eunchae's mic, taps the pack, and thumbs up out of frame.

"Ready," Chaewon says softly, like a blessing.

They move into the opening picture and the formation locks around Solar—the geometry they can hit even in their sleep. She widens her stance a hair, sets her toes on the tape that means do not drift, and imagines the movement before it starts: the shoulder line on count four, the switch into the first diagonal at seven, the camera pass on the first chorus when she'll drop her gaze for a heartbeat and then slice it back up. She tests her air—one deep inhale, one measured exhale—and tells herself the word she wrote last night when the dorm was quiet and the notebook lay under her palm like a warm stone.

Trying.

The track lifts in the monitors. The bass shows up first, low and patient. A few out-front staff clap silently on one and three, muscle memory. The riff comes in and the floor seems to lean toward them. Solar's chest loosens not because she's calm, but because the song fits. She doesn't have to find courage; she has to find the count.

The first verse feels like putting on a jacket you've broken in just enough. Her body knows where to go and why. Her arms slice the air and stop on a dime. Her hips make the line mean what the lyric says, not a copy of someone else's feeling but her own control. In the corner of her eye, she sees Kazuha's weight shift with hers, catching any wobble before it begins. Yunjin's mouth curves on a line the cameras love. Sakura's hands stay soft, then knife-sharp at the end of the phrase. Chaewon's head turns a fraction ahead of the beat so the camera has her eyes where it wants them. Eunchae glows.

By the first chorus, Solar isn't thinking about fainting. She isn't thinking about who might be in the hallway, about late-night texts, about the creepy stillness in Danielle's face the last time she passed the NewJeans wing. She isn't thinking about Jihoon's voice that showed up like a draft under a door and then disappeared when she pretended not to feel it. Her world is six bodies and the red glow of a tally light and the way the track insists.

Halfway through the chorus, the crane sweeps close. Solar lets her gaze glance off the lens and then away, exactly how they rehearsed, like she's flirting with the camera but married to the choreography. She feels the heat rising up her neck, sweat collecting at her hairline, the tape at the nape pulling in a familiar pinch. She notes each thing and moves on. On the floor, the spike tape is scuffed where hundreds of feet have obeyed it; her shoe lands on it anyway. The music becomes map and fuel.

They reset into the second verse and the room tightens. The producers out front shuffle one chair closer; someone whispers into a headset; the director leans toward the monitor and points with two fingers at a spot where he wants the next push-in. Solar's chest tugs once like it can't quite expand as much as she asks, and she counts a breath across eight beats, smoothing it. She thinks of Yunjin's voice saying you don't have to do this alone and lets the thought rise and fall with the count like a buoy.

Chaewon calls, quiet enough that only they hear, "Save breath."

Solar does. She chokes down the urge to exhale everything and leaves some room for the dance break—exactly as taught, exactly as trained, exactly as she promised she would after the practice room scare that made Eunchae cry and made Chaewon swear under her breath in a way that meant leader and sister at once. She doesn't glance at the mirror wall at the back; there's no mirror here, only the eyes of cameras and the memory of a mirror that taught her where to put her face.

Between phrases, she clocks more of the room than usual, because vigilance sits on her shoulder these days and refuses to get off. A rigger above them stands and checks a pulley, then sits again. The LED wall's edges hum. The arch on stage left—slim, faux neon, designed to look weightless—hangs in its trim and throws a tidy bend of light onto the floor. Nothing is wrong. Everything is prepared. Yet her skin has that thin, alert feeling she gets when a storm decides to skip the thunder and go straight to rain.

"Second pre—ready," Chaewon murmurs.

"Ready," Yunjin echoes, and the sound of her breath riding the word is a reminder that they do this with lungs and legs, not magic. Sakura's shoulder brushes Solar's for a fraction, not a stumble, a check. Kazuha adjusts a half-step back to widen their shape for the lens. Eunchae's hands flare exactly to the angle they charted on a whiteboard three nights ago, and she grins, tiny and genuine, like she's remembering this is fun.

Solar's heart does its tired flutter in the long beat before the dance break. She tells it not now and it listens, or pretends to. Her right wrist complains as she carves through the pre-chorus pattern and she adjusts the angle by a hair to keep the line clean without sending a flare up the tendon. She files the adaptation away for notes. She will tell Crusty on Discord later that the fix is not unlike adjusting a crosshair—you don't wrench, you nudge, and you let the tool do what it was built to do. The thought slips in and makes her mouth twitch for no one but herself.

The chorus returns, louder in her chest than in the room, and the cameras take their planned path: A-cam wide, B-cam over Sakuras's shoulder, crane dipping in toward Yunjin's face and then arcing back to catch the whole picture. The stage heat lifts the hair along Solar's jaw. Her thighs burn the good way. Every time she wants to let her focus drift to the perimeter, she drags it back to the center of the formation, to the place where her foot meets tape and the eight-count tells the truth.

"Last mark before break," Chaewon says, still calm, still metered. "Still with me."

"Still with you," Solar answers in her head, and on the outside she looks like a girl who never misses, who never stumbled, who was born in this light.

At the edges of the stage, crew kneel with their heads tilted, checking things they always check. A stylist stands by with a handful of pins she will never need. The director says, "Beautiful—hold your face open there," and someone nods and writes a timecode down. The rigger above shifts to a different view and stares at a trim point for a beat longer than usual. He says something into his mic that the girls can't hear. He stands; he sits. The arch on stage left hums a hair louder and then goes still.

Solar doesn't look up. She looks forward. She stops thinking about anything but the drop into the dance break and the breath she has banked for it. When Chaewon inhales, Solar feels it in her own ribs. When Yunjin's mouth sets at a certain angle, Solar knows the camera is about to love them. When Kazuha's foot swings back that particular way, Solar's ankle wants to mirror it so badly her muscles buzz.

The cut between set-up and go is crisp and visible to anyone who has ever learned a routine from counts and then transferred it to music: the shift of weight into the heel, the tiny lift in the chest, the moment the face stops being a person's and becomes the song's. Solar feels that switch flip in her bones. She leans into it.

The last thought that crosses her mind before the move starts is not about stamina or pride or fear of falling again. It is about the notebook. It is about the way the first page read as if it knew she would try to be brave when what she was, mostly, was tired. It is about the ink that bled a little when she wrote Trying and liked that it wasn't pretty. She picks that word up like a coin and holds it in her palm.

The bass line drops into the floor and they go.

Solar's body catches the first hit like she's been waiting all morning to be allowed to move that hard. The room narrows to the radius of her arms and the distance between her and the camera and the sound of sneakers gripping the floor in time. She doesn't think about the hallway. She doesn't think about the manager who asked her, again, if she needed a day. She doesn't think about anything except the next eight.

Behind the cameras, the producers lean forward. The director's pen hovers over the page where he marks the take. A clock in the corner counts up. The LED wall's gradient deepens and spills a little warmth onto the stage. The girls look like a single decision.

From the catwalk above, a small sound travels—a metal whisper, the kind a careful person hears and a tired person doesn't. The rigger stiffens, lifts a hand for the person on the next bay over, and leans to squint at a line that, on any other day, would not be interesting. He keys his mic. Down on the floor, the chorus swallows the first word the rigger says.

Solar feels nothing but the count. Her right foot plants. Her left foot swings. She draws her arm through the figure and feels the right wrist grumble and then behave. Kazuha's breath is where Solar expects it to be, and when she steps backward, the empty space is exactly where her heel wants to land. Chaewon's profile is a blade in the corner of the lens. Yunjin laughs a little in the back of her throat because she can't help loving the rush.

Eunchae, who always watches one thing too many because she's young and it hasn't broken her yet, glances up for a fraction, frowns at nothing she can name, and looks back down. She files the feeling in the same place Solar files the word Trying. The camera won't see it. The floor will feel it.

The break is two breaths away. Solar takes one. The other sits in her chest, waiting for her to need it. Her legs shake in a way that reads as energy and not as warning. She tells her body, not for the first time, You can have the pain later. The stage collects all the heat in the room and throws it back at her face. Her mouth curves because it has to. The track says now.

She goes.

The break lands clean. Solar feels the impact in her chest, every rib vibrating with the force of her breath. The beat eats the space between counts, and her body moves because it's supposed to, because this is the part where you prove you can be more than tired.

The sound doesn't belong to the song.

It unthreads the measure—first a whisper, then a groan, the kind of complaint metal makes when it's been asked to hold one inch too long. The cameras don't hear it; the cameras only love faces and angles. The crowd of staff doesn't notice yet, because the break is hitting and six girls are cutting the stage into clean geometry.

Solar's heel kisses the spike tape and pivots. Her lungs scrape against their own edges. Her right wrist flutters with fatigue from a hundred cuts that morning, but she keeps the line true. Chaewon's eyes flick sideways, a leader's radar, but there's no room to adjust. The chorus demands precision.

Above them, a line twangs—a sharp, plucked sound like a string stretched past its pitch.

On count four, the arch of light on stage left shifts a fraction. No one sitting at home would clock it, but the riggers do. One jolts upright on the catwalk, headset pressed to his ear. "Line three's—!"

The bass swallows the rest.

Solar doesn't hear him. The track drums through her pulse and her body obeys the count because that's what it's trained to do. She spins into formation. The heat in her chest says still standing. Her foot finds its mark. Her face stays open. That should be enough.

The groan snaps.

It isn't cinematic—no thunderclap, no catastrophic crash. It's a hinge surrendering, weight deciding it's tired of being a secret. The cable gives a little, just enough to let the arch tilt forward, the bottom edge angling down like a nod.

Eunchae's eyes widen mid-move. Kazuha checks a fraction, then returns to the line, mind counting even as her body plots for impact. Chaewon doesn't break character, but her shoulder slides toward Solar by instinct, a shield half-formed.

Solar doesn't see it; her back is turned in the spin.

The director half-rises from his chair, pen hovering. "Cut cameras—cut!"

Too late.

The arch drops again, a lazy swing. Its corner slices through the air above Solar's head and she feels it before she comprehends it: a rush of wind, a shadow sliding where it shouldn't. Instinct barks move but fatigue is a hand on her ankle, and she's one beat behind her own command.

She shifts late. Too late.

The edge grazes her shoulder—just a kiss of trim, enough to tilt the world. Her sneaker twists on tape, grip slipping on a slick of sweat. The floor is suddenly not where it should be. Her knees fold, her center caves.

She crumples.

It isn't dramatic. No scream, no outflung arms. Not a staged fall—just a quiet collapse, like a candle when oxygen runs out. Her cheek meets the stage, heat and grit and the bitter taste of rehearsal dust at her tongue's edge.

The music keeps going for a single, indifferent bar. The track doesn't care.

"Kill the track! Now!" someone from the table roars, and the song dies mid-syllable, the silence that follows too naked, too loud.

"Solar!" Chaewon's voice tears. Yunjin has already broken formation without thinking, dropping to her knees so fast her mic cord tugs. "Sol, hey—look at me, look at me."

Kazuha abandons her line wholesale, one arm sliding behind Solar's shoulders to soften the fall that's already happened, the other braced so nothing else can tip onto her. Sakura's hands flutter for a breath—hair, face, shoulder?—before she takes a breath and chooses, palm gentle against Solar's temple to keep her head from knocking the floor again. Eunchae freezes for half a heartbeat, then bolts, sneakers squeaking, tears already glassing her eyes. "Unnie—unnie—!"

The rigger's voice explodes through headsets. "Prop compromised! Soft-lock that line—secure the face—don't touch the frame on the floor!"

A chorus of copy answers from above. Onstage, a second later, two crew appear at the tilted arch's edges, hands hovering, waiting for the signal to move.

Solar pulls air like it's heavy. Her chest burns, her shoulder throbs where the trim kissed bone through fabric, but it's not pain that pins her—it's emptiness. The body's quiet refusal: you asked, I gave, there's nothing left.

"Don't... make it... a thing," she hears herself rasp, absurd and earnest. Her throat feels scraped raw. She tries to smile at Yunjin and almost manages it, a crooked thing that says please don't look at me like that. "I'm fine. Just—props with grudges."

"Shut up," Yunjin says, voice breaking on the up. She touches Solar's cheek with fingers that want to shake and won't let themselves. "Idiot."

Chaewon's face is carved from control, but her hands shake as she brushes sweat-dark hair back from Solar's eye. "We need med," she says without looking away, steel banding her words. "Now!"

"Here." A staffer drops to a crouch with a water bottle, someone else with the first-aid kit already unzipped. "We're cutting power to that bay. Do not move until we—"

"Concussion check?" another asks, already fishing a penlight out. He pauses, respectful foreign object hovering, waiting for permission.

"No cameras," Solar whispers, flinching from the thought more than the light. She blinks hard to pull the room into focus. "Please. Delete it. Don't let this—don't let this get out."

The director pinches the bridge of his nose, tension etched deep from the catwalk to the floor. "We'll make the call," he says to his assistant, to the room, maybe to the ceiling. "Cut the take before impact. Secure the rig. We reset when safety signs off. Talent—offstage."

"We're not resetting until she can stand," Chaewon snaps without raising her voice. She glances up at a rigger and then back down, calculating and caring in the same breath. "Solar. Breathe. In. Out."

Solar obeys. One breath that tastes like dust and hairspray and shame. Another that brings the edges of the world back in: Kazuha's steady anchor at her back, Sakura's soft voice counting without meaning to, Eunchae's hand squeezing her fingers like an anchor.

"Hospital?" a manager says, hovering at the edge of the circle, tone pitched low like he can sneak the word past the girl who always says no.

"No." Solar's answer is immediate and too loud for the strength she has. She swallows and tries again, softer but not weaker. "No hospital. I'm okay. Just—give me a minute."

Chaewon's jaw locks. Her eyes flash with a retort she swallows, because there are staff and lenses and a story that can ruin a comeback if it gets out of their hands. She nods once instead. "Minute."

"Help me sit," Solar says, and Yunjin and Kazuha move like they practiced it, one steadying shoulder, one bracing under ribs, the smallest lift so she can tuck her knees under. The world tilts and rights itself. Her knee yowls; her wrist complains; her pride hisses like a cat cornered. She breathes through all of it.

Eunchae makes a noise like she's been holding her breath since the creak. "Are you dizzy? Can you see me?" She leans so close that her eyelashes brush Solar's cheek. "How many fingers am I holding up?"

"Zero," Solar says, and Eunchae looks down at her own hands, baffled, because she's not holding any up. The joke is terrible, tossed like a paper plane into a storm. Yunjin barks a laugh anyway, wet and grateful for anything to hold.

"Offstage," the stage manager says, more gently this time. He looks at Solar directly. "We'll get the rig secured. We'll talk about the take later."

Solar nods. Nods again, because the first one feels like the room didn't register it. "Delete it," she says. "Please. Don't let this be the thing that trends."

"We'll handle it," he says, and he means it in the way professionals mean it—serious, but not omnipotent.

They help Solar to her feet—Yunjin at her left, Chaewon at her right, Kazuha a breath behind, Sakura ready in case the world tries another trick. Solar's legs tremble and then cooperate. She is upright. It feels like a small miracle and a small lie.

As they guide her toward the wing, the stage swarms in their wake. The arch hangs at a wrong angle, mouth open toward the floor, the neon edge no longer pretty. Two riggers on ladders work fast, threading a soft line to take the weight while another at the deck watches the angle like a hawk. A production assistant unspools caution tape with hands that shake when she thinks no one's watching, then catches herself, breathes, and steadies.

The red tally lights on the cameras have blinked off, but their lenses stare, blank and black. Solar angles her face away anyway, reflex. She hates that she thinks about where a fan edit would freeze-frame her, hates that the thought arrives before you're okay.

They cross the border where light becomes shadow and the heat softens into backstage cool. The drape brushes Solar's shoulder and leaves a dark stroke on her hoodie. Somewhere in the maze of cable and cases, a kettle clicks off. Somewhere else, a staffer mutters into a phone about insurance and blesses himself in a language Solar doesn't speak. The building hums around them, aware.

Two steps into the wing, and the air tenses again, invisible, electric. Solar feels it without knowing why; the girls feel it because she does. They don't stop, but their shoulders come in a fraction.

Across the gap, barely inside the shadow, Jungwon stands still as a cutout, eyes locked on Solar like he could hold her upright from six meters away. He's already taken two steps toward the stage—his body moved the second she fell—and been hauled back short. Jake's grip is set on his forearm: firm, insistent, a tether you don't see in photos.

"Don't," Jake says, voice low, as if teaching him a secret he hates. "There are cameras everywhere. If you go out there right now, it becomes a different story. For her. For you. Don't."

Jungwon doesn't look at Jake. He doesn't blink. His jaw ticks with the effort it takes not to tear free. He can see the damp at Solar's hairline, the fight it takes for her to lift her chin, the way Yunjin's hand is white at the knuckles where she refuses to let go. His palms are open, then fists, then open again, like he can't decide if he's about to pray or punch a wall.

"She's okay," Heeseung says quietly behind him. It isn't a platitude; it's a diagnosis from someone who has learned to read bodies. "She stood."

Jungwon swallows something that tastes like metal and the end of patience. He nods once. It feels like lifting a car.

He steps back enough that the girls can pass without colliding. Solar's gaze snags on him for a half-beat—shock, recognition, and something like relief colliding in a flicker—and then she looks away, because looking longer would undo her. Yunjin tracks the exchange and files it like ammunition she plans to use later.

They move past him into the corridor. The green room door opens ahead, a rectangle of softer light and the promise of water and a sofa and four girls pretending not to cry while they take turns telling Solar she's not allowed to die on a recording day. Chaewon nods to the manager hovering there, and he steps forward, tone pitched low enough to be kind without being intimate.

"Solar," he says. "We're going to check you over. No cameras. We'll issue a brief statement for safety and rescheduling. You don't have to do anything you don't want to right now. Understood?"

Solar's mouth tips. It's not a smile. It's gratitude shaped like one. "Understood. And... please make sure the clip doesn't live anywhere." Her voice thins on please again. "I don't want this to be... the thing."

"We'll pull it from the source," the manager says. "We'll remind everyone on set about confidentiality. Focus on breathing."

She nods. "I am."

Yunjin squeezes her hand. "Liar," she says, a little fond, a little furious.

Inside the green room, everything smells like hand sanitizer and tea and upholstery that's seen too many pep talks. Eunchae beelines for the mini-fridge and shoves an armful of bottled waters at no one in particular. Sakura flips a paper cup upright and then another, because doing something helps. Kazuha kneels in front of Solar with a cool pack and an expression that says don't argue. Chaewon takes the corner of the room nearest the door, already a gate and a sword.

"Off with the hoodie," Yunjin orders, and when Solar hesitates, she doesn't argue; she lifts the hem herself. The shoulder is already pinking where the trim grazed. Nothing torn. Nothing broken. Everything tired.

"It's not bad," Kazuha says, relief folding her voice. She applies the ice. "You'll bruise."

"I'm okay." Solar looks down at her hands. They're steady now, because she's sitting. She hates how good the chair feels. "I'm okay."

"We heard you," Chaewon says dryly. "We're ignoring it."

Outside, the corridor murmurs: a crew briefing, the shuffle of ladders being moved, someone laughing too loudly because that's how they shake adrenaline out of their limbs. The world moves on the way it always does when something almost happens. Inside, time gathers around the couch and slows.

From the wing, Jungwon hasn't moved far. Jake has let go, but he's still a presence at Jungwon's shoulder, a friend-shaped guardrail. Ni-ki hangs back with the quiet fidget of a kid who wants to run and knows better. Sunoo peeks toward the green room door, then schools his face into nonchalance when a staffer passes. Heeseung leans against a flight case like he might be asleep if not for the focus sharpened in his eyes.

Jungwon watches the doorway until the impulse to smash through it fades from urgent to steady. He can breathe again, technically. The breath tastes wrong.

Across the stage, in the shadow of a pillar and a tangle of cable, Danielle stands where a staff badge will look normal on camera. Her phone is in her hand, angle low, not obviously raised—not obvious to anyone not looking for it. She isn't smiling, exactly. Her mouth is flat, eyes unblinking as she frames the line of the tilted arch in the same screen that once held zoomed-in photos of two people at a rooftop edge.

A production assistant steps across her view for half a second. When he moves, her thumb is already moving. Not post, not yet. Save.

The director's voice rises on the other side of the room, harried but settling. "We'll reschedule this segment. Safety sign-off first. I want a full inspection on that bay. Who touched it last? Find out."

"On it," someone says, and means it.

In the green room, Solar leans back into the sofa and tries to let the cool pack talk the ache down. Her body wants to sag into sleep and the floor in equal measure. Yunjin kneels by her knee and keeps up a stream of nonsense—memes, food, a terrible imitation of Jake's accent—until Solar's mouth softens for real. Chaewon texts a single line to the group chat: Take five. We'll regroup after safety. Sakura lines up the empty cups in a neat row like a small, private ritual. Kazuha watches the door over Solar's head. Eunchae wipes her face with her sleeve when she thinks no one's looking and then curls up on the arm of the sofa, pressing her shoulder into Solar's like she can donate steadiness by osmosis.

Solar's phone, facedown on the table, buzzes once. Then again. Then again. She doesn't pick it up. She doesn't want to see who has already heard, what the algorithm is hungry for.

She closes her eyes for a moment, just long enough to remember ink on paper and the way her own handwriting looked when she wrote Tired and then Trying. The words are still in her; they haven't fallen out. She taps the notebook's shape in her pocket with one finger like knocking on a door.

Out by the wing, Jungwon drags a hand down his face and exhales. He could go now—walk away, go back to his rehearsal room, pretend he didn't see, pretend he doesn't know what he knows. He doesn't move.

In the rafters, a rigger tightens a knot and mutters to himself in relief. On the floor, someone wraps the arch's bad corner in foam like a bandage until a proper fix can be made. A red tally light on an idle camera blinks once and dies, a tiny heartbeat spent.

The manager who promised to handle it stands at the far end of the corridor, phone to his ear, nodding at nothing, listening to someone in PR say the right words: we'll keep it brief, safety issue, artist fine, reschedule. He says yes and understood and I'll check on her again and means every one of them.

None of it will matter to the clip already living in a phone across the room.

For now, no one knows that except the girl in the shadow with the too-steady eyes and the boy in the wing whose anger has finally learned how to be quiet. For now, the room is full of people who love Solar enough to let her lie to them and call it strength.

In an hour, the rain will start, soft against the windows no one brings on camera. The scaffolding at HYBE will creak in the dark like a tired house settling.

Before that, a notification will light up screens all over the city: a low-res clip, a breathless caption, a question sharpened into an accusation.

Inside the green room, Solar breathes in, breathes out. "I'm okay," she says, and the girls let her say it, because they will not fight her while she's still holding herself together. 

Yunjin takes the cool pack and replaces it with her hand. "You're here," she corrects gently. "That's enough."

At the wing, Jungwon stares at the door she's behind and doesn't move. He plans without admitting he's planning. He names the shape of the wrongness and stores it under his ribs with the word he can't stop hearing from her mouth.

Trying.

Somewhere down the hall, out of sight, Danielle's screen is already black. Not posted. Not yet. Just saved, labeled, and waiting. The cameras are off. The story isn't.










55: Clip, Caption, Consequence

The kitchen was quiet except for the low hum of the fridge and the faint hiss of water boiling on the stove. Solar sat at the end of the counter, scrolling on her phone, her thumb frozen above the glowing screen.

HYBE’s statement was already everywhere.

“Regarding the incident during Le Sserafim’s Unforgiven recording: Solar is fine. The company is conducting a thorough check of all equipment and taking additional safety measures. Fans should not worry.”

The words looked sterile even in bold font, flattened into corporate reassurance. No mention of how close it had been, of the way the prop had groaned above her head like thunder before breaking loose. No mention of the girls screaming her name, of Chaewon’s hands on her shoulders, of Yunjin’s voice cracking with panic. Just: fine.

Chaewon leaned over her own phone at the table, her braid slipping over one shoulder. She gave a sharp exhale through her nose. “Better than silence, I guess.”

Across from her, Eunchae hunched in her chair, clutching a mug with both hands. “People already clipped it, though. It’s all over TikTok.”

Solar winced, setting her phone down face-first on the counter. “Of course it is.”

Yunjin, leaning against the fridge with her arms crossed, narrowed her eyes at Solar. “Don’t pretend it didn’t shake you. You almost got taken out by a piece of stage equipment.”

Solar met her gaze evenly, even if her insides were still humming from the memory. “It won’t happen again.”

Kazuha, always the quiet one, tilted her head. “You don’t know that, Sol.”

Silence hung for a beat, until Sakura spoke up from the stove where she was making tea. Her voice was soft but cutting: “We all know how it got out.”

The room seemed to shrink. Solar felt her jaw tighten, her pulse tick against her throat.

“…Danielle,” she admitted finally, the name tasting bitter in her mouth. “It has to be her.”

Chaewon sat back slowly, her lips pressing into a thin line. Yunjin straightened from the fridge like a spark had gone off in her chest.

“I swear,” Yunjin muttered, voice trembling with fury. “If I ever catch her anywhere near you again, Sol, I’ll—” She broke off, shaking her head, pacing a short, jagged line across the kitchen tiles.

“Yun,” Solar said softly, though her tone carried more weariness than warning.

“I mean it,” Yunjin snapped, wheeling back around. “This isn’t just rumors anymore. This isn’t some dumb tweet she could’ve sent on a burner. She planned this. She wanted people to see you weak. And that’s—” Her voice cracked again, softer now, more protective than angry. “That’s not okay.”

Solar rubbed her palms together slowly, grounding herself in the friction. “I know.” Her voice was steady, though her chest felt tight. “But it’s fine. I’m going to figure it out. She’s working with Jihoon—I know she is. And if that’s true, I can’t just throw punches. I have to be smart about this.”

Yunjin groaned in frustration, dragging both hands over her face. “Smart’s one thing, Sol, but you don’t have to do it alone.”

Solar didn’t answer right away. Instead, she stared at the black screen of her phone, her reflection faint in the glass. When she finally spoke, it was quieter, but with a steel edge that hadn’t been there before.

“I won’t let them win. Not this time.”

The girls fell silent, each one exchanging glances over her bowed head. The kettle clicked off, the sound almost too loud in the kitchen. Sakura poured hot water into mugs, setting one gently in front of Solar without a word.

Solar wrapped her hands around it, though she didn’t drink. Her eyes lingered on the steam curling upward, as if reading a message only she could see.



Solar sat cross-legged on her bed, the glow of her phone painting her face in a pale blue wash. The dorm was hushed, the kind of silence that followed long rehearsals and sore muscles. Eunchae’s laughter had faded behind her door, Sakura’s kettle had clicked off hours ago. Even the city outside seemed muffled, as though the world had drawn its curtains.

Her thumb scrolled.

The comments stacked like bricks, one after another:

“She’s not well enough for this comeback.”
“Look at how slow she reacted.”
“Unprofessional.”
“She’s bringing the group down.”

Some defended her, of course—fans begging others to stop spreading hate, reminding everyone of Solar’s hard work, her dedication. But the other words were louder in her head. The ones that framed her as fragile, as a liability. The ones Danielle wanted out there.

She swallowed hard and set the phone on her knee, fingers tightening until the plastic bent beneath her grip. Her chest ached—not from the faint echo of the stage mishap, not from the sore burn in her wrist, but from the invisible weight of being watched, judged, measured.

She startled when the door clicked open.

Yunjin leaned against the frame, hair loose around her shoulders, holding two mugs of tea. Her eyes narrowed just slightly at the sight of the phone in Solar’s hand. “You’re still reading those?”

Solar forced a smile, quick and small, and slipped the phone face-down onto the blanket. “Just checking the schedule for tomorrow.”

Yunjin didn’t argue, but she didn’t believe it either. She set a mug on Solar’s nightstand and gave her a long look before leaving, the silence between them heavy with unspoken worry.

When the door clicked shut again, Solar pressed her palms to her eyes. The comments were still burned into the inside of her skull, looping like the hook of a song she couldn’t forget.

Across the city, the dorm lights of New Jeans glowed soft against the night. Minji sat curled on her bed with her laptop closed at her side, her phone bright in her hands. The video of Solar collapsing beneath the falling prop had already replayed too many times on her feed, each angle stitched with captions, hashtags, commentary that twisted her stomach tighter with every scroll.

She remembered the cracked door. Danielle’s hunched figure. The blurry zoomed-in photo of Solar and Jungwon, her expression unreadable. The realization that Danielle wasn’t just jealous—she was calculating.

Now the guilt wouldn’t let her breathe.

Minji opened a message window with Solar’s name. Her thumbs hovered. She typed fast, like she had to get it out before she lost her nerve:

Be careful. Danielle’s been—

Her pulse stuttered. She deleted the last word. Then the whole sentence.

The cursor blinked back at her, merciless.

She tried again: I should have said something sooner. I’m sorry.

Delete.

Her chest ached as she backspaced the last letter, leaving the field blank. She stared at it until her vision blurred, then shut the phone off and buried it beneath her pillow.

But the silence felt heavier now. Not protective—dangerous.




HYBE’s rehearsal studio smelled faintly of resin and sweat, the floor gleaming under the harsh lights as if it had been polished just to mock the bruises it collected. The mirrors stretched endlessly along the wall, doubling every movement, every falter, every shadow of fatigue.

Solar stood at the center, water bottle dangling loosely from her fingers, hoodie tied at her waist. Her skin was pale, almost translucent under the fluorescents, but her eyes—sharp, lit with something that bordered on reckless determination—cut through the haze of exhaustion.

“Let’s run it again,” she said, voice steady despite the way her chest was still rising and falling too quickly.

Chaewon frowned, tablet tucked under her arm. “We’ve already done it four times. The accident—”

“The accident doesn’t change the fact that our comeback is days away,” Solar interrupted. She twisted the cap of her water bottle closed with more force than necessary, setting it on the floor with a hollow thud. “We can’t slow down now.”

Sakura exchanged a glance with Chaewon, then stepped forward, voice soft but firm. “Sol, maybe just once more tonight, and then we clean sections tomorrow. Your shoulder—”

“It’s fine.” Solar rolled it back in demonstration, hiding the faint wince that followed. “See? Fine.”

Eunchae shifted nervously on her feet, tugging at the hem of her oversized hoodie. Yunjin crossed her arms, leaning against the mirror as though trying to gauge just how much fight was really in Solar’s words. Kazuha, quiet as always, simply watched, her expression unreadable but her eyes tracking every tremor in Solar’s hands, every extra breath she took.

Chaewon exhaled through her nose, the sigh of a leader who knew a losing battle when she saw one. “Alright,” she said finally, tone clipped but not unkind. “Once. Full out.”

The track clicked alive.

Unforgiven blasted through the studio speakers, the opening riff bold and demanding. The bass rattled in Solar’s ribs, chasing away the memory of the prop crash that had nearly flattened her. She shoved it down, shoved everything down—Jihoon’s shadowed words, Danielle’s smirk behind the cameras, the comments online dissecting her body as though it belonged to them.

All that mattered now was the choreography.

One, two, three—pivot. She snapped her head with the beat, ponytail slicing the air. Her body remembered even when her lungs protested, even when her knees screamed with each impact. She spun into formation, landing an inch off but correcting before anyone else could see. The mirror didn’t lie, but she held its gaze like a dare: you will not show me breaking.

Beside her, Yunjin’s movements had a razor’s edge, her eyes flicking to Solar’s reflection every few beats. Behind, Kazuha’s lines were precise, unshaken, but her gaze sharpened whenever Solar faltered. Even Eunchae, usually the one to get swept in the music’s joy, danced like she was watching Solar out of her peripheral vision, every step half-anchored to her.

The unspoken agreement settled among them: if Solar refused to protect herself, they would.

Chaewon called counts louder than usual, voice pushing them through transitions. “Five, six, seven—hit! Again—breathe, keep breathing!” She wasn’t talking to the group. She was talking to Solar.

Solar heard it but didn’t let herself react. Her chest burned, vision tunneling at the edges, but she forced her body to obey. Each movement became both war and prayer—war against the ache, prayer that her strength wouldn’t abandon her before the final pose.

They hit the chorus. Solar’s hands slashed clean arcs through the air, shoulders squared, jaw tight. The mirrors blurred with sweat on her lashes, but she blinked it away, refusing to miss a count.

“Bridge!” Chaewon shouted.

Solar’s right foot landed a fraction late, ankle wobbling. Kazuha stepped slightly closer in formation, ready to catch if she dropped. She didn’t—she clenched every muscle and forced the balance back, her face betraying nothing.

The dance break crashed down.

Solar’s body screamed stop, but her mind shouted louder: prove it. Prove she wasn’t weak, wasn’t fragile, wasn’t someone who fainted in practice or stumbled under a falling prop. Prove she was still Solar, the girl who carried herself like fire and would burn her own lungs if it meant not dimming in front of the world.

By the time they struck the final pose, Solar’s chest was heaving, knees trembling like they’d forgotten how to hold her weight. Her smile was sharp, defiant, though it quivered at the edges.

The music cut. The silence afterward was deafening.

For a moment, no one moved. The sound of Solar’s ragged breathing filled the room, louder than the song had been.

Then Chaewon lowered her tablet slowly, her expression tight. “Good,” she said carefully. “It’s good.”

Sakura stepped forward, slipping an arm subtly under Solar’s as though to steady her. “That’s enough for tonight,” she murmured.

Solar shook her head, forcing her legs straight, forcing her voice level. “I told you. I’m fine. It won’t happen again.”

Yunjin’s eyes darkened, her lips pressing into a line. She didn’t argue—not here, not now—but the weight of her silence said everything.

Kazuha picked up Solar’s water bottle and held it out. Her gaze lingered, steady and unyielding, before she finally spoke in her calm, almost whispery tone. “We’ll keep watch. Always.”

Solar blinked at her. The words weren’t dramatic, weren’t even particularly loud. But they carried the kind of quiet vow that couldn’t be argued with.

She took the bottle, unscrewed the cap, and drank. The water was lukewarm, but it slid down her throat like absolution. In the mirror, six girls stood shoulder-to-shoulder, sweat dripping, breath uneven, eyes locked on their reflections. Not just a group. A pact.




The hallway outside the auxiliary studio was a half-lit vein running through the building, all polished concrete and soundproofed doors with red tally bulbs sleeping dark above their frames. Past eleven, HYBE went quiet in strange ways; rooms still breathed music behind sealed walls, but the corridors themselves felt emptied out, as if they’d been exhaled.

Danielle sat in the shadow of an emergency exit sign, back against a cool stretch of wall where the security camera’s eye didn’t quite reach. Her legs were folded loosely, ankles crossed, one sneaker worrying a scuff mark into the floor. The blue light of her phone lit her face like a small, private stage.

The clip played again without sound—the arc of rigging dipping, the flash of Solar’s hair as she turned too late, the small, graceless collapse. The frame froze on the fall. Danielle pinched to zoom, but she didn’t need to. The moment had already burned into her. She flicked out to the post.

View count climbing. Comments stacking like teeth.

Is she okay??

This is why you rest idols fr.

She didn’t even move—the reflexes are gone.

A leader should know when to step back.

Underneath, the alt accounts added their push: not obvious, just… consistent. A phrase repeated across different handles, soft enough to pass as concern, rehearsed enough to land.

Her thumb slid into her Notes app. Half a dozen captions sat as drafts, some neutral, some sharper. She skimmed the one she’d scheduled for the morning—Sometimes the hardest thing is admitting you’re not ready yet. That one would pull the sympathetic comments into agreement, make the concern sound like loyalty.

Her mouth tipped, amused. Subtle always traveled farther than a scream.

The side door whispered open. Footsteps—too deliberate to be a random staffer, too heavy to be one of the kids coming back for a forgotten water bottle. Danielle didn’t look up until the shadow stopped at her left, a shape blocking the spill of light from the corridor corner.

Jihoon.

He didn’t speak. He took her in like a problem on a whiteboard—line by line, angle by angle—then closed the door behind him with a careful hand. Up close, he carried the smell of cold air and the edge of something metallic, like a key gripped too tight.

“That,” he said finally, voice so flat it made the word colder, “was not the deal.”

Danielle kept her eyes on the screen, let the clip loop once more. “Hello to you too.”

He didn’t bite. He stepped closer, the heel of his shoe scraping a line across the concrete. “We said optics. Pressure. You push her to make a mistake,” he said, measured, as if reciting notes from a meeting neither of them had signed their names to. “You do not put her under a drop.”

“She’s rattled,” Danielle replied, thumb flicking the refresh. The numbers ticked up. “That’s the point.”

Silence stretched just long enough for the hum of the vending machine down the hall to register. Then Jihoon’s hand hit the wall above her shoulder with a dull, controlled thud. Not a slam—but close. The sound startled the quiet; an exit sign buzzed in protest.

“Not like this,” he said, voice sharpening. “You put her under that thing. What if—” He stopped, jaw clamping as if the rest of the sentence were a taste he refused to admit to.

Danielle finally looked at him. The overhead emergency light carved his cheekbones into planes, lit the hard seam of his mouth. His expression was clean and ugly at once—anger hewn into something almost handsome if you didn’t know what sat behind it.

She let the smallest smile pull at her lip. “You still care? After everything?” Her tone went light, almost sing-song. “You’re weaker than I thought.”

The air between them cooled a few degrees. Jihoon’s gaze dropped to her hands. Her phone lay face-up on her knee, the paused frame of Solar’s fall gleaming with the kind of clarity that only a stabilized edit could catch. His eyes tracked the screen, then slid to the open Notes app peeking from the task switcher. Captions. Times. Handles she shouldn’t have known.

He reached down, not for the phone, but for her wrist. His fingers closed too tight, skin whitening beneath the pressure, a precise circle of control. Danielle didn’t flinch, though the bite of it flickered across her eyes.

“Don’t mistake me,” he said, leaning in until she could see the thread of a scar near his hairline she’d never noticed before. “I don’t care if she cries herself to sleep. But if you ever make me look like I’m part of killing her—” The last two words dragged low, like gravel. He glanced at the door, at the small blind corner of ceiling, calculating cameras without moving his head. When he looked back, his voice dropped to a whisper. “I’ll make sure you regret it.”

The grip tightened a fraction more—enough to promise bruises if it lasted. It didn’t. He released her in a clean motion, stepping back the exact half step that put his shoulder out of reach of anything she might throw.

Danielle shook out her hand slowly, letting the blood prickle back to the skin. Red marks were already surfacing where his fingers had sat. She studied them, unimpressed. “Relax,” she said, switching to a tone she might use to soothe a child. “No one died. And the safety officer signed off on everything before call time, remember? That’s on them.”

“That’s on you,” he snapped, losing a piece of the smoothness he wore like a suit. “Don’t confuse signed forms with innocence.”

She tilted her head. “Big word.”

His mouth flattened. “Don’t play clever here. You like to stir the water because you think you can see the bottom better than anyone else.” He glanced at the phone again, at the drafts list, the scheduling app open behind it like an exposed wire. “You don’t understand what happens when you make people believe they’re watching a drowning.”

“What happens,” she said, letting her thumb slide over the post again so the view count clicked higher, “is they keep watching.”

For a heartbeat, he just looked at her. Noticed the calculation that sat under her calm, the way she arranged cause and effect like furniture. Something sour moved under his expression, something like disdain or the kind of jealousy that didn’t look like romance so much as hunger for control.

“This isn’t about you winning,” he said. “This is about me not being tied to a body on a floor.”

“And yet,” she replied, eyes bright, “here you are.”

His laugh had no humor in it. “You really think I’m scared of you?”

“No,” she said. “I think you’re scared of her.” She nodded toward the frozen frame of Solar on her fall. “Or of what she does to you when you can’t decide what she does.”

His hand twitched. He didn’t hit the wall this time. He flattened his palm against it, as if pressing down a tremor. “You underestimate what control looks like.”

Danielle’s gaze flicked to the faint camera dome at the far end of the hall, then back. “I don’t underestimate anything. I measure.” She tapped the phone. “Watch the phrasing. See how they started with is she okay and now they’re at not fit for comeback? Give it two hours and we’ll have a thinkpiece about overwork. Tomorrow we float one about ‘responsible leadership.’” She shrugged, conversational. “You said humiliation. I’m delivering it in a form that sticks.”

“Humiliation,” he repeated. “Not hazard.” His jaw moved once, a slow grind. “Whatever you did to get that clip into circulation, keep it clean. No fingerprints. If a tech says manual adjustment anywhere on a report—if a rigger mentions a rebalanced line—”

She cut him off, smile thinning. “I’m not an idiot. And I’m not a tech.” Her tone cooled in a way that said do not ask me who is. “You wanted distance. I built it. Your name isn’t on anything.”

He studied her, looking for the seam, trying to decide whether the calm had seams at all. After a long beat, he stepped away from the wall. The room expanded by inches with the space he gave back.

“Good,” he said, the word shaped like a verdict. “Keep it that way.” He paused, angling his body toward the door, but not leaving yet. “And watch your mouth. You’re loud when you think you’re clever.”

Danielle let out a small breath that might have been a laugh. “And you’re sloppy when you think you’re calm.”

The look he gave her was cutting enough to leave frost on the floor. He opened the door a crack, listened for the empty hall, then slipped out with the careful ease of someone who had spent too much time memorizing exits.

The door sealed behind him. The hallway sighed back into quiet.

For a few seconds, Danielle didn’t move. The marks on her wrist pulsed dull heat; she pressed the knit cuff of her sweatshirt over them until the sting settled. When she lifted the fabric, the circles were darker. She put the wrist under the cold metal of the door handle briefly, let the chill erase the outline of his hand.

Her phone vibrated against her knee. New comments. A fan account with a hundred thousand followers had reposted the clip with a caption about “safety first.” The language was exactly what she’d predicted—kind, then not. The thread beneath it was already leaning toward consensus. Not a pile-on. Worse. A drift.

Danielle’s fingers moved, almost idly. She nudged two drafts from later to sooner. Shifted a caption word—tired to overextended, an adjective that sounded like empathy and landed like a diagnosis. Scheduled a soft push for 6:42 a.m., the hour when people commuted and scrolled and believed what felt like public knowledge because it echoed what they’d seen before breakfast.

A sound filtered in from somewhere above—the faint thud of bass, a count called in a leader’s voice. Someone was still running a section in the late-night studio. Danielle imagined the mirror fogging, the way dancers looked at themselves when no one else was supposed to be watching.

She slid to her photos. The zoomed image—the one she’d taken through a cracked door days ago—sat there, blurred and too close, Jungwon’s shoulder just out of frame, Solar’s face angled somewhere between tired and lit from within. Danielle looked at it without blinking. Then she backed out, locked the phone, and set it screen-down on her knee.

Her hand trembled once. Barely.

She stared at the door Jihoon had gone through, replaying the way his voice had broken on what if. It hadn’t been love. She knew what love sounded like. This had sounded like fear of losing control of a story he thought he was writing.

Danielle pressed her thumb to the power button and brought the screen back to life. The scheduled posts glowed in a neat list. The morning would come no matter who slept.

She stood, rolling her sleeve down carefully to hide the red marks, and slipped into the corridor with the posture of someone taking a casual walk. The camera at the far end caught her for a second—just a figure under cold lights with her hands in her pockets and her head tipped like she was listening to the building breathe.

Her smile, when it arrived, was small and private. Not triumph. Something quieter. A rehearsal of the next move.

Behind her, the door’s latch settled. Ahead, the hallway split into two clean lines—one toward the elevators, one toward the stairwell. Danielle chose the stairs. The phone buzzed again in her pocket. She didn’t check it until she’d turned the corner and vanished from the camera’s eye.




The dorm was quiet except for the hum of the fridge in the corner of the kitchen and the faint rise and fall of Hanni’s breathing from the next room. Minji sat curled on her bed, knees drawn up, the glow of her phone screen throwing pale light across her face. Her eyes stung, though she couldn’t tell if it was from exhaustion or the endless cycle of videos she couldn’t stop replaying.

The clip was everywhere now. Solar’s collapse looped on fan accounts, gossip pages, even news feeds that pretended neutrality but made sure to mention the words unprofessional and fragile. Each replay hit Minji’s chest like a dull hammer. The sight of Solar crumpling wasn’t just an accident anymore—it had become proof to strangers that she was weak, fuel for antis who sharpened their words in the comments.

Her thumb scrolled lower.

She’s not fit for this comeback.
Look at her—too slow to react.
Other idols push through worse. Maybe she doesn’t care anymore.

Minji bit her lip hard, forcing down the burn that rose at the back of her throat. She remembered that night in the practice building, Danielle staying behind when everyone else had left. She remembered the glimpse through a cracked door: Danielle bent over her phone, zooming in too far on a photo of Solar and Jungwon, the expression on her face all wrong. Not jealous. Not even curious. Just unreadable in a way that set Minji’s skin crawling.

She had known. She’d known something was brewing, and she had said nothing. Watching Solar fold to the ground under the falling rig, Minji’s silence had calcified into guilt. Now it sat in her stomach like stone.

She opened her chat with Solar. The cursor blinked in the empty field. For a long time, her fingers hovered without moving. What could she say? That she should’ve spoken up sooner? That Danielle was spiraling into something darker? That she still wasn’t sure what scared her more—Danielle’s games or her own feelings for the girl caught in the middle of them?

Finally, she typed.

I saw what happened. I should’ve warned you sooner. Danielle’s been acting strange. Please… just be careful. Don’t trust her.

Her thumb lingered over the send button, her pulse racing in her ears. Then, before she could talk herself out of it, she pressed down. The message flew into the thread, sitting there in its gray bubble like a confession she couldn’t take back.

Minji dropped the phone into her lap and pressed her palms together, squeezing until her knuckles ached. Her breath came shallow, uneven. She closed her eyes and whispered into the dark, barely audible: “I should’ve said it earlier.”

It wasn’t enough. It wouldn’t stop Danielle. But it was something. A line drawn, however thin. And it meant Solar wasn’t alone, even if Minji was the last one to say so.




The HYBE corridor was colder than the studio, air conditioners humming against the late-night heat that clung to the city outside. Jungwon walked with the rest of his group, their laughter echoing faintly behind him, but his mind was elsewhere. The image had replayed in his head all day—the scaffolding tilting, Solar’s body folding in on itself like she’d been hollowed out, the way the others had rushed to her while he’d stood chained backstage, Jake’s grip keeping him in place.

He told himself to look forward, to keep his pace steady. But then she appeared, coming out of a side rehearsal room, water bottle in hand. Her hair was damp against her temple, skin pale under the fluorescent lights. She didn’t notice him at first, too busy adjusting her hoodie sleeve over her wrist.

When her eyes finally lifted, they met his.

For a second, the world narrowed into just that line of sight. His gaze slid down almost against his will, catching on the faint discoloration near her shoulder—barely visible, but there. The ghost of a bruise where the prop had kissed too close. It was small. To anyone else, invisible. To him, it may as well have been a siren.

Jungwon’s jaw tightened. His hands flexed once at his sides, fingers curling into fists before he forced them loose. He didn’t stop walking. He didn’t speak. The others were still moving, and breaking formation now would mean questions he couldn’t answer.

But inside, something solidified. A quiet vow. If Danielle or Jihoon thought this was how they’d rattle Solar—by bruising her, breaking her body just enough to plant doubt—they were wrong. He would act. Not tonight. Maybe not tomorrow. But soon.

He passed her without a word. Still, she felt his stare linger long after he was gone. It burned into her skin, unsettling, steadying. A reminder that someone was watching. That someone knew.






















56: Splinters at Midnight

The dorm kitchen wore the soft chaos of a morning that started too early: chopsticks resting in a mismatched cup, a rice cooker blinking warm, a half-peeled tangerine abandoned beside the sink. Steam curled from the kettle like something slow and thoughtful. The air held the faint sweetness of toast and the sharper edge of instant coffee that someone had made too strong.

Solar eased onto the end of the bench and set both elbows on the table, palms cupped around nothing. The motion made the tendons along her right wrist pinch, a clean, familiar sting. She kept her face easy as she flexed her fingers under the table, rolling her thumb across the sore spot until the ache spread thin. The headache behind her eyes did what headaches do—sat there and insisted on being part of everything.

Chaewon was already dressed like she had three meetings before noon, braid neat, hoodie zipped, tablet propped against a mug. She scrolled with her thumb, eyes steady, not skimming so much as absorbing. Sakura moved through the kitchen like a set piece in a well-rehearsed play: kettle, mugs, teabags, the quiet competence of someone who believed in warm water as medicine. Yunjin stood near the open fridge, hair rumpled, assessing a container that might have been last night’s soup or last week’s mistake. Kazuha stretched an ankle on the cabinet toe-kick, posture long even in a dorm kitchen, and Eunchae perched on a stool with a bowl of cereal, chin in her palm, spoon idle.

No one said accident. No one said prop or leak or trend. The words hummed at the edges anyway, like the refrigerator motor, like the building’s breath.

“Eat,” Chaewon said at last, not quite looking up from the tablet. It wasn’t an order so much as a reminder of gravity.

Solar reached for the slice of toast Sakura slid in front of her. The bread was still warm, the butter soft. She took a careful bite. It tasted like nothing and like every morning she’d ever rehearsed through. She chewed, swallowed, and felt the food sit heavy, as if her stomach were skeptical.

Eunchae leaned over the table, hair slipping from her messy bun. “Unnie, I saw some comments—like, nice ones, actually—people saying it wasn’t your fault and the rigging was… you know.” She trailed off and stirred her cereal so aggressively a cornflake escaped to the table.

“People also said I’m unprofessional and slow,” Solar said lightly, as if reading the weather. She reached for the toast again, careful to keep the movement smooth. “Internet’s bilingual in praise and pitchforks.”

Yunjin closed the fridge, abandoned the soup container to whatever fate awaited it, and grabbed two mugs from the rack. “You’re pale,” she said simply, leaning a hip against the counter as if that would make the observation land softer.

“Harsh morning lighting,” Solar said. “It hates me personally.”

“Drink,” Sakura murmured, setting a mug in front of her. The steam carried peach, unexpected and sweet. “Not coffee,” Sakura added, anticipating the argument. “Tea. Your favorite.”

Solar let her hands wrap the mug. The heat felt like permission. “Peach tea fixes everything,” she said, aiming for flippant and hitting close enough to count.

Yunjin’s eyebrow went up, unconvinced but amused despite herself. “Put it on a billboard.”

Kazuha tipped her head. “Do we… tell staff about your wrist?” Her voice was soft, the question curled in cotton. “Just so they know to watch out for lifts today.”

Solar rotated the joint once, an easy circle. The pinch obeyed, doing exactly what it always did. “It’s fine. Tape and warmup. You know I’m a consummate professional,” she added with a smile that landed somewhere between joke and plea.

Chaewon finally lifted her eyes from the tablet and looked at Solar directly. It wasn’t the hard look of a leader pushing a point; it was the careful one, the kind that measured not just words but the space between them. “Schedule is tight,” she said, neutral. “MV blocking this afternoon. Camera rehearsal tomorrow. If anything feels off, we adjust. We don’t grind bone for pride.”

“Copy,” Solar said, light as breath.

Eunchae slid her bowl closer to Solar, the cereal now mostly soggy milk. “Do you want a bite?” she asked earnestly, then added, “I read somewhere that sugar helps headaches.” She glanced around like she might be arrested for pseudoscience.

Solar laughed, real enough that it surprised her. “I’ll pass, little menace.” She nudged Eunchae’s shoulder with her own. The touch was brief but it steadied something.

Sakura set another mug down, this one in front of Yunjin, and began quietly slicing a tangerine into tidy crescents. The knife clicked against the cutting board, a small rhythmic sound that filled the room without asking for notice. Kazuha peeled a banana with the seriousness of a surgeon and divided it into even coins, pushing half the plate toward Solar without comment. It felt like choreography they hadn’t needed to rehearse.

Chaewon locked the tablet and slid it aside. “HYBE wants us to stay off socials today,” she said. “Not because they think we can’t handle it,” she added quickly, preempting Yunjin’s eye roll, “but because it keeps the story small if we keep it small.”

“So we pretend the internet is a neighbor we can ignore and not a stadium we live in,” Yunjin said dryly, but she took her mug and sipped anyway, grimacing at the heat.

“Today is blocking and spacing,” Sakura said, stepping in where Chaewon left space. “We can practice precision without pushing heart rate. It will still count.”

Solar turned the mug between her palms, watching the tea’s surface quiver with each rotation. “We don’t have time for gentle,” she said, not challenging the plan so much as letting the sentence sit with the rest of them. “The accident doesn’t change the deadline.”

“Neither does pretending you’re made of steel,” Chaewon said, and the words were careful enough to land without bruise. “We can be fast and smart at the same time.”

Eunchae sank her spoon again, indecisive. “I can run point on water and towels,” she offered, eager to be useful, eager to help in a way that didn’t require permission.

“You’re on hydration,” Yunjin agreed immediately, relieved to grant an action. “Chief Water Officer. CWO.”

Eunchae lit up at the title. “I’ll make badges,” she said, then frowned at her cereal like it had wronged her. “Later.”

Kazuha watched Solar’s hands—always the hands, always the giveaway—and then looked up with a small, decisive nod. “We’ll keep eyes today,” she said. The sentence was simple, but it carried the weight of agreement: an unflashy vow.

Solar felt it land. The part of her that bristled at being watched tugged against the part of her that had curled on a studio floor and listened to the building groan over her head. She swallowed, and the peach tea went down sweeter than she expected.

“Last night,” Yunjin said, setting her mug down with a soft ceramic click, “Jake texted me. Said he heard we were still recording after.” She rolled one shoulder. “They were passing by. He—” She stopped herself, considered, and edited. “They were worried.”

Solar hid her startle in another sip. It wasn’t the first time the worlds overlapped, but every overlap felt like a lever she hadn’t meant to pull. “We finished the run,” she said. “We’re fine.”

“Fine,” Yunjin repeated, like she was trying on a coat to see if it fit. “Your favorite word.”

“It’s efficient,” Solar said. “Saves time.”

Chaewon’s phone vibrated on the table. She glanced at the screen, thumbed a quick reply, then looked up. “Manager wants us in the van in forty. Stretch here, then we go.” She paused, softer. “Do you want me to call for wraps? For your wrist?”

Solar looked down at her hand. The skin over the joint was unremarkable, a small landscape that had learned to lie. “I’ve got tape in my bag,” she said. “I’ll do it.”

Sakura slid the tangerine crescents onto a plate and pushed it into the middle of the table like an offering. “One,” she said, half-smiling at Solar. “For luck.”

Solar obediently took a slice. The burst of citrus cut clean through the morning. “Lucky~,” she agreed, and the table let the joke stand.

Eunchae hopped off her stool and went in search of badges, or at least a marker and a piece of flimsy cardboard. Kazuha rinsed her plate and stacked it neatly on the drying rack. Yunjin fished in the junk drawer until she found the good athletic tape, the one everyone hid from trainees who went through a roll a day. She tore off a foot, then another, and set both strips in front of Solar without ceremony.

“Wrap here and here,” Yunjin said, tapping the spots like a coach. “Not tight enough to numb, tight enough to remind.”

Solar’s mouth tugged. “When did you get your PhD in wrists?”

“Minor in ankles,” Yunjin said, deadpan. “Masters in keeping you from doing dumb things.”

Chaewon pushed back from the table and stretched her arms overhead until her shoulders clicked. “Okay,” she said, leader again. “We work the plan. We’re sharper than yesterday and kinder than pride. Everyone pack a spare hoodie—studio AC is rude.”

Sakura laughed softly. “Rude,” she echoed, like she was pocketing the word for later use.

They broke apart into the small business of leaving: mugs rinsed, chargers untangled, hair elastics found on doorknobs and wrists and the floor. Solar stayed a moment longer at the table, winding the tape around her wrist with practiced efficiency. The adhesive tugged at tiny hairs, anchored the skin. She flexed once, twice. The joint felt caged and, somehow, safer.

Her phone lit on the table with a brief glow—a Discord icon, a ping she didn’t open yet. The warmth that flared at the corner of her mouth surprised her. Later, she told herself. She’d answer later.

“Coming?” Chaewon asked from the doorway, soft enough that it wasn’t an order.

Solar stood and slid her chair back into place. “Yeah.” She grabbed her bag—the little black notebook heavy in the front pocket somewhere under a roll of grip socks and a pack of electrolyte powder—and slung the strap over her good shoulder. “Let’s go be sharp.”

Yunjin fell into step beside her, bumping hips just enough to jostle, just enough to say I’m here. Kazuha locked the door behind them. Sakura pocketed the key. Eunchae trotted ahead to press the elevator button three times like it worked better that way.

In the mirrored elevator, six faces looked back: sleep-soft and determined, joking and not joking, ordinary and carrying a thousand tiny fractures you couldn’t see unless you knew where to look. Solar kept her eyes on her own reflection, on the way the tape peeked out from her sleeve. She lifted her chin.

“It won’t happen again,” she said quietly again for the 50th time, to no one and to herself, as the doors slid open.

No one argued. They didn’t need to. They stepped into the hall and toward the day like a single thought, peach tea sweetness still on Solar’s tongue, the ache in her wrist held firm under the tape, the world waiting to be outdanced.



The dorm had gone quiet by the time Solar collapsed onto the couch. The others had disappeared into their rooms one by one—Chaewon with her tablet, Yunjin muttering something about vocal warmdowns, Eunchae insisting she wasn’t tired before promptly dozing off mid-sentence. The apartment was dim now, lit only by the blue wash of the television’s standby light and the glow of her phone against her face.

Her thumb scrolled, flick after flick, past the same kind of comments that had already burrowed too deep into her chest.
“She’s not well enough for this comeback.”
“Did you see how slow she reacted?”
“Unprofessional. She’s dragging the group down.”

The words stacked like weights she refused to acknowledge, though her hand tightened on the phone until the veins rose at her wrist. She exhaled through her nose, forcing herself not to read the next thread.

A soft ping broke through. Discord.

She blinked, surprised, and swiped over.

Super: Hey, Seoul superstar, don’t tell me you retired already.

Her lips quirked, small but real. A beat later—

Nero: Off-season scrims are actually boring without you.

And then, inevitably—

Rascal: We’ll wait. Just don’t forget—you’re still Shock.

Solar’s throat tightened. For a moment she simply stared at the messages, letting the words unfurl across the bruised part of her that the comments had clawed at. They weren’t elaborate. They weren’t even serious, not really—just boys behind keyboards, killing time between matches. But to her, it felt like the softest anchor in the storm.

Her fingers hovered, then tapped out a reply:

Solar: Comeback first. Soon.

She stared at it, considered deleting, then pressed send.

The tiny green check appeared. A warm thrum spread in her chest, chasing out some of the chill that had settled there since the accident.

She set the phone down on the cushion beside her, tilting her head back against the couch. The dorm was still quiet. Outside, Seoul buzzed faintly, muffled by glass and distance. And somewhere on the other side of the world, the Shock boys were waiting—loud, messy, loyal in their own way.

Another family. One she hadn’t lost.




The rooftop stairwell door creaked open with its usual metallic groan, a sound Solar had come to rely on. The city spread below her in a restless grid of headlights and neon, the hum of Seoul wrapping around the building like a pulse she could almost steady her own breath to. She pulled the hood of her practice jacket tighter, even though the night air was mild. Her skin was still slick from rehearsal, every muscle humming with the stubborn, desperate energy she had poured into each count.

She wasn’t supposed to be here. Not on the roof, not at midnight, not with tomorrow promising the first official Unforgiven stage. But the walls downstairs felt suffocating, the dorm too small, her head too loud. So she had come up here to breathe. To remind herself that the world was bigger than mirrored rooms and cameras that caught every falter.

Her phone buzzed faintly in her pocket. She ignored it. Probably another round of fan discourse, dissecting her slow step, her stumble, her weakness. She didn’t need to see it. She already knew what they were saying.

A scuff of shoe against concrete cut through the hum of the city. Solar stiffened, head snapping toward the shadow near the stairwell door.

Jihoon stepped out, casual as though he had been waiting for her. His hands were in his pockets, posture loose, expression unreadable in the spill of a single rooftop light. But his voice—low, smooth, almost gentle—curled into the air with calculated ease.

“Still pushing yourself after what happened?”

The words were a dagger wrapped in velvet. A check-in that wasn’t a check-in. A tone so falsely casual it made her stomach twist.

Her chest tightened. She had promised herself she wouldn’t freeze if she ever saw him again, but the sight of him—familiar, sharper in the dark, eyes gleaming like they knew too much—always knocked her balance. She forced herself upright, forced her voice to steady.

“Seriously?” she snapped. “We both know you don’t care. Don’t act like you didn’t have anything to do with it.”

The accusation cut through the air between them, raw and jagged. It was the first time she’d said it outright. No sidestepping, no polite silence. Just the truth she had carried like lead in her throat.

Jihoon’s smirk faltered. Just for a second. Then it slid back into place, but colder, the curve of his mouth harder, crueler. He took a slow step closer, eyes narrowing.

“You don’t mean that,” he said quietly, each word deliberate. “Let’s not forget everything I’ve done for you.”

Another step.

Solar’s pulse climbed into her ears. He was closer now, the rooftop light catching the sharp lines of his face. She refused to back away. “You’ve done nothing but make me weaker,” she spat. “Stop pretending you’re the victim.”

The words hit. His jaw tightened. The air shifted. The mask of false calm fractured.

Jihoon moved. One second he was two feet away, the next his hand shot out, clamping around her wrist—the injured wrist. Pain sparked instantly, white and electric, bolting up her arm. She gasped despite herself, the sound tearing from her throat before she could swallow it.

His grip tightened, fingers digging into bone and tendon, squeezing until her knees nearly buckled. He leaned in, his chuckle low, humorless, poisonous.

“Typical,” he murmured, breath hot at her ear. “Still as weak as ever. You should be thanking me.”

Her head snapped back, eyes blazing even as her arm screamed. “Thanking you?” she hissed. “For what? For ruining my life?”

Jihoon’s lips curled, a dry smirk that didn’t reach his eyes. “For keeping you from worse. You don’t even see it, do you? Without me, she’d—” He cut himself off, but the meaning was clear. Danielle.

The bile rose in Solar’s throat. Her whole body shook with fury, but the pain in her wrist pinned her to the spot. Blood pricked where his nails scraped, a sting that sharpened with each pulse of her heart.

“Let go,” she whispered, voice low but steady despite the tremor in her frame. “Let. Go.”

For a moment, she thought he wouldn’t. His grip lingered, cruelly steady, savoring the control. Then something shifted in his eyes. He looked down. Saw the thin bead of blood seeping from the cut his grip had carved into her skin. His smirk flickered—gone, replaced with something sharp and unreadable. Not guilt. Just calculation.

Abruptly, he released her.

Solar stumbled back two steps, clutching her wrist against her chest. Her skin burned where he’d touched her, the cut bright and small but throbbing. She pressed her sleeve over it, breath ragged, eyes locked on him like he might lunge again.

Jihoon’s face hardened. His voice dropped to a whisper, razor-thin. “Don’t push me, Solar. You won’t like what happens if you do.”

Then he turned. His footsteps echoed down the stairwell, measured, unhurried, like he hadn’t just crossed a line that could never be uncrossed.

Solar stood frozen, her back against the cold rooftop railing. Her chest rose and fell in shallow bursts, each inhale scraping her throat raw. Slowly, she peeled her sleeve back from her wrist. The cut glistened in the light, not deep, not obvious, but enough. Enough to mark. Enough to remind.

Her stomach churned. The words clawed up her throat before she could stop them.

“Not again.”

They left her lips in a whisper, broken and aching.

She closed her eyes, pressing her sleeve back over the wound, curling inward around it as if she could protect herself from the echo of his grip. Below, the city buzzed on, uncaring, alive. Above, the night pressed heavy, suffocating.

Solar stayed there long after his footsteps faded, heart hammering, wrist throbbing, the weight of the moment sinking into her bones. She told herself she wouldn’t break. Not here. Not now. Not for him.

But the silence answered back with only the sound of her own trembling breath.
















57: Backstage Confrontation

Backstage smelled like hairspray and hot lights and the metallic tang of wires that had been warmed by too many bodies and too much electricity. The corridor outside the dressing rooms was a moving river of people—PDs with headsets pressed to their ears, stylists herding rolling racks like sheepdogs, staff in black passing with armloads of microphones and labeled in-ears. Everywhere, the soft thud of rubber soles on industrial carpet and the hiss of doors opening and closing, opening and closing.

Le Sserafim’s door bore a white paper placard with their name in sharp block letters, a strip of pink tape under it like a smile that hadn’t quite been committed to. Inside, the air ran cooler, the fluorescent light flatter, everything suddenly too close. Someone had draped a towel over the mirror by instinct; someone else had lined up water bottles in a neat soldierly row.

Solar sank onto the narrow bench and let the adrenaline taper off in painful little drops. Her shoulder thrummed in a way that felt both dull and focused, a reminder of the arch that had kissed her too intimately days ago. Under her jacket sleeve, the tape on her wrist itched. She thought of the thin stinging line beneath it—the cut small enough to excuse, to hide, to pretend away—and flexed her fingers just to make sure they still belonged to her.

“You okay?” Eunchae asked, crashing onto the bench next to her with the graceless grace of the youngest. She already had an ice pack balanced on one knee like an accessory.

Solar pulled a laugh from somewhere. “Define okay.”

“Breathing, upright, iconic,” Yunjin said, sweeping in with two bottles and a roll of athletic tape she’d liberated from somewhere. Her cheeks were still ruddy from stage, hairline damp, eyes bright with the residual high that only live performance could feed. She pressed one bottle into Solar’s palm. “Hydrate before Chaewon sees you not hydrating.”

“I saw,” Chaewon said from the counter, where she was simultaneously answering a manager’s text, smoothing Sakura’s flyaways with her fingers, and reviewing the set cue list like she could bend time with her concentration. She turned, appraised Solar with that leader’s x-ray that always managed to be both clinical and kind. “Breathe, drink, stretch your neck. Two minutes, then we have photos.”
