Kazuha was already doing quiet leg swings in the sliver of floor between the couch and the garment rack, composure unruffled, the curve of her mouth the same as it had been onstage when the camera had found her. “You did well,” she said, and she didn’t throw the compliment like a lifeline; she set it down like a fact to be picked up at leisure.

Solar took the bottle and let the cold sweat on the plastic bleed into her palm. She unscrewed the cap and drank in careful swallows, felt the water hit a stomach too tight with adrenaline and willpower. The room was a soft clamor: Sakura humming under her breath as she separated lashes with a spoolie, Eunchae muttering something about a live-stay selfie, Yunjin telling some exaggerated version of the crowd’s scream to make Eunchae giggle.

The mirror didn’t get her today. She let herself look. Pale, yes. But alive in that crackling way live performance makes you—the eyes a little too bright, the smile too sharp for comfort. The shoulder that hurt didn’t betray her in the glass. The wrist didn’t either; the tape did its job, a clean medical white peeking from her cuff like the border of a secret.

Chaewon finished her text, pocketed her phone, and crossed the room. “Staff says we might have to do a quick pick-up of the bridge for the broadcast cut,” she said. “Camera two shadowed it.”

“We can do it,” Sakura said immediately, unbothered. She adjusted Solar’s mic wire with two deft fingers. “But after ice. And shoes off for five minutes. Ankles will thank us.”

“I’ll thank us,” Yunjin said, already unzipping her boot. “My left foot filed a complaint during the second chorus.”

Eunchae leaned in, stage whisper loud. “It was resolved on the third.”

Laughter rose and fell, easy as breath.

Solar pulled her sleeve down to be sure the tape didn’t show, then slid her hand into her pocket so she wouldn’t keep checking it. The notebook sat in her bag under the bench, squared against the canvas, a small gravity she felt even when she wasn’t touching it. Tired, she’d written. Trying. The words had felt heavy enough on the page. On stage, she’d tried to make them look light.

A staffer knocked twice and poked her head in. “Group photo in the hall in five,” she chirped, then spotted Solar and softened. “You were great. Really. The cameras loved you.”

“Thank you,” Solar said, automatic. She used the motion of standing to test her shoulder. It complained. Not loudly enough to be an excuse.

They lined up in the hall for the quick shot, the wall behind them a beige no-place that would be cropped out later. The flash popped; they did one serious, one silly, one where Eunchae did something suspicious with her hands that would become a meme for twelve hours and then vanish. Fans’ voices lapped faintly from beyond, a sound like weather you couldn’t quite influence.

“Back in,” Chaewon said, shepherding with a light hand, no fuss. “We’ll keep hair as is for the pick-up; everyone retouch lips. Solar—ice. That’s not a suggestion.”

Solar accepted the ice pack from Sakura without comment and pressed it to her shoulder, the cold a shock that shook a small gasp out of her. Yunjin watched her for two beats too long, then pretended to be mesmerized by a very boring label on the hairspray can.

“You held the last note like you were mad at it,” Yunjin said, casual.

“I was,” Solar said. The honesty surprised her, and Yunjin glanced up, amused and fond at once.

Another knock. This time, it was a staffer for a different group altogether, bowing and apologizing and asking if anyone had a spare strip of eyelash glue. Sakura produced one from thin air, and the girl bowed again and again, grateful. The door breathed in the sound of the corridor with each open, each close—other lives, other sets, a whole building of heartbeats doing their jobs.

Solar’s phone buzzed against the bench wood. She pulled it out and glanced at the screen. A Discord ping from an hour ago she hadn’t seen; the same thread from last night. Rascal: Proud of you, kid. Don’t forget to eat. A second later, Super: Clip looked sick. We’d boo the camera two guy for you if we could. Then Nero: When you’re back, we’re running Soldier on Ilios. Don’t argue. She smiled without trying to.

“You can smile, you know,” Yunjin said, catching it and pretending she hadn’t. “Won’t kill the brand.”

“Terrible for our mystique,” Solar said, and tucked the phone away like you tuck away a charm in a pocket you can reach without looking.

They were good together, the six of them. It wasn’t always perfect—what family is?—but it worked: the balance of pressure and care, the way one person’s sharpness smoothed another’s, the way worry turned into action instead of accusation. Solar loved them for it. She also knew how to keep parts of herself behind a shut door even when she was sitting beside them.

A runner popped in as if on elastic. “Pick-up in studio B in three! Just bridge to chorus, one camera. Quick-quick!”

“Shoes,” Chaewon said, and there they went again, reaching for boots and heels, zippers rasping, laces tugged tight. Sakura checked everyone’s mic packs by touch like a mother bird checking feathers. Kazuha caught Solar’s eye and lifted her brows: You good? Solar gave the smallest nod: Good enough.

They filed into the hall and moved with the current, bodies arranged into tidy lines by habit. The studio doors swallowed sound—the thump of bass from one room, a singer warming up scales from another, a PD counting down from five. Outside their assigned studio, they waited while a crew shifted a light. The corridor air was cooler here, or maybe it was just thinner.

“Ready,” Chaewon said, without looking around. It wasn’t a question. It didn’t have to be.

They did the thing—the thing their bodies knew when their minds were too loud—the split-second quiet, the click into place, the shift from girls in a hallway to performers in a frame. The music cue burred through the in-ears, they hit what they needed to hit, and then they were a line of breath again, released with the PD’s thumb-up.

“Perfect, thank you,” someone said, disembodied, from the monitor cluster. “Good to go. Congratulations.”

Back in the corridor, it was busier. Another group passed by, glittering and polite, noses powdered, hair sprayed into submission. Staff with clipboards, someone laughing too loudly, the stomp of a rolling case that needed new wheels.

Solar felt the world tilt a quarter-degree. Too many lights, too many voices. She put her fingers lightly on her taped wrist without thinking—just a touch—and then dropped her hand when she realized Yunjin had seen. Yunjin pretended she hadn’t.

“Two minutes,” Chaewon told the runner. “Then we’ll vacate.”

“Manager says van in fifteen,” the runner reported, and then was gone, sound swallowed by sound.

“That gives you exactly ninety seconds to sit before we pack up,” Sakura said, firm in the way only softness can be. She hooked a finger in Solar’s sleeve and tugged her toward a quieter side hallway, the kind that existed only to connect two more important places and so stayed empty by accident. “Here. Just breathe.”

Solar let herself be maneuvered into the margins. The hum of backstage faded a notch. Down the length of the hall, a door stood ajar, letting a slice of another world in: a monitor showing a replay, a stylist laughing, a PD counting on fingers.

Kazuha leaned against the wall opposite her, ankles crossed, eyes half-lidded but very much awake. Eunchae had found a plastic chair somehow and was perched on it backward, chin on the backrest, talk-typing an imaginary comment thread to herself. Yunjin hovered only long enough to confirm Solar had actually sat, then peeled off to fetch their garment bags before some other group made off with them by accident.

Solar breathed. In. Out. In, counting to four. Out, counting longer because longer made her heart slow. The wrist throbbed under the tape in a way that wanted attention. She didn’t give it any more than this.

Across the hall, a cluster of black-clad staff buzzed around a door with ENHYPEN printed on the placard. The door opened; laughter spilled out, a different timbre than their own, vowels stretched by different accents, a rhythm she’d begun to recognize because the building’s arteries overlapped whether anyone liked it or not.

One of the members—the tall one with iceberg calm, Heeseung—stepped out first, talking to a PD with a tablet. Jake followed, still unwrapping some snack he’d smuggled from nowhere. Sunoo, gleaming and chatty, gestured with a hand towel like it was a magic trick. And then—

Jungwon.

He had that post-stage look she knew in herself—eyes too bright, jaw set because adrenaline hasn’t learned to leave quietly. His hair was damp, pushed off his forehead, his jacket thrown on in a hurry to appease someone who wanted idols to look finished even off camera. His gaze moved like someone scanning a battlefield for hazards, or anchors, or both.

He didn’t see her at first. He said something to Jake; Jake laughed; Sunoo swatted at someone’s arm for a tease only they understood. Then Jungwon’s eyes lifted to the length of the hallway and landed on her.

Solar’s breath shortened before she could stop it. The corridor felt suddenly narrower. The hum of backstage dropped away as if someone had turned the volume knob with two careful fingers.

He paused, mid-step, like something had tugged invisibly at his shoulder. A man with a clipboard brushed by him, oblivious. He didn’t break eye contact.

Kazuha’s gaze flicked from Solar to the corridor and back. Sakura glanced over her shoulder, caught the line of it, and did the quietest thing she was best at: nothing. She gave Solar the space without making a show of giving space.

Solar set her water bottle on the low ledge by the wall because her hands had started to feel unreliable. She didn’t stand. She didn’t look away. The adrenaline from stage had mutated into something tighter and harder under her ribs.

Jungwon said something to Sunoo without looking away. Sunoo patted his shoulder, said something back, and swung around toward their room like this part of the path had always belonged to Jungwon alone. Jake hesitated half a second longer, then followed. The door eased shut behind them, leaving Jungwon in the middle of the hallway, the distance between him and Solar filling up with all the things neither of them had managed to say.

He took a step. Another.

Solar’s throat went dry. The tape on her wrist felt like a siren only she could hear.

He stopped a pace away, close enough that she could see the cut along his knuckle where a ring had scraped during some careless backstage moment, close enough to smell the faint sweetness of hairspray and the cleaner scent underneath it that was just him.

He opened his mouth, the muscles in his jaw working, and for a fraction of a second she saw it—the heat banked behind his composure, the way fury and fear can look like the same thing when you love someone you aren’t allowed to admit you love.

He hadn’t asked if she was okay.

He didn’t. Not yet.



The dressing room was quiet in the way empty rooms always were after a stage: perfume faint in the air, sequins catching dull light from the lone buzzing fixture overhead. Costumes sagged on their hangers, half-closed makeup palettes smeared the counters, and the mirror reflected only Solar, still seated where the others had left her.

She should have gone with them—smiling, joking, pretending like nothing was wrong. Instead she’d told the manager she needed a minute, her voice steady enough that they hadn’t argued. Now it was just her and the silence, and the ache in her body that no statement from HYBE could polish into reassurance.

Her gaze flicked to her bag where the black notebook sat buried between a towel and a crumpled water bottle. For a second she thought about pulling it out, opening to the words she’d dared to add, reminding herself she was still trying. But her hand stayed flat on the counter, nails pressing little half-moons into the wood.

The door opened without a knock. Solar looked up, heart snapping taut.

Jungwon slipped inside, closing the door behind him with a soft click. He didn’t speak right away, and he didn’t look like someone who had stumbled in by mistake. His dark hair still clung damp to his temples, his stage jacket half-off one shoulder. He was all sharp lines in the mirror reflection—jaw tight, eyes unreadable, body carrying the leftover pulse of his own performance.

“You shouldn’t be here,” Solar said finally, her voice softer than she’d meant.

He didn’t answer that. He stepped closer, gaze locked on her through the mirror. “Danielle and Jihoon. They’re the ones behind what happened. The stage, the rumors—everything.”

The words landed heavy. But Solar didn’t flinch, didn’t gasp. She only held his stare in the glass, her voice flat, steady: “I know, Won.”

The nickname froze him. His reflection faltered, as if the syllable had cut deeper than anything else she could have said. Slowly, he angled toward her, searching her face like he needed to confirm he hadn’t imagined it.

“You know?”

She turned at last, meeting him without the shield of the mirror. “Yeah. I know they’re working together. I know they’re behind it—all of it.”

Silence stretched, filled with the faint hum of the light overhead. His chest rose sharply once, then again, fists curling at his sides. “And you didn’t tell me?” His tone wasn’t cruel. It wasn’t even an accusation. It was raw, as if the words had been ripped out of something deeper.

Solar swallowed, her lips parting, but no answer came fast enough.

“I could’ve done something,” Jungwon said, his voice lower now, straining against itself. “I could’ve kept you from getting hurt.”

He was closer than she’d realized—standing just behind her chair, his reflection towering above hers in the mirror. The tension in his shoulders looked ready to break something, anyone.

Solar’s chest tightened. Without thinking, she lifted her hand, reaching up, her fingertips brushing against the heat of his cheek. The contact was light—barely there—but enough. His skin was warm, his jaw taut under her touch, the storm in him tethered for one suspended moment.

For her, the touch was selfish, grounding. Proof that he was real, here, not just another shadow orbiting the chaos of her life. For him, it was a reminder—maybe of what he was fighting against, maybe of what he couldn’t say aloud.

The air between them held. Her hand stayed at his cheek, his gaze locked on hers, the silence louder than any threat Jihoon or Danielle could have made.

For a long second, Solar thought she had managed to hide it. The hoodie sleeve had been her shield, the easy disguise she’d pulled over bruises and tremors before. But Jungwon’s eyes were too sharp, too exacting. They didn’t skim—they pierced.

His hand closed around her wrist, gentle but immovable. The kind of grip that told her he’d decided something, and no amount of twisting would make him let go.

“Don’t,” Solar hissed, low and desperate, yanking once. But he didn’t tighten—he simply held. It wasn’t force. It was refusal. His resolve pinned her in place more than his fingers did.

Slowly, the fabric rode up as he turned her wrist into the weak yellow glow of the mirror lights.

The mark was plain now, cruel in its simplicity. A raw slice across skin already mottled with fading bruises. The cut wasn’t deep, but it was fresh. The faint sheen of blood still clung to the edges, catching the light. On her—fragile wrist, already stiff from practice—it looked sharper, angrier.

Jungwon’s chest heaved once, a ragged inhale that sounded almost like disbelief. His eyes darkened. Not the kind of dark that shut things away, but the kind that brimmed with fury too heavy to contain.

“Don’t tell me this is from practice.” His voice was low, shaking in the space between restraint and explosion.

Solar’s lips parted. The words sat there, her instinct screaming to downplay, to dodge, to bury the truth under a practiced mask. “It’s fine—” she whispered, the syllables feather-thin, breaking as they left her.

“It’s not fine.” He cut across her with sudden force, though his voice never rose. It dropped lower instead, thick with self-loathing. “Every time I’m not there, they do this to you.”

The words landed like stones in her stomach. His grip hadn’t shifted, but she felt the trembling underneath—whether from his hand or her pulse, she couldn’t tell.

“Every damn time,” Jungwon repeated, quieter now, the fury bleeding into something heavier, like grief. His jaw clenched so tight it looked painful. His thumb pressed, accidentally, against the edge of her palm, and only then did she realize how careful he was being—even in his anger, he held her like she might shatter.

Solar’s throat worked around a knot. The old instinct—to brush it off, to twist it into a joke, to retreat behind a smile—faltered under the weight of his gaze. The heat of his protectiveness burned hotter than the sting in her wrist.

Her chest rose unevenly. She wanted to say something sharp, something that would remind him she didn’t need saving. But all she could feel was the unbearable closeness of him, the way his eyes had locked onto hers like the whole world could collapse around them and he wouldn’t move.

In that silence, his fury didn’t feel dangerous. It felt like a shield. And for the first time in longer than she cared to admit, Solar let herself imagine what it would be like if she actually leaned on it.

The silence pressed in, thick and fragile, like one wrong word might shatter it. Jungwon’s hand was still around her wrist, firm but no longer harsh, his thumb tracing the edge of the cut as if memorizing proof of what had been done to her. Solar’s breath hitched, her chest aching under the weight of the moment.

She should pull away. She should say something sharp, something to remind him that he wasn’t supposed to be this close, that his protectiveness wasn’t hers to lean on. But her body betrayed her—still, she stayed, suspended between the suffocation of his intensity and the unbearable safety it offered.

His jaw worked as though he was chewing back words that burned on his tongue. Finally, his voice broke the silence, low and raw. “I should’ve been there. I should’ve known, even if you didn’t tell me.” His gaze stayed fixed on her wrist, on the faint shimmer of blood. “I should’ve done something.”

The admission wasn’t sharp, but jagged with self-blame, cutting himself deeper with each word.

Solar frowned, the corner of her mouth trembling before she caught it. “Won,” she whispered, the nickname slipping out without permission. It startled her almost as much as it startled him, but she pressed on. “That’s not on you. It’s not like this is exactly your battle to fight.”

Her voice wavered, but her eyes searched his, trying to steady him with the truth she wanted to believe.

For the first time, he lifted his gaze from her wrist to her face. His eyes burned—not with rage aimed at her, but something fiercer, darker, protective down to the bone.
“It’s always been my battle, Sol,” he said, her name a vow in itself. “Since the day I saw you for the first time.”

Her breath caught. The words weren’t a confession, not exactly, but they struck her deeper than any declaration could have. Something in her chest twisted, fragile and electric all at once.

Jungwon bent his head, his hair shadowing his face as he leaned closer. Before she could draw back, before she could second-guess the fire crawling up her skin, he pressed his lips softly against the wound.

It wasn’t a kiss meant for romance. It was gentler, reverent, a promise carved into her skin like he was trying to erase the hurt with his mouth. Sparks flared up her arm, shooting into her chest until her ribs ached from how hard her heart pounded.

Her throat closed around a sound—half gasp, half plea—but nothing came out. She stared at him, frozen, every nerve caught between the pain in her wrist and the impossible tenderness of his touch.

When he pulled back, his lips brushed her skin one last time before lifting. His eyes locked on hers, dark and storming but softened at the edges. “Next time,” he whispered, his voice steady as steel, “I won’t stay back.”

Her lungs burned with the weight of the words. She clutched at her bag with her free hand, fingers brushing the spine of the notebook inside like it might anchor her. Because she knew—if Jungwon meant what he said, if he truly stepped forward next time—everything would change.












58: The Puzzle Pieces Form

The dorm was quiet in that stretched, muffled way it only got past midnight, when the city’s noise dulled into a hum outside the glass and the girls’ laughter had long since burned out. A kettle clicked in the kitchen; somewhere, Eunchae’s phone chimed before she silenced it with a quick, guilty tap. Solar padded out of the bathroom with damp hair curling against her cheeks, the sleeves of her oversized shirt tugged low over her hands.

She thought she was careful. She thought no one was looking.

But Yunjin was sprawled on the couch, half-watching some drama rerun on her tablet, and Eunchae was perched on the rug with a blanket around her shoulders like a cape. Both of their eyes flicked up when Solar crossed the room, and Yunjin’s sharp gaze caught the detail Solar had prayed would go unnoticed: the edge of white gauze peeking beneath her cuff when her sleeve slipped.

“Sol.” Yunjin’s voice wasn’t loud, but it was too steady, too deliberate. “What happened to your wrist?”

Solar froze for half a second, then tugged her sleeve down quickly, too quickly, her face arranging itself into practiced ease. “It’s nothing. Just… training strain. You know how it is.”

Eunchae frowned, her blanket sliding from her shoulders. “That didn’t look like strain.”

Solar gave a small laugh, airy, brittle. “Don’t be dramatic.” She moved toward her room, but Chaewon’s door cracked open before she could escape. Their leader leaned against the frame, hair mussed from bed but her eyes sharp, already tuned to the pitch of the room.

“Show us,” Chaewon said quietly.

Solar hesitated, one foot still angled toward her door. “Unnie, it’s late. I’m fine.”

“Show us.” Chaewon’s voice had no sharp edges, but it cut anyway.

The silence pressed down. Yunjin sat up straighter on the couch, Eunchae shifted nervously on the rug. Solar’s throat tightened, her body rebelling against the command even as her hand felt suddenly too heavy at her side.

Finally, with a sigh, she tugged the sleeve back.

The gauze was stained faint pink at the edge where the cut had bled through. Not catastrophic, not gory, but enough. Enough to draw the room into a single breathless silence.

Yunjin’s tablet slipped closed on her lap. Eunchae’s blanket slid fully to the floor. Chaewon’s jaw clenched once before she stepped forward, kneeling on the rug to take Solar’s wrist more gently than her words had been.

“This isn’t from practice,” Chaewon murmured, eyes scanning the cut with the precision of someone cataloging evidence.

Solar tugged lightly but Chaewon didn’t let go.

“Sol.” Yunjin’s voice trembled now, anger braided through worry. “This—this isn’t just you pushing too hard.”

Eunchae whispered, almost afraid of the answer: “Was it the stage accident?”

“No.” The word came out too fast. Too sharp. Solar bit her lip and tried to soften it. “It’s not like that. Just… dropped something. Clumsy.”

No one believed her.

Chaewon’s grip tightened by a fraction, not enough to hurt, but enough to anchor her there in the circle of their stares. “You’re lying.”

Solar’s stomach twisted. She wanted to laugh it off, to scatter their concern into something easier. But the air had turned heavy, their silence expectant.

Yunjin’s eyes narrowed. “The prop malfunction. Jihoon showing up again. Danielle’s leak. You think we haven’t noticed the timing?”

Eunchae nodded quickly, voice hushed. “They… they’re connected, aren’t they?”

Solar swallowed hard, the denial caught in her throat. The faces in front of her weren’t accusing, just raw with fear—and that made it worse.

She exhaled, long and slow. “Yeah,” she admitted, barely louder than a whisper. “They’re working together. I know.”

The words hit like a stone dropped in water. Ripples moved through the room—shock, then grim understanding.

Chaewon’s voice was low but fierce: “We need to involve someone. Staff, management—hell, even authorities.”

Solar ripped her wrist back then, cradling it against her chest. “No.”

“No?” Yunjin’s voice broke sharp. “Sol, look at you. First the accident, now this. You think it’ll stop here?”

Solar shook her head, a bitter smile twisting her lips. “You don’t get it. He knows too much to go down without a fight.”

“Knows too much about what?” Eunchae asked, eyes wide.

Solar’s throat closed around the truth. She looked away, shoulders curling in. “…About me. About my family.”

The silence that followed was louder than shouting.

Chaewon’s breath hitched, Yunjin’s fists clenched against her knees, Eunchae’s mouth fell open. They wanted to press, to demand clarity—but the look on Solar’s face stopped them. That mix of shame and stubbornness, of a girl carrying a secret too heavy to share.

Chaewon was the first to find words, her voice low and tight with control. “Then we keep watch. If you won’t protect yourself, we will. All of us.”

Yunjin leaned forward, fire in her eyes. “But you can’t keep shutting us out, Sol. You don’t have to fight them alone.”

Solar closed her eyes briefly, exhaustion dragging at her features. She wanted to believe them. She wanted to hand the weight over. But instead she whispered, “I’ll figure it out.”

The others exchanged looks, an unspoken pact forming in the space between them.

Even if Solar wouldn’t admit it, they knew now. And they weren’t going to let her break alone.

That text had already been sent.

The knock came sharp, impatient. Before Solar could even rise, the door burst open.

“Are you serious?”

Chaerin stormed in like a hurricane in heels, Gucci jacket half-slipped off one shoulder, phone still in her hand. Her hair was pulled back in a sleek ponytail, but her eyes—their family eyes—were wild, burning. She didn’t hesitate. She didn’t greet the others. She went straight to her sister.

“You’re hiding it again, aren’t you?”

Solar stiffened. “Chaerin—”

“Don’t even.” Chaerin yanked Solar’s sleeve up before she could resist. The bandage flashed in the overhead light, fresh and too white against skin that was already bruised from weeks of punishing rehearsals. Chaerin froze for half a second, then her face twisted with something equal parts fury and heartbreak.

Her voice broke. “I knew it. You let him do this again.”

The words were a blade thrown into the middle of the room. Eunchae flinched. Sakura’s lips parted in a silent gasp. Even Chaewon, so controlled, closed her eyes briefly, as if the weight of Solar’s teenage years was suddenly back in the room with them.

Solar exhaled hard through her nose, like she could blow away the memory, the accusation, the truth. “It’s not like that.”

“It’s exactly like that!” Chaerin’s pitch rose, desperate. “You think I don’t remember, Sol? All those nights you came home hiding bruises? Making excuses? Pretending you’d fallen during training? You’re doing it again!”

Her voice cracked on the last word.

Yunjin stood, stepping between them but not blocking. “Chaerin—she didn’t tell us either. We only just saw.” Her voice softened, trembling with guilt. “We thought maybe if we kept watch she’d finally let us—”

“You should’ve called me the second you knew,” Chaerin snapped, turning on Yunjin like a whip. Then she whirled back to her sister, grabbing Solar’s uninjured hand. Her grip was tight, shaking. “He doesn’t get to come back and hurt you again. Not now. Not ever.”

Solar’s eyes glossed with tears, but her jaw stayed set. “It’s not that simple.”

“It is that simple!” Chaerin shot back. “You call it what it is—abuse—and you don’t let him near you. You don’t let him keep doing this, hiding behind whatever excuses you’ve been making for him.” Her voice dropped, raw. “Because I can’t watch you disappear into that cycle again. I won’t.”

The silence after was heavy, full of unspoken history.

Chaewon finally spoke, calm but sharp. “She’s right, Solar. This isn’t just practice injuries anymore. This is Jihoon. And we can’t keep pretending it’s not.”

Solar’s voice was thin, breaking like thread. “If I go to the staff, if I go to the authorities… he won’t go down quietly. He knows things. About me. About our family. Things that would ruin us all.”

That stopped Chaerin cold. Her grip loosened just slightly. “…What?”

Solar swallowed, chest trembling. “He knows too much. He’s been sitting on it, waiting. And if I push him, if I make it public… it won’t just be me who pays.”

Chaerin’s face drained of color, but her fire didn’t die. She shook her head violently, ponytail swishing. “No. No, Sol—you don’t get to martyr yourself like this. You think you’re protecting us, but you’re not. You’re just bleeding yourself dry, same as before.”

Solar flinched at the word bleeding, her wrist pulsing under the bandage.

Yunjin reached out, placing a steadying hand on Chaerin’s arm. “We’re not going to let him take her apart again. Not this time. But we need to be smart about it.”

Chaerin let out a bitter laugh, swiping at her eyes. “Smart? What’s smart about letting him lay a finger on her?”

Solar whispered, almost pleading, “Please… just drop it tonight. I can’t…”

The fight in Chaerin’s face fractured, showing the hurt underneath. She crouched down suddenly, level with Solar, forcing her eyes to meet hers. Her voice was hoarse but steady. “You’re not sixteen anymore. You don’t have to carry this alone. I swear, I will burn the whole damn industry to the ground before I let him touch you again.”

The promise hung heavy in the dorm air.

And for the first time in years, Solar didn’t argue back.



The dorm still buzzed faintly with the aftershock of Chaerin’s outburst. Solar had laughed it off in front of her sister, but once the noise settled and the walls went quiet, the heaviness returned. Too many eyes on her. Too many worried voices she couldn’t answer.

She slipped her hoodie on, tugging the sleeves over her bandaged wrist, and muttered something about air. No one stopped her—though Yunjin’s eyes tracked her to the door like a tether she didn’t want to cut.

The city night hit sharp, neon bleeding across wet pavement from a drizzle earlier. Seoul’s hum was constant, grounding and dizzying all at once. Solar didn’t head toward anywhere in particular, just moved, sneakers scuffing sidewalks until her body found a rhythm that distracted from the ache in her wrist.

Her feet carried her toward HYBE’s familiar stairwell, the one she’d leaned against too many times with her chest aching. Or maybe the corner convenience store where Jungwon always seemed to appear, silent but steady. She didn’t know until she arrived, pulse stuttering, lungs pulling cold air as if it might scrub out the memory of Jihoon’s hand on her.

She pressed her back to the wall, whispering under her breath: You don’t need saving. You don’t. But her body betrayed her—the ache in her wrist, the hollowness under her ribs.

Back in the dorm, Chaerin sat on Solar’s bed, jaw tight. She couldn’t shake the image of the bandage, of Solar’s thin smile as if it explained anything. She grabbed her phone and, with little hesitation, fired off a text:

To: Jaehyun
“She’s hiding it. Jihoon hurt her again. She just left the dorm.”

Her thumb hovered a second before hitting send. Then she tossed the phone onto the bed, chewing the inside of her cheek. If Solar hated her for it later, fine. Better that than another bruise.



The night air pressed heavy against the HYBE building, damp with Seoul’s late spring humidity. Jungwon had been pacing for the better part of ten minutes, unable to shake the storm in his chest after the dressing room confrontation. He should’ve gone home with the others. He should’ve swallowed it down, told himself she was safe with her members. But the image of her wrist—the blood, the way her voice had trembled when she insisted she was “fine”—looped mercilessly in his mind.

So he lingered.

His sneakers scuffed against the concrete floor of a side stairwell, each step echoing louder than it should have. He leaned against the cool railing, jaw clenched, debating the sanity of following her. He wasn’t supposed to. If anyone saw him trailing Solar this late, there’d be questions, rumors, a hundred problems neither of them needed. And yet—his instincts clawed at him. Every time he told himself to leave, to let it go, something deeper refused.

She wasn’t fine. And he couldn’t pretend otherwise.

When he finally pushed the exit door open, the city lights hit him—neon bleeding into the skyline, distant horns rising from the streets below. He scanned the rooftop access path, half-expecting to find it empty.

But she wasn’t alone.

A few meters ahead, Solar stood stiffly, arms crossed, her hoodie sleeves tugged low over her hands. And beside her—taller, broader, cut from stone—was a man Jungwon didn’t recognize at first glance.

Then the sunglasses caught the light.

Even at night, he wore them. His face was unreadable, features carved into an unshakable mask. He didn’t stand like a manager or a staff member. He stood like someone who owned the space, protective, immovable.

Jaehyun.

Jungwon froze in the shadows near the door, heart thudding harder than it had in the practice rooms. He had heard Solar mention her older brother only in fragments—always formal, always tinged with distance—but seeing him here in the flesh made it real. This wasn’t just an idol surrounded by managers and stylists anymore. This was family.

Solar shifted, and the expression on her face said everything. She looked annoyed, her mouth tight as she gestured sharply with her hands. Annoyed, but also—Jungwon caught it—faintly resigned. Like a younger sibling caught sneaking out past curfew, cornered by someone who had every right to lecture her but none of the gentleness to make it easier.

Jaehyun stood silent, letting her words roll off him, his posture unyielding. He didn’t need to raise his voice. His presence alone filled the rooftop, a warning to anyone who might think of testing him—or her.

Jungwon’s stomach twisted.

Part of him wanted to stride forward, to plant himself at Solar’s side and tell this man that she wasn’t his to handle alone, that he—Jungwon—wasn’t going anywhere either. But another part knew what stepping into that moment would do. It would turn her family against him before he even had the chance to prove himself. It would strip her of the fragile control she had left.

So he stayed where he was, rooted in the shadows.

Solar’s voice rose faintly, carried by the night wind, but the words blurred. He didn’t need to hear them to understand. Her stance was sharp, shoulders set, but her eyes betrayed the exhaustion—dark crescents carved beneath them, her movements a fraction too brittle. She wanted Jaehyun to back off. He wouldn’t.

The sight clawed at Jungwon’s chest, feeding the restless fire already burning there. His hands curled into fists at his sides. The possessiveness he had fought to swallow since the accident surged higher now, mingling with the ache of helplessness.

She wasn’t fine. She hadn’t been fine for a long time. And if her family couldn’t see it—or if they saw it and still left her to fight on her own—then he would.

His jaw tightened. His pulse roared in his ears.

He forced himself to turn away, shoes scraping against the pavement as he slipped back into the stairwell before either of them could notice. The door clicked shut behind him, muffling the rooftop back into silence.

But the vow crystallized inside him, sharp and unbreakable:

Next time, I won’t hold back. Not from her. Not from Jihoon. Not from Danielle. And not from anyone who thinks they can stand between us.



















59: Shadow Whispers

The night was restless. Seoul's neon bled across puddled sidewalks, buzzing signs humming in the damp air like an endless pulse. Jungwon lingered at the edge of it, hood drawn low, shoulders stiff as though bracing against a storm no one else could feel.

He should've gone home hours ago. The others had. But his feet wouldn't carry him away. Not when his chest still echoed with the image of her wrist wrapped in white, not when every thought circled back to the way she kept insisting she was "fine" when she clearly wasn't.

So he hovered. Always just outside her orbit. Close enough to know if something went wrong, far enough to pretend he wasn't crossing a line.

His fists flexed inside his sleeves, nails biting skin. Is this protection or obsession? He didn't know anymore. Every instinct in him screamed to step forward, to put himself in her sightline and make it known—I'm here. I won't let anyone touch you again. But another voice warned that might smother her, push her away. She already lived under too many watchful eyes: managers, fans, her own siblings. Did she need his too?

The war raged quiet in his chest, the pull magnetic, relentless. His sneakers scuffed concrete as he shifted restlessly, shadows cutting sharp lines across his face.

And somewhere blocks away—maybe curled in a van seat, maybe brushing her teeth at the dorm, maybe just staring at her phone—Solar froze mid-thought. Her heart skipped, unprompted. His name crossed her mind without reason, like a whisper she couldn't shake. Won.

She pressed a palm against her chest, unsettled by the way it thudded harder than it should. It was nothing, she told herself. Just nerves. Just exhaustion.

But still—she felt it. The same way he did.

Jungwon lifted his head toward the high windows of HYBE, a muscle ticking in his jaw. He didn't know if she was awake, if she was pacing her room, if she was already asleep. All he knew was that the distance felt unbearable, and yet stepping forward felt dangerous.

So he lingered. Watching, waiting. A shadow caught between fear and desire.

The city's pulse thrummed steady beneath Jungwon's sneakers, a heartbeat he couldn't quite fall in rhythm with. He dragged his thumb along the edge of his sleeve, restless. His instincts were claws in his chest, scraping, demanding he step forward—go find her, make himself known. He clenched his jaw, grinding the thought back down.

It wasn't his place. Not yet.

The night air shifted, carrying the faint tang of gasoline and rain-slicked pavement. He adjusted his hood, forcing himself to blend further into the shadows, when the sharp staccato of heels on concrete sliced through the quiet.

He tensed immediately. Not hers. Too heavy. Too sharp.

From the corner of his vision, movement approached—confident, impatient, carrying the kind of energy that didn't apologize for intruding. The figure came into view beneath a streetlamp: a girl with Solar's eyes, but harder, lined with fire. Her Gucci jacket was slung half-off her shoulder like armor she couldn't be bothered to adjust, and her ponytail swayed like a whip with each step.

Chaerin.

Of course.

She didn't look surprised to find him standing there, half-buried in shadow. If anything, her lips curved into something wry, like she'd expected it.

"Well, well," she said, voice slicing the quiet like glass. "The infamous shadow-boy himself."

Jungwon stiffened, straightening. "What?"

"You know," Chaerin drawled, tilting her head. "Lurking in doorways. Standing in the dark like some overprotective stalker. Very mysterious. Very romantic. Very... creepy." She arched a brow, pausing just long enough for the jab to land. "Take your pick."

His ears burned beneath his hood, but he forced his face blank. "I wasn't—"

"—watching her?" Chaerin cut in smoothly, stepping closer until the lamplight caught the glitter of her earrings. "Please. Don't insult me. You've been watching her since the accident. Since before, probably. My sister may not see you, but I do."

He stayed silent, but his fingers curled tighter in his sleeves.

Chaerin smirked, but there was no humor in it—only sharpness, the same fire Solar carried but less restrained. "Relax, I'm not gonna rat you out to HYBE for loitering near their precious building. God knows they'd probably issue you a restraining order just for breathing near her at the wrong time."

That almost pulled a laugh from him, but he swallowed it down.

Chaerin's eyes, though—they were the real weapon. They studied him with unnerving precision, cataloging every twitch, every breath. It was disarming, like she could read the thoughts he'd buried deepest.

"You care about her," she said finally, voice flattening into something serious.

It wasn't a question.

Jungwon's throat bobbed. He thought about denying it, but what would be the point? The truth was carved into his bones. "Yeah."

Chaerin's smirk softened into something sharper—an edge honed into purpose. "Good."

That startled him. He blinked. "Good?"

"Yeah, because God knows she doesn't need another stone statue breathing down her neck." Her tone dripped with something heavier than sarcasm. "Jaehyun's got that role covered."

The name landed heavy between them. Jungwon had heard it, had seen the man with the ever-present sunglasses, that immovable presence beside Solar on the rooftop. Jaehyun was a wall—protective, yes, but cold, untouchable. Jungwon had stayed hidden in the shadows that night, partly because of him.

"Your brother," Jungwon muttered carefully.

"My brother," Chaerin confirmed, voice darkened by history. "And don't get me wrong, he loves her. We all do. But love isn't always what saves someone. Sometimes it just cages them tighter."

The words slipped under Jungwon's skin like a blade. He clenched his fists, nails biting harder. He wanted to argue, but the image of Solar's face—tired, defiant, cornered—rose too fast. He knew Chaerin was right.

Chaerin sighed, the sharpness cracking for a moment. She tugged her jacket fully onto her shoulder, folding her arms as if bracing herself. "Look, I don't like outsiders. Especially ones orbiting around my sister with puppy-dog eyes. But you..." Her gaze flicked up and down him, measuring. "You're different. You bleed when she bleeds. I can see it. And that's what she needs right now."

Jungwon's breath hitched. "What do you mean?"

"I mean," Chaerin said, stepping closer until they were barely a pace apart, "Solar doesn't need someone who watches from the sidelines. She doesn't need someone who makes promises from the shadows. She needs someone who will stand there—visible. With her. Even when it gets ugly. Especially then."

The words landed like a strike, reverberating through his chest.

Chaerin leaned in slightly, her eyes flaring. "Jaehyun will never be that. He'll protect her, sure, but from behind a wall of silence and rules. She doesn't need stone. She needs fire. She needs someone who won't let her drown in her own damn martyr complex."

Jungwon's jaw clenched. He wanted to say something—anything—but the knot in his throat was too thick.

Chaerin exhaled, stepping back, her smirk sliding back into place like armor. "So. Quit skulking in doorways. Quit acting like you're waiting for permission. You're not a ghost, Jungwon. You're flesh and blood, and she needs to see that."

Her words carved deep, sharper than anything Jaehyun had said without ever needing to raise his voice.

Chaerin pivoted, heels clicking against the wet concrete as she turned to go. She raised one hand in a casual wave over her shoulder. "Oh, and one more thing."

Jungwon's eyes narrowed. "What?"

Chaerin glanced back with a grin that didn't quite hide the ache in her eyes. "If you break her, I'll break you. Simple as that."

Then she was gone, swallowed by the city night, leaving him rooted in place. Jungwon stared after her, the echo of her words pressing into his chest. His breath came slow, heavy, like he'd just survived a fight he hadn't realized he was in.

He pulled his hood tighter, but for the first time in weeks, he didn't keep walking. He stepped forward.




The next morning bled into afternoon without asking permission. Music show day always did that—time shrinking and stretching around call times and camera notes, breaths counted in eight-beat phrases and quick swallows of lukewarm water. UNFORGIVEN season meant no empty corners; even the hallways felt scheduled, every square of tile mapped with tape and initials.

Le Sserafim's dressing room looked like a storm had been curated there on purpose: garment bags pooled like fallen clouds, makeup pallets open to galaxies of shimmer, the faint sting of hairspray layered over coffee and citrus hand sanitizer. Staff weaved in and out, murmuring about cue sheets and lighting changes and one camera that kept stuttering on crane swings. More bouquets arrived, card notes tucked under ribbon. Someone's gift fan set included a tiny cowboy hat keychain with glitter rim—Eunchae screeched softly when she found it, then tucked it onto her bag as if it were a talisman against exhaustion.

The girls were down the hall for a quick camera-block and a unit promo bumper, manager Kang shepherding them like a quiet planet with its moons in tow. Solar had begged five minutes to herself—"just to re-pin my mic tape"—and the stylists had given her the kind of indulgent look reserved for idols who never asked for anything. She was left alone with the hum of the vanity lights and the soft buzz of her in-ears charging in their case.

She slid into the chair nearest the mirror, the one with her name written in tidy black tape on the edge, and pressed her palms into her thighs until the tremor there subsided. The sleeve of her hoodie was pushed up just enough to peek at the gauze; she tugged it down again, automatic. The bandage didn't ache exactly—it pulsed, a metronome against the skin, a reminder. She stared at her reflection and saw the version of herself the public devoured: glossy hair, warm blush, eyeliner tight as a blade. The skin just under her eyes told on her anyway, a space where makeup couldn't quite erase the week.

Her phone sat facedown on the station. She flipped it over.

The last unread message wasn't new. It had been there long enough to burn its shape into her. Be careful. Danielle is... dangerous. Minji had sent it in the messy hours after the leak, after Jihoon's hand and HYBE's statement and the comments that called Solar unprofessional, unstable, unfit. There hadn't been anything else from her since. No follow-up. No soft apology for taking space. No late-night burst of honesty. The absence hurt with a gentleness that felt like cruelty.

Solar hovered her thumb above the text field, typed Are you safe? then deleted it. Typed I miss you then deleted that too. She settled for a small gray bubble: Thank you for telling me. She let it sit unsent, a lighthouse blinking with no one watching.

Somewhere in the hallway, a staffer yelled five minutes and the distant room answered with a chorus of got it!, warm and harried. Solar set the phone down and let the silence hurry around her. She breathed, counting it in fours. Her chest still felt like glass—thin, reflective, threatening to spiderweb under the right angle of pressure.

She hadn't posted anything personal since HYBE's statement went out—the one that flattened her into a paragraph of corporate concern and "recommitment to professionalism." It had stabilized the comments for exactly eighteen hours. Then the tide shifted the way tides do, pulled by moons she couldn't see. She'd watched her name scroll at the bottom of cable news, watched strangers dissect her like a case study.

Solar opened the camera. The screen threw the dressing room back at her, twin rows of lights and the messy comfort of their chaos. She turned the phone toward her station: the small strip of gaffer tape with SOLAR in handwriting, the coiled in-ear cable like a silver snake, the mic belt folded on itself like a promise. No face. No bandage. Just objects that implied a body.

She took a photo. It looked like proof of life.

In the caption field, she typed, deleted, typed again. still here. Too blunt. let's work hard today. Too safe. we keep moving. Too slogan. She swallowed and typed: on. One word. A button. A state. A refusal to be turned off.

Her thumb hovered. She thought of manager Kang's caution, of corporate eyes, of the way a single word could be a war. She hit Post.

For a handful of seconds nothing happened, and then everything did. Comments rolled in like applause and shrapnel, hearts blooming and arrhythmic. Proud of you. Take care of yourself. Stay in your lane. She's so strong. She's faking it. I love you I love you I love you. She closed the app before the mean ones could calcify. It wasn't that she believed them; it was that they stuck to the ribs, harder to digest.

Her reflection blinked at her from the mirror. She tapped her fingers against the vanity, counting clicks. Somewhere down the hall, one of the girls squealed—Eunchae's high, unmistakable sound—and Solar smiled before she remembered to stop. The smile stayed anyway, small and stubborn.

It was almost funny. After last night's dorm fault line, she'd expected the entire day to feel like walking on a roof in rain. Instead, the world had the audacity to keep spinning. The staff still reminded them to drink water. The MC still flubbed a line. A junior group still bowed ninety degrees when they passed them in the corridor, eyes wide with secondhand awe. Everything insisted on routine. Routine could save you or swallow you.

Her shoulder twinged. She rolled it and pictured choreography counts like ladder rungs. Four counts to flip the mic, eight to hit the knee sweep, two to smile like it was easy. Chaewon would catch her if she overreached; Kazuha would whisper a quiet you've got this that sounded like a fact; Sakura would give her that tiny nod that meant I see you; Yunjin would stand a centimeter closer than usual, an orbit tightening into protection.

Solar reached for her water and missed, then found it a second later, as if time itself had slipped. She capped it and thought about Jungwon with an irritability that felt suspiciously like relief.

She hadn't seen him since the rooftop—the shape of him held in her periphery by fear and the sunblocking presence of Jaehyun. She felt stupid that some part of her had wanted him to break the rules and walk straight through that boundary. You're not sixteen anymore, Chaerin had said, heat and history in her voice. Solar believed her. She was trying to. But the part of her that had learned to read the quiet—doors, footsteps, the way someone approached without sound—couldn't help listening for him anyway.

Now, alone in the humming room, she thought of him without meaning to. The air sharpened. The mirror showed nothing but her, but her skin arranged itself as if bracing for a hoodie sleeve to brush her arm. Her heart startled at the idea, like it remembered a touch that hadn't happened. She made a face at herself. "Get a grip," she whispered, but even her own reflection looked unconvinced.

A knock thudded against the frame and her pulse jumped—but it was only a stylist ducking in, arms full of steamer and a tangle of ribbon. "Two minutes," the stylist chimed, "they're wrapping camera block. Need to check your hairline lift."

Solar nodded, sat still while warm steam breathed along the edge of her hairline. The soft heat was almost enough to untie her shoulders. The stylist hummed something noncommittal, the lullaby song of all green rooms. "Your post is cute," she said absently. "Simple." It wasn't praise or warning, just observation. Solar smiled anyway.

When the door shut again, the room swallowed the last of the hallway chatter and left her inside the quiet. She let it settle like a blanket, heavy but not suffocating. The mirror reflected a girl who was both too much and not enough, both weapon and wound. She breathed in until her diaphragm pressed against costume boning and breathed out until her throat loosened.

Her phone buzzed.

For one reckless second she thought Minji, hope an animal she didn't like to see in daylight. It was only the team group chat, a flood of blue bubbles: Eunchae's selfie with a filter that gave her cat ears, Yunjin's caption who gave this child the internet, Kazuha's serene thumbs-up emoji, Chaewon's line: Hydrate. Don't argue. There was a private message under it, from Yunjin alone.

I'm outside. Not checking on you, just... loitering with purpose. Come out if you want a hug.

Solar's throat went tight in a different way. She didn't deserve how easy Yunjin made forgiveness look. She typed back, Two minutes. Don't let Kang catch you or he'll give us both a lecture. A typing bubble returned immediately. I'll fight him with a mic stand. Solar laughed, startled, and the sound unclenched something in her ribs.

She pocketed her phone and stood, letting her body weight roll through her feet like she'd been taught when panic tried to tip her. The poster on the opposite wall—some past season's glossy promo—looked back with an unbothered smile. She wondered who that girl was, what she knew. Maybe less. Maybe more.

The door cracked. Manager Kang poked his head in like a very polite ghost. "Two-minute call," he said. "They're lining up the dolly track again. Solar, you okay?"

She hated the question. Loved who it came from.
"On," she said, and his mouth twitched like he understood the joke she hadn't made out loud.

The hallway was colder, the AC pushing a manufactured winter against their flushed skin. Solar slipped into it and felt her edges define, like the air sharpened her outline. The sound of shoes on tile became a metronome: staffers, idols, assistants, security. Her world was movement and pause, pose and breath.

They passed an open door where another group's members were sitting cross-legged, phones out like prayer books. A second later a camera crew switched directions and nearly clipped her shoulder; she pivoted without thinking, manager Kang's hand hovering near her elbow but not touching. He always asked permission with space, not words. She breathed grateful.

At the end of the corridor, the girls materialized in a knot of sound and hairspray fog. Eunchae leapt onto Solar in a careful hug that respected mic packs. "You posted!" she cheered, like the whole day hinged on that act. "Your caption is so slay. Minimalist queen."

Yunjin's eyes scanned Solar's face with the kind of intensity that had made last night both a kindness and a knife. "You good?" she asked low. Not Are you okay?—You good? An invitation to answer with a nod. Solar nodded. Yunjin's mouth pressed into something like approval and she messed Solar's hair with two fingers, pretending the stylist wouldn't scream.

Chaewon's hand brushed Solar's arm like a checkpoint. "Stay with me first verse," she murmured, leader brain already calculating where to stand if Solar's legs shook. "Kazu, second chorus, stay left on the lift. Eunchae, stop stealing fan gifts mid-run."

"I would never," Eunchae lied, eyes huge, cowboy keychain glittering like guilt.

There was a buzzing behind all of it, a frequency Solar felt more than heard. It was the show, but it was also something else. The pull. She didn't turn her head; she had learned not to chase shadows. But her skin prickled along her collarbone and an old fear rose up, the shape of someone watching. She swallowed. Fed the fear a different name. Won. The syllable softened the inside of her cheek.

The stagehands called them in with headsets and hand gestures. They filed into the wings. The LED wall's wash painted everyone the same unreal color. Solar stood in the dark with her group and felt her body remember the count, the mute choreography of being part of something larger than herself. She flexed her fingers; the gauze tugged and then forgot itself.

The pre-record clicked in her in-ears. The crowd noise was layered and distant, like ocean from inside a shell. She thought about how many people wanted her to be only one thing. She thought about how many people loved her for trying to be two. Somewhere, someone had already clipped her caption and posted it; somewhere else, a stranger had decided what it meant. Here, in this one square of taped floor, none of that mattered.

They hit the stage and she was a switch thrown. The first verse slid across her tongue, muscle memory and decision. Chaewon was a half step to the right, steady as gravity. Yunjin cut a glance at her that said with me, and Solar met it with a breath that said I am. Kazuha's smile flashed quick, private, only visible from this angle. Sakura's presence was a lighthouse; Solar oriented herself to it.

When the song was over, the applause felt like rain. They ducked into the wings for quick notes before encore, staff surrounding them with efficient kindness. Manager Kang rattled off camera changes. "We need to tighten the diagonal on the bridge," he said. "Also—Solar, whatever you did at the end there, keep it. It read... honest."

Her ears warmed. She hadn't done anything. Or maybe she had—something small, a thing like permission.

Back in the dressing room between segments, the chaos resumed in miniature. A makeup artist blotted, a stylist retied a ribbon, someone's phone chimed with an appointment reminder nobody could make. Solar slipped to her chair, the mirror catching her with cheeks still flushed from stage, a few baby hairs rebelliously free. She pressed a cool water bottle to her neck and tried not to think about vultures with keyboards.

Her phone buzzed again. For the second time, hope reached out with silly fingers. She glanced.

on. Jungwon had liked her post. He hadn't commented, hadn't messaged. Just that silent tap of acknowledgment that said he'd been scrolling her world from inside his. It should've been nothing. It settled in her chest like a warm coin.

The door eased open to admit the steamer and a burst of voices from the hall, then closed again. The room, for a breath, was only her and the humming lights and the photo on her screen. She lifted the phone, traced the little heart he'd left like it might be tactile.

"You're ridiculous," she told herself, not entirely angry about it.

Solar set the phone down, smoothed her hoodie sleeve again without thinking, and leaned into the mirror. A smudge of glitter clung to her cheekbone. She rubbed it away and another spark caught light. She laughed softly, tired and a little delirious, and let it stay.

The laugh faded as quickly as it came, a bubble that rose and popped against the mirror. Quiet slid back in—thick, waiting. Solar's gaze drifted to the phone again, to the thread pinned to the top like a warning label:

Be careful. Danielle is... dangerous.

Minji's words had been sitting there long enough to ghost the shape of themselves onto her vision when she blinked. No follow-up. No are you okay? No midnight spill of everything unsaid. The silence had weight; it pressed between Solar's ribs like a book she couldn't close.

She unlocked the screen and scrolled nothing. Mentions. A schedule reminder. A blurred fan photo taken at an angle that made her jaw look fragile. Back to the message. The three dots did not appear. Her thumb hovered above the keyboard, typing muscle memory into the search for a reply—Are you safe?—then backing out like she'd stepped too close to a ledge.

What was Danielle doing right now? Crafting another leak, or worse—waiting for Solar to breathe out so she could cut the breath in half. Where was Jihoon? The question slid cold along her spine. His absence was never empty; it was a hallway light left on in an empty house, it was proof of planning.

She realized she'd started picking at the corner of the gauze. She flattened her palm over it, made herself stop. The bandage didn't hurt so much as remind—pulse, pulse, a metronome too close to bone.

The hallway hummed on the other side of the door: wheel squeak, laugh shard, a manager's clipped thirty seconds to standby tossed down the corridor like a frisbee. Solar's breath shortened to match it. She set the phone face-down and turned it face-up again, as if looking away might rewind something.

The handle turned.

The door opened with the softest hinge sound and her body reacted before her brain did—chair legs scraping, shoulders locking, pulse detonating against the gauze. For a split frame she was nowhere but inside the old fear: a hand at her wrist, a voice that knew how to make kindness into a trap, a room with no exits. The mirror caught her eyes blown wide, feral.

"It's just me, Sol."

His voice hit like a hand to the shoulder—firm, steady, not asking. The panic stuttered, tripped, and then collapsed into a rough breath that left her lightheaded.

Jungwon stood in the doorway, hood down despite the cameras roaming the hall, shoulders squared as if he'd made a decision and then built a spine around it. In his right hand, condensation slicked the curve of a peach tea can; a bead of water broke free and ran down his knuckles, then disappeared into the cuff of his sleeve. He took one step in and the small room shrank around his presence, not tighter—safer. The fluorescent hum seemed to tilt toward him and steady.

Solar exhaled, the sound catching on a laugh that wasn't laughter at all. "I—" The syllable fell apart in her mouth. He lifted the can a little, a peace offering or a declaration. The faint sugar-sweet of peach threaded into the hairspray and warm-steel smell of the steamer still cooling in the corner.

"You jumped," he said quietly, not accusing. Stating the fact so she wouldn't have to pretend it didn't happen.

She forced her fingers to unclench from the edge of the vanity. The world sorted itself back into objects: the mirror crowded with lightbulbs, the tidy strip of tape with her name, the little pile of hairpins entrusted to gravity on the counter. Jungwon, here.

Something unclasped low in her sternum. She let her shoulders drop a fraction and sank back into the chair like it might hold. "Hi," she started, then corrected herself, a softness she didn't permit anyone else sneaking into the syllable. "Hi, Won."

It lifted a weight she hadn't admitted she was carrying. She felt it go.

He didn't smile, exactly—his mouth made a shape between apology and resolve—but some line in his face eased, like her saying his name was permission he hadn't known he needed. He crossed the threshold fully and nudged the door with his heel until it latched, not loud, just sure. No more corridor noise. The dressing room held only the two of them and the quiet electric of the mirror lights.

He set the peach tea on the vanity, not directly in front of her, but within reach like a careful orbit. "Vending machine down the hall," he said, voice still low. "I remembered you always steal the peach ones from the bottom row."

The tiniest, ridiculous detail cracked something open inside her—some proof that he didn't just want the idea of protecting her, he wanted the practice of it, the specifics. Not drink something, but this. Not are you fine, but I saw what you reach for when you're not pretending.

Her throat tightened. "You remembered that?"

His gaze flicked to the bandage and back to her face, the way people look at a wound without wanting you to feel looked at. "I remember a lot of things," he said, and it should have sounded like a claim. It sounded like a promise he made to himself years ago that kept renewing.

Solar reached for the can. Cold bit her palm; the sweetness fogged the air when she cracked it open. Her hand trembled once, then steadied. She took a sip that slid peach and frost over the raw places, and the rush of sugar did something kind to her blood. She hadn't realized how thirsty she was until the taste told on her.

"I thought you were—" She stopped. Hiding wasn't the word; he'd never been cowardly. Gone wasn't true either. Jungwon had a way of being present without claiming credit for it. The rooftop. The hallway pauses. The like on the post that had felt like a coin pressed into her palm.

"Busy," he supplied, a ghost of humor, but his eyes stayed on her like they were holding her up from the inside. "I was. I am." He shifted a fraction closer, enough that the warmth of him reached across the strip of vanity like a space heater turned to low. "But I'm here."

She didn't ask why now because she already knew—because Chaerin's name burned in his posture like a new rule, because the last twenty-four hours had torn subtlety out of all of them. Because he'd stood in the shadows and decided he was done standing in the shadows.

Her body, traitor, leaned one degree toward him, so slight the mirror might have imagined it. For once the instinct didn't scare her. It soothed. The possessiveness she'd bristled at before—on other nights, in other rooms—settled against her like a weight blanket. Not suffocation. Gravity.

Memories flashed how they always did—in frames, not films. The dorm's thin light on gauze. Chaerin's voice breaking on again. Jaehyun's sunglasses catching the rooftop glow like a blade. Yunjin's you good? spoken like a hand at her back instead of a question. Minji's message frozen in gray. Danielle's shadow long as an alley.

Solar blinked and found only the now. A boy in her dressing room, hoodie collar softened by wear, hands relaxed at his sides because he knew she was watching them. A can of peach tea sweating a ring onto black tabletop tape. Her own reflection, less ghosted than she'd expected.

"Hi, Won," she said again, quieter, as if repeating it could keep this version of the world from slipping.

Something almost like light moved behind his eyes. He wasn't pretty in the way people called idols pretty; he was handsome in the way a cliff was handsome—edges softened by weather, still capable of breaking whatever tried to climb wrong. He took another step and then stopped himself, deference built into the brake. "Is it okay that I'm here?" he asked.

She could have said no and he would have gone without the door making a sound. She could have said I'm fine and he would have believed the word but not the way it sat on her. She surprised herself by saying the simplest truth. "Yes."

His breath left him like he'd been waiting to exhale for hours. He moved to the side of her chair rather than in front of it, so she didn't have to look up; so he could watch the mirror with her, not her reflection alone. It was an old trick for anxious people—share the gaze so you don't feel pinned by it—and she noticed that he knew it, that he had thought about the angle of his body as carefully as a lyric.

He didn't reach for her. He didn't have to. Presence did what touch might have done and more.

Outside, someone laughed too loud, and the laugh was cut off mid-sound by a door closing. A countdown started somewhere, one of the staffers ticking minutes on autopilot. Solar took another sip of peach tea and tasted Augusts from years when everything had felt solvable with sugar and sleep.

The mirror held them: one girl, one boy, and the space between like a bridge under construction—cordoned off, still, but already spanning something.

"Hi, Won," she said a third time, barely above breath, and this time it wasn't greeting. It was gratitude. It was don't move and don't vanish and I can do this if you stand right there.

His jaw set, gentle and final. "I'm here," he said again, like the line of a vow he meant to keep repeating until it wrote itself into her bones.

She let the words settle. Let them become the floor. Then she breathed and felt the breath go all the way to the edges.

The can of peach tea sweated quietly on the vanity, a small sun in condensation rings. Solar kept her hand wrapped around it, grounding herself in its cold weight, the proof that this was real. Jungwon wasn't a ghost at the edge of her awareness anymore. He was here, hoodie sleeves tugged past his wrists, jaw tight like he was biting down on something unsaid.

For a long moment, neither of them spoke. The hum of the mirror lights buzzed like a held breath.

Then he asked, softly but with that edge of insistence she recognized, "Are you okay?"

It wasn't the casual you good? Yunjin would toss at her. It wasn't the corporate version from Kang, polished and perfunctory. Jungwon's version dug deeper, like he was daring her to lie.

Solar's throat tightened. She stared into the mirror where both their reflections sat in parallel, hers tired and smudged, his steady, storm-pulled. She inhaled once, long, and then said it because she was too exhausted to dance around the truth.

"The girls found out," she admitted haltingly. "My siblings got involved. I'm just... trying to protect everyone."

The words cracked something open. Saying them made the weight shift from her chest onto the space between them, heavy as stone.

Jungwon didn't flinch. He leaned a fraction closer, enough that she felt the air change. "You can't destroy yourself to protect everyone else." His voice was low, controlled, but the anger was there—anger at the situation, at Jihoon, at Danielle, maybe even at her for thinking she had to carry it all.

Her fingers tightened around the can until it creaked. "If it keeps them safe," she shot back, defensive now, "then I will." The words rushed out sharp, like a shield she didn't have time to polish.

He straightened, eyes darkening. That possessive current—the one that used to make her wary, bristle like prey cornered—rose in his posture now. His hands curled into fists and then loosened, as if holding himself back. His voice came rough, final:

"That's why I'm here now, Sol. You're not getting hurt again. Not on my watch."

The words hit like a vow, vulgar in its intensity but clean in its intent. Not a request. A promise.

And instead of recoiling, Solar felt relief.

The tension that had been coiled under her ribs eased, slowly, like a knot loosening under steady hands. For the first time, his intensity didn't scare her—it steadied her. She let out a breath she hadn't realized she was holding, eyelids fluttering shut for half a second before opening again.

Her reflection in the mirror looked different: not lighter, not free, but anchored. Held in place by something outside herself.

She whispered, barely audible but certain, "Okay."

The hallway swelled again with the staff's call: "Encore line-up! Two minutes, girls!"

Solar startled, as if waking from a dream, the peach tea still cool in her hand. She set it carefully on the vanity, fingers lingering at the ring of condensation before stepping back.

Jungwon's reflection watched her as she reached for her mic belt. She looped it around her waist, the familiar tug of velcro sealing her back into the role the world demanded. Their eyes met once in the mirror, his gaze carrying the weight of the words he'd just given her. She didn't say anything this time, only breathed deep, then whispered to her own reflection:

"Let's work."

The room didn't argue. She slipped out, the door clicking softly behind her.

Silence reclaimed the dressing room. The peach tea sat on the vanity, half-finished, the condensation ring dark against the black tape with SOLAR written in neat marker.

Jungwon stood there longer than he should have, staring at the can like it was proof of something. He replayed the look in her eyes—how it shifted when she whispered Hi, Won, how her shoulders finally eased when he told her she wouldn't get hurt again. Relief. Trust.

It rattled him. He'd wanted to protect her from the first moment, but now it wasn't instinct—it was obligation. A vow carved into him as sharp as any scar.

Next time, he told himself, he wouldn't hover in the shadows. Next time, he'd step forward before she had to fall.

He finally left the room, pulling his hood up, footsteps muffled against the linoleum. The corridor was crowded, staff rushing idols between stages, cords snaking like veins along the floor.

And then, in the blur, he saw her.

Danielle.

Not close—just across the hall, framed by the glow of a stairwell exit. She wasn't looking at him, but at something on her phone, lips curved in a smile that didn't reach her eyes. For a heartbeat, the chaos around them dimmed.

His hands flexed into fists inside his sleeves. Every instinct screamed to march across the hall, to wipe that smug curve from her mouth, to demand what she was planning next. His pulse roared hot, restraint trembling.

But he didn't. Not yet.

Her head lifted as if she'd felt the weight of his stare. Their gazes caught for a single second—hers sharp, assessing; his dark, unmoving. Then a staffer stepped between them, breaking the line. When Jungwon looked again, she was gone.

He exhaled slow, dragging the fire back down into his chest. Not yet. But soon.

He turned toward the sound of Solar's laughter echoing faintly from the stage, and the vow crystallized in him like iron.

He would not just be a bystander anymore.
He would be her shield. And her weapon.












60: Shadows That Stay

The encore had wrung them out like wet laundry. Solar's pulse still echoed in her temples as she followed her members down the narrow backstage corridor, the air thick with hairspray, sweat, and the metallic bite of stage fog. Staffers darted around them with clipboards and walkie-talkies; someone yelled about a dolly track. Everything was chaos, the kind that had rules if you knew how to read it.

And there he was.

Jungwon leaned against the wall like a shadow given muscle and bone, hood pushed back, posture casual but unmoving. He didn't fidget like the rookies waiting for their turn. He didn't scroll his phone like the staff killing time. He just existed, calm as stone in a current. His eyes cut to Solar once, steady, before sweeping over the group as if cataloguing every variable in the hallway.

Eunchae spotted him first. "Whoa!" she squeaked, almost tripping over her own sneakers. She waved so enthusiastically her mic pack cord jolted loose. "Hello HYBE sibling!" she chirped, like she was greeting a long-lost cousin at a family barbecue.

Yunjin nearly dropped her water bottle. Her eyes darted between Jungwon and Solar, then back again, and a grin unfurled slow, wicked. "Oh my god," she said, dragging the words out. "Check out the new bodyguard."

Chaewon didn't even blink, just arched a brow and murmured, "Well, at least he's punctual." Her hand brushed Solar's back like punctuation—don't faint, keep walking.

Solar, of course, was blushing. Heat licked up her neck faster than she could smother it, and that was all the evidence Yunjin needed.

"GIRL," Yunjin hissed, voice pitched between scandalized and delighted. "Don't tell me—"

"Shut up," Solar muttered, tugging her hoodie sleeve over her hand like armor.

Kazuha, serene as always, tilted her head. "It's... kind of nice, though," she said softly. "Like extra insurance."

Eunchae gasped theatrically, clutching her cowboy keychain like a microphone. "Breaking news: Le Sserafim hires personal bodyguard, also happens to be Enhypen's leader, film at eleven!"

Even Sakura cracked a smile, shaking her head.

Through all of it, Jungwon didn't flinch. Didn't smile, didn't deflect, didn't excuse himself. Jungwon-coded to the bone—just steady, solid, letting the teasing roll off him like rain on stone. The only giveaway was the slight shift of his jaw, as if he were swallowing down a response.

Solar couldn't look at him directly; every time her eyes brushed his shape, her chest fluttered. But she didn't hate the teasing. Because under the chaos of it—under Eunchae's squeals and Yunjin's side-eyes—she caught the quiet truth humming in the air:

They were relieved.

For all their jokes, the girls knew what she knew—Jungwon would protect her in ways they couldn't.

And that realization, no matter how embarrassing the delivery, made the blush worth it.

They spilled back into the dressing room like a tide—costumes half-zipped, makeup smudged at the edges, adrenaline still buzzing in their veins. Staff flitted around with lint rollers and combs, but even their efficient hands couldn't smother the residual chaos.

Solar dropped into the chair at her station, chest still rising quick from the stage. She bent to unfasten her mic belt, and when she straightened, she saw him.

Jungwon had followed.

Not into the room, exactly—he stood in the doorway, one shoulder against the frame like a post assigned there. He wasn't scrolling his phone or trying to blend into the staff. His gaze skimmed over the room, settled on Solar, and stayed. Calm. Steady. Present.

Yunjin clocked it instantly. "Oh my god," she stage-whispered, leaning down to Solar's ear. "He's literally guarding the door, how could I ever compete?"

"Yunjin," Solar hissed, wishing the floor would open.

Chaewon, flipping through the encore cue notes, didn't even look up. "Let him stand there," she said evenly. "If anyone tries to drag Solar out, they'll have to go through him first." The corner of her mouth curved just slightly, betraying her approval.

Eunchae peeked over the back of the couch where she was cocooned in a blanket, eyes wide with delighted curiosity. She gave him a small, shy wave, then giggled when he acknowledged her with the barest nod.

Even Sakura's lips twitched, shaking her head at the whole scene.

Solar wanted to protest, to tell them all to stop staring, but her reflection betrayed her: pink-cheeked, hoodie sleeve tugged low over her wrist. She looked like a girl with something to hide—and something she didn't want to.

Jungwon shifted his weight, subtle, but enough to anchor the frame. He didn't flinch under their chatter, didn't retreat. His presence didn't feel intrusive; it was protective in a way that wrapped around the edges of the room. Obvious, yes—but not threatening. A wall built exactly where it needed to be.

And beneath the laughter and teasing, Solar could feel it—the quiet relief in the room. Her members had been carrying a fear they couldn't name, and now that fear had a counterweight. A presence.

Her stomach flipped, part mortification, part something dangerously close to gratitude.



The next morning, the hashtags were already there.

Solar's name trended in three variations, each uglier than the last. "#SolarScandal," "#UnfitIdol," "#DragDownENHYPEN." Screenshots spread faster than staff could blink: blurry candids from backstage, cropped cruelly close. One showed the condensation ring from Jungwon's peach tea on her vanity, captioned like it was a love letter. Another zoomed in on his shadow in a doorway, body angled just enough toward Solar to ignite a thousand theories.

The phrasing was always the same, as if rehearsed: She isn't just a problem for Le Sserafim. She's dragging Enhypen down with her.

Solar sat in the van on the way to another music show, thumb hovering over her phone as the feed refreshed. She didn't have to ask where it came from. Danielle's fingerprints were everywhere—calculated vagueness, bait too sharp to ignore.

The comments bit like acid:

She can't even focus on her own group, now she's ruining another's career.

Why is HYBE covering for her?

Poor Jungwon, he doesn't deserve this mess.

Eunchae leaned across the seat to peek, her breath fogging the window. "Unnie, don't look," she said gently, small hand tugging Solar's sleeve down to cover her bandage.

Solar snapped the phone shut, pulse rattling too fast. She wanted to believe in routine—stage, fan signs, music shows—but Danielle's shadow was threading itself through every second.
