Chaewon spoke from the front seat, voice even. "Don't engage. HYBE's already monitoring."

Yunjin muttered something sharper under her breath in English, loud enough that only Solar caught it. Bitch picked the wrong day.

Solar turned her face to the glass, watching Seoul blur by. Her own reflection stared back: tired, painted, carrying too much. She pressed her forehead against the cool glass and thought of how easily Danielle had turned a half-empty can into a weapon.

Her chest ached with the question that had no answer: what would she turn into next?



The music show building always felt like a hive: corridors too narrow for the swarm of idols, stylists, and staff, every corner buzzing with cameras. Solar was used to it—had learned how to smile at the right moments, how to keep her head down at the wrong ones. But when she turned the corner, her pulse stuttered.

Minji.

She was standing with the rest of NewJeans near the vending machines, hair braided back, oversized hoodie swallowing her frame between costume changes. The girls around her laughed at something Hanni was showing on her phone, but Minji wasn't looking at the screen. Her eyes were fixed on Solar.

It wasn't a glare. It wasn't warm either. Just... searching. Like she was trying to recognize someone she used to know.

Solar froze mid-step, heat rising unbidden to her cheeks. She felt suddenly aware of the gauze under her sleeve, the weight of her phone in her pocket, the phantom shape of Jungwon's peach tea still lingering in her hand.

Minji's mouth parted as if to say something—but then the moment broke. A staffer swooped in, ushering Solar along with a brisk "Le Sserafim, standby in two minutes!" and she was swept back into the current.

Their eyes didn't break until the corner took her.

Later, in the van, Solar opened her phone. No new messages. Just the same thread frozen at the top: Be careful. Danielle is... dangerous.

She hovered over Minji's name, thumb hesitating. The drafts folder already held too many ghosts: Are you okay?, I miss you, Do you hate me? She typed something else this time, something truer:

I wanted you to look at me like that again.

She stared at the sentence until her eyes blurred. Then she deleted it. The cursor blinked in the empty field, steady and merciless.

On Minji's end, in another van cutting through Seoul traffic, her phone sat heavy in her lap. She had half a note written in her drafts:

I can't stop thinking about you. But I don't know if being near me makes you safer... or worse.

She locked the screen before anyone could glance over her shoulder.

In another part of the building, the stairwell was quieter than the halls, but not silent. Somewhere below, a staffer's voice echoed about call times, and the hum of the building's AC rattled through the pipes. Jungwon leaned against the cool concrete wall, jaw tight, every muscle strung with the adrenaline still gnawing at his veins. He had told himself he'd wait, that HYBE's polished corridors weren't the place for this. But when Danielle slipped into the landing, phone in hand, that resolve cracked.

She saw him immediately. Of course she did. Danielle never missed an audience.

"Well," she said, voice lilting like she was greeting an old friend. Her eyes flicked down his frame, amused, before she tucked her phone into her back pocket. "If it isn't Solar's... shadow."

The word hit harder than it should have. Jungwon's fists curled before he forced them loose. He took a step forward, closing the distance until the harsh stairwell light cut angles into both their faces.

"Stay away from her." His voice came out low, deliberate, as if dragged up from the pit of his chest.

Danielle tilted her head, a smile tugging at her mouth like she was holding back laughter. "Oh? And here I thought you liked standing in the dark, watching her flounder."

He didn't bite. He couldn't. Every nerve screamed to shove her against the wall, to demand she stop speaking her poison aloud—but he kept himself still, steady. Solar didn't need more headlines.

"You don't get to talk about her," he said, quieter now, the kind of quiet that vibrated in the bones. "I know what you're doing. And it stops."

Danielle's smile widened, shark-sharp. "Does it?" She leaned in a fraction, enough to make the air between them feel dangerous. "Because from where I'm standing, it looks like the world is already starting to believe me. One little rumor at a time. And you can't protect her from all of it, Jungwon."

The way she said his name made his skin crawl.

He exhaled slow, forcing the fury back down where it couldn't spill. Not here. Not yet. But his eyes told her what his words didn't: if she touched Solar again, if she even breathed wrong in her direction, he would not hold back next time.

Danielle saw it. And she loved it.

"You're cute when you're angry," she murmured, stepping past him like she owned the space. "No wonder she likes having you around."

Her perfume lingered in the stairwell long after her footsteps faded.

Jungwon's chest heaved once, twice. He pressed the heel of his hand to the railing, grounding himself before the urge to follow consumed him. His heart thundered with a truth he hadn't spoken aloud yet: he didn't just want to guard Solar. He wanted to fight for her. To end this.

But not here. Not in a stairwell. Not until Jihoon was there too, the puppet master behind her strings.

He pushed off the wall, fury simmering under his skin like heat trapped beneath glass. Every step down the hall after that felt too loud, too heavy. Danielle's smirk was burned into his vision, taunting, daring him to slip.

He clenched his jaw. No. Not yet.

The corridor should have felt ordinary—just another stretch of tile and fluorescent light—but Solar felt the tension before she even looked up. Jungwon was there, storm still clinging to him. His hoodie hung heavy, jaw tight, like he'd just bitten back words he couldn't say aloud.

She almost asked. Almost pushed. But before the question could form, her wrist gave a sharp pulse as she adjusted her mic belt. She hissed softly and pressed her sleeve down, too late to hide it.

Jungwon's focus snapped like a magnet.

"Sol."

It was barely her name—more like a warning, or a plea. He stepped forward before she could retreat, his hand closing gently around hers. Not forceful. Not demanding. Just there. Anchoring.

Her breath stuttered. "It's fine," she started, the old reflex ready on her tongue. "It's—"

He peeled the fabric back just enough to see the edge of gauze. His thumb brushed the seam where skin met bandage, careful, reverent almost. His expression didn't twist in pity—only in a kind of quiet fury that had nothing to do with her.

"You've been pushing it." The words were low, certain. "I can feel it."

She wanted to pull away, to laugh it off like she always did. But the warmth of his hand around her wrist held her in place. It wasn't a cage. It was shelter.

For a heartbeat, she searched his face, waiting for the sharpness she'd learned to expect from men who thought possession meant ownership. But all she saw was steadiness—his brows knit, jaw locked, eyes burning with something that looked more like responsibility than demand.

Her defenses wavered. "If I stop moving," she whispered, "everything falls apart."

His gaze lifted to hers. "No," he said simply. "If you fall apart, everything else follows."

The silence stretched between them, humming with more electricity than any stage light. Solar swallowed hard, her pulse leaping under his thumb. The old instinct to argue—to carry it alone, to keep everyone else safe—rose up, then faltered. She was too tired to push him out. Too tired not to want the anchor he offered.

So instead, she let out a shaky laugh. "You sound like Chaewon."

Something in his expression softened, just barely. "Then maybe you should start listening."

It wasn't sharp. It wasn't even scolding. It was the gentlest thing he could've said—and it gutted her. Her throat tightened, eyes burning in a way she refused to let spill. She curled her fingers against his palm instead, a small, almost imperceptible squeeze. Not pulling away.

"Thank you," she said finally, the words trembling out before she could stop them. "For the tea. For... not letting me lie about it."

His thumb traced lightly over her wrist once, an unspoken vow. He didn't say the words again—not I'll protect you, not on my watch. He didn't need to. The promise was in his touch, in the way he was still standing there when he could've walked away.

Her chest eased, a knot loosening. For the first time in days, she felt something like steady ground. And maybe that was more dangerous than the pain.

When Jungwon finally eased back, releasing her wrist like it was something precious instead of broken, Solar didn't move. Her body wanted to—every survival instinct screamed at her to step away, reestablish distance, put the armor back on—but she stayed rooted to the spot, heat still tingling under her skin where his thumb had pressed.

She'd been touched before. Too many times. Hands that held her too tight, that turned affection into a chokehold, that made possession feel like erasure. Jihoon's shadow rose like bile in her throat—the way he used to grip her wrist to drag her out of rooms, his laugh low and cruel when she flinched. The way his love was always another word for control.

Jungwon's hand had felt nothing like that.

It wasn't soft—he wasn't delicate with her, not in the way some people tried to be when they thought she might break at a single wrong word. But it wasn't harsh either. It was steady. Firm in a way that told her he could hold on if she needed, but would let go the second she pulled back.

The difference carved itself into her chest like a truth she couldn't ignore.

Jihoon had wanted to own her.
Jungwon only wanted to protect her.

And for the first time, she realized she could feel the difference.

Her reflection in the metal frame of a backstage poster caught her eye—flushed cheeks, hair falling into her face, hoodie sleeve rumpled. She didn't look like the Solar who smiled for fans or the one HYBE polished for press releases. She looked raw. Anchored.

She thought about last night, about Yunjin's words, Chaewon's steady grip, Chaerin's firestorm promises. They all wanted to protect her too. But with Jungwon, it was different. His vow didn't feel like a choice—it felt like gravity. Like something outside of either of them, pulling them into an orbit neither could escape.

Her pulse jumped at the thought, half panic, half something dangerously close to relief.

She glanced up at him again. He hadn't moved far, still close enough that his presence wrapped around her like a shield. His expression had smoothed out, the storm he carried earlier quieted into something gentler, but his eyes stayed fixed on her like he was cataloguing every breath.

The intensity should've scared her. Once, it would've. But instead it steadied her.

"Okay," she whispered, not to him this time, but to herself. Like an agreement. Like permission.

She tugged her sleeve back down, covering the bandage again, but it didn't feel like hiding this time. It felt like a pause. Like she was allowed to put it away for a moment because someone else had seen it and hadn't turned away.

When she stepped past him, brushing close enough to feel the heat of his shoulder through the fabric of his hoodie, she didn't apologize for the space she took up. Didn't shrink herself to make the moment easier. She just moved, and he shifted with her, silent and unshakable, like her shadow had grown a body.



The van's headlights swept across the side of the HYBE building, catching in the glass panels before disappearing into the dark. Most of the staff had already cleared out, leaving the back lot hushed except for the low hum of AC units and the occasional slam of a stage door.

Jihoon leaned against the chain-link fence at the far end of the lot, hood pulled low. A cigarette burned slow between his fingers, the ember flaring each time he drew it in. The smoke curled lazy into the damp night air, carrying the sharp tang of something waiting.

He watched the door Solar had just disappeared through, lips twisting into the kind of smile that never reached his eyes.

From his pocket, his phone buzzed once. A new message lit the screen. Danielle.

She's wobbling. Just keep pressing. I'll keep the public busy.

Jihoon exhaled smoke through his teeth, thumb hovering over the keyboard. He typed, deleted, typed again. Settled on three words:

She's still mine.

He sent it, pocketed the phone, and dropped the cigarette butt to the pavement. The ember hissed out in a puddle.

For a long moment, he didn't move. Just stood there, jaw tight, eyes fixed on the building like it was prey he'd already caught.

Inside, laughter echoed faintly through the stairwell—a girl's voice bright, another sharp with teasing. He recognized Solar's tone even muffled by concrete, and something dark unfurled in his chest.

He pressed his hand flat against the fence, metal biting cold into his palm.

She thought she could run. Thought her sisters, her friends, even that kid from Enhypen could keep her safe.

But Jihoon had time.
And time, he knew, was a blade.

He pulled his hood lower and melted into the shadows, leaving the lot empty but not at peace.

















61: Stone and Shield

The morning after the latest music show carried the kind of quiet that wasn't really quiet at all. The dorm was full of small noises: the hum of the kettle, Eunchae's half-suppressed laughter at a TikTok, the shuffle of Kazuha stretching on the floor. It should have felt normal. It didn't.

Solar sat curled at the end of the couch, hoodie sleeves pulled over her hands, scrolling without really reading. The hashtags were still trending. Every flick of her thumb delivered another stranger's verdict: unstable, liability, unfit. She closed the app and pressed the phone face down on her knee like it might stop vibrating.

"Okay, team meeting." Chaewon's voice cut through the room with leader's clarity, even if her hair was still flattened from sleep. She set her mug on the coffee table and looked around until everyone was listening. "Jihoon is back in the picture. Danielle is escalating. We can't pretend anymore."

Eunchae's blanket slid off her shoulders as she sat up straighter. "Then we go public," she said, eyes fierce in a way that made her look older than seventeen. "Tell everyone exactly what he did."

"Absolutely not." Yunjin's tone was sharp, hands tightening around her own mug. "You think the internet is bad now? Imagine what they'll do if we drop that without proof. They'll shred her." She nodded toward Solar.

"It's not about the internet." Chaerin's voice came from the doorway, fire already in it. She'd shown up uninvited, hair still in a high ponytail from wherever she'd stormed in from. "It's about him. He hurt her once. He's hurting her again. You think I'm going to sit here while everyone sips tea and waits for him to move?"

Solar's stomach knotted. She wanted to tell her sister to lower her voice, but the words wouldn't come.

Jaehyun followed a beat later, silent until he dropped into the chair opposite. His sunglasses stayed on even indoors, his expression unreadable. When he spoke, it was in that low, formal tone that turned the room into a courtroom. "I'm already handling it."

Chaerin turned on him. "Handling it? With your shady backroom deals and 'contacts'? That's not handling, oppa, that's gambling with her life."

"And what would you do?" Jaehyun asked, calm as glass. "Throw fists? Light the building on fire? That kind of chaos doesn't protect her. It exposes her."

Chaerin's jaw clenched, but she didn't back down.

Yunjin glanced between them, then at Solar. "What about her choice in all this? Everyone's talking like she's not even in the room."

Solar forced a smile, thin and brittle. "I'm fine."

The word landed like ash. Nobody believed it.

Chaewon leaned forward, softer now. "Sol, you don't have to be fine. You just have to be honest with us. What do you want?"

Solar opened her mouth, then closed it. The truth caught in her throat. She wanted it to end. She wanted to stop being a headline. She wanted to stop being the reason everyone she loved was sharpening their weapons. But most of all, she wanted—

Her chest tightened. Jungwon's face flickered in her mind, the steady way he'd held her wrist like a vow without words.

"I just..." She swallowed. "I just want to protect everyone."

Chaerin swore under her breath. Jaehyun's head tilted slightly, the only sign he'd heard. Chaewon reached across the table, resting her hand over Solar's sleeve.

"We'll figure it out," Chaewon said firmly. "Together."

The kettle clicked off in the kitchen. The silence that followed wasn't relief. It was a truce. Temporary. Fragile.

And Solar felt the walls closing in, because she knew this wasn't protection anymore—it was crossfire.




The building had emptied the way it always did after music shows: first in waves of fans escorted out, then idols and stylists, then the skeletal staff left to clean up the wreckage of glitter and cables. By midnight, the hallways were hollow.

Jungwon should have been gone hours ago. Enhypen had finished their schedule and piled into vans, the others loud with laughter and exhaustion, but he'd told the manager he'd catch up. Nobody questioned him. He was always the steady one. Reliable. Predictable.

Not tonight.

His sneakers carried him down the back stairwell, every step echoing. His hands itched. The storm that Danielle had stirred in him days ago still hadn't settled, coiled tight in his chest. He needed an outlet, needed to burn it out before it scorched him alive. And somehow, he knew she would be here. People like her never left quietly.

When he pushed the exit door open, the alley was damp with drizzle, the pavement slick and shining under a single flickering streetlight. And there she was, exactly as he expected: Danielle, leaning against the hood of a staff car like it belonged to her, scrolling her phone with a smirk.

"You're persistent," she said without looking up.

Jungwon's jaw flexed. He shut the door behind him, the heavy click echoing like a gunshot. "Persistent?"

She finally glanced at him, her smile widening. "Solar's watchdog. Wherever she goes, there you are. I should start charging you rent for all the space you give me in your head."

He took a step closer. Then another. His shadow stretched long across the wet ground, swallowing the light between them.

"Stay away from her." His voice came low, flat, but it carried through the empty lot.

Danielle slipped her phone into her pocket, pushing off the car with a tilt of her head. "Or what? You'll growl at me again? Scowl me into submission? Please, Jungwon, you don't scare me."

The words were bait, and he knew it. But the anger still surged, unstoppable. He closed the last of the distance until only a meter separated them, his height and presence turning the space claustrophobic.

"You should be scared," he said, and this time his tone cracked—low, vulgar with fury.

Danielle blinked, her smile faltering for the first time.

"You think I don't know what you're doing?" Jungwon continued, his voice harsh. "The leaks. The rumors. Whispering to Jihoon like you're pulling the strings. You think it makes you powerful? It makes you pathetic."

Her jaw tightened. "Careful."

"No," he snapped, stepping closer still, forcing her back half a step against the car. "You don't get careful. You don't get to hide behind smirks and act like this is a game. You're destroying her life for what? Jealousy? Obsession? Tell me, Danielle—what do you win if she breaks?"

Danielle's eyes narrowed, but the mask was slipping. "She doesn't deserve—"

"Finish that sentence." His voice dropped to a growl, eyes sharp as blades.

The air between them bristled. Danielle swallowed, then smiled again, weaker now. "You're shaking."

He realized his fists were clenched so hard his nails bit into his palms. He forced them open, slowly, deliberately, and planted them on the car behind her, caging her in without touching. The rain dripping off the eaves pattered around them like a metronome.

"You're right," he said softly, dangerously. "I am shaking. Not because of you. Because of what I'll do if you don't stop."

For the first time, Danielle looked uncertain.

And then another voice slid through the alley.

"Well, well. The puppy's got teeth."

Jungwon's head snapped up. Jihoon stepped out from the shadowed corner of the lot, hood low, hands in his pockets like he'd been watching the whole time. His smirk mirrored Danielle's but carried more weight—older, meaner.

"Get away from her," Jungwon barked.

Jihoon chuckled, slow. "Protective, aren't you? Solar always did like boys who pretended to be knights. Makes her feel like she's worth saving."

The words hit like a fist. Jungwon's body surged forward on instinct, but he froze himself an inch short of lunging. That was what Jihoon wanted. A headline. A scandal. A weakness.

Instead, he straightened, forcing the fury back into control. "If you ever touch her again, if you even look at her the wrong way—I won't stop myself next time."

Danielle's smirk returned, emboldened by Jihoon at her side. "Big words for someone who's just a boy in a hoodie. You think threats make you dangerous?"

"I don't need threats," Jungwon said, every syllable like steel. "I need you to remember this moment. Remember that I'm watching. That I know. And the next time you try, it won't be words."

The silence that followed was thick, broken only by the drip of water from the roof.

Jihoon's smile widened. "You've got spirit. I'll give you that. But spirit doesn't win wars, kid. Shadows do. Secrets do. And I have plenty."

Jungwon's chest heaved once, twice. He wanted to punch that smirk off Jihoon's face, wanted to drag Danielle by the wrist to the edge of the building and demand she explain herself to the world. But he forced himself back. Solar couldn't afford his recklessness. Not here. Not now.

So he leaned closer, eyes locked on Jihoon's, his voice a blade: "Try me."

Then he turned, yanking open the exit door, the slam reverberating through the alley.

The moment his back was turned, his chest heaved with all the rage he hadn't released. His hands still shook. His body felt like a live wire, ready to snap. But under it all, one thing crystallized sharp and certain:

He wasn't just Solar's shadow anymore. He was her shield. Her weapon.

And Jihoon had just learned that firsthand.



The elevator hummed like a low threat as it sank four floors into the parking garage. Jungwon watched the numbers stutter by without actually seeing them, fists working open and closed at his sides to bleed out the tremor that wouldn't leave. The alley rain had dried in a cold sheen along his hoodie sleeves; the fabric clung to his forearms as if the night were still holding on.

The doors parted to concrete and exhaust. Sodium lights cast the garage in a bruise-yellow glow, striping the pillars like warning tape. Somewhere, a van idled, engine ticking over with the steady patience of people paid to wait.

"Jungwon."

The voice came from his left—calm, clipped, unmistakable. He turned.

Jaehyun stepped out from the shadow between two black SUVs like he'd been carved from it. Sunglasses on, at midnight, under a roof; the kind of man who wore armor as habit, not affect. He didn't need to raise his voice to fill the space. The sound of his name in Jaehyun's mouth felt like paperwork being stamped: official, final.

Of course he knows. Jungwon didn't ask how. Men like Jaehyun didn't find out; they were told, quickly.

"What do you want." Jungwon kept it flat, aiming for even and landing closer to exhausted.

Jaehyun didn't answer right away. He took him in the way a surgeon appraises a wound: the damp hoodie, the scraped knuckles Jungwon hadn't noticed yet, the vibration still living under his skin.

"You went to them," Jaehyun said. Not a question.

"I warned them." Jungwon met his sunglasses like they were eyes. "I told them to stay away."

Jaehyun's mouth made the smallest movement—something that might once have been a smile but had since retired. "And you believe warnings work on men like Jihoon."

Jungwon's jaw flexed. "It wasn't for him."

"For who, then? For the cameras? For yourself?" Jaehyun's head tipped a fraction. "Or was it so Solar could sleep tonight thinking you've put a leash on her monsters?"

Heat flared again under Jungwon's skin, gusting the embers Danielle had left there. He stepped closer, stopping a meter away—close enough to smell the sterile cologne, the cool scent of leather from the SUV behind Jaehyun, the faint trace of cigarette smoke that didn't belong to either of them.

"I don't need you to approve of me," Jungwon said.

"And I don't need you to involve yourself," Jaehyun returned, same tone, same volume, each word neat as a cut. "Especially not like that."

"Like what."

"Loud. Visible. Reckless."

Jungwon laughed once, humorless. "Visible is the point. He thrives in quiet. He counts on it." He didn't say like you do. He didn't need to; it hung there anyway, a blade with both their fingerprints.

Jaehyun removed his sunglasses slowly—as if the gesture itself were a warning—and folded them into his breast pocket. His eyes, bare, were not kinder. They were tired in a way Jungwon recognized, the kind of tired that doesn't sleep. "You misunderstand what quiet is for."

"Enlighten me."

"Quiet is a stage," Jaehyun said. "Not a hiding place. You move first when no one can see, and when the lights go up, the ending looks inevitable." He glanced past Jungwon at the elevator, at the camera in the corner whose tiny red LED was conspicuously dark. "I had this floor's CCTV looped twenty minutes ago. Your 'warning' will not exist on tape. You're welcome."

Jungwon's shoulders tensed instinctively, a defense against gratitude he didn't want to owe. "You were watching."

"I am always watching." The words would have sounded sinister from anyone else. From Jaehyun, they were simply inventory. "Because I know what it costs when I don't."

He said it without inflection, which made it worse. History lived in that line—years neither of them had shared and both could feel.

"I don't like your methods," Jungwon said.

"I don't require your endorsement." A flick of Jaehyun's gaze toward Jungwon's hands, the scrape along one knuckle beading a thin line of red. "I require your discretion."

Jungwon, who had spent the last hours strapping himself into restraint like a bomb harness, felt something stubborn go rigid in his spine. "I'm not lying to her."

"You already are."

The air thinned. Jungwon didn't move. "I haven't told her what happened tonight because she has a show at nine in the morning and sleep is not your specialty."

Jaehyun considered him for a beat, weighing the line for fault. "You think a single vanishing act of truth will save her. It won't. But your theatrics will hurt her." He stepped closer. He was not taller than Jungwon, but he occupied space as if the room conceded it. "He knows your name now in the context that matters. He will use you as a lever. Danielle already is."

"That's on me," Jungwon said. "Not her."

Jaehyun's lips thinned. "That's not how leverage works." He angled his head, and for the first time the veneer cracked, not with anger—something sharper, protective enough to cut. "Jihoon knows about our family. He knows where to sink a knife so it bleeds on every newspaper, not just us. Every move I make, I weigh whether it will trigger that blade. And you—" his gaze sliced back to Jungwon— "walked into his frame and handed him a second handle."

Jungwon's breath came short, a clean hit. He refused to step back. "So your answer is what? Wait until he decides the timing suits him? Let him choose the moment that breaks her."

"My answer is control the moment," Jaehyun said. The word control didn't shirk in his mouth; it sat there like a tool on a workbench. "I have the means to do that. Connections you do not. People who owe me favors. People who will never put Solar's name in a sentence they utter aloud."

"People like the ones who were 'watching' me tonight," Jungwon said, letting the contempt show.

Jaehyun didn't flinch. "Yes."

"And when he uses what he knows?" Jungwon pushed. "When he drags your past into the sun, yours and Chaerin's and Solar's—what then? What will your quiet buy you?"

There it was—the thing nobody said out loud in rooms with microphones. Jaehyun's face didn't change, but a nerve ticked in his jaw. "It buys time."

"Time is what he uses to hurt her," Jungwon said, and heard, in his own voice, the low rip of something breaking. "Every day we stand perfectly still is a day he gets to decide where she bleeds."

A door thumped at the far end of the garage. Neither of them looked. The world had shrunk to the two of them and the stripe of dirty light between them like a boundary that kept moving but never vanished.

"Do you love her." Jaehyun didn't ask it with curiosity. He asked it like a line in a contract that needed a signature.

Jungwon could have lied. He could have said that's not your business, could have deflected behind the righteous blandness idols are taught to wear in interviews. He didn't. "Yes." The word hit the concrete and stayed.

Something cooled in Jaehyun's eyes. Not disdain. Not approval. Calculation adjusting its variables. "Then you will do as I say."

Jungwon's laugh was a cut. "No."

"You're a child," Jaehyun said almost gently, which landed as insult anyway. "A good one. Disciplined. But a child to this kind of war. You don't yet understand the difference between a gesture that satisfies your conscience and a move that wins the board."

Jungwon took a step that erased the safe meter, until he could count the pores along the bridge of Jaehyun's nose. "I understand the difference between needing to be seen and needing to be believed. She needs to see me. Right now. She believes you by default. That's what stone buys—a presumption. I'm not stone." He let the next line be a blade. "And she doesn't need stone. She needs someone who bleeds when she does."

For a heartbeat, there was silence. Then: a flicker ran through Jaehyun—recognition, against his will. Chaerin's words from the night before, caught in a new mouth. He masked it quickly, but not before Jungwon saw the hit land.

Jaehyun spoke without acknowledging it. "Bleeding is not strategy."

"It's proof." Jungwon didn't blink. "And sometimes proof is what keeps a person from drowning while you negotiate for lifeboats."

A muscle in Jaehyun's cheek ticked. He turned slightly, as if to give himself space to leash whatever he wanted to say next. Over his shoulder, one of the SUVs' drivers stood at parade-rest beside the hood, eyes down, body tuned to a frequency of invisibility that was its own kind of violence. A life adjacent to rooms like this, conversations like this.

Jaehyun's phone buzzed. He didn't look. "Here is what will happen," he said, as if the buzz were punctuation. "You will not approach Jihoon again. You will not touch Danielle. If you see either of them, you walk away. You will not give them tape. You will not give them witnesses. You will not give them you."

"And if I do?" Jungwon asked, genuine curiosity ice-edged against the heat in his chest.

Jaehyun's eyes hardened into something that looked like family history, like promises made over hospital beds and doorways and court steps. "Then I make you disappear from her life."

The threat didn't land like bravado. It landed like logistics. Jungwon pictured it in an instant: a call; a schedule change; a PR line about conflicting commitments; a corridor that folded around Solar until he couldn't find the door anymore. It was almost elegant, the way Jaehyun's protection and danger wore the same clothes.

For a second, Jungwon wanted to swing. Not to hurt Jaehyun; to puncture the air, to make sound in a room that ate it. He didn't. Instead he said, "Try."

Jaehyun's head cocked, the barest click of respect—or warning. "Don't make me."

Silence again, wider now, the kind that felt like the breath before a verdict.

Jungwon broke it first, because he understood something about theaters and audiences too. He stepped back a half pace, enough to show he wasn't going to put his hands on someone Solar loved. "I won't tell her about tonight," he said. "Not now. Not until I can do it without making the fear worse." He watched Jaehyun's face, saw the calculation approve the shape if not the source. "But I'm not leaving. I'm not shrinking so your plan fits cleaner on paper."

"It's not on paper," Jaehyun said. "It's carved into people."

"And people bleed," Jungwon said. "Which means I'm qualified."

The smallest breath of a laugh moved through Jaehyun, there and gone. "You are stubborn."

"You are, too." Jungwon let his hands fall open at his sides, empty. He could feel the alley still in his muscles, the way Danielle's smile had wanted him to trip. He would not do that here. "You love her like a wall. I love her like a door. She needs both."

Jaehyun stilled. The metaphor sat between them like a coin with two faces. A car somewhere on the far level coughed to life; a flock of echoes took flight up the ramps.

Finally, Jaehyun said, "If she cries because of you, I'll end you." It was not a threat. It was a covenant.

Jungwon nodded once. "If she bleeds because of you, I will." He didn't need to raise his voice. The promise knew its own weight.

For the first time, the tension shifted, not easing—tilting. They were still opposite, but on the same slope. Jaehyun looked at him and seemed to see a new variable he hadn't accounted for, not entirely.

His phone buzzed again. This time he checked it. A name flashed so quickly Jungwon couldn't catch it. Jaehyun's jaw flexed as he read, a tiny movement that meant a large thing. "He's moving," he said, almost to himself. Then, to Jungwon: "Go home."

"No."

"Then go upstairs," Jaehyun amended, a concession so small it barely existed. "Stay visible where it is safe for you to be seen. I will handle the rest."

Jungwon wanted to laugh at safe. Nothing felt safe in a building where shadows had names and phone numbers. But he understood the instruction for what it was: keep Solar within eyeshot, within arm's reach, where Jaehyun's cameras and contracts could triangulate him. Be a piece on the board that the other player could account for—no surprises, not from him.

"Fine," he said, and the word tasted like surrender until he realized it wasn't. It was a position.

Jaehyun slid his sunglasses back on, which somehow felt like closing a door. "And Jungwon—" He waited until he had his attention. "If you intend to be part of her life, you will learn discretion as fluently as you speak devotion."

"I intend," Jungwon said. "To be part of her life."

They stood for one more heartbeat, the kind that stretches and thins but doesn't break. Then Jaehyun turned and rapped twice on the SUV hood. The driver moved like rewind, opening the door, the vehicle absorbing Jaehyun like a dark mouth. The engine purred angrier, then softened.

Jungwon didn't watch it go. He looked at his scraped knuckle instead, pressed his thumb to the bead of blood until it smeared away. A mark that meant he'd chosen restraint and it had still cost him something.

He headed for the elevator. At the last second, he looked up at the dead red LED above the camera lens, at the blank eye. He thought of Danielle's alley smile and Jihoon's voice slipping into the night like oil. He thought of Solar's wrist in his hand, the way relief had moved through her like heat when he said nothing at all and promised everything.

When the elevator doors opened, he stepped in and pressed the button for the lobby. As the doors slid shut, the garage narrowed to a slice. In it, he caught the reflection of his own face—tired, angry, set—and something else behind it: resolve, bright enough to cast its own light.

He would tell Solar the truth. Not tonight, when fear would make it a knife. Soon, when it could be a shield. He would stand where Jaehyun's cameras could see him and where Jihoon's reach couldn't, and he would learn the shape of discretion not to appease a brother but to keep a promise to a girl who had finally let herself lean.

Upstairs, the elevator chimed. A corridor waited—fluorescent washes, taped marks on the floor, a world of performance. Jungwon stepped out into it not as a boy who hovered, but as a line in the script that could no longer be crossed out.









62: Before the Fall

The dorm smelled like home the way only girl-group dorms could: faint citrus cleaner clinging to the floorboards, a ghost of hairspray, takeout boxes collapsed in the recycling bin. The hum of the washer ran in the background, overpowered by Eunchae's laughter spilling from the living room, where she and Kazuha were building some absurd tower out of water bottles.

Chaerin sat cross-legged on the couch, scrolling her phone with one hand, twirling her ponytail with the other. She was dressed like she'd come straight from the club—oversized Gucci jacket sliding off one shoulder, rings clicking against her screen—but her eyes kept darting toward her older brother near the door.

Jaehyun hadn't moved in ten minutes. He stood rooted like a sentry, long black coat buttoned despite the heater, sunglasses still on as if daylight could burst through the ceiling at any moment. His phone glowed in his hand, thumb gliding in deliberate motions. No one knew if he was texting contacts, blackmailing someone, or just scrolling the news. His presence made the air feel colder.

Solar hovered in the middle of the room, torn between wanting to laugh with Eunchae and the weight in her chest that had only grown since the rooftop. She pressed her sleeve down over her wrist without thinking, trying not to notice how Jaehyun's silence swallowed every other sound.

The dorm door was cracked for air. A knock, gentle but distinct, tapped against it.

Everyone's heads lifted.

Solar's heart startled in her chest.

The door nudged open, and Jungwon appeared in the frame. Hoodie, cap low, a convenience store bag hanging from his hand. His knock had been an announcement, not a request. He wasn't barging in—just letting them know he was there.

"Sorry," he said softly, almost awkward. "Door was open."

Solar rose before she realized she'd moved, relief blooming across her face like light through glass. He wasn't supposed to be here—not really—but somehow she'd been waiting for him anyway.

"You came," she breathed, and though it wasn't quite an invitation, it felt like an answer.

Jungwon's mouth twitched at the corner. He held up the bag. "Didn't know if you ate. Grabbed food. Peach Tea too."

Eunchae abandoned her tower instantly, bouncing toward him with unfiltered delight. "Now it's a party! Hi again, HYBE sibling!" she sang, peeking into the bag like it was treasure.

Kazuha's lips curved in her quiet way. "Always so thoughtful," she said simply, her words carrying more weight than their softness suggested.

Solar felt her ears heat. She wanted to tell them not to make it obvious—but part of her was glad they saw it.

Yunjin leaned against the back of the couch, arms crossed, a sly glint in her eyes. "Brave of you to walk straight into our dorm," she teased. "Most guys would've lost their nerve at the door."

The room's laughter broke the tension a little, but Solar's pulse wouldn't slow.

Because behind them, Jaehyun had slid his phone into his coat pocket. He hadn't spoken, but the weight of his stare—dark behind tinted lenses—shifted the room's gravity. Jungwon didn't flinch. He met the invisible line Jaehyun cast and held it, steady as stone.

The girls exchanged quick glances, and Solar felt it too: two different kinds of protection colliding in the same air. Wolves circling each other, and she was standing between them.



Danielle's reflection smiled back at her from the café window, the glass slick with evening condensation. She tilted her phone until the light hit just right, capturing the faint shadow of her grin, and pressed save. Another photo to feed the account when the time was right.

Across from her, Jihoon leaned back in his chair, one arm draped lazily along the top rail, the other stirring his untouched iced Americano. He hadn't looked at her once since they sat down, eyes instead fixed on the street outside, where strangers scrolled by with umbrellas half-cocked against a drizzle.

"You're sulking," Danielle said sing-song, nudging his shoe under the table.

"I'm calculating," he corrected, voice low, a curl of amusement in it. "Big difference."

She twirled her straw through melting ice, impatience painting her features. "You saw him, didn't you? Jungwon. He's already cracking. That fury in his eyes? Delicious." She licked the corner of her lip like she'd tasted it. "He's easier to bait than I thought."

Jihoon's mouth twitched. Not quite a smile. "You think you're clever for poking a kid who doesn't know his own leash."

"He's not a kid." Danielle leaned forward, elbows on the table, eyes bright and unblinking. "He's dangerous in all the right ways. To her. To ENHYPEN. To everything they've built around him. And all it takes is a nudge." She slid her phone across the table. On the screen, a folder of blurry photos—some real, some edited. A hooded boy at night, a girl with a mic pack slung over her shoulder, a convenience store sign glowing behind them. "People already want to believe they're sneaking around. Why not give them what they're hungry for?"

Jihoon tapped the screen with one finger, lazy but precise. "Half of these wouldn't survive a reverse image search."

"They won't care," Danielle shot back. "The story matters more than the proof. Plant a seed, water it with just enough scandal, and watch it grow into fact." She leaned closer, her perfume cutting through the bitter coffee air. "Imagine the headlines: Le Sserafim's Solar dragging ENHYPEN's leader down with her. The golden boy, ruined because he couldn't stay away from a mess."

For a long moment, Jihoon didn't answer. Then he sipped his coffee and let the ice clink. "Mess is a good word for you."

Danielle only smiled, sharp and unbothered.

He set the cup down with deliberate care. "Solar's already hanging by a thread. HYBE's statement bought her sympathy, but sympathy doesn't last. One more blow and even her group will feel it. Do that, and the backlash could scorch us too. I prefer timing."

"Timing bores me."

"That's why you're reckless." He finally looked at her, eyes dark, flat as stone. "You play for reaction. I play for collapse."

The words carried weight that silenced her smile for a moment. Danielle tilted her head, studying him. "Collapse takes too long. People want spectacle."

"You'll have spectacle." He leaned forward, voice dropping until it was almost a whisper, meant to curl around her ear. "But it'll be when I decide. When she thinks she can breathe again. That's when we'll take it from her."

Danielle's grin slithered back. "And what about her shiny new protector? Do we ruin him too, or let him burn himself out?"

Jihoon's gaze flicked to her phone again. The folder of photos. "He's already a liability. Leverage is only useful if you know how to pull it. And Danielle—" he paused, savoring the moment, "—you're too eager to yank. Patience."

"Patience," she echoed, mocking, but her eyes glimmered with mischief. "Fine. I'll wait. But when the stage is set, I want front row seats."

"Trust me." Jihoon picked up his coffee again, ice rattling like dice. "You'll get more than that."

Outside, the drizzle thickened into rain, streaking the glass and blurring the world into watercolor. Danielle traced a heart into the condensation with one fingertip, then crossed it out with a slash.




The dorm was loud in the way only shared spaces could be—Eunchae's laughter bubbling like a fountain in the living room, Yunjin humming under her breath as she scrolled through something on her phone, the low drone of Jaehyun's voice as he spoke into his headset in the entryway. Even when the girls tried to keep it down, the walls vibrated with their presence.

Solar's pulse still hadn't slowed since Jungwon stepped through the door. She'd accepted the bag from him earlier—Peach Tea tucked safely onto the counter like an offering no one dared to touch—but the static in the air hadn't dissipated. If anything, it pressed tighter, Jaehyun's silence sharpening against Jungwon's quiet solidity.

Solar felt the weight of both of them on her shoulders, and for once she wanted to shrug it off.

"Do you..." She turned to Jungwon, words hesitant, too loud in her throat. "Do you want to come to my room? Just to—talk."

The girls looked up instantly, Eunchae with wide, cartoon eyes, Yunjin with a grin sharp enough to cut. Chaewon raised an eyebrow but said nothing, and Kazuha tilted her head as if weighing the meaning of Solar's tone.

Jungwon glanced around once, registering every stare, and then nodded. "Sure."

Solar exhaled like she'd been holding her breath for hours. She led the way down the short hall, past Sakura's door with its line of neatly arranged shoes, past Eunchae's room where a poster hung crooked, until she reached her own. She nudged it open with her shoulder, stepping aside so he could follow.

The door clicked shut behind them, muffling the world into a manageable hum.

Solar's room was smaller than the weight it carried. The moment Jungwon stepped inside, his eyes swept over the space, cataloging each detail—not as someone unfamiliar with trophies and glossy posters, but as someone who knew exactly what they meant.

The shelves gleamed with evidence of two lives pressed into one: music show awards, fan-made collages of Le Sserafim, glossy posters of Solar in stage outfits, her face sharpened by studio lighting. Next to them sat a row of plaques and medals from another world entirely: Overwatch tournaments, player-of-the-match titles, the kind of recognition most idols never touched.

And there, newly placed among them, was a Grand Finals trophy. The 2025 San Francisco Shock's victory solidified in glass and gold.

Jungwon stared at it for a long beat. His reflection warped in its curves, bent and multiplied. He'd stood on stages too, held trophies under burning lights, but seeing hers—the collision of two impossible careers—made his chest twist.

His gaze slid next to her PC setup: dual monitors still humming in standby, headset hung neatly, keyboard edges worn from practice. It was a soldier's station in the middle of a starlet's shrine. He didn't look overwhelmed—just steady, quietly absorbing.

Solar's throat went dry under the weight of his silence. With Rascal, she remembered the almost existential shock that had rippled through him at the sight of her room. Jungwon, though... he didn't look like he was drowning. He looked like he understood the storm, because he lived in one too.

She sat at the edge of her bed, tugging her sleeves down, watching him. "Not what you expected?"

Jungwon's mouth curved faintly, eyes still on the Grand Finals trophy. "It's exactly what I expected. And still... it's a lot."

Solar laughed softly, without humor. "It always is."

He took the chair at her desk, spinning it toward her, posture loose but gaze intent. Silence filled the space until Solar broke it, voice low. "I've been protecting people my whole life. My sisters. My group. Even staff sometimes. It's just... what I do." Her eyes dropped to her hands, fingers fidgeting in her sleeves. "I don't know how to stop. Even when it tears me apart, I don't know how."

Jungwon leaned forward, elbows on his knees, gaze sharp. "Then let me be the one who doesn't stop." His voice carried heat, not loud but so fierce it scraped against the walls. "If you won't put yourself first, then I will. That's not a choice for you to make anymore, Sol. Not with me here."

Her heart clenched. The possessiveness in his tone—raw, vulgar in its certainty—should have scared her. Once, it would have. But right now, it steadied her like nothing else.

Her wrist pulsed beneath the sleeve, a dull ache she'd learned to ignore. She shifted unconsciously, rubbing it, and Jungwon's eyes tracked the motion instantly.

Without a word, he reached into the convenience bag he'd set down. Solar blinked as he pulled out a small brace, simple but sturdy, still folded in its packaging. He set it on the desk like it was nothing. "For your wrist. I knew you wouldn't do it yourself."

Solar's breath caught. She reached out slowly, fingertips brushing the edge of the package. "You thought of this?"

"I notice things," he said simply. His tone made it sound less like care and more like inevitability.

She swallowed, something tight unraveling in her chest. "Thank you."

He shook his head, leaning closer now, his presence filling the narrow space between bed and desk. "Don't thank me. Just let me keep showing up."

The words landed heavy, sinking deep. And then—hesitant but deliberate—his fingers grazed her jawline, a ghost of a touch. The pad of his thumb lingered just barely at her cheekbone, light enough to leave her wondering if she imagined it.

Solar froze, not out of fear but because of how badly she didn't want to move. Her breath hitched, eyelids fluttering once before she steadied them open. The faintest warmth bloomed where his touch had been, a tether across skin.

Jungwon drew his hand back as if nothing happened, but his eyes told a different story: sharp, burning, like he was holding the line of restraint with both fists.

Solar exhaled slowly, every nerve in her body buzzing. She'd spent weeks recoiling from intensity, from pressure. And here she was, craving it.

She whispered, voice trembling but certain: "Okay."

Jungwon's gaze didn't waver. For a heartbeat, neither of them moved. The room was too small, too quiet, their breaths the only sound.

Solar tried to look away, to ease the pull, but her eyes betrayed her. They flicked to his mouth before darting back up, heat flooding her face.

Jungwon noticed. She knew he did. His jaw flexed, a tiny tell, and his fingers twitched like he wanted to touch her again—more than just the graze across her cheek.

He leaned forward an inch, close enough that she felt the warmth of his breath against her skin. Not invasive, not demanding—just there. Waiting.

Her pulse thundered in her ears. She could smell the faint sweetness of Peach Tea still clinging to his hoodie, could see the faint shadow of exhaustion under his eyes, could feel the storm he was holding back in his body.

If she shifted forward even slightly, the space between them would disappear.

Her hand lifted halfway before she caught herself, fingers curling into her sleeve. She wasn't ready. She didn't know if she'd ever be ready. But God, she wanted to.

Jungwon pulled back the barest fraction, restraint written into every line of his posture. His voice was rough when he finally spoke: "Not yet."

The words weren't rejection. They were a promise.

Solar's chest tightened, but the knot under her ribs loosened at the same time. She nodded once, her throat too thick for words.

He stood then, slow and deliberate, giving her the dignity of space again. But before he turned, his hand brushed her shoulder lightly, grounding her.

"Brace your wrist," he murmured. "And... rest, if you can."

It was so mundane compared to the fire that had just filled the room that she almost laughed. Almost. Instead, she clung to the warmth where his touch lingered and whispered, softer than before: "Okay."

When the door shut behind him, the silence roared.

The quiet of her room clung to her even after Jungwon slipped out. Solar sat for a moment longer, palm pressed against her cheek where his fingers had lingered, before she finally forced herself to stand. Her pulse was still uneven, her thoughts tangled with everything she couldn't name.

The hallway was louder, reality resuming the second she opened her door. Eunchae and Kazuha were still in the living room, laughing over their collapsed bottle tower. Sakura had returned from practice with her hair damp, humming under her breath as she folded laundry into neat piles.

But it was the other two who anchored the space.

Chaerin was pacing, phone in hand, her voice sharp even though she wasn't speaking to anyone on the other end. "We should just call it out. Expose him. Expose them. Drag it into daylight where he can't twist it." Her words bit at the air, fury dressed as logic.

Jaehyun stood by the doorway like a statue, long coat still buttoned, sunglasses unmoved. His hands were clasped behind his back now, phone tucked away, his silence heavier than Chaerin's volume. When she finally paused for breath, he said, "No."

"No?" Chaerin snapped, spinning on him. "That's all you have to say? You saw her wrist, Jaehyun. You know exactly what he's doing."

His jaw tightened, but his voice remained flat. "And you think shouting about it will protect her? Exposure invites scrutiny. Scrutiny digs deeper. Jihoon knows things that don't belong on record. If you force his hand, he'll drag everything down with him."

Chaerin threw her hands up. "So we just sit here and wait? Pretend he doesn't exist? Hope he gets bored?"

"Control the variables," Jaehyun said, each word clipped, measured. "Move in the shadows. Quietly. Cleanly. That's how you dismantle someone like him."

Chaerin laughed, sharp and humorless. "Shadows. You mean your backdoor deals and your dirty strings. You think you can out-snake a snake? He's already slithering through cracks we can't see."

His voice dropped colder. "And Jungwon is no better. Charging in with his temper. Reckless. Visible. He'll make her a target faster than Jihoon ever could."

Solar flinched like he'd said it to her directly.

Chaerin's eyes flashed. "At least Jungwon's willing to fight for her. Not hide behind his coat and his cryptic contacts."

They were both shouting now, voices overlapping. Chaerin with heat and rage, Jaehyun with ice and certainty, their styles clashing until the room vibrated with it.

Solar stood in the middle of it, her sleeve tugged down tight over her wrist, her chest pulling too tight to breathe. It hit her then—not just the danger outside their walls, but the danger inside.

Her siblings weren't fighting Jihoon. Not really. They were fighting each other. And she was the excuse.

"They'll burn each other down," she thought, her throat aching, "before they let me break."

The laughter from the couch dulled into silence. Eunchae and Kazuha looked between them, wide-eyed. Even Sakura had stopped folding, the laundry limp in her hands.

No one moved to stop it. Not yet.

Because in that moment, Solar saw clearly: the war outside wasn't half as volatile as the one inside her own walls.

The dorm had gone still. Laughter and bickering had burned themselves out, the television left humming faintly in the living room as someone forgot to turn it off. Behind closed doors, her members' breaths had evened into sleep.

Solar lay curled on her side, hoodie pulled over her head, the dark pressing close around her. The kind of silence that should have been restful sat heavy instead, every muscle too aware of itself.

Her phone buzzed against the nightstand.

Her heart leapt stupidly—Minji? Jungwon?—but the screen showed neither. Just a notification from an account she didn't recognize. No profile picture. No posts. Just a name stitched together from nonsense letters.

She unlocked it with reluctant fingers.

A photo filled the screen. Blurry, grainy, but unmistakable: her, slipping out the side door of the music show. Jungwon half a step behind, hood up, his hand raised like he might have been reaching for her shoulder. The angle was crooked, as if shot from a hiding place.

The caption was only six words:

She's slipping. She'll drag him down with her.

Solar's stomach dropped, cold blooming through her chest.

Was it real? Doctored? She couldn't tell. It didn't matter. Someone was watching. Someone wanted this out.

The screen glowed against her face, pale light carving the dread deeper. She locked the phone, flipped it over, but the image had already burned itself into her mind—Jungwon's hooded figure at her back, her own body caught mid-step like she'd been running toward something or away.

She pressed the heels of her hands against her eyes, willing the image away, willing the fear away. But the words echoed anyway.

She'll drag him down with her. In the dark, Solar lay awake, pulse pounding, the silence no longer her own.

























63: Rumors in the Dark

The dorm should have been quiet.

Everyone else had given in to exhaustion hours ago—Chaewon slipping into her room with her nightly cup of tea, Sakura folding her laundry until the hum of the dryer lulled her, Eunchae whispering jokes until even Yunjin stopped answering. Now the hall was still, soft with the rise and fall of sleeping breaths behind closed doors.

But Solar lay awake, eyes open to the dark, the glow of her phone a square of harsh light against her face.

The anonymous photo had sunk into her chest like a stone. Blurry, grainy, maybe real, maybe not—but it didn't matter. The words beneath it mattered more.

She's slipping. She'll drag him down with her.

She'd flipped the phone over a dozen times already, shoved it under her pillow, tried to trick herself into ignoring it. Every time she closed her eyes, though, the image burned brighter—the curve of Jungwon's hood, her own body caught mid-step like she'd been running away. Or toward. She couldn't even tell anymore.

The group chat buzzed once, the soft chirp startling in the silence. Solar unlocked the phone and blinked at Eunchae's latest post: a filtered selfie with her stuffed bunny perched on her head, captioned "our dorm mascot approves of UNFORGIVEN era."

Yunjin had replied first: mascot needs to pay rent.
Kazuha's thumbs-up emoji glowed serene under it.
Chaewon's curt sleep landed like a final word.

Solar smiled faintly, but the warmth faded quickly. She typed nothing, just watched the thread scroll by, her members cushioning her in ways they didn't even realize. They'd been orbiting her differently these past few days—more hands on her elbow, more eyes watching for signs she might tip. It was protection, she knew. Love.

But under it, she felt fragile, like glass held too tightly.

She set the phone on her chest and stared at the ceiling. The city hummed beyond the window, Seoul never entirely sleeping, but inside it felt like the air was holding its breath.

Her mind replayed the week in loops: Jihoon's shadow, Danielle's smirk, Jaehyun and Chaerin's shouting match that afternoon, Jungwon's fingers brushing her jawline. Too much, all at once.

She turned on her side, tugging the blanket over her head, willing the noise to stop. Instead, her pulse quickened, as if her body knew what her thoughts wouldn't say out loud. She wasn't just scared for herself anymore. She was scared of what she might cost the people around her.

The photo's caption returned unbidden, cruel and simple: She'll drag him down with her.

Solar squeezed her eyes shut, but the dark didn't offer mercy.



The music show's backstage hallway buzzed like a hive. Stagehands in black darted between dressing rooms with coiled cables over their shoulders, staffers barked reminders about camera cues, idols shuffled past with jackets half-zipped and faces half-painted. It was the same chaos Solar had walked through a hundred times, but today it felt louder.

Because today, the whispers weren't just about camera-blocks and encore outfits.

"They said it's real," a girl from a rookie group murmured, her phone tilted toward her friend. "Look, the hood matches. It has to be him."

Her friend frowned. "Or it's doctored. You know how people are. But... if it is real—" She cut herself off, eyes darting to where Solar was passing.

Solar kept her gaze forward, steps even, but her stomach twisted.

The whispers followed like static. Words like scandal, reckless, inappropriate crackled at the edges of her hearing. Some tones were pitying, some sharp, some giddy with the thrill of gossip. Every syllable dug under her skin, not because she believed them but because everyone else seemed to.

Eunchae, walking beside her, clocked the shift instantly. She widened her eyes theatrically, leaned closer, and stage-whispered just loud enough: "Unnie, did you know people are talking about your dating life? How dare they." She flipped her hair, exaggerated and bubbly, her grin breaking through the tension like a sparkler.

Solar's lips twitched despite herself. "Not helping," she muttered.

"Helping exactly the right amount," Eunchae shot back, bumping her shoulder lightly.

Ahead, Yunjin had already noticed. Her gaze flicked to the rookie girls, sharp as a blade. "Keep walking," she said under her breath, low enough for Solar to hear but pitched to carry. The gossipers shrank back against the wall instantly, suddenly fascinated by their shoelaces.

Solar's chest eased half an inch. Yunjin's protectiveness wasn't subtle, but it worked.

They rounded the corner toward the lineup for rehearsal—and that's when she saw him.

Jungwon.

He stood down the hall, just outside Enhypen's dressing room, hood pushed back, his expression unreadable. To anyone else, he looked calm, almost bored. But Solar saw the stiffness in his shoulders, the way his gaze lingered on anyone whispering too close. He didn't move toward her, didn't break formation with his group's manager hovering nearby. But he didn't look away either.

The moment stretched, quiet in her chest even as the hallway swarmed.

Her pulse picked up.

When their eyes finally met, it was only for a second. Long enough for her to know he'd heard the rumors too. Long enough to feel that steady weight settle inside her again, like an anchor dropping through rough water.

Yunjin caught the flicker of Solar's blush and leaned close, whispering, "Focus. Don't give them a show."

Solar nodded, but the warmth at the base of her throat betrayed her anyway.

By the time their group was called toward the stage entrance, she forced her steps back into rhythm, back into idol timing. Still, the memory of Jungwon's presence down the hall clung to her, quiet but unshakable.



The hallway pressed like a tide—voices, footsteps, the constant shuffle of stagehands moving set pieces in narrow space. Solar kept her eyes low, her hood half-pulled, every sound rubbing at the raw edge of her nerves. The photo's caption looped in her mind like a curse: She'll drag him down with her.

She felt him before she saw him.

A shift in the air, a stillness threading itself into the chaos. Jungwon slid into step beside her, quiet, deliberate. Not hiding. Not pretending coincidence. His shoulder was just close enough that she could sense the heat through fabric, and the sound of her pulse drowned the backstage noise for a beat.

Her stomach knotted. Everyone had seen the whispers online. Everyone had an opinion. Being seen with him again—openly, like this—was reckless. Dangerous. She knew it.

But her lungs... her lungs unclenched anyway.

She risked a glance up. His hood was down, his expression unreadable but steady. He didn't look at her immediately, just at the crowd ahead, like he was measuring every angle of threat. When his gaze did cut toward her, it was only for a second. Enough to settle something inside her she hadn't realized was shaking.

Yunjin clocked him immediately, lips curling into a smile that was equal parts teasing and approval. "Rumors won't stop this tiger," she murmured, low enough for only their group to hear.

Eunchae's grin bloomed wide, but for once she didn't chirp a greeting—she just giggled behind her hand, eyes sparkling like she was watching a drama unfold live.

Solar's face burned hot, and she yanked her hood lower. "Don't," she muttered, but the defensiveness lacked bite.

Finally, Jungwon spoke, voice pitched just for her.
"Let them talk," he said, low and sure. "I'm not leaving you to face this alone."

The words hit sharp, carving past her worry. Her instinct screamed about the risk—photos, headlines, damage control. But her body betrayed her, loosening in his orbit, soaking up the steadiness he carried like armor.

By the time they reached the stage wings, she wasn't lighter. But she was steadier. And that was enough to breathe.

Danielle leaned against the wall outside her own dressing room, phone angled low in her palm. The hallway buzzed with the same frantic rhythm—staff calling cues, idols brushing past in a blur of sequins and eyeliner—but she existed outside it, untouchable.

On her screen, the photo glowed again. Grainy, blurred, nothing and everything all at once. She scrolled through the reposts with lazy precision: fan accounts dissecting the angle, forums spinning it into conspiracy, hashtags climbing. It spread like wildfire, and all she had to do was flick a match.

She didn't look up until she felt the shift.

Down the hall, Solar moved with her group, and beside her—of course—Jungwon. Not sneaking, not subtle. Visible. Bold. The sight made Danielle's smile curve, sharp and sweet at once.

How easy it was. How predictable.

Whispers clung to them like perfume, and Danielle drank it in. She didn't need to push harder—at least, not yet. A little scandal fed itself if you let it breathe. She typed out a quick message, something simple to one of her burner accounts: keep the clip circulating. emphasize how reckless it is.

Her reflection caught in the black glass of her phone: lip gloss gleaming, eyes alight with mischief that tilted just close to cruelty.

Jihoon would tell her to be careful. To wait, to let pressure build before striking. But Danielle liked the mess. She liked the spectacle.

And Solar, with her hood pulled low and her face tight, was the perfect stage.




The dorm was dark when Solar returned, only the faint glow of the city bleeding through the blinds. The others had already collapsed into sleep; she could hear Eunchae's soft snores from down the hall, the muffled rhythm of Chaewon's humidifier.

Solar slipped into her room, hoodie sleeves tugged low, and dropped her bag onto the chair. Her body ached from the day—camera lights, rehearsals, the relentless smile that never quite fit right—but her mind was worse.

Her phone buzzed on the desk.

She froze.

The notification preview blurred in the dim light, but her gut already knew. Another anonymous message. She opened it with trembling fingers.

This time, no photo. Just words:

Next time, it won't just be rumors.

Her pulse thundered in her ears. The silence of the dorm thickened, suffocating. She set the phone down face-first, as if hiding it could erase the threat.

Her gaze landed on the small notebook Jungwon had given her weeks ago. It sat on the shelf, quiet, waiting. She pulled it down and thumbed through the sparse pages—words scrawled in haste, one per day, survival in shorthand. On. Enough. Breathe.

Tonight, a single word wasn't enough.

She uncapped the pen, the ink blotting dark as her hand hovered. Her wrist trembled with the weight of the choice. Then, slowly, she wrote:

I'm scared of what'll happen if I admit I want to be close to you.

Her breath hitched.

She added another line, the pen scratching like a whisper against paper:

But I can't seem to stop it.

She stared at the words until they blurred, tears burning at the corners of her eyes but refusing to fall.

Closing the notebook gently, she pressed her palm against the cover, as if sealing the confession away. The city hummed outside, endless and indifferent.

Solar lay back against her pillow, the darkness pressing in. The phone buzzed once more from the desk, but she didn't look this time.

Her last thought before sleep clawed her under was simple, fragile, undeniable:

He steadies me. And that terrifies me most of all.











64: The Night Multiplies

The announcement came mid-morning, casual as though it wouldn't detonate the day:
"Le Sserafim and Enhypen, same rehearsal block. HYBE wants efficiency. Share the green room until camera run-through."

Efficiency. Solar nearly laughed.

By the time they filed into the rehearsal space, chaos had already taken root. Stylists darted between racks of sequined jackets and sharp blazers. Camera crew measured angles with tape, muttering about dolly tracks. A line of water bottles on the counter had already been rearranged by clashing sticky notes: SERAFIM ONLY written in Yunjin's all-caps, followed by a cheeky thanks for sharing scrawled in Sunoo's handwriting.

Eunchae squealed when she spotted him. "HYBE bestie!" she called, barreling toward Sunoo before any staff could stop her. He yelped as she hooked an arm through his, the two of them spinning once before Chaewon cleared her throat like a warning bell.

"Focus," Chaewon said, though her mouth twitched like she wanted to smile.

Across the room, Jake clapped Kazuha's shoulder as if they were old teammates. "No pressure, but you guys have the cleanest synchronization in the company. Teach me your secrets."

Kazuha tilted her head, serene. "Train harder."

Jake groaned, clutching his chest as the others laughed.

Solar tried to keep her breathing even, moving toward the corner where her mic belt had been set. The sight of her girls and their counterparts mingling, joking, felt almost normal. Almost safe. And yet—her skin prickled. Every reflective surface felt like an eye. Every staffer's glance lingered too long. She tugged at her sleeve until the gauze was hidden, forced her smile into something passable.

Then the door opened.

Chaerin strode in like she belonged there, Gucci jacket slipping off her shoulder, sunglasses perched in her hair. "I'm here to support my famous sister," she announced, as if anyone had asked.

Solar's stomach dropped.

Because the second Chaerin's gaze swept the room, it snagged like a magnet. Heeseung.

He'd been leaning against the far wall, casual, scrolling through his phone. The moment Chaerin walked in, he looked up—and the atmosphere shifted. A spark leapt invisible across the room. Chaerin's smirk widened, and Heeseung straightened without meaning to.

"Oh no," Solar muttered under her breath.

Yunjin caught it instantly, eyes lighting with wicked delight. "Wait. Wait wait wait."

Jake elbowed Heeseung, grinning. "Hyung, is there something you want to share with the class?"

Heeseung scowled, but his ears turned red. Chaerin, never one to back down, crossed the room slowly, like it was a runway, and said, "Miss me?" loud enough for everyone to hear.

Eunchae gasped, dramatic. Sakura covered her mouth, trying not to laugh.

Solar wanted to sink into the floor.

And then she caught Jungwon's reaction.

He hadn't moved from his spot near the doorway, arms crossed loosely, expression calm. But his gaze flicked between Chaerin and Heeseung, then to Solar, then back again. Not judgmental—just... taking it in. Measuring. Solar couldn't tell if she wanted to throttle her sister or bury her face in Jungwon's hoodie and never look up.

The room erupted with teasing and chatter, but underneath it all, Solar's nerves hummed. She couldn't shake the sensation of being watched, even here in a room full of laughter. Her body stayed tense, waiting for the next knife to fall.

The noise doubled with the addition of Enhypen's members fully settling into the room. Staff tried to impose order—cue sheets pinned to corkboards, assistants reminding everyone of camera order—but it was like herding cats in designer clothes.

Sunghoon had ended up perched on the edge of a table, watching Eunchae and Sunoo spin each other until they nearly crashed into a rack of costumes. His voice carried dry and unimpressed:
"You're going to tear a mic pack, and then you'll both cry."

"Jealous you weren't invited," Eunchae shot back, tongue out.

Sunghoon raised an eyebrow. "Jealous isn't the word." But his smirk gave him away, and Eunchae squealed louder just to annoy him.

Across the room, Sakura found herself cornered by Niki, who at sixteen had all the boldness of someone who didn't know when to stop.
"You have to admit," he said earnestly, "our choreo's harder. Way more stamina. You guys do vibes. We do acrobatics."

Sakura blinked at him once, then deadpanned, "Try vibing in heels."

Kazuha choked on her water bottle laughing.

Jake had gathered both Yunjin and Kazuha near the mirror, dramatically re-enacting some mistake from rehearsal. "And then the cameraman zoomed right as I slipped, so somewhere out there is a clip of me looking like I'm inventing a new genre of dance."

"You should own it," Yunjin advised, grinning. "Name it after yourself. The Jake Stumble."

"That's cold," Jake groaned, but he was smiling too.

Through all of it, Chaerin had made herself perfectly at home. She'd found a chair too close to Heeseung's and draped her jacket across the back like a territorial marker. Heeseung tried to play it cool, scrolling through his phone, but every so often his eyes cut toward her—and Chaerin always caught him.

"So," she said, tone casual but sharp enough to slice, "how's your voice holding up?"

Heeseung didn't look up. "Fine."

"Mm," Chaerin hummed, crossing her legs. "Guess the rumors about you over-practicing were wrong then."

That got him. He glanced up, narrowed eyes catching her smirk. "What rumors?"

"Wouldn't you like to know."

The girls noticed immediately—Chaewon's sigh said why is this my life, Yunjin's barely-contained laughter said this is premium entertainment, and Eunchae whispered a loud, "Ohhh, they're flirting," that made Solar want to crawl under the makeup table.

She tried to ignore it all, adjusting her mic belt, focusing on the safe repetition of tape and wires. But her nerves wouldn't settle. Every laugh seemed too sharp, every glance from passing staff too weighted. She tugged her sleeve lower and felt the prickling sensation of being observed, as if somewhere in the room—or outside it—someone's gaze had fixed on her.

And Jungwon saw it.

He hadn't joined the chaos, hadn't laughed at Jake's dramatics or teased Heeseung about Chaerin. He just stood a little apart, near the doorway, quiet but immovable. His eyes tracked her—not intrusive, not heavy, just... steady. Like he was waiting for her to look back and confirm what he already knew: that something was wrong.

Solar didn't. Not yet. She wasn't ready for what it would mean.




By the time rehearsal wrapped, Solar's head was pounding.

The camera crew barked adjustments—"Step two paces left," "hit the diagonal tighter"—and the staff scribbled notes, but the words blurred at the edges. Noise pressed in on all sides: Jake cracking another joke, Sunoo humming, Eunchae trying to harmonize with him, Yunjin snickering about it. Everything sounded almost normal, almost comforting.

But beneath it, Solar heard something else.

Two stylists behind the racks whispered low, thinking no one was close enough to hear.
"—photo's everywhere. Can't believe HYBE's letting it slide."
"She'll drag him down with her if it's true."

Solar's throat closed.

She fumbled with her water bottle, twisting the cap too hard until it squeaked. No one noticed. Or maybe they pretended not to.

Later, in the hallway, the whispers carried again. Two backup dancers passed, laughing until one lowered his voice:
"Guess idols think rules don't apply if you're HYBE's pet projects."

Her pulse skipped, icy and hot at once. She pressed herself against the wall to let them pass, sleeve tugged low. The gauze underneath felt like it glowed neon, like everyone could see right through to the injury, to the shame.

She wanted to tell herself it was just gossip, that it would burn out like every cycle did. But the anonymous messages still haunted her, the photo spreading wider each time she checked. And Danielle—she could feel Danielle's fingerprints in the way the narrative twisted cruel.

The others kept moving, chatter and laughter filling the space, but Solar lagged behind. Every lens felt aimed at her. Every phone in the crowd of staff looked like it could be recording. She tried to swallow air, but it caught like glass in her throat.

She thought of Jungwon.

The rumors were about them both, but he had more to lose. Enhypen's spotless reputation, his years of training, everything built so carefully. She pictured it crumbling because of her, because of her mess—Jihoon, Danielle, her family's shadows, her own inability to stay untouched.

The guilt settled like lead.

She remembered Jaehyun's warning in clipped syllables: If you fall, you drag her with you. The line twisted, warped—no, if she fell, she'd drag Jungwon down too. And maybe that was worse.

Solar blinked hard, her vision blurring until the LED signs in the hallway smeared into color. Yunjin brushed past her shoulder with a quick, grounding squeeze, murmuring, "Stay with us, Sol." Solar nodded, forced a smile, but the dread didn't shift.

When they reached the waiting area, the others dispersed to fix makeup or retie shoelaces. Solar sat down heavily, hands clenched in her lap. She thought of the notebook in her bag, the last sentence she'd scrawled. I can't seem to stop it.

And she hated herself for how true it was.




The building exhaled between run-throughs. For sixty seconds at a time, the hallways loosened their grip—carts paused, headset chatter dipped, the LED cue screens blinked to standby blue. Then some assistant director somewhere would shout reset and the whole place would lurch forward again like a train finding the next gear.

Solar used the lull to breathe. It didn't work.

She sat on the low bench outside the shared rehearsal room and stared at the scuffed toe of her boot like it might give her an answer. The air smelled like warm cables and hairspray. Across from her, a poster of last year's festival smile-policed the corridor with four perfect faces and no pores. Her phone lay face-down on her thigh as if that could muzzle it. It still felt hot. It still felt watched.

Yunjin drifted past with a paper cup and a radar for distress that never missed. She didn't ask you good?—that was a script Solar could lie through. She bumped Solar's knee with her own and murmured, "Water fountain's free. So is the little acoustic room by the freight elevator," then lifted her cup like a cheers and melted toward the dressing area. Chaewon, behind her, didn't so much as glance over; she just set a hand on Solar's shoulder for a heartbeat—a permission slip, a benediction—and called, "Three minutes. Don't get lost."

Kazuha steered Eunchae in the opposite direction with the promise of a vending machine treasure hunt. Sakura, ever the lighthouse, caught Solar's eye and gave the tiniest nod. Space. We've got you.

Solar swallowed. The bench felt like a ledge. She stood.

Jungwon was exactly where she knew he'd be and where she hadn't allowed herself to look: half in the doorway, half in the hall, arms crossed loose and jaw at an angle that read neutral to everyone who didn't know the language of restraint. He didn't move toward her. He didn't make her come to him. He waited until she closed half the distance, then pushed off the frame and fell into step beside her as if they'd planned it.

They didn't speak until the corridor bent left, past the loading dock where the concrete sweated and the cold crept up through the soles of your shoes.
