The "acoustic room" wasn't really a room; it was a storage closet someone had padded with leftover sound panels and a retired backdrop, the kind with clouds airbrushed so soft they looked edible. A velvet rope hung across the entrance as a joke. Jungwon lifted it. Solar ducked under.

Inside, the noise of the building went muffled, like someone had cupped a hand over the world. A thin strip light hummed overhead. The floor was a patchwork of gaffer tape and rubber mat, pocked where stands had bitten. It was ugly, temporary, perfect.

Jungwon shut them in and turned, bracing one palm on the door as if to keep the rest of the night out. He looked at her the way he had in her room—steady, unflinching, like he could wait out her silence forever.

"How long," he asked, voice low, "have you been bracing against a ghost?"

She huffed something that wasn't a laugh. "Which one?"

His mouth twitched. Not a smile. A tell. He dragged his gaze down to the face-down phone. "Who's messaging you?"

Solar's fingers tightened on the black glass until they ached. She shouldn't have been surprised he noticed. He noticed everything. The mini-flinch when an alert buzzed. The way she angled the screen away from light, from eyes. The new habit of tucking the phone under her leg like she could sit on the problem.

An old part of her—the part raised by "don't make trouble worse by naming it"—lifted its head. She made it lie down.

"Anonymous," she said. The word felt stupid, too small for the way it lived like a shadow under her ribs. "A throwaway. No posts, no icon. They sent the photo first. Then a message last night."

Jungwon's posture didn't change, but something behind his eyes sharpened. "What message."

"'Next time, it won't just be rumors.'" She tried to make it sound like she was quoting a meme. It came out like a confession.

Even the strip light seemed to hold its breath.

He didn't swear. He didn't threaten the air, or the wall, or say names. He just pivoted the question and set it between them like a marker. "And you didn't tell me because?"

Solar stared at the scuff on the mat by his shoe and watched her old certainty about self-containment try to reassert itself. Because I didn't want to hand you another weight to carry. Because the girls were finally breathing easier. Because Jaehyun's voice is in my head. Because if I say it, it's real. Because if I tell you, you'll make it yours.

Out loud, she settled for the truth that hurt the worst. "Because I don't want to be the reason your life explodes."

Something like anger flickered through his jaw and was gone, held on a leash so tight it hummed. He pushed off the door and came closer, not enough to crowd, enough that his voice landed in her bones when he said, "You don't get to decide what's too much for me."

Her throat closed. "It's my mess."

"It's our mess." His eyes didn't waver. "I'm in it. I stepped in it. I'm staying in it."

She wanted to deflect, to throw a joke on the ground like a smoke bomb—wow romantic, do you want matching hazmat suits?—but the look on his face made humor feel like disrespect. So she put her phone on the amp to keep from clinging to it, crossed her own arms like scaffolding, and told him the rest.

"They spread the photo. It's everywhere. People believe what they want to believe. Danielle..." The name tasted like metal. "I can feel her fingerprints in the way it moves. And Jihoon—" her voice thinned around the syllables; she forced it wider "—he's waiting. He's letting her dance so when he cuts the music it hurts more. I don't know what the next hit is. I just know it's coming."

Jungwon listened without asking how she knew, without derailing into the siblings or management or law. He didn't tell her she was paranoid. He didn't tell her she was right. He stood in front of her and took it like he was made for taking. When she ran out of words he glanced at her sleeve.

"Brace," he said.

"I—" She looked down at her wrist, where the edge of the brace he'd brought her two nights ago sat tucked under the cuff, loosened for stage. "It's here."

"What's the point if it's not tight." He said it like he was annoyed at physics. Then he stepped into her space and held out a hand—give. She did. He worked the strap with absurd gentleness, his fingers sure, the Velcro whispering as it slid through the slot and sealed. When he pressed his thumb along the plastic to smooth it, her pulse spiked like he'd touched skin. Maybe he had. The world had gotten very small.

"Wear it when you can," he said. "Ignore it when you have to. But stop punishing your body for what other people did."

Solar stared at the neat line of the strap like it was a sentence he'd written on her. "You're bossy."

"I'm right," he said, and when her mouth kicked up, his own did too, just enough to show relief he didn't say out loud.

Silence pooled again. Not empty—waiting.

She forced herself to look at him. "Jungwon. If this blows up, it won't just be me. It's your group. Your fans. Your everything. They'll call you irresponsible. They'll call you careless. They'll cut up your words and feed them back to you like proof. I could live with that for me. I don't think I could live with it for you."

He held her gaze until her eyes watered from trying not to blink. When he spoke, his voice was low, plain, the way you say the simplest truths.

"I trained my whole life to stand where I stand. I did not train to learn how to pretend I don't care about you."

The sentence landed somewhere behind her sternum and shook the scaffolding. She felt reckless gratitude and stupid grief all at once—the kind you get when someone hands you a glass of water after you've been swallowing dust and it hurts because it's too clean.

His mouth flattened like he'd hear an argument forming. He killed it before it was born. "Don't you dare decide what's too much for me. I choose this." A beat. He stepped closer until the heat of him bent the air. "I choose you."

There it was—not the vow hissed through his teeth in anger, not the rooftop oath he'd made to himself in the dark. This was daylight. It was the backstage, the stupid storage room with cartoon clouds and a strip light that hummed like a nerve, and he was saying it without the cover of fury or adrenaline. He said it like breathing.

Every old instinct in her screamed danger. Not because of him—because of what choosing meant. Because of the cost. Because of the ledger she'd spent her whole life keeping, where her safety line always ran through other people first. Because she knew exactly how fast public love could turn into a weapon.

And still her body loosened around his words like a fist opening.

"You can't control the internet," she said, and hated that she sounded like she was pleading with physics again.

"No," he agreed. "I can control where I stand."

He lifted his hand and stopped, giving her the out he always gave her, even when she didn't want it. When she didn't take it, his fingers found the hinge of her jaw. Not a graze this time, not a test. A touch that was both warning and promise. The pad of his thumb fit just under her cheekbone like he'd mapped it. She didn't breathe; she didn't look away.

"You're not ruining me," he said, each syllable deliberate. "You are not a ruin. You are not a career hazard. You are the only thing that's made any of this make sense in weeks."

Her laugh burst out wrong and wet. She caught it with her teeth. "That line would sound better in a drama."

"I'm not trying to be pretty," he said. "I'm trying to be clear."

Something in her buckled. She pressed her free hand flat to his chest because there was nowhere else to put it and she needed the world to stop moving long enough for the sentence he'd laid down to settle. His hoodie was warm, the fabric soft, the heartbeat under it maddeningly steady.

"Jaehyun thinks you're reckless," she said, because if she was going to tear a page out, she might as well rip the cleanest one.

Jungwon's thumb didn't leave her face. "Jaehyun is allowed to be wrong."

"He—" Her voice dipped, then climbed. "He hinted you did something. He didn't say what. He said you make me a target. He said you'll drag me down if you fall."

"And I said I'm not falling." He didn't flinch at the near-exposure of his own secret; he just rerouted it like a river around rock. His restraint was a living thing. "He plays in shadows. I don't. That's his lane. This is mine."

"What is this?" The question came out smaller than she meant. "This," she repeated, finding the edge with her teeth. "Us, like this."

He let the silence hold long enough for her to hear both answers—the one she'd already tucked into a notebook, the one that had been beating at the base of her throat since "not yet."

"It's not a rumor," he said finally. "It's not a game. It's not an accident." His hand dropped from her jaw to her shoulder, heavy in a way that stabilized rather than pinned. "It's me standing where you can see me. And it's you letting me."

She closed her eyes, because looking at him felt like stepping to a ledge and she was so tired of ledges. "I'm scared," she said into the humid closet air. "If I say yes to that, I don't know how to keep you safe."

"Then don't say yes to the internet," he said. "Say yes to me."

The stupidest, simplest thing. The kind that made you feel both childish and ancient when you realized how much you'd complicated your own life. The kind that made you want to cry because of course.

She opened her eyes. He was close enough that the darker flecks in his irises resolved into constellations. Close enough that she could see the exhaustion etched at the edges, the burn he'd been running on all week. Close enough that if she lifted her chin she could end the distance he'd promised to guard.

She didn't. Neither did he.

Somewhere beyond the door, a PA shouted, "Enhypen to standby," and the building snapped back to speed. The strip light buzzed louder like a fly.

Jungwon's hand dropped away; she felt the absence like a cold spot. He reached past her to the door, then paused with his fingers on the handle.

"I'm going to walk you back," he said.

"You shouldn't," she said automatically.

"I know." His mouth kicked up; it wasn't humor. It was defiance. "I'm still going to."

She inhaled like she'd learned in vocal warm-ups, filling everything low, pressing the breath into the floor. "Okay."

The hallway bit at them when they stepped out—brighter, colder, full of dying applause from a stage they couldn't see. He didn't touch her. He didn't have to. He set himself half a step to her left, where a body shields a line of sight, and matched his stride to hers. When an assistant with a headset and a mission tried to funnel between them, he shifted without looking and made space reappear as if he'd conjured it.

They crossed the patch of floor with the worst lighting, the one where every phone camera turned even saints into the villain of someone's story. Solar's skin prickled; she heard the sentence again—If you fall, you drag him with you—and then it altered, not with logic but with the fact of him there.

If he falls, I'm under him. If I fall, he's beside me.

At the rehearsal door, he stopped. The noise of two groups rose and fell inside—Sunoo laughing, Yunjin crowing about some in-joke, Chaerin's voice a whip crack as Heeseung tried to pretend he didn't like being hit with it.

"You don't have to—" she started, and he cut her off with a look that wasn't sharp so much as final.

"Stop telling me what I have to do," he said, and the edge in it was heat this time, not cold. Then gentler: "Text me if they send anything else. Even if it's nothing. Especially if it's nothing."

She nodded. "Jungwon?"

He waited.

"Thank you," she said. Not for the brace. Not for the walk. For choosing out loud.

He could have said don't thank me. He didn't. He said, "I'll be at the left wing." Then he tapped his knuckles once against the doorjamb like a ritual and turned down the hall toward his call.

Solar watched him go until the angle took him. She let the exhale fold her in half and come back up slow. When she pushed the rehearsal room door open, the noise tried to swallow her whole again. It didn't quite manage.

Yunjin clocked her immediately and arched a brow like you alive? Solar gave a small nod that wasn't just for show. Chaewon's eyes flicked to the clock, to Solar's wrist, to Solar's face; satisfaction softened her mouth. Eunchae bounded over with a grape jelly candy from the vending machine and shoved it into Solar's palm like a talisman. Kazuha offered her water without looking like it was an offering.

The room was still a storm. The internet was still a mouth. The ghost messages were still out there with their stupid, simple threats.

And yet—something in her had resettled its weight, moved from one foot to both. The floor was still moving. She was steadier on it.

Three minutes, Chaewon had said. They'd stolen four. Somewhere in that extra sixty seconds, a line had been drawn, not around her but to her.

Not a cage. A claim.



The screen light painted Danielle's face ghost-white in the dark of her room, but she didn't care. Her eyes glittered with it—hundreds of comments scrolling past, her name nowhere, Solar's name everywhere.

She bit into the corner of a macaron, crumbs sticking to her lip as she scrolled. The stan accounts had done their work beautifully. She didn't even need to push the photo anymore; it had been dissected, reposted, magnified a hundred times already. Some claimed it was doctored, some swore it wasn't. None of that mattered. It was noise, and she loved noise.

Her private group chat with a handful of fan account admins was a riot of messages:
"Exclusive: she's sneaking around with him backstage 👀"
"I've got a thread compiling everything shady she's done since last year"
"#SolarScandal trending in 8 countries rn lol we won"

Danielle tossed her phone onto the bedspread and laughed, giddy. It was almost too easy. A photo, a rumor, the right hands to hold it—and the whole internet convulsed like she was playing it on strings.

"Unfit," she said aloud, savoring the word like sugar. "Unstable. Dangerous." Each syllable a little crown. Solar's new titles, courtesy of her.

She padded across the room, tossing the empty macaron box into the trash with a ballerina spin. For a moment she imagined the headlines piling higher and higher until Solar suffocated under them. She pictured Jungwon, too, trying to haul her out—and sinking beside her. That was the masterpiece: two stars drowning in each other until nothing remained but bubbles.

Her laughter echoed, bright and ugly.

Jihoon didn't laugh.

He sat in a rented office two districts away, blinds closed, a glass of water sweating onto the desk beside his phone. The same photo sat on his screen, the comments like gnats buzzing. He scrolled with slow precision, thumb measured, as if the words might shift if he moved too quickly.

He wasn't interested in hashtags. Hashtags burned hot, then out.

He wanted embers.

Danielle was a firework—loud, dazzling, gone in a second. Useful for spectacle. Useless for control.

No, Jihoon preferred patience. He read the threads not for the noise but for the patterns. Which journalists picked up the whispers. Which rival stans amplified them. Which HYBE staff had unfollowed whom. Every ripple, every seam in the machine.

The photo wasn't the end. It wasn't even the real blow. It was pressure—constant, public, humiliating. A reminder to Solar of how little she could hide, how easily her image could collapse. The real hit would come when she was already cracking.

Jihoon set the phone down and leaned back, fingers steepled. He thought about her bandaged wrist, about the flinch she couldn't mask, about the way she curled her body like prey even when she stood onstage with all the lights in the world on her.

He smiled—not manic, not giddy, but razor-thin.

"Bleed for me, Solar," he murmured into the hum of the air conditioner. "Bleed enough, and you'll come crawling back yourself."

Danielle might have been dancing in her room, drunk on the chaos she'd uncorked. Jihoon only sipped his water and waited.

The silence around him was heavier than her laughter could ever be.




Solar was still perched on the edge of her bed when her phone buzzed. She'd been pretending to scroll, eyes on the same empty chat box, when the vibration cut through her haze.

[Won]: I'm stopping by. Left something for you.
Be there in five.

Her stomach lurched, equal parts dread and relief. Reckless. Comforting. He hadn't asked—he'd told her.

The second buzz came just as she tugged her hoodie sleeves down:

[Won]: At the door.

She pushed up from the mattress and slipped into the hall, heart thudding sharp.

The living room wasn't empty.

Chaerin was sprawled on the couch like she owned the place, hair twisted up, phone loose in her hand, smirk half-loaded. And in the corner, near the door, Jaehyun leaned against the wall—long coat hanging like armor, sunglasses absurd in the dim light, his phone glowing cold in his palm. She hadn't expected them still to be here, lingering like sentinels.

The knock came, soft but firm. Solar reached for the handle.

When she opened the door, Jungwon filled the frame—hood up, cap low, shoulders squared. His gaze went first to her, softened for a fraction, then cut to Jaehyun. And just like that, the air snapped taut.

"You shouldn't be here," Jaehyun said, voice low, formal, carrying the weight of command.

Jungwon's jaw ticked. "I'll be wherever she is."

The words cracked like flint. Chaerin muttered, "Oh, here we go," under her breath, but Solar barely heard. Her ears rang with the tension vibrating through the room.

Jaehyun pocketed his phone and straightened, wall turning soldier. "Do you think this is about loyalty? About showing up like some bodyguard? You're reckless. Visible. Every time you hover near her, you make her a target."

"She already is one," Jungwon snapped, the sharpest she'd ever heard him. "And hiding behind shadows hasn't stopped Jihoon or Danielle. I won't stand back and watch her bleed for your strategies."

"Strategies keep her alive," Jaehyun shot back. "You think charging into fire makes you noble? It makes you useful to the enemy. You're a weapon pointed the wrong way."

Solar stepped forward, hands raised, but neither man looked at her. They were locked, storm and stone.

Then Jaehyun slipped the knife in:
"Or should I remind her about the little stunt you already pulled? Confronting Jihoon and Danielle like a boy who thinks fists make him invincible?"

Solar froze.

Her gaze snapped to Jungwon. He didn't flinch—but the muscle in his jaw gave him away.

"You didn't tell me," she whispered.

The room stilled. Chaerin's smirk faded into wide eyes. Jaehyun's mouth curved, not into a smile but something crueler: satisfaction.

Solar's chest constricted, betrayal threading hot with fear. Jungwon had sworn openness. He had looked her in the eye and told her she wouldn't fight alone. And yet he had gone to Jihoon—Jihoon—without her knowing.

Her voice shook. "Why would you—do you have any idea what he's capable of? He's not Danielle. He doesn't care about hashtags or whispers. He'll ruin you. He'll ruin all of us."

Jungwon finally tore his gaze from Jaehyun to meet hers. His eyes burned dark, unreadable. "I know exactly what he is. And I don't care. I told you—I'm not letting him hurt you again. Not on my watch."

It should have steadied her. Once, those words had. But now they tangled with dread. Jihoon wasn't a rumor or a headline; Jihoon was knives, secrets, leverage. Jungwon didn't know how far he would go.

Her throat ached. "You should have told me."

"I didn't want you to carry it," Jungwon said, softer but no less fierce. "Not everything has to weigh on your shoulders, Sol. Some of it—" His fists curled, unclenched. "Some of it is mine to carry."

She blinked against the heat in her eyes. Anger, fear, something softer she hated herself for feeling. Because underneath it all, she was honored. Terrified, but honored, that he had made her burden his own.

Jaehyun cut in, cold as winter: "And that's why you'll both drown. Loyalty without strategy is suicide."

Solar snapped then, turning on him. "And secrecy without trust is just another kind of cage. You don't get to weaponize this against him. Not when you've kept your own shadows just as dark."

Jaehyun's jaw tightened, but he said nothing. Silence settled heavy, broken only by the hum of the fridge in the corner.

Solar looked between them—the brother carved of stone, the boy burning alive—and thought, yet again: They'll burn each other down before they let me break. And she didn't know whether that was salvation or doom.




















65: Storms Without Witness

Sleep never came.

Solar lay on her bed, hoodie still on, staring at the ceiling where the streetlight outside cut the blinds into fractured lines. Every time she closed her eyes she saw Jaehyun's stone face, heard Jungwon's vow, felt herself between them like a thread pulled taut.

Her phone was still warm from the last buzz. She turned it face down. She couldn't look at his words again—not tonight.

Instead she reached for the notebook. The one he'd pressed into her palm weeks ago: write something down, even if it's just one word.

She had, until now. Words that fit between breaths: On. Still. Fight. Tiny anchors.

But tonight the pen didn't stop at a word. Her wrist ached under the bandage as she pressed harder, the ink dragging long and certain.

Every time I tell myself to step back, I find myself closer to you.

She stared at the line, breath snagging. The page looked dangerous. The page looked true.

Her throat tightened as she added another.

You burn too bright, but I'm done pretending I don't want the fire.

Her hand shook; she dropped the pen. The words glowed like embers on paper, terrifying and magnetic all at once.

The notebook snapped shut when the door creaked.

Chaewon leaned against the frame, voice soft but steady. "You're still awake?"

Solar nodded.

Eunchae padded in behind her, blanket draped like a cape. "Told her. You never sleep when something's chewing on you." She plopped on the bed dramatically, nearly crushing Solar's leg, grinning. "We should get matching dark circles. Sisterhood branding."

Solar let out a laugh, thinner than she meant but real enough.

Yunjin poked her head in last, arms folded, eyes scanning like she expected intruders. "You good?" Not Are you okay?—just that sharp shorthand.

Solar nodded again, smaller this time.

Chaewon's mouth tightened but she didn't press. Yunjin's gaze lingered, suspicious but unwilling to corner her. Eunchae hummed a nonsense tune, blanket trailing, filling silence with something light.

Solar slid the notebook under her pillow. The paper still hummed in her chest. For the first time all night, her breathing slowed.

The weight in Solar's chest had just begun to ease when her phone buzzed again, muffled under the pillow. She froze, heart jerking.

Chaewon caught the flicker of panic across her face. "Go on," she said gently. "If it's him, you don't have to hide it from us."

Solar hesitated, then pulled the phone free. The screen lit up, but it wasn't Jungwon's name.

Shock Discord: #scrim-banter

Her lips parted before she could stop it.

Rascal: we saw ur stage. still cracked. even w glitter on ur cheek lol.
Smurf: she was glowing tho fr.
Super: yall think she's famous enough now to get us free hair spray at cvs??
Glister: i still don't understand korean hair products.

Solar's laugh came out soft, startled, but it shook something loose in her ribs.

Eunchae leaned closer, squinting at the messages. "They watch your stages?" she asked, delighted.

Solar nodded, scrolling as more pings rolled in.

Rascal: don't forget ur roots sol. overwatch > kpop.
Super: WRONG. kpop sol > overwatch sol. i said what i said.
Smurf: ...both.

Solar typed back quickly before she could think better of it: thanks. miss you guys.

The screen lit up in chaotic unison.

Rascal: we'll fly in w balloons if hybe lets us.
Super: free solar concerts when?
Glister: sakura noona dm'd nero again btw. 👀
Smurf: stop snitching glister.

Solar blinked. Her gaze cut sideways, instinctively, to Sakura's closed door down the hall.

Yunjin's brows shot up, catching it too. "...Wait a sec."

Solar shook her head fast, pocketing her phone before Eunchae could climb over her shoulder. "Don't start conspiracies."

Chaewon arched an eyebrow, the leader in her already cataloging another thread of worry.

But Eunchae only gasped dramatically, clutching her blanket like pearls. "Our dorm has subplots."

Solar laughed again, this one freer. For a moment, she let herself feel what the boys' messages gave her: a tether to another family, one that loved her without conditions, one that cheered for both sides of her double life.

Chaewon touched her wrist lightly, grounding her. "See? You're not as alone as you think."

Solar pressed her lips together, swallowing around the lump in her throat. She nodded, but when she lay back against the pillow, she could still feel the coal of fear under her ribs. The leaks, the rumors—they didn't stop just because the people who loved her did.

Still, for tonight, she let the warmth of the messages linger.




The next morning was supposed to feel routine. Music show day meant early call time, hair and makeup in the half-light, water bottles shoved into idle hands. The rhythm usually numbed Solar: eyeliner, in-ears, camera blocking. Steps and swallows and smiles.

But her phone was buzzing before she'd even sat in the chair. Too many notifications, a relentless cascade that made the stylist mutter and push the volume down.

"Don't," Yunjin warned, already scrolling her own feed. Her face was taut, mouth pressed flat. "Seriously, Sol. Don't look."

Solar looked anyway.

Hashtags bloomed like mold across the screen. #SolarScandal. #SolarExposed. #ProtectEnhypen. Images pasted over fan accounts—grainy stills, blurred clips. One looked like her in a hallway, voice spliced into sharp edges she'd never spoken. Another paired her silhouette with the edge of a hoodie that might have been Jungwon's. Every caption told the same story:

She's unstable.
Dragging him down with her.
HYBE covering up their affair.

Her chest iced over. Danielle's hand was all over this—the timing, the venom, the too-perfect way it targeted both her and him at once.

Eunchae came barreling over, nearly dropping her phone in outrage. "She edited your laugh!" Her voice cracked with disbelief. "Who even does that? Who takes someone's laugh and makes it sound like—like—" She shook her head violently, words breaking.

Chaewon's shadow fell over Solar's shoulder, a steadying hand pressing down, her thumb brushing once across fabric. "Ignore it," she said. Calm. Too calm.

Yunjin wasn't calm at all. She slammed her phone onto the vanity hard enough to make compacts rattle. "How long is HYBE going to let her rot in the open before they do something?"

As if summoned, a notification pinged. HYBE's official account. Statement already live.

Solar tapped it open, pulse roaring in her ears.

HYBE is aware of recent speculation surrounding LE SSERAFIM member Solar. We strongly refute the claims being spread and reaffirm our artist's commitment to professionalism and group activities. We will pursue legal action against malicious actors spreading false information.

It was the same tone as every other fire they'd tried to smother: distant, polished, cold. Her name pressed flat against corporate glass, her truth reduced to commitment and professionalism.

Solar's reflection in the mirror blurred. She blinked hard, willing it back.

Another ping. Another account. ENHYPEN's this time.

HYBE is aware of recent speculation surrounding our artists Solar (LE SSERAFIM) and Jungwon (ENHYPEN). These claims are baseless. We strongly refute the spread of false information and reaffirm both artists' professionalism and dedication to their group activities. Legal action will be taken against malicious actors.

Her breath stuttered.

Both their names. Bound together in corporate font, not in truth. Not in warmth. Not in anything real—just denial.

Eunchae leaned in, whispering like it might soften the blow: "At least they're protecting him too..."

Solar couldn't answer. Because she heard the silence under the words: Protecting him meant denying her. Protecting him meant framing her as a rumor he'd been caught in, a mistake to erase.

Yunjin swore quietly, fire in her eyes as she paced. Chaewon's hand pressed firmer on her shoulder, steady but heavy, like it knew she was unraveling.

Solar's palms pressed into her knees until they hurt. She had expected Danielle to hit again. She hadn't expected HYBE's protection to feel like a cage—built around Jungwon while she was left in the cold.

The hashtags pulsed, relentless. DragHerDown. ProtectJungwon.

Her throat locked. For the first time, she let herself wonder: standing beside him—wanting him—was it going to destroy them both?

The vanity lights glared against the glass. Her reflection looked back: cheeks flushed, eyes too hollow, a girl who was both weapon and wound.

She whispered to herself, almost a prayer, almost defiance: "Still here."

But even her own voice sounded fragile.



The halls of the broadcast station were designed for movement, not comfort. Narrow corridors with gaffer tape X's marking camera paths, peeling laminated signs taped above doorframes: Stage B →, Green Room 3 →. Lights buzzed in the ceiling, too bright, too cold. Staff moved like a current—runners, stylists, managers with clipboards and Bluetooth headsets.

Jungwon had learned how to disappear into that current. Shoulders low, hood up, his cap tugged down until his jawline cut shadows across his face. But tonight he didn't feel invisible. He felt like a storm shoved into skin, every step weighted with a fury he couldn't bleed out in rehearsal or vocal practice.

Danielle's leak had spread like gasoline on water—quick, slick, unstoppable. And HYBE's statement had only made it worse in his head. He could stomach his own name being dragged into the mess. What he couldn't stomach was hers being turned into a shield for him, her being twisted into the unstable one, the problem.

Every instinct in him screamed the same thing: find her.

And then he did.

She was leaning against the doorframe of a storage hallway, all soft curls and calculated innocence. Phone in her hand, lips curved into a smile too pleased to be casual.

Danielle.

Jungwon's pulse spiked. His shoes squeaked against the tile as he stopped dead.

She noticed instantly, sliding her phone into her pocket like she'd been expecting him. "Oh," she said, her voice a sugary lilt. "Leader-nim." She tilted her head, faux polite. "You look like you've seen a ghost."

"Stop." His voice came out rougher than he intended, a growl dragged across glass.

Danielle's brows lifted. "Stop what? Talking?" She stepped forward, her perfume sharp and floral in the sterile air. "Or maybe you mean stop... telling the truth?"

His fists curled so tight his nails bit his palms. "You think twisting her laugh into something cruel is the truth?"

"Funny," Danielle said lightly, circling him like a cat might a cornered dog. "I didn't hear you deny it." She leaned closer, her voice dropping. "Is it because you can't?"

Heat ripped through his chest. He wanted to shove her back, slam the wall beside her head, do anything that would silence the smirk playing on her lips. Instead he forced his arms to stay rigid at his sides.

"She has nothing to do with you," he said, voice low, vibrating with the effort it took not to roar.

"Doesn't she?" Danielle purred. "I see the way you look at her. Everyone sees it. The hashtags write themselves." She leaned even closer, whispering, "She'll ruin you, Jungwon. And I'll make sure of it."

The hallway blurred. For a moment all he could see was red, his vision tunneling until Danielle's face was the only thing in focus. The smirk. The glint of satisfaction in her eyes as if she wanted—no, needed—to push him past the edge.

He stepped forward, cornering her against the wall. His voice dropped to a hiss, vulgar and venomous. "Say her name again, and I'll—"

"Jungwon!"

A sharp voice cut through.

Heeseung.

The older boy strode into the hallway, one hand already grabbing Jungwon's shoulder, yanking him back before the fury could turn physical.

"Let go," Jungwon snarled, shrugging off the grip, but Heeseung tightened it, pinning him in place with quiet strength.

Danielle only laughed softly, brushing imaginary dust off her sleeve. "How chivalrous. Two knights for one girl."

Jungwon lunged again, but Heeseung shoved him hard against the opposite wall. "Enough," Heeseung barked, low and sharp enough to slice through the storm. "Not here. Not like this."

Jungwon's breath came in ragged bursts, chest heaving. His body trembled with the urge to throw her words back down her throat, but Heeseung's grip anchored him.

Danielle's smile widened, saccharine and triumphant. "See? You're exactly as easy to control as she is. One little rumor and you're both spiraling." She leaned her head to the side, eyes glinting. "Makes my job too easy."

Something in Jungwon snapped. He slammed his fist into the wall behind him, the crack echoing down the corridor. Staffers' voices echoed distantly—someone asking what the sound was, someone else dismissing it as a stagehand moving equipment.

"Careful," Danielle crooned, unfazed. "You wouldn't want your precious fans to see you like this. Or hers."

Heeseung shoved him again, harder this time. "Walk," he ordered, voice commanding in a way that left no room for argument.

Jungwon's jaw clenched so hard it hurt, but he forced his body to move, shoes dragging against the tile. His breath was still ragged, fire still clawing at his ribs, but Heeseung's presence was a barrier he couldn't cross.

Behind them, Danielle's laughter followed like perfume, sticky and suffocating.

They ducked into an empty rehearsal room, Heeseung slamming the door behind them.

"Are you out of your mind?" Heeseung snapped, turning on him. "Do you have any idea what she wanted? She wanted you to snap. She wanted witnesses. A camera. Anything."

Jungwon pressed his hands against his face, dragging them down until his cap nearly fell off. "I don't care."

"You should." Heeseung's voice dropped, stern but not unkind now. "Because if you don't, she wins. And she drags Sol down with you."

At the sound of her name, Jungwon's stomach twisted.

He dropped his hands, eyes burning. "I won't let that happen."

Heeseung studied him for a long moment, then sighed, shaking his head. "You can't fight fire by lighting yourself on it, Jungwon."

Jungwon didn't answer. Because he knew if Danielle taunted him again, he wasn't sure he'd stop at words.

And somewhere in the back of his head, he knew this was only the beginning.

Outside, the station hummed on: rehearsals, schedules, idols posing for fansigns. Solar was somewhere down the hall, probably staring at her phone, probably whispering the same vow to herself he'd made in his own head.

Still here.

But Danielle's laughter echoed louder than any promise.



The Lee family apartment was dark, curtains drawn tight against the city. The only light came from Jaehyun's phone, the glow stark against his sunglasses even indoors. He sat rigid at the edge of Solar's borrowed chair, posture formal as a blade unsheathed.

"Yes," he said into the receiver, voice low, precise. "The transfer goes through tonight. That's not a request."

A pause. Static fuzzed. Whoever was on the other end pushed back—murmurs of uncertainty, fear. Jaehyun's jaw tightened, but his tone didn't waver.

"You owe me," he said simply. "And debts don't dissolve because you grow a conscience."

Behind him, Chaerin paced the hall, pretending not to listen. Her phone glowed in her hand too, restless thumbs flicking through updates about Solar, about the storm Danielle had unleashed. But her ears caught every clipped word from her brother.

Then a new voice cut through the line. Smooth. Too calm. Jaehyun froze.

Jihoon.

"You're playing dangerous games, hyung," Jihoon said, almost amused. "Leaning on rats from alleys you'd rather the world forget? I know their names. I know yours. How long before someone else does too?"

Jaehyun's face didn't change. But Chaerin stopped pacing.

Jihoon's voice slithered through the line, steady and sure. "Push me, and I'll drag every skeleton your precious family buried into the sun. Solar won't look so untouchable when the truth stains her name."

Silence stretched, taut as wire.

Jaehyun's only response was measured, deadly quiet. "You mistake silence for fear. I am patient, Jihoon. Patience is sharper than rage."

Jihoon laughed, the sound ugly, crackling through cheap signal. "Then wait, hyung. Wait and watch your sister crumble. Or move against me, and watch your whole house collapse with her."

The line clicked dead.

Jaehyun lowered the phone, thumb lingering against the screen. His face was carved from stone, unreadable, but the air around him felt heavier.

Chaerin swallowed hard. For once, she didn't speak.

The room was quiet—too quiet—after the storm of Jungwon's fury, after the shrill noise of leaks and hashtags. But the quiet wasn't safety. It was dread, coiled and waiting.

And Jaehyun knew: the war had only just begun.









66: The World Knows

It started with a sound.

Not the usual chorus of morning chaos—hair dryers, garment bags sighing open, staffers calling call times down the corridor. This was smaller, closer. A hiss in Solar's earbud when she slipped it in out of habit. A soft click. Then a voice that was hers, except not.

I need you. Come to my door. Don't knock. Please.

She pulled the earbud out like it had burned her and stared at it in her palm. The room around her kept moving—makeup artist powdering Kazuha two chairs down, Eunchae arguing in a whisper with a stylist about glitter—but a new thread wove itself through the space, invisible and absolute. She felt it in the way Yunjin's eyes flicked to her and away again; in the way Chaewon's reflection, caught in the vanity mirror, went still for half a second like a metronome skipping a beat.

Solar turned her phone face up. The notification didn't belong to any app she recognized; it was a link preview pasted into a DM from a burner account with no avatar and a jumble of consonants for a name. The preview image was a waveform—spiky white teeth on a black background—and the caption above it read:

leaked voicemail from SOLAR to JW. receipts don't lie.

Underneath, the timestamp. Two nights ago.

Her thumb hovered, trembled, pressed. The audio opened in a browser everyone used and no one trusted. There was a burst of static, a breath she recognized, and then her voice again—her cadences, her soft Seoul vowels, the little hitch she hated. But the words were wrong. The words were someone else's hands wearing her fingerprints.

I can't do this without you, the voice said, small and wrecked. I'm at the door. Hurry. Before someone sees.

Even knowing—knowing—it wasn't real, her stomach lurched like she'd been caught. She let the phone slip against her thigh and felt the world tilt around that one absurd detail: not a photo, not a grainy clip, not a rumor with plausible deniability. A voice. Her voice.

Her breath found a rhythm without asking permission. Four in. Four out. Four in. She was half-aware of the makeup artist coming toward her with a cotton pad and then stopping, reading the temperature in the air the way pros did.

"What is it?" Yunjin's voice, low, already angry.

Solar didn't answer. She clicked the screen dark and then on again, like maybe the pixels would rearrange into a kind world. They didn't. The link had spread while she listened. Stan accounts had done what they did: ripped the file, reuploaded it, threaded it with threads. Someone had paired the audio with a blurry still of a stairwell camera—the kind of generically grimy CCTV you could find anywhere in Seoul—two figures in hoodies passing like ships. The caption did the rest:

same night. same building. do the math.

Her throat felt raw, though she hadn't said anything yet. She scrolled against her own better judgment. A longer post had gone up from a "source" account that specialized in conspiracy with a pretty gloss: screenshots of "entry logs" that looked like an intern's spreadsheet, red-circled timestamps lined up to the second; a "tip" from a "staffer" about seeing "two shadows" slip through Stage C at an hour that turned secrets into sins. None of it provable. All of it sticky.

She caught her reflection in the vanity: eyes a little too wide, liner too sharp to be human. The bandage peeking under her cuff felt suddenly louder than anything else about her.

"Solar?" Chaewon this time. Just her name, but packed full of a hundred leader-meanings.

"I'm fine," Solar said, and heard Danielle's laugh in her own mouth. She swallowed. "It's nothing."

Yunjin, already pulling her stool closer, gave a soft, disbelieving snort. "The last time 'nothing' trended in twelve countries, the company sent two lawyers and a fruit basket."

"Three lawyers," Eunchae corrected automatically, then flushed when both Chaewon and Yunjin shot her looks. "Sorry. Not the time."

Solar wished she could laugh. The sound in her ear had emptied her of that particular miracle.

Her phone lit again without her touching it. The link had migrated to her mentions, to threads with millions of eyes. Someone had run the clip through an "AI detector" that confidently declared it "80% authentic." Someone else had run it through a different detector that said "likely fabricated" in red letters that looked like guilt. It didn't matter which machine shouted what. The comments chose their own truth.

She forced herself to breathe in the way Coach had taught them when crying threatened to ruin a pre-record: into the belly, let the shoulders hang like coats, open the back of the throat like a tunnel. It helped. Or maybe it just gave her something to do with her mouth besides deny oxygen to her own lungs.

The manager group chat pinged. Manager Kang's name. She didn't open it.

From somewhere down the hall, a staffer called five minutes for camera block. The world would keep asking them to dance whether or not it believed their steps were sincere.

A new notification slid onto her screen—official this time, the tone so familiar it made her teeth ache before she read the words. HYBE's global account. She tapped it with the numb efficiency of a veteran.

HYBE is aware of the audio file currently circulating that purports to feature LE SSERAFIM's Solar. We strongly refute the veracity of this content. We reaffirm our commitment to protecting our artists from malicious fabrication and will pursue legal action against those responsible.

Her eyes tracked the font without absorbing the comfort it pretended to offer. The machine had chosen its line, and it wasn't her truth; it was the company's. She scrolled. There was a second post, mirrored on ENHYPEN's channels with slight modifications that made her fingers go cold.

Regarding speculation involving ENHYPEN's Jungwon: we confirm that Jungwon remains fully focused on group activities. We ask that fans refrain from spreading baseless rumors. ENHYPEN's schedule will proceed as planned.

Jungwon's name sat on the page like a polished stone. Protected. Framed. Removed from her, even in the denial.

There wasn't a line that said Solar remains fully focused. There wasn't a line that asked, politely or otherwise, to stop attaching her to him. There was the implication: He is fine. As for her... she will continue working.

Yunjin made a furious, incredulous noise that might one day be recorded as a new consonant. "So he gets a halo and you get—what?" She gestured at the blank space under the statement like it was a crime scene. "A footnote?"

"It's not about halos," Chaewon murmured, but the edge in her voice could have sliced fruit. "It's about leverage."

Solar set the phone face down so she wouldn't have to keep watching her own erasure loop in miniature. The vanity lights turned the metal edge of the table into a blade. She pressed the heels of her hands into her thighs and felt how solid she still was, how inconveniently physical.

The DM icon blinked again. Another burner. No avatar, no mutuals, the same breathless confidence of a shark that had just found a drop of blood.

got more. if u don't want it seen, you know where to find me.

She didn't. That was the trick. There was nowhere to take a conversation that wasn't a trap. She imagined answering anyway, imagined asking what do you want and receiving for you to split in two in public.

"Drink," the makeup artist said gently, as if she could read that exact sentence on Solar's face. She held out a water bottle and waited until Solar took a long swallow, until her lips and hands remembered the ritual of not shattering.

It wasn't like Danielle to be this... intimate. Photos and scheduling arrows and the soft power of suggestion had been her weapons of choice; this felt like something else. It felt like someone had sat with hours of interviews and fancams and private content and fed them through a grinder until the cadence of Solar's voice could be arranged into a confession. It felt like obsession dressed up as proof.

A shadow passed the doorway and Solar's heart did that traitor thing where it leapt before her head did. It wasn't him. Of course it wasn't. He was across the hall with his members, their laughter bouncing high against cinderblock walls, a sound she could pick out even through two doors and a storm.

The phone vibrated once more on the table. This time it wasn't a burner or a thread; it was a short text from a name that changed the architecture of her chest just by existing.

Won: Don't let it in. It's not you. I know your voice.

A stupid, unreasonable rush of relief swamped her. She typed, deleted, typed again.

They'll say you're reckless for even sending this.

The typing bubble appeared immediately.

Won: Let them.

Her throat closed on a sound that wasn't a laugh or a sob, just a small noise that meant something like okay and something like I hate this and something like thank you for knowing me even here. She slid the phone under the edge of a towel like she could hide both of them from the room.

"Solar," Manager Kang said from the doorway, eyes soft, tone professional. "We're up." He looked like he wanted to add something else—sorry or I see you or this is unfair—but the hallway behind him was already moving, and his job was to move them with it.

"Coming," she said, and her voice sounded like hers. The real hers. She took one breath for luck and another for fury.

As they filed out, the corridor chewed them up and put them where live people went to look like icons. Staff waved them through bottlenecks with two-finger cues. A junior group lined the wall and bowed; Eunchae bowed back twice as low because she was chaos even when she was polite. Kazuha squeezed Solar's elbow the way ballerinas did when they were about to jeté off a cliff together. Chaewon walked ahead with her head straight and her phone clenched in one hand like a prayer.

At the corner where the hall narrowed to a throat, the noise of the stage bled toward them—bass, crowd, the metallic breath of microphones. Solar could almost pretend the building was singing to her. She could almost pretend the audio threading itself through her mentions didn't exist. She could almost pretend her name hadn't been stapled to his in a font that made it look like a decision made by weather.

She didn't look left toward the door where she knew Enhypen would be queued. She didn't need to. Her skin had learned the geography of him. The pull was a map folded into her pocket.

"Ready?" Chaewon asked, not looking back.

Solar reached up, tugged the cuff of her sleeve down until the bandage disappeared, and flexed her fingers. The brace there was a hard truth against her skin. She wanted to rip it off and she wanted to kiss the person who had put it on her and told her to stop punishing herself for other people's sins.

"On," she said. Not to the manager. Not to the cameras. To herself.

They stepped into the wing and the light turned them into myths again. The crowd made the sound a crowd makes when the people it loves appear—something between ocean and thunder. Solar stood in the dark, marked her square of taped floor, and felt her body slot into the choreography of survival. Breathe. Turn. Don't look toward the left wing. Don't think about machines that decide what is human. Don't think about voices wearing your clothes.

Think about the beat and the count and the way the first note slides across your teeth like a promise.

Behind her eyes, the waveform still pulsed, ridiculous and monstrous. She let it. Let it sit next to his text and HYBE's statement and the way her members had oriented themselves around her like planets that loved gravity on purpose.

The downbeat shook the floor.

Solar smiled, sharp enough to cut herself if she wasn't careful, and opened her mouth to sing.




The dressing room smelled faintly of hair spray and strawberry candy—the kind a stylist had slipped them earlier, promising sugar would keep their voices awake. Jungwon dropped into the couch beside Niki, tugging at the cuff of his hoodie until it slid over his knuckles. The others were sprawled across the room in varying degrees of exhaustion: Heeseung slouched against the armrest like gravity hated him personally, Sunghoon still fiddling with a comb as if his reflection owed him more, Jake perched on the edge of the coffee table with the nervous energy of someone who couldn't sit through silence.

On the wall, the mounted TV buzzed to life. The live feed shifted into LE SSERAFIM's logo, then cut to the stage. The camera swept across Solar at center, jaw sharp under the lights, mic glittering in her hand like a blade.

Instantly, the room tilted toward the screen.

"Yo," Heeseung said, pointing with the lazy authority of a leader who'd just found leverage. "That's your cue, Jungwon. Trending number one again—not because of Bite Me, but because of you and your girl."

"She's not—" Jungwon started, but Sunghoon cut in, voice flat and merciless.

"Bodyguard aura," Sunghoon said. "You don't even stand like an idol anymore. You stand like a hired shadow. Pretty soon HYBE's gonna give you a badge."

The room laughed. Even Jay, usually reserved, cracked a grin.

Niki sprawled further into the cushions, smirking like he'd been waiting for his line. "Why drag it out? Just marry her already. Save us all the hashtags."

The laughter doubled, ricocheting off the walls. Heeseung slapped his knee, Jake nearly choked on his candy.

Jungwon's jaw flexed. He didn't rise to the bait—but he didn't deny it either. He just stared at the screen where Solar moved like she was carved for that stage, every line of her body telling the crowd that she belonged, even if half the internet was debating otherwise. His silence said more than words could have.

The others noticed. The laughter tapered into smirks, exchanged glances, the kind of unspoken camaraderie that meant we know, you know, let's not pretend.

"Bodyguard, husband, whatever," Sunghoon muttered. "Just don't forget to hit your formation next stage."

Jungwon didn't answer. He kept watching the screen, eyes locked on the way Solar lifted her chin at the final beat like defiance was choreography. His heart hit every count with her.

Later, when the noise had drained out of the room and the others were caught up in their own chaos—arguing about food delivery, gaming on phones, trading gossip—Jungwon slipped into the hall.

Solar was there, leaning against the wall outside LE SSERAFIM's dressing room, sipping from a water bottle like it might anchor her. Her shoulders looked straighter than yesterday, but her eyes still carried that storm.

She noticed him instantly. She always did.

"You shouldn't be here," she said softly, almost apologetic. "Every step you take near me feeds them."

Her words landed heavy. The hallway lights buzzed overhead, flickering like they knew they were eavesdropping.

Jungwon stopped just short of her, gaze steady, voice low enough that only she could hear. "Let them watch."

Her fingers tightened on the bottle. "Won—"

"I mean it." His stance was immovable, that bodyguard aura the others mocked now sharpened into something deliberate. "I'm not hiding in shadows anymore. If they want to turn this into a scandal, fine. Let them. I'll be here either way."

She exhaled sharply, frustration tangling with something warmer. "You don't understand. Every camera, every post—it doesn't just touch you. It claws at me. At the girls. At everything I've worked for."

His eyes darkened, jaw tense, but his tone softened like a blade wrapped in velvet. "And you don't understand, Sol. They can rip me apart all they want—I'll still choose this. I'll still choose you."

The hallway seemed to fold smaller around them. Somewhere down the corridor, staff voices called call times, but none of it breached the bubble forming between them.

Her chest ached, torn between dread and desire. Every instinct screamed at her to pull away, to keep distance, to protect him. And yet—she craved it. His nearness, his immovable claim, the way his presence calmed the paranoia even as it fueled the rumors.

Her water bottle was slick in her hand. She gripped it tighter, as though that could keep her steady. "You terrify me," she whispered, the confession slipping out raw, trembling.

Jungwon tilted his head, eyes dark but steady, like he'd been waiting to hear those words. "Good," he murmured. "Because that means you feel it, too."

Her throat closed around a thousand denials that wouldn't come. He was too close now, not touching her, but the heat of him pressed against her like a second skin. She could smell the faint trace of cologne, sweat from rehearsal still clinging to his hoodie, sharp and human and undeniably him.

He lifted a hand, slow enough that she could have stopped him. She didn't. His fingers brushed the edge of her sleeve, just grazing the fabric near her wrist as though testing how much weight she'd let him carry.

Her breath caught. She should've stepped back. She didn't.

"You think distance protects me," he said, voice a rough whisper. His gaze flicked to her lips, then back to her eyes. "But it kills me. Every day I don't close this space, it eats at me."

Her pulse stuttered. She wanted to say no, wanted to tell him he was reckless, that the whole world was watching. But her body betrayed her, leaning a fraction closer, like her gravity had already chosen.

"Won..." Her voice cracked, feather-light but desperate.

His thumb shifted higher, grazing her jawline now, the barest touch—so slight she could pretend it hadn't happened. But it had. It burned.

"Not yet," he said, echoing his own words from before, but this time they sounded like a promise instead of restraint. His hand lingered just long enough to leave a ghost of heat on her skin, then dropped back to his side.

The loss of contact was almost worse than the touch itself. She had to grip the bottle again to stop herself from swaying toward him.

Down the hall, a staffer's laugh broke the silence, a reminder that reality still existed. That cameras could be anywhere. That their names were hashtags braided together.

Jungwon leaned back just slightly, enough to give her breath but not enough to break the pull. "Let them think what they want," he said low. "I'll still be here tomorrow. And the day after. Until you stop fighting me."

Her lips parted, but no sound came. Because the truth was, she wasn't fighting him anymore. Not really.

He stepped back first, his expression unreadable except for the faintest twitch of his mouth—like he'd just won a battle she didn't remember agreeing to fight.

And then he was gone, leaving her pressed against the wall, skin burning where his fingers had touched, heart hammering out the secret she hadn't written down yet:

She didn't want him to stop.




Jungwon slipped back into the dressing room, hoodie still pulled low like it could hide the storm in his chest. The others barely glanced up at first—Sunoo was scrolling his phone, Jake had stolen Niki's charger, and Heeseung was stretched across the couch like a king in exile.

Then Niki narrowed his eyes, leaning forward with the precision of a younger brother catching an older one in a lie. "You were gone," he said flatly. "Like... suspiciously gone."

Heeseung perked up immediately. "Don't tell me you got lost between here and the bathroom. That'd be embarrassing for our fearless leader."

Sunghoon smirked into the mirror, not bothering to turn around. "More like he found something better to guard than his bladder."

Jake let out a laugh that cracked halfway into a wheeze. "Bro disappeared for ten minutes and comes back looking like he just walked through a K-drama hallway scene."

Even Sunoo, who usually stayed out of the pile-ons, chimed in without looking up: "Check his pulse. Looks accelerated."

The room dissolved into chuckles, the kind that stacked on each other until the air itself vibrated with boy-coded chaos.

Jungwon tugged his hoodie tighter, jaw ticking. He could've argued, but he didn't. His silence, the faint color warming his ears, did more than any denial could have.

"Bodyguard my ass," Niki said, flopping back onto the cushions. "You're halfway to writing vows."

"Let him," Heeseung added lazily. "At least then the hashtags would have a happy ending."

The TV on the wall flickered back to life with the replay of Le Sserafim's stage. Solar filled the screen again—chin lifted, eyes sharp, the world's noise falling away under the weight of her presence.

Every head turned toward the image. Jungwon did too, and this time, he didn't bother to hide the way his chest pulled toward it.

The others saw. They didn't press further, but their smirks lingered like punctuation.



The apartment was quiet in that sharp way only late nights could manage. Even the city outside felt muted, headlights dragging across the blinds like ghosts.

Jaehyun stood at the window, sunglasses still on though the room was dim, his coat draped heavy across his shoulders. The light of his phone painted his jaw in stark relief, unreadable as he scrolled, typed, deleted, sent. He didn't pace—Jaehyun never wasted movement. He was a figure cut from stone, carved to withstand storms rather than bend in them.

Chaerin, however, was a storm. She stalked the length of the living room in her designer sweats, ponytail swinging like a whip. Her voice cut through the silence.

"We should be blasting him, not hiding. Expose Jihoon for what he is. Burn Danielle's little empire to the ground. Let the world choke on the truth for once."

Her nails tapped furiously against her phone screen as if she could will the posts into existence herself. Her fire was pure, reckless—the same streak that Jungwon carried in his blood, mirrored here in her glare.

"You want fire?" Jaehyun said, voice flat, eyes never leaving the glass reflection of his phone. "Fire spreads. Fire leaves nothing standing. You're asking for chaos we can't contain."

Chaerin threw her hands up. "Chaos is already here! Sol's wrist is bandaged again, she can't even breathe without rumors painting her as unfit, and what are we doing? Sitting here while HYBE releases half-truths and Jihoon sharpens his knife?"

The sharp sound of a soda can cracking punctured the room. Juyeon slouched deeper into the couch, scrolling stan accounts with one hand while sipping with the other. His voice was casual, but his words bit.

"They're eating her alive out there," he muttered, eyes glued to his screen. "Solar trending with Jungwon, fancams dissected frame by frame, hashtags looping like vultures. And Danielle's feeding them more every hour. It's a buffet for antis."

He tilted his phone toward the room, showing a headline with Solar's blurred photo beside Jungwon's stage smile. "Romance or Reckless? The Downfall of an Idol Duo." Juyeon smirked without humor. "Catchy, isn't it?"

Chaerin spun on him. "Don't you get it? They'll destroy her. And us, if we don't act."

From the far end of the couch, Yein set her textbook down, her expression pale. She had been quiet the whole time, but now her voice trembled with something that wasn't just fear—it was reason.

"What if Jihoon really does it?" she asked, small but sharp. "What if he exposes everything? The family's past. The things we've tried to bury. You think HYBE can shield us from that? You think the public won't turn Solar into the villain for it?"

The question hung heavy. Even Chaerin hesitated, the weight of their skeletons pressing in.

Jaehyun finally pocketed his phone, turning from the window with the slow inevitability of a man who always ended arguments. His expression was unreadable behind the tinted lenses, but his voice was enough.

"Jihoon won't expose anything," he said evenly. "He's too careful. If he opens his mouth, he burns himself too. That's why he hasn't acted yet. He's waiting for us to break first."

"And what?" Chaerin snapped. "We just sit here? Wait until he hurts Sol again? Until Danielle drags her down so far she can't get back up?"

Jaehyun's jaw flexed once, barely visible. "We survive because we don't make noise. Because we hold the line in silence. That's what keeps this family alive."

Juyeon let out a dry laugh, scrolling again. "Yeah, well, guess who doesn't do silence? Your boy Jungwon. He's out there confronting Danielle like it's his personal hobby. Not exactly subtle."

Yein's head whipped up. "What?"

Even Chaerin froze. "He... what?"

For the first time, Jaehyun's gaze shifted, the faintest crack in his stone mask. "You think I don't know?" he asked quietly. "He's reckless. Loud. A boy who believes fire is the only way forward. I warned him, and he ignored me."

Chaerin's face flickered between shock and something close to grudging admiration. "Good," she whispered. "At least someone's willing to—"

"Stop." Jaehyun's voice cut like a blade, sharper than her fury. "Don't confuse recklessness for strength. Jungwon is out of my control, but you? You are not. And you will not drag this family into his war."

Silence fell. Only Juyeon's scrolling filled the space, punctuated by faint sighs of disgust at whatever comment he read next. Yein curled back toward her book, but her knuckles stayed white on the page.

Jaehyun turned back to the window, his reflection cold, immovable.

"This family doesn't survive because of fire," he said at last, voice low enough to chill. "We survive because of silence. Jungwon hasn't learned that yet."

The words lingered like smoke, and none of them dared to answer.



Back at the Le Sserafim dorm, it was quieter than it should've been—muted footsteps, muffled laughter from somewhere down the hall, the faint hum of the city outside the glass. Solar sat cross-legged on her bed with the notebook Jungwon had given her open on her lap, pen balanced between her fingers.

She stared at the blank page for a long time. Every time she wrote lately, it felt like carving pieces out of herself—small, sharp truths that wouldn't survive anywhere but here.

Finally, the words slipped out, ink catching the weight she couldn't speak aloud:

Everyone wants to protect me in their own way. But protection is just another kind of cage. And I don't know which walls will break first—the ones around me, or the ones inside me.

She capped the pen and let the notebook fall shut, the soft sound like a secret sealed.

Outside, a car horn echoed. The air in the room thickened, and Solar lay back against her pillow, staring at the ceiling. Love, loyalty, fear, manipulation—they all pressed down like invisible walls.

And in the silence, she couldn't tell which was more dangerous: the enemies waiting outside, or the cages built by the people who swore they loved her.


67: Still Strength

The stage lights still clung to Solar's skin as if they hadn't realized the performance was over. Sweat dried under the collar of her sequined jacket, her pulse still drumming from the encore chant echoing in her ears. Le Sserafim had ducked into the dressing room in a storm of laughter and water bottles, Eunchae humming the last chorus on loop until Chaewon hushed her with a towel to the head.

Solar had smiled, nodded at the staffers offering quick notes, let herself be touched up with powder and hairspray like she was a canvas being prepared for another showing. She laughed at Yunjin's joke about stage fog and tripped cords. She was there. Present enough to pass.

But the dread hadn't left her chest since she woke up.

Her phone buzzed against the vanity, screen flashing with a notification. She wiped her palm on her jacket before picking it up.

[Manager Kang]: HYBE execs in Meeting Room 4. Come now.

Her heart pinched. Of course. They'd watched the stage. Maybe they wanted reassurance. Maybe they wanted her to say the right words in the right order, to prove she was still salvageable despite the hashtags and the doctored audio clips.

She glanced around the room. Chaewon was bent over a staffer's clipboard, Yunjin was busy wiping glitter off Eunchae's cheek, Sakura and Kazuha were comparing fan gifts. Nobody looked up when Solar stood, jacket half-zipped.

"I'll be back," she murmured, not sure anyone heard.

The hallway swallowed her quick footsteps. It smelled of floor wax and stale coffee, the kind of corridor every backstage had, where voices blurred into the mechanical whir of air conditioning. She followed the numbers on the doors until she reached 4.

The handle was already ajar, the door cracked. She pushed it open gently, breath held—

—and froze.

No execs. No clipboards. Just Jihoon.

He was leaning against the far table, hands tucked into the pockets of his black jacket, like he'd been waiting for hours. His smile was slow, cruel, curling without ever reaching his eyes.

"Seriously?" His voice was a razor dipped in honey. "You're still that gullible?"

Solar's stomach dropped.

She spun on her heel, reaching for the door—but it slammed shut with a violent kick before she could grip it. Jihoon was faster than she remembered, or maybe she'd forgotten the way fear made him seem everywhere at once.

"Going somewhere?" he drawled, stepping closer.

"Move." Her voice cracked but she forced it steady. "Now."

"Relax." He tilted his head, all mock concern. "I just wanted to talk. Like old times."

"Old times are dead." She edged sideways, looking for an angle to slip past him.

His hand shot out, clamping around her wrist. The hurt one.

Pain spiked like a firework under her skin. She hissed, jerking back, but his grip only tightened. The cast, already loosened from weeks of wear, groaned under the pressure.

"Still so fragile," he murmured, eyes glittering. "Some things don't change."

Solar's teeth grit. With a surge of anger, she twisted hard, ripping her arm free. The cast snapped, clattering to the floor. The sudden release left her wrist throbbing, but she stood taller, cradling it against her chest.

Jihoon blinked at the broken cast, then barked out a laugh. "Look at you. Thinking you've grown claws." He stepped closer, the air between them shrinking. "You really believe you're stronger than me now?"

"I know I am." Her voice shook, but the words cut clean.

Something sharp flickered across his face. The smile vanished.

He moved fast, slamming her back into the wall. Her breath punched out of her lungs as his arm pinned her shoulders, his other hand closing around her wrist again. Pressure built, white-hot, her bones protesting.

"Don't play hero, Sol," he hissed. His breath smelled faintly of alcohol and mint, a cocktail she remembered too well. "You'll always be the same scared little girl who let me decide how strong you were."

Her heart thrashed. The wall dug into her spine. She tried to shove him off with her free hand, nails scraping against his jacket, but he pressed harder, thumb digging into her pulse point.

The world narrowed to pain and panic.

"See?" His voice dropped to a hiss. "Still breakable."

Something inside her rebelled. A memory of Jungwon's voice—Not on my watch. A memory of Chaerin's fire, Jaehyun's cold vow, Yunjin's unflinching gaze.

"I'm not sixteen anymore," she spat, forcing the words through clenched teeth.

Her body surged. She wrenched her arm sideways, ignoring the tearing pain. Jihoon staggered half a step from the force, surprise flashing in his eyes.

Solar shoved him back, hard enough that his shoulder clipped the table. Papers slid to the floor. Her chest heaved, fury shaking her limbs.

"Touch me again," she said, voice raw, "and I swear you'll regret it."

For a beat, silence.

Then Jihoon's lips curved in something uglier than a smile.

"Cute," he sneered. His hand darted up, gripping her throat—not enough to choke, just enough to remind her he could. His face hovered inches from hers, venom dripping from every syllable. "You think this new little world of yours changes anything? I made you weak once. I can do it again."

Her wrist screamed under the weight of his earlier hold. Fear prickled under her skin, but anger burned hotter, daring her to fight.

Still, her breath hitched. Her vision tunneled.

Jihoon leaned in closer, voice low, poisonous: "Maybe I should prove it."

Jihoon's hand pressed just firm enough at Solar's throat to make the walls feel closer. Not crushing—yet. Just the reminder of pressure, the promise of what he could do if he wanted. The weight of it was worse than the pain: the way he leaned in like he owned the moment, like she hadn't fought her way out of this cycle once before.

Her lungs pulled shallow breaths, too quick. Each one scraped her chest raw.

Don't freeze. Don't let him win.

Her pulse battered against his palm. Her body screamed move, but fear anchored her feet. Every nerve flickered between fight and collapse, muscle memory dragging her back to years she had buried. His words echoed—scared little girl.

The corner of his mouth curled. He felt her hesitation, her stumble, like it was proof he was right.

"You've always needed someone to handle you," he whispered, breath seeping against her cheek. "First me, now what? That boy who follows you around like a stray? What's his name? Jung—"

"Don't," she snapped, voice sharp despite the tremor beneath it.

His laugh was a low, awful thing. "Oh, I see. He's the new shield. Cute. Does he know what you're really like when it's just you? How fast you break when someone pushes the right way?"

Rage flared hot. Her fingers curled into fists at her sides, nails digging into her palms until crescents bit her skin.

Not weak. Not anymore.

Her free hand shot upward, shoving at his chin, forcing his face away. It was clumsy, fueled by instinct, but it made him stumble half a step back. Air rushed into her chest, ragged and greedy.

For a flicker of a moment, victory sang in her veins.

Then Jihoon's expression snapped into something sharper. His eyes, dark and cold, stripped the brief triumph away.

"You really don't learn, do you?"

He lunged. His grip clamped down on her wrist again—raw skin screaming where the cast had been. He twisted, sharp enough to make the joint cry out, to make her gasp. The pain jolted up her arm like lightning.

"Stop—" she choked, struggling against the leverage, her back hitting the wall again. "Let me go!"

He didn't. His voice dropped, low and deliberate, each word meant to pin her harder than his hands.

"You think you're stronger. But all I see is the same girl who begged me not to tell anyone her secrets. Who'd do anything to keep her precious family from finding out what kind of life she really lived." His smile was venom. "Maybe I should tell them. Maybe I should tell everyone."

Her chest collapsed inward. The old terror clawed up her ribs—of exposure, of shame, of the past she had spent years locking away.

"No," she rasped, throat burning.

"Yes," he hissed. His thumb pressed against the bone of her wrist until her vision flickered white. "Imagine the headlines. Perfect little Solar, idol of two worlds, built on lies. Wonder what your fans will think when they see the truth."

Her body trembled, but fury sparked in the cracks of fear. She shoved against his chest, nails dragging, and spat the words like fire:

"You don't get to own me anymore."

Jihoon's grip wrenched harder, sharp enough to make her knees weaken. The crack of strained bone echoed in her ears, whether real or imagined. Pain shot up her arm, hot and searing.

"You're mine to break," he snarled.

Solar's heart thundered. She twisted again, shoving, scratching, refusing to fold. Even as tears pricked her eyes, even as pain seared down her arm, she bit out a single word, steady and fierce:

"No."

The room vibrated with silence. His eyes blazed with the promise of what came next. His hand lifted, ready to pin her harder, to prove his point—

And then—

The door slammed open.

The sound cracked through the room like a gunshot.

Jihoon's head whipped toward it just as a blur of black hoodie and fury surged inside. Jungwon.

His hand locked onto Jihoon's shoulder before words could even form. The force of it sent Jihoon staggering back, grip ripping free from Solar's wrist. She gasped, cradling her arm against her chest as the sudden absence of pressure sent her body reeling.

Jungwon's face was carved in stone, his eyes molten. The fury there wasn't loud—it was volcanic, silent in the way mountains were silent before they destroyed.

"You," Jungwon said, voice low, each syllable vibrating with restrained violence.

Jihoon laughed breathlessly, straightening, trying to brush it off as if he hadn't been caught mid-attack. "What, the puppy thinks he's a wolf now?"

Jungwon didn't answer with words. His fist connected with Jihoon's jaw in a crack that reverberated through the room.

Jihoon stumbled back into the table, clutching his face, shock flashing before the mask returned. "Crazy bastard—"

"Touch her again," Jungwon growled, stepping forward, "and I'll make sure you can't touch anyone ever again."

His chest heaved, breath sharp, but his gaze never left Jihoon. Solar stood frozen against the wall, arm trembling as she held her wrist, her heart splitting between terror and something else—something that rose with every word Jungwon spat, every line of his body angled like a shield between her and Jihoon.

Jihoon spat blood onto the floor, then straightened with a twisted smile. "Didn't know idols had it in them." His eyes flicked past Jungwon to Solar, slow and deliberate. "This won't end here. You'll see."

He shoved past Jungwon, staggering toward the door with a limp swagger.

The silence that followed was deafening.

Solar's breath came too fast, too shallow. Her back slid against the wall until she caught herself, forcing her legs to hold. Her wrist throbbed like it had been set on fire, but she barely felt it over the rush in her chest.

Jungwon turned, shoulders still rigid, chest still rising in jagged breaths. The fury hadn't faded from his eyes—not entirely—but now it was directed at the sight of her, cradling herself in the corner.

For once, he didn't soften.

He just stared.

And then his gaze dropped to her lips.

Her breath hitched.

"Won—" she whispered, not sure if it was thanks or plea or something in between.

His jaw clenched. He closed the space between them in a stride, one hand rising to brace the wall beside her head, the other hovering just shy of her arm like he was holding back from touching her too hard. His face was inches from hers, fury still radiating off him like heat.

And before she could say another word—he kissed her.

It wasn't gentle. It wasn't planned. It was hot, desperate, angry—a clash of teeth and breath and the sound of her gasp swallowed into his mouth.

Every ounce of restraint, every week of circling, every pull they'd fought—it broke here.

For Solar, the world collapsed into the taste of him, the weight of him, the way his kiss said everything he hadn't dared: mine, mine, mine.

Her free hand fisted in his hoodie. Her body, trembling seconds ago from fear, trembled now from the sheer force of gravity between them finally snapping.

When he pulled back, breath ragged, his eyes burned. Not soft. Not apologetic. But sure.

"Not on my watch," he whispered, voice raw. And she couldn't bring herself to disagree.

The kiss ended, but the air didn't loosen.

Solar's chest still heaved against his, her lips tingling, her pulse roaring like she'd sprinted miles. Jungwon's hand was still braced against the wall by her head, his forehead nearly pressed to hers, both of them drowning in the silence left behind.

Neither moved. Neither dared.

Finally, Jungwon drew in a breath sharp enough to hurt. He pulled back just an inch, his eyes flicking across her face like he was searching for something—permission, regret, anything that might tell him whether he'd just ruined them both.

"I shouldn't have—" he began, voice low and ragged.

Solar's lips parted, but no words came. Her throat burned. She didn't know how to say that it hadn't felt wrong, that it had felt like something waiting under her skin for months, years maybe. She didn't know how to say she wasn't sure if she was more terrified of Jihoon or of what she wanted right now.

Jungwon swallowed hard, his jaw tight. He pushed a hand through his hair, pacing a half-step back before forcing himself still again. His chest rose, fell. The fury that had flared so violently minutes ago was still in him, but it had dimmed into something more uncertain. Something human.

He let his eyes drop to her arm.

Her sleeve was pushed back, the makeshift bandage loose and crumpled where Jihoon had grabbed. Dark marks were already blooming on her skin, a map of his violence laid bare.

Jungwon's face hardened. He stepped closer, but this time his voice was steadier, careful. "Did he—" He stopped, tried again. "Did he bring it back? The injury?"

Solar glanced down at her wrist, then back at him. Her mouth opened, then closed. No sound came. Her chest trembled instead, breaths unspooling uneven, tears pushing sharp behind her eyes.

She shook her head once, but the movement was fractured, not conviction—like her body wasn't sure of anything anymore.

Jungwon's throat worked. He reached out slowly, his fingers grazing the edge of the loose bandage before he pulled back again like it might burn him. "I'll buy you another cast," he murmured. "A better one. One that—" His voice cracked faintly. "One that'll hold."

That undid her.

The tears that had been clinging to the edges of her lashes finally broke free, sliding down before she could stop them. Her hand came up too late to hide it.

She hated crying in front of anyone. She hated it more when it was because of him, because of Jihoon, because of the way her body remembered what it was like to be powerless. But the tears came anyway, hot and fast, betraying her.

Jungwon didn't flinch. He stepped forward and wrapped his arms around her.

It wasn't careful at first. It was desperate—like if he didn't hold her right now, the world might take her again. His hoodie pressed rough against her cheek, his heartbeat a rapid stutter beneath it.
