Solar's fists hovered for a breath, resisting, before her body gave in. She gripped the back of his hoodie, clung to it like the only solid thing in the room. Her sob hitched against his chest, muffled, shame tangling with relief.

"I'm sorry," she choked out, words warped by tears. "I'm so sorry, I—"

"Stop," Jungwon cut in, voice fierce but soft, low against her hair. His arms tightened. "You don't say that. Not about this. Not to me."

Her tears soaked the fabric at his chest. He didn't let go.

The minutes blurred, their breathing slowly syncing, the silence thick with everything they couldn't yet say.

At last, he pulled back just enough to see her face, his hand brushing the damp hair from her temple. His thumb hovered along her cheekbone, not quite touching, like he was afraid one more move might shatter the fragile air they'd built.

His eyes searched hers, still dark, still burning, but steadier now. "There's no going back after this," he said quietly. It wasn't a question. It was a truth laid bare.

Solar's lips trembled. She nodded once, small but certain, even as fear pressed cold at the base of her spine.

Because he was right. There wasn't.























68: After They Felt It

The dorm lights were dimmed low, the kind of quiet where every small sound carried. Eunchae's blanket rustled as she shifted closer on the couch, wide eyes fixed on Solar like she was listening to a ghost story. Chaewon sat opposite her, posture straight, arms folded across her chest like she was holding herself back from pacing. Yunjin was right beside Solar, knee brushing hers, hand poised like she wanted to reach out but was waiting for permission.

Solar had been staring at her bandaged wrist for so long it almost didn't feel like hers anymore. The ache was back—not the sharpness from Jihoon's grip, but the phantom kind, the kind that came when memory lived under skin.

She swallowed hard, her throat tight. "It was him," she said finally. The words dropped into the circle like stones. "Jihoon. He tricked me into a room. Grabbed me. Same as before."

Eunchae's hand flew to her mouth. Chaewon's jaw clenched so hard Solar heard the grind of her teeth. Yunjin's hand found her wrist at last, careful but steady, grounding.

Solar blinked fast. "He—he tried to break it again. The same way. Said I was still that weak girl. Said nothing's changed." Her voice faltered, cracked. "But I fought back this time."

"Damn right you did," Yunjin muttered, but her voice wobbled with anger.

Chaewon leaned forward, her tone clipped but fierce. "You should've told staff immediately. Or called us. Or—" She broke off, sighing hard through her nose, one hand pinching the bridge of it. "Of course you didn't. You never do."

Solar's shoulders sagged under the weight of that truth. "I'm sorry," she whispered.

"It's not about sorry." Chaewon's voice softened, but her eyes burned. "It's about you not bleeding alone anymore."

The silence pressed heavy until Eunchae blurted out, "But then—what about Jungwon?" Her voice squeaked, the way it always did when she was nervous but trying to be brave. "You said he was there too, right? That he stopped Jihoon?"

Solar froze. She hadn't meant to tell them everything, but the kiss still burned on her lips like it was branded there. She couldn't keep it in.

"Yeah," she said, voice small. "He—he pulled Jihoon off me. Hit him." Her chest rose and fell like she was running though she hadn't moved. "And then... he kissed me."

Eunchae's blanket tumbled to the floor. "WHAT—"

Yunjin shot her a look sharp enough to slice through the air. "Not now, maknae."

But even Yunjin's composure cracked; her eyes widened, flicked to Chaewon, back to Solar. Chaewon closed her eyes briefly, exhaled through her nose like she was forcing herself to stay calm.

Solar bit her lip. "It wasn't planned. It wasn't... soft. It was—" She broke off, heat crawling up her neck. "It was angry. And desperate. And it felt like... like we couldn't stop it."

The girls traded looks. A whole conversation passed between them in silence.

Finally, Chaewon spoke, voice low but firm. "You know what this means, Sol."

"I know," Solar whispered. "The rumors. The hashtags. If anyone ever saw—" Her breath hitched, eyes stinging. "I'll ruin him. He's ENHYPEN's leader. HYBE's golden boy. And I'm..." She gestured vaguely at herself, at the bandage, at the storm that clung to her like a shadow. "I'm the one they already think is unstable."

Yunjin's hand tightened on hers. "Stop. You don't get to talk about yourself like that. You're not ruining him. You didn't ruin anything."

"But I—"

"No." Yunjin's voice cut sharp, protective. "He made a choice, Sol. Same as you. Stop acting like you're poison."

Chaewon's eyes softened at that, though her mouth stayed set. "The company won't see it that way. You know that."

Eunchae, quieter now, murmured, "But we see you that way. And we're not going anywhere."

Solar's throat worked as she blinked fast, tears slipping free before she could stop them. For the first time, she didn't rush to hide them.

Chaewon leaned in, brushing a strand of hair from Solar's face. "We'll deal with whatever comes. Together. But promise us one thing."

"What?"

"No more hiding," Chaewon said firmly. "Not from us. Not from him. Not even from yourself."

Solar nodded, the motion tiny but real. Her tears wet her lips as she whispered, "Okay."

Yunjin nudged her with a soft smile. "Good girl. Now drink water before you dehydrate. You cry like a faucet."

Eunchae laughed through her own tears, the sound wobbly but warm. For a second, the room felt like a shield around Solar—a sisterhood woven tighter than rumor, tighter than fear.

And still, in the back of her mind, the memory of Jungwon's kiss pulsed like a heartbeat she couldn't shut off.




The ENHYPEN dorm was always loud in waves—peaks of chaos, then valleys of phones and half-naps. Tonight sat somewhere in the middle. The TV still hummed with the feed from the music show, Le Sserafim's encore just fading into commercials. Empty takeout containers littered the coffee table, the room smelling faintly of fried chicken and fabric softener.

Heeseung sprawled across one end of the couch like a king in exile, thumb scrolling, expression half-bored until his mouth curved. "Yo. HYBE dropped another statement." He tilted his screen so the others could see. "Guess who's the golden boy?"

Jungwon didn't look up.

Jake leaned over, squinting, then barked a laugh. "'We categorically deny any misconduct involving ENHYPEN or its members.' Wow. That's PR-speak for 'Jungwon is the company heirloom.'"

Sunghoon, comb still in hand like he couldn't abandon vanity even in downtime, muttered, "Check the fine print. Solar's name buried like a footnote." His tone was dry, factual, but the cut landed.

Niki snorted, flopping onto the armrest nearest Jungwon. "Translation: you're the diamond, hyung. She's the kindling. First spark, they'll torch her before they ever touch you."

That earned a ripple of uncomfortable laughter—half-joke, half-truth.

Even Sunoo, who usually stayed away from the sharp edges of the group's humor, spoke up softly. "They're eating her alive out there." His gaze flicked to Jungwon, hesitant. "If you're gonna keep standing next to her, you'll be eaten too."

Jake jabbed a finger toward Jungwon, grin creeping back. "But hey—at least you're trending. Solar and Jungwon hashtags. Forget Bite Me—guess the real concept is bodyguard romance."

"Bodyguard? Nah," Niki corrected, eyes glinting. "He's got husband aura now. Next comeback, we'll rebrand."

The room laughed again, rougher this time, the kind that covered discomfort with noise.

Jungwon tugged at his hoodie sleeves, jaw locked. He didn't rise to the bait. Didn't even smirk. The silence stretched—then snapped.

"Jihoon hurt her tonight."

The words landed like a rock through glass. The laughter stopped mid-breath.

Jake's grin vanished. Sunghoon set his water bottle down with a muted clink. Even Niki, so quick with his mouth, froze.

Heeseung sat up straighter, phone sliding forgotten into his lap. His voice was quieter now, carrying the weight of someone who already half-knew. "Tell us."

Jungwon's eyes finally lifted, and the fury there was volcanic. "He lured her. Grabbed her wrist. Tried to break her again." His hands flexed, as if remembering the moment they'd ripped Jihoon away. "I was there. I stopped him."

The room tilted toward him, collective gravity pulling in. No jokes now. Just silence, bracing.

"Did he—" Sunoo started, then stopped, words swallowed by the heavy air.

"She's okay," Jungwon said, voice rough, a promise and a lie at once. "But this doesn't end unless I end it."

The boys exchanged glances—an unspoken agreement that this was bigger than banter, bigger than fandom drama or trending tags. Heeseung dragged a hand down his face, muttering, "Fuck."

Niki leaned forward, elbows on his knees, grin gone, replaced with something sharper. "So what now, hyung?"

Jungwon didn't answer. His silence was answer enough.

The silence stretched, thicker than the walls of the dorm. The TV cycled from commercials to some late-night rerun, ignored. Jungwon sat rigid, fists loose now but trembling faintly, the aftershock of rage not fully burned out.

Heeseung was the first to break it, voice low, steady. "You didn't answer Niki. What now?"

Jungwon's jaw flexed. He looked down at his hands, then up at the screen where the encore replayed, Solar center under the lights, chin lifted like defiance was a second skin. His throat worked once before he said it.

"I kissed her."

The words landed heavier than the Jihoon revelation.

Jake choked on his drink, sputtering. "You what—"

Sunghoon's brows shot up. "Backstage? As in—"

"Yes," Jungwon cut in, blunt. His voice carried none of their chaos. It was iron, final. "After I pulled Jihoon off her."

The room fractured.

Jake whistled low, half in disbelief, half in awe. "Well, damn. That's not bodyguard aura anymore. That's—"

"Suicidal," Sunghoon interrupted, sharp. His gaze was unflinching, clinical. "You know what this means if it gets out. Forget rumors—this isn't deniable anymore."

"Do you regret it?" Sunoo asked softly, searching his face.

Jungwon's answer was immediate. "No."

That stilled them all again.

He leaned forward, elbows on his knees, shoulders carved from tension. "She's been breaking alone for years. I won't let her anymore. I don't care if the whole world sees it."

Niki grinned faintly, but it wasn't teasing—it was sharp, almost proud. "Finally. Hyung's not just playing shadow. He's in it."

Jake exhaled, leaning back, running a hand through his hair. "Shit's gonna blow up. But... honestly? Respect."

Heeseung didn't speak for a long moment. His eyes stayed fixed on Jungwon, the leader weight heavy in his stare. Finally, he nodded once. "Then stand on it. Don't half it. If you're choosing this—choose it all the way."

On the wall, the TV cut to Le Sserafim's encore again, Solar hitting the final note, sweat shining like armor. The boys' attention shifted toward it instinctively.

Jungwon didn't look away from her.

His voice dropped, quiet but certain, threaded with that vow that had been growing sharper with every step:

"I already did."




That night, the dorm was dark except for the faint glow of Seoul spilling through the blinds. Solar lay flat on her back, the ceiling a pale blur above her. The others slept in tangled rhythms—Eunchae's soft snore, Chaewon's steady breathing, the occasional rustle of Yunjin's sheets. She should've felt safe in the cocoon of her group, but her chest still buzzed with the echo of Jihoon's hand on her wrist, the scrape of his voice dragging her back into sixteen.

And then—the kiss.

Her lips still tingled with it, phantom heat flooding in whenever she closed her eyes. She pressed her face into her pillow, guilt spiking alongside longing. If anyone saw, if anyone knew—what would it do to him? To them? He had so much to lose, more than she ever could.

She turned onto her side, pulling the blanket tighter around her shoulders like it could cage the conflict inside her. Her throat burned with the words she wouldn't say out loud. What if I break you too?

Across the city, in the quiet hum of the Enhypen dorm, Jungwon sat in his bunk with the blue glow of his phone painting his jaw. The others were asleep, sprawled like overworked kids—Jake mumbling in half-dream, Niki clutching his game console even in unconsciousness.

But Jungwon couldn't shut down. His mind was still wired, knuckles raw from how close he'd come to losing control. He opened a search bar instead, practical instinct soothing the adrenaline. Sports medicine. Stabilizing braces. Wrist support designed for athletes. He found one—durable, discreet, black. Added it to his cart.

Then he hesitated, thumb hovering, before tossing in a second item: a tiny keychain, something simple, a charm shaped like a crescent moon. It wasn't loud. It wasn't obvious. But it would be hers.

His pulse slowed as he checked out, the motion deliberate, grounding. If she wouldn't protect herself, then he would. If she wouldn't ask, then he'd keep showing up until she understood she didn't have to.

He set his phone on the mattress, leaning back, staring into the dark. The kiss replayed with brutal clarity—her breath against his mouth, the shock in her eyes, the way her body hadn't pulled away. For the first time, he wasn't just furious at Jihoon, or Danielle, or the world. He wanted her, fully, dangerously, in a way he couldn't undo.

In the silence, Solar clutched her blanket, whispering to herself: "What if I break you too?"

And miles away, Jungwon's lips shaped his own vow into the dark: "Not letting go. Not now."



















69: Unnamed Magnetism

The morning light slipped through the dorm blinds in thin bars, stripes of gold across the mess of coffee mugs, curling extension cords, and discarded costumes. Promotions were almost done, but the exhaustion wasn't. Solar sat cross-legged on the couch with her hoodie zipped high, chewing at a nail she'd already destroyed yesterday, when the doorbell rang.

Chaewon padded over, hair tied back in a messy bun, and accepted a small brown package from the courier. No sender listed, just Solar's name scrawled across the label in black pen.

"Delivery for you," Chaewon said, lifting it with one brow arched. "Secret admirer vibes."

Solar's stomach turned. Packages were dangerous these days. Fans sent things, haters sent worse. She reached for it cautiously, ready to tell Chaewon to trash it, but the weight was wrong—solid, dense, not the feather-light paranoia of merch or poisoned gifts.

She peeled the tape back.

Inside: a sleek black wrist brace, the kind athletes wore, reinforced with metal stays. Nestled beside it were neatly stacked Peach Tea packets—her brand. And at the bottom, a keychain: tiny, enamel, shaped like a silver star.

Solar's chest caught.

There was a note. Folded once, handwriting sharp and deliberate.

Stop pretending you don't need this. —W

Her throat closed.

"...oh my God," Eunchae squealed, plopping onto the couch beside her. "This is—this is a care package! No—no, it's a love letter disguised as protein powder." She held up the wrist brace like it was incriminating evidence. "Look at this. He's basically saying, 'Marry me and hydrate.'"

Yunjin leaned against the counter, arms crossed, her expression softer than her tone. "It's not a love letter. It's worse. It's proof. He's choosing to show up, no matter what HYBE or hashtags say."

Solar wanted to shrink into the cushions. The keychain glinted like it knew something she didn't.

Chaewon crouched low, steady gaze on Solar's face. "You do realize this complicates everything, right? HYBE's already working overtime to cover Jungwon, and you..." She trailed off, eyes darting to the wrist brace in Solar's lap. "They're not protecting you like they are him."

The words landed heavy. She knew it already, had seen the statements online—corporate shields built high and wide for Jungwon, while she was left bare. But hearing it out loud from Chaewon, the responsible one, the leader... it burned.

Eunchae wasn't listening. She clipped the star keychain onto Solar's bag with a grin. "There. Now you can't lose it. Lucky charm."

"Eunchae—" Solar tried to protest, but the younger girl just grinned wider, unbothered.

Yunjin cut through the noise with a quieter voice. "Do you feel safer, though? Knowing he's out there. That he's watching?"

Solar looked down at the note again. Her fingers trembled as she folded it back into her palm. She hated the truth of it. She did feel safer. Like he was building walls where hers had crumbled. But safe wasn't the same as free.

"I don't know what I feel," she admitted, voice small.

The girls didn't push. Chaewon reached over and squeezed her knee; Eunchae hummed some off-key melody meant to distract; Yunjin stayed leaning against the counter, eyes steady but kind.

The package sat open on the coffee table, humming with presence. Solar tugged the wrist brace onto her arm. It fit perfectly, snug, supportive, unyielding. Like him.

Her chest hurt.

Because it didn't just feel like a gift.
It felt like a claim.



The first ripple came through Twitter. A blurry, low-res photo of Chaerin from three years ago—back when she was still spiraling, hair bleached and uneven, eyeliner smudged like armor. She was leaning against a club wall, a drink in her hand, laughing with someone older and nameless.

The caption was the kill shot: Not the first Lee sibling with a messy past. Makes you wonder about the others, doesn't it?

By noon, the photo was everywhere. Hashtags bloomed ugly and fast: ChaerinScandal, NotSoPerfectFamily, LeeSisters.

Solar's group chat with the girls lit up with links, screenshots, Yunjin's curses typed fast enough to trip autocorrect. Chaewon, calm as ever, just texted: Don't engage. Don't scroll. Don't feed it.

But Solar scrolled anyway. She read comment after comment that didn't even stay on Chaerin. They twisted toward Jaehyun, toward Yein, toward her. This family's dirty. She's unfit to be an idol. No wonder she clings to Jungwon.

Her ears buzzed.

By the time HYBE released a statement, her hands were cold. The words were so familiar she could've written them herself: denial, concern, protection of "our artist's reputation." But the name wasn't hers. The name was Jungwon's.

HYBE's golden boy. Protected like a national treasure.
Solar wasn't even mentioned.

She sat at the kitchen table in the dorm with her phone screen glowing pale against her wrist brace. Eunchae kept babbling about how stupid people online were, trying to make her laugh. Yunjin sat close, shoulder pressed against hers, muttering that it didn't mean anything, that everyone knew Danielle's fingerprints were all over this.

But Solar couldn't unclench her jaw. The statement made it clear—HYBE would shield Jungwon with their whole body. She was a disposable limb.

And then came the second blow.

When she went to change for rehearsal, she found it: a photo slipped into her locker, paper glossy and cruel. Jungwon, caught from behind, walking down the street near the dorms. The photo was tilted like the camera had been held low, stalking.

On the back, scrawled in red pen: Not so untouchable.

Her stomach dropped.

The room spun around her—girls laughing at something down the hall, staff shouting call times, the fluorescent light buzzing overhead. But all she could see was the photo in her hand, and all she could hear was Jihoon's voice in her memory: You think he can protect you? You think he's stronger than me?

Her wrist brace felt suddenly tight, like it was squeezing instead of holding.

She shoved the photo deep into her bag before anyone else could see it. Her chest hurt, the kind of hurt that didn't come from being hit but from waiting for the hit to land.

Because this wasn't about her anymore. Jihoon was circling Jungwon. Hunting him. And it was Solar who'd painted the target on his back.



Solar hadn't expected to see him again so soon. Not here. Not like this.

Her phone buzzed earlier in the evening, Jaehyun's name on the screen with a message that made her blood run cold: Come to the apartment. We need to talk. No explanation. No invitation. Just command. She almost didn't go, but some part of her knew she'd regret not showing up more than she'd regret obeying.

When she arrived, the apartment was already taut with silence. Jaehyun stood by the window, black coat hanging heavy from his shoulders, sunglasses still on though the room was dim. Chaerin slouched on the couch, biting her nail and looking like she was waiting for a fight. Juyeon was half-curled in a chair, thumbs flicking over his phone, smirk permanent. Yein sat quietly in the corner, notebook open, pen frozen mid-sentence like she'd been interrupted.

Then the door opened again.

Jungwon stepped in, summoned by the same kind of clipped message. Solar's heart stumbled. She hadn't seen him since the kiss — that heated, furious break neither of them had dared to define. Now here he was, hoodie pulled low, hands in his pockets, posture braced as if he'd walked straight into an ambush.

Her brother had gotten his number somehow. Jaehyun always had his ways, invisible strings tugging through the dark.

The tension thickened instantly. Jungwon didn't look at her, not really — just one flicker of his eyes, enough to set her pulse off-beat, before locking onto Jaehyun with the sharp wariness of a soldier cornered.

The Lee apartment was a quiet graveyard of tension. Midnight painted the windows dark; only the lamplight carved their faces sharp.

Jaehyun stood by the window, black coat heavy, sunglasses still in place as if he needed barriers even indoors. His phone rested in his palm, thumb idle, unread messages glowing like embers.

Solar sat rigid on the couch, knees pressed together, fingers curling in and out of fists. Across from her, Jungwon claimed an armchair, shoulders tense, hoodie hood low. It was the first time she'd seen him since the kiss. A flicker of his eyes brushed hers—enough to unravel her stomach—before he locked on Jaehyun with the focus of a hawk circling prey.

Her brother had texted them both separately, somehow pulling Jungwon's number like he pulled everything else—out of shadows, out of silence. And now, here they were.

Chaerin slouched at one end of the couch, her body buzzing with energy she refused to sit still. Juyeon leaned in a chair, scrolling stan accounts, smirk in place like the world was a bad play. Yein sat in the corner, notebook open but pen frozen.

Jaehyun finally looked up, voice cold enough to crack glass.
"Enough games. Danielle's escalation tonight wasn't about you, Solar. It was about Chaerin."

The room stilled.

"She's spreading family rot now," Jaehyun continued. "Dragging names she shouldn't even know. That doesn't happen by chance. That happens when someone rattles the cage so hard, they go digging deeper." His eyes cut like blades toward Jungwon.

Solar's chest tightened. "You can't blame him for what she's doing—"

"Can't I?" Jaehyun's voice sliced over hers. "First Danielle leaks pictures. Then doctored audio. Now she touches family. And what's the common factor? Jungwon's confrontations. His fists. His fury."

Jungwon didn't flinch. His voice was low, tight. "She escalates because Jihoon feeds her. That's not on me. That's on them."

Jaehyun stepped forward, shoes whispering against the rug.
"And what happens the day your rage is the headline instead of hers? Hm? Who's to say they don't already have footage of that... passionate moment you were so focused on you forgot about the cameras around you?"

The air froze.

Solar's blood turned molten; her cheeks flared. Jungwon's jaw flexed, unreadable, the picture of a boy carved out of control.

Jaehyun let the silence hang before dropping the knife point.
"Don't worry. I deleted it." He tucked his phone back into his pocket. "But it's proof of exactly what I mean. Reckless. Evidence waiting to burn you alive."

His gaze turned on Solar, sharp and merciless.
"That includes you. You know what happens when you get attached to boys like this. You lose sight of everything you've worked for. Everything I've kept clean for you."

Her hands shook against her knees. "Don't you dare—"

"I do dare," Jaehyun snapped, the sunglasses glinting like armor. "You think this is loyalty? It's chaos. It's a cage. And you're walking into it blind."

Chaerin shot up from the couch, fire sparking. "Finally someone willing to fight for her and you're tearing him apart? He's the only one with the balls to actually—"

"Sit. Down." Jaehyun's command cracked the air. Chaerin froze mid-step, fury raw in her face, but dropped back down like her body was wired to obey him.

Juyeon didn't look up from his phone, voice lazy but pointed. "This is rich. Solar's being eaten alive online, and the two of you are in here measuring egos like it's sport."

"Shut it, Juyeon," Chaerin snapped again.

Yein finally spoke, her voice soft but cutting. "What happens if Jihoon exposes the rest? The things even we don't know? What then?"

The words hung heavier than anything else said.

Solar surged to her feet, voice shaking with fury. "Enough. Stop fighting about how to control me. I'm not yours to manage."

The silence that followed wasn't relief. It was war postponed.

Jaehyun's face stayed stone, unreadable. Jungwon's chest rose and fell like he was holding back another explosion. Solar's heart hammered against her ribs, her words echoing into the stillness like they belonged to someone braver.

The clash wasn't resolved. If anything, the fault line had only deepened.

The silence thickened, but she didn't wait for anyone to break it. Her pulse pounded so loud it drowned the room. She grabbed her jacket from the back of the couch, yanked it on with sharp movements.

"Sol—" Jungwon started, voice taut with restraint.

She didn't look at him. Couldn't. "Stay. Argue. Measure. I don't care. I'm done being the rope in your tug-of-war."

Her boots hit the hallway like gunfire. She shoved the apartment door open hard enough that it rattled against the frame, then slammed shut behind her.

Inside, the apartment seemed smaller, vacuumed of oxygen.

Chaerin stood halfway up again, fists trembling, eyes darting between Jaehyun and Jungwon. "Happy now? You broke her."

Juyeon's phone screen glowed in the dim light. His smirk had thinned, but his words were still venom-laced. "She's out there alone again. And all of us are still in here pretending we know what's best."

Yein closed her notebook softly, a quiet punctuation to Solar's exit. Her face was pale, her voice nearly a whisper: "She shouldn't have to scream to be heard."

Jaehyun didn't move from the window. His shadow stretched long across the rug, sunglasses still a mirror to the room. "She'll come back. She always comes back."

Jungwon's fists curled at his sides. "And when she does," he said, low and dangerous, "it won't be because of your silence."

For the first time, Jaehyun's jaw ticked beneath his stoicism. But he didn't reply.

The apartment stayed heavy, the kind of silence that wasn't peace but the prelude to another war.

"I'm not letting her walk out there alone," Jungwon said, voice flat enough to be a fact.

No one stopped him. The door clicked behind him with a gentleness that felt like defiance.




The stairwell was colder than the room had any right to be. Concrete swallowed sound; each step made a small, lonely echo. Solar was already a flight down, hair pulled into a messy knot like she'd tried to trap her heartbeat there and failed. When his foot hit the landing she flinched, pivoting hard, eyes bright with leftover fury and something that wasn't quite fear.

"You couldn't even give me five minutes?" she asked. The words came sharp, but her hands were trembling where they gripped the railing.

"I couldn't give you five seconds out here alone," he said.

They stood two steps apart, the stairwell light stuttering above them. A moth traced a doomed circle around the bulb and thumped it softly, over and over, like a warning no one understood.

Solar exhaled, the breath leaving her like she'd been punched. "You know he's going to blame you for everything until the sun burns out."

"I noticed," Jungwon said. He didn't move closer, but his presence crowded the air the way a doorway does—you knew it was there, even with your eyes closed. "He's allowed to hate my methods. He doesn't get to leave you alone with... this."

She barked a humorless laugh. "This. What a tidy word."

The trembling in her fingers finally slowed. She angled her arm against the rail, the black brace peeking from the cuff of her sleeve. He clocked it without meaning to—the exact model he'd ordered. The strap lay flat, fitted properly.

"It fits?" he asked, and the question sounded ridiculous the second it left him: too domestic for a stairwell full of ghosts.

Her mouth tugged. Not quite a smile. "Bossy gift. But yeah. It fits."

They let that sit between them for a beat, the smallest plank thrown over a ravine.

"Jaehyun said something," Solar added, eyes sliding past him as if the concrete could answer for him. "About cameras. That there could've been footage." Heat crept up her neck. "He said he deleted it."

Jungwon's jaw ticked. "Of course he did."

"You're not... mad?"

"I'm grateful," he said, and the word surprised both of them by how honest it felt. "And I'm still not going to pretend a camera is a reason to leave you alone."

She looked at him for a long time then, as if searching for the edge of his certainty and not finding it. Her voice dropped. "He said I lose myself with boys like you."

He let that land. "Do you?"

"I don't know." She swallowed. "I know I'm tired of being a prize people fight over. I know I hate that you and Jaehyun think this is about proving who's right."

"It's not," he said immediately, then caught himself, softened. "It's not for me. I'm not trying to win against him. I'm trying to stand with you."

The light buzzed; the moth kept flinging itself at fate. Solar rubbed her thumb along the seam of the brace, gathering threads into a sentence.

"There's something else," she said. "Danielle's mess is loud, but Jihoon..." She fished in her pocket and pulled out a photo, folded twice. She didn't hand it to him; she held it up like an accusation against the air. "This was in my locker." She flipped it. The glossy back flashed red scrawl: Not so untouchable.

He didn't need to see the front to know what it was. The fury that climbed his spine was familiar now, too familiar, but he kept it on a leash. "He's following me."

"He wants me to know he's following you," she said. The line between her brows deepened. "That's the point."

"Then let him know I see him, too," Jungwon said, the leash straining. "Let him learn what it feels like to be prey."

"Don't," she snapped, too fast, and then softer, "Don't go hunting because he goaded you. That's what he wants. Proof you're rash. Proof you're dangerous."

"Dangerous to who?" Jungwon asked. "Him?"

"Dangerous to you," she said, and the words pulled the air out of the stairwell. "Your career. Your boys. He's trying to prove you're not the protector I think you are. He's trying to make me watch you fall."

The echo of that truth rang around the concrete. Jungwon breathed through it, counting on the exhale the way he'd learned to on stage.

"I hear you," he said. "I do. I won't give him the spectacle he wants." A beat. "But I'm not stepping back from you to keep my name tidy."

Her eyes flicked up, fast. "It's not about tidy."

"Then call it what it is," he said, gentle but unflinching. "It's fear. And you're allowed to be afraid. But don't spend me for me. My name is mine to spend."

That tugged a startled sound out of her, somewhere between a scoff and a breathy laugh. "You talk like you've got infinite credit."

"I don't." His mouth quirked. "I'm just not swiping your card."

For the first time since she'd stormed out, the hard set of her shoulders eased. The air loosened a millimeter. Somewhere above them a door opened and closed, distant voices moving past, oblivious to the small battlefield on the stairs.

"What do you want from me, then?" she asked. There wasn't defensiveness in it this time; there was fatigue and something like hope's cautious cousin.

"Tell me what you need when it's dark like this," he said. "Tell me when to step forward, and when to be a wall behind you. I can be either." A beat. "I'm not here to make choices for you."

Her throat worked. That was the part that always snagged her—control offered back like a gift. Her fingers went to the edge of the brace again, tugged it tighter. When she spoke, it was almost a whisper.

"Tonight? Walk me to the end of the block. Then let me go the last stretch alone." She swallowed. "I need to feel like I can still move without being escorted."

"Okay," he said, like a vow.

They pushed through the stairwell door to the street. Night draped the block in thin, humid darkness, neon licking the edges of puddles left by an earlier rain. A cat darted under a parked scooter. A convenience store buzzed two corners down, fluorescent promise against the gloom.

They didn't walk close. Cameras lived everywhere in this city—on corners, in doorbells, in the bored hands of passersby. He gave her half a pace of space, matching his footfalls to hers, alert in that weird, quiet way that had become second nature around her. A car rolled past, slow; his eyes tracked it until the taillights pinched into distance.

Halfway down the block she stopped at the vending machine outside the convenience store, the one that always ate coins and sometimes spat miracles. She stared at the buttons like they could tell her what to do next.

"Peach tea?" he asked, because it felt like a language they shared now.

Her mouth twitched. "You're insufferable."

But when he fed the machine and the can clunked down, she took it. The metal was cold through the brace. She didn't open it. She just held it with both hands, as if it could anchor her to this exact second.

"You're wearing the keychain," he said, noticing the small silver star winking from her bag strap.

"Eunchae clipped it on and declared it law," she said, a ghost of a smile. "Rebellion would cause a diplomatic incident."

He nodded solemnly. "Never cross the maknae."

They stood like that for a moment: two kids under a flickering sign, pretending the world wasn't sharpening its knives. Traffic hissed. Somewhere above them, a TV bled laughter through a half-open window.

"Jaehyun deleted the footage," she said, returning to the wound because it was easier than ignoring it. "He thinks that proves he's right. That you and I... that we're reckless."

"Maybe we are," Jungwon said. "But we're not careless."

"What's the difference?"

"Careless forgets where the cameras are," he said. "Reckless remembers and decides it's worth it."

Her breath snagged. The can in her hands dented softly.

"I'm not asking you to risk everything," she said.

"I'm telling you I will," he answered, and then—because he heard his own stubbornness, because he didn't want to be Jaehyun with a new coat of paint—he added, "If you want me to."

The can's tab clicked once under her thumb. She didn't open it. "I hate this," she said, voice thin. "I hate how much easier it is to breathe when you're here."

"That's not something to hate."

"It is when it makes me stupid."

"You're not stupid, Sol."

She looked at him then, really looked, and the soft part under her ribs hurt. The ambient streetlight cut his cheekbones into something mythological; his eyes were all current. He hadn't touched her, not once since the door. It was a discipline she felt in her bones.

"End of the block," she reminded, because if she didn't, she might say yes to the gravity between them and call it mercy.

He nodded. They walked the last yards in a quiet that didn't feel empty. When the dorms came into view, she slowed. The security camera over the door blinked its red dot, a little mechanical heartbeat.

"This is where you let me go," she said, stopping just short of the pool of light.

He stopped too. For a second the urge to close the distance—just to set his hand against the side of her face, to match the shape of his palm to her—is almost loud enough to hear. He swallowed it.

"Text me when you're inside," he said.

"You first," she countered, and then rolled her eyes at herself, because compromise had turned them both into idiots.

"Deal," he said.

She hesitated, then tipped her face up just a fraction, eyes catching his. "Thank you for the brace."

"Stop pretending you don't need it," he said, and the tiniest grin ghosted across his mouth when she huffed a laugh.

He stepped back one pace, then another, as she moved forward into the camera's red eye. The door beeped at her keycard. She slipped inside without turning again.

Jungwon stayed where he was for a beat, watching the door like it could breathe. His phone vibrated in his pocket. He didn't have to check to know: a single star emoji.

He typed back: on.

Across the street, a figure paused at the far corner and then kept walking, just another shadow in a city that had too many of them. Jungwon clocked it, filed it, and didn't chase. Not tonight.

He drew his hood up and headed the other direction, counting his steps until the block curved, until the red camera dots thinned, until he could feel his heartbeat without tasting metal.

Two phones lit the dark, miles apart:

—Solar's screen: a tiny star above a single word.

—Jungwon's: her reply landing like a pulse in his hand.

Not peace. Not yet. But a truce they'd written themselves, on concrete and breath and a night that didn't take sides.

Solar's room was dark except for the thin blue glow of her phone screen. She sat on the edge of her bed, hair still damp from a too-fast shower, hoodie sleeves pulled over her hands. The noise of the dorm had faded—Eunchae already asleep, Yunjin humming faintly in the kitchen, Chaewon's door clicking shut. Quiet should've meant rest, but tonight it only made the weight in her chest louder.

Her phone buzzed. A Discord ping.

She blinked at it, mind catching up a second too slow. Shock server.

[crusty]: season's starting. prep block schedule drops tomorrow. you in?
[rascal]: don't flake. we need you.
[glister]: 🫡🫡🫡

Solar stared, thumb hovering. It felt like a different lifetime—the headset, the team calls, the Grand Finals trophy that still sat on her shelf. But here it was again, sharp and real: the off-season was over. The Shock was calling her back.

Her pulse picked up. Promotions with Le Sserafim weren't even done; their schedules still ran until next week. HYBE would flip if she even mentioned a tournament. And yet... she couldn't ignore it. Not when she'd promised the boys, not when she knew the community was watching, waiting to see if she'd stand or fold.

Two lives, colliding again. No clean compartments left.

She dropped her head into her hands, whispering to no one, "How the hell am I supposed to do this?"

Another buzz jolted her. A notification from Twitter—except not just any trend.

#SolarScandal
#ProtectEnhypen

She swallowed hard, thumb pressing against the crack of the screen protector. The hashtags pulsed like open wounds. She didn't click. She didn't have to.

Somewhere, someone was typing her downfall into existence. Somewhere, Danielle was laughing, feeding the beast. Somewhere, Jihoon was waiting, patient as a knife tucked behind a smile.

Solar pushed the phone facedown on her bed. But the dark didn't comfort. She could still feel it—the invisible eyes, the walls closing.

And outside, in the Seoul night, a shadow lingered across the street. Smoke curled from a lit cigarette. Jihoon's face glowed in the ember. His gaze wasn't on her window.

It was down the block.

On Jungwon.























70: What Happiness Costs

The fluorescent hum of the rehearsal hallway felt different now—less like a battlefield, more like a place where the war was finally quieting. Banners of Unforgiven and Bite Me still hung from the corkboard walls, but half the staff were already packing up boxes of old props, trading yawns as they stacked cables. Both groups had been running on fumes for weeks. Now, with the last stages almost behind them, there was relief threaded into the exhaustion, like finally unclenching a muscle you forgot you’d been holding.

Le Sserafim’s girls slumped along one couch in the shared green room, legs tangled over each other’s like they’d stopped bothering with boundaries a long time ago. Eunchae balanced a bag of chips on her knees, shaking it dramatically every time someone reached for one. Yunjin had stolen Sakura’s water bottle, sipping it with obnoxious slowness until Sakura started poking her arm in retaliation. Chaewon was quiet, as usual, but her gaze swept the room like she was mentally ticking boxes, already thinking about what came next.

Across from them, the Enhypen boys were sprawled in their own brand of chaos. Jake had his phone plugged into the speakers, cycling through random playlists until Sunghoon yelled at him to pick one and stop torturing them. Niki was stretched flat on the carpet like a starfish, scrolling mindlessly, tossing in commentary whenever it suited him. Heeseung had his hood pulled up, humming under his breath while he adjusted the strap of his guitar case—he’d brought it out of habit, as if downtime without practice made him itchy.

Jungwon sat closer to Solar than he probably should’ve, their shoulders not quite touching. His hoodie sleeve brushed against hers every time he shifted, subtle, like he didn’t notice. Solar noticed. Every nerve did. She kept her eyes on Eunchae instead, who was rattling the chip bag like a maraca.

“You realize you’ve eaten three-quarters of that yourself,” Yunjin deadpanned.

“Energy for the finale,” Eunchae replied cheerfully, mouth full. “I’m bulking.”

“On potato starch?” Kazuha asked, and the room cracked up.

The boys joined in without hesitation, their laughter mingling with the girls’ like this wasn’t supposed to be unusual.

“Honestly, respect,” Jake said, leaning forward to snag a chip before Eunchae smacked his hand away. “You’d fit right in with us. Niki lives off worse.”

“Hey,” Niki mumbled from the carpet, “at least I don’t drink three Pepsis before noon.”

“That was one time,” Jake protested, but his grin gave him away.

The girls giggled, Eunchae practically glowing from the attention. Solar caught herself smiling too, in spite of everything. There was something oddly grounding about seeing both groups like this—worlds that usually only touched in carefully curated collabs now overlapping like they were just… kids in the same building, tired and teasing each other.

Her phone buzzed in her pocket. Instinct sent a jolt through her chest—Jihoon? Danielle? Another shadow waiting to pounce? She fumbled it out and saw only a notification from Weverse, a trending post about their stage. She exhaled, shaky but trying to hide it.

Jungwon’s eyes flicked to her. Just one glance, sharp as a blade, like he’d read the panic anyway. He didn’t say anything, but the brush of his sleeve against hers lingered longer this time.

“Solar,” Eunchae piped up, crumbs at the corner of her mouth. “You’re spacing. Are you plotting how to steal my chips?”

The others laughed again, diffusing the tension she’d tied herself into. Solar forced a grin, shaking her head. “Wouldn’t dare. Those look like a health hazard.”

“Exactly,” Eunchae said proudly, hugging the bag.

Yunjin threw a pillow at her. The boys howled. For a few moments, Solar let herself laugh too. It didn’t erase the dread coiled at the base of her spine, but it softened it—like pressing a warm hand over a bruise.

The green room had fallen into a comfortable buzz, the kind that only came after weeks of shared exhaustion. For once, it didn’t feel like rival worlds pressed against each other. Le Sserafim’s girls had half a couch conquered, Eunchae holding court with her chip bag like it was treasure, while the Enhypen boys sprawled in a lopsided circle across the floor.

Solar perched at the end of the couch, close enough that Jungwon could claim proximity but not quite touch. Still, his hoodie sleeve brushed hers every few seconds—enough to make her pulse skip, enough to remind her that the kiss hadn’t been a fever dream. Every time she adjusted to make space, he leaned in again. Not obvious. Not deliberate. Just… steady, like gravity.

When Eunchae offered him a chip, Jungwon leaned forward to take one—his other hand shifting back, brushing against Solar’s on the cushion. Not quite holding. Not quite not.

Solar’s eyes flew wide. The kind of what do you think you’re doing? stare that had saved her from chaos a hundred times before.

Jungwon didn’t flinch. He chewed his chip slow, gaze sliding to hers with a flicker that said what if I was?

Her breath hitched. She pulled her hand back under the guise of fixing her hoodie sleeve. Her reflection in the vanity mirror across the room looked flushed.

The others kept teasing, oblivious.

“Solar spacing again,” Eunchae announced, dramatic gasp. “She’s plotting how to steal my chips, I know it.”

“I’m plotting how to hide you from your dentist,” Solar shot back, grateful for the deflection.

The room cracked up.

Before the laughter could die, the door opened. Chaerin swept in like she owned the building, designer bags dangling from her wrist.

“Sis!” she sang, heels clicking against the tile. “Congratulations on the wrap-up. You slayed, obviously. So I brought gifts.”

She tossed one sleek bag toward Yunjin, another at Eunchae (who squealed like she’d just been handed a winning lottery ticket), then one each for Sakura, Chaewon, and Kazuha. Perfume boxes, glossy accessories, things that screamed Chaerin’s Gucci-core love language.

“You didn’t have to—” Chaewon started, but Chaerin waved it off, smirking.

“I did. It’s called taste. You’re welcome.”

Jungwon shifted subtly beside Solar, arms crossing like armor. His eyes tracked Chaerin with quiet disapproval—he still hadn’t forgiven her firecracker meddling at the rooftop. Solar caught the shift and pressed her knee against his for just a second, a wordless don’t.

Chaerin dug into her bag again and pulled out one last box, smaller, wrapped in glossy paper. She hesitated. Everyone noticed.

“Uh-oh,” Yunjin said, grin sharklike. “Who’s that for?”

Chaerin’s ears went pink. “It’s nothing,” she muttered, and promptly chucked the box across the room. It landed squarely in Heeseung’s lap.

He blinked down at it. “...Me?”

“You didn’t see that,” Chaerin snapped, turning away too fast.

But Heeseung’s grin spread slow, infuriating. “Guess I did.”

The room erupted—gasps, whistles, Eunchae’s high-pitched “ooohhhh”. Even Jake leaned over with a wide-eyed “broooo.”

Heeseung unwrapped the box casually, like he did this every day. Inside sat a sleek silver bracelet, understated but expensive. He slipped it onto his wrist with a confidence that made Chaerin choke on her own bravado.

“Fits perfect,” he said, flashing it at her. “Thanks.”

Chaerin rolled her eyes so hard it was theatrical. “Don’t read into it.”

“Too late,” Sunghoon deadpanned.

Solar buried her laugh into her hand. Beside her, Jungwon didn’t laugh. He watched the easy exchange—the gift, the spark, the simplicity of it—and his jaw ticked.

Solar felt the shift immediately. She nudged him, whispering under the noise. “Don’t start.”

He leaned in, voice low enough for only her: “I’m not. Just… watching. Wishing it was that simple.”

Her throat tightened. She wanted to ask what part? The giving? The public part? The not having to hide? But she didn’t. Her wide eyes met his again, and for a moment the room’s chaos blurred.

Eunchae’s shriek pulled her back—she’d stolen Heeseung’s bracelet to “test the quality,” and he was mock-wrestling it back while Chaerin yelled at both of them. The boys egged it on, the girls groaned.

Solar let out a shaky laugh. But the echo of Jungwon’s words stayed pressed against her ribs.

Wishing it was that simple.




The dorm was quieter than usual when they got back from the joint rehearsal. Eunchae had collapsed dramatically onto the couch with her shoes still on, Kazuha was scrolling on her phone with a serene little hum, Sakura had disappeared into her room with a face mask packet, and Yunjin was already raiding the fridge like they hadn’t just eaten staff-provided catering two hours ago.

Solar lingered in the entryway, tugging at the sleeves of her hoodie, still replaying the subtle brushes of Jungwon’s hand earlier—how his knuckles had hovered like he wanted to hold hers. The heat in her chest hadn’t cooled even after they’d parted ways, even after Chaerin’s antics with Heeseung had distracted the entire room.

She was halfway to slipping into her room when Chaewon’s voice caught her.

“Solar. A minute?”

Leader-tone. Not sharp, not cold—just the kind that carried weight.

Solar hesitated, then followed Chaewon into the kitchen. The lights buzzed overhead, pale against the warm scent of leftover coffee. Chaewon leaned against the counter, arms folded, her expression soft but serious.

“You seemed… close with Jungwon today.”

The words were gentle, not prying, but they landed like a stone in Solar’s stomach. She swallowed, shifting her weight. “We’re friends,” she said, too fast.

Chaewon’s eyebrow arched. “Friends who’ve already crossed lines. Don’t try to dodge it. I’m not blind.”

Solar flushed, fingers curling against the hem of her hoodie. “It’s… complicated.”

“I know it is.” Chaewon’s voice lowered. “That’s why I’m talking to you now, before it gets more complicated.”

Solar blinked at her, surprised. She’d expected a lecture, but Chaewon’s tone was careful, protective in a way that made her chest ache.

“You know I want you to be happy,” Chaewon said. “We all do. But Solar—HYBE has already made it clear. When it comes down to it, they’ll shield Jungwon before they shield you. You’ve seen it in the statements.”

Solar’s throat tightened. She hated that it was true.

“And you’re not just an idol anymore,” Chaewon pressed, her arms uncrossing as she leaned forward slightly. “You’re balancing esports on top of this. That’s already more than most people could handle. And now—if you add Jungwon into the mix…”

Solar whispered, almost defensive, “I’m not saying I can’t.”

“I know,” Chaewon said softly. “But I’m worried you’ll try so hard to prove you can that you’ll destroy yourself in the process. Idol. Gamer. Girlfriend. That’s three different lives you’re trying to live, Solar. Even the strongest person has limits.”

The words pierced in the way only truth could. Solar’s chest tightened, breath snagging.

Chaewon sighed, her voice dipping into something achingly maternal. “I just… I don’t want to see you break. Not again. Not when you’ve worked so hard to stand on both stages.”

Solar stared down at her hands, the sting of tears threatening but unspilled. Chaewon reached out, her fingers brushing lightly against Solar’s arm—a grounding touch.

“I’m not telling you not to care for him,” Chaewon added. “But I need you to think about what it will cost you. What it could cost all of us. If this gets out—if it’s more than the rumors—HYBE won’t hesitate to use you as the scapegoat. That’s not fair, but it’s reality.”

Solar’s lips parted, but no words came out. She hated that she couldn’t argue.

Chaewon let her silence stand, then gave her a small, tired smile. “Get some rest, okay? I only said this because I care. Don’t carry it alone.”

And with that, she slipped out of the kitchen, leaving Solar pressed against the counter, heart pounding like she’d been scolded—but it hadn’t been scolding at all. It was worse: it was love wrapped in fear.

Solar lingered a moment longer, staring at the dim reflection of herself in the microwave door. Three lives, Chaewon had said. Idol. Gamer. Girlfriend.

She wasn’t sure which one would collapse first.

That’s what drove her into her hoodie fifteen minutes later, slipping her phone and wallet into her pocket, the urge for cold night air gnawing at her. Her chest was too full, her thoughts too loud.

And maybe, just maybe, the craving for Peach Tea would settle her nerves.




The city at night always felt different without security staff in tow. Quieter, even if the traffic still hummed and neon still blinked across the river. Solar tugged her hoodie lower over her face, mask pulled high, hands jammed in her pockets as she walked. She told herself it was just for Peach Tea—just the comfort of something familiar and sweet after Chaewon’s words had cracked her open.

The little convenience store near the dorm was half-empty, fluorescent lights buzzing tiredly above rows of instant ramen and bottled drinks. Solar padded straight to the cooler in the back, yanking open the door with fingers that still trembled faintly.

Her heart stuttered when she saw her.

Minji. Standing at the next cooler over, a bottle of water dangling loosely in one hand, hair tucked behind her ear. She looked the same and not the same—still that perfect composure, but her shoulders sagged like even she wasn’t immune to exhaustion. Their eyes met in the glass reflection first, then for real.

Solar froze.

“Solar.” Minji’s voice was quiet, careful, like saying her name too loudly might shatter the moment.

Solar’s throat was dry. “Minji.”

Neither moved for a beat. The cooler hummed between them, a mechanical sigh.

“You’re out late,” Minji murmured finally, closing her cooler door.

“So are you,” Solar managed. Her hand hovered over the Peach Tea cans, fingers brushing the cold metal. It felt absurdly intimate to be caught doing something so mundane, after everything that had exploded online.

Minji’s gaze flicked down to Solar’s hand. “Still your favorite.”

Solar’s chest tightened. “Some things don’t change.”

Silence again. Not empty, not hostile—just weighted with everything unspoken.

Minji stepped closer, the faint scent of her perfume cutting through the sterile air of the store. Solar’s pulse kicked hard.

“I saw the leaks,” Minji said softly. “About your family. About you.”

Solar’s hand clenched around the can. “Everyone saw.”

“That doesn’t mean I’m not allowed to worry.”

Solar glanced at her sharply. “Worrying about me isn’t safe for you, Minji. Not right now.”

Minji’s eyes softened, something raw shining through. She lifted a hand almost without thinking, fingers brushing Solar’s cheekbone. The touch was featherlight, a single spark against skin.

The cooler door sighed shut behind Solar’s hand, Peach Tea sweating against her palm. Minji was still standing there, still too close, the hum of the fluorescent lights suddenly deafening.

“Look…” Minji’s voice wavered, but her eyes burned steady. “About Danielle. What she’s doing to you…” She trailed off, fists curling tight around the bottle of water. “I want to do something. I can’t just stand by and watch her ruin you.”

Solar blinked, caught off guard by the rawness. “Minji—”

“I don’t understand why she’s so hellbent on destroying you,” Minji pushed, anger fraying the edges of her usually perfect composure. “Every post, every rumor—it’s deliberate. It’s not just cruel, it’s obsession... I just feel like I have to do something. ”

Solar’s throat closed. She hadn’t expected this kind of fury from Minji, not directed at one of her own group members. “You can’t,” she said quietly, words dragging out of her like confession. “She’s with Jihoon.”

The name landed heavy. Minji’s expression flickered, confusion shading into something darker. “Jihoon?”

Solar nodded, tightening her grip on the can. “They’re working together. That’s why it never stops. Why it keeps getting worse.”

For a second Minji just stared, chest rising like she’d been punched. “That makes sense,” she whispered finally. “God. That makes too much sense.”

Solar gave a bitter laugh, trying to lighten the air. “Honestly, I’m just waiting for the next leak to say I’m secretly with you. That would really send everyone into a frenzy.”

But Minji’s face hardened. She shook her head sharply. “She won’t. She can’t.”

“Why not?” Solar challenged, forcing humor she didn’t feel.

“Because I’m New Jeans,” Minji said, low and certain. “If she drags me into it, she drags all of us. Danielle’s reckless, but she’s not stupid enough to sabotage her own group.”

The words hit Solar like cold water. She knew Minji was right, but the implication stung: Danielle could play with her reputation like it was disposable, but Minji’s was untouchable because of the group wrapped around her like armor.

“You sound sure,” Solar muttered.

Minji’s eyes softened again, the anger slipping into something more fragile. “I just don’t want you to think you’re alone in this. Even if I can’t step in… not the way I want to.”

Solar’s breath caught. The weight of the world was pressing on her shoulders, but here Minji was, quietly burning at her side.

“You always say the things I can’t let myself believe,” Solar whispered.

Minji’s hand twitched, like she wanted to reach for her again but didn’t dare. “Then believe them anyway.”

The staffer’s “closing in ten!” call shattered the bubble, but the look between them lingered like something carved in glass—dangerous, sharp, impossible to ignore.

At the register, Solar’s joke replayed in her head, now laced with a hollow ache. Secretly with you. It was the one rumor she almost wished Danielle would leak—because at least then, it would feel real.





















71: The Alligator Bites

The notification came while Solar was still curled up on the dorm couch, knees tucked beneath her hoodie, the TV flickering muted reruns of a music show.

Rascal [Discord]: Back already. Where's our carry idol?

It was followed by a string of emojis from Smurf — sparkles, trophies, and for some reason, a carrot. Super spammed reaction GIFs. Glister typed only: ㅋㅋㅋ

Solar's throat closed up and loosened all at once. It had been weeks since the Shock had felt like more than ghosts in her notifications, but there they were, her chaotic family crashing back into her world with the same blunt force as always.

Yunjin leaned over the couch. "They're back, aren't they?"

Solar nodded, cheeks warming. "Tournament's close. Crusty must've pulled them together for prep."

"Then you better answer before Rascal flies across the ocean to roast you in person."

Solar grinned despite the heaviness that had been strangling her lately. She typed back quickly: Still cracked. Still here. Don't make me carry harder than I already do.

Rascal immediately replied with a sticker of a cat holding a knife. Super spammed: SHE'S BACKKKKKK LET'S GOOOO.

Her phone buzzed again, this time a direct ping from Rascal: Don't ghost us. Scrims start soon. You still one of us.

The quiet weight in those words sank deeper than she wanted to admit.

Across the room, Sakura and Kazuha were sprawled out with Eunchae on the floor, half-buried in a pile of blankets. Sakura's phone kept lighting up, her thumbs moving faster than usual. Solar wouldn't have thought twice about it—Sakura texted plenty—but there was an edge to it tonight. The faint crease between her brows, the way she tilted her phone at odd angles as though she didn't want anyone catching the screen.

Eunchae noticed first, of course. "Unnie, who are you texting like that? You look like you're trying to hack into the Pentagon."

Kazuha peered over, her dancer's grace making the intrusion almost gentle. "It's not a game, is it? You don't game..."

Sakura froze. For half a second, guilt flashed across her face, gone just as quickly. She plastered on a soft smile. "Just...an old friend."

Solar's stomach turned with recognition. Nero. It had to be. She remembered the way Sakura stiffened whenever Nero's name came up during Shock practices, the briefest flicker of something complicated in her eyes.

Eunchae giggled, rolling onto her back. "Must be some friend. You look like you're gonna set the phone on fire."

Sakura flushed, tucking the device under her blanket. "It's nothing."

But the room didn't buy it. Kazuha tilted her head curiously. Yunjin shot Solar a knowing look, eyebrows raised as if to say, clocked it.

Solar forced a smile, her heart split between warmth at having her Shock family back and the uneasy knot of Sakura's secretive texting. It wasn't her business—but she couldn't shake the feeling that Nero was another storm waiting just off the coast.

Her phone buzzed again.

Smurf [Discord]: No excuses this season. You're here, right? Not half-idol, half-gamer. Just Shock.

Solar's thumb hovered over the keyboard, her chest tightening. Before she could answer, Rascal beat her to it:

Rascal [Discord]: She's ours too. Let her be both.

The message sat there, glowing, until Solar's eyes blurred. She blinked rapidly, swallowing the lump in her throat.

For a moment, the chaos of promotions, the rumors, the weight of Jungwon's kiss, Jihoon's threats—all of it quieted. The Shock were back. And in their own, messy, reckless way, they were reminding her of something she'd almost forgotten: she belonged.




The headset pressed snug against Solar's ears, drowning out the dorm's background noise. For the first time in days, her world shrank to a glowing screen, the clack of her keys, and the familiar callouts of her Shock teammates.

"Solar, you're cracked—focus their Zen!" Super's voice boomed through Discord, half command, half meme.

She grinned despite herself. "On it!" Her fingers danced across the keyboard, and Rascal's laughter filled the comms when her perfectly timed burst deleted the enemy support.

For twenty straight minutes, Solar wasn't Solar the idol, or Solar the scandal magnet. She was just Solar the gamer, holding her lane, hearing Smurf's calm tone and Nero's chaotic trash talk overlap like they always had. When the victory screen flashed, she threw her head back and laughed—a real, sharp laugh that cut through the haze of paranoia she'd been dragging like a chain.

"Good shit, Sol," Rascal said. "See? Still Shock. Doesn't matter what the clowns online say."

Her chest warmed. Yeah. This—this she could do.

The scrim ended, the boys bantering as Crusty's voice dipped in to talk about their next block, and Solar pulled her headset off. Her hair was damp against her temples, her heartbeat steady. She leaned back in her chair, savoring that brief pocket of peace.

Her phone buzzed.

Eunchae.

why are u trending again lol

The knot slammed back into Solar's chest so hard she nearly dropped the phone.

"What—" she muttered, scrambling to open the app. The notifications were already exploding. Her name, Jungwon's name, side by side in hashtags climbing into the tens of thousands.

Not again.

She bolted from her chair and padded down the hall, clutching the phone like it was a live wire. The glow from the living room spilled into the darkened hall; Eunchae was perched on the couch, phone held out like she'd been waiting.

"Unnie," Eunchae said, serious now, "you need to see this."

Chaewon and Yunjin were already there, Kazuha curled in the corner, Sakura scrolling so fast her thumb blurred. Solar's stomach bottomed out.

Onscreen was the clip. Grainy, like it had been filmed in secret. A sliver of a rehearsal room—her and Jungwon at the edges of the frame. Her wrist in his hand, his voice muffled but low, intense. Someone had spliced in an audio overlay, distorted but just clear enough to sound like him saying: You don't listen. You'll never change.

The next cut: her pulling her arm back, her expression twisted mid-motion into what could be read as flinching.

It was fake. She knew it instantly, in the pit of her bones. She remembered that day—remembered Jungwon handing her the new brace, his touch gentle, his voice almost too soft to hear. But in this edit, it looked like violence. Like history repeating.

The caption burned across the screen:

"Funny how patterns repeat. Like abuser, like lover."

The sound of her own blood roared in her ears.

"No," she whispered. "That's not—"

"It's spliced," Chaewon said firmly, but her jaw was tight. "Look at the cuts, the light changes. It's fake."

"But it doesn't matter if it's fake," Yunjin said. Her voice was sharp, not at Solar but at the world beyond their walls. "They've already decided it's real."

Eunchae leaned forward, her usual brightness gone. "The comments are brutal, unnie. They're saying you're cursed. That you ruin everyone you're close to."

Solar's breath caught. She swayed, catching herself on the arm of the couch. She thought she'd grown numb to hate, but this—this was different. This wasn't calling her unprofessional or messy. This was weaponizing her silence, her scars, and now dragging Jungwon into the same fire.

Kazuha finally looked up, her eyes wide but calm. "It feels intentional. Like they knew exactly what to stitch together to make people believe it."

Sakura's phone buzzed with a fresh notification. "They're saying HYBE should step in. But—" she hesitated, eyes flicking toward Solar, "it's all about Jungwon. Not you."

Solar squeezed her eyes shut. The bile in her throat threatened to choke her. Of course. Of course it would be. He was Enhypen's leader, HYBE's golden boy. She was... expendable.

Her phone buzzed again. The notifications wouldn't stop. Hashtags morphing into new ones: #ProtectJungwon, #SolarOut. Thousands of strangers rewriting her life into a tragedy they could consume.

She pressed the heel of her palm to her eye, fighting for air. "It's happening again."

Chaewon's hand landed on her shoulder, steady and warm. "No. Listen to me—it's not. You're not sixteen. You're not trapped with him. This is different."

"But it feels the same," Solar rasped. Her chest heaved. "Everyone's saying he's going to regret me. That I'll destroy him too."

"You won't," Yunjin cut in, fierce. "You hear me? You won't."

But Solar couldn't unclench the memory Jihoon had pressed into her—you'll always be weak, you'll always need me. And now the internet had picked up the chorus, rewriting her life with their poison.

Her screen buzzed again. Another wave of hashtags. She couldn't look. She shoved the phone face down onto the couch like it might burn her.

The room was quiet except for the faint buzz of electricity, the tick of the old wall clock. The girls' presence was a buffer, but the weight of the clip clung to her, thick and choking.

Somewhere, out there, Danielle was laughing. Jihoon was watching. And the world was starting to believe them.



The building felt different on "farewell" nights. Lighter and heavier at the same time—like everyone was holding their breath and finally starting to let it go. The practice wing still hummed, but it was a softer hum: speakers turned down low, someone's laughter ricocheting off mirrors, gaffer tape curled at the corners where feet had chewed it for weeks.

Le Sserafim and Enhypen had just wrapped their last joint rehearsal block for the goodbye stages. Staff wheeled garment racks like migrating herds. A PA walked by with a box of mic batteries, counting under her breath. The LED wall in the far studio looped highlights from both comebacks: UNFORGIVEN's whipcord swagger bleeding into Bite Me's razor elegance. It looked unreal, even to Solar—like she'd watched a documentary about a girl who happened to share her face.

She slipped out of the main room on the exhale, through the gap where the door never closed flush because the hinge had been kicked one too many times. The side practice room was dim, lights halved. A cleaning spray bottle sat abandoned on the piano, leaving the faint, high smell of alcohol in the air. The mirrors kept the last of the stage in their glass—glitter dust, fingerprints, the ghost of a formation.

Solar pressed her palms to the cool barre and felt the sting in her forearms go quiet. Her phone stayed facedown on the bench. She'd made it twenty minutes without looking. That felt like breaking a world record.

Knuckles tapped twice against the doorframe: not a question, not an order—just a small sound asking permission.

Jungwon stood there in his hoodie and sweats, hair mussed from rewiring it for the last chorus. He didn't fill a room so much as change the air in it; even standing still, he felt like a held breath. Behind him, down the hall, she could still hear the boys' voices climbing over one another—Jake's easy tenor, Niki's endless bit, Heeseung clapping once, loud, like a period. But Jungwon had stepped out of that gravity and into hers.

"I knocked," he said, mouth tipping, as if to prove he could follow rules.

"You did." Solar tried on a smile. It sat wrong and slid off. "You should be stretching."

"I did." He countered it with a small shrug. "You weren't in the room."

Her pulse did that traitor thing—one clean kick and a scatter. The doctored clip tried to claw its way back into the front of her brain; she shoved it down, steadying on the barre.

He crossed the floor, not touching, but every inch closer shifted the calibration inside her. The mirror doubled him—two Jungwons and her own reflection between them: glossy, tired, braced against something she couldn't name without breaking it.

"You iced?" he asked, chin dipping toward her wrist without letting his eyes drop to it. The brace he'd bought her—sleek, low-profile, athlete-grade—peeked from the hoodie cuff like a secret he'd wrapped around her.

"I'm fine." Her voice was steadier than she felt. "Don't start a war with my physiotherapist."

A breath ghosted out of him, almost a laugh. "Not my best PR move."

"None of your PR moves are your best," she said lightly, and watched it hit him: the way his jaw softened, the way his eyes spoke before he did.

He stopped close enough for the raw scent of rehearsal—laundry detergent and skin— to drown out the alcohol in the air. His fingers twitched once at his side, quiet as a thought.

"Solar," he said.

The way he said her name made the room too small.

"Don't," she said, because it was easier to stop a word than a hand.

"Don't what?" It wasn't defensive. It was real confusion, edged with restraint. "Talk to you? Stand here? Breathe?"

"Touch me," she said, fast, before she could lose her nerve. She flicked her gaze toward the mirror, which might as well have been a window. "There are eyes everywhere tonight. Cameras. Phones. People who want to prove the worst thing."

His mouth hardened, not toward her but toward the world. "They don't get to decide if I can be near you."

Her throat worked. "They do if they film it."

He didn't break eye contact. "Let them record me standing where I'm supposed to stand."

She wanted to say it soothed her. It did and it didn't. The comfort landed; the consequences rose like tide around it.

"I can't—" Solar took a breath that snagged halfway down. "I can't keep handing them a script. Danielle is already writing it. You saw the clip, right?"

His nostrils flared. She had her answer. "I saw a lie that looks expensive."

"People don't buy truth," she said. "They buy production value."

He took another step, slower, the way you approach a wild thing that could either bolt or lean into your hand. "I know what your face looks like when you're angry. I know what your voice does when you're scared. That video wasn't either."

"HYBE doesn't care what you know," she said, an edge slipping into the words before she could blunt it. "They care what plays."

"I'm not them."

"I know." She did. She knew it down to the bone. But they were still there—the suits who pressed out statements like starched shirts, the ones who'd already shown her where she existed in the hierarchy. "They will protect you before they even remember I'm a person."

Something in him flashed—hurt, then heat. "I didn't ask for that."

"I know," she repeated, softer, because she'd meant it as a confession, not an accusation. "But it still happens. And every time you—" she stopped, stomach flip-flopping at how close he was, "—every time you do something like this, the world sets me on fire and hands you a coat."

His hand lifted, reflexive. He caught it halfway up, fingers hovering six inches from her sleeve like a picture of a touch. Something flickered across his face: the memory of the last time he'd closed that distance and how it had ended—heat, breath, a vow sealed with a mouth.

"I'm not trying to make it worse," he said, voice low. "I'm trying to make it less lonely."

The ache was immediate. She felt it like a bruise under the skin. "It is less lonely," she said. "That's the problem. It makes me forget how loud it gets when you leave the room."

"I don't want to keep leaving the room," he said simply.

The unspoken part hung there like a struck note: I don't want to leave you.

They looked at each other in the mirror, not directly—through that thin layer of glass where everything was truer because you could pretend it wasn't. Music bled through the wall, a tech blasting tomorrow's cue tracks while he coiled cables. A staffer laughed down the hall. Somewhere, someone dropped a mic pack and swore.

"Chaewon talked to me," Solar said, the admission catching, small and sharp. "She did her leader thing. Reality talk. Logistics and consequences and...love, basically, but with numbers."

A corner of his mouth tipped. "Sounds like her."

"She's worried about you," Solar said. "And me. And Le Sserafim. And your group. And—" She huffed out a breath. "She's worried I'm going to try to hold all of it and then drown."

"You won't," he said.

"You don't know that."

"I know you don't drown without setting the water on fire first."

A helpless, involuntary laugh bubbled up. It made her eyes sting worse. "That's not comforting."

"It is to me."

He shifted a fraction, close enough that she could see the pale crescent scar under his eye she hadn't noticed before, the one that only shows if you lean in. She thought, wildly, of the cast he'd ordered for her, the way he'd slid it around her wrist like he was fitting her for armor.

"Jungwon," she said, saying his name like a tug on a tether. "We still haven't—" she gestured vaguely between them, a helpless, embarrassed sweep, "—talked. About...whatever we think this is."

His eyes didn't flinch. "We will."

"When?" she asked, half a plea, half a challenge.

"When you're not bracing for impact every second," he said. "When I'm not two steps from punching a wall because someone edited the sound of your breath."

"Not helpful," she said, and managed to smile.

"I'm trying not to be helpful," he said. "I'm trying to be honest."

"Honesty gets you killed," she said without thinking, remembering the rooftop, remembering sixteen.

"Silence kills slower," he said. "And I'm done with slow."

Her phone buzzed on the bench. One buzz. Then two. Then the long trill of a pinned push alert. Solar didn't move. She could feel Jungwon watching the way her shoulders tensed, like a deer listening for a twig snap.

"Don't," he said softly. "Not yet."

"I have to." She didn't know if that was true. But it felt true. She picked up the phone.

The HYBE logo sat above a block of text, black on white, corporate and careful.

HYBE has become aware of maliciously edited content circulating online falsely characterizing interactions involving ENHYPEN member Jungwon. We condemn the creation and spread of such fabrications. We will be taking legal action against the original posters and any accounts continuing to defame our artist. We ask fans and the media to refrain from speculation and to respect the artist's privacy.

Regarding other individuals depicted or referenced in said content, we will not be commenting further at this time.

It kept going—lawyer words, PR words—but the damage had already been done by the second paragraph. The way they had built a wall around him, the way they'd left her outside it with the weather.

Solar read it twice. Her chest did that hollow-then-heavy pendulum swing. She turned the screen toward him.

"Don't you see, Won?"

He read fast. He always did. His face emptied out—not blank, not numb. Focused. His eyes stalled on the line about "other individuals." Then lifted to her.

"I see a company defending their asset," he said evenly. "And I see them pretending you don't have a name."

"It's not pretending," she said. The bitterness surprised her with its clean edge. "They're good at what they do. They made you clean. They made me...implied."

He started to speak. Stopped. Tried again. "This isn't you versus me."

"It becomes that," she said. "Every time they hit 'post.'" She swallowed hard. "Every time you stand too close to me in a hallway, someone turns my face into a weapon and yours into a shield."

He took the thought like a punch and didn't step back. "Then I'll be a shield. So what?"

"Shields break," she said. "And I don't want to be the thing breaking you."

"That's not how this works."

"It's exactly how this works." She pushed the heels of her hands into her eye sockets until stars burst behind her lids. "You know it is. You feel untouchable because they keep proving you are. I'm not. I'm—" she broke off, breath hitching in a sound too close to a laugh. "I'm a risk assessment."

His hands curled once and uncurled, like he was forcing them to remember gentle. "You're a person," he said. "You're—" He bit down on the word, the one that kept almost living in his mouth lately, the one he wasn't going to give her here, now, under half-dead fluorescents and a recording light that could be on without either of them knowing. "I'm not going to let a PDF tell me what you are to me."

"It's not the PDF I'm scared of." She slid the phone onto the bench like it was hot. "It's all of it. The boys you love. My girls. The tour. The tournament. My family. Your company. Every time I breathe near you, someone pays for it. And it's never you."

"That's not fair," he said quietly.

"I know." The apology was a soft thing she couldn't shape right. "It's just...true."

They stood there in the too-bright, too-dim room, the kind of silence that makes you hear the electricity in the walls. The mirror tried to translate them and failed.

Down the hall, a stage manager stuck his head into a doorway and called, "Run-of-show meeting in ten! Leaders only!" Chaewon's voice answered yes. Heeseung's carried a casual okay. The world kept moving like it had somewhere important to be.

Jungwon's hand lifted again. This time it almost made it all the way—hovered, hovered, then touched. Barely. The edge of his knuckles brushed the sleeve at her wrist, a whisper of contact that didn't show in mirrors and wouldn't get anybody sued. It was nothing. It set the room on fire.

"I can step back in a hallway," he said, eyes never leaving hers. "I can keep my hands to myself until there are walls that deserve to see it. But I won't step out of your life. Don't ask me."

Her throat closed. She wanted to be brave and say Don't go anywhere. She wanted to be careful and say Please leave before someone walks by. What came out was small and truer.

"I'm tired," she whispered.

"I know." His voice softened until it wasn't a voice, it was a place. "Go with your girls. I'll take whatever's coming from my side."

"That's the part that scares me," she said, mouth tipping, near-hysterical. "You look at the storm like it's a door."

"It is," he said. "And you've already walked through worse."

She stared at him until her eyes stung again. The hallway clattered with footsteps. The ten-minute call shrank to seven.
