The phone buzzed one more time. She picked it up with trembling fingers, almost against her will. The photo filled her screen again, plastered across a trending post with thousands of retweets. Her own face stared back at her, sixteen and lost, paired with words she couldn't erase: Ask her what she was doing at sixteen... and who let her get there.

Her stomach turned. She wanted to smash the phone. Instead, she set it down carefully, as if any sudden movement might shatter the fragile balance she had left.

"I can't let them see this," she whispered to the empty room. Not her girls. Not her siblings. Not Jungwon. Especially not Jungwon. If he looked at that version of her, would he still reach for her hand? Or would he see what Jihoon wanted him to see—a weakness, a flaw, a girl not worth the fight?

The thought was unbearable.

She curled onto her side, pulling her knees tight to her chest, trying to fold herself into something small enough to hide. But the internet didn't let you hide. Not when it smelled blood. Not when Danielle was still out there, feeding it piece by piece. Not when Jihoon knew exactly how to turn her past into a weapon.

The dorm felt too quiet now. Too fragile. Every normal sound outside her door felt like a lie. She pressed her forehead into her pillow, and for the first time since the rooftop, she let herself think it: maybe Jaehyun was right. Maybe the storm couldn't be fought with fire. Maybe silence was the only way to survive.

But silence had never been her strength.




The Lee apartment always smelled faintly of sandalwood and something sharper, like Jaehyun insisted on airing out his anger with incense. Solar stood at the threshold long enough for the weight of the place to settle on her shoulders. The city lights spilled through the wide window, catching on the polished floors and the neat rows of furniture, everything lined up the way Jaehyun liked: precise, orderly, cold.

Chaerin was sprawled on the couch in a neon hoodie and leather shorts, a contrast so loud it looked like rebellion incarnate. Beside her, Heeseung perched on the armrest, one leg hooked casually, his dark hair falling into his eyes. He looked comfortable here—too comfortable. Solar blinked at that. When had he started spending this much time in their home?

"You're late," Jaehyun's voice cut through, clipped as ever. He didn't turn from the window, his sunglasses still perched even though night had swallowed the city. His reflection stretched across the glass, an immovable figure. "I don't like waiting."

Solar set her bag down, resisting the urge to roll her eyes. "You called me ten minutes ago. I came straight here."

"Straight here," he echoed, like the words carried another meaning. He finally turned, and the air in the room thickened. "And yet the internet got here first."

The silence cracked. Chaerin sat up, bristling. "Oh, here we go again."

Jaehyun ignored her, his gaze slicing toward Solar. "You saw it, didn't you?"

She didn't answer. She didn't need to. The photo was already carved into her eyelids.

Jaehyun stepped forward, slow, deliberate. "This is what happens when you think you can outrun history. It doesn't matter what you're doing now. What matters is what people can make them believe you did then."

Solar's hands curled into fists. "It was doctored. Cropped. Danielle twisted it."

"And the public doesn't care," he snapped. "They see what they're told to see. And what they see is weakness."

"Don't call her weak." Chaerin was on her feet now, her voice hot. "She's stronger than anyone. She's still standing while everyone else is trying to tear her apart."

Jaehyun's head tilted, clinical. "Strong? Strong is staying silent and clean. Strong is protecting the family name instead of dragging it through the dirt."

Heeseung cleared his throat, hesitant but firm. "With respect, hyung... Solar didn't drag anyone anywhere. People are targeting her, not the other way around. And Jungwon—" he paused, carefully, "—he doesn't deserve your hate either. He's doing what he can."

Solar's breath hitched. The way Heeseung said Jungwon's name, steady and loyal, stung more than it soothed.

Jaehyun turned on him, voice dropping to ice. "This is not your business."

Heeseung didn't flinch. "Maybe not. But if you're going to stand here and act like Jungwon's the problem, then yeah—it is my business. He's my leader. He's my brother. And—" his gaze flicked toward Chaerin, softening just slightly, "—I don't think I'm out of line to care about what she cares about."

The tension snapped taut. Chaerin flushed, torn between embarrassment and pride. Solar could see it—the spark in her sister's eyes, the way she almost smiled before Jaehyun's voice crushed the moment.

"You are a guest in this house," Jaehyun said, sharp enough to cut. "And if you want to keep stepping foot in it, you'll remember where your boundaries are."

Heeseung's jaw tightened. He looked ready to argue, but Chaerin's hand found his wrist, squeezing once. A warning. He stayed quiet, though his eyes burned.

"Jae," Solar said, forcing herself forward, into the crossfire. "This isn't about Jungwon. Or Heeseung. Or Chaerin. This is Danielle. This is Jihoon. You know it."

Jaehyun's gaze cut into her like glass. "And who let them get this close? Who stood there with her guard down, letting him believe he could play hero?" His words were daggers, aimed to wound. "You don't understand what you're dealing with. Jungwon's recklessness is going to break you faster than Danielle ever could."

Chaerin's temper snapped. "Why do you hate him so much? He's not Jihoon!"

Jaehyun's mouth pressed into a thin line. For the first time, a flicker of something almost human crossed his expression. Pain, maybe. Regret, quickly buried. "No," he said quietly. "But fire burns the same, no matter who lights it."

Solar's chest constricted. "Stop," she said, her voice fraying. "You don't get to stand there and compare them. You don't get to act like you know him."

"I know enough." Jaehyun's voice rose, thunder cracking in the confined space. "I know what happens when you fall for the wrong kind of boy. I know how much it costs to clean up the wreckage."

Solar flinched. The words dragged shadows she'd tried to bury, old nights and blurred edges. Sixteen. Jihoon's grip. The sharp taste of shame. She shoved it down. Not here. Not now.

Chaerin's voice trembled with fury. "What are you even talking about, Jae? You make it sound like—like—" She broke off, lost in her own confusion. "What the hell are you hiding?"

Jaehyun didn't answer. He turned back toward the window, sunglasses reflecting the city's glow. "Don't ask questions you aren't ready to hear answers to."

The silence after that was brutal. Chaerin's hand was still tight on Heeseung's wrist, her eyes blazing. Heeseung's shoulders stiffened, but he stayed quiet. Solar stood in the center of it all, heart pounding, guilt clawing higher.

Her phone buzzed in her pocket, insistent, like the world outside refused to wait. She ignored it. For once, she couldn't look. Not when everything inside this room already felt like it was crumbling.

Solar's lungs felt raw. Her siblings' voices layered with Heeseung's defense, Jaehyun's barbs, Jungwon's absence. Every piece of it cut at her in different ways until it felt like her skin couldn't hold her together anymore.

"I can't—" The words cracked out before she could stop them. Every eye turned to her, and it made her skin crawl. "I need air."

She didn't wait for Jaehyun's clipped response, or for Chaerin's half-formed protest. She just shoved her shoes on and stepped into the hall, shutting the door on the static behind her.



Seoul at night was its own kind of noise, but it was one she could almost trick herself into finding soothing. Neon light bounced off puddles left from a brief evening shower, car horns stitched through the streets, and clusters of students laughed too loudly outside fried chicken shops.

Solar walked fast, hood pulled up, mask tugged high. Her phone buzzed in her pocket with the kind of relentless rhythm that told her another hashtag was trending, another rumor taking flight. She didn't check it.

Her head already played back the worst of them:
She was at parties before she was even legal.
Her whole family's dirty. Look at Jaehyun's business ties.
Jungwon deserves better than a scandal magnet like her.

Each one hooked into her ribs and dragged.

Jaehyun's words gnawed at her, too. The way he'd looked at her when he said it wasn't fake. The way his jaw ticked when she'd tried to argue.

Her mind threw her the faintest memory from earlier—the smudge of purple under Jungwon's makeup as he passed her in the hallway. The wince he'd tried to hide during rehearsal. She'd buried it deep, convinced herself it was lighting, shadows, exhaustion. But Jaehyun's voice made it echo. It's not fake, Solar.

Her throat closed. She wanted to scream at the city, tell it to stop watching, stop speculating, stop turning her into a headline.

Instead, her feet carried her toward the only place that had ever felt hers when everything spun too fast. The convenience store at the corner near HYBE—fluorescent-lit, smelling faintly of ramen packets and peach tea bottles. The place she'd run into Jungwon too many times for coincidence, where quiet moments tucked themselves between aisles of instant meals and cheap candy.

She pressed her hands deeper into her hoodie pocket as she walked, jaw trembling with the effort not to let the tears rise. She could almost hear Yunjin's voice in her head: Let me carry it with you.

But Yunjin wasn't here.

And Solar was so tired of needing someone to carry her.

The glass doors of the store slid open as she approached, spilling warm light onto the wet pavement. Her chest lifted in a shaky breath, bracing for the small relief of ordinary shelves and humming freezers.

She wasn't ready for what waited inside.

The fluorescent hum of the convenience store wrapped around Solar like static. She tugged her hood lower as she slipped inside, the automatic doors sighing shut behind her.

It was late enough that the streets outside were thinning, neon signs blurring against damp pavement. The kind of hour where no one should notice an idol sneaking out in slippers, clutching at scraps of normalcy.

Her slippers squeaked faintly against the linoleum as she headed toward the refrigerated drinks. She didn't need to think about it—her body knew the path by heart. Third aisle, second door, middle shelf. Peach Tea, her one ritual she'd clung to like it was proof she still had a self outside the leaks and the labels.

But when she reached for the door handle, another hand was already there.

Her breath stalled.

Minji.

The fluorescent light made her skin look too pale, like porcelain that might crack under one more wrong headline. Her black hoodie hung loose, sleeves covering half her hands. She blinked once, slow, recognition clicking into place like a knife unsheathed.

"Solar," she said softly. Not her stage name. Not the careful, public version. Just her.

Solar's pulse scrambled. She almost laughed—of course. The universe had replaced Jungwon with Minji again. The store that used to mean relief now seemed determined to confront her with every tangled thread in her life.

Minji didn't move her hand from the fridge door. Her gaze flicked over Solar's face like she was memorizing every crack. "You look..." She stopped herself, searching for a gentler word. "...tired."

Solar swallowed. Her throat ached from holding too much in. "I could say the same to you."

Something flickered across Minji's expression, quick and bitter. "We both know why I'm here."

Danielle.

The word didn't need to be spoken. It sat between them, sour as vinegar.

Solar's hand slipped into her hoodie pocket, nails biting into her palm. "You shouldn't—"

"I know." Minji cut her off, voice firmer than Solar expected. "I know what it means to say too much. To do too much. But I can't watch her keep doing this to you."

The words hit like a stone dropped into water. Ripples spread through Solar's chest, dangerous in how much they threatened to undo her composure.

"She wants to break you," Minji whispered. Her hand finally slipped from the fridge door, falling to her side. "And I hate that I can't stop her."

Solar's laugh cracked down the middle, brittle. "You think I don't know that?" She gestured vaguely, helplessly, toward the glowing screen of her phone in her pocket. "She's already breaking me."

Minji's eyes softened, and for a moment, Solar hated it. Hated the pity, the tenderness, the reminder that people could still look at her like she was worth saving.

"I'm fine," Solar lied, voice sharper than intended.

Minji didn't flinch. She stepped closer, slow, deliberate, like she was approaching a frightened animal. "You're not."

The air went taut. Solar's chest burned, and she realized too late that her eyes were stinging. Her body betrayed her with a single tear sliding hot down her cheek.

Minji moved before Solar could stop her. The sleeve of her hoodie brushed Solar's cheek, wiping the tear with careful gentleness. The kind that said I see you breaking, and I'll hold the pieces even if you don't ask me to.

Solar's breath hitched. She should've stepped back. She should've laughed it off. But she didn't.

Because Minji's sleeve lingered, hand hovering near Solar's face like she wanted to cup her cheek and didn't dare. And Solar—aching, hollow, desperate—let herself lean into the warmth for one stolen second.

Her tears came faster then, quiet and stubborn, sliding against Minji's sleeve.

Without speaking, Minji pulled her into a hug. Not the stiff, one-arm kind idols gave on camera. A full embrace, arms wrapping tight, her chin pressing lightly against Solar's shoulder.

And she didn't let go.

Solar froze for half a heartbeat. Then something in her cracked, and she sank into it. Just for a moment. Just for the length of a breath where she could pretend the world wasn't watching, wasn't waiting to devour her mistakes.

Minji smelled faintly of laundry soap and mint gum. Nothing fiery, nothing dangerous—just soft, ordinary comfort. It was everything Jungwon's fire wasn't, and that made it terrifying in a different way.

Because she didn't want to pull away.

Her hands curled into Minji's hoodie, clinging like she was sixteen again and desperate for anything that felt like safety. Her chest heaved against Minji's steady one, her body betraying how much she needed to be held.

"Solar," Minji murmured, voice muffled against her hair. "You don't have to carry it all. Not with me."

Solar's throat closed around the words she wanted to say. That she couldn't. That she shouldn't. That Minji was too close, too dangerous, too right.

Her silence said it for her.

And then the air shifted.

Solar felt it before she saw it—the way her skin prickled, the way her body knew someone else had stepped into their orbit. She lifted her head, slow, dread pooling in her stomach.

The automatic doors had sighed open again.

Jungwon stood in the entryway.

Hood up, mask tugged low, but his eyes—those sharp, unreadable eyes—were visible. And they were locked on her.

On them.

His jaw tightened. The kind of small, lethal movement that meant more than shouting ever could. His fists flexed once at his sides, knuckles pale against the dark fabric of his sleeves.

He didn't say a word.

Didn't need to.

The sight of him there, storm bottled tight in his stillness, was enough to make the fluorescent air taste electric.

Minji felt it too. Solar sensed her stiffen, arms loosening around her, though she didn't step back entirely. She lingered, as if daring Jungwon to misinterpret.

Solar's heart thundered so loud it drowned out the refrigerator hum. She opened her mouth, but no sound came.

Jungwon's gaze flicked once—Minji's hand still at Solar's shoulder, Solar's tear-streaked face tilted toward her. Then back to Solar.

He didn't move further into the store. He just stood there, his presence a wall of heat and tension, like the storm outside the glass doors had finally followed her in.

For a moment, no one breathed.

Solar felt caught between two gravities: Minji's soft pull still anchoring her close, Jungwon's magnetic fire sparking from across the room.

The cage of the world was closing in. But here, in this too-bright, too-small store, it was her own heart writing the script.

The silence pressed too heavy. Solar finally pulled back from Minji, but not far—just enough that the loss of warmth felt sharper than the comfort itself. Her cheeks burned, whether from tears or the weight of Jungwon’s stare, she couldn’t tell.

“Won…” she whispered, like saying his name might diffuse the storm coiled in his posture.

It didn’t.

Jungwon didn’t answer. His jaw flexed once more, sharp under the store’s fluorescent glare, before his mask slid back up into place. He looked like he could burn through both of them with a single glance.

Minji straightened, chin tilting up slightly as if to say she wasn’t ashamed. Her hand still hovered near Solar’s arm, a quiet anchor.

Solar wanted to shrink into the shelves, disappear into the glass refrigerator doors. But she couldn’t move. Couldn’t breathe.

Because Jungwon did move—finally. Not toward her. Not toward Minji. Just one step further inside, heavy enough to say I’m here. I saw. And I’m not walking away.

The air crackled, unbearable. Solar’s pulse ricocheted against her ribs. She felt like the whole store had turned into a stage with three unwilling actors, the script unwritten but the ending inevitable.

For a split second, she imagined the tabloids catching this moment: Minji’s hoodie sleeve still damp from her tears, Jungwon’s stare a blade, her own face trapped in the middle. It would take one photo, one caption, to end them all.

And still, she didn’t step away from either of them.

The automatic doors hummed shut behind Jungwon with a hiss, sealing the cage.
